topics.title: Hrs. 30 Min. Prerequisites (if any) None Course Type Lab Course Assessment Method (CIE: 50 Marks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Hrs. 30 Min. Prerequisites (if any) None Course Type Lab Course Assessment Method (CIE: 50 Marks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

Under the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** (aligned with NEP 2020), every B.Tech programme is delivered as a structured combination of **Theory courses**, **Lab / Practical courses**, **Project courses**, and **Audit / Value-Added courses**. The metadata block associated with each course describes four mandatory administrative parameters that govern the **delivery, eligibility, classification, and evaluation** of that course.

For the present course reference **PCPOL607** (a **Programme Core** laboratory course), the parameters are formally defined as:

> **CIE 50 Marks: Continuous Internal Evaluation** — the *sum total of marks* allotted for in-semester, lab-internal assessment conducted by the **parent institution** (not the University). It is **not** added to the End Semester Examination (ESE) marks; instead, the lab course carries a **separate pass requirement** in CIE as mandated by the 2024 Scheme regulations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Course Hours = 30 Hours** is the *aggregate contact-time* prescribed for the **entire laboratory course** (all experiments + viva + record evaluation cycles), **not** for a single experiment. This is mapped against the standard 1-credit-to-30-hour rule of the NEP 2020 framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prerequisites: None** is an *administrative declaration* that this is a **standalone laboratory course** — it can be registered for in the same semester it is offered, without needing to clear any earlier course. The faculty, however, may still expect **assumed knowledge** from earlier foundational lab courses in the same programme.

| Parameter | Value | Formal Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Hrs** | **30** | Total contact hours for the lab course |
| **Prerequisites** | **None** | No pre-courses required for registration |
| **Course Type** | **Lab** | Practical / Hands-on evaluation course |
| **Assessment** | **CIE 50 Marks** | Continuous Internal Evaluation only — no ESE |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Lab" Intuition

Imagine you are enrolling in a **cooking school**:

- The **30 Hours** is the *total time the chef-instructor will spend with you* in the kitchen over the whole semester — not the time to cook one dish.
- **No Prerequisites** means you do **not** need to have finished "Knife Skills 101" before joining "Baking Basics" — you can walk in raw.
- **Lab Course Type** is like a **kitchen practical** — your grade depends on what you *actually cook and present*, not on a written theory paper.
- **CIE 50 Marks** is the *continuous tasting* by the head chef across all your dishes — there is **no final restaurant exam**; the chef is grading every dish you plate during the term.

> [!TIP]
> A Lab-only course (CIE 50, no ESE) is a **"continuous performance" course** — your *every practical session* contributes to your final mark. You cannot "blow off" the first half and recover at the end.

---

## 1.3 Standard KTU Constants for Lab Courses

- **Total Marks** = **50** (CIE only, out of 50)
- **Pass Minimum** = **50 %** of CIE (i.e., **≥ 25 / 50**)
- **Total Contact Hours per Lab Course** = **30 hours** (typically 10 experiments × 3 hours)
- **Standard Class Strength per Lab Batch** = Maximum **30 students** (split into smaller sub-batches of 15 or less)
- **Minimum Experiments Required** = **At least 8 to 10 experiments** (decided by the course committee)

> [!WARNING]
> Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, a *Lab-only* course has **no ESE component**. Therefore, the **ESE 50 marks** rule that applies to theory courses does **not apply** here. Misreading this is a common valuation pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1: Course Hours = 30 Hrs

The **30 hours** is the **cumulative laboratory contact time** prescribed for the course over **one full semester** (typically 14–16 instructional weeks). The KTU 2024 Scheme maps this as follows:

- **1 Lab Credit = 30 Hours** of practical contact time.
- The course PCPOL607 carries **1 credit** (some lab courses carry 2 credits → 60 hours).
- These 30 hours are distributed as:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_i + T_{\text{viva}} + T_{\text{record}}
$$

where $t_i$ is the duration of the $i^{th}$ experiment, $N$ is the total number of experiments, $T_{\text{viva}}$ is the time spent on continuous viva, and $T_{\text{record}}$ is the time for record evaluation.

### Typical Hour Distribution (KTU Standard)

| Activity | Hours (per semester) | % of Total |
|---|---|---|
| Conducting Experiments (8–10 experiments) | **22 – 26 Hrs** | ~75 – 85 % |
| Continuous Viva & Discussion | **2 – 4 Hrs** | ~7 – 13 % |
| Record Checking & Internal Tests | **2 – 4 Hrs** | ~7 – 13 % |
| **Total** | **30 Hrs** | **100 %** |

---

## 2.2 Component 2: Prerequisites — None

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (NEP-aligned), the syllabus can declare a course as having **"Nil"** prerequisites, which is a formal administrative choice. This indicates:
> 1. **No academic pre-courses** are required for registration.
> 2. The course can be taken **in any semester** in which it is offered.
> 3. The course may still expect **assumed prior knowledge** (e.g., from earlier foundation courses) but does not enforce it as a registration barrier.

### Why "Prerequisites: None"?

- Allows **flexibility** in semester registration (NEP 2020 philosophy).
- Encourages **interdisciplinary** lab skills development.
- Reduces **registration bottlenecks** for slow-track learners.

---

## 2.3 Component 3: Course Type = Lab

A **Lab course** in KTU 2024 Scheme is operationally defined as a course whose **assessment is fully based on in-semester performance** — there is **no University-conducted End Semester Examination (ESE)** for this course.

### Operational Sub-Classification of Lab Courses

| Sub-Type | Description | ESE Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure Lab (CIE 50)** | Entirely continuous assessment | **No ESE** |
| **Lab + Theory Embedded** | Some lab work, but combined with theory ESE | ESE present |
| **Project Lab** | Term-project based | CIE + ESE (viva) |

The current course (PCPOL607) falls into the **"Pure Lab"** category since the assessment is **CIE 50 Marks only**.

---

## 2.4 Component 4: CIE 50 Marks — The Internal Evaluation Engine

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) for a **50-Mark Lab** is structured by the University as a **mandatory internal marking scheme**. The faculty cannot assign marks arbitrarily — they must follow the indicative weightage below:

### Standard CIE 50-Mark Split (Indicative)

| Component | Marks | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **Experiment Performance** (in-lab conduct) | **20 – 24** | Every session |
| **Lab Record / Observation Book** | **10 – 12** | Periodic check |
| **Continuous Viva Voce** | **6 – 8** | Every session |
| **Internal Lab Tests (2 tests)** | **8 – 10** | Twice per semester |
| **Total** | **50** | — |

### Formula for CIE Final Mark

$$
M_{\text{CIE}} = M_{\text{perf}} + M_{\text{record}} + M_{\text{viva}} + M_{\text{test}}
$$

Subject to:

$$
M_{\text{CIE}} \leq 50, \qquad M_{\text{CIE}}^{\text{min pass}} \geq 25
$$

where $M_{\text{perf}}$ = experiment performance marks, $M_{\text{record}}$ = record mark, $M_{\text{viva}}$ = viva mark, $M_{\text{test}}$ = test mark.

---

## 2.5 The 30-Hour ↔ CIE-50 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **30 hours of contact time** is the *resource budget* that must be optimally spent to **generate sufficient evidence** for awarding **50 CIE marks**. Universities use this hours-to-marks ratio to audit whether labs are:
> - Conducting the **required number of experiments** (≥ 8).
> - Allowing **meaningful viva** (≥ 2 hours aggregate).
> - Holding **at least 2 internal lab tests**.

The audit check is essentially:

$$
\frac{T_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{exp}}} \approx 3 \text{ hours / experiment}
$$

$$
\frac{T_{\text{viva}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \geq 0.07
$$

These are *soft constraints* used during academic audits.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Lab Hours | $T_{\text{total}} = \mathbf{30\ Hrs}$ | Per semester, per course |
| 2 | Credits | $1 \text{ Credit} = 30 \text{ Hrs}$ | NEP 2020 rule |
| 3 | Number of Experiments (typical) | $N_{\text{exp}} = 8 \text{ to } 10$ | $T_{\text{total}} / N_{\text{exp}} \approx 3$ hrs |
| 4 | Total CIE Marks | $M_{\text{CIE}} = \mathbf{50}$ | No ESE for lab-only |
| 5 | Pass Minimum | $M_{\text{pass}} = 0.50 \times M_{\text{CIE}} = \mathbf{25}$ | Mandatory |
| 6 | Performance Component | $M_{\text{perf}} \approx 0.40$ to $0.48 \times M_{\text{CIE}}$ | $\approx 20$–$24$ marks |
| 7 | Record Component | $M_{\text{record}} \approx 0.20$ to $0.24 \times M_{\text{CIE}}$ | $\approx 10$–$12$ marks |
| 8 | Viva Component | $M_{\text{viva}} \approx 0.12$ to $0.16 \times M_{\text{CIE}}$ | $\approx 6$–$8$ marks |
| 9 | Internal Test Component | $M_{\text{test}} \approx 0.16$ to $0.20 \times M_{\text{CIE}}$ | $\approx 8$–$10$ marks |
| 10 | Prerequisite Constraint | $\text{Preq} = \phi \text{ (empty set)}$ | No blocking pre-course |
| 11 | Course Type | Type = **Lab** | Pure practical evaluation |
| 12 | ESE Marks | $M_{\text{ESE}} = 0$ | Not applicable |

> [!NOTE]
> The word *Prerequisites* must be read **literally as a set** in formal course-graph theory — it is the *set of courses that must be cleared before registration*. **{None}** = the empty set $\phi$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: How 30 Hours Are Allocated to a Standard Lab

We start from the KTU workload norm:

$$
1 \text{ Credit} \equiv 30 \text{ Contact Hours} \equiv T_{\text{total}}
$$

Assume the academic committee decides to run **$N = 10$ experiments** over the semester. Each experiment requires:

1. **Pre-lab briefing** (objectives, theory) — approx. $t_b = 0.25$ hr
2. **Hands-on conduction** — approx. $t_c = 2.00$ hr
3. **Post-lab wrap-up + observation submission** — approx. $t_w = 0.50$ hr
4. **Viva (continuous)** — approx. $t_v = 0.25$ hr

The total per-experiment time is:

$$
t_{\text{exp}} = t_b + t_c + t_w + t_v
$$

$$
t_{\text{exp}} = 0.25 + 2.00 + 0.50 + 0.25
$$

$$
t_{\text{exp}} = 3.00 \text{ hours / experiment}
$$

For $N = 10$ experiments:

$$
T_{\text{exp, total}} = N \times t_{\text{exp}} = 10 \times 3.00 = 30 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_{\text{exp, total}} = T_{\text{total}} = 30 \text{ hrs} \quad \blacksquare
$$

This is the **ideal-fit case** where the 30-hour budget is **fully consumed** by experiments. If $N = 8$:

$$
T_{\text{exp, total}} = 8 \times 3.00 = 24 \text{ hours}
$$

The remaining 6 hours are used for **2 internal lab tests** (3 hrs each):

$$
T_{\text{tests}} = 2 \times 3.00 = 6 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_{\text{exp, total}} + T_{\text{tests}} = 24 + 6 = 30 \text{ hrs} \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CIE Mark Distribution Logic

The 50 CIE marks must reflect the **time-intensity** of each lab activity. Define the **time-weight fraction** of an activity as:

$$
w_i = \frac{T_i}{T_{\text{total}}}
$$

For the case $N = 10$ experiments with continuous viva in each:

- $T_{\text{perf (in-lab conduct)}} = N \times t_c = 10 \times 2.00 = 20$ hrs
- $T_{\text{record}} = $ record-checking sessions $\approx 4$ hrs (spread across semester)
- $T_{\text{viva}} = N \times t_v = 10 \times 0.25 = 2.5$ hrs
- $T_{\text{tests}} = 2 \times 1.5 = 3$ hrs (model tests of 1.5 hr each)
- $T_{\text{wrap-up}} = N \times t_w = 10 \times 0.50 = 5$ hrs
- $T_{\text{briefing}} = N \times t_b = 10 \times 0.25 = 2.5$ hrs

**Total time accounted for:**

$$
T_{\text{acc}} = 20 + 4 + 2.5 + 3 + 5 + 2.5 = 37 \text{ hrs}
$$

Some activities (briefing + wrap-up) overlap with the experiment session itself, so effective contact is **30 hrs**.

### Mark Allocation by Proportional Time-Weight (Indicative)

$$
M_i = M_{\text{CIE}} \times \frac{T_i}{T_{\text{total, effective}}}
$$

For the dominant **performance + viva** block (which the evaluator can directly observe):

$$
M_{\text{perf + viva}} = 50 \times \frac{20 + 2.5}{30} = 50 \times 0.75 = 37.5
$$

This is then split as:

$$
M_{\text{perf}} = 24, \qquad M_{\text{viva}} = 6, \qquad M_{\text{perf + viva}} = 30
$$

The remaining 20 marks are split as:

$$
M_{\text{record}} = 10, \qquad M_{\text{test}} = 10
$$

$$
M_{\text{CIE}} = 24 + 6 + 10 + 10 = 50 \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pass-Mark Calculation

The pass minimum in KTU 2024 Scheme is **50 %** of CIE total:

$$
M_{\text{pass}} = 0.50 \times M_{\text{CIE}}
$$

$$
M_{\text{pass}} = 0.50 \times 50
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{\text{pass}} = 25 \text{ marks}}
$$

For distinction:

$$
M_{\text{distinction}} \geq 0.75 \times M_{\text{CIE}} = 0.75 \times 50 = 37.5
$$

So **≥ 38 marks** → Distinction.
For first class:

$$
M_{\text{first class}} \geq 0.60 \times M_{\text{CIE}} = 0.60 \times 50 = 30
$$

So **≥ 30 marks** → First Class.

| Grade Band | Marks Range | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| $M \geq 38$ | Distinction | $\geq 0.75 \times M_{\text{CIE}}$ |
| $30 \leq M < 38$ | First Class | $\geq 0.60 \times M_{\text{CIE}}$ |
| $25 \leq M < 30$ | Second Class (Pass) | $\geq 0.50 \times M_{\text{CIE}}$ |
| $M < 25$ | **FAIL** | $< 0.50 \times M_{\text{CIE}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> A common pitfall: students compute 50% of 100 thinking the total is 100. For a CIE-50 course, **pass = 25 / 50**, not 25/100. The denominator is **always the CIE total**, not 100.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Course-Graph Interpretation of "Prerequisites: None"

In formal **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** terms, every course is a node and every prerequisite is a directed edge. Let the course set be $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$. The prerequisite function is:

$$
P : C \to 2^C
$$

where $P(c_i)$ is the set of courses that must be cleared before $c_i$ can be registered.

For PCPOL607:

$$
P(\text{PCPOL607}) = \phi
$$

where $\phi$ denotes the **empty set**. This means:

$$
\forall c_j \in C, \quad c_j \notin P(\text{PCPOL607})
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\text{PCPOL607 can be registered in any semester } s \text{ where it is offered}
$$

This is a **sink-like (in-degree zero) registration node** in the course graph.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Parameters at a Glance

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Operational Role | Failure Mode if Missed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total Hours | $T_{\text{total}}$ | **30 Hrs** | Defines lab contact budget | Under-utilization → audit issue |
| Prerequisites | $P$ | **None** | Allows open registration | None (this is permissive) |
| Course Type | $\tau$ | **Lab** | Defines assessment style | Confusion with theory paper |
| CIE Marks | $M_{\text{CIE}}$ | **50** | Maximum mark obtainable | Misreading as out-of-100 |
| Pass Mark | $M_{\text{pass}}$ | **25** | Minimum to pass | Failing the course |
| ESE | $M_{\text{ESE}}$ | **0** | No university exam | Wasted ESE prep time |
| Credits | $k$ | **1** | Equivalent to 30 hrs | Wrong credit count in CGPA |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

> **Scenario:** A student in PCPOL607 scores the following internal marks:
> - Performance: 18 / 24
> - Record: 7 / 10
> - Viva: 4 / 6
> - Internal Tests: 8 / 10
>
> **Find:** (i) Total CIE mark, (ii) Pass/Fail status, (iii) Classification.

### Step 1 — Total CIE

$$
M_{\text{CIE}} = 18 + 7 + 4 + 8 = 37 \text{ marks}
$$

### Step 2 — Pass Check

$$
M_{\text{pass}} = 0.50 \times 50 = 25
$$

$$
M_{\text{CIE}} = 37 \geq 25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PASS}
$$

### Step 3 — Classification

$$
M_{\text{CIE}} = 37 \geq 30 \text{ and } < 38
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{First Class (with 1 mark short of Distinction)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Students should track their running CIE total from the **first experiment** itself. A mid-semester projection allows time to recover the missing 1 mark for Distinction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: The 30-Hour Functional Architecture

```mermaid

    H2 --> H3["Wrap-up Block t_w = 0.50 hr"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Course Registration Path

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    OpenSemester --> Registered: P equals phi
    Registered --> AttendingLab: T_total equals 30 hrs
    AttendingLab --> InternalTests: 2 tests per semester
    InternalTests --> CIEEvaluation: M_CIE equals 50
    CIEEvaluation --> PassCheck: M_pass equals 25
    PassCheck --> Passed: M_CIE >= 25
    PassCheck --> Failed: M_CIE < 25
    Passed --> Graded
    Failed --> ReRegister
    ReRegister --> AttendingLab
    Graded --> [*]
```

> [!TIP]
> The state diagram above captures the **lifecycle of a student in this lab course** — from initial registration (no prerequisites blocking) through continuous evaluation to final grading. Notice that the **fail-state routes back to re-attendance**, not directly to a University re-exam (since there is no ESE).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model]`

**Q:** A B.Tech Lab course is described as: "Hrs. 30, Prerequisites — None, Course Type — Lab, Assessment — CIE 50 Marks." Explain what this metadata tells you about:
(a) the credits awarded, and
(b) the assessment pattern.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(a) **Credits:** Under the NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Scheme, **1 Credit = 30 Contact Hours** for laboratory courses. Since the course is **30 hours**, it is awarded **1 Credit**.

(b) **Assessment Pattern:** The course is of type **"Lab"** with **CIE 50 Marks** — this means it has **Continuous Internal Evaluation only**, with **no End Semester Examination (ESE)**. Marks are awarded continuously across in-lab performance, record, viva, and internal tests, summing to 50. The **pass minimum is 25 marks (50 % of 50)**.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: Award **(1 mark)** for credit calculation, **(1 mark)** for "no ESE" recognition, and **(1 mark)** for pass-mark logic.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model]`

**Q:** What does "Prerequisites: None" mean in the context of a KTU 2024 Scheme Lab course? Give one advantage of this declaration.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Meaning:** "Prerequisites: None" means that the **set of pre-courses required for registering in this lab is empty** (formal symbol $\phi$). A student can register for the course in any semester it is offered, **without needing to have cleared any earlier course**.

**Advantage:** It provides **flexibility in academic planning** under the NEP 2020 framework — students can take this lab course as an **additional skill-acquisition** credit without being blocked by upstream course failures (e.g., a slow-track learner can still pick up the lab).

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: Award **(1 mark)** for the literal meaning, **(1 mark)** for the formal "empty set" interpretation, and **(1 mark)** for the advantage.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model]`

**Q (a)** A KTU 2024 Scheme Lab course (PCPOL607) is offered for 30 hours with CIE 50 marks. The CIE split is: Performance 24, Record 10, Viva 6, Internal Test 10. Compute the **total CIE**, the **pass mark**, and the **classification** if a student scores: Performance 19/24, Record 8/10, Viva 5/6, Test 7/10. **[7 marks]**

**Q (b)** Why is the "Course Type = Lab" classification important for **assessment design**? Differentiate between a Lab-only course and a Theory course under KTU 2024 Scheme. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Compute the actual CIE obtained:

$$
M_{\text{perf}} = 19, \quad M_{\text{record}} = 8, \quad M_{\text{viva}} = 5, \quad M_{\text{test}} = 7
$$

$$
M_{\text{CIE, obtained}} = 19 + 8 + 5 + 7
$$

$$
M_{\text{CIE, obtained}} = 39 \text{ marks}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the pass mark:

$$
M_{\text{pass}} = 0.50 \times M_{\text{CIE, max}} = 0.50 \times 50
$$

$$
M_{\text{pass}} = 25 \text{ marks}
$$

**Step 3:** Pass/Fail check:

$$
M_{\text{CIE, obtained}} = 39 \geq 25 = M_{\text{pass}}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{STUDENT PASSES}
$$

**Step 4:** Classification:

$$
\text{Distinction threshold} = 0.75 \times 50 = 37.5
$$

$$
\text{First-class threshold} = 0.60 \times 50 = 30
$$

Since $M_{\text{CIE}} = 39 \geq 37.5$:

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Classification: DISTINCTION}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: 2 marks for total, 2 marks for pass mark, 1 mark for pass/fail, 2 marks for classification.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**1. Why "Course Type = Lab" matters for assessment design:**

- It dictates that the course has **no ESE** and is **100% internally evaluated**.
- The 30 contact hours must be **fully used** to generate the 50 CIE marks (experiment performance, viva, record, internal tests).
- Students are graded on **hands-on skills, observation, and report writing**, not on pen-and-paper theory recall.
- Faculty must design **rubrics** for evaluating lab conduct — there are no theory-style "mark schemes".

**2. Differentiation Table:**

| Aspect | Lab-only Course (CIE 50) | Theory Course (ESE + CIE) |
|---|---|---|
| ESE Marks | **0 (No ESE)** | Typically 50 or 70 |
| CIE Marks | **50 (Full)** | 20 to 50 (partial) |
| Evaluation Style | Continuous, in-semester | Continuous + End-semester exam |
| Skill Focus | Hands-on, observation | Conceptual, analytical |
| Pass Rule | ≥ 25 / 50 | ≥ D-grade / ESE + CIE combined |
| Credits (typical) | 1 (30 hrs) | 3 to 4 (45 to 60 hrs) |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: 3 marks for assessment-design explanation, 4 marks for the differentiation table with at least 4 distinct parameters.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model]`

**Q (a)** A Lab course has 30 hours of contact time. If 8 experiments of 3 hours each are conducted, compute:
(i) the hours used for experiments,
(ii) the hours remaining,
(iii) how the remaining hours are used (assume 2 internal tests of equal duration). **[7 marks]**

**Q (b)** Explain why "Prerequisites: None" is **not the same** as "no assumed prior knowledge" in a Lab course. Illustrate with a real-world engineering example. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Hours for experiments:**

$$
T_{\text{exp, total}} = N \times t_{\text{exp}} = 8 \times 3 = 24 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Hours remaining:**

$$
T_{\text{remain}} = T_{\text{total}} - T_{\text{exp, total}} = 30 - 24 = 6 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Distribution for 2 internal tests of equal duration:**

$$
T_{\text{per test}} = \frac{T_{\text{remain}}}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ hours / test}
$$

**Result Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Total Contact Hours | **30 hrs** |
| Experiments (8 × 3 hrs) | **24 hrs** |
| Internal Test 1 | **3 hrs** |
| Internal Test 2 | **3 hrs** |
| **Sum Verification** | $24 + 3 + 3 = \mathbf{30\ hrs}$ ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: 2 marks for (i), 1 mark for (ii), 4 marks for (iii) with the equal-distribution logic.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Conceptual Distinction:**

- **"Prerequisites: None"** is an **administrative/registration-level** declaration — it specifies that **no formal pre-course** is required for the student to *enroll* in the lab.
- **"No assumed prior knowledge"** is a **pedagogical/learning-level** claim — it would imply the student can succeed **without any background** in the subject.

These two are **not the same**. A course can have **"Prerequisites: None"** in its registration metadata but still **assume** the student has done, for instance, foundational courses in physics, mathematics, or basic programming in earlier semesters.

**Real-World Engineering Example:**

Consider a lab course on **"Polymer Processing Lab"** (a PC course in a Polymer / Chemical Engineering programme):

- **Administrative "Prerequisites: None"**: A first-year B.Tech student can technically register for it.
- **Assumed Knowledge**: The student is *expected* to know basic organic chemistry, heat-transfer principles, and material balances from Semester 1–3 courses. Without these, the student cannot *meaningfully interpret* the experimental results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two notions operate at **different levels**:
> - Registration level (gatekeeping) — controlled by *Prerequisites*.
> - Learning level (comprehension) — controlled by *Course Content prerequisites* (often unstated).

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: 3 marks for distinguishing the two concepts, 2 marks for explaining why the distinction matters, 2 marks for the engineering example with substance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic**
> 1. **Confusing 50% as "25 out of 100":** Pass mark is 25/50, NOT 25/100. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Assuming ESE exists:** There is NO ESE for a Lab-only CIE-50 course. Do not write ESE-style answers.
> 3. **Equating 1 Credit with 60 hours:** For LAB courses, **1 Credit = 30 Hours** (not 60 as in some theory courses).
> 4. **"Prerequisites: None" ≠ "Anyone can do well":** Students sometimes skip preparation assuming no prior knowledge is needed. Marks will reflect this.
> 5. **Hours are PER COURSE, not per experiment:** A common error is interpreting "30 Hrs" as the duration of one experiment.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Total Contact Hours = 30 Hrs** — this is the **per-course, per-semester** contact time, not per experiment.
- ✅ **Course Type = Lab** — implies **100% internal evaluation**, **no ESE**, **hands-on assessment**.
- ✅ **CIE = 50 Marks** — the *maximum* CIE mark a student can earn in this course.
- ✅ **Pass Mark = 25** (50 % of 50) — must be secured to pass.
- ✅ **Distinction ≥ 38** marks; **First Class ≥ 30** marks; **Second Class ≥ 25** marks.
- ✅ **Prerequisites = None** means *no formal pre-course is required for registration* — but the course may still **assume** prior knowledge from foundational courses.
- ✅ **1 Lab Credit = 30 Hours** under the NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Scheme.
- ✅ **CIE is split** into Performance (20–24), Record (10–12), Viva (6–8), and Internal Test (8–10) marks.
- ✅ **At least 8–10 experiments** are expected in 30 hours (typically 3 hours per experiment).
- ✅ **There is NO End Semester Examination (ESE)** for a Lab-only CIE-50 course — do not write ESE-style exam answers.
- ✅ **No re-exam**: Failure means **re-registering** the course (in a subsequent semester / academic year).
- ✅ **Audit check**: $\frac{T_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{exp}}} \approx 3$ hrs is the *soft norm* used in academic audits.
- ✅ **Course-graph interpretation**: "Prerequisites: None" $\Leftrightarrow P(c) = \phi$ (empty prerequisite set).
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme alignment**: Lab-only courses fit the NEP 2020 vision of **continuous skill-based evaluation** rather than one-shot high-stakes examinations.
- ✅ **Mark-distribution awareness**: Tracking your running CIE from experiment 1 lets you project final grade and trigger remedial sessions if below the 25-mark threshold.
- ✅ **Real-world analogy**: A Lab course is like a "kitchen practical" — every dish you cook is tasted; there is no final restaurant exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ESE 50 Marks) Continuous Internal Evaluation Marks (CIE): Attendance Preparation/Pre-Lab Work
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** ESE 50 Marks) Continuous Internal Evaluation Marks (CIE): Attendance Preparation/Pre-Lab Work # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology) In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)**, laboratory courses follow a **dual-tier evaluation architecture** comprising two distinct phases of assessment: - **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE):** A continuous, day-to-day assessment conducted by the **course faculty** throughout the semester. For a standard lab course, this carries a weightage of **50 marks**. - **End Semester Evaluation (ESE):** A terminal, external assessment conducted at the close of the semester, typically carrying **50 marks** (as indicated in the topic header "ESE 50 Marks"). Within the **CIE component**, two sub-instruments are specifically highlighted for **Module Experiment 2**: | Sub-Instrument | Symbolic Notation | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Attendance | $A_{cie}$ | Quantifies physical presence & engagement | | Preparation / Pre-Lab Work | $P_{cie}$ | Quantifies cognitive readiness before the experiment | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CIE Total = Attendance Marks + Pre-Lab Marks + In-Lab Performance + Record/Viva** *(Break-up varies by course. For PCPOL607, the explicit sub-weights for attendance and pre-lab work must be verified from the official lab manual issued by the institution.)* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist" Intuition Imagine you are a **commercial airline pilot**: - **Pre-Lab Work = Pre-Flight Checklist:** Before every flight, the pilot reviews weather data, flight plan, fuel calculations, and aircraft systems. A pilot who skips the checklist is a hazard. Similarly, before each lab experiment, you review the **aim, principle, procedure, apparatus list, theory formulas, and safety guidelines**. This cognitive warm-up is your **Pre-Lab Work**. - **Attendance = Being in the Cockpit:** A pilot can only fly the plane by being physically present in the cockpit. Likewise, a student can only perform the experiment by being physically present in the laboratory. Attendance is non-negotiable — the act of being there is the foundational prerequisite. - **In-Lab Performance = Flying the Aircraft:** Once present and prepared, you execute the experiment with precision, record observations, and troubleshoot. - **Record & Viva = Post-Flight Documentation & Debriefing:** The logbook (analogous to the aircraft's black box) and the oral viva (analogous to the post-flight debrief with air traffic control) consolidate learning. > [!NOTE] > **Why CIE matters more than ESE for lab courses:** In lab courses, CIE accumulates **over 12–14 weeks of continuous engagement**, while ESE is a single-event assessment. A student who scores **45/50 in CIE** but only **30/50 in ESE** still passes with **75/100**, which is far safer than relying on a one-shot ESE performance. ## 1.3 Standard Constants & Metrics (KTU Norms) The following are the **standard institutional constants** typically followed across KTU-affiliated engineering colleges for lab CIE: - **Minimum Attendance Requirement:** **75%** to be eligible to appear for ESE. - **Condonation Limit:** Up to **10%** shortage (i.e., 65–74.99%) may be condoned by the college/university under medical or genuine grounds, subject to applicable rules. - **Detention Threshold:** Below **65%** attendance → student is **detained** and cannot appear for ESE. - **Total Lab Sessions per Module:** Typically **8–12 sessions** per semester per lab course. - **Pre-Lab Submission Window:** Usually **before the start of the lab session** (e.g., 5–10 minutes prior), with some institutions allowing submission at session start. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strictly note:** The above are **institutional reference benchmarks** in line with KTU 2024 scheme philosophy. The exact split between Attendance and Pre-Lab work for PCPOL607 must be cross-referenced with your **course-specific lab manual** issued by your institution at the start of the semester. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of CIE — Step-by-Step Deconstruction The CIE for a lab experiment is **not a single grade** — it is a **composite score** assembled from multiple discrete observations. The logic flow is as follows: ### Step 1 — Attendance Calculation The faculty maintains a **roll-call register** (physical or digital LMS-based). At the end of the semester, the attendance percentage is computed: $$\text{Attendance \%} = \left( \frac{N_{\text{present}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \right) \times 100$$ where: - $N_{\text{present}}$ = number of sessions attended by the student - $N_{\text{total}}$ = total number of lab sessions conducted ### Step 2 — Pre-Lab Work Verification Before each experiment, the student is expected to submit a **pre-lab entry in the record notebook** containing: 1. **Aim / Objective** of the experiment 2. **Apparatus / Materials / Chemicals** required 3. **Theory / Principle** (with relevant equations) 4. **Procedure** (concise flow) 5. **Precautions / Safety Notes** 6. **Observation Table** (blank, pre-formatted) The faculty evaluates this **on the spot** (or collects the record) and awards marks. The faculty typically uses a **rubric** with descriptors such as: - *Excellent (Full marks):* All six components complete, neat, accurate, with self-drawn diagrams. - *Average (Partial marks):* Some components missing or incomplete. - *Poor (Low marks):* Pre-lab submitted late, missing theory, or copied verbatim from peers. ### Step 3 — Award Mapping to 50-Mark CIE A representative **CIE Marks Distribution Template** (to be verified against your course manual): | CIE Component | Typical Weightage | Evaluator | Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | Attendance | **5 marks** | Lab Faculty | Cumulative (end-sem) | | Pre-Lab Preparation | **5–10 marks** | Lab Faculty | Per experiment | | In-Lab Performance & Observation | **15–20 marks** | Lab Faculty | Per experiment | | Record Notebook | **5–10 marks** | Lab Faculty | Periodic checks | | Viva Voce (Internal) | **5–10 marks** | Lab Faculty | Per experiment / Module | | **CIE TOTAL** | **50 marks** | — | — | > [!NOTE] > **Crucial "Why" Insight:** Attendance and Pre-Lab carry **smaller weightage** individually, but they function as **gatekeepers**. A student with 100% attendance but poor pre-lab preparation will struggle in the In-Lab phase, dragging the entire CIE down. Conversely, a brilliant pre-lab student with poor attendance will eventually be **detained**. **Both must be optimized.** ## 2.2 KTU Reference Cheat Sheet > **Subsection Note:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ is intentionally rendered using `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. | Parameter | Symbol / Formula | Typical Value | Engineering Significance | |---|---|---|---| | CIE Total | $CIE_{total}$ | **50 marks** | Continuous internal benchmark | | ESE Total | $ESE_{total}$ | **50 marks** | Terminal external benchmark | | Course Total | $T$ | $T = CIE_{total} + ESE_{total} = \mathbf{100 \text{ marks}}$ | Final course score | | Minimum Attendance | $A_{min}$ | **75 %** | Eligibility threshold for ESE | | Condonation Range | $A_{cond}$ | **65 % – 74.99 %** | Discretionary medical/valid exemption | | Detention Threshold | $A_{det}$ | **< 65 %** | Not eligible for ESE | | Attendance Marks | $M_{att}$ | **5 marks** (typical) | Pro-rata to attendance % | | Pre-Lab Marks | $M_{pre}$ | **5–10 marks** | Per experiment rubric | | Pre-Lab Components | $k$ | $k = \mathbf{6}$ | Aim, Apparatus, Theory, Procedure, Precautions, Observation Table | | Sessions per Semester | $N_{total}$ | **8 – 14** | Per lab course | | Sessions Attended | $N_{present}$ | $\le N_{total}$ | Per student | ## 2.3 Engineering Utility of the CIE Framework The CIE scheme is **engineered** to achieve specific **pedagogical outcomes**: 1. **Bloom's Taxonomy Alignment:** Attendance covers *Reception* (Level 1), Pre-Lab covers *Comprehension & Application* (Levels 2–3), and In-Lab execution covers *Analysis, Synthesis & Evaluation* (Levels 4–6). The CIE thus maps to **Revised Bloom's Cognitive Domain** in a graded manner. 2. **Continuous Improvement Pedagogy:** Unlike a single high-stakes exam, CIE provides **formative feedback loops** — students can correct mistakes week-over-week. 3. **Professional Skill Mapping:** Pre-Lab mirrors the **engineering practice of "planning before execution"** — a real industry engineer never starts a project without a design document, risk assessment, and procedure (similar to a pre-lab). 4. **Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Compliance:** Each CIE instrument is tied to specific **Course Outcomes (COs)** and **Program Outcomes (POs)** as mandated by NBA and NEP 2020. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Under the **NEP 2020-aligned OBE framework**, CIE instruments must be **CO-mapped**. When you submit your pre-lab, your faculty is not just checking neatness — they are evaluating whether your preparation demonstrates attainment of a specific **Course Outcome (e.g., CO2: "Apply the principles of polymer processing to conduct experiments")**. Always align your pre-lab content with the **CO statements** in your syllabus. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Attendance Marks Calculation (Pro-Rata Method) This is the **most commonly used algorithm** by KTU institutions to convert attendance percentage into a 5-mark (or 10-mark) attendance score. ### Given: - Maximum attendance marks = $M_{att}^{max}$ - Student attendance % = $A_{\%}$ - Penalty threshold = $A_{pen}$ (typically 75 %) ### Algorithm: The faculty uses a **tiered (piecewise linear) pro-rata model**: $$ M_{att} = \begin{cases} M_{att}^{max} \times \left( \dfrac{A_{\%} - A_{pen}}{100 - A_{pen}} \right) & \text{if } A_{\%} \ge A_{pen} \\ 0 & \text{if } A_{\%} < A_{pen} \text{ and not condoned} \end{cases} $$ ### Worked Example: **Assume** $M_{att}^{max} = 5$ marks, $A_{pen} = 75$ %. **Case 1: Student has 100 % attendance** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{att} &= 5 \times \left( \frac{100 - 75}{100 - 75} \right) \\ &= 5 \times \left( \frac{25}{25} \right) \\ &= 5 \times 1 \\ &= 5 \text{ marks} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Full attendance yields **full marks**. ✓ **Case 2: Student has 90 % attendance** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{att} &= 5 \times \left( \frac{90 - 75}{100 - 75} \right) \\ &= 5 \times \left( \frac{15}{25} \right) \\ &= 5 \times 0.6 \\ &= 3 \text{ marks} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 90 % attendance yields **3 out of 5 marks**. ✓ **Case 3: Student has 80 % attendance** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{att} &= 5 \times \left( \frac{80 - 75}{100 - 75} \right) \\ &= 5 \times \left( \frac{5}{25} \right) \\ &= 5 \times 0.2 \\ &= 1 \text{ mark} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** 80 % attendance yields **only 1 out of 5 marks**. Marginal performance! **Case 4: Student has 70 % attendance (condonable but penalized)** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{att} &= 5 \times \left( \frac{70 - 75}{100 - 75} \right) \\ &= 5 \times \left( \frac{-5}{25} \right) \\ &= 5 \times (-0.2) \\ &= -1 \text{ mark} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Negative result is clipped to **0 marks**. The student loses all attendance marks but may still be condoned to appear for ESE. > [!WARNING] > **Anti-Hallucination Note:** Some institutions use a **simpler linear model** ($M_{att} = M_{att}^{max} \times \frac{A_{\%}}{100}$) or a **discrete tier model** (e.g., 100% → 5 marks, 90–99% → 4 marks, 80–89% → 3 marks, 75–79% → 2 marks). **Verify the exact model from your institution's lab manual** before relying on these formulas for grade prediction. ## 3.2 Derivation: Pre-Lab Rubric Scoring Model A **typical 6-component pre-lab rubric** (out of 5 or 10 marks) uses the following additive model: $$ M_{pre} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot s_i $$ where: - $k$ = number of pre-lab components (= 6 in the standard model) - $w_i$ = weight assigned to component $i$ - $s_i$ = score (0, 0.5, or 1) awarded by faculty for component $i$ ### Standard Equal-Weight Rubric (each component = $\frac{M_{pre}^{max}}{6}$ marks): | Component $i$ | Description | $w_i$ | Max Sub-Marks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Aim clearly stated | $\frac{1}{6}$ | $M_{pre}^{max}/6$ | | 2 | Apparatus list complete | $\frac{1}{6}$ | $M_{pre}^{max}/6$ | | 3 | Theory with equations | $\frac{1}{6}$ | $M_{pre}^{max}/6$ | | 4 | Procedure flow correct | $\frac{1}{6}$ | $M_{pre}^{max}/6$ | | 5 | Safety precautions | $\frac{1}{6}$ | $M_{pre}^{max}/6$ | | 6 | Observation table pre-formatted | $\frac{1}{6}$ | $M_{pre}^{max}/6$ | | **Total** | — | **1.0** | **$M_{pre}^{max}$** | ### Worked Example: 10-Mark Pre-Lab A student submits a pre-lab with the following faculty ratings: - Aim: $s_1 = 1$ (fully correct) - Apparatus: $s_2 = 1$ (complete) - Theory: $s_3 = 0.5$ (equations listed but derivation missing) - Procedure: $s_4 = 1$ (clear flow) - Precautions: $s_5 = 0$ (forgot to mention) - Observation Table: $s_6 = 1$ (pre-formatted with units) $$ \begin{aligned} M_{pre} &= \sum_{i=1}^{6} \left( \frac{10}{6} \right) \cdot s_i \\ &= \frac{10}{6} \times (1 + 1 + 0.5 + 1 + 0 + 1) \\ &= \frac{10}{6} \times 4.5 \\ &= 1.6667 \times 4.5 \\ &= 7.5 \text{ marks} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The student loses **2.5 marks** by omitting precautions and partially completing the theory section. ## 3.3 Composite CIE Score for Module Experiment 2 Assuming a typical split for a single experiment within the broader CIE pool: $$ CIE_{\text{exp}} = M_{att} + M_{pre} + M_{\text{in-lab}} + M_{\text{record}} + M_{\text{viva}} $$ For a **representative experiment** (e.g., 8–10 marks per experiment × ~8–10 experiments ≈ 50 total CIE marks for the lab), the per-experiment contribution to CIE is: $$ CIE_{\text{final}} = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{exp}} CIE_{\text{exp},j} $$ where $N_{exp}$ = number of experiments in the semester. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CIE Evaluation (Module Experiment 2) The following **Mermaid flowchart** maps the **functional flow** of how the CIE system processes a student's performance data for a single lab experiment, with focus on the Attendance and Pre-Lab Work instruments: ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Lab Session Start
Time T0"] --> node2["Attendance Marking
Roll Call Register"] node2 --> node3{"Attendance
Percentage Check"} node3 -->|"A% ge 75%"| node4["Eligible to Proceed
Attendance Marks Awarded"] node3 -->|"65% le A% lt 75%"| node5["Condonation Review
Medical Valid Grounds"] node3 -->|"A% lt 65%"| node6["Detention Flag
Not Eligible for ESE"] node4 --> node7["Pre-Lab Submission Check
Record Notebook Verification"] node7 --> node8{"Pre-Lab Rubric
Evaluation"} node8 -->|"All 6 Components Complete"| node9["Full Pre-Lab Marks
Mpre equals Mpre Max"] node8 -->|"Partial Submission"| node10["Pro-Rata Marks
Mpre equals Sum wi times si"] node8 -->|"Missing or Late"| node11["Penalty or Zero Marks
Faculty Discretion"] node9 --> node12["In-Lab Execution
Observation and Recording"] node10 --> node12 node11 --> node12 node12 --> node13["Record Notebook Audit
Periodic Faculty Check"] node13 --> node14["Viva Voce Assessment
Oral Questioning"] node14 --> node15["Composite CIE Score
Sum of All Sub-Instruments"] node15 --> node16["CIE Total Out of 50
Reported to University"] style node1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000 style node2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000 style node3 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#000 style node6 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 style node7 fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000 style node8 fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000 style node12 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00838F,color:#000 style node15 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000 style node16 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Pie-Chart: Representative CIE Marks Distribution (50-Mark CIE) ```mermaid pie showData title CIE 50 Marks - Typical Distribution "Attendance" : 5 "Pre-Lab Work" : 10 "In-Lab Performance" : 20 "Record Notebook" : 10 "Viva Voce" : 5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The pie chart values are **representative benchmarks** for illustration. The **exact split for PCPOL607** must be confirmed from the **official course syllabus and lab manual** issued by your institution. Different KTU colleges may use slightly varied distributions, but the **five-instrument structure** (Attendance, Pre-Lab, In-Lab, Record, Viva) is highly standardized across the 2024 scheme. ## 4.3 Functional Flow: Student Action → Faculty Evaluation → CIE Credit ```mermaid flowchart LR actA["Student Action:
Present in Lab"] --> evalA["Faculty Evaluation:
Roll Call"] actB["Student Action:
Pre-Lab Prepared"] --> evalB["Faculty Evaluation:
Rubric Scoring"] actC["Student Action:
Execute Experiment"] --> evalC["Faculty Evaluation:
Observation Check"] actD["Student Action:
Maintain Record"] --> evalD["Faculty Evaluation:
Notebook Audit"] actE["Student Action:
Answer Viva"] --> evalE["Faculty Evaluation:
Oral Assessment"] evalA --> cie["CIE Composite
Out of 50"] evalB --> cie evalC --> cie evalD --> cie evalE --> cie cie --> ese["Eligible for ESE 50 Marks"] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]** ### **Q1. [3 Marks]** Define **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** as applicable to laboratory courses under the KTU 2024 scheme. List any **four** sub-instruments used to compute the CIE total. **Model Answer:** Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) is a **continuous, faculty-conducted assessment process** spread across the entire semester, designed to evaluate a student's day-to-day engagement, preparation, and performance in a laboratory course. Under the KTU 2024 scheme, CIE carries **50 marks** for a typical lab course and is **internal to the institution** (i.e., not externally evaluated). **Four sub-instruments of CIE are:** 1. **Attendance** — quantifies physical presence in lab sessions. 2. **Pre-Lab Work / Preparation** — evaluates cognitive readiness before the experiment. 3. **In-Lab Performance** — assesses the student's execution, observation, and data-recording skills during the experiment. 4. **Record Notebook & Viva Voce** — evaluates documentation quality and oral understanding. *(Any four of the five-instrument structure are acceptable.)* --- ### **Q2. [3 Marks]** State the **minimum attendance percentage** required for a student to be eligible to appear for the End Semester Evaluation (ESE) in a KTU 2024 scheme lab course. What happens if attendance falls below this threshold? **Model Answer:** The **minimum attendance requirement** for eligibility to appear for the ESE is **75%** of the total lab sessions conducted during the semester. If attendance falls: - **Below 65%** → The student is **detained** and is **not eligible** to appear for the ESE. The student must re-register for the course in a subsequent semester. - **Between 65% and 74.99%** → The shortage may be **condoned** by the competent authority (Head of Department / Principal) on valid grounds (e.g., medical reasons with certificate), subject to institutional and university rules. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Pattern, Dec 2024 / May 2025 Series Style]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain, with a suitable example, how the **attendance marks** are computed in a typical KTU 2024 scheme lab course. Assume the maximum attendance marks are 5 and the penalty threshold is 75%. Compute the attendance marks for a student with 88% attendance. **Model Solution:** **Explanation:** The attendance marks in a KTU lab course are computed using a **pro-rata (piecewise linear) model** that rewards students with attendance higher than the minimum threshold (75%). The formula is: $$ M_{att} = \begin{cases} M_{att}^{max} \times \left( \dfrac{A_{\%} - 75}{100 - 75} \right) & \text{if } A_{\%} \ge 75 \\ 0 & \text{if } A_{\%} < 75 \text{ (without condonation)} \end{cases} $$ **Interpretation of the Formula:** - A student with **100% attendance** receives the **full $M_{att}^{max}$** (e.g., 5 marks). - A student with attendance **exactly at 75%** receives **0 marks** (they are eligible for ESE but get no attendance credit). - A student between 75% and 100% receives **proportionally scaled marks**. - This model **incentivizes** students to attend **every session**, not just the minimum required. **Numerical Computation:** Given: $M_{att}^{max} = 5$ marks, $A_{\%} = 88$%. $$ \begin{aligned} M_{att} &= 5 \times \left( \frac{88 - 75}{100 - 75} \right) \\ &= 5 \times \left( \frac{13}{25} \right) \\ &= 5 \times 0.52 \\ &= 2.6 \text{ marks} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer:** The student with 88% attendance receives **2.6 marks out of 5** in the attendance component. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **six essential components** that a student must include in the **pre-lab preparation entry** of the record notebook before each experiment. Why is pre-lab work critical for achieving the Course Outcomes (COs)? **Model Answer:** **The six essential components of a pre-lab entry are:** 1. **Aim / Objective** — A precise, single-sentence statement of what the experiment intends to determine or verify. 2. **Apparatus / Materials / Chemicals Required** — A complete list of all equipment, glassware, reagents, and consumables needed, with specifications. 3. **Theory / Principle** — A concise theoretical background, including relevant equations, laws, or principles that the experiment will validate. 4. **Procedure** — A step-by-step, sequential flow of the experimental method (often in numbered points or a flowchart). 5. **Precautions / Safety Notes** — Specific warnings about hazardous chemicals, electrical equipment, or procedural pitfalls. 6. **Observation Table** — A pre-formatted, blank table with column headers and units, ready to be filled during the experiment. **Criticality for Course Outcomes (COs):** Pre-lab work is the **primary cognitive instrument** for CO attainment in a lab course. The COs typically require students to *"apply theoretical knowledge to experimental scenarios"* and *"analyze experimental data using domain principles."* Without a strong pre-lab entry: - The student **cannot identify** which CO is being assessed. - The student **cannot connect theory to practice**, leading to mechanical, rote execution. - The student **cannot troubleshoot** deviations because the underlying theory is not internalized. - The student **fails the CO attainment test** during in-lab performance and viva. Pre-lab preparation is therefore the **bridge between Bloom's Comprehension (Level 2) and Application (Level 3)** in the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy, making it indispensable for OBE-compliant learning. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat flowchart, illustrate the **complete CIE evaluation flow** for a single lab experiment in a KTU 2024 scheme course. Highlight the decision points where a student can lose marks. **Model Solution:** *(Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1 of these notes for the full flow.)* **Key Decision Points Where Marks Can Be Lost:** 1. **Attendance Check Point:** If attendance < 75% without condonation → student gets **0 attendance marks** and may be detained. 2. **Pre-Lab Rubric Evaluation:** Missing components (e.g., no precautions listed, incomplete theory) → pro-rata **deductions**. 3. **In-Lab Performance:** Poor observation recording, equipment mishandling, failure to follow procedure → **deductions by faculty on the spot**. 4. **Record Notebook Audit:** Late submission, missing entries, untidy records → **periodic penalties**. 5. **Viva Voce:** Inability to explain theory, procedure, or results → **loss of oral assessment marks**. **Composite Loss Scenario:** A student who loses marks at all five decision points can drop from a potential **50/50 CIE** to as low as **20–25/50**, severely impacting the final course grade. --- #### **(b) [7 Marks]** A student in PCPOL607 attended 11 out of 12 lab sessions and submitted a pre-lab with 5 out of 6 components rated as "complete" and 1 component rated as "partially complete" (score 0.5). If the maximum attendance marks = 5 and maximum pre-lab marks = 10, compute the **total CIE marks obtained from these two instruments alone**. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute Attendance Percentage:** $$ A_{\%} = \left( \frac{11}{12} \right) \times 100 = 91.67\% $$ **Step 2 — Compute Attendance Marks (pro-rata, threshold = 75%):** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{att} &= 5 \times \left( \frac{91.67 - 75}{100 - 75} \right) \\ &= 5 \times \left( \frac{16.67}{25} \right) \\ &= 5 \times 0.6667 \\ &= 3.33 \text{ marks} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Pre-Lab Marks (rubric with $s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5, s_6 = 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 0.5$):** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{pre} &= \sum_{i=1}^{6} \left( \frac{10}{6} \right) \cdot s_i \\ &= \frac{10}{6} \times (1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0.5) \\ &= \frac{10}{6} \times 5.5 \\ &= 1.6667 \times 5.5 \\ &= 9.17 \text{ marks} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Composite CIE from These Two Instruments:** $$ CIE_{2} = M_{att} + M_{pre} = 3.33 + 9.17 = 12.5 \text{ marks} $$ **Answer:** The student earns **12.5 marks out of 15** from the Attendance and Pre-Lab instruments combined, having lost **2.5 marks** (0.67 from attendance shortfall and 0.83 from the partial pre-lab component, and rounding adjustments). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the 75% threshold:** Students often write "$M_{att} = 5 \times (A_\% / 100)$" without accounting for the penalty threshold, leading to **over-scoring** in calculation answers. > 2. **Confusing CIE with ESE:** Students mix up which marks are internal (CIE = 50) and which are external (ESE = 50). **Always re-read the question stem** for explicit marks mention. > 3. **Ignoring condonation rules:** Many answers do not mention the 65%–74.99% condonation window, which is a **frequently asked sub-part** in viva and theory questions. > 4. **No CO mapping in pre-lab:** Pre-lab entries are increasingly required to **state the relevant CO** at the top. Students who omit this lose 0.5–1 mark in the rubric. > 5. **Unit errors in observation tables:** Pre-formatted tables must have **units in the column headers** (e.g., "Temperature (°C)" not just "Temperature"). This is a common **1-mark deduction** trigger. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2 CIE (Attendance & Pre-Lab Work)** - **CIE = 50 marks** (internal, continuous), **ESE = 50 marks** (external, terminal). **Total = 100 marks**. - **Minimum attendance = 75%** to be eligible for ESE. **Below 65% = Detention**. - **Condonation window = 65%–74.99%** (subject to valid grounds and institutional approval). - **Attendance marks** use a **pro-rata model** with **threshold = 75%** and **ceiling = 100%**. Formula: $$ M_{att} = M_{att}^{max} \times \left( \frac{A_{\%} - 75}{25} \right) $$ - **Pre-Lab Work = 6 components**: Aim, Apparatus, Theory, Procedure, Precautions, Observation Table. - **Pre-Lab Rubric** is typically **equal-weight** with each component carrying $\frac{M_{pre}^{max}}{6}$ marks. - **Pre-Lab is the CO-attainment bridge** between Bloom's Comprehension and Application levels. - **Attendance + Pre-Lab are gatekeepers**, not just marks. They prevent detention and enable in-lab success. - **Faculty evaluates pre-lab on the spot** using a **0 / 0.5 / 1** descriptor scale per component. - **Late or missing pre-lab = severe penalty** (often zero marks for that instrument). - **Record the CO code** at the top of your pre-lab entry — this is a **2024 scheme mandate** under OBE. - **Observation tables must have units** in column headers — a 1-mark deduction trap. - **Verify the exact CIE split** for PCPOL607 from your **institution-issued lab manual** before assuming the standard 5/10/20/10/5 distribution. - **The CIE system is engineered to reward consistency**, not last-minute cramming. A student who attends **all 12 sessions** and prepares **all 12 pre-labs** will almost always score **45+/50 in CIE**.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** experiments # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory module **PCPOL607 / Module Experiment 2**, the term **"experiments"** refers to the structured **optical polarization experiments** performed in the engineering physics laboratory to investigate the behavior of plane-polarized light through analyzers, polarizers, optically active substances, and reflection surfaces. The flagship experiment of this module is the **Verification of Malus's Law**, supplemented by the **Determination of Specific Rotation of Sugar Solution** and **Verification of Brewster's Law**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > *Module Experiment 2* trains the student to experimentally verify the foundational laws of polarization optics — namely, how light intensity transforms when plane-polarized light traverses an analyzer, and how optically active media rotate the plane of polarization. **Malus's Law (Formal Statement):** When a beam of completely plane-polarized light of intensity $I_0$ is incident on an analyzer, the intensity $I$ of the transmitted light varies as the **square of the cosine of the angle $\theta$** between the transmission axis of the analyzer and the plane of polarization of the incident light. $$I = I_0 \cos^2\theta$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are pushing a long stick through a narrow vertical slit in a fence: - If the stick is perfectly **vertical** (parallel to the slit) — it passes through easily. → **Maximum intensity** transmitted. - If the stick is tilted at some angle $\theta$ — only the vertical component of your push gets through. → **Reduced intensity**. - If the stick is **horizontal** (perpendicular to the slit) — nothing passes through. → **Zero intensity**. The "vertical component" of your push is proportional to $\cos\theta$, and since light intensity depends on the *square* of the electric field amplitude, the transmitted intensity is $\cos^2\theta$. That is the essence of Malus's Law! > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants Used:** > - Speed of light in vacuum: **$c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$** > - Wavelength of sodium D-line (for optical activity): **$\lambda = 5893 \text{ Å}$** (commonly used reference) > - Standard concentration for cane sugar: usually prepared as **$\mathbf{0.05 \text{ to } 0.20 \text{ g/cc}$** ## 1.3 Learning Outcomes Aligned with KTU 2024 COs After completing this experiment, the student will be able to: - **CO1:** Recall the laws governing polarized light transmission. - **CO2:** Apply Malus's Law to predict intensity at any analyzer angle. - **CO3:** Analyze experimental data graphically to verify $\cos^2\theta$ dependence. - **CO4:** Evaluate the specific rotation of optically active substances (sugar solution). - **CO5:** Determine Brewster's angle for a glass plate. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Theory — Malus's Law When **unpolarized light** (random polarization) passes through a **polarizer (Polaroid)**, it emerges as **plane-polarized** with intensity reduced to **half** of the incident intensity (because only the component along the transmission axis survives from the randomly distributed electric field vectors). $$I_{\text{after polarizer}} = \frac{I_{\text{incident}}}{2}$$ This plane-polarized light then falls on the **analyzer**, whose transmission axis makes an angle $\theta$ with the plane of polarization. Let the electric field amplitude of the incident polarized light be $E_0$. The component of $E_0$ along the analyzer's transmission axis is: $$E_t = E_0 \cos\theta$$ Since intensity is proportional to the square of the electric field amplitude: $$I = E_t^2 = E_0^2 \cos^2\theta = I_0 \cos^2\theta$$ ### Special Cases: | Condition | Angle $\theta$ | Transmitted Intensity | |---|---|---| | Analyzer parallel to Polarizer | $0°$ | $I = I_0$ (Maximum) | | Analyzer at $45°$ | $45°$ | $I = \frac{I_0}{2}$ | | Analyzer crossed with Polarizer | $90°$ | $I = 0$ (Minimum) | | Analyzer at $60°$ | $60°$ | $I = \frac{I_0}{4}$ | ## 2.2 Specific Rotation (Optical Activity) Certain substances (e.g., **sugar solution, quartz, tartaric acid**) rotate the plane of polarization as light passes through them. This property is called **optical activity**, and the substances are called **optically active**. The angle of rotation $\theta$ is given by: $$\theta = S \cdot C \cdot l$$ Where: - $S$ = Specific rotation (in degrees) - $C$ = Concentration of solution (in g/cc) - $l$ = Length of solution column (in decimeters, dm) Solving for specific rotation: $$[S] = \frac{\theta}{C \cdot l}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference:** For cane sugar (sucrose) at sodium D-line wavelength ($5893 \text{ Å}$), the accepted specific rotation at $20°C$ is approximately **$[\alpha]_{D}^{20} = +66.5°$** (positive sign indicates **dextrorotatory**, i.e., rotates plane to the right). ## 2.3 Brewster's Law (Supplementary) When light strikes a dielectric surface at the **Brewster angle** $\theta_B$, the reflected light is completely plane-polarized perpendicular to the plane of incidence: $$\tan\theta_B = \mu = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$ Where $\mu$ is the refractive index of the denser medium relative to the rarer one. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I = I_0 \cos^2\theta$ | Malus's Law | W/m² | | 2 | $I_{\text{pol}} = I_{\text{inc}}/2$ | Intensity after polarizer | W/m² | | 3 | $E_t = E_0 \cos\theta$ | Transmitted E-field component | V/m | | 4 | $\theta = S \cdot C \cdot l$ | Optical rotation equation | degrees | | 5 | $[S] = \frac{\theta}{C \cdot l}$ | Specific rotation formula | deg·cc/(g·dm) | | 6 | $\tan\theta_B = n$ | Brewster's Law | dimensionless | | 7 | $\theta_B + r = 90°$ | Geometry of Brewster angle | degrees | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **LCD Displays:** Liquid crystals act as analyzers; voltage controls the angle, modulating transmitted intensity per Malus's Law. - **Polaroid Sunglasses:** Block horizontally polarized glare using crossed polarizers. - **Optical Fiber Sensors:** Polarization-maintaining fibers use these principles. - **Sugar Industry:** Polarimeters use specific rotation to measure sugar concentration in industrial solutions. - **Photography:** Circular polarizer filters reduce reflections using Brewster's angle principle. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Malus's Law ### Starting Point: Consider a plane-polarized electromagnetic wave traveling along the x-axis, with its electric field oscillating in the y-z plane. The electric field of the incident polarized light can be represented as: $$\vec{E}_{\text{inc}} = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kx) \hat{j}$$ (Polarized along y-direction) ### Step 1: Component Along Analyzer Axis Let the analyzer's transmission axis make an angle $\theta$ with the y-axis (polarization direction). The transmitted component of $E_0$ along the analyzer axis is: $$E_t = E_0 \cos\theta$$ ### Step 2: Intensity Relationship Intensity of an electromagnetic wave is proportional to the square of the electric field amplitude: $$I \propto E^2$$ Therefore: $$\frac{I}{I_0} = \frac{E_t^2}{E_0^2}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute the Component Substituting $E_t = E_0 \cos\theta$: $$\frac{I}{I_0} = \frac{(E_0 \cos\theta)^2}{E_0^2} = \cos^2\theta$$ ### Step 4: Final Malus's Law Expression $$\boxed{I = I_0 \cos^2\theta}$$ ### Step 5: Special Case Verification - At $\theta = 0°$: $I = I_0 \cos^2(0) = I_0$ ✓ (Maximum transmission) - At $\theta = 90°$: $I = I_0 \cos^2(90°) = 0$ ✓ (Crossed polarizers — extinction) - At $\theta = 45°$: $I = I_0 \cos^2(45°) = I_0/2$ ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation of Specific Rotation Formula ### Starting Point: Experimentally, **Biot's Law** states that the rotation $\theta$ of the plane of polarization by an optically active solution is: 1. Proportional to the length of the solution column $l$ 2. Proportional to the concentration $C$ of the solute $$\theta \propto l \cdot C$$ ### Step 1: Introducing Proportionality Constant Let $S$ be the constant of proportionality (specific rotation): $$\theta = S \cdot C \cdot l$$ ### Step 2: Solving for Specific Rotation Rearranging: $$S = \frac{\theta}{C \cdot l}$$ ### Step 3: Units of Specific Rotation - $\theta$ → degrees - $C$ → g/cc - $l$ → decimeters (dm) Therefore, units of $S$: $$[S] = \frac{\text{degrees}}{(\text{g/cc}) \cdot (\text{dm})}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Sample KTU Problem) **Problem:** A polarizer-analyzer system is set such that the transmitted intensity is $\frac{I_0}{3}$. Find the angle between the polarizer and analyzer axes. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Apply Malus's Law: $$I = I_0 \cos^2\theta$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the given ratio: $$\frac{I_0}{3} = I_0 \cos^2\theta$$ **Step 3:** Cancel $I_0$ on both sides: $$\frac{1}{3} = \cos^2\theta$$ **Step 4:** Take square root: $$\cos\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$$ **Step 5:** Calculate the angle: $$\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \approx 54.74°$$ **Verification:** $\cos^2(54.74°) = (0.577)^2 \approx 0.333 = 1/3$ ✓ ## 3.4 Sample Problem — Specific Rotation **Problem:** A 10 cm long polarimeter tube is filled with a sugar solution of concentration 0.1 g/cc. The plane of polarization is rotated by $13°$. Find the specific rotation. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Convert units to standard form: - $l = 10 \text{ cm} = 1 \text{ dm}$ - $C = 0.1 \text{ g/cc}$ - $\theta = 13°$ **Step 2:** Apply the specific rotation formula: $$[S] = \frac{\theta}{C \cdot l}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute values: $$[S] = \frac{13°}{0.1 \times 1}$$ **Step 4:** Final answer: $$[S] = 130° \cdot \frac{\text{cc}}{\text{g} \cdot \text{dm}}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of Malus's Law Experimental Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR A["LIGHT SOURCE
Monochromatic Lamp"] --> B["POLARIZER
Fixed Polaroid"] B --> C["SAMPLE TUBE
Polarimeter Tube
with Sugar Solution"] C --> D["ANALYZER
Rotatable Polaroid
with Angular Scale"] D --> E["DETECTOR
Photometer or
Light Sensor"] D --> F["ANGULAR READOUT
Vernier Scale
Degree Measurement"] G["POWER SUPPLY
Regulated DC"] --> A H["DATA TABLE
I vs theta"] --> I["GRAPH
I vs cos square theta
Should be linear"] E --> H F --> H H --> I style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow of Light Through the Polarizer-Analyzer System ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Unpolarized Light
Intensity I incident"]) --> P1["POLARIZER
Transmission axis vertical"] P1 --> P2["Plane Polarized Light
Intensity I by 2"] P2 --> P3{"ANALYZER
at angle theta
from polarizer"} P3 --> P4["Component E zero cos theta
transmitted through analyzer"] P4 --> P5["OUTPUT
Intensity I = I zero cos square theta"] style Start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style P5 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Polarimeter Setup for Specific Rotation ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Monochromatic

Observation Point

Half Shade Fields"] style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Expected Graph: Verification of Malus's Law ``` I (Intensity) | I0 |* (θ = 0°) | * | * | * | * I0/2 | - - - - * - - - (θ = 45°) | * | * | * | * | * 0 |________________________*___ θ (degrees) 0 15 30 45 60 75 90 Plot of I vs θ follows cos²θ curve ``` ``` I (Intensity) | | * | * | * | * | * | * | * | * 0 |*_____________________________ cos²θ 0 1 Linear Plot of I vs cos²θ Slope = I₀ (should pass through origin) ``` ## 4.5 Tabular Representation of Sample Readings | Angle θ (degrees) | cos θ | cos²θ | Observed Intensity I | Calculated $I_0 \cos^2\theta$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 1.000 | 1.000 | $I_0$ | $I_0$ | | 15 | 0.966 | 0.933 | $0.933 I_0$ | $0.933 I_0$ | | 30 | 0.866 | 0.750 | $0.750 I_0$ | $0.750 I_0$ | | 45 | 0.707 | 0.500 | $0.500 I_0$ | $0.500 I_0$ | | 60 | 0.500 | 0.250 | $0.250 I_0$ | $0.250 I_0$ | | 75 | 0.259 | 0.067 | $0.067 I_0$ | $0.067 I_0$ | | 90 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0 | 0 | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]** **State Malus's Law. What is the transmitted intensity when the analyzer is at 30° with the polarizer?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Malus's Law:** When a beam of plane-polarized light of intensity $I_0$ is incident on an analyzer, the intensity $I$ of the transmitted light is given by: $$I = I_0 \cos^2\theta$$ where $\theta$ is the angle between the plane of polarization of incident light and the transmission axis of the analyzer. **Calculation at $\theta = 30°$:** $$I = I_0 \cos^2(30°) = I_0 \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{3I_0}{4}$$ **Hence, the transmitted intensity is $\mathbf{\dfrac{3I_0}{4}}$.** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define specific rotation of an optically active substance. Write the formula for specific rotation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition:** The specific rotation of an optically active substance is defined as the rotation of the plane of polarization produced by a solution of unit concentration (1 g/cc) contained in a tube of unit length (1 decimeter) at a given temperature $T$ and wavelength $\lambda$ of light used. It is denoted by $[\alpha]_\lambda^T$. **Formula:** $$[\alpha]_\lambda^T = \frac{\theta}{C \cdot l}$$ Where: - $\theta$ = angle of rotation observed (degrees) - $C$ = concentration of solution (g/cc) - $l$ = length of polarimeter tube (dm) **Example:** For cane sugar, $[S] = +66.5°$ (positive indicates dextrorotatory). --- ## PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Always remember to **convert length from cm to dm** when calculating specific rotation — this is the most common mistake costing 2-3 marks. > - In graph plotting, **label both axes with proper units** and **mention the slope value** explicitly. > - For numerical problems, **show every substitution step** — partial steps without justification will not fetch full marks. > - Do NOT forget to mention the **standard temperature (20°C)** and **wavelength (sodium D-line, 5893 Å)** in specific rotation answers. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Choice 1]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Malus's Law. A polarizer and analyzer are oriented such that the maximum intensity of the transmitted light is $I_0$. Find the angle at which the intensity is reduced to $\dfrac{I_0}{2}$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the experimental setup to verify Malus's Law. What graphs are plotted and what are the expected results? #### Model Solution: **(a) Derivation of Malus's Law:** Let $E_0$ be the amplitude of the electric field vector of plane-polarized light incident on the analyzer. The transmission axis of the analyzer makes an angle $\theta$ with the plane of polarization. **Component of $E_0$ along the analyzer axis:** $$E_t = E_0 \cos\theta$$ **Since intensity is proportional to the square of amplitude:** $$I = E_t^2 = E_0^2 \cos^2\theta = I_0 \cos^2\theta$$ This is Malus's Law. **Calculation for $I = I_0/2$:** $$\frac{I_0}{2} = I_0 \cos^2\theta$$ $$\cos^2\theta = \frac{1}{2}$$ $$\cos\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$ $$\boxed{\theta = 45°}$$ **(b) Experimental Setup and Procedure:** **Apparatus Required:** 1. Monochromatic light source (sodium lamp or laser) 2. Two polaroids — one fixed (polarizer), one rotatable (analyzer) mounted on a graduated circular scale 3. Light detector / photometer / lux meter 4. Power supply and connecting wires **Procedure:** 1. Place the polarizer and analyzer in line with the light source such that their axes are parallel ($\theta = 0°$). 2. Note the maximum intensity $I_0$ from the detector. 3. Rotate the analyzer in steps of 10° or 15° from 0° to 90°. 4. Record the corresponding transmitted intensity $I$ at each angle. 5. Calculate $\cos^2\theta$ for each angle. 6. Plot two graphs: - **Graph 1:** $I$ vs $\theta$ (should follow cos²θ curve) - **Graph 2:** $I$ vs $\cos^2\theta$ (should be a **straight line passing through the origin**) **Expected Results:** - Graph 1 should match the cosine-squared curve. - Graph 2 should be linear with slope equal to $I_0$, verifying Malus's Law. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of optical activity. A sugar solution of concentration 0.08 g/cc is taken in a polarimeter tube of length 20 cm. The plane of polarization is rotated by 10.64°. Calculate the specific rotation of sugar. **(b) [7 Marks]** What is Brewster's Law? A glass plate has refractive index 1.5. Find the Brewster angle and the angle of refraction at this condition. #### Model Solution: **(a) Optical Activity and Specific Rotation Calculation:** **Phenomenon:** Certain substances (sugar, quartz, tartaric acid) have the property of rotating the plane of polarization of plane-polarized light as it passes through them. This property is called **optical activity**, and the substances are called **optically active substances**. **Biots Law:** $$\theta = S \cdot C \cdot l$$ **Calculation:** Given: - $C = 0.08 \text{ g/cc}$ - $l = 20 \text{ cm} = 2 \text{ dm}$ - $\theta = 10.64°$ $$[S] = \frac{\theta}{C \cdot l}$$ $$[S] = \frac{10.64°}{0.08 \times 2}$$ $$[S] = \frac{10.64}{0.16}$$ $$\boxed{[S] = 66.5° \cdot \text{cc} \cdot \text{g}^{-1} \cdot \text{dm}^{-1}}$$ This matches the standard value for cane sugar (sucrose), confirming dextro-rotation. **(b) Brewster's Law and Calculations:** **Statement:** When unpolarized light strikes a transparent dielectric surface at a particular angle of incidence $\theta_B$ (called the **Brewster angle** or **polarizing angle**), the reflected light is completely plane-polarized perpendicular to the plane of incidence. **Mathematical Form:** $$\tan\theta_B = \mu = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$ For light going from air ($n_1 = 1$) to glass ($n_2 = 1.5$): $$\tan\theta_B = 1.5$$ $$\theta_B = \tan^{-1}(1.5)$$ $$\boxed{\theta_B \approx 56.31°}$$ **Angle of Refraction at Brewster's Angle:** At Brewster's angle, the reflected and refracted rays are mutually perpendicular: $$\theta_B + r = 90°$$ $$r = 90° - 56.31° = 33.69°$$ **Verification using Snell's Law:** $$n_1 \sin\theta_B = n_2 \sin r$$ $$1 \times \sin(56.31°) = 1.5 \times \sin(33.69°)$$ $$0.832 = 1.5 \times 0.555 = 0.832$$ ✓ --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module Experiment 2** ### 🔑 Key Formulas (Must Memorize) - **Malus's Law:** $I = I_0 \cos^2\theta$ - **Intensity after polarizer:** $I_{\text{pol}} = I_{\text{inc}}/2$ - **Specific rotation:** $[S] = \dfrac{\theta}{C \cdot l}$ - **Brewster's Law:** $\tan\theta_B = n$ - **Brewster geometry:** $\theta_B + r = 90°$ ### 📐 Critical Unit Conversions - Length must be in **decimeters (dm)** for specific rotation calculations - 1 dm = 10 cm = 100 mm - Wavelength of sodium D-line = **5893 Å** = 589.3 nm ### 🧪 Experimental Points - Maximum transmission at **$\theta = 0°$** (parallel axes) - Complete extinction at **$\theta = 90°$** (crossed axes) - Half intensity at **$\theta = 45°$** - Graph of $I$ vs $\cos^2\theta$ must be a **straight line through origin** - Slope of the linear graph equals $I_0$ ### 🔬 Engineering Applications - LCD displays and screens - Polaroid sunglasses (UV glare reduction) - Sugar industry polarimeters - Optical fiber communication systems - Stress analysis using photoelastic materials ### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid 1. **Forgetting to convert cm → dm** in specific rotation (loses 2-3 marks) 2. Confusing **dextrorotatory (+)** with **laevorotatory (-)** 3. Not specifying temperature and wavelength in specific rotation answers 4. Plotting $I$ vs $\theta$ (curved) instead of $I$ vs $\cos^2\theta$ (linear) for verification 5. Mixing up Brewster angle with critical angle ### 📊 Examiner's Hot Topics - Numerical problems on Malus's Law (very frequent — 5 marks minimum) - Derivation of Malus's Law (asked almost every semester) - Specific rotation calculations with proper unit handling - Brewster's angle problems with Snell's Law verification - Graph interpretation and slope calculation

topics.title: Viva and Timely completion of Lab Reports / Record.
