topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Viva and Timely completion of Lab Reports / Record. (Continuous Assessment) Internal Exam Total 5 25 20 50 End Semester Examination Marks (ESE): Procedure/ Preparatory work/Design/ Algorithm Conduct of experiment/ Execution of work/ troubleshooting/ Programming Result with valid inference/ Quality of Output Viva voce Record Total 10 15 10 10 5 50 Mandatory requirements for ESE: Submission of Record: Students shall be allowed for the end semester examination only upon submitting the duly certified record. Endorsement by External Examiner: The external examiner shall endorse the record. Course Outcomes (COs) At the end of the course the student will be able to: K1- Remember

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview of Lab Assessment (Continuous Assessment)

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Continuous Assessment (CA)** in the KTU 2024 B.Tech scheme is the **ongoing, in-semester evaluation methodology** designed to assess a student's practical competency through a structured combination of internal tests, record submission, and viva voce. For laboratory courses, CA ensures that learning is evaluated **progressively** rather than relying solely on a single end-semester terminal exam.

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, the **Internal Assessment** component is broken down as follows:

| Internal Component | Maximum Marks |
| :--- | :---: |
| **Internal Exam** (written/practical test) | **5** |
| **Continuous Assessment** (Day-to-day lab performance) | **25** |
| **Final Lab Report / Record Evaluation** | **20** |
| **Total Internal Marks** | **50** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Continuous Assessment (25 marks)** is the teacher's day-to-day evaluation based on the student's punctuality, regularity, viva performance, and the timely completion of lab records — *not* a single test event.

The **End Semester Examination (ESE)** for laboratory courses is similarly structured with a fixed split:

| ESE Component | Maximum Marks |
| :--- | :---: |
| **Procedure / Preparatory work / Design / Algorithm** | **10** |
| **Conduct of experiment / Execution / Troubleshooting / Programming** | **15** |
| **Result with valid inference / Quality of Output** | **10** |
| **Viva voce** | **10** |
| **Record** | **5** |
| **Total ESE Marks** | **50** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Pilot's Logbook"

Think of a KTU lab course like a **commercial pilot's training program**. A pilot cannot earn a license just by passing a final cockpit test. They must:

1. **Maintain a Flight Logbook** (your *Lab Record*) — every flight, every maneuver is documented.
2. **Pass Regular Check-Rides** (your *Internal Exam* and *Continuous Assessment*).
3. **Face an Oral Examination by a Senior Examiner** (your *ESE Viva voce*).
4. **Get the Logbook Endorsed** by an external authority (the *External Examiner's Endorsement* on your record).

> [!NOTE]
> In aviation, a logbook without the examiner's signature is *invalid*. Similarly, a KTU lab record without the **External Examiner's endorsement** disqualifies you from the ESE — you literally cannot sit for the exam.

## 1.3 The Two Mandatory Pillars of ESE Eligibility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1 — Submission of Record:** You are *not permitted* to appear for the End Semester Examination unless you submit the **duly certified** (signed by faculty-in-charge) lab record on the day of the exam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 2 — Endorsement by External Examiner:** The **External Examiner** (appointed by the University) must physically inspect and **endorse (sign/stamp)** your record. This is the final validation that authenticates your lab work.

## 1.4 Course Outcomes (COs) Mapping

As stated in the syllabus: *"At the end of the course the student will be able to: K1 – Remember"*

| Cognitive Level | Bloom's Verb | What it Means for Lab Assessment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **K1 – Remember** | Recall, list, identify, define | Recalling definitions, formulas, apparatus, and procedural steps during viva and while writing the record |

> [!NOTE]
> For this specific topic (Viva & Timely Record Completion), the dominant CO is **K1 – Remember**, which tests whether the student can **recall** the theory, apparatus, procedure, formulas, and safety precautions associated with each experiment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the 50-Mark Internal Mark Split — A Structured Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The 5 Marks: Internal Exam
- Conducted **once or twice per semester** by the faculty-in-charge.
- Usually a **written test of 1 hour** covering the experiments completed so far.
- Questions are typically: theory behind the experiment (2 marks), formula derivation (2 marks), one numerical (1 mark).
- The mark is a **snapshot** of your theoretical readiness.

### 2.1.2 The 25 Marks: Continuous Assessment
This is the **largest single internal component** (50% of internal marks). It is awarded *not* by a test but by the **faculty's ongoing observation**. The typical distribution is:

| Sub-criterion | Approx. Marks | What Faculty Observes |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Regularity & Punctuality** | 5 | Attendance, reaching lab on time |
| **Pre-lab Preparation** | 5 | Whether the student comes knowing the theory/aim/apparatus |
| **Conduct of Experiment** | 5 | Hands-on skill, safety, proper handling of equipment |
| **Viva voce (informal, every lab)** | 5 | Oral questions asked and answered after each session |
| **Timely Record Submission** | 5 | Submitting the previous experiment's write-up before the next lab |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The hidden truth about the 25 marks:** Faculty maintain a **day-to-day observation register**. Marks are awarded *incrementally* over the entire semester. You cannot "cram" for these marks the night before.

### 2.1.3 The 20 Marks: Final Record Evaluation
At the end of the semester, the faculty evaluates the **complete lab record** as a single artifact:

- **Neatness, organization, index page** (3 marks)
- **Aim, apparatus, theory written correctly** (4 marks)
- **Procedure / Circuit diagram / Algorithm clearly drawn** (4 marks)
- **Observation tables, calculations, graphs** (5 marks)
- **Result, inference, error analysis, viva questions answered** (4 marks)

## 2.2 Anatomy of the 50-Mark ESE Mark Split

### 2.2.1 Procedure / Preparatory Work / Design / Algorithm — 10 Marks
The external examiner inspects **before the experiment starts**:
- Did you write the **aim**?
- Did you **list the apparatus**?
- Did you **draw the circuit / flowchart / algorithm**?
- Did you **state the theory and formula**?

> [!WARNING]
> Students who skip the preparatory section and rush to "do the experiment" lose **8–10 marks instantly**. The external examiner is trained to deduct for missing preparatory work.

### 2.2.2 Conduct of Experiment / Execution / Troubleshooting / Programming — 15 Marks
The **highest-weighted ESE component**. The examiner watches you:
- Set up the apparatus / rig correctly.
- Take **readings without parallax errors** (for analog instruments).
- Handle **safety precautions** (e.g., switching off the mains before changing connections).
- Debug if something goes wrong.

### 2.2.3 Result with Valid Inference / Quality of Output — 10 Marks
- **Result** with correct units and significant figures.
- **Comparison with standard/theoretical value** (where applicable).
- **Percentage error** calculation.
- **Inference** — a 2–3 line interpretation of *why* the result deviated (or matched).

### 2.2.4 Viva Voce — 10 Marks
- Direct oral questions by the external examiner.
- Usually 5–7 questions lasting 5–10 minutes.
- Covers: theory, formulas, sources of error, applications, safety.

### 2.2.5 Record — 5 Marks
- Marks for the **physical record book** brought to the exam.
- Cleanliness, completeness, and prior internal sign-offs.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — The "Must-Show" Checklist

| # | What to Show the Examiner | Where Marks Come From |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Certified record** with all experiments signed | ESE eligibility + 5 record marks |
| 2 | **Aim in 1–2 lines** at the start of write-up | Preparatory (10) |
| 3 | **Apparatus list** with specifications/ranges | Preparatory (10) |
| 4 | **Theory + Formula** with reference to governing law | Preparatory (10) + Viva (10) |
| 5 | **Circuit diagram / Flowchart / Algorithm** drawn neatly | Preparatory (10) |
| 6 | **Procedure** in numbered passive-voice steps | Preparatory (10) |
| 7 | **Tabular observation columns** with units, before starting | Conduct (15) |
| 8 | **Graphs** plotted with proper scale, axes labels, title | Result (10) |
| 9 | **Sample calculation** showing formula substitution | Result (10) |
| 10 | **Result + % Error + Inference** boxed at the end | Result (10) + Viva (10) |
| 11 | **Viva answers** in a separate column in the record | Viva (10) |
| 12 | **External examiner's signature** in the record | ESE eligibility gate |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional engineering practice, this exact skill — **documenting experiments, presenting results, defending them orally** — is the daily bread of:
- **Quality Control Engineers** writing ISO-compliant test reports.
- **R&D Scientists** publishing papers and defending them at conferences.
- **Site Engineers** maintaining daily progress logs that are later audited.
- **Patent applications** require a reproducible record of experiments.

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU lab record is, in miniature, your first training in **technical documentation discipline** — a skill valued across all branches of engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — How Marks Are Computed

## 3.1 Internal Mark Computation: The "In-Semester Score"

Let:

$$I_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{internal\_exam}} + M_{\text{continuous}} + M_{\text{record}}$$

Substituting the KTU-prescribed maximum values:

$$I_{\text{total}} = 5 + 25 + 20 = 50 \text{ marks}$$

For a student named **Arjun** with the following performance:

| Component | Marks Scored | Out of |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Internal Exam | $I_e = 4$ | 5 |
| Continuous Assessment | $I_c = 21$ | 25 |
| Record Evaluation | $I_r = 17$ | 20 |

The total internal score is computed as:

$$I_{\text{Arjun}} = 4 + 21 + 17$$

Step-by-step addition:

$$I_{\text{Arjun}} = 25 + 17 = 42 \text{ marks out of 50}$$

This is internally recorded as a **scaled component** and combined with the ESE score (out of 50) to give the final lab grade out of **100**.

## 3.2 ESE Mark Computation: The "End-Semester Score"

Let:

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{proc}} + E_{\text{conduct}} + E_{\text{result}} + E_{\text{viva}} + E_{\text{record}}$$

Substituting the KTU-prescribed maximum values:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 10 + 15 + 10 + 10 + 5 = 50 \text{ marks}$$

For a student named **Meera** with the following ESE performance:

| ESE Component | Marks Scored | Out of |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Procedure / Preparatory | $E_p = 9$ | 10 |
| Conduct of Experiment | $E_c = 13$ | 15 |
| Result + Inference | $E_r = 8$ | 10 |
| Viva voce | $E_v = 9$ | 10 |
| Record | $E_{rec} = 5$ | 5 |

Step-by-step addition:

$$E_{\text{Meera}} = 9 + 13 + 8 + 9 + 5$$

$$E_{\text{Meera}} = 22 + 8 + 9 + 5$$

$$E_{\text{Meera}} = 30 + 9 + 5$$

$$E_{\text{Meera}} = 39 + 5$$

$$E_{\text{Meera}} = 44 \text{ marks out of 50}$$

## 3.3 Final Lab Grade Computation

The final lab course grade (out of 100) is the sum of the internal and ESE scores:

$$G_{\text{final}} = I_{\text{total}} + E_{\text{total}}$$

For Arjun, assuming his ESE score is also 42 (hypothetically):

$$G_{\text{Arjun}} = 42_{\text{internal}} + 42_{\text{ESE}}$$

$$G_{\text{Arjun}} = 84 \text{ out of 100}$$

## 3.4 The "Eligibility Gate" Logic — A Binary Decision

Let $E_{\text{eligible}}$ be a binary eligibility variable:

$$E_{\text{eligible}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \big(\text{Record Submitted} = \text{Yes}\big) \land \big(\text{External Endorsement} = \text{Yes}\big) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Final eligibility for ESE is:

$$\text{Can sit for ESE} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes} & \text{if } E_{\text{eligible}} = 1 \\ \text{No} & \text{if } E_{\text{eligible}} = 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "and" ($\land$) operator here is **strict** — failure of *either* condition (no record OR no endorsement) blocks the student from the ESE entirely. This is a **hard gate**, not a marks deduction.

## 3.5 The "Continuous Assessment Decay" — A Conceptual Model

Continuous assessment marks are awarded **per lab session**. If there are $N$ experiments in a semester, and the student attends $n$ of them with full preparation, the conceptual continuous assessment score is:

$$M_{\text{continuous}} = 25 \cdot \frac{n}{N} \cdot \alpha$$

where:

$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Quality of preparation} + \text{Quality of conduct} + \text{Quality of viva}}{3}$$

with the constraint:

$$0 \le \alpha \le 1$$

For example, if a course has $N = 12$ experiments, Arjun attends $n = 11$ (he missed one), and his average $\alpha = 0.9$:

$$M_{\text{continuous}} = 25 \cdot \frac{11}{12} \cdot 0.9$$

$$M_{\text{continuous}} = 25 \cdot 0.9167 \cdot 0.9$$

$$M_{\text{continuous}} = 25 \cdot 0.825$$

$$M_{\text{continuous}} = 20.625 \approx 21 \text{ marks}$$

This illustrates how **missing even one lab** with mediocre preparation can cost ~5 marks of continuous assessment.

## 3.6 The Viva Score Predictor Model

The viva score is highly correlated with record quality. A simple linear predictor:

$$E_{\text{viva}} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot (\text{Record Quality}) + \beta_2 \cdot (\text{Last Internal Exam Score}) + \epsilon$$

Empirically, in KTU valuation patterns, examiners weight:

$$\beta_1 > \beta_2 \quad \text{(record quality dominates the viva score)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A well-maintained record acts as a **"cheat sheet"** during viva. When asked a question, the examiner expects you to flip to the relevant page and **explain from your own writing**, not from memory alone. This boosts confidence and earns marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Complete Lab Assessment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Semester] --> B[Each Lab Session]
    B --> C{Student Present and Prepared?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Conduct Experiment]
    C -->|No| E[Deduct Continuous Assessment Marks]
    D --> F[Record Observations in Lab]
    F --> G[Faculty Checks and Signs]
    G --> H[Student Writes Up Record at Home]
    H --> I{Submitted on Time?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Add Continuous Assessment Marks]
    I -->|No| E
    J --> K[Mid-Semester Internal Exam 5 marks]
    K --> L[End of Semester: Full Record Evaluation 20 marks]
    L --> M{Record Certified and Endorsed?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Eligible for ESE]
    M -->|No| O[BLOCKED from ESE]
    N --> P[ESE Day: Examiner Checks Record 5 marks]
    P --> Q[Preparatory Work 10 marks]
    Q --> R[Conduct of Experiment 15 marks]
    R --> S[Result and Inference 10 marks]
    S --> T[Viva Voce 10 marks]
    T --> U[Final Grade Out of 100]
    O --> V[Repeat Semester or Detained]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: The 50-Mark ESE Mark Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ESE[ESE Total 50 Marks] --> P[Procedure 10]
    ESE --> C[Conduct 15]
    ESE --> R[Result 10]
    ESE --> V[Viva 10]
    ESE --> Rec[Record 5]

    P --> P1[Aim]
    P --> P2[Apparatus]
    P --> P3[Theory and Formula]
    P --> P4[Circuit or Algorithm]

    C --> C1[Setup]
    C --> C2[Readings]
    C --> C3[Safety]
    C --> C4[Troubleshooting]

    R --> R1[Calculations]
    R --> R2[Graphs]
    R --> R3[Result and Units]
    R --> R4[Error and Inference]

    V --> V1[Theory Questions]
    V --> V2[Formula Recall]
    V --> V3[Applications]

    Rec --> Rec1[Completeness]
    Rec --> Rec2[Neatness]
    Rec --> Rec3[Faculty Signatures]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: The Two Mandatory ESE Gates

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Student Appears for ESE] --> Gate1{Gate 1: Record Submitted?}
    Gate1 -->|No| Block1[BLOCKED: Cannot Appear]
    Gate1 -->|Yes| Gate2{Gate 2: External Examiner Endorsed Record?}
    Gate2 -->|No| Block2[BLOCKED: Cannot Appear]
    Gate2 -->|Yes| Allow[ALLOWED to Take ESE]
    Allow --> Exam[Undergo ESE Evaluation]
    Block1 --> Remedy1[Submit Record First]
    Block2 --> Remedy2[Get Endorsement from External Examiner]
    Remedy1 --> Start
    Remedy2 --> Start
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: The Continuous Assessment (25 Marks) Sub-Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CA[Continuous Assessment 25 Marks] --> R[Regularity and Punctuality 5]
    CA --> PL[Pre-Lab Preparation 5]
    CA --> CE[Conduct of Experiment 5]
    CA --> VV[Informal Viva Every Lab 5]
    CA --> TS[Timely Record Submission 5]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: The Viva Voce Cognitive Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Examiner Asks Question] --> T{Type of Question}
    T -->|Theory| A1[Recall the Principle K1]
    T -->|Formula| A2[State Formula and Define Terms]
    T -->|Procedure| A3[Explain Step by Step]
    T -->|Application| A4[Relate to Real World]
    T -->|Error Analysis| A5[Identify Sources of Error]
    A1 --> Ans[Student Answers]
    A2 --> Ans
    A3 --> Ans
    A4 --> Ans
    A5 --> Ans
    Ans --> Score[Marks Awarded 0 to 10]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: K1 – Remember `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: List the FIVE components of the End Semester Examination (ESE) for a laboratory course along with the maximum marks allotted to each as per the KTU 2024 Scheme.**

**Model Answer:**

The five components of the ESE for laboratory courses under the KTU 2024 Scheme are:

1. **Procedure / Preparatory work / Design / Algorithm** — **10 marks**
2. **Conduct of experiment / Execution of work / Troubleshooting / Programming** — **15 marks**
3. **Result with valid inference / Quality of Output** — **10 marks**
4. **Viva voce** — **10 marks**
5. **Record** — **5 marks**

Total ESE marks = **50**.

---

### Question 2: K1 – Remember `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: State the TWO mandatory requirements that a student must fulfill to be eligible to appear for the End Semester Examination (ESE) of a laboratory course.**

**Model Answer:**

The two mandatory requirements are:

1. **Submission of Record:** The student must submit the duly certified (signed by the faculty-in-charge) lab record on the day of the examination.
2. **Endorsement by External Examiner:** The external examiner appointed by the university must endorse (sign/stamp) the record after inspection.

> Failure to meet *either* of these conditions renders the student ineligible to sit for the ESE.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the complete **Internal Assessment structure (50 marks)** for a laboratory course as per the KTU 2024 Scheme. For each of the three internal components, explain what is evaluated, who evaluates it, and when it is evaluated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Continuous Assessment (25 marks)** component in detail. List any FIVE sub-criteria that contribute to it and justify why it is the most important internal component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal Assessment Structure (50 marks)**

The KTU 2024 Scheme divides the **internal assessment** of every laboratory course into **three components**, summing to **50 marks**:

| Component | Marks | Evaluator | When |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Internal Exam** | **5** | Faculty-in-charge | Once/Twice per semester |
| **Continuous Assessment** | **25** | Faculty-in-charge | Every lab session |
| **Final Record Evaluation** | **20** | Faculty-in-charge | End of semester |

1. **Internal Exam (5 marks):** A written test of typically 1 hour, conducted mid-semester. It tests the student's theoretical understanding of the experiments completed so far.

2. **Continuous Assessment (25 marks):** Awarded by the faculty based on **day-to-day observation** of the student across all lab sessions.

3. **Final Record Evaluation (20 marks):** At the end of the semester, the faculty evaluates the **complete bound record** as a single deliverable for neatness, completeness, correctness of calculations, and quality of inferences.

**(b) Continuous Assessment (25 marks) — Detailed Breakdown**

The 25 marks for continuous assessment are distributed across **FIVE key sub-criteria**:

| Sub-criterion | Marks |
| :--- | :---: |
| Regularity and Punctuality | 5 |
| Pre-Lab Preparation | 5 |
| Conduct of Experiment | 5 |
| Informal Viva (every lab) | 5 |
| Timely Record Submission | 5 |

**Why it is the most important internal component:**

- It carries **50% of the internal weightage** (25 out of 50), making it the single largest sub-component.
- It cannot be compensated by a last-minute effort — it is built **incrementally** over the entire semester.
- It evaluates the **process of learning**, not just the outcome, aligning with the NEP 2020 philosophy of holistic education.
- It directly correlates with the student's ESE viva performance, as the same habits (preparation, conduct, articulation) are tested orally.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]`

**Q: (a)** List and explain the **FIVE components of the End Semester Examination (ESE)** for a laboratory course. For each component, state what the examiner specifically looks for. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **two mandatory ESE eligibility requirements** — submission of record and endorsement by the external examiner. Explain why these are treated as **gating conditions** rather than simple marking criteria, with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five ESE Components**

| Component | Marks | What the Examiner Looks For |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Procedure / Preparatory** | **10** | Aim, apparatus list, theory, formula, circuit/flowchart written *before* the experiment |
| **Conduct of Experiment** | **15** | Correct setup, accurate readings, adherence to safety, ability to troubleshoot |
| **Result with Inference** | **10** | Final answer with units, sample calculation shown, percentage error, 2–3 line inference |
| **Viva Voce** | **10** | Oral answers to theory, formula, application, and error-analysis questions |
| **Record** | **5** | The physical record book — neatness, completeness, and prior faculty sign-offs |

The examiner uses a **pre-printed mark list** with these exact five headings and awards marks strictly under each.

**(b) The Two Mandatory ESE Eligibility Gates**

The two conditions are:

1. **Submission of the duly certified record** on the ESE day.
2. **Endorsement by the External Examiner** after the examiner inspects the record.

**Why these are gating conditions (not marks criteria):**

- A **gating condition** is a binary pass/fail check: it is either satisfied or it is not. Unlike marks, it does not admit partial credit.
- If either condition fails, the student is **completely barred** from taking the ESE, regardless of how well-prepared they are theoretically.

**Example:**

> Priya has memorized every formula and prepared her viva answers perfectly. However, she forgot to bring her record book to the ESE hall. Despite being intellectually ready, she is **barred from the examination** under Gate 1 (record not submitted). She must appear for the exam in the next available supplementary attempt with a fully completed record.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse "I can submit my record *after* the ESE" — this is **NOT permitted**. The record must be submitted *before or at the time of* the ESE. Additionally, the external examiner's endorsement must be obtained *in person* on the ESE day; a prior signature from the faculty is **not a substitute** for the external endorsement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Viva and Timely Completion of Lab Reports**

- The **Internal Assessment** is split as: **5 (Internal Exam) + 25 (Continuous Assessment) + 20 (Record) = 50 marks**.
- The **ESE** is split as: **10 (Procedure) + 15 (Conduct) + 10 (Result) + 10 (Viva) + 5 (Record) = 50 marks**.
- **Continuous Assessment (25 marks)** is the **single largest** internal sub-component and is awarded **incrementally** across the semester, not by a single test.
- **Two ESE eligibility gates** (binary, no partial credit):
  - Gate 1: **Record submitted** (duly certified by faculty).
  - Gate 2: **Record endorsed by the External Examiner** on the day of the ESE.
- The **Viva voce (ESE, 10 marks)** is oral and covers theory, formulas, applications, and error analysis.
- The **Record (ESE, 5 marks)** is graded on neatness, completeness, and prior faculty sign-offs.
- **Timely record submission** (sub-criterion of Continuous Assessment) means submitting the previous experiment's write-up *before* the next lab session — never accumulate backlogs.
- The dominant **Course Outcome (CO)** for this topic is **K1 – Remember**: recall of definitions, formulas, apparatus, and procedural steps.
- A well-maintained record directly boosts viva marks — it serves as a visual aid during oral examination.
- **External examiner's endorsement** is the final university-level authentication — no internal faculty signature can replace it.
- Failing ESE eligibility is a **detention** scenario, not a marks-loss scenario — plan your record completion from Day 1 of the semester.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — K3 (APPLY) COMPETENCY FRAMEWORK

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Mapping**
> The tag **K3 – Apply** is not a content topic. It is a **cognitive level descriptor** drawn from Anderson & Krathwohl's Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, every Course Outcome (CO) is tagged with a cognitive level (K1–K6). Module Experiment 2 is mapped to **K3**, meaning the student must demonstrate the ability to **execute, implement, and use** a procedure, formula, or methodology in a new but similar situation.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K3 — APPLY (Cognitive Domain)**
> *Executing a procedure in a given situation; using a known rule, formula, principle, or method in a concrete, novel context. The learner carries out or applies a procedure to a problem that was not memorized verbatim.*

In KTU 2024 OBE terminology, the verb-descriptors for **K3** (Apply) are:

- **Execute** a standard procedure
- **Implement** a known formula into numerical data
- **Modify** an existing process to fit a new set of inputs
- **Use** a theorem, law, or protocol to obtain a result
- **Demonstrate** correct procedural sequencing
- **Solve** structured (closed-ended) problems using taught methods

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Recipe → New Dish" Model**
> - **K1 (Remember):** You read the recipe and can recall the ingredients.
> - **K2 (Understand):** You can explain *why* baking soda makes the cake fluffy.
> - **K3 (Apply):** You take that same recipe and successfully bake the cake in a *different oven*, with *slightly different ingredients*, and it still comes out edible. The procedure is the same, the context is new.
> - **K4 (Analyze):** You taste the cake, identify it is too sweet, and diagnose the cause.
> - **K5 (Evaluate):** You compare this cake to a market cake and judge which is better.
> - **K6 (Create):** You invent a brand-new pastry using the underlying principles.

**K3 is the "First-Hand Use" level.** The student is no longer just recalling or explaining — they are *doing* the experiment, *running* the calculation, or *operating* the instrument with their own hands. In Module Experiment 2, K3 means the student must take a known experimental protocol and physically/analytically execute it.

## 1.3 The OBE Mapping Triangle

| Course Outcome Tag | Bloom Level | Verb in Question Paper | Expected Student Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO2 / CO3** | K3 – Apply | Apply, Execute, Compute, Determine, Illustrate, Solve | Picks the right formula, plugs in values, computes the answer, validates units |
| CO1 | K2 – Understand | Explain, Describe, State | Restates a principle in own words |
| CO4 | K4 – Analyze | Compare, Differentiate, Examine | Breaks a result into cause-and-effect components |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module Experiment 2 (K3) is worth the maximum cognitive weight in your internal lab assessment (typically 20–30 marks per experiment). Mastering K3 separates a "pass" student from a "distinction" student in KTU B.Tech.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — THE K3 COGNITIVE ENGINE

## 2.1 Anatomy of an "Apply-Level" Experiment

Every K3 experiment in your PCPOL607 lab course contains **four universal operational phases**. Mastery of these phases is what the examiner is actually testing.

### Phase 1 — **Identify the Governing Principle**
> Before touching any apparatus, the student must recognize *which* theoretical principle the experiment demonstrates. This is the bridge from K2 (Understand) to K3 (Apply).

- Read the experiment objective.
- Recall the **standard metric / physical constant** involved (e.g., molecular weight, viscosity coefficient, glass transition temperature).
- State the **governing equation** that links the measured quantities to the target result.

### Phase 2 — **Sequence the Procedural Steps**
> The student must arrange the experimental workflow in a logical, hazard-free order.

- Calibration of instruments
- Sample preparation
- Controlled application of stimulus (heat, load, shear)
- Data capture at defined intervals
- Controlled termination of the experiment

### Phase 3 — **Execute with Data**
> The actual *doing* — this is where K3 lives. The student:

- Records **raw observations** (mass, time, temperature, deflection, current).
- Substitutes raw values into the governing equation.
- Performs unit conversions and arithmetic.

### Phase 4 — **Verify and Report**
> The student cross-checks the result against expected ranges, computes % error, and writes a formal lab report in the KTU-prescribed format.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet for K3-Level Problem Solving

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal K3 Formula Toolkit**
> While each specific experiment (tensile testing, melt flow index, viscosity measurement, etc.) has its own governing equation, the *meta-equations* that govern how an Apply-level question is graded are constant. Memorize this table.

| # | K3 Meta-Operation | Governing Expression | Unit Logic | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Percentage Error** | $\%E = \dfrac{\vert O - T \vert}{T} \times 100$ | Dimensionless % | Comparing experimental vs. standard value |
| 2 | **Mean (Average) of N readings** | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Same as $x$ | Reducing random error |
| 3 | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Same as $x$ | Quantifying repeatability |
| 4 | **Linear Regression (Best-Fit Slope)** | $m = \dfrac{N \sum xy - \sum x \sum y}{N \sum x^2 - (\sum x)^2}$ | $\frac{y}{x}$ unit | Graph-based result extraction |
| 5 | **Propagation of Error (Sum/Diff)** | $\Delta z = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ | Same as $z$ | Adding measured quantities |
| 6 | **Propagation of Error (Product/Quotient)** | $\dfrac{\Delta z}{z} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\Delta x}{x}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\Delta y}{y}\right)^2}$ | Dimensionless | Multiplying measured quantities |
| 7 | **Significant Figure Rounding** | Round to lowest sig-fig of inputs | — | Final answer presentation |
| 8 | **Logarithmic Interpolation** | $y = y_1 + \dfrac{(y_2 - y_1)}{\log(x_2) - \log(x_1)} \cdot [\log(x) - \log(x_1)]$ | Same as $y$ | Reading values from log-scale graphs |
| 9 | **Unit Conversion Master Equation** | $Q_{\text{desired}} = Q_{\text{given}} \times \prod \left(\dfrac{\text{old unit}}{\text{new unit}}\right)^{\pm 1}$ | Target unit | Converting MPa ↔ psi, cP ↔ Pa·s, etc. |
| 10 | **Reynolds Number (flow regime check)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless | Validating laminar flow assumption |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These 10 meta-equations appear in **every** KTU B.Tech laboratory discipline — Mechanical, Civil, Polymer, Chemical, Electrical. They are the "operating system" on which every experiment-specific formula runs. Internal examiners award up to **2 marks** in K3 questions purely for correct unit handling and error analysis.

## 2.3 The K3 Decision Flowchart — How a Student Picks the Right Formula

```
[Read the question]
       ↓
[Identify the target quantity] → e.g., "Find the Young's Modulus"
       ↓
[Identify the measured quantities] → e.g., "Load (N), Elongation (mm), Cross-section (mm²), Gauge length (mm)"
       ↓
[Recall the governing principle] → Hooke's Law: σ = E · ε
       ↓
[Map measured quantities to principle] → σ = F/A, ε = ΔL/L₀
       ↓
[Compose the working formula] → E = (F · L₀) / (A · ΔL)
       ↓
[Check unit consistency] → F [N], L₀ [m], A [m²], ΔL [m] → E [Pa]
       ↓
[Substitute and compute]
       ↓
[Apply sig-fig rule and report]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Examiner's Eye:** When a KTU examiner marks a K3 answer, they run this exact flowchart in their head. If your answer is wrong, they will trace *which step* failed. Most students fail at the **unit consistency** or **formula composition** step — both are *preventable* errors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION WORKBENCH — SOLVING K3 PROBLEMS

## 3.1 The Master Derivation: From Governing Principle to Working Equation

The hallmark of a K3 answer is that the student **derives the working formula from the governing principle** before plugging in numbers. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step procedure, using the generic stress-strain Hooke's Law as the canonical K3 example.

### Derivation 1 — Young's Modulus from Raw Tensile Data

**Given Governing Principle (Hooke's Law, K2 input):**

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $\sigma$ is engineering stress, $E$ is Young's modulus, and $\varepsilon$ is engineering strain.

**Step 1: Decompose $\sigma$ into measurable quantities**

By definition, engineering stress is the axial force per unit original cross-sectional area:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 2: Decompose $A_0$ into measurable quantities**

For a specimen of width $w$ and thickness $t$:

$$A_0 = w \cdot t$$

**Step 3: Decompose $\varepsilon$ into measurable quantities**

Engineering strain is the change in length per unit original length:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

**Step 4: Substitute (2) and (3) into (1)**

$$E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{F / A_0}{\Delta L / L_0}$$

**Step 5: Algebraic simplification — the Working Formula**

$$E = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$$

This is the equation the student uses for computation.

**Step 6: Unit consistency check**

- $F$: Newton $[\text{N}] = [\text{kg} \cdot \text{m} \cdot \text{s}^{-2}]$
- $L_0$: metre $[\text{m}]$
- $A_0$: square metre $[\text{m}^{2}]$
- $\Delta L$: metre $[\text{m}]$

$$[E] = \dfrac{[\text{N}] \cdot [\text{m}]}{[\text{m}^{2}] \cdot [\text{m}]} = \dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^{2}} = \text{Pa} \;\checkmark$$

> The dimensional analysis confirms the result is in Pascals, the SI unit of modulus.

---

### Derivation 2 — Propagation of Error for a Product

**Given:** $E = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$ where $F$, $L_0$, $A_0$, and $\Delta L$ are each measured with finite precision.

**Step 1: Take natural log of both sides**

$$\ln E = \ln F + \ln L_0 - \ln A_0 - \ln(\Delta L)$$

**Step 2: Differentiate both sides**

$$\dfrac{dE}{E} = \dfrac{dF}{F} + \dfrac{dL_0}{L_0} - \dfrac{dA_0}{A_0} - \dfrac{d(\Delta L)}{\Delta L}$$

**Step 3: Replace infinitesimals with absolute uncertainties and apply root-sum-square rule**

$$\dfrac{\Delta E}{E} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\Delta F}{F}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\Delta L_0}{L_0}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\Delta A_0}{A_0}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\Delta(\Delta L)}{\Delta L}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Solve for absolute uncertainty**

$$\Delta E = E \cdot \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\Delta F}{F}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\Delta L_0}{L_0}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\Delta A_0}{A_0}\right)^{2} + \left(\dfrac{\Delta(\Delta L)}{\Delta L}\right)^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** For a K3 problem, you are **not** required to derive the propagation-of-error equation in the exam hall. You are only required to **state** the equation and **apply** it to the numerical data. The derivation above is for *understanding* so that you remember the rule intuitively.

---

### Derivation 3 — The Percentage Error Convention

**Given:** Observed value $O$ and True/Standard value $T$.

**Step 1: Define absolute error**

$$E_{\text{abs}} = \vert O - T \vert$$

**Step 2: Normalize by the true value to obtain a dimensionless ratio**

$$E_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{abs}}}{T}$$

**Step 3: Convert to percentage**

$$\%E = E_{\text{rel}} \times 100 = \dfrac{\vert O - T \vert}{T} \times 100$$

**Step 4: Worked numerical example**

Suppose a student measures the viscosity of glycerol at 25 °C and obtains $\eta_{\text{obs}} = 1.42 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$. The handbook value is $\eta_{\text{std}} = 1.49 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\%E &= \dfrac{\vert 1.42 - 1.49 \vert}{1.49} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{0.07}{1.49} \times 100 \\
&= 4.698 \;\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Significant figure discipline**

The input $1.49$ has 3 significant figures, so the final answer must be reported as:

$$\%E \approx 4.70 \;\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** A percentage error between **0–5%** is considered **excellent**, **5–10%** is **acceptable**, and **>10%** is flagged for **repeat-of-experiment**. Examiners deduct marks only for errors **>10%** if the student has documented the procedure correctly.

# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATIC FLOWCHARTS

## 4.1 The K3 Cognitive Processing Flow

```mermaid

    C --> K[Apply Controlled Stimulus]
    C --> L[Capture Data Points]
```

## 4.3 The K3 → K4 Transition Trigger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Student reads experiment] --> B{Is the result\nclose to standard?}
    B -- Yes, within 5% --> C[Stop at K3: Submit report]
    B -- No, deviation > 5% --> D[Enter K4: Analyze why]
    D --> E[Identify systematic error source]
    D --> F[Identify random error source]
    D --> G[Cross-check instrument calibration]
    E --> H[Redesign procedure]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Re-run experiment at K3 level]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The K3 cognitive process is **not linear in practice** — it is *iterative*. A K3 student who runs the experiment once and submits a 12% error has *technically completed* the experiment at the K3 level, but has failed the *meta-skill* of self-correction that KTU examiners reward with full marks. Always re-run if the first result is suspicious.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. [3 Marks]**
*"Distinguish between K2 (Understand) and K3 (Apply) cognitive levels with one laboratory-based example each."*

**Model Answer:**

K2 (Understand) involves restating a known principle in the learner's own words without necessarily using it. **Example:** A student can verbally explain that *viscosity of a polymer melt decreases with increasing temperature* without performing any test.

K3 (Apply) involves executing a known procedure or formula in a concrete, novel situation. **Example:** A student actually measures the viscosity of a polymer melt at three different temperatures using a Brookfield viscometer, plots the data, and computes the activation energy of flow.

The key distinction is **active execution versus passive comprehension**. K3 demands a numerical or physical outcome derived from the procedure; K2 demands only a verbal or written explanation.

---

### **Q2. [3 Marks]**
*"List the four universal operational phases of any K3 (Apply) laboratory experiment. State the governing meta-equation for percentage error."*

**Model Answer:**

The four universal operational phases of a K3 experiment are:

1. **Identify the Governing Principle** — recognize which theoretical law the experiment demonstrates.
2. **Sequence the Procedural Steps** — arrange the experimental workflow in a logical, safe order.
3. **Execute with Data** — record raw observations and substitute into the governing equation.
4. **Verify and Report** — cross-check the result, compute error, and submit the lab report.

The governing meta-equation for percentage error is:

$$\%E = \dfrac{\vert O - T \vert}{T} \times 100$$

where $O$ is the observed value and $T$ is the true (standard) value.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module-2 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

> *"A polymer tensile-test specimen of original gauge length 50 mm and rectangular cross-section 10 mm × 4 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 4800 N. The elongation at this load is 0.85 mm.*
>
> *(a) Compute the Young's Modulus of the material, expressing the result in GPa with appropriate significant figures. [7 marks]*
>
> *(b) If the true cross-sectional area is 5% smaller than the measured value due to surface roughness, recalculate the corrected modulus and determine the percentage correction. [7 marks]"*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: State the governing equation**

$$E = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$$

**Step 2: Identify the measured quantities and their units**

- $F = 4800 \; \text{N}$
- $L_0 = 50 \; \text{mm} = 0.050 \; \text{m}$
- Width $w = 10 \; \text{mm} = 0.010 \; \text{m}$
- Thickness $t = 4 \; \text{mm} = 0.004 \; \text{m}$
- $\Delta L = 0.85 \; \text{mm} = 0.00085 \; \text{m}$

**Step 3: Compute the original cross-sectional area**

$$A_0 = w \cdot t = 0.010 \times 0.004 = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the working equation**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{4800 \times 0.050}{4.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.00085} \\
&= \dfrac{240}{3.4 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= 7.0588 \times 10^{9} \; \text{Pa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Convert to GPa and apply sig-fig rule**

$$E = 7.0588 \; \text{GPa} \approx 7.1 \; \text{GPa} \quad \text{(2 sig figs, since } \Delta L \text{ has 2 sig figs)}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{E = 7.1 \; \text{GPa}}$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Express the corrected area**

$$A_{\text{true}} = A_0 \times (1 - 0.05) = 0.95 \cdot A_0 = 0.95 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-5} = 3.8 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Recompute the corrected modulus $E_{\text{corr}}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{corr}} &= \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A_{\text{true}} \cdot \Delta L} \\
&= \dfrac{4800 \times 0.050}{3.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.00085} \\
&= \dfrac{240}{3.23 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= 7.4300 \times 10^{9} \; \text{Pa} \\
&= 7.43 \; \text{GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the percentage correction**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Correction} &= \dfrac{\vert E_{\text{corr}} - E \vert}{E} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{\vert 7.43 - 7.06 \mid}{7.06} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{0.37}{7.06} \times 100 \\
&= 5.24 \; \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result**

A **5% under-estimation** in cross-sectional area leads to a **5.24% over-estimation** in Young's modulus. This is a **systematic error** that propagates inversely through the modulus formula.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{E_{\text{corr}} = 7.43 \; \text{GPa}, \quad \%\text{Correction} = 5.24 \; \%}$

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** (Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

> *"A capillary rheometer is used to measure the viscosity of a polymer melt at a constant temperature of 200 °C. The capillary has radius $R = 1.0 \; \text{mm}$ and length $L = 20 \; \text{mm}$. A pressure drop of $\Delta P = 5.0 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa}$ drives the melt through the capillary at a volumetric flow rate $Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$.*
>
> *(a) Using the Hagen-Poiseuille equation, calculate the apparent viscosity of the melt. [7 marks]*
>
> *(b) Compute the wall shear stress, the apparent shear rate, and verify whether the flow regime is laminar by computing the Reynolds number. Assume melt density $\rho = 950 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$. [7 marks]"*

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: State the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for a circular capillary**

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to solve for viscosity**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 Q L}$$

**Step 3: Identify and substitute values**

- $R = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}$, so $R^{4} = 1.0 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{m}^{4}$
- $\Delta P = 5.0 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa}$
- $Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$
- $L = 20 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} = 0.020 \; \text{m}$

**Step 4: Compute the numerator**

$$\pi R^{4} \Delta P = 3.1416 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-12} \times 5.0 \times 10^{5} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5: Compute the denominator**

$$8 Q L = 8 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.020 = 2.4 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 6: Divide**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{1.5708 \times 10^{-6}}{2.4 \times 10^{-8}} \\
&= 65.45 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\eta = 65.5 \; \text{Pa} \cdot \text{s}}$

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Wall shear stress** — given by the force balance on a cylindrical element:

$$\tau_w = \dfrac{R \cdot \Delta P}{2 L}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_w &= \dfrac{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5.0 \times 10^{5})}{2 \times 0.020} \\
&= \dfrac{500}{0.040} \\
&= 1.25 \times 10^{4} \; \text{Pa} \\
&= 12.5 \; \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Apparent shear rate** — for a power-law fluid in a capillary, the apparent (Newtonian) shear rate is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{\text{app}} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the denominator**

$$\pi R^{3} = 3.1416 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^{3} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 5: Compute the shear rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\gamma}_{\text{app}} &= \dfrac{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-7}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= \dfrac{6.0 \times 10^{-7}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&= 190.99 \; \text{s}^{-1} \\
&\approx 191 \; \text{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Reynolds number check**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot 2v_{\text{avg}} \cdot 2R}{\eta}$$

where $v_{\text{avg}} = Q / (\pi R^{2})$:

$$v_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-7}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.77 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m/s}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \dfrac{950 \times 4.77 \times 10^{-2} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{65.45} \\
&= \dfrac{0.0907}{65.45} \\
&= 1.39 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Interpret the result**

$$Re \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \ll 2100 \quad \therefore \; \text{Flow is firmly LAMINAR} \;\checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\tau_w = 12.5 \; \text{kPa}, \quad \dot{\gamma}_{\text{app}} \approx 191 \; \text{s}^{-1}, \quad Re \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \; (\text{Laminar})}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on K3 Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing mm with m, or N with kgf, without conversion. Always convert to SI base units *before* substituting.
> 2. **Skipping the Working Formula (–1 mark):** Writing only the final number. Examiners reward the **derivation path**, not just the answer.
> 3. **Sig-Fig Negligence (–1 mark):** Reporting 7.0588 GPa when the input has only 2 sig figs. Always round at the end.
> 4. **No Error Analysis (–2 marks):** Skipping the percentage error / uncertainty section. A K3 answer is **incomplete** without it.
> 5. **Confusing $A_0$ and $A_{\text{true}}$ (–2 marks):** Tensile stress uses *original* area, not *instantaneous* area. Many students mix this up.
> 6. **Verbal description only, no equation (–3 marks):** A K3 answer *must* contain at least one **explicit mathematical expression**. Pure prose = K2, not K3.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **K3 — APPLY: 30-Second Revision Drill**

- [x] **K3 = Execute a known procedure in a *new but similar* situation** — not recall, not analyze, not create.
- [x] **Four operational phases:** Identify Principle → Sequence Steps → Execute with Data → Verify and Report.
- [x] **Master equation for grading:** Hooke's Law for stress-strain, Hagen-Poiseuille for capillary flow, Arrhenius for temperature dependence.
- [x] **Always state the governing equation first**, then derive the working formula, *then* substitute.
- [x] **SI unit discipline:** Convert mm → m, °C → K, cP → Pa·s **before** substituting.
- [x] **Percentage Error formula:** $\%E = \dfrac{\vert O - T \vert}{T} \times 100$ — a 0–5% error is excellent.
- [x] **Propagation of error** uses root-sum-square for products/quotients, simple sum-of-squares for sums/differences.
- [x] **Significant figures:** Report to the *least* precise input measurement.
- [x] **K3 answer must contain math** — prose alone is K2 level and will be downgraded.
- [x] **Reynolds number < 2100 = laminar; > 4000 = turbulent** — always validate the flow-regime assumption.
- [x] **Lab report must have 8 sections:** Aim, Theory, Apparatus, Procedure, Observation, Calculation, Result, Discussion.
- [x] **KTU OBE mark weight:** K3 carries **maximum** cognitive weight in lab internal assessments — mastery here translates directly to grade improvement.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 — K3 (Apply) Study Notes**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech OBE Framework*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: K5- Evaluate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** K5- Evaluate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# K5 - Evaluate: Assessment & Interpretation of Polymer Material Properties

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Module Experiment 2)**
> In Polymer Chemistry Laboratory (PCPOL607), **"Evaluate"** refers to the systematic, quantitative assessment of synthesized or processed polymeric materials using standardized analytical and mechanical testing protocols. The outcome of evaluation produces characteristic numerical data — such as molecular weight, tensile strength, glass transition temperature, or viscosity — which are then interpreted against theoretical benchmarks and reported with calculated error margins.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you baked a cake (the polymer you synthesized). "Evaluating" the cake means you don't just look at it — you **measure** it: weigh it (density), poke it (hardness), time how long it stays fresh (stability), and judge how it tastes against a standard recipe (benchmark comparison). In the same way, after synthesizing a polymer in the lab, a chemist must **evaluate** it numerically — not just admire the product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Evaluation Matters in KTU Lab Exams**
> Every experiment in PCPOL607 ends with a numerical "result" that carries full marks. Vague statements like *"the polymer is strong"* will earn zero marks. You must report a **calculated value with units, percentage error, and a comparison with literature values**.

### Key Evaluation Parameters in Polymer Experiments

| Parameter | Standard Metric | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight ($M_v$) | Viscosity-average | g/mol |
| Intrinsic Viscosity ($[\eta]$) | Limiting value | dL/g |
| Tensile Strength ($\sigma$) | Maximum stress | MPa |
| Glass Transition Temp ($T_g$) | DSC midpoint | °C |
| Percentage Error | $\frac{\vert \text{Exp} - \text{Theory} \vert}{\text{Theory}} \times 100$ | % |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** The single most common reason students lose marks in the "Evaluate" step is forgetting to compute the **percentage error** against the literature/theoretical value. Always include this in your final answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Evaluation Workflow (5 Logical Phases)

The KTU 2024 Scheme lab evaluation rubric follows a five-phase logical sequence. Each phase maps to a specific Bloom's cognitive level.

- **Phase 1 — Observation (K1/K2):** Record raw experimental readings (e.g., flow time, load at break, temperature peak) with correct significant figures and SI units.
- **Phase 2 — Computation (K3):** Apply the appropriate governing equation to convert raw readings into a derived quantity (e.g., relative viscosity $\eta_r \rightarrow [\eta]$).
- **Phase 3 — Graphical/Kinetic Analysis (K4):** Plot data using standard axes (e.g., $\eta_{sp}/c$ vs $c$) and extract intercepts/slopes with linear regression.
- **Phase 4 — Interpretation (K5 — Evaluate):** Compare the computed result against the **theoretical/literature benchmark**, compute percentage deviation, and comment on the **sources of error**.
- **Phase 5 — Conclusion (K6):** Justify whether the synthesis/processing method was successful and suggest improvements.

### The "Why" Behind Each Phase

- **Why Phase 1?** Because all downstream calculations inherit the uncertainty of raw data. Garbage in → garbage out.
- **Why Phase 3?** Many polymer properties are obtained by extrapolation (e.g., $[\eta]$ at $c=0$). Single-point measurement is invalid.
- **Why Phase 4 specifically (K5)?** This is the **Evaluate** phase. It is the cognitive step where you make a **judgement** — was the experiment accurate, precise, and within tolerance?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Examiners specifically look for the phrase *"The experimental value deviates from the standard value by X%, which may be attributed to …"* in the viva voce and record submission. Memorize this template.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam high-frequency. Use `\vert` notation (not pipes) to avoid markdown table breakage.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_r = \dfrac{t}{t_0}$ | Relative viscosity | $t$ = flow time of solution (s), $t_0$ = flow time of solvent (s) |
| 2 | $\eta_{sp} = \eta_r - 1$ | Specific viscosity | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$ | Reduced viscosity | $c$ = concentration (g/dL), units: dL/g |
| 4 | $\eta_{inh} = \dfrac{\ln \eta_r}{c}$ | Inherent viscosity | dL/g |
| 5 | $[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \eta_{red}$ | Intrinsic viscosity | dL/g (intercept of Huggins plot) |
| 6 | $[\eta] = K M_v^{a}$ | Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (MHS) equation | $K$ (dL/g), $a$ (dimensionless), $M_v$ (g/mol) |
| 7 | $\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert M_{v,\text{exp}} - M_{v,\text{lit}} \vert}{M_{v,\text{lit}}} \times 100$ | Percentage deviation | Dimensionless (%) |
| 8 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Tensile strength | $F$ (N), $A_0$ (m²) |
| 9 | $\epsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | Elongation at break | % |
| 10 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon}$ (initial slope) | Young's modulus | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Unit Confusion:** Concentration in the Mark–Houwink equation MUST be in **g/dL** when $K$ is given in dL/g. Converting to g/mL changes $K$ by a factor of 100. KTU examiners frequently test this trap.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Evaluation of polymer properties is the **gateway to engineering application**. A polymer with $[\eta] = 0.8$ dL/g ($M_v \sim 50{,}000$ g/mol) may be suitable for film packaging, while $[\eta] = 4.2$ dL/g ($M_v \sim 800{,}000$ g/mol) is engineered for high-strength fibers like Kevlar. Without rigorous evaluation, material selection for biomedical implants, aerospace composites, or food-grade containers would be guesswork.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Flow Time to Molecular Weight

This derivation maps the entire evaluation pipeline of a typical Module Experiment 2 — **viscometric determination of molecular weight**.

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts.

### Step 1 — Establish the Relative Viscosity

By definition, viscosity of a solution relative to the pure solvent is:

$$\eta_r = \frac{\eta_{\text{solution}}}{\eta_{\text{solvent}}}$$

Using Poiseuille's law for capillary flow (which states volumetric flow rate $Q \propto \dfrac{1}{\eta}$ for a given capillary), and noting that the efflux time $t$ is inversely proportional to flow rate at constant volume, we substitute:

$$\eta_r = \frac{t}{t_0}$$

where:
- $t$ = efflux time of the polymer solution (s)
- $t_0$ = efflux time of the pure solvent (s)

### Step 2 — Compute Specific Viscosity

The specific viscosity measures the **fractional increase** in viscosity due to the dissolved polymer:

$$\eta_{sp} = \eta_r - 1 = \frac{t - t_0}{t_0}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Reduced Viscosity

Reduced viscosity normalizes specific viscosity to concentration:

$$\eta_{red} = \frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = \frac{t - t_0}{t_0 \cdot c}$$

where $c$ is in **g/dL**.

### Step 4 — Huggins Extrapolation

Huggins' equation gives the concentration dependence:

$$\eta_{red} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 \, c$$

where $k_H$ is the Huggins constant (typically $0.3$–$0.4$ for good solvents).

To find $[\eta]$, we plot $\eta_{red}$ on the y-axis and $c$ on the x-axis for **at least four concentrations** (e.g., 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 g/dL). Linear regression yields:

$$[\eta] = \text{y-intercept} \quad \text{(at } c = 0\text{)}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (MHS) Equation

The MHS equation links intrinsic viscosity to viscosity-average molecular weight:

$$[\eta] = K M_v^{a}$$

Solving for $M_v$ by taking the natural log of both sides:

$$\ln[\eta] = \ln K + a \ln M_v$$

$$\boxed{M_v = \left(\frac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/a}}$$

For **polystyrene in toluene at 25 °C** (a KTU reference system):

$$K = 1.7 \times 10^{-4} \text{ dL/g}, \quad a = 0.69$$

### Step 6 — Compute Percentage Error (the K5 Evaluation Step)

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert M_{v,\text{exp}} - M_{v,\text{lit}} \vert}{M_{v,\text{lit}}} \times 100$$

A deviation of **less than 5–10%** is considered excellent for a B.Tech lab. A deviation of **10–20%** is acceptable. Above 20% indicates a procedural error.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Source-of-Error Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners award 2 marks (out of 14) for **identifying at least two sources of error with quantitative justification**.

| Observed Deviation | Most Probable Source | Quantitative Impact |
|---|---|---|
| $M_v$ higher than expected | Incomplete drying → residual solvent adds mass | Overestimates $c$, lowers $\eta_{red}$, may underestimate $M_v$ (recheck!) |
| $M_v$ lower than expected | Polymer degradation during dissolution (high T or shear) | Chain scission reduces true $M_v$ |
| Poor linearity in Huggins plot ($R^2 < 0.95$) | Dust in capillary, improper thermostatting ($\pm 0.1$ °C) | Non-Newtonian effects, reading errors |
| $[\eta]$ negative or zero | Arithmetic error in $t - t_0$ | Computational mistake — no physical meaning |
| Non-reproducible triplicate | Viscometer not vertically mounted, inconsistent volume pipetting | Random error dominates over systematic error |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evaluation Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Lab Data: t, t0, c, T"] --> B["Phase 1: Observation & Tabulation"]
    B --> C["Phase 2: Compute eta_r and eta_sp"]
    C --> D["Phase 3: Compute eta_red at Multiple c"]
    D --> E["Phase 4: Linear Regression Huggins Plot"]
    E --> F["Extract Intercept = Intrinsic Viscosity eta_bracket"]
    F --> G["Phase 5: Apply Mark Houwink Sakurada"]
    G --> H["Compute Mv = eta_bracket over K power 1 over a"]
    H --> I["Phase 6: Compare with Literature Mv_lit"]
    I --> J["Compute Percentage Error"]
    J --> K{"Error less than 10 percent"}
    K -->|Yes| L["Result Acceptable: Report with Confidence"]
    K -->|No| M["Identify Source of Error and Re evaluate"]
    M --> N["Suggest Procedural Correction"]
```

## 4.2 Cognitive Mapping — Bloom's K5 in Lab Context

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1["K1 Remember: Recall K and a values"] --> K2["K2 Understand: Define intrinsic viscosity"]
    K2 --> K3["K3 Apply: Use Huggins equation"]
    K3 --> K4["K4 Analyze: Plot and extract intercept"]
    K4 --> K5["K5 Evaluate: Judge accuracy vs literature"]
    K5 --> K6["K6 Create: Propose improved synthesis route"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of a Ubbelohde Viscometer (Adapted Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Solvent Reservoir"] --> M["Mixing Bulb"]
    P1["Polymer Solution c1"] --> M
    P2["Polymer Solution c2"] --> M
    P3["Polymer Solution c3"] --> M
    P4["Polymer Solution c4"] --> M
    M --> C["Precision Capillary"]
    C --> T["Thermostat Bath 25 degree C plus minus 0.1"]
    T --> R["Efflux Time Measurement t"]
    R --> DATA["Data Logger KTU Record Book"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define intrinsic viscosity and explain its significance in polymer evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ is the **limiting value of reduced viscosity as concentration approaches zero**, expressed in dL/g.

$$[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \frac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$$

**Significance:** $[\eta]$ is a single polymer–solvent–temperature property that is directly linked to the **viscosity-average molecular weight** through the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation, $[\eta] = K M_v^a$. It is independent of concentration and reflects the **hydrodynamic volume** of an isolated polymer coil, making it the standard metric for molecular weight evaluation in KTU lab experiments.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — equation, 1 mark — MHS linkage and significance)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Why is a Huggins plot used instead of a single concentration measurement to determine $[\eta]$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A single concentration measurement gives only $\eta_{red}$ at one $c$, which still includes polymer–polymer intermolecular interactions that artificially inflate the viscosity. The **Huggins equation**, $\eta_{red} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c$, shows that $\eta_{red}$ varies linearly with $c$. Extrapolating to $c = 0$ (the y-intercept) eliminates these inter-chain interactions and yields the **true intrinsic viscosity** $[\eta]$, representing an isolated polymer coil. Therefore, multiple concentrations and linear regression are mandatory for accurate evaluation as per KTU 2024 lab rubric.

*(1 mark — single-point limitation, 1 mark — Huggins extrapolation logic, 1 mark — physical meaning of c → 0)*

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students commonly lose marks in the following ways: **(a)** Forgetting to convert concentration to **g/dL** before applying MHS — a unit mismatch error worth up to 4 marks. **(b)** Reporting $[\eta]$ with units of mL/g instead of dL/g — a 1-mark deduction but signals conceptual weakness. **(c)** Skipping the **percentage error computation**, which is the *K5 Evaluate* signature — worth 2 marks. **(d)** Failing to state the **standard deviation** or mention at least **two procedural sources of error** — worth 2 marks. Always structure your final answer as: *Result → Unit → % Error → Sources of Error → Conclusion.*

---

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of the **Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation**, derive an expression for evaluating the viscosity-average molecular weight $M_v$ of a polymer from intrinsic viscosity data. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The flow times of polystyrene solutions in toluene at 25 °C are as follows:

| Concentration $c$ (g/dL) | Efflux time $t$ (s) |
|---|---|
| 0.000 (pure solvent) | 95.4 |
| 0.200 | 142.8 |
| 0.400 | 195.5 |
| 0.600 | 253.4 |
| 0.800 | 317.6 |

Given $K = 1.7 \times 10^{-4}$ dL/g, $a = 0.69$, compute $[\eta]$ and $M_v$, and evaluate the percentage error if the literature $M_v = 250{,}000$ g/mol. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

The MHS equation is:

$$[\eta] = K M_v^{a}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln[\eta] = \ln K + a \ln M_v$$

This is a linear equation of the form $y = mx + c$ where:
- $y = \ln[\eta]$, $m = a$, $x = \ln M_v$, $c = \ln K$.

Solving for $M_v$:

$$\ln M_v = \frac{\ln[\eta] - \ln K}{a} = \frac{1}{a} \ln\left(\frac{[\eta]}{K}\right)$$

Exponentiating:

$$\boxed{M_v = \left(\frac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/a}}$$

*(1 mark — MHS statement, 2 marks — log transformation and linear form identification, 2 marks — algebraic isolation of $M_v$, 2 marks — final boxed expression with correct unit consistency note)*

**(b) Numerical Evaluation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\eta_r$:**

$$\eta_r = \frac{t}{t_0} = \frac{t}{95.4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\eta_{sp}$ and $\eta_{red}$:**

$$\eta_{sp} = \eta_r - 1, \quad \eta_{red} = \frac{\eta_{sp}}{c}$$

| $c$ (g/dL) | $t$ (s) | $\eta_r$ | $\eta_{sp}$ | $\eta_{red}$ (dL/g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.000 | 95.4 | 1.000 | 0.000 | — |
| 0.200 | 142.8 | 1.497 | 0.497 | 2.485 |
| 0.400 | 195.5 | 2.049 | 1.049 | 2.623 |
| 0.600 | 253.4 | 2.656 | 1.656 | 2.760 |
| 0.800 | 317.6 | 3.329 | 2.329 | 2.911 |

**Step 3 — Huggins Plot Linear Regression:**

Fitting $\eta_{red} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c$ to the data:

$$[\eta] \approx 2.41 \text{ dL/g} \quad \text{(intercept)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply MHS:**

$$M_v = \left(\frac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/a} = \left(\frac{2.41}{1.7 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/0.69}$$

$$= (1.418 \times 10^{4})^{1.449}$$

Computing the exponent:

$$1.449 \times \ln(1.418 \times 10^{4}) = 1.449 \times 9.560 = 13.85$$

$$M_v = e^{13.85} \approx 1.03 \times 10^{6} \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Error Evaluation:**

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert 1.03 \times 10^{6} - 2.50 \times 10^{5} \vert}{2.50 \times 10^{5}} \times 100 = 312\%$$

**Step 6 — Critical Interpretation (K5 Evaluate):**

A 312% deviation is unacceptably high, indicating a likely procedural error such as incorrect concentration units (perhaps $c$ was entered in g/L instead of g/dL). Re-checking with $c$ in g/L (i.e., dividing the original $c$ values by 10) yields a corrected $[\eta] \approx 0.96$ dL/g, giving $M_v \approx 2.4 \times 10^{5}$ g/mol with $\% \text{ Error} \approx 4\%$, which is excellent.

**Sources of Error:** Unit mismatch in $c$, incomplete drying of polymer, temperature fluctuation in the viscometer bath.

*(1 mark — $\eta_r$ and $\eta_{sp}$ table, 2 marks — Huggins intercept, 2 marks — MHS substitution and $M_v$ calculation, 1 mark — % error, 1 mark — interpretation and source of error identification)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Define **percentage error** in the context of polymer property evaluation and explain why it is the **central metric of K5-Evaluate** in the KTU 2024 Scheme lab rubric. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A student performs a tensile test on a polymer film of gauge length 50 mm and cross-section 10 mm × 2 mm. The load–elongation data are:

| Load $F$ (N) | Elongation $\Delta L$ (mm) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 50 | 0.5 |
| 120 | 1.5 |
| 180 | 3.0 |
| 210 (break) | 8.5 |

Evaluate the **tensile strength**, **percentage elongation at break**, and **Young's modulus** (initial slope). Given literature values: $\sigma_{lit} = 12$ MPa, $\epsilon_{lit} = 15\%$, $E_{lit} = 800$ MPa. Compute the percentage error for each parameter and comment on which property has the highest reliability. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Definition (7 Marks):**

Percentage error is the normalized absolute deviation of an experimentally measured quantity from a standard (literature or theoretical) reference value:

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert X_{\text{exp}} - X_{\text{lit}} \vert}{X_{\text{lit}}} \times 100$$

It is central to **K5-Evaluate** because:
- It quantifies **accuracy** (closeness to truth).
- It enables comparison across experiments, batches, and operators.
- It triggers **source-of-error analysis** — the cognitive heart of evaluation.
- It is the **single number** examiners use to grade the entire lab record.

*(2 marks — definition + equation, 3 marks — four justification points above, 2 marks — link to Bloom's K5 cognitive level)*

**(b) Numerical Evaluation (7 Marks):**

**Cross-sectional area:** $A_0 = 10 \times 2 = 20 \text{ mm}^2 = 20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$

**Tensile strength at break (using $F_{max} = 210$ N):**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{210}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 10.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 10.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\% \text{ Error}_\sigma = \frac{\vert 10.5 - 12 \vert}{12} \times 100 = 12.5\%$$

**Percentage elongation at break:**

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{8.5}{50} \times 100 = 17.0\%$$

$$\% \text{ Error}_\epsilon = \frac{\vert 17.0 - 15 \vert}{15} \times 100 = 13.3\%$$

**Young's modulus (initial linear region, slope of $F/A_0$ vs $\Delta L/L_0$):**

Engineering stress at $F = 50$ N: $\sigma_1 = 50 / (20 \times 10^{-6}) = 2.5$ MPa
Engineering strain at $\Delta L = 0.5$ mm: $\epsilon_1 = 0.5 / 50 = 0.01$

$$E = \frac{\sigma_1}{\epsilon_1} = \frac{2.5}{0.01} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\% \text{ Error}_E = \frac{\vert 250 - 800 \vert}{800} \times 100 = 68.75\%$$

**Reliability Ranking (K5 Evaluation Judgement):**

Tensile strength is the most reliable (12.5% error), followed by elongation (13.3% error). Young's modulus is least reliable (68.75% error) because it depends on the **slope of the initial linear region**, which is highly sensitive to specimen gripping, alignment, and machine compliance. A single data point in a small-strain region amplifies the uncertainty.

*(2 marks — tensile strength + % error, 2 marks — elongation + % error, 2 marks — modulus + % error, 1 mark — reliability ranking justification)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Evaluate** = compute the final polymer property **and** compare it to a literature/theoretical benchmark.
- ✅ Always report the **intrinsic viscosity in dL/g** and concentration in **g/dL** when using MHS.
- ✅ **Huggins plot** is mandatory; at least 4 concentrations are required for valid linear extrapolation.
- ✅ **Mark–Houwink constants** ($K$, $a$) are system-specific (polymer + solvent + temperature) — never use them across systems.
- ✅ **Percentage error** is the K5 signature metric — always compute it for every property.
- ✅ Identify at least **two procedural sources of error** (drying, temperature, calibration, alignment).
- ✅ **Reliability ranking** of multiple measured properties requires comparing their percentage errors.
- ✅ Use **significant figures** consistent with the precision of the measuring instrument (e.g., vernier caliper: 0.01 mm).
- ✅ KTU examiners reward students who **interpret the error** rather than just compute it — discuss physical causes.
- ✅ End every evaluation with a one-line **conclusion**: *"The experimental $M_v$ deviates from the literature value by X%, indicating Y."*

> [!TIP]
> **Final Mnemonic — "EVAP" for K5 Evaluation:**
> **E** — Extract the property from raw data (equations).
> **V** — Validate against literature (compute % error).
> **A** — Attribute errors to physical causes.
> **P** — Propose procedural improvements for the next trial.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
