<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Course Outcome (CO)** is a specific, measurable statement that describes what a student is expected to know, understand, or be able to demonstrate after the successful completion of a course or experiment module. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, each laboratory experiment module must articulate a minimum of 4–5 Course Outcomes, each precisely tagged to a single **Bloom’s Knowledge Level (KL)** ranging from **K1 (Remember)** to **K6 (Create)**.

The **Bloom’s Knowledge Level (KL)** is the cognitive depth indicator derived from the **Revised Bloom’s Taxonomy (Anderson & Krathwohl, 2001)**, adopted by KTU to ensure Outcome-Based Education (OBE) compliance. For an experiment in latex technology, the CO statements must be action-oriented and begin with a measurable action verb corresponding to the chosen KL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory CO Construction Rule:**
> Every CO statement MUST begin with a **single measurable action verb** (e.g., *Develop, Determine, Analyze, Illustrate, Define*) and MUST NOT contain ambiguous verbs such as *understand, learn, know, be familiar with*.

## 1.2 Bloom’s Knowledge Levels — The Six-Tier Cognitive Hierarchy

The six Bloom’s Knowledge Levels adopted by KTU are arranged in ascending order of cognitive complexity. A **higher KL number indicates a higher-order thinking skill** required from the student.

| KL Code | Cognitive Level | Definition | Example Action Verbs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **K1** | Remember (Recall) | Retrieve, recognize, and recall basic facts and terminology | Define, List, Recall, State, Name, Identify |
| **K2** | Understand (Comprehend) | Explain ideas, concepts, and interpret information | Illustrate, Summarize, Describe, Classify, Explain |
| **K3** | Apply | Use information in new situations, execute procedures | Execute, Implement, Solve, Use, Demonstrate, Perform |
| **K4** | Analyze | Draw connections, differentiate, organize, attribute | Analyze, Compare, Contrast, Differentiate, Examine |
| **K5** | Evaluate | Justify, critique, judge based on criteria | Determine, Justify, Assess, Conclude, Validate, Recommend |
| **K6** | Create | Produce original work, design, combine elements into a coherent whole | Develop, Design, Formulate, Construct, Plan, Create |

> [!NOTE]
> In the present latex experiment module, the COs span from **K1 to K6**, demonstrating a **vertically integrated learning progression** where a student moves from basic definitions (K1) all the way to the design and development of concentration processes (K6). This is the ideal KTU pattern — a single module must NOT be restricted to lower-order levels only.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase Mastery Model"

Imagine learning latex technology as **climbing a six-step staircase**, where each step is a Bloom’s Knowledge Level:

- **Step 1 (K1 — Remember):** You memorize the word *"creaming"* and its textbook definition.
- **Step 2 (K2 — Understand):** You *illustrate* (draw/explain) how creaming actually separates latex particles.
- **Step 3 (K3 — Apply):** You *execute* the laboratory procedure to cream a latex sample.
- **Step 4 (K4 — Analyze):** You *analyze* the compounded latex product for stability and viscosity.
- **Step 5 (K5 — Evaluate):** You *determine* the optimum compounding ingredients and their proportions.
- **Step 6 (K6 — Create):** You *develop* an entirely new method/protocol to concentrate the latex by creaming.

The **Course Outcomes (CO1 → CO5)** of this module are the **landing platforms on each staircase step** that certify the student has reached that level. A student who only reaches K1 is considered a *passive learner*, while one who reaches K6 is a *mastery learner* — exactly what **NEP 2020** and **KTU 2024 Scheme** aim to cultivate.

## 1.4 What is a CO-PO Mapping? (Formal Definition)

A **CO-PO Mapping** is a correlation matrix that quantifies the strength of relationship between each **Course Outcome (CO)** of a course and each **Program Outcome (PO)** of the B.Tech program. In KTU 2024, there are **12 Program Outcomes (PO1–PO12)**, and the correlation strength is expressed on a **3-point Likert scale**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Correlation Scale (Mandatory):**
> - **1** = Slight (Low) — Minimal or indirect relationship
> - **2** = Moderate (Medium) — Substantial relationship
> - **3** = Substantial (High) — Strong, direct relationship
> - **“—” (dash)** = No correlation

## 1.5 Program Outcomes (PO1–PO12) — Quick Reference

For the benefit of students, the 12 POs defined by KTU (aligned with NBA graduate attributes) are summarized below:

| PO Code | Program Outcome (Abbreviated) |
|---|---|
| **PO1** | Engineering Knowledge |
| **PO2** | Problem Analysis |
| **PO3** | Design/Development of Solutions |
| **PO4** | Conduct Investigations of Complex Problems |
| **PO5** | Modern Tool Usage |
| **PO6** | The Engineer and Society |
| **PO7** | Environment and Sustainability |
| **PO8** | Ethics |
| **PO9** | Individual and Team Work |
| **PO10** | Communication |
| **PO11** | Project Management and Finance |
| **PO12** | Life-Long Learning |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Perfect Course Outcome (CO) Statement

Every KTU 2024-compliant CO statement has **three mandatory structural components**:

**Structure:** `[Action Verb (KL-tagged)] + [Learning Content / Topic] + [Application / Context / Condition]`

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition

- **Step 1 — Select the Bloom’s Level (KL):** Decide what cognitive depth the experiment demands. For a hands-on experiment on latex creaming, the natural choice ranges from K1 (define terminology) to K6 (develop a new method).
- **Step 2 — Select the Action Verb:** Choose a verb *exclusively* from the approved KTU verb list corresponding to that KL. The verb must be **observable and measurable**.
- **Step 3 — Specify the Learning Content:** Identify the *exact* technical content the CO addresses — e.g., "concentration of latex by creaming", "compounding ingredients", "latex compounds", "properties of latex", "teamwork".
- **Step 4 — Specify the Application Context:** State the *practical setting* in which the learning will be demonstrated — e.g., "through laboratory experiment", "through group activity", "with manufacturing methods".

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this structure matters:** Without a measurable action verb, the CO becomes a "wish-list" rather than an "outcome". KTU evaluators (NBA auditors and the Board Examination Committee) will reject any CO that does not follow this three-part structure.

## 2.2 Decoding the Module's Five Course Outcomes

The following table provides the complete breakdown of the five COs assigned to **Module Experiment 2 (Latex Technology Laboratory)**:

| CO # | CO Statement (Verbatim) | Action Verb | KL | Cognitive Demand |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO1** | Develop skill to concentrate the latex by creaming. | *Develop* | **K6** | Create (Highest) |
| **CO2** | Determine the skill to prepare compounding ingredients. | *Determine* | **K5** | Evaluate |
| **CO3** | Analyze skill in the preparation of latex compounds. | *Analyze* | **K4** | Analyze |
| **CO4** | Illustrate the properties of latex with manufacturing methods. | *Illustrate* | **K2** | Understand |
| **CO5** | Define teamwork skills through group activities. | *Define* | **K1** | Remember (Lowest) |

### 2.2.1 CO1 — K6 (Create): "Develop skill to concentrate the latex by creaming"

- **Cognitive Demand:** The student must *originate* a method or procedure. In the laboratory, this translates to designing a fresh experimental setup or protocol for latex concentration using the **creaming technique**, where the addition of a creaming agent (e.g., ammonium alginate) causes the larger rubber particles to rise and form a concentrated cream layer.
- **Engineering Significance:** The creaming method is a critical industrial process for producing high-solid-content natural rubber latex (60–70% dry rubber content) used in glove and condom manufacturing.

### 2.2.2 CO2 — K5 (Evaluate): "Determine the skill to prepare compounding ingredients"

- **Cognitive Demand:** The student must *judge, validate, or decide* on the optimal composition. This means deciding *which* and *how much* of each compounding ingredient (e.g., vulcanizing agents, accelerators, antioxidants, stabilizers) is required to achieve the desired latex properties.

### 2.2.3 CO3 — K4 (Analyze): "Analyze skill in the preparation of latex compounds"

- **Cognitive Demand:** The student must *break down* the latex compound into its constituent parts and examine the relationship between the compounding ingredients and the resulting properties (viscosity, total solid content, mechanical stability time).

### 2.2.4 CO4 — K2 (Understand): "Illustrate the properties of latex with manufacturing methods"

- **Cognitive Demand:** The student must *explain* the link between latex properties (T.S., DRC, viscosity, pH, MST) and the corresponding manufacturing methods (dipping, foaming, casting, extrusion).

### 2.2.5 CO5 — K1 (Remember): "Define teamwork skills through group activities"

- **Cognitive Demand:** The student must *recall* the basic definition of teamwork and demonstrate it by actively participating in a group-based laboratory activity. This CO addresses **PO9 (Individual and Team Work)** and **PO12 (Life-Long Learning)**.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — CO Construction & Mapping

| # | Item | Formula / Rule | KTU Mandatory Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CO Count per Module** | $\text{Minimum COs} = 4$ to $6$ | KTU 2024 mandates **4–6 COs per experiment module** |
| 2 | **KL Range** | $\text{KL}_{CO} \in \{\text{K1}, \text{K2}, \text{K3}, \text{K4}, \text{K5}, \text{K6}\}$ | At least **one K1 and one higher (K4–K6) CO** required |
| 3 | **Action Verb** | $\text{Verb}_{CO} \in \text{Bloom's Approved Verb List}$ | Must be **observable & measurable** |
| 4 | **CO-PO Correlation Value ($C_{ij}$)** | $C_{ij} \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ | $0$ = No correlation; $1$ = Slight; $2$ = Moderate; $3$ = Substantial |
| 5 | **Average CO-PO Mapping (Course-level)** | $\text{Mean}_j = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{ij}}{n}$ | Used for **NBA SAR (Self-Assessment Report)** calculations |
| 6 | **Attainment Threshold (CO)** | $\text{Attainment} \geq 60\%$ | Set by Departmental Consultative Committee (DCC) |
| 7 | **Target Attainment Level** | $\text{Target} \in [50\%, 70\%]$ | Defined for **each PO** annually |
| 8 | **Number of POs (B.Tech KTU 2024)** | $N_{PO} = 12$ | Fixed for the entire program |
| 9 | **Bloom's Verb Compliance Check** | $\text{If Verb} \notin \text{List} \Rightarrow \text{CO REJECTED}$ | KTU Board Exam / NBA audit checkpoint |
| 10 | **Total Course COs (per syllabus)** | $N_{CO} = 5$ (typical for lab) | Mapped against all $N_{PO}=12$ Program Outcomes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The "—" (dash) symbol in the CO-PO table is **NOT** a zero by default in some departmental software. KTU 2024 standard treats "—" as **no correlation (value 0)**, while the explicit numerical values **1, 2, 3** indicate increasing strength. When computing averages, **only the non-dash cells are summed and averaged** over the number of COs ($n$).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of CO-PO Mapping

The CO-PO mapping is **NOT an academic exercise** — it serves critical engineering education purposes:

1. **NBA Accreditation Compliance:** The National Board of Accreditation (NBA) requires every engineering program in India to demonstrate **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. The CO-PO mapping matrix is the **primary evidence** submitted in the Self-Assessment Report (SAR).
2. **Continuous Curriculum Improvement:** By analyzing *which POs receive low mapping values across all courses*, the department can identify **curricular gaps** and introduce new courses/experiments.
3. **Industry Alignment:** POs map to industry-expected graduate attributes (engineering knowledge, ethics, teamwork, communication, lifelong learning). Strong CO-PO mapping = industry-ready graduates.
4. **Faculty Workload Distribution:** Mapping data helps in **balancing the cognitive load** across semesters and courses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Verifying the Bloom’s Knowledge Level of Each CO

The single most important skill a KTU student must demonstrate in the Board Examination is the **ability to justify the Bloom’s Level assigned to a given CO statement**. The following is the **KTU board-valuation-step-by-step method** for justifying each CO.

### 3.1.1 Justification for CO1 — "Develop skill to concentrate the latex by creaming" → **K6 (Create)**

**Step 1 — Identify the action verb.**
The action verb is *"Develop"*.

**Step 2 — Check the verb against the KTU approved verb list for each KL.**

- *Develop* appears **ONLY** in the **K6 (Create)** approved verb list.
- It does NOT appear in K1, K2, K3, K4, or K5 lists.

**Step 3 — Confirm the cognitive operation.**
The word *Develop* implies **originating, designing, or producing something new** — for example, designing a novel creaming protocol, formulating a new concentration strategy, or combining existing elements into a new process.

**Step 4 — Conclude the KL assignment.**

$$\text{Verb} = \text{Develop} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{KL} = \text{K6 (Create)}$$

**Step 5 — Justify in exam answer (board-style 2-line write-up):**
> *"The verb 'Develop' belongs exclusively to the K6 (Create) category of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy, as it requires the student to *originate* a new method or protocol to concentrate latex by creaming. The cognitive operation goes beyond mere application or analysis and demands the production of a novel solution."*

---

### 3.1.2 Justification for CO2 — "Determine the skill to prepare compounding ingredients" → **K5 (Evaluate)**

**Step 1 — Identify the action verb.**
The action verb is *"Determine"*.

**Step 2 — Check the verb against approved lists.**

- *Determine* appears in the **K5 (Evaluate)** approved verb list (also: Assess, Conclude, Validate, Recommend).

**Step 3 — Confirm the cognitive operation.**
*Determine* requires the student to **make a judgment or reach a conclusion** based on criteria — for instance, deciding the correct proportion of zinc oxide, sulphur, and accelerators for a given latex formulation.

**Step 4 — Conclude the KL assignment.**

$$\text{Verb} = \text{Determine} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{KL} = \text{K5 (Evaluate)}$$

**Step 5 — Board-style justification:**
> *"The verb 'Determine' falls under K5 (Evaluate) since the student is required to *judge* and *decide* on the appropriate compounding ingredients. Evaluation involves making decisions based on defined criteria, such as desired mechanical strength, ageing resistance, and cure characteristics."*

---

### 3.1.3 Justification for CO3 — "Analyze skill in the preparation of latex compounds" → **K4 (Analyze)**

**Step 1 — Identify the action verb.**
The action verb is *"Analyze"*.

**Step 2 — Check the verb against approved lists.**

- *Analyze* is the **canonical verb** for **K4 (Analyze)** (also: Compare, Contrast, Differentiate, Examine).

**Step 3 — Confirm the cognitive operation.**
*Analyze* requires the student to **break down the compound into constituent parts** and examine the **cause-effect relationships** between ingredient ratios and compound properties (e.g., viscosity, total solids, mechanical stability time).

**Step 4 — Conclude the KL assignment.**

$$\text{Verb} = \text{Analyze} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{KL} = \text{K4 (Analyze)}$$

**Step 5 — Board-style justification:**
> *"The verb 'Analyze' is the signature verb of K4 (Analyze), which requires the student to *dissect* the latex compound preparation process, identify the role of each ingredient, and examine how they interact to influence final product properties."*

---

### 3.1.4 Justification for CO4 — "Illustrate the properties of latex with manufacturing methods" → **K2 (Understand)**

**Step 1 — Identify the action verb.**
The action verb is *"Illustrate"*.

**Step 2 — Check the verb against approved lists.**

- *Illustrate* is listed in the **K2 (Understand)** category (also: Summarize, Describe, Classify, Explain).

**Step 3 — Confirm the cognitive operation.**
*Illustrate* requires the student to **explain or demonstrate** the relationship between latex properties (T.S., DRC, pH, viscosity) and the corresponding manufacturing methods (dipping, casting, foaming).

**Step 4 — Conclude the KL assignment.**

$$\text{Verb} = \text{Illustrate} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{KL} = \text{K2 (Understand)}$$

**Step 5 — Board-style justification:**
> *"The verb 'Illustrate' belongs to K2 (Understand) as it requires the student to *explain* and *depict* the connection between latex properties and manufacturing methods. This is a comprehension-level activity, not a creation or analysis activity."*

---

### 3.1.5 Justification for CO5 — "Define teamwork skills through group activities" → **K1 (Remember)**

**Step 1 — Identify the action verb.**
The action verb is *"Define"*.

**Step 2 — Check the verb against approved lists.**

- *Define* is the **foundational verb** of **K1 (Remember)** (also: List, Recall, State, Name, Identify).

**Step 3 — Confirm the cognitive operation.**
*Define* requires the student to **recall and state** the meaning of a term — in this case, "teamwork skills" — typically in the context of a group laboratory activity.

**Step 4 — Conclude the KL assignment.**

$$\text{Verb} = \text{Define} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{KL} = \text{K1 (Remember)}$$

**Step 5 — Board-style justification:**
> *"The verb 'Define' is the lowest-order cognitive verb in Bloom's Taxonomy, classified under K1 (Remember). It requires the student to *recall* the textbook or standard definition of teamwork, demonstrating the most basic level of cognitive engagement."*

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Verification of the CO-PO Mapping Table

The provided CO-PO mapping matrix must be **interpreted column-wise for each PO** and **row-wise for each CO**. The following is a KTU board-style analysis.

### 3.2.1 Row-wise Analysis (CO-level mapping strength)

The complete matrix provided is:

| CO \ PO | PO1 | PO2 | PO3 | PO4 | PO5 | PO6 | PO7 | PO8 | PO9 | PO10 | PO11 | PO12 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO1** | 2 | — | 2 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| **CO2** | 2 | 2 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| **CO3** | 2 | 2 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| **CO4** | 2 | — | 3 | — | — | — | — | — | 2 | — | — | — |
| **CO5** | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | 2 | — | — | 2 |

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Justification for CO4 (Highest Mapping: PO3 = 3)

**Step 1 — Identify the high-correlation cell.**
CO4 has a value of **3** at PO3, which is the *maximum possible correlation* in the KTU 3-point scale.

**Step 2 — Justify the substantial (3) correlation with PO3 (Design/Development of Solutions).**
CO4 states: *"Illustrate the properties of latex with manufacturing methods."*
This directly requires the student to **map latex properties to manufacturing process design** — a clear design/development activity. The student must connect *property X* to *process Y*, which is a design mapping operation. Therefore:

$$C_{(CO4, PO3)} = 3 \;\text{(Substantial)}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the "—" entries are justified.**
- CO1 mapped to PO2: *"—"* — This is correct because CO1 (creaming) is more about *creating* a process than *analyzing* a complex problem (which PO2 demands).
- CO1 mapped to PO6, PO7, PO8, PO9, PO10, PO11, PO12: *"—"* — Acceptable, as the verb *Develop* in the latex context does not explicitly engage society, environment, ethics, teamwork, communication, finance, or lifelong learning.

**Step 4 — Verify moderate (2) correlations.**
- CO1–PO1 = 2: *Develop* requires engineering knowledge of latex chemistry → **Moderate (2)** is correct.
- CO1–PO3 = 2: *Develop* a creaming process → design activity → **Moderate (2)** is correct.
- CO2–PO1 = 2: *Determine* compounding ingredients requires engineering knowledge → **Moderate (2)**.
- CO2–PO2 = 2: *Determine* involves problem analysis (choosing the right ingredient ratios) → **Moderate (2)**.
- CO3–PO1 = 2 and CO3–PO2 = 2: Same logic as CO2 → **Moderate (2)**.
- CO4–PO1 = 2: *Illustrate* properties requires engineering knowledge → **Moderate (2)**.
- CO4–PO9 = 2: *Illustrate* with manufacturing methods may involve team discussion → **Moderate (2)**.
- CO5–PO9 = 2: *Define* teamwork **directly** maps to PO9 (Individual and Team Work) → **Moderate (2)**.
- CO5–PO12 = 2: Group activities foster lifelong learning → **Moderate (2)**.

**Step 5 — Conclude mapping consistency.**
All cells with numerical values (1, 2, 3) are **internally consistent with the CO verbs and PO definitions**. The "—" (dash) entries represent **no meaningful correlation** and are correctly assigned.

### 3.2.3 Column-wise Average (Illustrative Computation for PO1)

$$\text{Mean}_{PO1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} C_{(CO_i, PO1)}}{5} = \frac{2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 0}{5} = \frac{8}{5} = 1.6$$

$$\text{Mean}_{PO1} = 1.6 \;\text{(on a 0–3 scale)}$$

This value of **1.6** indicates that **PO1 (Engineering Knowledge)** has a *moderate* overall mapping strength across the experiment module, which is appropriate for a hands-on laboratory course.

### 3.2.4 Column-wise Average for PO9 (Team Work)

$$\text{Mean}_{PO9} = \frac{0 + 0 + 0 + 2 + 2}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

This value of **0.8** indicates **slight-to-moderate** mapping for PO9 — appropriate because the experiment does involve group activity (CO5) and design discussions (CO4), but not all COs are team-based.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Bloom’s Knowledge Level Hierarchy (K1 → K6)

This diagram visualizes the **vertical cognitive progression** of the five COs in the experiment module, mapping each CO to its corresponding Bloom’s Knowledge Level.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1["K1 REMEMBER<br/>Define, List, Recall<br/>CO5: Define teamwork skills"]
    K2["K2 UNDERSTAND<br/>Illustrate, Explain, Summarize<br/>CO4: Illustrate properties of latex"]
    K3["K3 APPLY<br/>Execute, Implement, Solve<br/>Reserved for K3 application tier"]
    K4["K4 ANALYZE<br/>Analyze, Compare, Examine<br/>CO3: Analyze latex compound prep"]
    K5["K5 EVALUATE<br/>Determine, Assess, Validate<br/>CO2: Determine compounding ingredients"]
    K6["K6 CREATE<br/>Develop, Design, Formulate<br/>CO1: Develop creaming method"]
    K1 --> K2
    K2 --> K3
    K3 --> K4
    K4 --> K5
    K5 --> K6
    classDef lowest fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class K1 lowest
    class K2,K3 mid
    class K4,K5 high
    class K6 high
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The arrow direction ($\rightarrow$) signifies **increasing cognitive complexity**. CO5 (K1) is the lowest-order outcome, while CO1 (K6) is the highest-order outcome. The module deliberately spans the entire K1–K6 spectrum, satisfying KTU's "vertical integration" requirement for laboratory courses.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — CO-PO Mapping Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

This diagram renders a **functional architecture flow** showing how each Course Outcome (CO) connects to the relevant Program Outcomes (PO) via the 1–2–3 correlation scale.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COs["COURSE OUTCOMES MODULE 2"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **CO4 (K2)** has the **strongest single correlation (value 3)** with **PO3 (Design Solutions)**. This is because illustrating the link between latex properties and manufacturing methods is fundamentally a *design mapping* task — the student must architect a property-to-process correspondence. This is a typical KTU pattern: a low-KL CO can still map strongly (value 3) to a high-complexity PO.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Three-Point Correlation Scale Legend

```mermaid

    class L3 highScale
    class LD dashScale
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

> Define a **Course Outcome (CO)**. List the six Bloom's Knowledge Levels (K1–K6) as per the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy adopted by KTU 2024.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Course Outcome (CO)** is a specific, measurable statement that describes what a student is expected to know, understand, or demonstrate upon successful completion of a course or experiment module. It uses an **observable action verb** aligned to a **Bloom's Knowledge Level (KL)**.

The six Bloom's Knowledge Levels (in ascending cognitive order) are:

1. **K1** — Remember (Recall)
2. **K2** — Understand (Comprehend)
3. **K3** — Apply (Execute)
4. **K4** — Analyze (Differentiate)
5. **K5** — Evaluate (Judge)
6. **K6** — Create (Design/Develop)

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Pattern:** 1 mark for CO definition + 2 marks for the six KLs in correct order (½ mark each, no partial credit for wrong ordering).

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

> What is the **CO-PO mapping** in the context of KTU 2024 OBE framework? State the 3-point correlation scale used.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **CO-PO mapping** is a correlation matrix that quantifies the strength of relationship between each **Course Outcome (CO)** and each of the **12 Program Outcomes (PO1–PO12)** of the B.Tech program. It is the primary evidence required for **NBA accreditation** under Outcome-Based Education.

The **3-point correlation scale** is:

- **1** = Slight (Low) — minimal/indirect relationship
- **2** = Moderate (Medium) — substantial relationship
- **3** = Substantial (High) — strong, direct relationship
- **"—"** (dash) = No correlation

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Pattern:** 2 marks for the definition + 1 mark for the scale (with all three values listed).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

> **(a)** Justify the **Bloom's Knowledge Level (KL)** assigned to each of the following Course Outcomes of Module Experiment 2 (Latex Technology Laboratory). State the action verb, cognitive operation, and final KL for each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> - **CO1:** Develop skill to concentrate the latex by creaming.
> - **CO2:** Determine the skill to prepare compounding ingredients.
> - **CO3:** Analyze skill in the preparation of latex compounds.
> - **CO4:** Illustrate the properties of latex with manufacturing methods.
> - **CO5:** Define teamwork skills through group activities.
>
> **(b)** For the CO-PO mapping table given below, compute the **column-wise average mapping value** for PO1, PO2, PO3, PO9, and PO12. Comment on the strongest and weakest mapped POs. **(7 Marks)**
>
> | CO \ PO | PO1 | PO2 | PO3 | PO9 | PO12 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | CO1 | 2 | — | 2 | — | — |
> | CO2 | 2 | 2 | — | — | — |
> | CO3 | 2 | 2 | — | — | — |
> | CO4 | 2 | — | 3 | 2 | — |
> | CO5 | — | — | — | 2 | 2 |

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| CO | Action Verb | Cognitive Operation | Final KL | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO1** | *Develop* | Originate a new creaming method/protocol | **K6 (Create)** | 1.5 |
| **CO2** | *Determine* | Judge/validate appropriate compounding ingredients | **K5 (Evaluate)** | 1.5 |
| **CO3** | *Analyze* | Dissect latex compound into constituents, examine cause-effect | **K4 (Analyze)** | 1.5 |
| **CO4** | *Illustrate* | Explain/depict the property–manufacturing method linkage | **K2 (Understand)** | 1.5 |
| **CO5** | *Define* | Recall the textbook meaning of teamwork | **K1 (Remember)** | 1.0 |

> **Total = 7 marks** (1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.0)

> **Board-Style Summary Line (Mandatory):**
> *"The five COs span the entire K1–K6 cognitive spectrum, satisfying the KTU 2024 requirement of vertical integration in laboratory modules."*

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify the non-dash values for each PO column.**

| PO | Non-dash values across CO1–CO5 |
|---|---|
| PO1 | 2 (CO1), 2 (CO2), 2 (CO3), 2 (CO4) |
| PO2 | 2 (CO2), 2 (CO3) |
| PO3 | 2 (CO1), 3 (CO4) |
| PO9 | 2 (CO4), 2 (CO5) |
| PO12 | 2 (CO5) |

**Step 2 — Compute the column-wise average (using $n = 5$ for denominator in KTU standard).**

$$\text{Mean}_{PO1} = \frac{2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 0}{5} = \frac{8}{5} = 1.6$$

$$\text{Mean}_{PO2} = \frac{0 + 2 + 2 + 0 + 0}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

$$\text{Mean}_{PO3} = \frac{2 + 0 + 0 + 3 + 0}{5} = \frac{5}{5} = 1.0$$

$$\text{Mean}_{PO9} = \frac{0 + 0 + 0 + 2 + 2}{5} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

$$\text{Mean}_{PO12} = \frac{0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 2}{5} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate the results.**

| PO | Average Mapping | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| PO1 | **1.6** | Strongest mapped — Moderate-to-Substantial |
| PO2 | 0.8 | Slight-to-Moderate |
| PO3 | 1.0 | Moderate |
| PO9 | 0.8 | Slight-to-Moderate |
| PO12 | 0.4 | **Weakest mapped** — Slight |

**Step 4 — Comment on strongest and weakest mapped POs.**

- **Strongest Mapped PO:** **PO1 (Engineering Knowledge)** with an average of **1.6**, indicating that this experiment module strongly engages the student's engineering knowledge of latex chemistry, creaming kinetics, and compounding science.
- **Weakest Mapped PO:** **PO12 (Lifelong Learning)** with an average of **0.4**, because only one CO (CO5) explicitly addresses it. The department may consider adding a reflective learning component (e.g., a viva or report) to strengthen PO12 mapping in future revisions.

> **Total = 7 marks** (2 marks for identifying values, 3 marks for computations, 2 marks for the comment)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

> **(a)** Explain the **three mandatory structural components** of a well-formed Course Outcome (CO) statement as per KTU 2024. Rewrite the following COs by correcting any structural defects and justify the Bloom's Knowledge Level for each corrected CO. **(7 Marks)**
>
> - (i) *"Understand the latex creaming process."*
> - (ii) *"Students will learn about compounding ingredients."*
> - (iii) *"Develop skill to concentrate the latex by creaming."*
>
> **(b)** Construct a **CO-PO mapping matrix** for a hypothetical Module on *"Preparation and Testing of Rubber Vulcanizates"* with 4 COs, ensuring that **PO3 (Design Solutions)** and **PO9 (Team Work)** are each mapped with at least one value of **3 (Substantial)**. Justify each strong correlation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State the three mandatory components of a well-formed CO.**

A KTU 2024-compliant CO statement must contain:

1. **An Action Verb** (from the approved Bloom's verb list) — observable and measurable.
2. **The Learning Content** — the specific technical topic the CO addresses.
3. **The Application Context** — the practical setting in which the learning is demonstrated (e.g., "through laboratory experiment", "in industry", "via group activity").

**Step 2 — Identify defects in each CO.**

| Original CO | Defect | Corrected CO |
|---|---|---|
| (i) *"Understand the latex creaming process."* | "Understand" is **NOT** in the approved Bloom's verb list. | *"Illustrate the latex creaming process in a laboratory setting."* → **K2** |
| (ii) *"Students will learn about compounding ingredients."* | "Learn" is **NOT** observable. Subject "Students will" is unnecessary. | *"Determine the appropriate compounding ingredients for a target latex product."* → **K5** |
| (iii) *"Develop skill to concentrate the latex by creaming."* | **No defect** — well-formed. | *"Develop skill to concentrate the latex by creaming."* → **K6** (as given in the syllabus) |

**Step 3 — Justify each KL (1 mark per CO + 1 mark for the structural components).**

- (i) *Illustrate* is a K2 verb requiring the student to *explain* the creaming process — **K2 (Understand)**.
- (ii) *Determine* is a K5 verb requiring the student to *judge* the appropriate ingredients — **K5 (Evaluate)**.
- (iii) *Develop* is the canonical K6 verb requiring the student to *create* a new concentration method — **K6 (Create)**.

> **Total = 7 marks** (2 marks for the three structural components + 3 marks for CO corrections + 2 marks for KL justifications)

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Define 4 COs for the hypothetical module.**

| CO # | Hypothetical CO Statement | KL |
|---|---|---|
| **CO1** | *Design a vulcanization protocol for natural rubber using sulphur and accelerators.* | K6 |
| **CO2** | *Evaluate the cross-link density of the prepared vulcanizate by swelling test.* | K5 |
| **CO3** | *Analyze the tensile strength, hardness, and elongation of the vulcanizate.* | K4 |
| **CO4** | *Define the safety protocols for hot-press vulcanization equipment through team-based demonstration.* | K1 |

**Step 2 — Construct the CO-PO mapping matrix ensuring PO3 and PO9 each have a value of 3.**

| CO \ PO | PO1 | PO2 | PO3 | PO4 | PO5 | PO6 | PO7 | PO8 | PO9 | PO10 | PO11 | PO12 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO1** | 2 | 2 | **3** | 2 | — | — | — | — | 2 | — | — | — |
| **CO2** | 2 | 3 | 2 | 2 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| **CO3** | 2 | 2 | 2 | **3** | 2 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| **CO4** | — | — | — | — | 2 | 2 | 2 | 2 | **3** | 2 | — | 2 |

**Step 3 — Justify the value-3 correlations (each worth 1.5 marks).**

- **(CO1, PO3) = 3 — Substantial:** The verb *Design* explicitly requires the student to *create* a vulcanization protocol, which is a direct **Design/Development of Solutions** activity. Therefore, the correlation is **Substantial (3)**.
- **(CO2, PO2) = 3 — Substantial:** The verb *Evaluate* in the context of *swelling test analysis* demands deep **Problem Analysis** (interpreting Flory-Rehner equations, identifying experimental error sources) — thus **Substantial (3)**.
- **(CO3, PO4) = 3 — Substantial:** The verb *Analyze* in the context of *tensile/hardness testing* requires the student to **Conduct Investigations of Complex Problems** (statistical analysis, ASTM standard compliance) — **Substantial (3)**.
- **(CO4, PO9) = 3 — Substantial:** The verb *Define* coupled with *"team-based demonstration"* explicitly addresses **PO9 (Individual and Team Work)** — **Substantial (3)**.

> **Total = 7 marks** (2 marks for CO design + 2 marks for matrix construction + 3 marks for justifications of value-3 cells)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in CO/CO-PO Questions:**
> 1. **Using non-approved verbs:** "Understand", "Learn", "Know", "Be familiar with" are **NOT** in the KTU approved Bloom's verb list. Using them will fetch **ZERO marks** for the action-verb component.
> 2. **Confusing K1 with K2:** *Define* is K1, *Describe* is K2, *Explain* is K2, *Illustrate* is K2. Do NOT assign K1 to a verb that involves explanation.
> 3. **Miscounting the "—" dash:** Some students treat the dash as a value of 1 instead of zero. **The dash indicates NO correlation (value 0) and is excluded from the denominator in some averaging conventions, but KTU 2024 standard includes it as 0 in the $n$ count.**
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the cognitive operation:** Justifying a CO is NOT only about naming the verb — the examiner expects a 1–2 line description of the **cognitive operation** the verb triggers.
> 5. **Mapping all COs to all POs:** KTU 2024 explicitly discourages "blanket mapping" where every CO gets a 1 or 2 with every PO. **Selective, justified mapping** (as in the given table) is the correct approach.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam!):**

- [x] A **Course Outcome (CO)** is a measurable statement with an action verb, learning content, and application context.
- [x] **Bloom's Knowledge Levels (K1–K6)** are: Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate → Create.
- [x] **K1 (Define/List/Recall) → K2 (Illustrate/Explain) → K3 (Execute/Implement) → K4 (Analyze/Compare) → K5 (Determine/Assess) → K6 (Develop/Design).**
- [x] The given module's COs map to **K1, K2, K4, K5, and K6** — a **vertically integrated** KTU 2024 pattern.
- [x] **CO-PO correlation values:** **1 = Slight**, **2 = Moderate**, **3 = Substantial**, **— = No correlation (0)**.
- [x] The **strongest correlation in the given table** is **(CO4, PO3) = 3**, justified by the *design mapping* nature of illustrating latex properties with manufacturing methods.
- [x] The **average mapping for PO1** is **1.6** (strongest), and for **PO12** is **0.4** (weakest).
- [x] KTU 2024 mandates **4–6 COs per experiment module**, with at least one K1 and one higher-order (K4–K6) CO.
- [x] **PO12 (Lifelong Learning)** is the most frequently under-mapped PO; departments often compensate via reflective viva or report-writing components.
- [x] The verbs *Understand, Learn, Know, Familiar* are **BANNED** in KTU 2024 CO statements.
- [x] The CO-PO mapping matrix is the **primary NBA accreditation evidence** — incorrect mapping can derail the entire program's accreditation cycle.
- [x] Always justify a CO by stating the **verb → cognitive operation → KL** triplet in your exam answer.
- [x] **"Develop" = K6, "Determine" = K5, "Analyze" = K4, "Illustrate" = K2, "Define" = K1** — these are the five verbs of the current module and must be memorized verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 2: Moderate (Medium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - Polymer Chemistry & Technology Lab / Polymer Characterization Lab
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 2: Moderate (Medium Molecular Weight Polymer) — Determination of Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight (M̄v) by Capillary Viscometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

**Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight (M̄v)** of a polymer is defined as the molecular weight of a hypothetical monodisperse polymer whose solution, in a given solvent at a specified temperature, has the same **intrinsic viscosity [η]** as the polydisperse polymer sample under identical conditions. It lies in the range:

$$
M_n \;<\; \bar{M}_v \;<\; \bar{M}_w
$$

where **M_n** is the number-average molecular weight and **M̄_w** is the weight-average molecular weight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Boilerplate):** *The viscosity-average molecular weight is the molecular weight determined from the measurement of the intrinsic viscosity of a polymer solution using the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (MHS) empirical equation.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Density" Intuition

Imagine a **city road network**. The number of vehicles is huge and varied — small two-wheelers, cars, trucks, buses. If you measure the **average "slowness"** that traffic contributes to a road, **heavy vehicles (high M) contribute more to slowing down traffic than light vehicles (low M)**.

- **Relative viscosity (η_rel)** → how "slow" the traffic becomes when cars are added (ratio to empty road).
- **Specific viscosity (η_sp)** → the *extra* slowness due to the cars.
- **Reduced viscosity (η_red)** → extra slowness per car, at a particular car-density.
- **Inherent viscosity (η_inh)** → how slowly a *single car* (in the limit of zero density) moves in the crowd.
- **Intrinsic viscosity [η]** → the slowness when traffic is *infinitely dilute* — the **pure, isolated contribution** of the polymer chain to flow resistance.
- **M̄_v** → the "effective size" of the average car, weighted by how much it slows traffic (i.e., weighted by mass).

Just as traffic flow resistance is dominated by large trucks, **intrinsic viscosity is dominated by the heaviest chains in the distribution**, so **M̄_v is closer to M̄_w than to M_n.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bold Per Protocol)

- **Solvent viscosity (η₀)** — viscosity of pure solvent, in **Pa·s** or **cP (centipoise)**, where **1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s**.
- **Concentration (c)** — in **g/dL** (grams per decilitre) for polymer solutions; this is the *KTU-preferred unit* for the MHS equation.
- **Intrinsic viscosity [η]** — in **dL/g** (numerically equal to the y-intercept of the Huggins/Kraemer plot).
- **Mark–Houwink constants K and a** — dimensionless-specific for a polymer–solvent–temperature triplet (tabulated).
- **Temperature** — must be stated (commonly **25 °C** or **30 °C**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why c in g/dL and not kg/m³?** Because the MHS constants K are tabulated in dL/g units in classical polymer literature (Brandrup, Immergut, Grulke). Using SI units without converting K causes answers off by a factor of **100⁰ᐧ⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰⁰** — a classic KTU board answer error.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Experiment

The experiment follows a tightly defined logical pipeline. Each step is **non-skippable** in KTU valuation.

**Step 1 — Prepare stock solution.**
A known mass **m (g)** of dry polymer is dissolved in a known volume **V (mL)** of solvent to yield concentration **c₀ = m/V (g/dL)**.

**Step 2 — Calibrate the viscometer.**
Pure solvent is drawn into the Ostwald / Ubbelohde capillary. Efflux time **t₀ (s)** is measured ≥ 3 times; mean is taken. This gives the baseline viscosity ratio.

**Step 3 — Dilute serially.**
Typically **5 dilutions** are prepared: c₁, c₂, c₃, c₄, c₅, each ≈ ½, ⅓, ¼, ⅕, ⅙ of c₀. Efflux time **tᵢ** is measured for each.

**Step 4 — Compute viscosities.**

For each concentration:

$$
\eta_{rel,i} = \frac{\eta_i}{\eta_0} \approx \frac{t_i}{t_0} \quad \text{(since same density, same capillary)}
$$

$$
\eta_{sp,i} = \eta_{rel,i} - 1
$$

$$
\eta_{red,i} = \frac{\eta_{sp,i}}{c_i} \quad \text{(dL/g)}
$$

$$
\eta_{inh,i} = \frac{\ln(\eta_{rel,i})}{c_i} \quad \text{(dL/g)}
$$

**Step 5 — Extrapolate to c → 0.**
Plot **η_red vs c** (Huggins plot) and **η_inh vs c** (Kraemer plot). Both lines should be **straight**, and:

$$
[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \eta_{red} = \lim_{c \to 0} \eta_{inh}
$$

At c = 0, Huggins and Kraemer lines give a **common intercept [η]**. A **self-consistency check** uses:

$$
k_H + k_K = \frac{1}{2} \quad \text{(ideal: } k_H \approx 0.3\text{–}0.4, \; k_K \approx -0.1\text{ to }0.1)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the MHS equation.**

$$
[\eta] = K \, \bar{M}_v^{\,a}
$$

Rearranged:

$$
\bar{M}_v = \left( \frac{[\eta]}{K} \right)^{1/a}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this experiment called "Medium Molecular Weight" categorization?**
> Because for the **K values tabulated in the 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵ dL/g range** and **a ≈ 0.5–0.8** (good solvent, flexible coil), the resulting M̄v lies in the **30 000 – 300 000 g/mol** band — the *medium* range. Low-Mv samples give efflux times barely above t₀ (poor signal), and high-Mv samples cause non-Newtonian shear-thinning in capillaries (systematic error).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Serial dilution** (not separate weighings) is used to keep total polymer mass constant and avoid concentration errors.
- **Same viscometer for t₀ and tᵢ** cancels the Hagen–Poiseuille geometric constant K_cap = π r⁴ t / (8 L V).
- **Both Huggins and Kraemer plots** are mandatory: their convergence at c = 0 verifies the *linear* viscosity-concentration regime, which is the *assumption underlying the MHS equation.*

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (KTU) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relative viscosity | η_rel = t / t₀ | dimensionless | t = efflux time of solution |
| 2 | Specific viscosity | η_sp = η_rel − 1 | dimensionless | excess viscosity |
| 3 | Reduced viscosity | η_red = η_sp / c | dL/g | Huggins y-axis |
| 4 | Inherent viscosity | η_inh = (ln η_rel) / c | dL/g | Kraemer y-axis |
| 5 | Huggins equation | η_red = [η] + k_H[η]² c | dL/g | linear, slope = k_H[η]² |
| 6 | Kraemer equation | η_inh = [η] + k_K[η]² c | dL/g | linear, slope = k_K[η]² |
| 7 | MHS equation | [η] = K M̄vᵃ | dL/g | K in dL/g, a dimensionless |
| 8 | M̄v calculation | M̄v = ([η]/K)^(1/a) | g/mol | **mandatory rearrange** |
| 9 | Schulz–Blaschke | η_sp/c = [η] (1 + k_B η_sp) | dL/g | single-point alternative |
| 10 | Stock concentration | c₀ = m / V | g/dL | m in g, V in mL → ÷1000 |
| 11 | Diluted concentration | cᵢ = c₀ × (V_pipette / V_total) | g/dL | use volumetric ratio |
| 12 | Hagen–Poiseuille | η = (π r⁴ ΔP t) / (8 L V) | Pa·s | geometry cancels in ratio |
| 13 | Self-consistency | k_H + k_K ≈ 0.5 | — | must be checked |
| 14 | Temperature | 25 ± 0.1 °C or 30 ± 0.1 °C | °C | thermal bath mandatory |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** The K in MHS has a **dimensionality of dL/g**, NOT mL/g. Students who write **K = 0.0001 dL/g** as **K = 0.1 mL/g** introduce a **1000× error**. Always cross-check Brandrup-Immergut table units.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer process design**: Injection moulding, blow-moulding, fibre spinning all require M̄v within ±10 % of specified values.
- **Quality control in PET, PVC, Nylon-6,6** manufacture.
- **Coatings industry**: Ink viscosity at high shear depends directly on M̄v.
- **Biomedical polymers**: Drug-release kinetics of hydrogels depend on M̄v of the matrix polymer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Hagen–Poiseuille to η_rel = t / t₀

For a capillary viscometer, Poiseuille's law gives volumetric flow rate:

$$
Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}
$$

Solving for viscosity:

$$
\eta = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P \, t}{8 L V}
$$

For pure solvent (subscript 0) and solution (subscript i), at the **same hydrostatic head ΔP** and using the **same viscometer** (same r, L, V):

$$
\frac{\eta_i}{\eta_0} = \frac{t_i}{t_0}
$$

Because the density correction (η = ρ × kinematic) is < 1 % for dilute polymer solutions, we set:

$$
\eta_{rel,i} = \frac{t_i}{t_0}
$$

This is the **operational equation** KTU examiners test in Part A.

## 3.2 Derivation: Huggins Equation from Einstein's Viscosity Law

Einstein's rigid-sphere result:

$$
\eta_{rel} = 1 + 2.5 \phi \quad \text{(φ = volume fraction of solute)}
$$

For a real polymer coil, hydrodynamic interactions, coil overlap, and interpenetration give an expansion in concentration c (proportional to φ for dilute solutions):

$$
\eta_{sp} = 2.5 \phi + k_1 \phi^2 + k_2 \phi^3 + \dots
$$

Dividing by c (proportional to φ) and defining [η] as the **intrinsic contribution per unit mass**:

$$
\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c + \dots
$$

Truncating at the second-order term gives the **Huggins equation**:

$$
\eta_{red} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c
$$

Similarly, expanding ln(η_rel) = ln(1 + η_sp) ≈ η_sp − η_sp²/2 + ... leads to the **Kraemer equation**:

$$
\eta_{inh} = [\eta] + k_K [\eta]^2 c
$$

Adding the two slopes:

$$
k_H [\eta]^2 + k_K [\eta]^2 = \frac{1}{2}[\eta]^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_H + k_K \approx \frac{1}{2}
$$

This is the **Kraemer self-consistency rule**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Viscosity-Average Molecular Weight (M̄v)

The Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (MHS) equation is empirical:

$$
[\eta] = K M^a
$$

For a polydisperse polymer, [η] is the **molecular-weight-weighted sum** of individual intrinsic viscosities. Since [η]_i = K Mᵢᵃ:

$$
[\eta]_{polymer} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i \, [\eta]_i}{\sum_i N_i M_i} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i \cdot K M_i^a}{\sum_i N_i M_i} = K \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i}
$$

By definition, **M̄v** is the molecular weight that satisfies [η] = K M̄vᵃ, so:

$$
\bar{M}_v = \left( \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^{1+a}}{\sum_i N_i M_i} \right)^{1/a}
$$

This is why M̄v is **mass-averaged** (closer to M̄w), not number-averaged.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Given:** A medium-Mv polymer sample.
- Solvent efflux time **t₀ = 102.4 s**
- Stock: **m = 0.50 g** polymer dissolved in **V = 100 mL** solvent → **c₀ = 0.5 g/dL**
- Solvent for dilution = 10 mL added each step
- 5 dilutions prepared by adding 5, 5, 5, 5, 5 mL solvent to 10 mL aliquots of progressively diluted solution (cumulative dilution pattern below).
- MHS constants: **K = 0.000476 dL/g, a = 0.79** (typical PVC/THF system, 25 °C).

| Dilution i | cᵢ (g/dL) | tᵢ (s) | η_rel = tᵢ/t₀ | η_sp | η_red = η_sp/cᵢ | η_inh = ln(η_rel)/cᵢ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.2500 | 218.7 | 2.1357 | 1.1357 | 4.543 | 3.030 |
| 2 | 0.1667 | 173.4 | 1.6934 | 0.6934 | 4.160 | 3.141 |
| 3 | 0.1250 | 150.2 | 1.4668 | 0.4668 | 3.734 | 3.150 |
| 4 | 0.1000 | 137.6 | 1.3438 | 0.3438 | 3.438 | 3.135 |
| 5 | 0.0833 | 129.4 | 1.2637 | 0.2637 | 3.165 | 3.110 |

**Huggins linear regression** (η_red vs c):

$$
\eta_{red} = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 \, c
$$

Using least-squares on the 5 data points:

- **Intercept [η] ≈ 2.91 dL/g**
- **Slope k_H[η]² ≈ 6.50** → k_H ≈ 6.50 / (2.91)² ≈ 0.767
- **R² = 0.999**

**Kraemer linear regression** (η_inh vs c):

- **Intercept [η] ≈ 3.16 dL/g** (slight discrepancy indicates curvature)

> Take the **mean of Huggins and Kraemer intercepts**: **[η] ≈ 3.04 dL/g** (this is the KTU standard practice when agreement is within 5 %).

**Apply MHS equation:**

$$
\bar{M}_v = \left( \frac{[\eta]}{K} \right)^{1/a} = \left( \frac{3.04}{0.000476} \right)^{1/0.79}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
\frac{3.04}{0.000476} = 6386.55
$$

$$
\ln(6386.55) = 8.761
$$

$$
\frac{8.761}{0.79} = 11.090
$$

$$
\bar{M}_v = e^{11.090} = 65{,}900 \;\text{g/mol}
$$

> **Result: M̄v ≈ 6.6 × 10⁴ g/mol**, which falls squarely in the **medium molecular weight** band (3 × 10⁴ – 3 × 10⁵), consistent with the experiment title.

## 3.5 Sensitivity / Error Analysis Matrix (KTU Examiner's Favorite)

| Error Source | Magnitude | Effect on [η] | Effect on M̄v | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ±0.1 °C bath fluctuation | ±0.4 % in η | ±0.4 % | (0.4/a) % | Thermostat ±0.05 °C |
| ±0.5 s in t₀ timing | ±0.5 % | ±0.5 % | (0.5/a) % | Use digital stopwatch, ≥5 trials |
| Concentration prep error ±2 % | ±2 % | ±2 % | (2/a) % | Volumetric flasks A-grade |
| Capillary non-Newtonian shear | 1–5 % | biased high | biased low | Use Ubbelohde (suspended level) |
| Polymer adsorption on wall | drift | lowers [η] | lowers M̄v | Pre-rinse with solution |

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Viscometry Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Sample] --> B[Drying in Vacuum Oven 60 C 24 h]
    B --> C[Weigh m grams on Analytical Balance]
    C --> D[Dissolve in Volumetric Flask V mL]
    D --> E[Stock Solution c0 g per dL]
    E --> F[Pipette 10 mL into Viscometer]
    F --> G[Thermostat Bath 25 C wait 10 min]
    G --> H[Measure Efflux Time t1]
    H --> I[Add 5 mL Solvent In-situ]
    I --> J[Mix and Degass]
    J --> K[Measure t2 t3 t4 t5 t6]
    K --> L[Compute eta rel eta sp eta red eta inh]
    L --> M[Huggins Plot eta red vs c]
    L --> N[Kraemer Plot eta inh vs c]
    M --> O[Linear Regression]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Common Intercept eta dL per g]
    P --> Q{Intercept Agreement within 5 percent}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Adopt Mean eta]
    Q -- No --> S[Check kH plus kK equals 0.5]
    S --> T[Investigate Non Newtonian Effect]
    T --> U[Reject Outlier or Repeat]
    R --> V[Apply MHS Equation]
    U --> V
    V --> W[Compute Mv g per mol]
    W --> X[Report Category Medium MW Band]
```

## 4.2 Viscometer Schematic — Ubbelohde Suspended-Level

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VISC[Ubbelohde Viscometer]
        V1[Reservoir Bulb 10 mL] --> V2[Capillary r approx 0.5 mm]
        V2 --> V3[Measuring Bulb 5 mL calibrated]
        V3 --> V4[Lower Bulb suspended level]
        V4 --> V5[Side Arm for Atmospheric Pressure equilibration]
    end
    THERM[(Thermostat Bath 25.0 C plus or minus 0.05)] -.Surrounds.-> V3
    STOP[Digital Stopwatch plus or minus 0.01 s] -.Measures.-> V3
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Huggins–Kraemer Extrapolation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Y1[eta red axis dL per g] --> P1[Huggins Line slope kH eta squared]
    Y2[eta inh axis dL per g] --> P2[Kraemer Line slope kK eta squared]
    X1[Concentration c g per dL] --> P1
    X1 --> P2
    P1 --> INTR[Common Y intercept at c equals 0]
    P2 --> INTR
    INTR --> LAB[Label eta intrinsic viscosity in dL per g]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree for "Medium MW" Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Compute Mv] --> Q{Mv in dL per g}
    Q -- less than 30000 --> LOW[Low Molecular Weight Plasticizer Like]
    Q -- 30000 to 300000 --> MED[Medium Molecular Weight Engineering Plastic Band]
    Q -- greater than 300000 --> HIGH[High Molecular Weight UHMW Like]
    MED --> APP[Typical Applications PET PVC Nylon 6 6 Polystyrene]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define intrinsic viscosity. How is it determined experimentally for a polymer solution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Intrinsic viscosity **[η]** is defined as the **limiting value of the reduced viscosity (η_sp/c) or the inherent viscosity (ln η_rel / c) as the concentration c approaches zero**. Mathematically:

$$
[\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0} \frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = \lim_{c \to 0} \frac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c}
$$

It is determined experimentally by:
1. Measuring efflux times **t₀** (pure solvent) and **tᵢ** (solutions of varying concentration cᵢ) in a capillary viscometer at constant temperature.
2. Computing **η_red = (tᵢ/t₀ − 1)/cᵢ** and **η_inh = ln(tᵢ/t₀)/cᵢ**.
3. Plotting **η_red vs c** (Huggins) and **η_inh vs c** (Kraemer); both extrapolate linearly to the common y-intercept = **[η]**, in **dL/g**. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation? Mention its significance in polymer characterization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The MHS equation relates the intrinsic viscosity to the viscosity-average molecular weight:

$$
[\eta] = K \bar{M}_v^{\,a}
$$

where **K (dL/g)** and **a (dimensionless)** are empirical constants for a given **polymer–solvent–temperature triplet**.

**Significance:**
- Provides a **simple, fast, and inexpensive method** to estimate **M̄v** from a single viscosity measurement.
- Useful for **quality control** in industrial polymer production.
- Validates polymerization reactions by tracking M̄v vs. conversion. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Module 2, Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **determination of viscosity-average molecular weight (M̄v) of a medium molecular weight polymer** by capillary viscometry. List the formulae used and explain the **Huggins and Kraemer extrapolation procedure**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The efflux times of a polymer solution at five different concentrations in an Ostwald viscometer are tabulated below. Solvent efflux time **t₀ = 98.6 s**. MHS constants: **K = 1.4 × 10⁻⁴ dL/g, a = 0.71** (polymer–solvent system at 25 °C).

| cᵢ (g/dL) | 0.40 | 0.32 | 0.26 | 0.22 | 0.18 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| tᵢ (s) | 178.3 | 158.0 | 144.6 | 137.0 | 130.8 |

Calculate **[η]** and **M̄v**. Comment on whether the polymer lies in the **medium molecular weight** category. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks

1. **Apparatus** (1.5 marks): Ostwald or Ubbelohde viscometer, constant-temperature bath (±0.05 °C), 10 mL pipette, 5 mL pipette, stopwatch (±0.01 s), volumetric flask.
2. **Procedure** (2 marks):
   - Prepare stock solution c₀ = m/V.
   - Pipette 10 mL into viscometer; equilibrate 10 min; record t₁.
   - Add 5 mL solvent in-situ → c₂ = (10×c₀)/15; record t₂.
   - Repeat for c₃, c₄, c₅.
3. **Calculations** (1.5 marks): Apply formulae from §2.3 (rows 1–4).
4. **Extrapolation** (2 marks):
   - Plot η_red vs c → Huggins; intercept = [η].
   - Plot η_inh vs c → Kraemer; intercept = [η].
   - Verify k_H + k_K ≈ 0.5.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute η_rel, η_sp, η_red, η_inh** (2 marks):

| cᵢ | tᵢ | η_rel = tᵢ/98.6 | η_sp | η_red | η_inh |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.40 | 178.3 | 1.808 | 0.808 | 2.020 | 1.481 |
| 0.32 | 158.0 | 1.602 | 0.602 | 1.881 | 1.512 |
| 0.26 | 144.6 | 1.467 | 0.467 | 1.796 | 1.508 |
| 0.22 | 137.0 | 1.389 | 0.389 | 1.768 | 1.485 |
| 0.18 | 130.8 | 1.327 | 0.327 | 1.817 | 1.504 |

**Step 2 — Huggins linear regression** (1.5 marks):

$$
\eta_{red} = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 c
$$

Fitting yields: **[η] ≈ 1.46 dL/g**, slope ≈ 1.40, hence k_H ≈ 0.66.

**Step 3 — Kraemer regression** (0.5 marks): gives [η] ≈ 1.51 dL/g.

**Step 4 — Mean [η]** (0.5 marks): [η] ≈ (1.46 + 1.51)/2 = **1.485 dL/g**.

**Step 5 — Apply MHS** (2 marks):

$$
\bar{M}_v = \left( \frac{1.485}{1.4 \times 10^{-4}} \right)^{1/0.71} = (10607)^{1.408}
$$

$$
\ln(10607) = 9.269; \quad \frac{9.269}{0.71} = 13.055; \quad \bar{M}_v = e^{13.055} \approx 4.66 \times 10^5
$$

**Step 6 — Classification** (0.5 marks): **M̄v ≈ 4.66 × 10⁵ g/mol**, which lies in the **medium-to-high boundary range**; with the K and a given, it is **medium molecular weight (engineering plastic grade)**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Module 2, Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **Huggins equation** starting from Einstein's viscosity relation for rigid spheres. Show that the **Kraemer slope constant k_K is related to the Huggins constant k_H by k_H + k_K ≈ 0.5.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting intrinsic viscosity [η]** of a polymer in solution. Why are tabulated **K and a values** valid only for the exact polymer–solvent–temperature triplet specified? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Einstein's relation:**

$$
\eta_{rel} = 1 + 2.5 \phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \eta_{sp} = 2.5 \phi
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Convert to mass concentration:**
Since φ = c/ρ (effective hydrodynamic volume per unit mass ≈ 1/ρ for dilute coils), substitute:

$$
\eta_{sp} = 2.5 c [\eta]_0 + k_1 c^2 + k_2 c^3 + \dots
$$

where [η]₀ is the **first-order intrinsic viscosity**.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Truncate to second order:**

$$
\frac{\eta_{sp}}{c} = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 c
$$

This is the **Huggins equation**.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Kraemer equation:**

$$
\ln \eta_{rel} = \eta_{sp} - \frac{\eta_{sp}^2}{2} + \frac{\eta_{sp}^3}{3} - \dots
$$

$$
\frac{\ln \eta_{rel}}{c} = [\eta] + k_K[\eta]^2 c
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Adding the two:**

Slope sum = (k_H + k_K)[η]². But ln expansion gives coefficient −½[η]² for the η_sp² term, and re-expressed:

$$
k_H + k_K = \frac{1}{2}
$$

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**Factors affecting [η]** (3.5 marks):
1. **Molecular weight** — [η] ∝ M^a (MHS).
2. **Polymer chain flexibility** — Stiffer chains → larger [η].
3. **Solvent quality** — Good solvent → expanded coil → higher [η]; theta solvent → compact coil → lower [η].
4. **Temperature** — Increases [η] in good solvents; θ-temperature gives minimum.
5. **Branching** — Branched polymer has **lower** [η] than linear at the same M.
6. **Shear rate** — At high shear, chains align → [η] decreases (non-Newtonian).

**Why K, a are triplet-specific** (3.5 marks):
- K and a depend on **polymer–solvent interactions** (χ parameter), **temperature**, and **chain stiffness**.
- A change in solvent (e.g., THF → DMF for PVC) alters the coil expansion factor α³, changing both K and a.
- A change in temperature alters free volume and χ, again changing K and a.
- Therefore, K and a must be cited from the **exact same polymer–solvent–temperature** reference (Brandrup-Immergut / Polymer Handbook). Using K, a for a different system can give M̄v errors of **orders of magnitude**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PCPOL607 Experiment 2:**
> 1. **Units of K** — KTU examiners specifically check whether K is in **dL/g**. Writing K in mL/g or cm³/g costs **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Missing t₀ timing** — Failing to report t₀ or using only one t₀ measurement → −1 mark.
> 3. **Single-point intrinsic viscosity** — Using only one concentration and applying the Schulz–Blaschke equation is **not allowed** in KTU Module 2; the experiment **requires** ≥ 5 dilutions and a **Huggins–Kraemer extrapolation**.
> 4. **Ignoring the Kraemer self-consistency check** (k_H + k_K ≈ 0.5) → −1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the temperature** in the report → −0.5 mark.
> 6. **Reporting M̄v with the wrong power of 10** — write **6.6 × 10⁴ g/mol**, not 66000 without scientific notation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **[η]** is a **limiting viscosity number** at **c → 0**, in **dL/g**.
- **η_rel = t / t₀** (Poiseuille ratio).
- **η_sp = η_rel − 1**; **η_red = η_sp / c**; **η_inh = ln η_rel / c**.
- **Huggins plot**: η_red vs c → intercept = [η], slope = k_H[η]².
- **Kraemer plot**: η_inh vs c → intercept = [η], slope = k_K[η]².
- **Self-consistency**: k_H + k_K ≈ 0.5.
- **MHS equation**: [η] = K M̄vᵃ; rearrange to **M̄v = ([η]/K)^(1/a)**.
- **K in dL/g** — verify units before plugging in.
- **Medium MW** band ≈ **3 × 10⁴ – 3 × 10⁵ g/mol**.
- **Temperature** strictly controlled at **25 °C or 30 °C** (whichever is the MHS reference).
- **Ubbelohde viscometer** preferred over Ostwald (suspended level → no kinetic energy correction).
- **M̄v lies between M_n and M̄_w**, closer to M̄_w for broad distributions.
- **Branching and shear degradation** can bias the result toward lower [η].
- **Always plot** both Huggins and Kraemer; report **mean [η]** when intercepts agree within 5 %.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
