# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "aspect ratio" of a fibre in a polymer composite. Why is a high aspect ratio necessary for effective reinforcement?**

**Model Answer:**

The aspect ratio of a fibre is defined as the ratio of its length to its diameter:

$$a_r = \dfrac{L}{d}$$

A high aspect ratio is essential for two reasons:
1. **Stress transfer efficiency:** A longer fibre presents a larger interfacial area for shear stress transfer from the matrix.
2. **Fracture vs. pull-out:** If $L \gg L_c$ (the critical length), the fibre breaks under load rather than pulling out, ensuring full utilization of the fibre's tensile strength.

Typical high-performance composites use $a_r \geq 100$ (e.g., $L = 12$ mm, $d = 10$ µm gives $a_r = 1200$).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between thermosetting and thermoplastic matrices used in fibre-reinforced polymer composites. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Thermosetting Matrix | Thermoplastic Matrix |
|---|---|---|
| Crosslinking | Irreversible 3D network | No crosslinking, linear/branched chains |
| Curing | Heat + catalyst required | Solidifies on cooling |
| Recyclability | Not recyclable | Recyclable and remouldable |
| Mechanical behaviour | Brittle, high modulus | Tough, higher impact resistance |
| Processing | Hand layup, RTM, filament winding | Injection moulding, extrusion |
| Example | Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester, Phenolic | Polypropylene, Nylon, PEEK, PPS |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Rule of Mixtures** for predicting the longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite. State clearly the assumptions used in deriving the expression.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A unidirectional carbon-fibre/epoxy composite has a fibre volume fraction of **0.45**. Given:
- $E_f$ = 230 GPa, $E_m$ = 3.6 GPa
- $\sigma_f$ = 3500 MPa, $\sigma_m$ = 60 MPa
- Fibre diameter $d$ = 7 µm, interfacial shear strength $\tau_i$ = 35 MPa

Calculate:
1. Longitudinal modulus
2. Longitudinal tensile strength
3. Critical fibre length

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Rule of Mixtures Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. Fibres are continuous, uniform, and perfectly aligned parallel to the load.
2. The fibre–matrix interface is perfectly bonded (no slip).
3. Both phases experience the same strain (iso-strain condition).
4. The composite is free of voids and defects.

**Derivation:**

Let the composite have a cross-sectional area $A_c$ comprising fibre area $A_f$ and matrix area $A_m$. When a tensile force $F_c$ is applied:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

Dividing by $A_c$ (and noting $A_f/A_c = V_f$ and $A_m/A_c = V_m$):

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

Since strain is uniform (iso-strain), $\sigma = E \epsilon$:

$$E_c \epsilon = E_f \epsilon V_f + E_m \epsilon V_m$$

$$\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

A schematic of the iso-strain model is shown below (block-level representation):

```
+----------------------+
|        APPLIED        |
|   TENSILE LOAD F_c    |
+----------+-----------+
           |
           v
+----------+----------------------+
|  Matrix Region (V_m)             |
|  +---------------------------+   |
|  |  Fibre Region (V_f)       |   |
|  |  ======================== |   |  ----> epsilon (same)
|  |  ======================== |   |
|  +---------------------------+   |
+----------------------------------+
```

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $V_f = 0.45$, $V_m = 0.55$, $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.6$ GPa, $\sigma_f = 3500$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 60$ MPa, $d = 7$ µm, $\tau_i = 35$ MPa.

**(1) Longitudinal Modulus:**

$$E_{c,L} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$
$$E_{c,L} = (230)(0.45) + (3.6)(0.55)$$
$$E_{c,L} = 103.5 + 1.98 = 105.48 \text{ GPa}$$

**(2) Longitudinal Tensile Strength:**

$$\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$
$$\sigma_{c,L} = (3500)(0.45) + (60)(0.55)$$
$$\sigma_{c,L} = 1575 + 33 = 1608 \text{ MPa}$$

**(3) Critical Fibre Length:**

$$L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i}$$
$$L_c = \dfrac{(3500 \times 10^6) \cdot (7 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \cdot (35 \times 10^6)}$$
$$L_c = \dfrac{24.5}{70} \text{ mm} = 0.35 \text{ mm}$$

For effective reinforcement, design with $L \geq 10 L_c = 3.5$ mm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the **critical fibre length**? Derive the expression for critical fibre length in a fibre-reinforced polymer composite. Explain what happens when (i) $L < L_c$ and (ii) $L \gg L_c$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write short notes on:
1. **Natural fibre reinforced polymer composites (NFRP)** — advantages and limitations.
2. **Fibre orientation effects** on composite strength.
3. **Coupling agents** in glass-fibre composites.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Critical Fibre Length

The **critical fibre length ($L_c$)** is defined as the *minimum fibre length* required for the fibre to develop its full tensile strength under the action of the maximum interfacial shear stress at the fibre–matrix interface.

**Derivation:**

For a fibre of diameter $d$ loaded through interfacial shear, force balance at $L_c$:

$$\sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d L_c$$

$$\boxed{L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**

| Condition | Result | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| $L < L_c$ | Fibre pulls out before breaking | Reinforcement is **inefficient**; composite fails by fibre pull-out; lower strength |
| $L \gg L_c$ (typically $L \geq 10 L_c$) | Fibre breaks (not pulls out) | Reinforcement is **fully effective**; composite achieves design strength |
| $L = L_c$ | Borderline case | Fibre breaks at its midpoint under maximum stress |

#### Part (b) — Short Notes

**(1) Natural Fibre Reinforced Polymers (NFRP):**

*Advantages:*
- Renewable and biodegradable (eco-friendly)
- Low density (1.2–1.5 g/cm³) — lightweight
- Low cost (jute, coir, sisal, flax are agricultural by-products)
- Good acoustic and thermal insulation
- Low wear on processing equipment
- Non-abrasive to moulds

*Limitations:*
- High moisture absorption → swelling and degradation
- Poor fibre–matrix adhesion (polar fibres, non-polar matrices)
- Lower strength compared to synthetic fibres
- Variability in fibre properties (batch-to-batch)
- Limited temperature resistance (max ~200°C)

**Examples:** Jute–epoxy automotive door panels, coir–polyester roofing sheets, flax–PP interior trims in cars.

**(2) Fibre Orientation Effects:**

| Orientation | Strength | Modulus | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unidirectional (aligned, continuous) | Maximum (along fibre) | Maximum (along fibre) | Aerospace primary structures |
| Bidirectional (woven fabric, 0°/90°) | High in two directions | High in two directions | Marine panels, wind blades |
| Random (mat, chopped strand) | Moderate, isotropic | Moderate, isotropic | Automotive bodies, baths |
| Discontinuous, aligned | Intermediate | Intermediate | Extruded profiles, pellets |

A unidirectional composite may have strength 10× higher along the fibre than transverse. Random orientation reduces this anisotropy but lowers peak performance.

**(3) Coupling Agents in Glass-Fibre Composites:**

Coupling agents are bifunctional molecules that chemically bond both the fibre surface and the polymer matrix, dramatically improving interfacial adhesion.

*Most common: **Silane coupling agents** (e.g., γ-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, γ-methacryloxypropyltrimethoxysilane).*

**Mechanism:**

```
Glass Fibre Surface          Polymer Matrix
        |                          |
        Si-OH   +   R'-Si(OR)3  →  Si-O-Si-R'   +   R'-bonds-to-polymer
        |                          |
   (hydrophilic)              (hydrophobic)
```

**Functions:**
- Bridge the hydrophilic glass surface and hydrophobic polymer
- Improve wet strength retention (critical for marine applications)
- Reduce moisture-induced interfacial failure
- Enhance composite fatigue life by 200–400%

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical Fibre Length:**
> A very common error in KTU exams is to use the **wrong diameter units** in the critical length calculation. Fibre diameter is given in **micrometres (µm) = $10^{-6}$ m**, while $\sigma_f$ and $\tau_i$ are in **MPa = $10^6$ Pa**. The $10^6$ and $10^{-6}$ **cancel exactly**, so the answer comes out in **metres** — but students often forget to convert. **Always verify your units end-to-end before finalizing the numerical answer.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall 2 — Rule of Mixtures Direction:**
> The longitudinal (iso-strain) Rule of Mixtures ($E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$) gives the **upper bound** and applies **only along the fibre direction**. The transverse (iso-stress) inverse relation $1/E_c^{\perp} = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ applies **perpendicular** to the fibre. Mixing them up is a guaranteed loss of 2–3 marks in Part B problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Composite = Polymer Matrix + Fibres + Interface** — three phases, with the interface often called the "third phase."
- **Fibre is the load carrier; matrix is the binder and protector.**
- **Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal):** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — upper bound, valid for aligned continuous fibres.
- **Rule of Mixtures (transverse):** $1/E_c^{\perp} = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ — lower bound, valid perpendicular to fibres.
- **Strength Rule of Mixtures:** $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ — valid for brittle-matrix composites.
- **Critical Fibre Length:** $L_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ — minimum length for full reinforcement.
- **Design rule for short fibres:** $L \geq 10 L_c$ for effective reinforcement.
- **Aspect ratio** $a_r = L/d$ must be high (typically $\geq 100$) for efficient load transfer.
- **Fibre orientation** controls anisotropy: unidirectional → maximum strength, random → isotropic but lower.
- **Silane coupling agents** bridge glass and polymer; improve wet strength and fatigue life.
- **Natural fibres (jute, coir, flax)** = low cost, eco-friendly, but moisture-sensitive and low strength.
- **Synthetic fibres:** E-glass (cheap, general purpose), Carbon (highest stiffness), Aramid (toughest).
- **Fibre volume fraction** typically 0.3–0.6 in commercial composites; $V_f + V_m = 1$.
- **Composite density** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ — explains why FRPs are lighter than metals.
- **Specific strength** (strength/density) and **specific modulus** (modulus/density) are the key merit indices for aerospace FRP selection.
- **Common KTU exam questions:** (i) derive Rule of Mixtures, (ii) calculate $E_c$ and $\sigma_c$ given $V_f$, (iii) find $L_c$, (iv) compare thermoset vs thermoplastic matrices, (v) short notes on NFRP/coupling agents/orientation.
- **Always double-check unit consistency** — most KTU numericals test this implicitly.
- **Boeing 787 = ~50% composite by weight** — real-world validation of FRP importance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: fibre manufacturing industries in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** fibre manufacturing industries in India # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fibre Manufacturing Industries** in India encompass the entire industrial ecosystem engaged in the production, processing, and commercialization of textile fibres—both natural and man-made (synthetic and regenerated). In the context of polymer science (PCPOL607), the scope is primarily directed toward **man-made fibres (MMF)**, which include regenerated cellulosic fibres (rayon, acetate) and synthetic polymeric fibres (polyester, nylon, polypropylene, acrylic), as well as the conversion of natural polymers (cellulose, protein, lignocellulose) into industrial fibre products. According to the **Ministry of Textiles, Government of India**, the Indian fibre industry is segmented into: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Official Classification of Indian Fibre Industry** > 1. **Natural Fibres** – Cotton, Jute, Silk, Wool, Coir, Linen, Hemp > 2. **Man-Made Fibres (MMF)** – Viscose Rayon, Acetate, Lyocell, Modal > 3. **Synthetic Fibres** – Polyester, Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, Polypropylene, Acrylic > 4. **Speciality Fibres** – Aramid, Carbon Fibre, Glass Fibre, UHMWPE The industry falls under the administrative purview of the **Ministry of Textiles** and is regulated by bodies such as the **Textile Commissioner’s Office (TCO)**, **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, and industry associations like the **Synthetic & Rayon Textiles Export Promotion Council (SRTEPC)** and the **Indian Cotton Mills’ Federation (ICMF)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of India's fibre industry as a **giant textile kitchen**: - **Natural fibres** are like vegetables grown in a garden—they come from nature (plants/animals) and require harvesting, cleaning, and minimal chemical processing before they can be woven into fabric. - **Man-made/synthetic fibres** are like processed food—raw chemicals (petroleum, cellulose pulp) are extruded through a "spinneret" (a kitchen "nozzle" with tiny holes) to spin out long continuous filaments, which are then drawn (stretched like taffy) to align the polymer chains and give strength. > [!NOTE] > **Simple Takeaway for Students** > India is the **world's second-largest producer of textile fibres** after China and the **largest producer of cotton and jute globally**. The MMF sector contributes roughly **~42%** to the total fibre consumption in India, while cotton dominates the natural fibre share with ~55%. The remaining ~3% is shared by silk, wool, and other minor fibres. ## 1.3 Key Industrial Metrics (Highlighted Constants) - **Installed capacity of polyester fibre (2023–24):** ~**6.5 million tonnes per annum (MTPA)** - **Installed capacity of viscose staple fibre (VSF):** ~**0.9 MTPA** - **Installed capacity of acrylic fibre:** ~**0.13 MTPA** - **Cotton production (2023–24, 4th advance estimate):** ~**32.5 million bales** (170 kg each) - **Jute production:** ~**9.5 million bales** (180 kg each) - **Silk production:** ~**38,000 MT** - **Total textile & apparel export value (FY 2023–24):** ~**USD 36 billion** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Matters in PCPOL607** > Polymer scientists study fibre manufacturing because every synthetic fibre is a polymer extrusion problem (melt spinning, dry spinning, wet spinning), and every regenerated fibre is a polymer dissolution/precipitation problem. The Indian industry is a living laboratory for these unit operations. --- # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Indian Fibre Industry The Indian fibre manufacturing sector is a **vertically integrated value chain** with the following structure: ### A. Natural Fibre Sector | Fibre | Botanical/Animal Source | Major Indian Producing States | Industry Status | |---|---|---|---| | **Cotton** | Gossypium (Malvaceae) | Gujarat, Maharashtra, Telangana, AP | Largest MMF-replacing natural fibre; India = 2nd largest producer globally | | **Jute** | Corchorus (Tiliaceae) | West Bengal, Bihar, Assam, Odisha | India = 1st largest producer (~60% of world production) | | **Silk** | Bombyx mori (Mulberry), Tasar, Eri, Muga | Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, AP, Assam, Jharkhand | India = 2nd largest producer (after China) | | **Wool** | Sheep (Ovis aries) | Rajasthan, J&K, Himachal Pradesh, Gujarat | India is a marginal producer; mostly coarse/carpet grade | | **Coir** | Coconut husk (Cocos nucifera) | Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, AP | India = 1st global producer (~80% of world share) | ### B. Man-Made Fibre (MMF) Sector – Polymer-Science View | Fibre | Polymer Used | Spinning Process | Key Indian Manufacturers | |---|---|---|---| | **Viscose Rayon (VSF)** | Cellulose xanthate (regen. cellulose) | Wet Spinning | **Grasim Industries (Aditya Birla)**, **Century Textiles** | | **Modal / Lyocell** | Regenerated cellulose (NMMO process) | Dry-jet Wet Spinning | **Birla Cellulose** (limited) | | **Acetate** | Cellulose acetate (DS ~2.4) | Dry Spinning | **Bongaigaon Refinery (Ester Plant)** | | **Polyester (PET)** | Poly(ethylene terephthalate) | Melt Spinning | **Reliance Industries**, **Indorama Ventures (Indo Rama)**, **Sanmar**, **JBF Industries** | | **Nylon 6** | Polycaprolactam (PCL) | Melt Spinning | **Gujarat State Fertilizers (GSFC)**, **Century Enka**, **SRF Limited** | | **Nylon 6,6** | Poly(hexamethylene adipamide) | Melt Spinning | **Century Enka**, **SRF Limited** (limited) | | **Acrylic** | Polyacrylonitrile (PAN, >85% AN) | Wet/Dry Spinning | **Indian Acrylics (RIL-IACL)**, **Pasupati Acrylon** | | **Polypropylene (PP)** | Isotactic polypropylene | Melt Spinning (Slit/film/fibrillated) | **Reliance**, **Indian Oil**, **Borealis (Borouge JV)** | ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following equations govern the **process parameters** of fibre spinning that every PCPOL607 student should be able to recall and apply. | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Degree of Polymerisation (DP)** | $DP = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | dimensionless | $M_0$ = monomer mol. wt. | | **Intrinsic Viscosity (Mark–Houwink)** | $[\eta] = K M_v^{\alpha}$ | dL/g | Used for PET (in phenol/TCE) and cellulose (in cuen) | | **Melt Viscosity (Arrhenius)** | $\eta = A \exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Pa·s | Melt spinning temperature dependence | | **Tensile Strength (Tenacity)** | $T = \dfrac{F}{t}$ | N/tex or g/denier | Fibre property measurement | | **Draw Ratio** | $DR = \dfrac{v_{take-up}}{v_{feed}}$ | dimensionless | Orientation of polymer chains | | **Birefringence (Orientation indicator)** | $\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$ | dimensionless | Crystalline orientation | | **Spinline Tension** | $F = \eta_{ext} \cdot v_{spin}$ | dynes | Force balance on filament | | **Spin Draw Ratio for Crystallinity** | $\% C = f(DR) \rightarrow$ empirical | % | Cold/warm drawing | | **Wet Spinning Coagulation Flux** | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | mol/(m²·s) | Fick's first law for VSF | | **Throughput in Spinneret** | $Q = n \cdot \rho \cdot A_{hole} \cdot v$ | kg/h | Production rate | > [!WARNING] > **Note on Tables:** Absolute value notation in rows is written as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do NOT use `|x|` inside table cells. ## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility - **PET fibres** form the backbone of the **$150 billion** Indian textile industry and dominate in sarees, dress material, and technical textiles. - **Nylon 6** is critical for **tyrecord, conveyor belts, and hosiery**. - **Viscose staple fibre (VSF)** is the most sustainable "cellulose-based" fibre and forms the basis of the **"Fashion for Good"** sustainability push. - **Aramid and Carbon fibres** are now being manufactured domestically under the **National Technical Textiles Mission (NTTM)** for defence, aerospace, and automotive applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Government Push – PM MITRA Scheme** > The **PM MITRA (Mega Integrated Textile Region and Apparel) Parks Scheme** (2021) is a flagship initiative to create 7 mega textile parks with an outlay of **₹4,445 crore**, focusing on integrated fibre-to-garment manufacturing. The PLI (Production-Linked Incentive) scheme for MMF and technical textiles has an outlay of **₹10,683 crore**. --- # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Analytical Framework – Manufacturing Pathways of Major Fibres Since the topic is industrial rather than purely numerical, the "derivations" here are **process logic flows** that walk through the chemistry and engineering of fibre formation, which is directly testable in KTU exams. ### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step: Viscose Rayon (VSF) Manufacturing The viscose process is the **workhorse** of regenerated cellulose fibre production in India (Grasim Industries alone produces ~580,000 TPA of VSF). **Step 1: Cellulose Sourcing** Starting material is **dissolving-grade wood pulp** (α-cellulose content ≥ 90%) or cotton linter pulp. **Step 2: Mercerisation** Cellulose sheets are steeped in **18–20% NaOH** at 18–25 °C to form **alkali cellulose**: $$ \text{Cell-OH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Cell-O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ **Step 3: Pressing & Shredding** Excess alkali is pressed out (giving a press ratio of ~3.0), and the alkali cellulose is mechanically shredded to increase surface area for ageing. **Step 4: Ageing (Xanthation Prep)** The shredded alkali cellulose is aged for 18–48 hours at controlled temperature (22–28 °C) and humidity. During ageing, the **average DP reduces from ~1000 to ~250–350** via oxidative depolymerisation, which is essential for spinning. **Step 5: Xanthation** Aged alkali cellulose is reacted with **CS₂ (carbon disulphide)** in a vacuum xanthator at 25–30 °C to form **sodium cellulose xanthate**: $$ \text{Cell-O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{CS}_2 \rightarrow \text{Cell-O-C(=S)-S}^-\text{Na}^+ $$ This converts the insoluble cellulose into a soluble derivative. **Step 6: Dissolution (Viscose Formation)** The yellow xanthate crumbs are dissolved in dilute NaOH (4–6%) to form the **"viscose dope"** with cellulose concentration of 7–9% and viscosity 30–60 poise. **Step 7: Ripening & Filtration** The dope is ripened (stored at 15–20 °C for 24–48 h) to partially hydrolyse xanthate groups back, achieving the desired **salt index (5–12)**. It is then filtered and de-aerated. **Step 8: Wet Spinning (Filament Formation)** The viscose dope is extruded through a spinneret (thousands of holes, 50–80 μm diameter) into a coagulation bath containing: $$ \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \;(8\text{–}12\%) + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \;(15\text{–}22\%) + \text{ZnSO}_4 \;(1\text{–}2\%) \;\; \text{at } 45\text{–}55\,^\circ\text{C} $$ The key chemical regeneration reaction is: $$ \text{Cell-O-C(=S)-S}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Cell-OH} + \text{CS}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 $$ The cellulose is **regenerated** as a continuous filament. **Step 9: Drawing & Cutting** The filament bundle is drawn (DR ~1.2–1.5) to orient the chains, washed, and cut into staple fibre (1.4–6.7 dtex). **Yield:** ~**0.5–0.6 tonne of CS₂ recovered per tonne of fibre** (modern closed-loop systems). --- ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step: Polyester (PET) Melt Spinning PET is the **king of Indian synthetic fibres** with installed capacity exceeding 6.5 MTPA. **Step 1: Polymerisation of PET** PET is synthesised by esterification of **terephthalic acid (PTA) / dimethyl terephthalate (DMT)** with **ethylene glycol (EG)**, followed by polycondensation: $$ \text{PTA} + \text{EG} \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}} \text{BHET} \xrightarrow{-\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{PET} $$ Intrinsic viscosity (IV) for fibre-grade PET: **0.62–0.65 dL/g** (measured in phenol/TCE 60:40). **Step 2: Melt Extrusion** PET chips are dried to <50 ppm moisture (hydrolysis prevention) and fed to an extruder. Polymer melt temperature: **285–295 °C** (above $T_m \approx 260\,^\circ\text{C}$). **Step 3: Spinning** The melt is metered through a **spinneret pack** (spinneret + filter + distributor) with hole diameters of 0.15–0.30 mm. **Step 4: Quench & Solidification** Filaments are cooled by cross-flow air in a quench chimney at 18–25 °C. **Step 5: Spin Finish Application** Spin finish (lubricant + antistatic agent) is applied at ~0.5–1.5% on fibre weight. **Step 6: Drawing (Drawing)** Filaments are drawn between godets at temperatures of 90–130 °C with **DR = 3.5–5.0**, inducing molecular orientation and crystallinity. **Step 7: Heat Setting & Winding** Drawn filaments are heat-set at 180–220 °C (crystal stabiliser) and wound at 3000–4500 m/min (modern high-speed spinning). > [!NOTE] > **Cross-Connection for Examiners** > A standard 14-mark question may require you to compare the viscose process with PET melt spinning in terms of polymer state (dissolution vs. melt), spinning method (wet vs. melt), and key unit operations. ### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix – Comparative Process Overview | Process Parameter | Viscose Rayon (VSF) | Polyester (PET) | Nylon 6 | Acrylic (PAN) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Polymer state** | Solution in NaOH | Melt | Melt | Solution in DMF/DMSO | | **Spinning method** | Wet | Melt | Melt | Wet / Dry | | **Polymer conc. / IV** | 7–9% cellulose | IV 0.62–0.65 dL/g | IV 0.95–1.15 dL/g | 18–22% PAN | | **Spinning temp.** | 45–55 °C bath | 285–295 °C | 260–275 °C | 25–90 °C bath | | **Throughput speed** | 50–120 m/min | 3000–6000 m/min | 400–1500 m/min | 100–400 m/min | | **Key recovery loop** | CS₂, H₂SO₄, ZnSO₄ | None (melt) | None (melt) | DMF / DMSO | --- # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Indian Fibre Industry – Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture flow** of the Indian fibre manufacturing ecosystem, showing how raw materials, processes, products, and regulatory bodies are interlinked. ```mermaid graph TD rawA[Natural Raw Materials
Cotton Jute Silk Wool Coir] --> procA[Natural Fibre Processing
Ginning Retting Degumming Scouring] rawB[Petrochemical Feedstocks
PTA EG Caprolactam Acrylonitrile Propylene] --> procB[Polymer Synthesis
Polycondensation Polymerisation] rawC[Cellulosic Pulp
Wood Pulp Cotton Linters] --> procC[Regenerated Cellulose
Xanthation Dissolution Ripening] procA --> productA[Natural Fibre Yarns
Cotton Yarn Jute Hessian Silk Yarn] procB --> prodMelt[Melt Spinning Lines
PET Nylon PP] procC --> prodWet[Wet Spinning Lines
VSF Acetate] prodMelt --> prodLine[Draw Twisting Texturising] prodWet --> prodLine productA --> prodLine prodLine --> finalProd[Final Textile Products
Apparel Home Textile Technical Textile] regBody[Regulatory and Policy Bodies
Ministry of Textiles TCO BIS SRTEPC] --> procA regBody --> procB regBody --> procC regBody --> finalProd policy[Government Initiatives
PM MITRA PLI Scheme NTTM] --> procB policy --> finalProd export[Export Markets
USA EU Bangladesh UAE] --> finalProd finalProd --> export ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram: Viscose Rayon Manufacturing (Unit Operations) ```mermaid graph LR pulpA[Wood Pulp Sheets] --> step1[Mercerisation in NaOH 18 percent] step1 --> step2[Pressing and Shredding] step2 --> step3[Ageing at 25 C for 24 h] step3 --> step4[Xanthation with CS2] step4 --> step5[Dissolution in NaOH] step5 --> step6[Ripening and Filtration] step6 --> step7[Wet Spinning into Acid Bath] step7 --> step8[Drawing and Cutting] step8 --> step9[Washing and Drying] step9 --> step10[Final VSF Bale] chem1[CS2 Recovery Loop] --> step4 chem2[Na2SO4 Recovery] --> step7 chem3[ZnSO4 Recycle] --> step7 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: PET Fibre Manufacturing (Melt Spinning Line) ```mermaid graph LR feedA[PET Chips Drying 50 ppm] --> extrd[Extruder at 290 C] extrd --> spn[Spinneret Pack
0.2 mm holes] spn --> qch[Quench Chimney
Cross flow air 20 C] qch --> fin[Spin Finish Applicator] fin --> god1[Godet 1 Feed Roll] god1 --> drw[Draw Zone 130 C
DR 4.0] drw --> god2[Godet 2 Take up] god2 --> hset[Heat Set 200 C] hset --> wind[High Speed Winder
4000 m per min] wind --> prodF[POY FDY DTY Yarn] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Tip for Students** > - The arrows always indicate the direction of material flow. > - In the PET line, **Godet 1 → Draw Zone → Godet 2** is where molecular orientation (crystallinity) is induced. The ratio of godet speeds = Draw Ratio (DR). > - In the viscose line, the **acid bath** is the heart of the regeneration chemistry; recovery of CS₂ and salts is what makes the process economically viable. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Style] **"Briefly classify man-made fibres with suitable examples."** **Model Answer:** Man-made fibres are classified into two broad categories: 1. **Regenerated Cellulosic Fibres (Semi-Synthetic):** Fibres derived from naturally occurring polymers (cellulose) that are chemically dissolved and re-extruded. Examples: **Viscose Rayon, Modal, Lyocell, Cellulose Acetate**. 2. **True Synthetic Fibres:** Fibres synthesised entirely from low-molecular-weight monomers via polymerisation reactions. Examples: **Polyester (PET), Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, Acrylic (PAN), Polypropylene (PP)**. The classification is based on the origin of the polymer backbone: regenerated fibres have a natural polymer rebuilt into a fibre form, whereas synthetic fibres have a fully synthetic polymer backbone. **[3 Marks: 1 for category identification + 1 for examples + 1 for distinction]** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Style] **"List the major players in the Indian polyester fibre industry and their installed capacities (approximate)."** **Model Answer:** Major players in the Indian polyester staple and filament yarn industry include: | Manufacturer | Approx. Installed Capacity (TPA) | Product Mix | |---|---|---| | **Reliance Industries Ltd. (RIL)** | ~2,400,000 | PSF, PFY, POY, FDY, DTY | | **Indorama Ventures (Indo Rama Synthetics)** | ~700,000 | PSF, PFY | | **Sanmar Group (Siva Group)** | ~250,000 | PSF, PFY | | **JBF Industries** | ~250,000 | PSF, PFY | | **Wellknown Polyesters** | ~180,000 | PFY | | **Amit Spinning** | ~120,000 | PSF | India's total installed polyester capacity is ~**6.5 MTPA**, making it one of the largest polyester-producing nations globally. **[3 Marks]** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) – 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, Module Internal Choice]** **(a)** With the help of a process flow diagram, describe the **manufacture of viscose rayon (VSF)** from wood pulp, highlighting the key chemical reactions involved. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare the properties of **viscose rayon, polyester (PET), and acrylic (PAN)** fibres in terms of tenacity, moisture regain, and major end uses. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): The manufacture of Viscose Staple Fibre (VSF) involves the following sequential steps: **Step 1 – Pulp Preparation:** Dissolving-grade wood pulp (α-cellulose ≥ 90%) is the raw material. **Step 2 – Mercerisation:** Pulp sheets are treated with **18% NaOH** to form **alkali cellulose**. **Step 3 – Ageing:** Alkali cellulose is aged at 22–28 °C for 18–48 h, reducing DP from ~1000 to ~300. **Step 4 – Xanthation:** Reaction with **CS₂** forms **sodium cellulose xanthate**: $$ \text{Cell-O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{CS}_2 \rightarrow \text{Cell-O-C(=S)-S}^-\text{Na}^+ $$ **Step 5 – Dissolution:** Xanthate is dissolved in dilute NaOH to form viscose dope (~8% cellulose). **Step 6 – Ripening:** Dope is stored to achieve a **Salt Index of 5–12** (partial hydrolysis of xanthate). **Step 7 – Wet Spinning:** Dope is extruded into an acid bath of H₂SO₄/Na₂SO₄/ZnSO₄, regenerating cellulose: $$ \text{Cell-O-C(=S)-S}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Cell-OH} + \text{CS}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 $$ **Step 8 – Post-treatment:** Drawing, washing, desulphurisation with NaOH, bleaching, and cutting into staple. **Key Recovery Systems:** CS₂, H₂SO₄, and ZnSO₄ are recovered in closed loops for economic and environmental reasons. **Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2. **[7 Marks: 1 for raw material + 1 for each key step + 1 for chemical reaction + 1 for recovery + 1 for diagram]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): | Property | Viscose Rayon | Polyester (PET) | Acrylic (PAN) | |---|---|---|---| | **Tenacity (g/denier)** | 1.5–2.5 (dry), 1.0–1.5 (wet) | 4.0–6.0 | 2.0–3.5 | | **Moisture Regain (%)** | ~13 (very high, comfortable) | ~0.4 (hydrophobic) | ~1.5–2.5 | | **Major End Uses** | Dress material, lining, non-woven, hygiene products | Apparel, home textile, technical textile, PET bottles | Sweaters, blankets, carpets, knitting yarn | | **Tactile property** | Soft, breathable, drapes well | Smooth, wrinkle-resistant | Wool-like, warm | | **Chemical class** | Regenerated cellulose | Polyester (aromatic) | Polyacrylonitrile (vinyl) | **Comparative Statement:** Viscose is the most absorbent and breathable but has the lowest wet strength; PET is the strongest and most dimensionally stable but lacks comfort for tropical wear; Acrylic is the wool-substitute with moderate absorbency. **[7 Marks: 2 for table + 2 for explanation + 1 for classification + 2 for comparative statement]** --- ### Question B (Choice 2) – 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Internal Choice]** **(a)** Describe the **melt spinning process for polyester (PET) fibre** with a schematic flow and discuss the role of **draw ratio, spin finish, and heat setting** in determining the final fibre properties. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **government initiatives and policy framework** that have shaped the Indian fibre manufacturing sector in the last decade, with special reference to the **PM MITRA Parks** and the **PLI Scheme for textiles**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): **Melt Spinning of PET — Process Outline:** 1. **Drying of PET chips** to moisture < 50 ppm. 2. **Extrusion** in a screw extruder at **285–295 °C**. 3. **Metering & Filtration** through a spinneret pack. 4. **Spinning** through a spinneret (hole dia 0.15–0.30 mm) into air. 5. **Quenching** by cross-flow air in a chimney. 6. **Spin finish application** (~0.5–1.5% on weight). 7. **Drawing** between godets at 90–130 °C, DR = 3.5–5.0. 8. **Heat setting** at 180–220 °C. 9. **Winding** at 3000–4500 m/min. **Role of Key Process Variables:** - **Draw Ratio (DR = v_take-up / v_feed):** A higher DR increases **molecular orientation** along the fibre axis, raising **tenacity, modulus**, and reducing **elongation at break**. However, excessive DR leads to filament breakage. - **Spin Finish:** Acts as a **lubricant** (reduces fibre–metal friction), **antistatic agent** (discharges static built up during high-speed winding), and **binder** (holds filaments together as a yarn). It determines downstream processability on texturising machines. - **Heat Setting:** Achieves **dimensional stability** by completing secondary crystallisation. The crystallites "lock in" the molecular orientation imparted during drawing. Set temperature is the upper limit for thermal service (e.g., ironing temperature of polyester fabric). **Diagram:** Refer to Mermaid PET schematic in Section 4.3. **[7 Marks: 2 for process + 2 for DR + 2 for finish/heat setting + 1 for diagram]** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Government Initiatives in the Indian Fibre Sector:** **1. PM MITRA Parks (Mega Integrated Textile Region and Apparel):** - Announced in **2021**, with an outlay of **₹4,445 crore**. - **7 mega textile parks** proposed across India (e.g., Tamil Nadu, Telangana, Karnataka, Madhya Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, Gujarat). - Aim: Integrated fibre → yarn → fabric → garment → export chain in one zone, reducing logistics cost and improving competitiveness. - Each park: ~**1,000+ acres**, plug-and-play infrastructure, common effluent treatment, worker hostels. **2. PLI Scheme for MMF and Technical Textiles (2021):** - Outlay: **₹10,683 crore** over 5 years. - Targets: **Production-Linked Incentives** for incremental sales of MMF, technical textiles, and high-end products. - 67 applications approved covering **101 product categories**. - Expected to attract investment of ~₹19,000 crore and generate ~7.5 lakh jobs. **3. National Technical Textiles Mission (NTTM):** - Outlay: **₹1,480 crore** (2019–2024). - 4 pillars: (i) R&D, (ii) market development, (iii) export promotion, (iv) education & training. - Focus on **geotextiles, agrotextiles, defence textiles, sports textiles, and protective textiles**. **4. Other Key Policies:** - **TUFS (Technology Upgradation Fund Scheme)** for capex subsidies. - **RoDTEP** (Remission of Duties and Taxes on Exported Products) for export refund. - **Hank Yarn Obligation** for handloom weavers. - **Cotton MSP (Minimum Support Price)** to protect farmer income. **Impact on Fibre Industry:** - **Diversification** from cotton to MMF (target: MMF share from 42% → 65% by 2030). - **Reduction in import dependence** for high-end fibres (aramid, carbon, UHMWPE). - **Employment generation** in tier-2 and tier-3 cities. **[7 Marks: 1.5 for each of the 4 initiatives + 1 for impact assessment]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing viscose and cellulose acetate** – Viscose is regenerated cellulose; acetate is cellulose *acetate ester* (DS 2.4). Mixing these costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Writing "spinning" without specifying wet/melt/dry** – A generic answer loses 1 mark. Always mention the type. > 3. **Forgetting the recovery loop in the viscose process** – CS₂ is toxic; mentioning its recovery demonstrates engineering maturity and is worth 0.5–1 bonus mark. > 4. **Mixing up Nylon 6 and Nylon 6,6** – Note that Nylon 6 is from caprolactam (single monomer); Nylon 6,6 is from HMDA + adipic acid (two monomers). Indian plants mainly produce Nylon 6. > 5. **No mention of the Government initiatives** – A 14-mark Module 2 question often includes a policy/government part; missing it costs 3–4 marks. > 6. **Confusing tenacity units (g/den vs N/tex)** – 1 g/den = 8.83 mN/tex. Always state units in fibre property tables. > 7. **Writing "polyester is a natural fibre"** – This is a serious factual error; 0.5 mark penalty + answer loses coherence. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for PCPOL607 — Fibre Industries in India** ### Key Facts to Memorise - ☐ India is the **2nd largest** global producer of textile fibres (after China). - ☐ India is the **largest** producer of **jute and coir** in the world. - ☐ MMF share in Indian fibre basket: **~42%**; target: **65% by 2030**. - ☐ Largest MMF company: **Reliance Industries** (~2.4 MTPA polyester capacity). - ☐ Largest VSF producer: **Grasim Industries (Aditya Birla Group)** (~580,000 TPA). - ☐ **Largest exporter** of jute goods: India. ### Key Process Identifiers - ☐ **Viscose Rayon** = Wet spinning + acid bath regeneration of cellulose. - ☐ **PET** = Melt spinning at 285–295 °C, DR 3.5–5.0, heat set at 200 °C. - ☐ **Nylon 6** = Melt spinning of caprolactam-polymerised polyamide; IV 0.95–1.15 dL/g. - ☐ **Acrylic (PAN)** = Wet spinning of PAN/DMF solution into aqueous coagulation bath; wool substitute. - ☐ **Cellulose Acetate** = Dry spinning of cellulose acetate (DS 2.4) in acetone evaporation columns. ### Key Chemical Reactions - ☐ Xanthation: $\text{Cell-O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{CS}_2 \rightarrow \text{Cell-O-C(=S)-S}^-\text{Na}^+$ - ☐ Viscose regeneration: $\text{Xanthate} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Cell-OH} + \text{CS}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ - ☐ PET polycondensation: $\text{PTA} + \text{EG} \rightarrow \text{BHET} \rightarrow \text{PET} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ - ☐ Nylon 6 ring-opening: $\text{Caprolactam} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Polyamide} \;\; (\text{via } \epsilon\text{-amino caproic acid})$ ### Key Companies - ☐ **Reliance Industries** – Polyester & PP fibre (largest). - ☐ **Grasim Industries** – VSF (largest in India). - ☐ **Century Enka** – Nylon 6 tyre cord. - ☐ **SRF Limited** – Nylon 6,6 and technical textiles. - ☐ **Indian Acrylics** – PAN fibre. - ☐ **Bongaigaon Refinery (IOCL)** – Cellulose acetate. ### Government Policies to Remember - ☐ **PM MITRA** (₹4,445 cr; 7 parks). - ☐ **PLI for Textiles** (₹10,683 cr; 5 years). - ☐ **NTTM** (₹1,480 cr). - ☐ **TUFS**, **RoDTEP**, **MSP for cotton**. ### High-Yield Comparison Table - ☐ **Cotton vs Polyester** – Cotton is natural, breathable (moisture regain 8%), biodegradable; PET is synthetic, hydrophobic, wrinkle-free, but non-biodegradable. - ☐ **Viscose vs Modal/Lyocell** – Viscose uses CS₂ (toxic); Modal/Lyocell uses NMMO solvent (closed loop, greener). - ☐ **Nylon 6 vs Nylon 6,6** – Nylon 6 from caprolactam; Nylon 6,6 from hexamethylenediamine + adipic acid; Nylon 6,6 has higher melting point (~255 °C vs ~220 °C). ### Numerical Figures to Remember - ☐ Polyester installed capacity: **6.5 MTPA** - ☐ VSF installed capacity: **0.9 MTPA** - ☐ Acrylic installed capacity: **0.13 MTPA** - ☐ Cotton production: **32.5 million bales** (2023–24) - ☐ PET IV for fibre grade: **0.62–0.65 dL/g** - ☐ Cellulose concentration in viscose dope: **7–9%** - ☐ Acid bath temperature in viscose: **45–55 °C** - ☐ PET melt spinning temperature: **285–295 °C** - ☐ PET draw ratio: **3.5–5.0** - ☐ High-speed PET winding: **3000–4500 m/min** > [!NOTE] > **End of Topic Recap.** This concludes the KTU 2024 Scheme–aligned study notes on *Fibre Manufacturing Industries in India* for PCPOL607 Module Experiment 2. The above content is structured for both rapid revision and deep analytical study, in line with KTU examination patterns.

topics.title: present status and future prospects Fibres used in polymer industry-types and sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** present status and future prospects Fibres used in polymer industry-types and sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **polymer fibre** is defined as a unit of matter characterised by a high ratio of length to diameter (typically $\geq 500{:}1$, often reaching $1000{:}1$ or more), flexibility, and a polymeric chemical structure that confers the mechanical integrity required for processing into yarns, fabrics, and reinforced composites. Under the *Textile Fiber Products Identification Act* and BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) conventions, a fibre is any natural or synthetic substance possessing a **length of at least 1000 times its effective diameter**, suitable for being spun, woven, knitted, or otherwise processed into textile or industrial structures.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, polymer fibres are broadly classified into two super-families:

1. **Natural Polymer Fibres** – produced directly by biological systems (plants, animals, insects).
2. **Man-Made (Synthetic / Regenerated) Polymer Fibres** – produced by chemical synthesis or chemical modification of natural polymers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module emphasises that students must distinguish *between fibres of polymeric origin* and *inorganic fibres* (e.g., glass, carbon, ceramic). The latter, though used in industry, are **excluded** from the polymer fibre classification because their backbone is non-polymeric (silicate, graphitic, or oxidic).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **rope** versus a **steel wire** of the same thickness. The rope (made of twisted cotton strands) is *flexible*, *soft*, and *absorbs water*. The steel wire is *rigid*, *strong in tension*, and *waterproof*. A **polymer fibre** is essentially an *engineered, microscopic rope* — millions of long-chain molecules aligned and packed together. *Natural* fibres are ropes that nature has already twisted (cotton, silk, wool), while *synthetic* fibres are ropes that human chemists spin from liquid polymers extruded through tiny holes (spinnerets), much like a spider extrudes silk.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Metric (BOLD):** The global polymer fibre market exceeded **USD 180 billion in 2024**, with a projected CAGR of approximately **5.2 %** through 2030. Polyester alone accounts for roughly **54 %** of worldwide fibre consumption, making it the single most dominant fibre class in industrial history.

### 1.3 Fundamental Classification Framework

The KTU 2024 scheme recognises the following **master classification**:

| S.No. | Class | Origin | Representative Examples |
|:-----:|:------|:-------|:------------------------|
| 1 | **Natural Plant (Cellulosic)** | Botanical | Cotton, Jute, Flax, Hemp, Coir, Banana fibre |
| 2 | **Natural Animal (Proteinic)** | Zoological | Wool, Silk, Cashmere, Mohair |
| 3 | **Regenerated Cellulosic** | Chemical modification of natural cellulose | Viscose Rayon, Modal, Lyocell (Tencel), Cuprammonium Rayon |
| 4 | **Regenerated Proteinic** | Chemical modification of natural protein | Azlon, Casein fibre |
| 5 | **Synthetic (Thermoplastic)** | Petrochemical polymerisation | Polyester (PET), Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, Polypropylene, Polyethylene |
| 6 | **Synthetic (Acrylic / Modacrylic)** | Vinyl polymerisation | Polyacrylonitrile (PAN), Modacrylic |
| 7 | **Elastomeric** | Segmented block copolymer | Spandex (Lycra), Elastane |
| 8 | **High-Performance / Specialty** | Aromatic / heterocyclic polymerisation | Aramid (Kevlar, Nomex), UHMWPE (Dyneema, Spectra), PBI, PBO (Zylon) |
| 9 | **Bio-based / Sustainable** | Microbial / bio-refinery fermentation | PLA fibre, PHA fibre, Lyocell variants |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Decomposition of Fibre Formation

A polymer fibre, whether natural or synthetic, is the macroscopic manifestation of **anisotropic alignment of polymer chains along the fibre axis**. This alignment is produced through one of the following dominant mechanisms:

#### Step 1 — Polymerisation / Biosynthesis
* **Natural:** Ribosomes, photosynthesis, or specialised glandular cells assemble monomers (glucose → cellulose; amino acids → fibroin/keratin) into long chains.
* **Synthetic:** Step-growth (polycondensation) or chain-growth (addition) polymerisation yields polymers of controlled molecular weight, $M_w$, typically in the range **$10{,}000$ to $200{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$**.

#### Step 2 — Spinning (Molecular Orientation)
* **Melt Spinning:** Polymer melt is forced through a spinneret (multi-hole die) at temperatures **20–40 °C above the melting point**. Used for polyester, nylon, polypropylene.
* **Dry Spinning:** Polymer dissolved in volatile solvent; the solvent evaporates in a heated chamber. Used for acrylic, spandex precursors.
* **Wet Spinning:** Polymer solution is coagulated in a non-solvent bath. Used for viscose rayon, lyocell, aramid.
* **Dry-Jet Wet Spinning:** Used for ultra-high-performance fibres like Kevlar and Zylon.
* **Gel Spinning:** Used for UHMWPE (Dyneema) — a gel is extruded and ultra-drawn to align chains.
* **Electro-spinning:** Produces nano-fibres (sub-micron diameter) for filtration, tissue scaffolds.

#### Step 3 — Drawing (Crystallinity Enhancement)
Drawing stretches the as-spun filament, aligning polymer chains parallel to the fibre axis. The **draw ratio** $\lambda$ is defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{L_{\text{drawn}}}{L_{\text{undrawn}}}$$

Typical commercial draw ratios range from **3:1 (polyester) to 30:1 (UHMWPE)**. Drawing increases **tensile strength**, **initial modulus**, and reduces **elongation at break**.

#### Step 4 — Heat Setting / Annealing
Stabilises the oriented, semi-crystalline structure against thermal shrinkage. For polyester, heat-setting is conducted at **190–220 °C** under controlled tension.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in KTU exams:** Question stems on "describe fibre spinning" almost always require explicit identification of (a) the polymer state (melt/solution/gel), (b) the coagulation/cooling medium, and (c) the role of subsequent drawing. Loss of any one of these is a common 2-mark deduction.

### 2.2 The Why & How of Fibre Selection

Engineers select a polymer fibre based on five interlinked performance axes:

1. **Mechanical:** tenacity, modulus, elongation, fatigue resistance.
2. **Thermal:** glass transition ($T_g$), melting point ($T_m$), LOI (Limiting Oxygen Index).
3. **Chemical:** resistance to acids, alkalis, solvents, UV, hydrolysis.
4. **Aesthetic / Comfort:** moisture regain, dyeability, handle, drape.
5. **Economic / Sustainability:** feedstock cost, recyclability, carbon footprint.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Critical Numerical Relations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Notation Safeguard:** All absolute value bars and separators are written using `\vert` and `\mid` to prevent markdown parsing errors.

| # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Typical Magnitude | Units |
|:-:|:--------------------|:--------|:------------------|:------|
| 1 | Draw Ratio | $\lambda = L_{\text{drawn}} \big/ L_{\text{undrawn}}$ | 3 – 30 | dimensionless |
| 2 | Linear Density (Tex) | $\text{Tex} = m \big/ L$ where $m$ in g, $L$ in km | 0.1 – 50 | $\text{g} \big/ \text{km}$ |
| 3 | Denier | $\text{Denier} = 9 \times \text{Tex}$ | 1 – 15 | denier |
| 4 | Tenacity (Specific Strength) | $\sigma_{sp} = F \big/ (\text{Tex} \times 9.80665)$ | 20 – 450 | $\text{cN/tex}$ |
| 5 | Initial Modulus | $E_i = d\sigma \big/ d\varepsilon \bigg\vert_{\varepsilon \to 0}$ | 0.5 – 200 | $\text{GPa}$ |
| 6 | Moisture Regain | $MR = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | 0.4 – 14 | % |
| 7 | Crystallinity (XRD/DSC) | $\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100$ | 20 – 80 | % |
| 8 | Hermans Orientation Factor | $f = \dfrac{3 \langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$ | 0.0 (iso) – 1.0 (aligned) | dimensionless |
| 9 | Mark-Houwink Intrinsic Viscosity | $[\eta] = K M^a$ | $K, a$ tabulated per polymer-solvent | $\text{dL/g}$ |
| 10 | LOI Classification | $\text{LOI} < 21$ (combustible); $21 \leq \text{LOI} < 28$ (slow-burning); $\text{LOI} \geq 28$ (self-extinguishing) | Cotton 18, Wool 25, Nomex 28, PBO 68 | % O$_2$ |
| 11 | Number-Average DP | $\overline{DP}_n = \overline{M}_n \big/ M_0$ | 100 – 5000 | repeat units |
| 12 | Glass Transition (Fox Eqn., Copolymer) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$ | composite rule | K |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility Across Industries

Polymer fibres are not confined to apparel. Their real-world industrial footprint is vast:

* **Geotextiles & civil engineering:** Polypropylene and polyester non-wovens for soil stabilisation, road construction, and erosion control.
* **Automotive:** Nylon 6,6 and PET fibres in tyre cord, seat belts, airbags; aramid in brake reinforcement.
* **Aerospace & defence:** Aramid (Kevlar) in ballistic vests; UHMWPE in lightweight armour; carbon-fibre hybrids.
* **Medical:** Biodegradable PLA, PHA, and chitosan fibres in sutures, scaffolds, and wound dressings.
* **Electronics:** Conductive polymer fibres (PEDOT:PSS-coated) in wearable sensors and e-textiles.
* **Filtration:** Melt-blown polypropylene for N95-grade and HEPA filtration.
* **Marine:** HMPE ropes outperforming steel by weight on offshore rigs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** A modern commercial airliner uses approximately **800–1000 kg of carbon-fibre-reinforced polymer (CFRP)** and additional aramid/hybrid laminates, contributing to roughly **15–20 % weight savings** versus all-aluminium airframes — a direct consequence of fibre-specific strength ($\sigma/\rho$) optimisation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Relationship Between Tenacity, Tensile Strength, and Linear Density

The KTU examiner frequently asks students to convert between the *engineering units* of fibre strength (cN/tex) and the *SI units* of stress (Pa or MPa). The derivation below demonstrates the exact conversion chain.

**Given:**
* Force at break, $F = 5.0 \text{ N}$
* Linear density, $\text{Tex} = 1.5 \text{ tex}$ (i.e., $1.5 \text{ g}$ per $\text{km}$)
* Fibre density, $\rho = 1.38 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (PET)

**Step 1 — Convert force to centinewtons.**

$$F = 5.0 \text{ N} = 500 \text{ cN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute tenacity in cN/tex.**

$$\text{Tenacity} = \frac{F \text{ (cN)}}{\text{Tex (g/km)}}$$

$$\text{Tenacity} = \frac{500 \text{ cN}}{1.5 \text{ tex}} = 333.33 \text{ cN/tex}$$

**Step 3 — Convert Tex to an areal cross-section approximation for SI stress.**

The mass per unit length is:

$$m/L = 1.5 \text{ g/km} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}$$

The cross-sectional area is therefore:

$$A = \frac{m/L}{\rho} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}}{1380 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 1.087 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute tensile strength (engineering stress) in Pa and MPa.**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{5.0 \text{ N}}{1.087 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2}$$

$$\sigma = 4.6 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa} = 4600 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of consistency.**

A direct conversion formula relating tenacity to tensile strength is:

$$\sigma (\text{MPa}) = \text{Tenacity (cN/tex)} \times \rho (\text{g/cm}^3) \times 9.80665$$

Applying the values:

$$\sigma = 333.33 \times 1.38 \times 9.80665 = 4510 \text{ MPa} \approx 4600 \text{ MPa}$$

The minor discrepancy (≈ 2 %) is due to rounding in the density, confirming the derivation's internal consistency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Favourite:** Such unit-conversion derivations are appearing in 2024-scheme Part B questions with increasing frequency. Memorising the factor **9.80665** and the chain *Tenacity → Stress → Modulus* is a high-yield strategy.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Fox Equation for Copolymer $T_g$

A common KTU problem gives monomer $T_g$ values and weight fractions and asks for the resulting fibre $T_g$ (relevant for acrylic, vinyl, and spandex fibres).

**Given for a PAN-co-MMA copolymer fibre:**
* Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) homopolymer: $T_{g,1} = 95^\circ \text{C} = 368 \text{ K}$
* Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) homopolymer: $T_{g,2} = 105^\circ \text{C} = 378 \text{ K}$
* Weight fractions: $w_1 = 0.7$ (PAN), $w_2 = 0.3$ (PMMA)

**Step 1 — Apply the Fox equation:**

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.7}{368} + \frac{0.3}{378}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 1.902 \times 10^{-3} + 7.937 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 2.696 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T_g$:**

$$T_g = \frac{1}{2.696 \times 10^{-3}} = 371 \text{ K} = 98^\circ \text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check.** The result lies between 95 °C and 105 °C, closer to 95 °C due to higher PAN fraction — consistent with physical intuition.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting a Fibre for a Given Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matrix matters:** KTU 2024 questions on "compare and recommend a fibre for [application]" require students to justify choice using **quantitative property comparisons** rather than vague qualitative claims. The matrix below is the analytical template.

| Application | Critical Property Window | Best Polymer Fibre Choice | Justification (Quantitative) |
|:------------|:--------------------------|:--------------------------|:------------------------------|
| Tyre cord (passenger car) | Tenacity $\geq 60$ cN/tex; modulus $\geq 4$ GPa; $T_g > 120^\circ$ C | **Nylon 6,6** (or PET) | Nylon 6,6 tenacity ≈ 80 cN/tex, $T_g \approx 50^\circ$ C with adhesive dip ↑ $T_g$ to 120 °C |
| Ballistic vest (military) | Specific energy absorption $\geq 30 \text{ J}\cdot\text{m}^2/\text{kg}$ | **Aramid (Kevlar 29/49)** or **UHMWPE (Dyneema)** | Kevlar 29: $E \approx 70$ GPa, $\sigma \approx 3.6$ GPa; Dyneema: $\sigma/\rho$ ≈ 10× steel |
| Hot-gas filtration (> 200 °C) | LOI $\geq 28$; $T_m > 250^\circ$ C | **Nomex (meta-aramid)** or **PPS (Polyphenylene Sulphide)** | Nomex LOI = 28, continuous service to 200 °C; PPS to 190 °C with superior chemical resistance |
| Biodegradable medical suture | Hydrolytic degradability under physiological pH | **PLA (Polylactic acid)** | PLA hydrolyses to lactic acid in 6–24 months, $T_g \approx 60^\circ$ C, easy melt-spinning |
| High-altitude parachute cord | Ultra-low density + high tenacity | **UHMWPE (Spectra 1000)** | $\rho = 0.97$ g/cm³ (floats on water!), tenacity ≈ 35 cN/tex |

### 3.4 Property Comparison of Selected Polymer Fibres

| Fibre | Density (g/cm³) | Tenacity (cN/tex) | Modulus (GPa) | $T_g$ (°C) | $T_m$ (°C) | LOI (%) | Moisture Regain (%) |
|:------|:---------------:|:------------------:|:-------------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|:-------:|:-------------------:|
| Cotton | 1.52 | 25 – 35 | 5 – 12 | — | — | 18 | 7 – 8.5 |
| Wool | 1.31 | 10 – 18 | 2 – 5 | — | — | 25 | 13 – 18 |
| Silk (degummed) | 1.34 | 30 – 45 | 8 – 15 | — | — | 23 | 11 |
| Viscose Rayon | 1.52 | 18 – 25 | 4 – 6 | — | — | 19 | 11 – 13 |
| Polyester (PET) | 1.38 | 35 – 60 | 8 – 12 | 70 – 80 | 255 – 265 | 20 – 22 | 0.4 |
| Nylon 6 | 1.14 | 35 – 60 | 2 – 4 | 47 | 220 | 20 – 22 | 3.5 – 4.5 |
| Nylon 6,6 | 1.14 | 40 – 80 | 3 – 5 | 50 | 255 | 20 – 22 | 4.0 – 4.5 |
| Acrylic (PAN) | 1.17 | 20 – 35 | 3 – 5 | 85 – 95 | 320 (decomp.) | 18 | 1.5 – 2.5 |
| Spandex (Lycra) | 1.21 | 5 – 10 | 0.05 – 0.1 | — | 230 – 250 | 20 | 0.8 – 1.2 |
| Aramid (Kevlar 49) | 1.44 | 190 – 220 | 70 – 130 | 250 (no melt) | 500 (decomp.) | 28 – 30 | 3.5 |
| UHMWPE (Dyneema) | 0.97 | 250 – 400 | 50 – 150 | — | 144 – 152 | 20 | 0 |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define a polymer fibre. List the two broad classes with two examples each.**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 model]**

**Model Answer:**
A polymer fibre is a slender, flexible strand of polymeric material with length at least 1000× its diameter, possessing adequate tensile strength for textile or industrial processing.

1. **Natural polymer fibres:** Cotton (cellulosic), Wool (proteinic).
2. **Man-made polymer fibres:** Polyester (PET), Nylon 6,6.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for classification with examples.)*

#### **Q2. What is the difference between melt spinning and dry spinning? Give one fibre produced by each.**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Melt Spinning | Dry Spinning |
|:--------|:--------------|:-------------|
| Polymer state | Molten polymer (above $T_m$) | Polymer dissolved in volatile solvent |
| Coagulation medium | Cool air quench | Hot chamber — solvent evaporation |
| Energy intensity | Lower | Higher (solvent recovery needed) |
| Example fibre | **Polyester (PET), Nylon 6,6** | **Acrylic (PAN), Spandex precursor** |

*(2 marks for comparison, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

##### **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify polymer fibres used in industry with suitable examples. Discuss the present status of synthetic fibre consumption globally.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (5 marks):**

* **Natural polymer fibres:**
  * *Cellulosic (plant):* Cotton, Jute, Flax, Hemp, Coir, Banana fibre.
  * *Proteinic (animal):* Wool, Silk, Mohair, Cashmere.

* **Man-made polymer fibres:**
  * *Regenerated cellulosic:* Viscose Rayon, Modal, Lyocell, Cuprammonium.
  * *Synthetic thermoplastic:* Polyester (PET), Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, Polypropylene, Polyethylene.
  * *Synthetic vinyl:* Acrylic (PAN), Modacrylic.
  * *Elastomeric:* Spandex / Elastane.
  * *High-performance:* Aramid, UHMWPE, PBO, PBI.

**Present status (2 marks):**
As of 2024, global fibre production exceeds **120 million metric tonnes/year**, of which **synthetic fibres ≈ 68 %** (polyester dominant at ~54 %), **natural cellulosic ≈ 25 %** (cotton dominant), and **wool/silk ≈ 1.5 %**. India is the **second-largest fibre producer** globally and the **largest producer of jute** and the **second-largest of silk**. The shift toward **recycled PET (rPET)** and bio-based alternatives is a defining industrial trend.

##### **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the properties and applications of **Nylon 6,6** and **Polyester (PET)** fibres. Why is Nylon 6,6 preferred for tyre cord?

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Nylon 6,6 | Polyester (PET) |
|:---------|:----------|:----------------|
| Tenacity (cN/tex) | 40 – 80 | 35 – 60 |
| Initial modulus (GPa) | 3 – 5 | 8 – 12 |
| $T_m$ (°C) | 255 | 260 |
| Moisture regain (%) | 4.0 – 4.5 | 0.4 |
| Dye-ability | Acid dyes; easier | Disperse dyes; needs carrier |
| Hysteresis / Energy loss | High (10 – 15 %) | Low (4 – 6 %) |
| Cost (relative) | Higher | Lower |

**Why Nylon 6,6 for tyre cord (3 marks):**
1. **High tenacity and toughness:** Sustains dynamic shock loads during rolling.
2. **High fatigue resistance:** Withstands repeated flexing over the tyre's service life.
3. **Superior adhesion to rubber:** The amide groups form strong hydrogen bonds with resorcinol-formaldehyde-latex (RFL) dip.
4. **Thermal stability up to 150 °C** with the RFL dip system.
5. **Better conversion economics** for high-denier industrial yarns.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *moisture regain* with *moisture content*. Regain uses the **dry weight** as the denominator, not the wet weight. Mixing these leads to a 1-mark deduction per occurrence.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

##### **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the manufacturing process of **viscose rayon** with a neat flow diagram. Why is it called a *regenerated* fibre?

**Model Solution:**

**Manufacturing flow (6 marks):**

1. **Pulping:** Dissolving-grade wood pulp (α-cellulose ≥ 90 %) is sheeted and shredded.
2. **Steeping:** Treated with **18 – 20 % NaOH** at 20 – 25 °C for 1 – 2 h → **alkali cellulose (soda cellulose)**.
3. **Pressing & shredding:** Excess alkali squeezed out, then shredded into crumbs.
4. **Ageing (Pre-ripening):** Controlled oxidative degradation reduces $\overline{DP}_n$ from ~1000 to ~300 – 500.
5. **Xanthation:** Reacted with **CS$_2$** to form **sodium cellulose xanthate** → orange crumbs.
6. **Dissolution:** Dissolved in dilute NaOH → **viscose dope** (8 – 10 % cellulose).
7. **Ripening:** Viscose held at 15 – 20 °C for 24 – 72 h to achieve optimum xanthate decomposition and ripening index.
8. **Wet spinning:** Dope extruded into a **spinning bath** containing **H$_2$SO$_4$ (8 – 12 %), Na$_2$SO$_4$ (15 – 20 %), ZnSO$_4$ (0.5 – 1 %)** at 40 – 55 °C. Coagulation and regeneration occur simultaneously, regenerating cellulose II.
9. **Drawing, washing, desulphurisation, bleaching, drying** → finished rayon filament.

**Why "regenerated" (1 mark):**
Because the *natural cellulose polymer* is dissolved and then *re-precipitated as a continuous filament*; the chemical identity of the polymer (cellulose) is restored, hence the name **regenerated cellulose fibre**.

##### **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **future prospects** of polymer fibres in the context of **sustainability, smart textiles, and circular economy**.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Sustainability & bio-based feedstocks (3 marks):**
* **PLA fibre** from corn-starch or sugarcane — biodegradable, compostable, suitable for non-woven disposables and apparel.
* **PHA (polyhydroxyalkanoate) fibre** from microbial fermentation — fully marine-degradable.
* **Bio-based PET (part-PET)** using mono-ethylene glycol from sugarcane; **bio-nylon** from castor-oil-derived sebacic acid.
* **Lyocell / Tencel** as a closed-loop solvent-spun cellulose substitute for viscose, eliminating CS$_2$ toxicity.
* **Recycled PET (rPET):** Bottle-to-fibre technology is the fastest-growing segment, projected to exceed **15 million tonnes by 2030**.

**2. Smart and functional textiles (2 marks):**
* **Conductive polymer fibres** (PEDOT:PSS, polypyrrole-coated) for e-textiles, biosignal monitoring.
* **Phase-change-material (PCM) microencapsulated fibres** for thermoregulating apparel.
* **Photonic and chromic fibres** for camouflage and fashion.
* **Self-healing polymer fibres** using microvascular networks and Diels-Alder reversible chemistry.

**3. Circular economy (2 marks):**
* **Chemical recycling of PET:** glycolysis, methanolysis, and enzymatic (PETase) depolymerisation back to monomers.
* **Design for recyclability:** Mono-material garments (e.g., 100 % PET or 100 % cotton blends).
* **Industrial symbiosis:** textile-to-textile recycling streams.
* **Carbon footprint reduction:** Industry target — **50 % reduction in CO$_2$e per kg fibre by 2030** (Textile Exchange roadmap).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Future-prospect questions are marked on **specific named examples + quantitative targets**, not vague statements like *"biodegradable fibres will replace synthetics."* Any unsubstantiated claim is treated as zero-content by the examiner.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Fibre Questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing tenacity in MPa directly without acknowledging the linear-density basis (cN/tex). Always state *which* unit.
> 2. **Spelling of "lyocell," "spandex," "elastane," "modacrylic"** — examiners are strict; misspelt polymer names are penalised.
> 3. **Reversing $T_g$ and $T_m$** in tables. *Glass transition is below melting* — for semi-crystalline polymers, this is unambiguous.
> 4. **Confusing viscose (regenerated cellulose) with cellulose acetate (a derivative, not a regenerated fibre in the strict KTU sense).**
> 5. **Forgetting to state the spinning process** when asked "how is the fibre made?" — "melt-spun" or "wet-spun" is *non-negotiable* in a 7-mark answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* A **polymer fibre** has length $\geq 1000 \times$ diameter and is composed of long-chain macromolecules.
* **Master classification:** *Natural (cellulosic + proteinic)*, *Regenerated (cellulosic)*, *Synthetic (thermoplastic + vinyl + elastomeric)*, *Specialty / High-Performance (aramid, UHMWPE, PBO, PBI)*, *Bio-based (PLA, PHA)*.
* **Synthetic fibres ≈ 68 %** of global fibre production; **polyester alone ≈ 54 %**.
* **Five spinning technologies:** melt, dry, wet, dry-jet wet, gel; **electrospinning** for nano-fibres.
* **Draw ratio** $\lambda$ controls molecular orientation and crystallinity → directly determines tenacity and modulus.
* **Tenacity (cN/tex) conversion:** $\sigma(\text{MPa}) = \text{Tenacity} \times \rho \times 9.80665$.
* **MOISTURE REGAIN** is calculated on **dry weight**, not wet weight.
* **Fox equation** governs the $T_g$ of copolymers; PAN-co-MMA fibre examples are standard.
* **Viscose rayon** is a regenerated cellulose fibre made via xanthate route (CS$_2$ / NaOH).
* **High-performance fibres** (Kevlar, Dyneema, Zylon) are defined by **aromatic / chain-extended** backbones and **liquid-crystal spinning**.
* **Future direction:** Recycled PET (rPET), bio-based PLA/PHA, lyocell closed-loop, conductive and self-healing smart fibres, chemical recycling (PETase, glycolysis).
* **Key 2024-scheme industry numbers to remember (BOLD):** Global fibre output > **120 Mt/yr**; synthetic share **≈ 68 %**; polyester share **≈ 54 %**; rPET projected **15 Mt by 2030**.
* **Always name the spinning process** when introducing any synthetic fibre — this is a *must-state* in KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
