topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** chemical composition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Composition (Polymer Context):** In polymer science and technology, *chemical composition* refers to the **identity, type, and relative molar proportion of the monomeric units (repeat units) that constitute a macromolecular chain**. It is a primary structural parameter that governs all physical, mechanical, thermal, and rheological properties of a polymeric material.

For copolymers, chemical composition is specifically quantified by the **mole fraction** (or mass fraction) of each comonomer incorporated into the polymer chain, often denoted as $F_1$ and $F_2$ for a binary copolymer system. The composition of a copolymer almost never matches the feed ratio of monomers due to **differential reactivity** of monomers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Recipe Analogy"** — Imagine you are baking a cake. The **chemical composition** of your polymer is like the **recipe card** that lists exactly how much of each ingredient (flour, sugar, eggs) actually went *into* the final cake. Just as the cake's taste depends not on what you *bought* (the feed), but on what you *mixed in* (the actual composition), a copolymer's properties (glass transition, crystallinity, solubility) depend on the **real molar ratio of monomers** incorporated into the chain, not just the ratio you loaded into the reactor.

### 1.3 Key Constituents of Chemical Composition Analysis

| Component | Description | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Repeat Unit** | The smallest structural unit whose repetition defines the polymer chain | Foundational concept |
| **Mole Fraction ($F_i$)** | Moles of monomer $i$ in copolymer ÷ Total moles of repeat units | Primary measurable |
| **Mass Fraction ($w_i$)** | Mass of monomer $i$ ÷ Total mass of repeat units | Used in gravimetric analysis |
| **Conversion ($p$)** | Fraction of total monomer consumed at a given time | Critical for composition drift |
| **Reactivity Ratio ($r_i$)** | Tendency of a growing chain-end to add its own vs. the other monomer | Determines composition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (used universally in KTU evaluation):**
> - Molecular weight of repeat unit: **M₀ in g/mol**
> - Copolymer composition equation: **Mayo–Lewis (1944)**
> - Elemental analysis tolerance: **±0.3% (absolute)**
> - NMR solvent standards: **CDCl₃, DMSO-d₆**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The chemical composition of a copolymer is governed by the **terminal model** of copolymerization, which assumes that the reactivity of a propagating chain end depends **only on the identity of the terminal monomer unit**, not on earlier units. This gives rise to four propagation rate constants and two reactivity ratios.

### 2.2 The Four Propagation Steps (Binary System: M₁ + M₂)

1. **Step 1:** $\sim M_1^\bullet + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{11}} \sim M_1 M_1^\bullet$ (rate = $k_{11}[M_1^\bullet][M_1]$)
2. **Step 2:** $\sim M_1^\bullet + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{12}} \sim M_1 M_2^\bullet$ (rate = $k_{12}[M_1^\bullet][M_2]$)
3. **Step 3:** $\sim M_2^\bullet + M_2 \xrightarrow{k_{22}} \sim M_2 M_2^\bullet$ (rate = $k_{22}[M_2^\bullet][M_2]$)
4. **Step 4:** $\sim M_2^\bullet + M_1 \xrightarrow{k_{21}} \sim M_2 M_1^\bullet$ (rate = $k_{21}[M_2^\bullet][M_1]$)

### 2.3 Reactivity Ratios — The "Why" Behind Composition

$$
r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} \qquad r_2 = \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}}
$$

- **$r_1 > 1$** : Radical ending in $M_1$ prefers to add $M_1$ (homopropagation favored).
- **$r_1 < 1$** : Radical ending in $M_1$ prefers the *other* monomer (heteropropagation favored).
- **$r_1 \cdot r_2 = 1$** : **Ideal copolymerization** (azeotropic at $f_1 = F_1$).
- **$r_1 = r_2 = 1$** : **Random/ideal copolymer** (no preference, Bernoullian statistics).
- **$r_1 = r_2 = 0$** : **Strictly alternating copolymer**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_1 = \dfrac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2}$ | **Mayo–Lewis copolymer composition equation** | Mole fraction in copolymer |
| 2 | $f_1 = \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_1] + [M_2]}$ | Feed mole fraction of M₁ | $0 \le f_1 \le 1$ |
| 3 | $F_1 = \dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_1] + d[M_2]}$ | Instantaneous copolymer mole fraction of M₁ | $0 \le F_1 \le 1$ |
| 4 | $f_1_{\text{az}} = \dfrac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2}$ | Azeotropic feed composition (no drift) | Only valid if $0 \le f_1 \le 1$ |
| 5 | $w_1 = \dfrac{F_1 M_1}{F_1 M_1 + (1-F_1) M_2}$ | Mass fraction from mole fraction | $w_1 + w_2 = 1$ |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \dfrac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}$ | Rearranged Mayo–Lewis (ratio form) | Useful for Fineman–Ross plot |
| 7 | $G = r_1 H - r_2$ | **Fineman–Ross linear form** | $G = X(X/R - 1)$, $H = X^2 / R$ |
| 8 | $\eta_{sp}/c = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 c$ | **Huggins equation** (viscosity, linked to $M_W$) | $c$ in g/dL |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Chemical composition dictates the **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** via the Fox equation, **oxygen permeability** (vital for food packaging films), **biodegradability** (PLA–PHB copolymers), **tackiness of pressure-sensitive adhesives** (acrylics), and **drug-release kinetics** in pharmaceutical polymer conjugates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mayo–Lewis Copolymer Composition Equation

We begin with the rates of consumption of monomers $M_1$ and $M_2$. The rate at which $M_1$ enters the copolymer equals the rate at which radicals ending in $M_1$ add either $M_1$ or $M_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{d[M_1]}{dt} &= k_{11}[M_1^\bullet][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^\bullet][M_1] \\
-\frac{d[M_2]}{dt} &= k_{12}[M_1^\bullet][M_2] + k_{22}[M_2^\bullet][M_2]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the steady-state assumption (Bodenstein principle):** The concentrations of the two radical types remain constant, so the rate of forming $M_1^\bullet$ equals the rate of consuming it.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{21}[M_2^\bullet][M_1] = k_{12}[M_1^\bullet][M_2]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the radical concentration ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{[M_1^\bullet]}{[M_2^\bullet]} = \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio of consumption rates (definition of copolymer composition):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{k_{11}[M_1^\bullet][M_1] + k_{21}[M_2^\bullet][M_1]}{k_{12}[M_1^\bullet][M_2] + k_{22}[M_2^\bullet][M_2]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide numerator and denominator by $k_{12}[M_1^\bullet][M_2]$ and substitute the radical ratio from Step 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} &= \frac{ \frac{k_{11}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]} + \frac{k_{21}[M_1]}{k_{12}[M_2]} \cdot \frac{[M_2]}{[M_1]} \cdot \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} }{1 + \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}} \cdot \frac{[M_2]}{[M_1]} } \\[6pt]
&= \frac{ \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}} \cdot \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} + 1 }{ 1 + \frac{k_{22}}{k_{21}} \cdot \frac{[M_2]}{[M_1]} } \cdot \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the reactivity ratios $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to mole fractions.** Let $f_1 = \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_1]+[M_2]}$ and $F_1 = \dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_1]+d[M_2]}$. Algebraic rearrangement (substitute $f_1 f_2$ denominators and clear fractions) gives the celebrated Mayo–Lewis equation:

$$
\boxed{\; F_1 = \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2} \;}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem.** Styrene ($M_1$) and acrylonitrile ($M_2$) are copolymerized at a feed mole fraction $f_1 = 0.60$. Given $r_1 = 0.40$ and $r_2 = 0.04$, find the instantaneous copolymer composition $F_1$.

**Step 1 — Compute $f_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_2 = 1 - f_1 = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator of Mayo–Lewis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2 \\
  &= (0.40)(0.60)^2 + (0.60)(0.40) \\
  &= (0.40)(0.36) + 0.24 \\
  &= 0.144 + 0.240 = 0.384
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Denominator of Mayo–Lewis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2 \\
  &= 0.144 + 2(0.60)(0.40) + (0.04)(0.40)^2 \\
  &= 0.144 + 0.480 + 0.0064 = 0.6304
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final composition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 = \frac{N}{D} = \frac{0.384}{0.6304} = 0.609
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Although the feed was 60% styrene, the copolymer contains **~60.9% styrene by mole**. The slight enrichment (and constant value) signals that the system is operating *near* its azeotrope, where $F_1 \approx f_1$ and composition drift is minimal.

## 3.3 Azeotropic Composition Derivation

For a constant composition copolymer (no drift), we require $F_1 = f_1$. Setting the Mayo–Lewis expression equal to $f_1$ and solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_1 &= \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1(1-f_1)}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2f_1(1-f_1) + r_2(1-f_1)^2} \\[4pt]
f_1 \cdot D &= N \\[4pt]
f_1[r_1 f_1^2 + 2f_1(1-f_1) + r_2(1-f_1)^2] &= r_1 f_1^2 + f_1(1-f_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding and cancelling the common $r_1 f_1^2$ term yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 f_1^2(1-f_1) + f_1 r_2(1-f_1)^2 &= f_1(1-f_1) \\[4pt]
2f_1 + r_2(1-f_1) &= 1 \quad \text{(divide by } f_1(1-f_1)) \\[4pt]
\boxed{\; f_{1,\text{az}} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2} \;}
\end{aligned}
$$

This composition only lies between 0 and 1 (physically realizable) when $(r_1 - 1)(r_2 - 1) > 0$, meaning both ratios must be simultaneously greater or smaller than unity.

## 3.4 Fineman–Ross Linearization (for Reactivity Ratio Determination)

Starting from $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \dfrac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}$, define $X = \dfrac{[M_1]}{[M_2]}$ and $R = \dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{F_1}{F_2}$. Rearrangement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{X(R-1)}{R} = -r_2 + r_1 \cdot \frac{X^2}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $G = \dfrac{X(R-1)}{R}$ and $H = \dfrac{X^2}{R}$. Then $G = r_1 H - r_2$. A plot of $G$ vs $H$ gives a straight line with **slope = $r_1$** and **intercept = $-r_2$**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Chemical-Composition Analysis Pipeline

Below is a Mermaid block-level functional architecture describing the experimental workflow for determining chemical composition in a copolymer (typical Module 2 experiment).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Preparation] --> B[Reactor Charging with M1 and M2]
    B --> C[Initiator Addition and Polymerization]
    C --> D[Reaction Quenching at Target Conversion]
    D --> E[Precipitation and Purification of Copolymer]
    E --> F[Purified Copolymer Sample]
    F --> G1[Elemental CHN Analysis]
    F --> G2[1H NMR Spectroscopy]
    F --> G3[FTIR Spectroscopy]
    G1 --> H[Compute Mole Fraction F1 and F2]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Apply Mayo Lewis Equation to Extract r1 and r2]
    I --> J[Fineman Ross Linear Plot]
    J --> K[Reactivity Ratios r1 and r2]
    K --> L[Report Copolymer Composition and Reactivity]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual State Diagram: Composition Drift Over Conversion

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FreshFeed: f1 defined
    FreshFeed --> InitialCopolymer: First few chains form with F1
    InitialCopolymer --> DriftPhase: M1 consumed faster if r1 greater than 1
    DriftPhase --> CompositionShift: f1 in reactor changes
    CompositionShift --> LateStageCopolymer: F1 deviates from f1
    LateStageCopolymer --> AzeotropeCheck: If f1 az, F1 equals f1 constant
    AzeotropeCheck --> FinalPolymer: Isolated product has averaged F
    FinalPolymer --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting a Composition-Measurement Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to determine copolymer composition] --> B{Is a specific atom present in only one monomer?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Elemental Analysis e.g. N in acrylonitrile]
    B -- No --> D{Are proton environments distinct in NMR?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use 1H NMR integration]
    D -- No --> F{Are functional groups IR active and unique?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use FTIR with calibration curve]
    F -- No --> H[Use UV Visible spectroscopy at lambda max]
```

## 4.4 Functional Map: How Composition Affects Downstream Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chemical Composition F1] --> B[Glass Transition Tg via Fox Equation]
    A --> C[Crystallinity and Melting Point]
    A --> D[Mechanical Modulus and Tensile Strength]
    A --> E[Solubility and Swelling]
    A --> F[Optical Transparency]
    A --> G[Permeability to Gases]
    B --> H[Final Application Suitability]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`
**Define the term "reactivity ratio" in copolymerization and state the special case when both $r_1$ and $r_2$ equal zero.**

**Model Answer:**
The reactivity ratio of monomer $M_1$, denoted $r_1$, is defined as the ratio of the rate constant for a propagating chain ending in $M_1$ adding another $M_1$ to the rate constant for that chain adding $M_2$:

$$
r_1 = \frac{k_{11}}{k_{12}}
$$

Analogously, $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$. **Special case $r_1 = r_2 = 0$:** This indicates that a chain ending in $M_1$ can *only* add $M_2$ and vice versa, producing a **strictly alternating copolymer** with the maximum possible alternation (regular head-to-tail alternation of units). A canonical example is the **maleic anhydride–styrene** alternating copolymer.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**What is meant by "azeotropic composition" in a copolymerization? Write the expression for the azeotropic feed mole fraction.**

**Model Answer:**
An **azeotropic composition** in copolymerization is the specific feed mole fraction $f_{1,\text{az}}$ at which the composition of the copolymer being formed **at any instant is identical** to the feed composition in the reactor. Consequently, no composition drift occurs as conversion proceeds, and the resulting copolymer is compositionally homogeneous.

$$
f_{1,\text{az}} = \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2}
$$

The azeotrope is **physically realizable** only when the product $(r_1 - 1)(r_2 - 1) > 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Mayo–Lewis copolymer composition equation starting from the four propagation steps of the terminal model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A copolymerization of styrene ($M_1$, $M_1 = 104$ g/mol) and butadiene ($M_2$, $M_2 = 54$ g/mol) is carried out with $f_1 = 0.30$ in the feed. The reactivity ratios are $r_1 = 0.50$ and $r_2 = 1.40$. Calculate **(i)** the instantaneous mole fraction of styrene in the copolymer $F_1$, **(ii)** the instantaneous mass fraction $w_1$, and **(iii)** comment on whether the system exhibits an azeotrope. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the four elementary propagation steps with their rate expressions for a binary system $M_1$ and $M_2$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express the rates of disappearance of $M_1$ and $M_2$ and form their ratio.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the steady-state (Bodenstein) approximation to equate the formation and consumption rates of $M_1^\bullet$ and $M_2^\bullet$, leading to the radical concentration ratio.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute the radical ratio back into the consumption ratio and simplify using $r_1, r_2$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Convert to mole-fraction form and arrive at the final boxed Mayo–Lewis equation.

*Full derivation is provided in Section 3.1 of these notes — reproduce it line by line in the answer script.*

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**(i) Instantaneous mole fraction $F_1$:** With $f_1 = 0.30$, $f_2 = 0.70$, $r_1 = 0.50$, $r_2 = 1.40$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2} \\
&= \frac{(0.50)(0.09) + (0.30)(0.70)}{(0.50)(0.09) + 2(0.30)(0.70) + (1.40)(0.49)} \\
&= \frac{0.045 + 0.210}{0.045 + 0.420 + 0.686} \\
&= \frac{0.255}{1.151} = 0.2216
\end{aligned}
$$

**$F_1 = 0.222$ (styrene is incorporated less than its feed ratio).**

**(ii) Mass fraction $w_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 &= \frac{F_1 M_1}{F_1 M_1 + (1-F_1) M_2} \\
&= \frac{(0.2216)(104)}{(0.2216)(104) + (0.7784)(54)} \\
&= \frac{23.05}{23.05 + 42.03} = \frac{23.05}{65.08} = 0.354
\end{aligned}
$$

**$w_1 = 0.354$ (mass fraction of styrene in the copolymer).**

**(iii) Azeotrope check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{1,\text{az}} &= \frac{1 - r_2}{2 - r_1 - r_2} = \frac{1 - 1.40}{2 - 0.50 - 1.40} = \frac{-0.40}{0.10} = -4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $f_{1,\text{az}} \notin [0,1]$, **no azeotrope exists** for this system. This is consistent with the criterion $(r_1 - 1)(r_2 - 1) = (-0.50)(+0.40) = -0.20 < 0$. Composition will drift strongly with conversion: butadiene is preferentially incorporated first, depleting $M_2$ in the feed.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTu University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define the Fineman–Ross method for determining reactivity ratios. Starting from the Mayo–Lewis equation, derive the linearized Fineman–Ross relation $G = r_1 H - r_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were obtained for the free-radical copolymerization of methyl methacrylate ($M_1$) and vinyl acetate ($M_2$):

| Feed $f_1$ | 0.20 | 0.35 | 0.50 | 0.65 | 0.80 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Copolymer $F_1$ | 0.32 | 0.47 | 0.58 | 0.67 | 0.78 |

Determine $r_1$ and $r_2$ using the Fineman–Ross method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Fineman–Ross is a graphical linearization of the Mayo–Lewis equation that converts composition data into a straight-line plot from which reactivity ratios $r_1$ and $r_2$ are obtained as the slope and negative intercept.

**Derivation (6 marks):** Start from the Mayo–Lewis ratio form:

$$
\frac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \frac{[M_1]}{[M_2]} \cdot \frac{r_1 [M_1] + [M_2]}{[M_1] + r_2 [M_2]}
$$

Define $X = [M_1]/[M_2]$ and $R = d[M_1]/d[M_2] = F_1/F_2$. Then:

$$
R = X \cdot \frac{r_1 X + 1}{X + r_2}
$$

Cross-multiplying and rearranging:

$$
R(X + r_2) = X(r_1 X + 1) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; RX + R r_2 = r_1 X^2 + X
$$

$$
R r_2 = r_1 X^2 + X - RX = r_1 X^2 - X(R - 1)
$$

Divide by $R$:

$$
r_2 = r_1 \frac{X^2}{R} - \frac{X(R-1)}{R}
$$

Rearrange into the standard Fineman–Ross form $G = r_1 H - r_2$:

$$
\underbrace{\frac{X(R-1)}{R}}_{G} = r_1 \underbrace{\frac{X^2}{R}}_{H} - r_2
$$

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute $X$, $R$, $G$, $H$ for each feed.**

| $f_1$ | $F_1$ | $X = f_1/f_2$ | $R = F_1/F_2$ | $G = X(R-1)/R$ | $H = X^2/R$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.20 | 0.32 | 0.250 | 0.471 | -0.281 | 0.133 |
| 0.35 | 0.47 | 0.538 | 0.887 | -0.068 | 0.327 |
| 0.50 | 0.58 | 1.000 | 1.381 | 0.276 | 0.724 |
| 0.65 | 0.67 | 1.857 | 2.030 | 0.943 | 1.699 |
| 0.80 | 0.78 | 4.000 | 3.545 | 2.872 | 4.513 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression $G = r_1 H - r_2$.** Using the data above, perform a least-squares fit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{H} &= 1.479, \quad \bar{G} = 0.748 \\
\sum (H_i - \bar{H})(G_i - \bar{G}) &\approx 6.78 \\
\sum (H_i - \bar{H})^2 &\approx 13.66 \\
r_1 &= \frac{6.78}{13.66} \approx 0.50 \\
r_2 &= r_1 \bar{H} - \bar{G} = (0.50)(1.479) - 0.748 \approx 0.74 - 0.748 \approx -0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $r_1 \approx 0.50$, $r_2 \approx 0.00$ (within experimental error $r_2 \approx 0$, indicating near-alternating behavior of vinyl acetate). The methyl methacrylate radical strongly prefers methyl methacrylate, while the vinyl acetate radical has negligible preference — leading to a moderately random copolymer with mild drift.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign error in $r_2$ intercept:** The Fineman–Ross intercept is **$-r_2$**, not $+r_2$. Many students write $G = r_1 H + r_2$, leading to wrong sign on $r_2$.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the Mayo–Lewis denominator:** The factor of **2** in $2 f_1 f_2$ comes from the symmetry $k_{12}[M_1^\bullet][M_2] = k_{21}[M_2^\bullet][M_1]$ and is frequently dropped.
> 3. **Confusing mole fraction and mass fraction:** $F_1$ and $f_1$ are *mole* fractions. Converting to mass fraction requires multiplication by $M_i$ and renormalization.
> 4. **Azeotrope range check:** Always verify $0 \le f_{1,\text{az}} \le 1$. A negative or $> 1$ value means **no azeotrope**; do not silently take the modulus.
> 5. **Steady-state assumption skipped:** Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for stating the **Bodenstein steady-state assumption** in the derivation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Chemical composition** of a copolymer is the **mole ratio of monomer units** actually incorporated — not the feed ratio.
- ✅ The **Mayo–Lewis equation** is the central relationship: $F_1 = (r_1 f_1^2 + f_1 f_2) / (r_1 f_1^2 + 2 f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^2)$.
- ✅ **Reactivity ratios** $r_1, r_2$ are the dimensionless measures of monomer selectivity; $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$, $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$.
- ✅ **$r_1 r_2 = 1$** → ideal (Bernoullian) copolymerization; **$r_1 = r_2 = 0$** → strictly alternating.
- ✅ **Azeotrope exists** iff $(r_1 - 1)(r_2 - 1) > 0$, and $f_{1,\text{az}} = (1-r_2)/(2 - r_1 - r_2)$.
- ✅ **Fineman–Ross linearization:** $G = r_1 H - r_2$, with $G = X(R-1)/R$ and $H = X^2/R$.
- ✅ Experimental techniques for composition: **¹H NMR** (gold standard), **elemental (CHN) analysis**, **FTIR** with calibration, **UV–Vis**.
- ✅ Always apply the **steady-state approximation** to derive composition equations — KTU marks are awarded for this step.
- ✅ Always **check realizability** of azeotrope values; cite $0 \le f_{1,\text{az}} \le 1$ explicitly in answers.
- ✅ Remember: feed ratio $f_1$ ≠ copolymer ratio $F_1$ (unless at azeotrope).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Polymer Properties

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCPOL607 (Polymer Chemistry / Polymer Technology)**, **Module Experiment 2** focuses on the **experimental determination and characterization of polymer properties**. Polymers—long-chain macromolecules composed of repeating structural units (mers) linked by covalent bonds—exhibit a unique spectrum of properties that are dictated by their **molecular architecture, chain configuration, degree of polymerization, intermolecular forces, and processing history**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Properties** are the measurable physical, mechanical, thermal, rheological, chemical, electrical, and optical responses of a polymeric material to external stimuli (force, heat, light, chemicals, electric field). These properties are **not intrinsic to the monomer** but emerge from the collective behavior of the entire macromolecular chain assembly.

The properties of polymers are broadly classified into the following categories, each evaluated using standardized experimental techniques (ASTM/ISO standards):

| Category | Example Properties |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Mechanical** | Tensile strength, Young's modulus, elongation at break, impact strength, hardness, flexural strength |
| **Thermal** | Glass transition temperature ($T_g$), melting temperature ($T_m$), Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT), Vicat softening point, thermal conductivity, coefficient of thermal expansion |
| **Rheological** | Melt Flow Index (MFI), viscosity, shear rate dependence, viscoelastic behavior |
| **Physical** | Density, crystallinity, water absorption, melt density, refractive index |
| **Chemical** | Solubility, chemical resistance, swelling behavior, hydrolytic stability |
| **Electrical** | Dielectric strength, volume resistivity, surface resistivity, dielectric constant |
| **Optical** | Transparency, haze, gloss, refractive index |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍝 Real-World Analogy — The Spaghetti Bowl**
>
> Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti. The **single strand** represents an individual polymer chain. Now imagine the difference between:
> - **A dry, loose bundle of spaghetti** → This represents an **amorphous polymer** below its $T_g$ (glassy, brittle, rigid).
> - **A bundle where strands are tangled and overlapping randomly** → This represents an **amorphous polymer** above $T_g$ but below $T_m$ (rubbery, flexible, soft).
> - **A bowl where the strands are aligned in parallel bundles** → This represents a **semi-crystalline polymer** (stronger, denser, with both ordered crystalline regions and disordered amorphous regions).
>
> **The "properties" of the polymer are how this "spaghetti bowl" responds when you:**
> - **Pull it** → Mechanical properties (tensile strength)
> - **Heat it** → Thermal properties ($T_g$, $T_m$)
> - **Pour it through a hole** → Rheological properties (MFI, viscosity)
> - **Dip it in water** → Physical/chemical properties (water absorption, swelling)
> - **Pass electricity through it** → Electrical properties (dielectric strength)

## 1.3 Why Properties Matter in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The **selection of a polymer for a specific engineering application** (e.g., PET bottles, HDPE pipes, PMMA aircraft windows, PTFE gaskets, Nylon gears) is **entirely driven by its properties**. No single polymer is "best" — engineers choose based on a property profile. Module Experiment 2 trains you to **measure, compare, and interpret** these properties using industry-standard methods.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Polymer Properties — Theoretical Framework

### 🔬 A. Mechanical Properties

These describe the response of a polymer to applied **force, stress, or deformation**.

#### 2.1.1 Tensile Properties (ASTM D638)

A dog-bone shaped specimen is pulled at a constant crosshead speed in a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) until failure. The stress-strain curve reveals critical parameters.

**Key Mechanical Parameters:**

- **Tensile Strength ($\sigma_t$)**:
  - The maximum stress a polymer can withstand while being stretched before breaking.
  - $$\sigma_t = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0}$$
  - Where: $F_{max}$ = maximum load (N), $A_0$ = original cross-sectional area (m²)
  - **Units**: Pa (or MPa in SI practice)

- **Young's Modulus / Modulus of Elasticity ($E$)**:
  - Measure of stiffness; ratio of stress to strain in the elastic region.
  - $$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F/A_0}{\Delta L / L_0}$$
  - **Units**: Pa or MPa

- **Elongation at Break ($\varepsilon_b$)**:
  - The percentage increase in original length at the moment of rupture.
  - $$\varepsilon_b = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$$
  - Where $L_f$ = final length at break, $L_0$ = original gauge length

- **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)**:
  - Stress at which the material begins to deform plastically (permanent deformation).

#### 2.1.2 Impact Strength (ASTM D256 — Izod / Charpy)

- Measures the ability of a polymer to absorb energy from a sudden impact.
- $$I = \frac{E_{absorbed}}{t}$$
- Where $E_{absorbed}$ = energy absorbed by specimen during fracture (J), $t$ = thickness of specimen at notch (m)
- **Units**: J/m

#### 2.1.3 Hardness (ASTM D2240 — Shore A / Shore D)

- Resistance to localized plastic deformation (indentation).
- Shore A: softer elastomers and flexible plastics
- Shore D: harder plastics and rigid polymers

### 🌡️ B. Thermal Properties

#### 2.1.4 Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)

- The temperature at which an **amorphous polymer** transitions from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state.
- Below $T_g$: segmental motion is frozen → glassy, rigid
- Above $T_g$: chain segments gain mobility → rubbery, flexible

#### 2.1.5 Melting Temperature ($T_m$)

- The temperature at which a **crystalline polymer** transitions from a solid ordered state to a viscous liquid (melt).

#### 2.1.6 Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT) (ASTM D648)

- The temperature at which a polymer specimen deflects by a specified amount under a specified load.
- Indicates short-term heat resistance.

#### 2.1.7 Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE, $\alpha$)

$$\alpha = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0 \cdot \Delta T}$$

- **Units**: °C⁻¹ or K⁻¹

#### 2.1.8 Thermal Conductivity ($k$)

$$q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$

- Fourier's law of heat conduction in polymers; polymers are generally **thermal insulators** with $k \approx 0.1$–$0.5$ W/(m·K).

### 💧 C. Rheological Properties

#### 2.1.9 Melt Flow Index (MFI) / Melt Mass-Flow Rate (MFR) (ASTM D1238)

- Mass of polymer (in grams) that flows through a capillary of specified diameter and length in **10 minutes** under a specified temperature and load.
- **MFR**:
$$MFR = \frac{m \times 600}{t}$$
- Where $m$ = mass of extrudate (g), $t$ = time of flow measurement (s), 600 = 600 seconds (= 10 min)
- **Units**: g/10 min
- **Inverse relationship with melt viscosity** — higher MFI = lower viscosity = easier processing

#### 2.1.10 Melt Viscosity ($\eta$)

$$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

- Where $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa), $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)
- Polymers are **non-Newtonian** (shear-thinning pseudoplastic).

### 🧪 D. Physical & Chemical Properties

#### 2.1.11 Density ($\rho$)

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

- Used to identify polymers and calculate crystallinity.

#### 2.1.12 Water Absorption (ASTM D570)

$$W_A = \frac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100\%$$

- Critical for polymers used in marine, biomedical, and outdoor applications.

#### 2.1.13 Percent Crystallinity ($\chi_c$)

$$\chi_c = \frac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100\%$$

- Where $\rho_c$ = density of fully crystalline polymer, $\rho_a$ = density of fully amorphous polymer, $\rho_s$ = density of sample

### ⚡ E. Electrical Properties

#### 2.1.14 Dielectric Strength

$$E_d = \frac{V_b}{t}$$

- Where $V_b$ = breakdown voltage, $t$ = thickness. Units: kV/mm.

#### 2.1.15 Volume Resistivity ($\rho_v$)

$$\rho_v = R_v \cdot \frac{A}{t}$$

- Units: Ω·cm

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Standard |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------|
| 1 | Tensile Strength | $\sigma_t = F_{max}/A_0$ | MPa | ASTM D638 |
| 2 | Young's Modulus | $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$ | MPa | ASTM D638 |
| 3 | Elongation at Break | $\varepsilon_b = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$ | % | ASTM D638 |
| 4 | Impact Strength | $I = E_{abs}/t$ | J/m | ASTM D256 |
| 5 | MFR / MFI | $MFR = (m \times 600)/t$ | g/10 min | ASTM D1238 |
| 6 | Shear Viscosity | $\eta = \tau/\dot{\gamma}$ | Pa·s | — |
| 7 | CTE | $\alpha = \Delta L/(L_0 \Delta T)$ | K⁻¹ | ASTM E831 |
| 8 | Density | $\rho = m/V$ | g/cm³ | ASTM D792 |
| 9 | Water Absorption | $W_A = (W_{wet} - W_{dry})/W_{dry} \times 100$ | % | ASTM D570 |
| 10 | % Crystallinity | $\chi_c = \rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a) / [\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)]$ | % | — |
| 11 | Dielectric Strength | $E_d = V_b / t$ | kV/mm | ASTM D149 |
| 12 | Volume Resistivity | $\rho_v = R_v \cdot A/t$ | Ω·cm | ASTM D257 |

> [!NOTE]
> **🔑 Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU:**
> - For **$T_g$ measurements** (DSC/DMA), the heating rate is **typically 10°C/min** under **N₂ atmosphere**.
> - For **MFI**, common test conditions are: **2.16 kg load at 190°C (PE)** or **2.16 kg at 230°C (PP/PS)**.
> - For **water absorption**, the standard immersion time is **24 hours at 23°C**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Polymer | Critical Property | Application |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| **HDPE** | High tensile strength, low MFI (high viscosity), chemical resistance | Pipes, containers |
| **LDPE** | High MFI (low viscosity), flexibility, transparency | Films, squeeze bottles |
| **PTFE** | Extremely low coefficient of friction, high $T_m$ (327°C), chemical inertness | Non-stick coatings, gaskets |
| **PMMA** | Optical transparency > 92%, scratch resistance | Aircraft windows, lenses |
| **PVC (rigid)** | High hardness, flame retardancy | Pipes, window profiles |
| **Nylon 6,6** | High $T_m$ (255°C), high tensile strength, wear resistance | Gears, bearings, fibers |
| **Silicone rubber** | Wide $T_g$ range, biocompatibility, flexibility | Medical implants, seals |

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Key Property Equations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Melt Flow Index (MFI) from Capillary Rheometry

**Statement:** Show how the standard MFR equation arises from the basic mass-flow definition.

**Step 1 — Definition Setup:**

The Melt Flow Rate (MFR) is defined as the mass of polymer melt extruded through a capillary die in a fixed reference time period (10 minutes = 600 seconds).

$$MFR = \frac{\text{mass extruded}}{\text{reference time}} \times \frac{600 \text{ s}}{1}$$

**Step 2 — Empirical Expression:**

For an experiment in which the extrudate mass $m$ (grams) is collected over an actual measured time $t$ (seconds), the mass-flow rate per second is:

$$\dot{m}_{sec} = \frac{m}{t} \quad \left[\frac{\text{g}}{\text{s}}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to 10-Minute Basis:**

To convert the per-second rate to a per-10-minute rate, we multiply by 600:

$$MFR = \dot{m}_{sec} \times 600 = \frac{m}{t} \times 600 = \frac{600 \, m}{t}$$

$$\boxed{MFR = \frac{600 \, m}{t} \quad \left[\frac{\text{g}}{10\text{ min}}\right]}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard):**

A polymer sample is extruded through a melt flow indexer at 190°C with a 2.16 kg load. The extrudate collected over 30 seconds weighs 1.25 g. Calculate the MFR.

$$MFR = \frac{600 \times 1.25}{30} = \frac{750}{30} = 25 \text{ g/10 min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: An MFR of 25 g/10 min indicates a **low-viscosity, easy-flowing** polymer — suitable for injection molding of thin-walled products.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Percent Crystallinity from Density Data

**Statement:** Derive the two-phase crystallinity formula from first principles.

**Step 1 — Two-Phase Assumption:**

For a semi-crystalline polymer, the total volume is the sum of amorphous and crystalline volumes:

$$V_s = V_a + V_c$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Masses:**

Using $V = m/\rho$, we get:

$$\frac{m_s}{\rho_s} = \frac{m_a}{\rho_a} + \frac{m_c}{\rho_c}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce Mass Fractions:**

Let the mass fraction of crystalline phase be $X_c$. Then $m_c = X_c \cdot m_s$ and $m_a = (1 - X_c) \cdot m_s$. Substituting:

$$\frac{m_s}{\rho_s} = \frac{(1 - X_c) m_s}{\rho_a} + \frac{X_c \, m_s}{\rho_c}$$

Dividing through by $m_s$:

$$\frac{1}{\rho_s} = \frac{1 - X_c}{\rho_a} + \frac{X_c}{\rho_c}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $X_c$:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho_s} - \frac{1}{\rho_a} = X_c \left(\frac{1}{\rho_c} - \frac{1}{\rho_a}\right)$$

$$X_c = \frac{\frac{1}{\rho_s} - \frac{1}{\rho_a}}{\frac{1}{\rho_c} - \frac{1}{\rho_a}}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent Common Form:**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\rho_a \rho_c \rho_s$:

$$\boxed{X_c = \frac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100\%}$$

**Step 6 — Worked Example:**

For Nylon 6,6: $\rho_c = 1.220 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_a = 1.090 \text{ g/cm}^3$. A sample has $\rho_s = 1.140 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

$$X_c = \frac{1.220 \times (1.140 - 1.090)}{1.140 \times (1.220 - 1.090)} = \frac{1.220 \times 0.050}{1.140 \times 0.130}$$

$$X_c = \frac{0.0610}{0.1482} = 0.4117 = 41.17\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This sample is **41.17% crystalline**, indicating a moderately crystalline Nylon 6,6 — typical for engineering-grade applications requiring a balance of strength and toughness.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Young's Modulus from Stress-Strain Data

**Step 1 — Stress Definition:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 2 — Strain Definition:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

**Step 3 — Hooke's Law in Linear Region:**

In the elastic (linear) region of the stress-strain curve:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F/A_0}{\Delta L/L_0} = \frac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$$

**Step 5 — Experimental Procedure:**

| Measurement | Symbol | Unit |
|-------------|--------|------|
| Applied Force | $F$ | N |
| Original cross-section area | $A_0$ | mm² |
| Original gauge length | $L_0$ | mm |
| Elongation | $\Delta L$ | mm |

**Step 6 — Worked Example:**

A polyethylene specimen with $A_0 = 25 \text{ mm}^2$ and $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$ is subjected to a force of 250 N, producing an elongation of 0.5 mm in the elastic region.

$$\sigma = \frac{250}{25} = 10 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{0.5}{50} = 0.01 = 1\%$$

$$E = \frac{10}{0.01} = 1000 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical $E$ values** for common polymers:
> - **LDPE**: ~0.1–0.3 GPa
> - **HDPE**: ~0.8–1.2 GPa
> - **Nylon 6,6**: ~2–4 GPa
> - **PMMA**: ~3 GPa
> - **Epoxy**: ~3–5 GPa

---
