# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Melt Flow Index (MFI) and state its significance.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Melt Flow Index (MFI)**, also called **Melt Mass-Flow Rate (MFR)** as per ASTM D1238, is defined as the **mass of polymer (in grams) that flows through a standard capillary die (2.095 mm diameter, 8 mm length) in 10 minutes** under a specified temperature and load (commonly 2.16 kg at 190°C for polyethylene).
>
> **Mathematical Expression:**
>
> $$MFR = \frac{600 \times m}{t} \quad [\text{g/10 min}]$$
>
> **Significance:**
> 1. **Indicates processability** — Higher MFI means lower melt viscosity, easier molding/extrusion.
> 2. **Inverse indicator of molecular weight** — Higher MFR implies lower $M_w$.
> 3. **Quality control parameter** in polymer manufacturing.
> 4. **Helps select polymer grade** for specific applications (injection molding vs. blow molding vs. extrusion).

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between $T_g$ and $T_m$ for a polymer.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Glass Transition ($T_g$) | Melting Point ($T_m$) |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **Nature** | Second-order thermodynamic transition | First-order thermodynamic transition |
| **Applies to** | Amorphous and semi-crystalline polymers | Only crystalline regions of polymers |
| **Molecular event** | Onset of large-scale segmental chain mobility | Disruption of 3D crystalline lattice |
| **Volume change** | Change in slope of V vs T curve | Sharp discontinuous volume change |
| **Enthalpy change** | No latent heat ($\Delta H = 0$) | Latent heat of fusion ($\Delta H_f > 0$) |
| **Physical change** | Glassy → Rubbery | Crystalline solid → Viscous liquid |
| **Magnitude** | $T_g \approx \tfrac{2}{3} T_m$ (in Kelvin) | Always higher than $T_g$ (if both exist) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Example**: For **Nylon 6,6**: $T_g \approx 50\text{°C}$, $T_m \approx 255\text{°C}$. For **PS**: $T_g \approx 100\text{°C}$, no $T_m$ (fully amorphous).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `OPTION A`

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

#### **(a)** Explain in detail the stress-strain behavior of polymers. Discuss the differences between brittle, ductile, and tough polymers with appropriate stress-strain curves and examples. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction to Stress-Strain Testing:**

The stress-strain curve is the fundamental output of a tensile test (ASTM D638). It plots **stress ($\sigma$) on the Y-axis** (in MPa) against **strain ($\varepsilon$) on the X-axis** (in %). The curve reveals the entire mechanical response of the polymer from initial loading to final fracture.

**2. Typical Regions of the Stress-Strain Curve:**

| Region | Description | Molecular Interpretation |
|--------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Elastic Region (Hookean)** | Linear $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ relationship; deformation is reversible | Bond stretching and small-angle rotation; chains store elastic energy |
| **Yield Point** | Stress reaches maximum before plastic deformation | Onset of chain slippage and segmental rearrangement |
| **Plastic Region (Necking)** | Stress drops; large irreversible deformation | Chains slide past each other; orientation along tensile axis |
| **Strain Hardening** | Stress rises again before break | Chain alignment and orientation-induced crystallization |
| **Fracture** | Specimen breaks | Covalent bond rupture and chain scission |

**3. Classification of Polymer Behavior:**

**(a) Brittle Polymers** (e.g., PS, PMMA, unmodified epoxy):
- High Young's modulus (steep slope)
- Break occurs in the elastic region — no yield
- Low elongation at break (< 5%)
- Low toughness (small area under curve)
- Failure mode: Sudden, catastrophic

**(b) Ductile Polymers** (e.g., LDPE, PTFE):
- Low Young's modulus (gentle slope)
- Long plastic region with extensive necking
- Very high elongation at break (200–700%)
- Moderate toughness
- Failure mode: Gradual elongation

**(c) Tough Polymers** (e.g., Nylon 6,6, PC, ABS):
- Moderate modulus
- Distinct yield point followed by extensive plastic deformation
- High elongation (50–150%)
- Very high toughness (large area under curve)
- Failure mode: Yield then break after long elongation

**4. Schematic Representation:**

| Type | Curve Shape | Area Under Curve (Toughness) |
|------|-------------|------------------------------|
| Brittle | Linear up to fracture | Small |
| Ductile | Gentle slope, large strain | Moderate |
| Tough | Yield + large plastic region | Large |

**5. Energy Absorption:**

$$\text{Toughness} = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

The area under the stress-strain curve represents the energy absorbed per unit volume before fracture.

---

#### **(b)** A polymer specimen of cross-sectional area 40 mm² and gauge length 60 mm was subjected to a tensile test. The maximum load recorded was 1200 N, and the elongation at break was 18 mm. Calculate: (i) Tensile strength, (ii) Young's modulus if the elastic strain at 600 N load was 0.005, and (iii) Percent elongation at break. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Cross-sectional area $A_0 = 40 \text{ mm}^2$
- Original gauge length $L_0 = 60 \text{ mm}$
- Maximum load $F_{max} = 1200 \text{ N}$
- Elongation at break $\Delta L_b = 18 \text{ mm}$
- At $F = 600$ N, elastic strain $\varepsilon = 0.005$

**(i) Tensile Strength:**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{1200 \text{ N}}{40 \text{ mm}^2} = 30 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = 30 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(ii) Young's Modulus:**

First, calculate the stress at the elastic point:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{600 \text{ N}}{40 \text{ mm}^2} = 15 \text{ MPa}$$

Then apply Hooke's Law:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{15 \text{ MPa}}{0.005}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3000 \text{ MPa} = 3 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(iii) Percent Elongation at Break:**

$$\varepsilon_b = \frac{\Delta L_b}{L_0} \times 100\% = \frac{18 \text{ mm}}{60 \text{ mm}} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_b = 30\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mismatch**: Students frequently write $A_0$ in cm² instead of mm², producing erroneous MPa values. Always convert all dimensions to consistent SI/engineering units **before** substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting the "600" factor in MFR**: Many students compute only $m/t$ instead of $600m/t$, leading to answers 600× too small.
> 3. **Mixing up elastic vs. plastic modulus**: Use the slope **only in the linear elastic region**, not the post-yield region.
> 4. **Crystallinity formula sign error**: Ensure $\rho_c > \rho_s > \rho_a$ — wrong ordering gives negative crystallinity.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert % strain to decimal** when using $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$. A strain of 0.5% = 0.005 (decimal).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `OPTION B`

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

#### **(a)** Discuss in detail the thermal properties of polymers. Explain the concept of glass transition temperature ($T_g$) with a neat sketch of specific volume vs. temperature curve. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction to Thermal Properties:**

Thermal properties describe how a polymer responds to heat. The most fundamental thermal property is the **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)**, which dictates the upper-use temperature for many amorphous plastics.

**2. Specific Volume vs. Temperature Curve (Sketch Description):**

For an amorphous polymer, the specific volume (inverse of density) plotted against temperature shows two distinct linear regions:

- **Region 1 (Below $T_g$)**: Glassy state — slope is smaller (smaller coefficient of thermal expansion)
- **Region 2 (Above $T_g$)**: Rubbery/liquid state — slope is larger (higher CTE)
- **At $T_g$**: Slope changes discontinuously — the transition point

```
Specific
Volume
   |
   |          /  (Rubbery - higher CTE)
   |         /
   |        /
   |-------/ ← $T_g$
   |      /
   |     /    (Glassy - lower CTE)
   |____/
   ------------------------→ Temperature
       0°C    $T_g$
```

For a **semi-crystalline polymer**, the curve additionally shows:
- A **step at $T_g$** (amorphous regions transition)
- A **discontinuity at $T_m$** (crystalline regions melt) — sharp drop in specific volume due to phase change.

**3. Molecular Interpretation of $T_g$:**

Below $T_g$:
- Polymer chains are "frozen" — only vibrational motion
- Free volume is minimal
- Material behaves as a rigid glass

At and above $T_g$:
- Long-range segmental motion becomes possible (5–10 monomer units cooperative motion)
- Free volume increases
- Material transitions to rubbery state

**4. Factors Affecting $T_g$:**

| Factor | Effect on $T_g$ |
|--------|-----------------|
| Chain stiffness (rigid backbone) | Increases $T_g$ |
| Bulky side groups | Increases $T_g$ |
| Polar groups / H-bonding | Increases $T_g$ |
| Crosslinking | Increases $T_g$ |
| Plasticizers | Decreases $T_g$ |
| Flexible chains | Decreases $T_g$ |
| Molecular weight | Slight increase |

**5. Empirical Rule (Boyer-Beaman):**

$$\frac{T_g}{T_m} \approx \frac{2}{3} \quad \text{(both in Kelvin)}$$

**6. Methods to Determine $T_g$:**

- DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry) — ASTM D3418
- DMA (Dynamic Mechanical Analysis) — ASTM D4065
- DTA (Differential Thermal Analysis)
- Dilatometry (specific volume method)

---

#### **(b)** A polyethylene sample is tested in a melt flow indexer at 190°C with a 2.16 kg load. The extrudate collected over 60 seconds weighs 2.5 g. The density of fully crystalline PE is 1.000 g/cm³ and fully amorphous PE is 0.855 g/cm³. The actual sample density is measured as 0.952 g/cm³. Calculate: (i) MFR, and (ii) Percent crystallinity. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Test temperature = 190°C
- Load = 2.16 kg
- Mass of extrudate $m = 2.5$ g
- Collection time $t = 60$ s
- $\rho_c = 1.000 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $\rho_a = 0.855 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $\rho_s = 0.952 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**(i) Melt Flow Rate (MFR):**

$$MFR = \frac{600 \times m}{t} = \frac{600 \times 2.5}{60}$$

$$MFR = \frac{1500}{60}$$

$$\boxed{MFR = 25 \text{ g/10 min}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is a medium-to-high MFR grade, indicating relatively low melt viscosity — suitable for **injection molding applications**.

**(ii) Percent Crystallinity:**

$$X_c = \frac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100\%$$

Substituting:

$$X_c = \frac{1.000 \times (0.952 - 0.855)}{0.952 \times (1.000 - 0.855)} \times 100\%$$

$$X_c = \frac{1.000 \times 0.097}{0.952 \times 0.145} \times 100\%$$

$$X_c = \frac{0.097}{0.13804} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{X_c = 70.27\%}$$

**Interpretation:** This PE sample is **~70% crystalline**, consistent with **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene)**. The high crystallinity contributes to higher density, stiffness, tensile strength, and chemical resistance — making it suitable for pipe, container, and film applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Additional Pitfalls**
> 1. **DSC curve interpretation**: When drawing DSC thermograms, students often mislabel $T_g$ (a step change in baseline) vs. $T_m$ (an endothermic peak). Memorize: $T_g$ = baseline shift; $T_m$ = peak.
> 2. **Confusing MFI with MVR**: MFI is **mass-based** (g/10 min); MVR is **volume-based** (cm³/10 min). They are **not the same**.
> 3. **Crystallinity sign issues**: If you get a negative crystallinity, you've likely swapped $\rho_c$ and $\rho_a$ values — crystalline density must be higher.
> 4. **ASTM standard wrong application**: Don't cite ASTM D638 for impact testing — that's ASTM D256 (Izod/Charpy).
> 5. **HDT ≠ $T_g$ ≠ Vicat**: HDT is measured under load (0.45 or 1.82 MPa); Vicat uses a needle penetration method. They are different test methods and give different values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2: Properties**

### 🎯 Core Concepts to Memorize:
- [ ] Polymer properties are classified into **7 categories**: Mechanical, Thermal, Rheological, Physical, Chemical, Electrical, Optical.
- [ ] **$T_g$** applies to amorphous regions; **$T_m$** applies only to crystalline regions.
- [ ] For semi-crystalline polymers: $T_m > T_g$ (always).
- [ ] **Boyer-Beaman rule**: $T_g/T_m \approx 2/3$ (in Kelvin).
- [ ] MFI is **inversely** proportional to molecular weight and melt viscosity.

### 🔢 Must-Know Formulas:
- [ ] $\sigma_t = F_{max}/A_0$
- [ ] $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$
- [ ] $MFR = 600m/t$ — **never forget the 600**
- [ ] $X_c = \rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a) / [\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)] \times 100$
- [ ] $W_A = (W_{wet} - W_{dry})/W_{dry} \times 100$

### 📋 Key ASTM Standards to Recall:
- [ ] **ASTM D638** → Tensile properties
- [ ] **ASTM D256** → Impact strength (Izod/Charpy)
- [ ] **ASTM D2240** → Hardness (Shore A/D)
- [ ] **ASTM D1238** → Melt Flow Rate
- [ ] **ASTM D648** → Heat Deflection Temperature
- [ ] **ASTM D570** → Water Absorption
- [ ] **ASTM D792** → Density
- [ ] **ASTM D257** → Electrical Resistivity
- [ ] **ASTM D149** → Dielectric Strength
- [ ] **ASTM D3418** → $T_g$ by DSC

### 🧠 Polymer Behavior Reminders:
- [ ] **Brittle** (PS, PMMA): High modulus, low elongation, no yield.
- [ ] **Ductile** (LDPE, PTFE): Low modulus, very high elongation.
- [ ] **Tough** (Nylon, PC, ABS): Distinct yield + large plastic region.
- [ ] **Polymers are viscoelastic** — they exhibit both viscous and elastic responses (time-dependent).
- [ ] **Most polymers are shear-thinning** (pseudoplastic) — viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate.

### 🏭 Engineering Application Match:
- [ ] **High MFI → Injection molding**
- [ ] **Low MFI → Blow molding, extrusion**
- [ ] **High $T_g$ → Heat-resistant applications**
- [ ] **Low water absorption → Marine/outdoor use**
- [ ] **High crystallinity → Better chemical resistance & strength**

### ⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid:
- [ ] Don't confuse **toughness** (energy absorption) with **strength** (peak stress).
- [ ] Don't confuse **MFI** with **viscosity** — they are inversely related.
- [ ] Don't apply $T_m$ to fully amorphous polymers (PS, PMMA, PC) — they only have $T_g$.
- [ ] Always include **units** in final answers — KTU evaluators deduct marks for missing units.
- [ ] Always show **derivation steps** in 14-mark questions — partial credit depends on showing work.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: concept of order in fibres and polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** concept of order in fibres and polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept of Order (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> The *concept of order* in fibres and polymers refers to the **degree of structural regularity** exhibited by macromolecular chains across multiple hierarchical length scales — ranging from atomic-scale stereoregularity (configurational order), through segmental chain conformation (conformational order), to supra-molecular packing (crystallinity, orientation, and mesophase order). Higher order ⇒ greater anisotropy, higher tensile strength, lower elongation, and improved thermal/chemical resistance.

In polymer science, **order** is *not* a single quantity but a **multi-level hierarchy**. A polymer sample is rarely fully crystalline or fully amorphous — it exists as a **semicrystalline** continuum where crystalline lamellae are embedded in an amorphous matrix. The relative proportion and orientation of these regions govern virtually every macroscopic property of the material.

## 1.2 Hierarchical Levels of Order

| Level | Length Scale | Type of Order | Example |
|-------|--------------|---------------|---------|
| **Level 1** | 0.1 – 1 nm | *Configurational order* (tacticity) | Isotactic, syndiotactic, atactic PP |
| **Level 2** | 1 – 10 nm | *Conformational order* (chain shape) | Helix, zigzag, random coil |
| **Level 3** | 10 – 100 nm | *Crystallographic / Lamellar order* | Folded-chain lamellae |
| **Level 4** | 0.1 – 10 µm | *Supramolecular / Morphological order* | Spherulites, fibrils |
| **Level 5** | >10 µm | *Macroscopic / Textural order* | Fibre axis orientation, skin-core |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Crowd in a Stadium"**
> 
> Imagine a stadium full of people (polymer chains):
> - **Atactic / amorphous polymer** = Random crowd milling about, no formation, no pattern. They can flow easily (rubbery behaviour).
> - **Isotactic + crystalline polymer** = Crowd arranged in tight, organised rows (like spectators in tier seating). They move together, resist pulling apart, and have predictable direction-dependent properties.
> - **Drawn fibre** = The same organised crowd, but *all facing the same direction along the field*. Now the rows are not just ordered internally, but **aligned** — pulling along the rows is very hard (high tensile strength), but pulling sideways is easy (anisotropy).
> 
> **Order** is the *degree of crowd organisation*. **Orientation** is the *direction in which the rows face*.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Density of fully crystalline polyethylene (theoretical):** $\rho_c = \mathbf{1.00 \, g/cm^3}$
- **Density of fully amorphous polyethylene:** $\rho_a = \mathbf{0.855 \, g/cm^3}$
- **Typical crystallinity range for commercial polymers:** 0 % (atactic polystyrene) to **~95 %** (ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene, UHMWPE fibres such as Dyneema®)
- **Wavelength of Cu-Kα X-ray radiation (XRD standard):** $\lambda = \mathbf{1.5418 \, \text{Å}}$
- **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** marks the *loss of conformational order* in the amorphous phase.
- **Melting temperature ($T_m$)** marks the *loss of crystallographic order* (3D lattice).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board High-Yield Concept**
> The difference $T_m - T_g$ is a direct empirical indicator of the *breadth of the usable semi-ordered regime* for a polymer. A large $T_m - T_g$ window (e.g., PET: $T_m \approx 260 \, °C$, $T_g \approx 75 \, °C$) signals high potential crystallinity and processability for fibre spinning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Hierarchies of Order — Explained

### 2.1.1 Configurational Order (Tacticity)
*Configurational order* refers to the **spatial arrangement of substituents around stereocentres** in the repeat unit. For a vinyl polymer $\ce{-(CH_2-CHR)_n-}$, three tactic forms exist:

- **Isotactic** — All R-groups on the *same* side of the chain backbone when drawn in planar zigzag. Chain can crystallise.
- **Syndiotactic** — R-groups alternate regularly. Chain can crystallise.
- **Atactic** — R-groups random. Cannot crystallise (e.g., atactic PS → amorphous).

$$P_i = \text{fraction of meso (m) dyads}, \qquad P_s = \text{fraction of racemo (r) dyads}$$

$$P_i + P_s = 1$$

### 2.1.2 Conformational Order
*Conformation* refers to the **instantaneous spatial arrangement of atoms achievable by rotation around single bonds** (without breaking any bond). Common regular conformations:

- **Planar zigzag (all-*trans*)** — Found in polyethylene, polyamides (nylon 6,6). Periodicity $\approx 2.54 \, \text{Å}$.
- **Helical (3₁, 2₁, etc.)** — Found in isotactic polypropylene ($3_1$ helix, pitch $6.65 \, \text{Å}$), PTFE ($13_6$ helix).
- **Random coil** — Amorphous polymer melt or glass.

### 2.1.3 Crystallographic Order (Crystallinity)
The regular 3-D packing of polymer chains into a **unit cell**. Common polymer crystal systems:

- **Orthorhombic** — Polyethylene, PET, nylon 6,6
- **Monoclinic** — Polypropylene, polyoxyethylene
- **Triclinic** — Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, β-phase)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Polymers are **never 100 % crystalline**. Even highly ordered polymers like UHMWPE have crystallinity $\leq 95$ % because of *chain folding defects*, *chain ends*, and *entanglements* trapped in the amorphous regions.

### 2.1.4 Morphological Order
The supra-molecular architecture formed by the self-assembly of crystalline lamellae:

- **Lamellae** — Thin platelet crystals (~10 nm thick).
- **Spherulites** — Spherical aggregates of radial lamellae (typical of bulk crystallised polymers, 1 µm – 1 mm).
- **Fibrils / Shish-kebab** — Oriented crystals formed under flow or stress (typical of fibres).
- **Fringed micelle** (fringed fibril) — Old model, partially correct for fibres.

### 2.1.5 Orientation Order (in Fibres)
The alignment of polymer chains (or their crystallographic axes) along a reference direction — the **fibre axis**. Quantified by the **Hermans orientation function** $f$:

$$f = \frac{3 \langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the chain axis and the fibre (draw) axis. $f$ ranges from **0** (isotropic) to **1** (perfect alignment).

## 2.2 Why Order Matters in Fibres

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A textile fibre needs **mechanical strength + flexibility + processability**. Order delivers *strength* (covalent bonds aligned along load axis), and the amorphous fraction delivers *flexibility and dye uptake*. The art of fibre engineering is **optimising the balance** between crystalline order (strength) and amorphous content (toughness, dyeability).

### Engineering Examples

| Fibre | Crystallinity | Orientation $f$ | Typical Tensile Strength |
|-------|---------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Cotton (cellulose I) | ~70 % | ~0.85 | 0.3 – 0.8 GPa |
| Silk (Fibroin, β-sheet) | ~60 % | ~0.95 | 0.5 – 1.0 GPa |
| Nylon 6,6 (drawn) | ~50 % | ~0.90 | 0.5 – 1.0 GPa |
| Kevlar 49 (PPTA) | ~95 % | ~0.99 | 2.8 – 3.6 GPa |
| Dyneema (UHMWPE) | ~95 % | ~0.99 | 2.5 – 3.5 GPa |
| Glass fibre | 100 % amorphous | N/A | 3.5 GPa |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | Degree of crystallinity (density method) | % |
| 2 | $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_m}{\Delta H_m^0} \times 100$ | Degree of crystallinity (DSC, enthalpy method) | % |
| 3 | $\Delta H_m^0$ (PE) $= 293 \, \text{J/g}$, (PP) $= 207 \, \text{J/g}$, (PET) $= 140 \, \text{J/g}$ | Heat of fusion of 100 % crystalline polymer | J/g |
| 4 | $f = \dfrac{3 \langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$ | Hermans orientation function | dimensionless |
| 5 | $n_d = \dfrac{n_\parallel - n_\perp}{\bar{n}}$ | Birefringence (optical anisotropy measure) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\lambda_{draw} = \dfrac{L_{after}}{L_{before}}$ | Draw ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sigma = E \, \varepsilon$ (Hooke's law, along chain) | Stress–strain along oriented direction | Pa |
| 8 | $d_{hkl} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \sin \theta}$ | Bragg's law (XRD) | Å |
| 9 | $\Delta n \propto f (n_\parallel^c - n_\perp^c)$ | Birefringence–orientation relation | dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_m = T_m^0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{2 \sigma_e}{\Delta H_f \, l} \right)$ | Gibbs–Thomson melting point depression by lamellar thickness $l$ | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Equation 10 (Gibbs–Thomson) is **the single most-tested formula** in KTU 2024 scheme questions on "concept of order". It links *morphological order* (lamellar thickness $l$) to *thermal behaviour* ($T_m$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace composites** — Carbon and Kevlar fibres exploit near-perfect orientation order ($f \to 1$) to achieve specific strength 5–10× that of steel.
- **Biomedical sutures** — Bioresorbable fibres (PLLA, PGA) are drawn to align chains and accelerate in-vivo strength loss.
- **Smart textiles** — Shape-memory polymer fibres rely on oriented amorphous domains whose relaxation upon heating drives actuation.
- **Recycling & sorting** — XRD/DSC fingerprint of crystalline order is used in textile recycling lines to separate PET from cotton.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Degree of Crystallinity by Density Method

**Statement to Prove:** The bulk density $\rho$ of a semicrystalline polymer is a linear combination of crystalline and amorphous densities:

$$\rho = X_c \, \rho_c + (1 - X_c) \, \rho_a$$

where $X_c$ is the volume (or mass, depending on convention) fraction crystallinity. KTU standard convention is **mass fraction**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1.** Consider a unit mass of semicrystalline polymer containing mass $X_c$ of crystalline phase and $(1 - X_c)$ of amorphous phase. So,

$$m_c = X_c, \qquad m_a = 1 - X_c$$

**Step 2.** The volume occupied by the crystalline phase is,

$$V_c = \dfrac{m_c}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{X_c}{\rho_c}$$

**Step 3.** The volume occupied by the amorphous phase is,

$$V_a = \dfrac{m_a}{\rho_a} = \dfrac{1 - X_c}{\rho_a}$$

**Step 4.** Total volume of the sample is $V = V_c + V_a$, so,

$$V = \dfrac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \dfrac{1 - X_c}{\rho_a}$$

**Step 5.** The bulk (measured) density is the reciprocal of specific volume,

$$\rho = \dfrac{1}{V} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \dfrac{1 - X_c}{\rho_a}}$$

**Step 6.** Invert both sides to obtain an explicit relation between $X_c$ and $\rho$. Multiply numerator and denominator by $\rho_c \rho_a$:

$$\rho = \dfrac{\rho_c \rho_a}{\rho_a X_c + \rho_c (1 - X_c)}$$

**Step 7.** Rearrange for $X_c$. Take reciprocal:

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{\rho_a X_c + \rho_c (1 - X_c)}{\rho_c \rho_a}$$

**Step 8.** Expand the numerator and group the $X_c$ terms:

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{(\rho_c - \rho_a) X_c}{\rho_c \rho_a} + \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_c \rho_a} = \dfrac{(\rho_c - \rho_a) X_c}{\rho_c \rho_a} + \dfrac{1}{\rho_a}$$

**Step 9.** Subtract $1/\rho_a$ from both sides:

$$\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_a} = \dfrac{(\rho_c - \rho_a) X_c}{\rho_c \rho_a}$$

**Step 10.** Multiply both sides by $\rho \rho_c \rho_a / (\rho_c - \rho_a)$:

$$X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho (\rho_c - \rho_a)}$$

$$\boxed{X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100 \,\%}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Expected Format)

> **Q:** A polyethylene sample has bulk density $\rho = 0.965 \, \text{g/cm}^3$. Given $\rho_c = 1.000 \, \text{g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_a = 0.855 \, \text{g/cm}^3$, calculate the % crystallinity.

**Solution:**

$$X_c = \dfrac{1.000 \times (0.965 - 0.855)}{0.965 \times (1.000 - 0.855)}$$

$$X_c = \dfrac{1.000 \times 0.110}{0.965 \times 0.145} = \dfrac{0.110}{0.1399}$$

$$\boxed{X_c \approx 0.786 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 78.6 \,\%}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Hermans Orientation Function from Birefringence

**Statement to Prove:** The Hermans orientation function $f$ is bounded in $[-0.5, 1]$ and equals zero for an isotropic sample.

### Derivation

**Step 1.** Define the orientation distribution function $N(\theta)$ such that $N(\theta) \, d\theta$ is the fraction of chain segments whose axis makes an angle between $\theta$ and $\theta + d\theta$ with the reference (fibre) axis. Normalisation gives,

$$\int_0^{\pi} N(\theta) \sin \theta \, d\theta = 1$$

**Step 2.** The average of $\cos^2 \theta$ is,

$$\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = \int_0^{\pi} \cos^2 \theta \, N(\theta) \sin \theta \, d\theta$$

**Step 3.** For a perfectly aligned sample, $\theta = 0$ for all segments, so $\cos^2 \theta = 1$. For a perfectly isotropic sample, by symmetry $\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = 1/3$.

**Step 4.** Hermans defined the orientation function as the deviation from isotropy, scaled to be unity for perfect alignment:

$$f = \dfrac{\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1/3}{1 - 1/3} = \dfrac{3 \langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

**Step 5.** **Verification of bounds:**
- If $\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = 1$ (perfect alignment): $f = (3 - 1)/2 = 1$ ✓
- If $\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = 1/3$ (isotropic): $f = (1 - 1)/2 = 0$ ✓
- If $\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = 0$ (chains perpendicular to reference): $f = -1/2$ ✓

$$\boxed{f \in [-0.5, \, 1]}$$

### Worked Example

> **Q:** A polyester fibre exhibits birefringence $\Delta n = 0.18$. The intrinsic birefringence of perfectly oriented PET is $\Delta n^0 = 0.22$. Calculate the Hermans orientation function.

**Solution:** For low birefringence samples, $\Delta n \approx \Delta n^0 \cdot f$.

$$f = \dfrac{\Delta n}{\Delta n^0} = \dfrac{0.18}{0.22} = 0.818$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.82 \;\; (\text{highly oriented})}$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Gibbs–Thomson Equation for Lamellar Thickness

**Step 1.** Consider a polymer crystal lamella of thickness $l$ with two parallel fold surfaces, each of surface free energy $\sigma_e$ (in J/m²). The extra surface free energy per mole of repeat units is,

$$\Delta G_{\text{surf}} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_e \, V_m}{l}$$

where $V_m$ is the molar volume of the repeat unit.

**Step 2.** At equilibrium melting $T_m$, the bulk free energy of fusion vanishes. For an infinitesimally thin lamella, $\Delta G_{\text{fusion}} = -\Delta G_{\text{surf}}$.

$$\Delta H_f \left( 1 - \dfrac{T_m}{T_m^0} \right) = -\dfrac{2 \sigma_e V_m}{l}$$

**Step 3.** Rearrange for $T_m$:

$$T_m = T_m^0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{2 \sigma_e V_m}{\Delta H_f \, l} \right)$$

$$\boxed{T_m = T_m^0 \left( 1 - \dfrac{2 \sigma_e}{\Delta H_f \, l} \right) \quad \text{for mass basis } (\Delta H_f \text{ in J/g})}$$

**Step 4.** **Interpretation:** Thinner lamellae ⇒ lower melting point ⇒ *less morphological order* at the lamellar level. This is why **annealing increases $T_m$** (it thickens lamellae, increasing $l$) — a direct demonstration of *increased morphological order*.

### Worked Example

> **Q:** Polyethylene lamellar thickness $l = 15 \, \text{nm}$. Given $T_m^0 = 414 \, \text{K}$, $\sigma_e = 90 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{J/m}^2$, $\Delta H_f = 293 \, \text{J/g}$, $\rho_c = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate $T_m$.

**Solution:** First convert $\Delta H_f$ to volumetric basis: $\Delta H_f^{\text{vol}} = \rho_c \Delta H_f = 1000 \times 293 = 2.93 \times 10^5 \, \text{J/m}^3$.

$$T_m = 414 \left( 1 - \dfrac{2 \times 0.090}{2.93 \times 10^5 \times 15 \times 10^{-9}} \right)$$

$$T_m = 414 \left( 1 - \dfrac{0.180}{4.395} \right) = 414 \times 0.9590$$

$$\boxed{T_m \approx 397 \, \text{K} \;\; (124 \, °C)}$$

This matches the experimentally observed melting point of solution-grown PE single crystals — confirming the link between *lamellar thickness* and *morphological order*.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Each "Order Level" Affects a Property

| Property Affected | Configurational | Conformational | Crystallographic | Morphological | Orientation |
|-------------------|-----------------|----------------|------------------|---------------|-------------|
| Density $\rho$ | Indirect | Weak | **Strong ↑** | Moderate | Weak |
| Tensile strength $\sigma_t$ | Indirect | Weak | **Strong ↑** | Moderate | **Very strong ↑** |
| Elongation at break $\varepsilon_b$ | Indirect | Weak | ↓ | ↓ | **Very strong ↓** |
| Optical clarity | Indirect | Indirect | ↓ (scattering) | ↓ | ↓ |
| Dye uptake | Indirect | Indirect | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |
| Chemical resistance | Indirect | Indirect | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Melting point $T_m$ | Indirect | Indirect | **Strong ↑** | Moderate (lamellar $l$) | Weak |
| Glass transition $T_g$ | Indirect | Indirect | ↑ (constraint) | Weak | Weak |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** A common student misconception is that all five orders are equivalent. They are *not* — **orientation has a much larger effect on mechanical properties than crystallinity**, while **crystallinity has a much larger effect on chemical and thermal resistance**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — Hierarchical Order in Polymers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Level 1 - Configurational Order - Tacticity Isotactic Syndiotactic Atactic"] --> nodeB["Level 2 - Conformational Order - Chain Shape Planar Zigzag Helix Random Coil"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Level 3 - Crystallographic Order - Unit Cell Lamellar Folding 3D Lattice"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Level 4 - Morphological Order - Spherulites Fibrils Shish Kebab Fringed Micelles"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Level 5 - Macroscopic Orientation - Fibre Axis Draw Ratio Skin Core"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Engineering Properties - Strength Modulus Dye Uptake Thermal Resistance"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Quality Metrics - Crystallinity Xc Hermans Function f Birefringence dn"]
    nodeA --> nodeH["Measurement Techniques - NMR XRD DSC Polarised Optical Microscopy"]
    nodeB --> nodeH
    nodeC --> nodeI["Wide Angle X Ray Scattering WAXS"]
    nodeD --> nodeJ["Small Angle X Ray Scattering SAXS POM"]
    nodeE --> nodeK["Birefringence Sonic Modulus XRD Pole Figure"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["Concept of Order - KTU Module Experiment 2"]
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeG --> nodeL
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Order Generation in Fibre Spinning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM["Polymer Melt - Random Coils - Amorphous - Isotropic f=0"] --> nodeN["Spinline - Flow Alignment - Incipient Orientation"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Solid Fibre As Spun - Low Crystallinity - f ~ 0.5"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["Drawing Stage - Cold or Hot Draw - Chain Alignment"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["Annealing - Lamellar Thickening - Crystallinity Increase"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["Finished Fibre - High Crystallinity - f ~ 0.95"]
    nodeM --> nodeS["Slow Cooling - Crystallisation Nucleation"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Spherulitic Morphology - Bulk Casting"]
    nodeO --> nodeU["Skin Core Structure - Shear Induced Orientation"]
    nodeR --> nodeV["Application - Textile Composite Rope"]
    nodeT --> nodeV
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree: Selecting a Fibre Based on Required Order

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeW["Required Property Profile"] --> nodeX{"Primary Need"}
    nodeX -- High Strength + Low Weight --> nodeY["Kevlar PPTA - Liquid Crystalline Order - f near 1"]
    nodeX -- High Chemical Resistance + Marine Rope --> nodeZ["UHMWPE Dyneema - Gel Spun - High Crystallinity + Orientation"]
    nodeX -- Moisture Management + Comfort --> nodeAA["Cotton Cellulose I - Natural Hierarchical Order"]
    nodeX -- High Temperature Resistance --> nodeAB["Nomex Aramid - Hydrogen Bonded Sheet Order"]
    nodeX -- Biodegradable + Medical --> nodeAC["PLLA PGA - Moderate Crystallinity - Drawn for Strength"]
    nodeY --> nodeAD["Concept of Order Drives Selection"]
    nodeZ --> nodeAD
    nodeAA --> nodeAD
    nodeAB --> nodeAD
    nodeAC --> nodeAD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Hint:** When asked *"Why is Kevlar so strong?"*, the correct layered answer is:
> 1. **Configurational order** — *para*-linked aromatic amide backbone.
> 2. **Conformational order** — Rigid rod-like conformation, no random coil.
> 3. **Crystallographic order** — Hydrogen-bonded sheets, near-perfect 3-D order.
> 4. **Orientation order** — Liquid-crystalline spinning aligns chains to $f \to 1$.
> 
> All four levels contribute — and this is what makes the question a high-weightage "concept of order" answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define *configurational order* in polymers. How does it differ from *conformational order*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Configurational order** refers to the *spatial arrangement of atoms or substituents that cannot be altered without breaking chemical bonds* — typically the stereochemistry (tacticity) at chiral centres in the repeat unit. *Example:* isotactic polypropylene has all $\ce{-CH3}$ groups on the same side of the backbone — a high level of configurational order.
> 
> **Conformational order** refers to the *spatial arrangement achievable by rotation around single bonds*, without breaking any bond. *Example:* the planar zigzag (*all-trans*) versus helical (*tg*) conformations of polyethylene versus isotactic polypropylene.
> 
> **Key difference:** Configurational order is *fixed* by synthesis (requires bond breaking to change). Conformational order is *dynamic* and changes with temperature, solvent, and stress.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is the *Hermans orientation function* $f$? What do the values $f = 0$, $f = 1$, and $f = -0.5$ signify?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Hermans orientation function is defined as,
> 
> $$f = \dfrac{3 \langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$
> 
> where $\theta$ is the angle between the chain axis and the reference (draw) axis.
> 
> - **$f = 0$** ⟹ *isotropic* sample — chains randomly oriented (e.g., undrawn melt, atactic polymer melt).
> - **$f = 1$** ⟹ *perfect alignment* — all chains parallel to the reference axis (e.g., theoretical UHMWPE fibre).
> - **$f = -0.5$** ⟹ *chains perpendicular* to reference axis (e.g., transverse direction in a uniaxially drawn sheet).
> 
> **Range:** $f \in [-0.5, \, +1]$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Module Experiment 2, KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the *hierarchical levels of order* in polymers with suitable examples for each level. How does *orientation* differ from *crystallinity* in determining the mechanical properties of a drawn fibre?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polyethylene terephthalate (PET) sample has density $1.380 \, \text{g/cm}^3$. Given $\rho_c = 1.455 \, \text{g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_a = 1.335 \, \text{g/cm}^3$, calculate the degree of crystallinity. Comment on how this value would change if the fibre were subjected to a 4× cold draw.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Hierarchical Order [7 marks]

The five hierarchical levels of order in polymers are:

**(i) Configurational Order [1 mark]** — Stereoregularity at chiral centres. *Example:* Isotactic vs atactic polypropylene. Only stereoregular forms can crystallise.

**(ii) Conformational Order [1 mark]** — The regular shapes polymer chains adopt via bond rotation. *Example:* PE adopts *all-trans* planar zigzag (period $2.54 \, \text{Å}$); iPP adopts $3_1$ helix.

**(iii) Crystallographic Order [1 mark]** — 3-D periodic packing of chains into unit cells. *Example:* Orthorhombic unit cell of PE ($a = 7.4 \, \text{Å}$, $b = 4.93 \, \text{Å}$, $c = 2.54 \, \text{Å}$).

**(iv) Morphological Order [1 mark]** — Supramolecular assembly of lamellae into spherulites, fibrils, or shish-kebab structures. *Example:* Bulk-crystallised PP showing characteristic Maltese-cross pattern under crossed polarisers.

**(v) Orientation Order [1 mark]** — Directional alignment of chains along a reference axis (e.g., fibre axis). *Example:* Drawn PET fibre with $f \approx 0.9$.

**Orientation vs Crystallinity in Drawn Fibres [2 marks]:**

| Aspect | Crystallinity | Orientation |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| What it measures | 3-D packing regularity | Angular alignment along axis |
| Effect on tensile strength | Moderate (increases with order) | **Dominant** (rises sharply with $f$) |
| Effect on modulus | Moderate | **Dominant** |
| Effect on elongation | Decreases | **Decreases sharply** |
| Required for dyeing | Lower crystallinity needed | Lower orientation preferred |

**Key conclusion:** *Orientation* is the single most important parameter governing the *tensile* properties of a drawn fibre. Two fibres of identical crystallinity can have vastly different tensile strength depending on $f$. *Crystallinity* governs *chemical and thermal* resistance more than mechanical anisotropy.

#### Part (b) — Degree of Crystallinity Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:** $\rho = 1.380 \, \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_c = 1.455 \, \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_a = 1.335 \, \text{g/cm}^3$.

**Apply the formula** [3 marks for setup, 2 marks for computation, 2 marks for comment]:

$$X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$$

**Substituting values** [1 mark each step]:

$$X_c = \dfrac{1.455 \times (1.380 - 1.335)}{1.380 \times (1.455 - 1.335)}$$

$$X_c = \dfrac{1.455 \times 0.045}{1.380 \times 0.120}$$

$$X_c = \dfrac{0.06548}{0.1656} = 0.3954$$

$$\boxed{X_c \approx 39.5 \,\%}$$

**Comment on cold drawing (4×) [2 marks]:** Cold drawing at 4× draw ratio will *substantially increase* both crystallinity and orientation. The crystallinity is expected to rise to approximately 45 – 55 % due to *strain-induced crystallisation*. The Hermans orientation function $f$ will rise sharply from $\approx 0.3$ (as-spun) to $\approx 0.85$ – 0.90 (drawn). The combined effect: tensile strength increases 3–5×, elongation drops from ~200 % to ~20 %.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme Supplementary]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a suitable diagram, describe the *fringed micelle* and *folded-chain lamella* models of polymer morphology. Which model is more appropriate for highly drawn fibres, and why?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define *birefringence*. The birefringence of an undrawn PET film is $\Delta n = 0.040$ and of a 4× drawn PET fibre is $\Delta n = 0.180$. The intrinsic birefringence of perfectly oriented PET is $\Delta n^0 = 0.220$. Calculate the Hermans orientation function in both cases and comment.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Morphological Models [7 marks]

**Fringed Micelle Model [3 marks]:**
Proposed by Hermann, Gerngross, and Abitz (1932). Concept: a single polymer chain participates simultaneously in *several* crystalline micelles (small ordered domains), with the intervening chain segments existing in the amorphous matrix. The chain is thus "fringed" between crystallites. *Diagram (described verbally):* a disordered mass of long lines (chains), with regular short segments (micelles) marked by hatching, where each line enters and exits multiple micelles.

*Applicability:* Better suited to *partially crystalline bulk polymers* with low crystallinity and slow crystallisation.

**Folded-Chain Lamella Model [3 marks]:**
Proposed by Keller, Fischer, and Till (1957) from solution-grown single crystals. Concept: a polymer chain folds back on itself periodically (every 10–20 nm), forming thin lamellar platelets ~10 nm thick. The chain axis is *perpendicular* to the lamellar surface. Stacks of lamellae grow radially to form spherulites.

*Applicability:* Best for *solution-grown or melt-crystallised* polymers with lamellar thickness controlled by supercooling.

**Which model suits drawn fibres? [1 mark]**

The *folded-chain lamella + fibrillar extension under draw* model is more appropriate for drawn fibres. Under drawing, lamellae tilt and break into *microfibrils* consisting of alternating crystalline blocks and amorphous tie-molecules. This *microfibrillar* model (Peterlin, 1971) explains why drawn fibres have high strength along the draw axis — the *tie molecules* bear the load between crystalline blocks.

#### Part (b) — Birefringence and Orientation [7 marks]

**Definition [1 mark]:** Birefringence $\Delta n = n_\parallel - n_\perp$ is the difference in refractive indices for light polarised parallel and perpendicular to the orientation direction. It is a direct measure of *optical anisotropy*, and hence of *molecular orientation*.

**Relation [1 mark]:** $\Delta n \approx \Delta n^0 \cdot f$ for low–moderate orientation.

**Case 1 — Undrawn Film [2 marks]:**

$$f_1 = \dfrac{\Delta n}{\Delta n^0} = \dfrac{0.040}{0.220} = 0.182$$

This indicates a *lowly oriented* film — chains are largely in random-coil conformation with only modest alignment from extrusion flow.

**Case 2 — 4× Drawn Fibre [2 marks]:**

$$f_2 = \dfrac{\Delta n}{\Delta n^0} = \dfrac{0.180}{0.220} = 0.818$$

This is a *highly oriented* fibre — chains closely aligned along the fibre axis.

**Comment [1 mark]:** Drawing at 4× has increased the orientation function by a factor of ~4.5. This explains the experimentally observed 4–6× increase in tensile modulus and strength upon drawing PET fibres. The relationship $\Delta n \propto f$ is the most common industrial method of *online monitoring* of fibre orientation in spinning lines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "tacticity" with "crystallinity".** Tacticity is a *configurational* property of the chain. Crystallinity is a *supramolecular* packing property. A polymer can be isotactic but *not* crystallise (e.g., if cooled too fast — kinetically trapped). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion.
> 
> 2. **Stating "polymers are 100 % crystalline"** in highly oriented fibres. *No polymer is fully crystalline.* Always state the actual % (e.g., "95 % for UHMWPE"). Loss: 1 mark.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting units in crystallinity formula.** KTU expects the *density method* answer to clearly state $\rho_c$ and $\rho_a$ values and units ($\text{g/cm}^3$). Loss: 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Reporting Hermans function outside $[-0.5, +1]$.** This signals a calculation error. Loss: 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up "amorphous" and "isotropic".** Amorphous = no 3-D order. Isotropic = same properties in all directions. An *oriented amorphous* polymer (e.g., drawn atactic PS) is amorphous *but anisotropic*. Examiners reward the distinction with 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision — "Concept of Order in Fibres and Polymers"**
> 
> ✅ **Order is hierarchical**, not a single concept — five levels: *configurational, conformational, crystallographic, morphological, orientation*.
> 
> ✅ **Configurational order** = stereoregularity (tacticity). Fixed by synthesis.
> 
> ✅ **Conformational order** = chain shape (zigzag, helix, coil). Dynamic.
> 
> ✅ **Crystallographic order** = 3-D lattice packing (orthorhombic, monoclinic, etc.).
> 
> ✅ **Morphological order** = spherulites, lamellae, fibrils, shish-kebab.
> 
> ✅ **Orientation order** = alignment of chain axes along fibre axis; quantified by **Hermans function $f$**.
> 
> ✅ **Degree of crystallinity formula (density):** $X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$.
> 
> ✅ **Degree of crystallinity formula (DSC):** $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_m}{\Delta H_m^0} \times 100$.
> 
> ✅ **Hermans orientation:** $f = \dfrac{3 \langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$; range $[-0.5, +1]$.
> 
> ✅ **Birefringence relation:** $\Delta n \approx \Delta n^0 \cdot f$.
> 
> ✅ **Gibbs–Thomson:** $T_m = T_m^0 \left(1 - \dfrac{2 \sigma_e}{\Delta H_f l}\right)$ — thinner lamellae ⇒ lower $T_m$.
> 
> ✅ **No polymer is 100 % crystalline** — max ~95 % (UHMWPE).
> 
> ✅ **Orientation $\gg$ crystallinity** for tensile strength; **crystallinity $\gg$ orientation** for chemical/thermal resistance.
> 
> ✅ **Drawing increases both $X_c$ and $f$** — drawing is the primary industrial method to *engineer order* in synthetic fibres.
> 
> ✅ **Natural fibres** (cotton, silk) achieve order via biological self-assembly; **synthetic fibres** (Kevlar, Dyneema) achieve it via *liquid-crystalline spinning* or *gel spinning*.
> 
> ✅ **Kevlar's exceptional strength** comes from *all four* levels of order (configurational + conformational + crystallographic + orientation) — a textbook case study.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: crystallinity and orientation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** crystallinity and orientation

# Crystallinity and Orientation in Polymers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Crystallinity** in polymers refers to the degree of structural order within a polymer matrix, characterized by the presence of ordered three-dimensional arrays of polymer chains (crystallites or lamellae) coexisting with disordered (amorphous) regions. It is quantified as the **degree of crystallinity (%Xc)**, defined as the weight fraction or volume fraction of the crystalline phase in a semi-crystalline polymer.

**Orientation** refers to the preferential alignment of polymer chains or crystalline lamellae along a specific direction, typically introduced by mechanical deformation (drawing, stretching, extrusion, or flow). Quantified using the **Hermans orientation function (f)** which varies from 0 (random/isotropic) to 1 (perfect alignment along reference axis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Semi-crystalline polymers like **HDPE, PP, Nylon-6, Nylon-66, PET, and POM** exhibit partial crystallinity. Amorphous polymers like **PS, PMMA, and PC** have no crystalline order. Crystallinity significantly affects mechanical strength, optical clarity, density, and thermal behavior.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti dumped haphazardly onto a plate — this is an **amorphous polymer** where chains are tangled randomly. Now imagine the same spaghetti after you've neatly aligned the strands parallel to each other, bundled them into tight packets, and stacked them in an orderly fashion — this represents a **crystalline oriented polymer**.

**Real-world analogy**:
- **Crystallinity** = The fraction of bookshelf that is neatly organized vs. the scattered papers.
- **Orientation** = Whether the organized books all stand upright facing one direction or are rotated randomly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering**: A polymer's tensile strength, modulus, optical transparency, chemical resistance, and barrier properties are all dramatically influenced by how much crystallinity exists AND how the chains are oriented. For example, **PET bottles** achieve their strength through **biaxial orientation** during blow molding, while **HDPE pipes** use **chain orientation** to enhance creep resistance.

### Physical Constants & Standards

- **Density of perfectly crystalline PE (ρc)**: **1000 kg/m³** (1.00 g/cm³)
- **Density of perfectly amorphous PE (ρa)**: **855 kg/m³** (0.855 g/cm³)
- **Standard reference temperature for crystallinity**: **298 K (25°C)**
- **X-ray wavelength for Cu-Kα radiation**: **λ = 1.5418 Å** (most common lab source)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Concept Breakdown

#### A. Degree of Crystallinity — Three Primary Methods

**Method 1: Density Gradient Method (Most Common in KTU Labs)**

The crystallinity is calculated from measured sample density using the two-phase model:

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Polymer sample is placed in a density gradient column (typically water/CaCl₂ or water/Na₂S₂O₃).
2. The sample floats at the level matching its density (ρ).
3. Reference densities of 100% crystalline (ρc) and 100% amorphous (ρa) phases are known from literature.
4. Crystallinity by weight or volume is calculated using additive density rule.

**Step-by-step logic for Mass Fraction:**
- The specific volume of sample = sum of specific volumes of crystalline and amorphous contributions.
- Rearrangement yields crystallinity.

#### B. Molecular Orientation — Quantitative Measurement

**Hermans Orientation Function (f):**

The second Legendre polynomial $\langle P_2(\cos\theta) \rangle$ is used:

$$f = \frac{3\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the chain axis and the reference (drawing) direction.

**Interpretation:**
- $f = 1$ → perfectly aligned (uniaxial orientation)
- $f = 0$ → random orientation
- $f = -0.5$ → perfectly perpendicular alignment (rare in practice)

#### C. Birefringence Method for Orientation

$$\Delta n = f \cdot \Delta n^0_{max}$$

where $\Delta n$ is measured birefringence and $\Delta n^0_{max}$ is the intrinsic birefringence of a perfectly oriented sample.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Variables |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\%X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | Mass-fraction crystallinity from density | $\rho$ = sample density (g/cm³) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \dfrac{1-X_c}{\rho_a}$ | Two-phase additive specific volume model | Linear mixing rule |
| 3 | $V_c = \dfrac{\rho - \rho_a}{\rho_c - \rho_a}$ | Volume-fraction crystallinity | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $f = \dfrac{3\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$ | Hermans orientation function | $-0.5 \leq f \leq 1$ |
| 5 | $\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = \dfrac{\int_0^{\pi/2} I(\phi)\cos^2\phi \sin\phi \, d\phi}{\int_0^{\pi/2} I(\phi)\sin\phi \, d\phi}$ | Average from X-ray azimuthal scan | $I(\phi)$ = intensity at azimuth $\phi$ |
| 6 | $\Delta n = f \cdot \Delta n^0$ | Birefringence–orientation correlation | $\Delta n^0$ = intrinsic birefringence |
| 7 | Bragg: $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Crystallite spacing from XRD | $d$ = d-spacing (Å), $\theta$ = Bragg angle |
| 8 | Scherrer: $L_{hkl} = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$ | Crystallite size from XRD peak width | $L$ = crystallite size (nm), $K = 0.9$ |
| 9 | $T_m = T_m^0 \left(1 - \dfrac{2\sigma_e}{\Delta H_f \cdot L}\right)$ | Gibbs–Thomson: melting vs. lamellar thickness | $L$ = lamellar thickness |
| 10 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Basic density (for density gradient column) | g/cm³ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging industry**: Biaxially-oriented polypropylene (BOPP) films use controlled crystallinity and orientation to achieve high clarity and barrier properties.
- **Fiber technology**: Drawn Nylon-6 fibers achieve tensile strength > **800 MPa** due to near-perfect chain orientation ($f \approx 0.95$).
- **Medical implants**: Ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene (UHMWPE) for hip replacements requires controlled crystallinity for wear resistance.
- **3D printing**: Crystallinity control affects warping, dimensional stability, and mechanical anisotropy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Mass-Fraction Crystallinity from Density

**Starting Principle:** The two-phase model assumes that specific volume of the semi-crystalline polymer is the linear sum of the specific volumes of the two phases (crystalline and amorphous).

**Step 1 — Specific Volume Definition:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \text{specific volume of sample}$$

**Step 2 — Linear Mixing Rule (Mass-Fraction Basis):**

Let $X_c$ be the mass fraction of crystalline phase, then mass fraction of amorphous phase is $(1 - X_c)$.

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \frac{1 - X_c}{\rho_a}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the right-hand side:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \frac{1}{\rho_a} - \frac{X_c}{\rho_a}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $X_c$:**

$$\frac{X_c}{\rho_a} - \frac{X_c}{\rho_c} = \frac{1}{\rho_a} - \frac{1}{\rho}$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $X_c$ on the left:**

$$X_c \left( \frac{1}{\rho_a} - \frac{1}{\rho_c} \right) = \frac{1}{\rho_a} - \frac{1}{\rho}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $X_c$:**

$$X_c = \frac{\dfrac{1}{\rho_a} - \dfrac{1}{\rho}}{\dfrac{1}{\rho_a} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_c}}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to common-denominator form (cleaner for numerical substitution):**

$$X_c = \frac{\rho_c(\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho(\rho_c - \rho_a)}$$

**Step 8 — Multiply by 100 to express as percentage:**

$$\%X_c = \frac{\rho_c(\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Given:**
- Sample: HDPE
- Measured density: $\rho = 0.952$ g/cm³
- $\rho_c$ (100% crystalline PE) = $1.000$ g/cm³
- $\rho_a$ (100% amorphous PE) = $0.855$ g/cm³

**Calculate: % Crystallinity**

**Step 1:** Numerator

$$\rho_c(\rho - \rho_a) = 1.000 \times (0.952 - 0.855) = 1.000 \times 0.097 = 0.097$$

**Step 2:** Denominator

$$\rho(\rho_c - \rho_a) = 0.952 \times (1.000 - 0.855) = 0.952 \times 0.145 = 0.13804$$

**Step 3:** Crystallinity

$$\%X_c = \frac{0.097}{0.13804} \times 100 = 70.27\%$$

**Result:** The HDPE sample has approximately **70.27% crystallinity** (typical for high-density polyethylene).

### Derivation 2: Hermans Orientation Function from X-Ray Azimuthal Scan

**Starting Principle:** When polymer chains are oriented, the X-ray diffraction intensity from crystalline planes varies with azimuthal angle $\phi$. Perfect orientation gives sharp peaks; random orientation gives a uniform ring.

**Step 1:** The mean-squared cosine of the angle between chain axis and reference (draw) direction is:

$$\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = \frac{\int_0^{\pi/2} I(\phi) \cos^2\phi \sin\phi \, d\phi}{\int_0^{\pi/2} I(\phi) \sin\phi \, d\phi}$$

**Step 2:** For discrete experimental data points (numerical integration using Simpson's rule):

$$\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = \frac{\sum_i I(\phi_i) \cos^2\phi_i \sin\phi_i \, \Delta\phi_i}{\sum_i I(\phi_i) \sin\phi_i \, \Delta\phi_i}$$

**Step 3:** The Hermans orientation function:

$$f = \frac{3\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

**Worked Example (simulated data):**
Suppose azimuthal scan gives intensity only at $\phi = 0°$ (sharp peak — perfect orientation).

Then $\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = 1$, so:

$$f = \frac{3(1) - 1}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

For perfectly random distribution: $\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = 1/3$, so:

$$f = \frac{3(1/3) - 1}{2} = \frac{1 - 1}{2} = 0$$

### Derivation 3: Crystallite Size via Scherrer Equation

**Starting Principle:** Width of X-ray diffraction peak is inversely proportional to crystallite size due to finite coherent diffraction domain.

$$L_{hkl} = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta_B}$$

where $K = 0.9$ (shape factor), $\beta$ is full width at half maximum (radians), $\theta_B$ is Bragg angle.

**Derivation (Brief):**
The Scherrer equation arises from the fact that small crystallites cause constructive interference to occur over a wider angular range. The coherence length $L$ is inversely proportional to the peak width:

$$\beta \approx \frac{\lambda}{L \cos\theta}$$

For spherical-like crystallites, the shape factor $K \approx 0.9$ converts this into a more accurate size estimate.

**Worked Example:**
- $\lambda = 1.5418$ Å (Cu-Kα)
- $\beta = 0.005$ rad
- $\theta_B = 23.5° = 0.410$ rad

$$L_{hkl} = \frac{0.9 \times 1.5418}{0.005 \times \cos(23.5°)} = \frac{1.38762}{0.005 \times 0.9171} = \frac{1.38762}{0.004586} = 302.6 \, \text{Å} \approx 30.3 \, \text{nm}$$

This is typical for polyethylene crystallites.

### Derivation 4: Volume Fraction Crystallinity

Starting from the same two-phase model but on volume basis:

$$V_c = \frac{\rho - \rho_a}{\rho_c - \rho_a}$$

**Derivation Step-by-Step:**

$$V_c \rho_c + (1 - V_c)\rho_a = \rho$$

$$V_c \rho_c + \rho_a - V_c \rho_a = \rho$$

$$V_c (\rho_c - \rho_a) = \rho - \rho_a$$

$$V_c = \frac{\rho - \rho_a}{\rho_c - \rho_a}$$

### Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Method

| Method | Best For | Sample Type | Accuracy |
|--------|----------|-------------|----------|
| Density gradient | Bulk crystallinity | Films, fibers | ±2-3% |
| Wide-Angle X-ray Diffraction (WAXD) | Crystallite size, crystal structure | Films | ±5% |
| Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) | Heat of fusion → %Xc | Powder, films | ±5% |
| Birefringence | Molecular orientation | Transparent films | High |
| Sonic modulus | Average orientation | Fibers | Moderate |
| Polarized optical microscopy | Spherulite size | Thin films | Qualitative |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Figure 1: Two-Phase Model of Semi-Crystalline Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph semiPolymer["SEMI CRYSTALLINE POLYMER"]
        subgraph crystRegion["CRYSTALLINE LAMELLAE"]
            chain1["Chain A aligned and folded"]
            chain2["Chain B aligned and folded"]
            chain3["Chain C aligned and folded"]
        end
        subgraph amorphRegion["AMORPHOUS REGION"]
            tanA["Random coil A"]
            tanB["Random coil B"]
            tanC["Random coil C"]
            tanD["Random coil D"]
        end
        c["Lamellar thickness L"]
        a["Amorphous thickness"]
        tieM["Tie molecules connect lamellae"]
    end
    chain1 --- crystRegion
    tanA --- amorphRegion
    chain2 -. tieM .-> tanA
```

### Figure 2: Hermans Orientation Function — Physical Meaning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["POLYMER CHAIN DISTRIBUTION"] --> B{"Orientation State"}
    B -->|f = 1| C["PERFECT UNIAXIAL ALIGNMENT"]
    B -->|f = 0| D["RANDOM ISOTROPIC"]
    B -->|f = -0.5| E["PERFECT PERPENDICULAR"]
    C --> F["XRD: Sharp equatorial arcs"]
    D --> G["XRD: Uniform Debye ring"]
    E --> H["XRD: Sharp meridional arcs"]
    F --> I["High tensile strength along axis"]
    G --> J["Equal properties in all directions"]
    H --> K["Strength perpendicular to axis"]
```

### Figure 3: Experimental Flowchart for Crystallinity Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["POLYMER SAMPLE"] --> Q{"Sample Type"}
    Q -->|Film Fiber| R1["Density Gradient Column Method"]
    Q -->|Film Fiber| R2["Wide Angle X Ray Diffraction WAXD"]
    Q -->|Powder Film| R3["Differential Scanning Calorimetry DSC"]
    R1 --> M1["Measure rho at equilibrium"]
    R2 --> M2["Measure peak areas and width"]
    R3 --> M3["Measure delta H fusion"]
    M1 --> C1["Apply two phase density formula"]
    M2 --> C2["Apply Scherrer and area integration"]
    M3 --> C3["Compare with delta H 100 percent crystalline"]
    C1 --> OUT["REPORT PERCENTAGE CRYSTALLINITY"]
    C2 --> OUT
    C3 --> OUT
```

### Figure 4: Orientation Measurement — Birefringence Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LS["WHITE LIGHT SOURCE"] --> POL["POLARIZER at 45 degrees"]
    POL --> SAM["POLYMER FILM with oriented chains"]
    SAM --> COMP["COMPENSATOR Berek or Ehringhaus"]
    COMP --> ANAL["ANALYZER crossed with polarizer"]
    ANAL --> DET["EYEPIECE or PHOTODETECTOR"]
    SAM -.-> ORI["Draw direction vertical"]
    ORI -.-> REF["Reference axis for f calculation"]
    DET --> BR["Measure retardation delta"]
    BR --> CALC["Apply Delta n = delta divided by thickness"]
    CALC --> F["Calculate Hermans f from Delta n"]
```

### Figure 5: Effect of Draw Ratio on Orientation & Crystallinity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DR0["Draw Ratio lambda equals 1"] -->|Isotropic| F0["f = 0"]
    DR0 -->|Low Crystallinity| C0["Xc equals 20 to 30 percent"]
    DR2["Draw Ratio lambda equals 2 to 3"] -->|Partial Alignment| F2["f = 0.3 to 0.5"]
    DR2 -->|Strain Induced Crystallization| C2["Xc equals 40 to 50 percent"]
    DR5["Draw Ratio lambda equals 5 to 10"] -->|High Alignment| F5["f = 0.85 to 0.95"]
    DR5 -->|High Crystallinity| C5["Xc equals 60 to 75 percent"]
```

```mermaid
    subgraph SPH["SPHERULITE STRUCTURE"]
        NUC["NUCLEATION CENTER"]
        NUC --> RAD1["Lamellar fibril along radius 1"]
        NUC --> RAD2["Lamellar fibril along radius 2"]
        NUC --> RAD3["Lamellar fibril along radius 3"]
        NUC --> RAD4["Lamellar fibril along radius 4"]
        NUC --> RAD5["Lamellar fibril along radius 5"]
        NUC --> RAD6["Lamellar fibril along radius 6"]
        RAD1 --> TWIST["Chain twist along fibril"]
        RAD2 --> TWIST
        RAD3 --> TWIST
        RAD4 --> TWIST
        RAD5 --> TWIST
        RAD6 --> TWIST
    end
    SPH -.-> BTW["Amorphous tie chains between lamellae"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define the term **degree of crystallinity** in a polymer. State TWO physical properties that are significantly affected by the degree of crystallinity.

**Model Answer:**

The **degree of crystallinity (%Xc)** of a polymer is defined as the weight (or volume) fraction of the polymer that exists in the ordered, three-dimensionally arranged crystalline phase relative to the total polymer mass (or volume).

$$\%X_c = \frac{\text{Weight of crystalline phase}}{\text{Total weight of polymer}} \times 100$$

**Two physical properties affected:**
1. **Density** — Increases with crystallinity because crystalline packing is denser than amorphous random coils.
2. **Optical transparency** — Highly crystalline regions scatter light; amorphous polymers are typically transparent.

*(Other valid answers: tensile strength, melting point, chemical resistance, impact strength, modulus, gas barrier property.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
What is **molecular orientation** in polymers? Define the **Hermans orientation function** and state its limiting values.

**Model Answer:**

**Molecular orientation** is the preferential alignment of polymer chains (or crystalline lamellae) along a particular reference direction, usually induced by mechanical deformation such as drawing, extrusion, or fiber spinning.

The **Hermans orientation function** $f$ is defined as:

$$f = \frac{3\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the polymer chain axis and the reference (draw) direction.

**Limiting values:**
- $f = 1$ — Perfect alignment of all chains parallel to the draw direction.
- $f = 0$ — Random orientation (isotropic).
- $f = -0.5$ — Perfect perpendicular alignment (rare, occurs in some biaxially stretched samples along transverse direction).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Model]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive an expression for the **mass-fraction crystallinity** of a semi-crystalline polymer from measured density. State clearly all assumptions used in the derivation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A sample of **HDPE** is measured to have a density of **0.948 g/cm³** at 25°C. Given that the density of 100% crystalline PE ($\rho_c$) is **1.000 g/cm³** and the density of 100% amorphous PE ($\rho_a$) is **0.855 g/cm³**, calculate:
   1. The mass-fraction crystallinity.
   2. The volume-fraction crystallinity.
   3. Comment on whether the polymer is more likely to be **HDPE, LDPE, or LLDPE**.

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Assumption (Two-Phase Model):**
A semi-crystalline polymer is assumed to be a physical mixture of two distinct phases — a perfectly crystalline phase (density $\rho_c$) and a perfectly amorphous phase (density $\rho_a$) — with no intermediate or partially ordered phase.

Let $X_c$ = mass fraction of crystalline phase.
Let $(1 - X_c)$ = mass fraction of amorphous phase.

The specific volume of the polymer ($\frac{1}{\rho}$) is the mass-fraction-weighted average of the specific volumes of the two phases:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \frac{1 - X_c}{\rho_a}$$

Expanding:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{X_c}{\rho_c} + \frac{1}{\rho_a} - \frac{X_c}{\rho_a}$$

Rearranging:

$$X_c\left(\frac{1}{\rho_a} - \frac{1}{\rho_c}\right) = \frac{1}{\rho_a} - \frac{1}{\rho}$$

$$X_c = \frac{\dfrac{1}{\rho_a} - \dfrac{1}{\rho}}{\dfrac{1}{\rho_a} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_c}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\rho_a \rho_c \rho$:

$$X_c = \frac{\rho_c(\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho(\rho_c - \rho_a)}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\%X_c = \frac{\rho_c(\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $\rho = 0.948$ g/cm³, $\rho_c = 1.000$ g/cm³, $\rho_a = 0.855$ g/cm³.

**(b.1) Mass-fraction crystallinity:**

Numerator:

$$\rho_c(\rho - \rho_a) = 1.000 \times (0.948 - 0.855) = 0.093$$

Denominator:

$$\rho(\rho_c - \rho_a) = 0.948 \times (1.000 - 0.855) = 0.948 \times 0.145 = 0.13746$$

Therefore:

$$\%X_c = \frac{0.093}{0.13746} \times 100 = 67.66\%$$

**(b.2) Volume-fraction crystallinity:**

$$V_c = \frac{\rho - \rho_a}{\rho_c - \rho_a} = \frac{0.948 - 0.855}{1.000 - 0.855} = \frac{0.093}{0.145} = 0.6414 = 64.14\%$$

**(b.3) Polymer identification:**

Typical crystallinity ranges:
- **HDPE**: 60 – 80% crystallinity
- **LLDPE**: 40 – 55% crystallinity
- **LDPE**: 30 – 45% crystallinity

With $\%X_c = 67.66\%$, the polymer is most likely **HDPE** (High-Density Polyethylene).

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **X-ray diffraction (WAXD) method** for determining crystallinity and orientation in a polymer sample. Discuss how the **Hermans orientation function** is calculated from azimuthal scan data.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The following azimuthal intensity data were obtained from an X-ray diffraction scan of a drawn polypropylene (PP) fiber at the (040) reflection plane:

| Azimuthal Angle φ (°) | Intensity I(φ) (a.u.) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 100 |
| 10 | 95 |
| 20 | 80 |
| 30 | 50 |
| 40 | 20 |
| 50 | 5 |
| 60 | 1 |

Calculate the **Hermans orientation function (f)** for this sample and comment on the orientation state.

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — XRD Method Explanation:**

**Wide-Angle X-ray Diffraction (WAXD)** is one of the most powerful techniques for studying polymer crystallinity and orientation.

**For Crystallinity:**
- A semi-crystalline polymer gives both sharp crystalline peaks (Bragg reflections from ordered lamellae) and a broad amorphous halo.
- The crystalline peaks are integrated to obtain $A_c$ (total crystalline peak area).
- The amorphous halo area is $A_a$.
- Crystallinity index:

$$\%X_c = \frac{A_c}{A_c + A_a} \times 100$$

**For Orientation:**
- When the polymer is oriented, the crystalline Debye–Scherrer rings become concentrated into arcs.
- An azimuthal scan records the intensity $I(\phi)$ at constant Bragg angle $\theta$ as a function of azimuthal angle $\phi$.
- The mean-squared cosine is:

$$\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = \frac{\sum_i I(\phi_i) \cos^2\phi_i \sin\phi_i \, \Delta\phi_i}{\sum_i I(\phi_i) \sin\phi_i \, \Delta\phi_i}$$

- The Hermans function is:

$$f = \frac{3\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

Use $\Delta\phi = 10° = 0.1745$ rad. Build the calculation table:

| φ (°) | φ (rad) | $\cos^2\phi$ | $\sin\phi$ | I(φ) | $\cos^2\phi \cdot \sin\phi$ | $\sin\phi$ | $I(\phi)\cos^2\phi\sin\phi$ | $I(\phi)\sin\phi$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 1.000 | 0.000 | 100 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| 10 | 0.1745 | 0.970 | 0.174 | 95 | 0.169 | 0.174 | 16.05 | 16.53 |
| 20 | 0.3491 | 0.883 | 0.342 | 80 | 0.302 | 0.342 | 24.16 | 27.36 |
| 30 | 0.5236 | 0.750 | 0.500 | 50 | 0.375 | 0.500 | 18.75 | 25.00 |
| 40 | 0.6981 | 0.587 | 0.643 | 20 | 0.377 | 0.643 | 7.55 | 12.86 |
| 50 | 0.8727 | 0.413 | 0.766 | 5 | 0.316 | 0.766 | 1.58 | 3.83 |
| 60 | 1.0472 | 0.250 | 0.866 | 1 | 0.217 | 0.866 | 0.22 | 0.87 |

(Note: For φ = 0°, $\sin\phi = 0$, so this point contributes zero to both integrals — properly handled.)

**Sums (excluding the φ=0 term since $\sin 0 = 0$):**

Numerator sum:

$$\sum I(\phi)\cos^2\phi\sin\phi = 16.05 + 24.16 + 18.75 + 7.55 + 1.58 + 0.22 = 68.31$$

Denominator sum:

$$\sum I(\phi)\sin\phi = 16.53 + 27.36 + 25.00 + 12.86 + 3.83 + 0.87 = 86.45$$

Therefore:

$$\langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle = \frac{68.31}{86.45} = 0.790$$

Hermans function:

$$f = \frac{3(0.790) - 1}{2} = \frac{2.370 - 1}{2} = \frac{1.370}{2} = 0.685$$

**Comment:** Since $f = 0.685$ (positive and closer to 1 than to 0), the polypropylene fiber is **highly oriented** along the draw direction. A typical undrawn PP film gives $f \approx 0$, while a highly drawn industrial PP fiber (draw ratio > 6:1) can approach $f = 0.95$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Formula Inversion**: Students frequently invert the crystallinity formula. Always confirm $\rho_c > \rho > \rho_a$ or the answer becomes negative — a clear sign of error.
> 
> 2. **Mixing Mass and Volume Fractions**: The Hermans function uses $\cos^2\theta$ in the AVERAGE — not $\cos\theta$. Forgetting the square is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting $\sin\phi$ Weighting in Azimuthal Scans**: The area element on a sphere requires $\sin\phi \, d\phi$ weighting. Using only $\cos^2\phi$ without the sine weighting is wrong and yields an incorrect $\langle \cos^2\theta \rangle$.
> 
> 4. **Units of β in Scherrer Equation**: $\beta$ must be in **radians**, NOT degrees. Forgetting conversion yields crystallite sizes off by ~57×.
> 
> 5. **Misidentifying ρa and ρc**: Density of crystalline phase is always **higher** than amorphous phase. Reversing them gives negative crystallinity.
> 
> 6. **Sign of f**: A negative f does NOT mean "anti-oriented"; it indicates preferred PERPENDICULAR orientation. Don't write negative orientation as impossible.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — KTU Exam Focus:**

- ✅ **Crystallinity** = ordered crystalline regions + disordered amorphous regions coexisting in semi-crystalline polymer.
- ✅ **%Xc** by density method: $\%X_c = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$
- ✅ **Volume-fraction crystallinity**: $V_c = \dfrac{\rho - \rho_a}{\rho_c - \rho_a}$
- ✅ **Density rule of thumb**: $\rho_c > \rho > \rho_a$ always (crystalline is denser).
- ✅ **HDPE** is 60–80% crystalline; **LDPE** is 30–45% crystalline.
- ✅ **Hermans orientation function** $f$ ranges from $-0.5$ to $+1$.
- ✅ **f = 1**: perfectly aligned; **f = 0**: random; **f = -0.5**: perfectly perpendicular.
- ✅ **Birefringence** $\Delta n = f \cdot \Delta n^0$ links optical and orientation measurements.
- ✅ **XRD crystallinity**: ratio of crystalline peak area to total (peak + halo) area.
- ✅ **Scherrer equation**: $L = K\lambda/(\beta\cos\theta)$, with $K = 0.9$ and $\beta$ in radians.
- ✅ **Bragg's law**: $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ — needed to compute d-spacing of crystalline planes.
- ✅ **Strain-induced crystallization** occurs during drawing — orientation increases crystallinity.
- ✅ **Spherulites** are radial arrays of lamellar crystallites observed under polarized optical microscopy.
- ✅ **Amorphous polymers** (PS, PMMA, PC): no crystallinity, transparent, no melting point (only $T_g$).
- ✅ **Crystallinity increases**: density, tensile strength, modulus, melting point, chemical resistance.
- ✅ **Crystallinity decreases**: optical clarity, impact strength, elongation at break (in some cases).
- ✅ **Draw ratio** $\lambda = L_{\text{final}}/L_{\text{initial}}$ — increases both orientation and crystallinity.
- ✅ **Tie molecules** connect adjacent lamellae and provide mechanical strength.
- ✅ **Glass transition ($T_g$)** is an amorphous phase property; **melting ($T_m$)** is a crystalline phase property.
- ✅ **Cu-Kα radiation** ($\lambda = 1.5418$ Å) is the standard laboratory X-ray source for polymer XRD.
- ✅ **Gibbs–Thomson equation** relates melting point depression to lamellar thickness.
