> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** orientation and fibre properties 9 2 Methods of investigating fibre structure. use of animal and mineral fibres. Detailed study of fibre properties such as mechanical properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Molecular Orientation in Fibres

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Molecular orientation in a fibre refers to the degree to which the long-chain polymer molecules (macromolecules) are aligned parallel to the fibre axis. The *orientation factor* (also called the *Hermans orientation function*) $f$ quantitatively expresses this alignment, ranging from $0$ (perfectly random, isotropic) to $1$ (perfect alignment along the fibre axis).

Mathematically stated as:

$$f = \frac{3 \langle \cos^{2} \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between a molecular segment and the fibre axis, and $\langle \cos^{2} \theta \rangle$ is the average of $\cos^{2} \theta$ over all molecular segments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** Orientation is *the single most important structural parameter* controlling tensile strength, initial modulus, and optical birefringence of synthetic fibres. Higher draw ratio → higher orientation → stronger, stiffer fibre.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Spaghetti Bundle:**
> Imagine a bundle of dry spaghetti. When the noodles are randomly oriented (you just drop them in a box), pulling the ends gives almost no strength — they slip past each other. Now, line up all the noodles parallel to each other and glue the ends. Pulling now gives enormous strength because every noodle bears load together. **Fibre orientation works exactly the same way.** During spinning and drawing, the polymer chains are "lined up" like parallel noodles, dramatically increasing the fibre's load-bearing capacity.

## 1.2 Fibre Structure — Hierarchical Concept

A fibre's internal architecture exists at multiple length scales:

| Level | Structural Entity | Typical Dimension |
|-------|------------------|-------------------|
| Macro | The fibre itself | 5 – 500 µm diameter |
| Micro | Fibrils | 0.1 – 1 µm |
| Nano | Crystallites + amorphous domains | 5 – 50 nm |
| Molecular | Polymer chains | 0.1 – 1 nm |

Each level contributes distinct properties, and the *interplay* between crystalline and amorphous regions — along with the orientation of tie molecules bridging them — is what gives fibres their characteristic mechanical performance.

## 1.3 Methods of Investigating Fibre Structure — Overview

The investigative toolbox spans physical, optical, spectroscopic, and thermal techniques. The principal methods are:

1. **Wide-Angle X-ray Diffraction (WAXD)** — quantifies crystallinity and crystalline orientation
2. **Small-Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS)** — probes long-period spacing and fibrillar architecture
3. **Birefringence measurement** — measures total (crystalline + amorphous) orientation
4. **Sonic modulus technique** — estimates amorphous orientation
5. **Electron Microscopy (SEM/TEM)** — visualizes surface and internal morphology
6. **Density / Density Gradient Column** — calculates crystallinity via specific volume
7. **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** — measures melting, crystallization, glass transition
8. **Infrared & Raman Spectroscopy** — detects conformational changes (e.g., *trans/gauche* ratio)
9. **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)** — probes molecular mobility

## 1.4 Animal and Mineral Fibres — Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Animal fibres** are protein-based natural fibres obtained from animals. Chemically, they are **keratin** (wool, hair) or **fibroin** (silk). They exhibit a hierarchical folded protein structure with sulphur-containing amino acid crosslinks.
>
> **Mineral fibres** are inorganic, non-metallic fibres derived from natural minerals (asbestos) or manufactured from molten glass, slag, stone, or alumina. They possess high thermal stability and fire resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Although synthetic polymers dominate modern textile markets, animal and mineral fibres remain industrially vital for carpets, fire-resistant textiles, composites, and filtration media. Their inclusion in the syllabus is **exam-relevant**.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Orientation Factor — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Hermans Orientation Function

The Hermans orientation function $f$ is the *single most quoted number* in fibre-orientation studies:

$$f = \frac{3 \langle \cos^{2} \theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

**Boundary Values:**
- $f = 1$ : Perfect uniaxial alignment parallel to fibre axis
- $f = 0$ : Perfect random orientation (isotropic)
- $f = -\frac{1}{2}$ : Perfect perpendicular alignment (theoretical, e.g., in liquid crystal transverse domains)

### 2.1.2 Practical Determination via Birefringence

The birefringence $\Delta n$ of a fibre is the difference between refractive indices parallel and perpendicular to the fibre axis:

$$\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$$

The **average orientation factor** can be computed via:

$$f_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta n}{\Delta n_{\max}}$$

where $\Delta n_{\max}$ is the maximum birefringence for a perfectly oriented sample (a property of the polymer itself).

### 2.1.3 Crystalline vs. Amorphous Orientation

A two-phase model expresses the total birefringence as a sum:

$$\Delta n = f_{c} \, x_{c} \, \Delta n_{c}^{\circ} + f_{a} \, (1 - x_{c}) \, \Delta n_{a}^{\circ}$$

where the subscripts $c$ and $a$ refer to crystalline and amorphous phases, $x_{c}$ is the crystallinity fraction, and $\Delta n^{\circ}$ values are the intrinsic birefringences.

## 2.2 Mechanical Properties — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Stress-Strain Behaviour of Fibres

A typical synthetic fibre stress-strain curve contains:

1. **Elastic region** (Hookean) — recoverable bond stretching
2. **Yield point** — onset of irreversible chain motion
3. **Plastic drawing region** — neck propagation, chain alignment
4. **Strain-hardening region** — orientation hardening
5. **Fracture** — ultimate tensile strength point

### 2.2.2 Key Mechanical Quantities

**Tensile Strength (Tenacity):**

$$\sigma_{T} = \frac{F_{\text{break}}}{A_{\text{initial}}}$$

Usually reported as **specific tenacity** in $\text{cN/tex}$ where $\text{tex} = \rho \times A \times L$ in appropriate units.

**Young's Modulus (Initial Modulus):**

$$E = \left. \frac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon} \right|_{\varepsilon \to 0}$$

**Work of Rupture (Toughness):**

$$W_{R} = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_{b}} \sigma(\varepsilon) \, d\varepsilon$$

### 2.2.3 Empirical Strength-Draw Relationship

For many synthetic fibres, tenacity increases with draw ratio following an empirical law:

$$T = T_{0} + K \ln(\lambda)$$

where $\lambda$ is the draw ratio and $K$ is a polymer-specific constant.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Domain of Validity |
|---------|------------------|---------------|--------------------|
| $f = \frac{3\langle\cos^{2}\theta\rangle - 1}{2}$ | Hermans orientation function | Dimensionless | $-0.5 \le f \le 1.0$ |
| $\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$ | Birefringence | Dimensionless | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| $f_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta n}{\Delta n_{\max}}$ | Average orientation from optics | Dimensionless | $0 \le f \le 1$ |
| $E_{\text{sonic}} \propto \langle \cos^{2} \theta \rangle$ | Sonic modulus estimates orientation | GPa | Sound velocity $\sim$5 km/s |
| $\sigma_{T} = \frac{F_{b}}{A_{0}}$ | Tensile strength | MPa or GPa | Engineering stress |
| $T = T_{0} + K \ln(\lambda)$ | Tenacity vs. draw ratio | cN/tex | Moderate draw ratios |
| $\varepsilon_{b} = \frac{L_{b} - L_{0}}{L_{0}}$ | Elongation at break | % or fraction | Always positive |
| $W_{R} = \int \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Toughness (work of rupture) | J/g or MPa | Whole curve integral |
| $L_{\text{long period}} = \frac{\lambda_{x}}{2\theta}$ | Long period from SAXS | nm | Bragg-like scattering |
| $x_{c} = \frac{\rho_{c}(\rho - \rho_{a})}{\rho(\rho_{c} - \rho_{a})}$ | Crystallinity from density | Fraction | Two-phase model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Exams (2024 Scheme):** Always write the **units** beside numerical answers. Examiners deduct marks when units are missing.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **High-modulus fibres** (Kevlar, UHMWPE) → body armour, ropes, composites
- **High-tenacity fibres** (Nylon, PET) → tyre cord, conveyor belts
- **Low-modulus, high-elongation fibres** (elastane, wool) → apparel comfort
- **Mineral fibres** (glass, basalt) → thermal insulation, fire protection
- **Animal fibres** (wool, silk) → luxury textiles, carpets

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hermans Orientation Function

We want a measure of alignment that is **zero for random orientation** and **unity for perfect alignment**.

### Step 1: Define the Angular Distribution

For a molecular segment making an angle $\theta$ with the fibre axis, the probability of finding a segment in solid angle $d\Omega$ is:

$$P(\theta) \, d\theta \propto f(\theta) \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

The average value of $\cos^{2}\theta$ is:

$$\langle \cos^{2} \theta \rangle = \frac{\int_{0}^{\pi} \cos^{2}\theta \, f(\theta) \sin\theta \, d\theta}{\int_{0}^{\pi} f(\theta) \sin\theta \, d\theta}$$

### Step 2: Evaluate for a Random Distribution

For isotropic (random) orientation, $f(\theta) = \text{constant}$, so:

$$\langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle_{\text{random}} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\pi} \cos^{2}\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta}{\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta}$$

Computing the numerator using substitution $u = \cos\theta$:

$$\int_{0}^{\pi} \cos^{2}\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = \int_{-1}^{1} u^{2} \, du = \frac{2}{3}$$

The denominator equals $\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta = 2$.

Therefore:

$$\langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle_{\text{random}} = \frac{2/3}{2} = \frac{1}{3}$$

### Step 3: Construct a Function Vanishing for Random and Equal to 1 for Perfect Alignment

We seek a function $g(\langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle)$ with:
- $g(1/3) = 0$ (random)
- $g(1) = 1$ (perfect alignment)

The simplest such linear function is:

$$g = A \langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle + B$$

Substituting the two conditions:

$$A \cdot \frac{1}{3} + B = 0$$
$$A \cdot 1 + B = 1$$

Subtracting: $A \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 1 \Rightarrow A = \frac{3}{2}$.

Then $B = -\frac{1}{2}$, giving the famous Hermans function:

$$f = \frac{3 \langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

**Verified.**

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Orientation in Drawn PET

**Given:** A PET fibre has been characterized and the following data are obtained:
- WAXD crystalline orientation factor: $f_c = 0.92$
- Birefringence: $\Delta n = 0.18$
- Intrinsic crystalline birefringence: $\Delta n_c^{\circ} = 0.29$
- Intrinsic amorphous birefringence: $\Delta n_a^{\circ} = 0.20$
- Density crystallinity: $x_c = 0.40$

**Find:** (a) Average orientation factor (b) Amorphous orientation factor

### Part (a): Average Orientation Factor

From the optical birefringence:

$$f_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta n}{\Delta n_{\max}}$$

Assuming $\Delta n_{\max}$ for PET is $0.29$ (perfect crystal orientation):

$$f_{\text{avg}} = \frac{0.18}{0.29} = 0.621$$

### Part (b): Amorphous Orientation Factor

Using the two-phase model:

$$\Delta n = f_c \, x_c \, \Delta n_c^{\circ} + f_a \, (1 - x_c) \, \Delta n_a^{\circ}$$

Substituting:

$$0.18 = (0.92)(0.40)(0.29) + f_a \, (0.60)(0.20)$$

$$0.18 = 0.1067 + 0.12 \, f_a$$

$$f_a = \frac{0.18 - 0.1067}{0.12} = \frac{0.0733}{0.12} = 0.611$$

**Interpretation:** The crystalline phase ($f_c = 0.92$) is more highly oriented than the amorphous phase ($f_a = 0.61$). This is typical because crystals orient faster during drawing.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Crystallinity from Density

**Given:** PET fibre, sample density $\rho = 1.380 \text{ g/cm}^3$, crystalline density $\rho_c = 1.455 \text{ g/cm}^3$, amorphous density $\rho_a = 1.335 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

**Find:** Mass fraction crystallinity.

Applying the formula:

$$x_c = \frac{\rho_c(\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho(\rho_c - \rho_a)}$$

Step-by-step:

$$\rho - \rho_a = 1.380 - 1.335 = 0.045$$

$$\rho_c - \rho_a = 1.455 - 1.335 = 0.120$$

$$\rho_c \times (\rho - \rho_a) = 1.455 \times 0.045 = 0.06548$$

$$\rho \times (\rho_c - \rho_a) = 1.380 \times 0.120 = 0.16560$$

$$x_c = \frac{0.06548}{0.16560} = 0.395 \approx 39.5\%$$

## 3.4 Mechanical Property Tabulation — Animal, Mineral & Synthetic Fibres

| Fibre Type | Density (g/cm³) | Tenacity (cN/tex) | Initial Modulus (cN/tex) | Elongation (%) |
|------------|-----------------|-------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| **Wool** | 1.31 | 1.0 – 1.7 | 20 – 50 | 25 – 40 |
| **Silk (Mulberry)** | 1.34 | 3.5 – 5.0 | 60 – 120 | 15 – 25 |
| **Asbestos (Chrysotile)** | 2.55 | 1.5 – 2.5 | 100 – 250 | 1 – 5 |
| **Glass fibre (E-glass)** | 2.54 | 5.0 – 8.0 | 270 – 350 | 3 – 5 |
| **Basalt fibre** | 2.65 | 4.0 – 6.0 | 250 – 320 | 3 – 4 |
| **PET (synthetic, drawn)** | 1.38 | 4.0 – 8.0 | 80 – 250 | 10 – 30 |
| **Nylon 6,6** | 1.14 | 4.0 – 9.0 | 30 – 60 | 16 – 35 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** Notice that **mineral fibres have high density and high modulus but low elongation** → brittle. **Wool has low modulus but very high elongation** → resilient and soft. Each fibre type has a *property profile* suited to specific end-uses.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Methods of Structure Investigation

| Technique | Probes | Quantitative Output | Sample Form | Limitation |
|-----------|--------|---------------------|-------------|------------|
| **WAXD** | Crystallite registry | Crystallinity %, crystal size, $f_c$ | Fibre bundle or powder | Needs crystalline order |
| **SAXS** | Long period / voids | Long spacing $L$, invariant | Thin bundle | Weak signal for low contrast |
| **Birefringence** | Total orientation | $\Delta n$, $f_{\text{avg}}$ | Single fibre or film | Optical quality required |
| **Sonic Modulus** | Amorphous orientation | $E_{\text{sonic}}$ → $f_a$ | Fibre bundle | Empirical calibration |
| **SEM** | Surface morphology | Micrographs, diameters | Any conductive sample | Surface only |
| **TEM** | Internal structure | Lattice fringes, lamellae | Ultra-thin section (<100 nm) | Complex sample prep |
| **DSC** | Thermal transitions | $T_m$, $T_g$, $\Delta H_f$ | Few mg fibre | Heating rate dependent |
| **FTIR / Raman** | Bond conformations | *trans/gauche* ratio | Powder or fibre | Quantitative band assignment tricky |
| **NMR** | Molecular mobility | $T_1$, $T_2$ relaxation | Fibre bundle | Expensive, slow |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Fibre Structure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D --> E[REPEAT UNIT ATOMIC SCALE 0.1 nanometres]

    A -. Tensile Strength .-> F[FINAL FIBRE PROPERTY]
    B -. Surface Texture .-> F

    D -. Tenacity Toughness .-> F

```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Define the Hermans orientation function. What are its boundary values and what do they signify physically?**

**Model Answer:**
The Hermans orientation function $f$ is a quantitative measure of molecular alignment in a polymer fibre, defined as:

$$f = \frac{3 \langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between a molecular segment and the fibre axis. **Boundary values:**
- $f = 1$: Perfect uniaxial alignment parallel to the fibre axis (all chains aligned)
- $f = 0$: Perfect random (isotropic) orientation — chains in all directions equally
- $f = -0.5$: Theoretical perpendicular alignment — chains perpendicular to fibre axis

It is *the* fundamental quantity used to relate processing conditions (draw ratio, spinning speed) to fibre properties (modulus, tenacity).

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**List any three methods of investigating fibre structure and state the specific structural feature each method probes.**

**Model Answer:**

| Method | Structural Feature Probed |
|--------|--------------------------|
| Wide-Angle X-ray Diffraction (WAXD) | Crystallinity percentage, crystal size, crystalline orientation $f_c$ |
| Birefringence measurement | Total (average) orientation factor $f_{\text{avg}}$ via refractive index difference |
| Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) | Melting temperature $T_m$, glass transition $T_g$, degree of crystallinity via $\Delta H_f$ |
| Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) | Surface morphology, fibre diameter, defects |
| Sonic Modulus | Amorphous orientation $f_a$ via sound velocity |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse **crystalline orientation** with **amorphous orientation**. WAXD gives **$f_c$**, birefringence gives **$f_{\text{avg}}$**, sonic modulus gives **$f_a$**. Be careful to distinguish these in the exam.

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of molecular orientation in fibres. Discuss the Hermans orientation function, its boundary values, and the experimental methods used to determine crystalline, amorphous, and average orientation factors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the mechanical properties of fibres in detail, including tenacity, initial modulus, elongation at break, and work of rupture. Compare these properties for wool, silk, glass fibre, and a typical synthetic fibre like PET. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Answer (a):**

**Concept of Molecular Orientation:**
Molecular orientation refers to the alignment of polymer chain segments along the fibre axis. During fibre spinning (melt spinning, dry spinning) and subsequent drawing, polymer chains that were originally coiled in a random configuration become progressively aligned along the draw direction. This alignment dramatically improves the mechanical, optical, and thermal properties of the fibre.

**Hermans Orientation Function:**
The degree of orientation is quantified by the Hermans orientation function:

$$f = \frac{3 \langle \cos^{2}\theta \rangle - 1}{2}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between a chain segment and the fibre reference axis, and the brackets denote averaging over all segments. Boundary values are $f = 1$ (perfect alignment), $f = 0$ (random), and $f = -0.5$ (transverse alignment).

**Experimental Determination Methods:**

1. **Wide-Angle X-ray Diffraction (WAXD):** Measures the angular spread of crystalline reflections (e.g., the azimuthal width of the (110) or (200) equatorial arcs in PET). The crystalline orientation factor $f_c$ is calculated as:

$$f_c = \frac{3 \langle \cos^{2}\phi_{hkl} \rangle - 1}{2}$$

where $\phi_{hkl}$ is the azimuthal angle of the $hkl$ reflection.

2. **Birefringence Method:** Measures the difference in refractive indices parallel and perpendicular to the fibre axis:

$$\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$$

The average orientation factor is:

$$f_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta n}{\Delta n_{\max}}$$

3. **Sonic Modulus Technique:** Measures the velocity of sound $v$ along the fibre. The dynamic modulus is $E = \rho v^{2}$. This modulus correlates with the amorphous orientation. The orientation factor is calculated as:

$$f_a = 1 - \left(\frac{E_{\text{sound, sonic}}}{E_{\text{sound, max}}}\right)$$

**Effect of Processing:**
Higher draw ratios, higher spinning speeds, and lower temperatures during drawing all lead to higher $f$ values. Commercial high-tenacity fibres typically have $f \geq 0.85$.

### **Model Answer (b):**

**Mechanical Properties of Fibres:**

1. **Tenacity (Tensile Strength):**
   Tenacity is the breaking load per unit linear density of the fibre, measured in $\text{cN/tex}$ or $\text{g/denier}$. It represents the maximum stress the fibre can sustain. The formula is:

$$T = \frac{F_{\text{break}}}{\text{tex}} \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma = \frac{F_{\text{break}}}{A_{0}}$$

2. **Initial Modulus (Young's Modulus):**
   The slope of the initial linear region of the stress-strain curve. It measures the fibre's resistance to elastic deformation. High-modulus fibres (e.g., glass, aramid) deform less under load.

$$E = \left.\frac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon}\right|_{\varepsilon \to 0}$$

3. **Elongation at Break:**
   The percentage increase in length at the breaking point:

$$\varepsilon_b = \frac{L_b - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$$

High elongation indicates a ductile, flexible fibre.

4. **Work of Rupture (Toughness):**
   The total energy absorbed per unit mass (or volume) before fracture, equal to the area under the stress-strain curve:

$$W_R = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma(\varepsilon) \, d\varepsilon$$

**Comparison of Fibre Properties:**

| Property | Wool | Silk | Glass Fibre | PET (Drawn) |
|----------|------|------|-------------|-------------|
| Tenacity (cN/tex) | 1.0 – 1.7 | 3.5 – 5.0 | 5.0 – 8.0 | 4.0 – 8.0 |
| Initial Modulus (cN/tex) | 20 – 50 | 60 – 120 | 270 – 350 | 80 – 250 |
| Elongation at break (%) | 25 – 40 | 15 – 25 | 3 – 5 | 10 – 30 |
| Work of rupture | Moderate | Moderate | Low (brittle) | High |

**Discussion:**
- **Wool** has low tenacity and modulus but very high elongation, giving it excellent resilience and warmth — ideal for apparel.
- **Silk** has balanced strength, modulus, and elongation — historically prized for luxurious fabrics.
- **Glass fibre** has very high tenacity and modulus but very low elongation — strong but brittle, ideal for composites.
- **PET (drawn)** combines good tenacity with moderate modulus and decent elongation — versatile industrial and apparel fibre.

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Describe in detail the methods used to investigate fibre structure. Explain the principle and quantitative outputs of WAXD, SAXS, birefringence, and DSC techniques. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a detailed note on animal fibres (wool and silk) and mineral fibres (asbestos and glass fibre). Compare their chemical composition, hierarchical structure, and key mechanical properties. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Answer (a):**

**Investigative Methods for Fibre Structure:**

**1. Wide-Angle X-ray Diffraction (WAXD):**
- **Principle:** A monochromatic X-ray beam strikes the fibre bundle. Crystalline regions diffract the beam according to Bragg's law: $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$. The diffraction pattern contains sharp arcs (crystalline) and diffuse halos (amorphous).
- **Quantitative Outputs:** Crystallinity percentage (from peak area ratio), crystallite size (Scherrer equation: $D = K\lambda / (\beta \cos\theta)$), and crystalline orientation factor $f_c$.

**2. Small-Angle X-ray Scattering (SAXS):**
- **Principle:** Scattering at angles $< 2^{\circ}$ probes long-period structures (alternating crystalline and amorphous lamellae, typically 5–30 nm).
- **Quantitative Output:** Long period $L$:

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{2\theta_{\text{peak}}}$$

**3. Birefringence Measurement:**
- **Principle:** A polarized light beam is passed through the fibre. The refractive index depends on orientation relative to the polarization direction. Two refractive indices $n_{\parallel}$ and $n_{\perp}$ are measured.
- **Quantitative Output:** Total birefringence $\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$, giving the average orientation factor $f_{\text{avg}}$.

**4. Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC):**
- **Principle:** The sample and an inert reference are heated at a controlled rate. Heat flow differences reveal phase transitions.
- **Quantitative Outputs:** Glass transition temperature $T_g$ (step in baseline), melting temperature $T_m$ (endothermic peak), heat of fusion $\Delta H_f$ (proportional to peak area). Crystallinity:

$$x_c = \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^{\circ}} \times 100\%$$

### **Model Answer (b):**

**Animal Fibres:**

**Wool (Keratin-based):**
- **Chemical Composition:** A protein called keratin, rich in cysteine residues that form disulphide (S–S) crosslinks between chains.
- **Structure:** Three-level hierarchy — (i) cuticle (outer scales), (ii) cortex (spindle cells containing ortho- and para-cortices), (iii) microfibrils of $\alpha$-helical protein chains embedded in a matrix.
- **Properties:** Moderate tenacity, very high elongation, excellent moisture absorption (~18%), good thermal insulation due to crimp.

**Silk (Fibroin-based):**
- **Chemical Composition:** Fibroin protein with $\beta$-sheet crystalline domains and amorphous regions.
- **Structure:** Two fibroin filaments cemented by sericin gum; $\beta$-sheets aligned along the fibre axis give high strength.
- **Properties:** Highest tenacity among natural fibres, smooth lustrous surface, moderate elongation.

**Mineral Fibres:**

**Asbestos:**
- **Chemical Composition:** Hydrated magnesium silicate, formula $\text{Mg}_3\text{Si}_2\text{O}_5(\text{OH})_4$ (chrysotile form).
- **Structure:** Hollow, tubular microfibrils; naturally occurring mineral with excellent thermal stability.
- **Properties:** Fire-resistant, good tensile strength, but **health hazard** (carcinogenic, banned in many countries).

**Glass Fibre:**
- **Chemical Composition:** Primarily $\text{SiO}_2$ (50–65%) with $\text{CaO}$, $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, $\text{B}_2\text{O}_3$ additions.
- **Structure:** Amorphous (glassy) inorganic network; can be produced as continuous filaments or staple.
- **Properties:** Very high tensile strength, very high modulus, low elongation, brittle, fire-resistant, chemically stable.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Wool | Silk | Asbestos | Glass Fibre |
|---------|------|------|----------|-------------|
| Chemical class | Protein (keratin) | Protein (fibroin) | Silicate mineral | Amorphous silicate glass |
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.31 | 1.34 | 2.55 | 2.54 |
| Tenacity | Low-Moderate | High | Moderate | Very High |
| Elongation | Very High | Moderate | Very Low | Very Low |
| Thermal stability | Low (chars) | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| Health concern | None | None | Yes (banned) | Possible (fine dust) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse mineral fibre *asbestos* with synthetic mineral fibres like glass. Asbestos is a *naturally occurring* mineral; glass fibre is a *manufactured* mineral fibre.
> 2. **Always** mention the *health hazard* of asbestos — examiners look for this. A complete answer on asbestos must note its ban/restriction in modern usage.
> 3. **Drawing ≠ drawing of conclusion:** In orientation questions, "drawing" refers to mechanical stretching, not "drawing a diagram." Misinterpreting this loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Units matter:** tenacity in cN/tex, modulus in GPa, elongation in % — *never* omit units in numerical answers.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 2 Topic:**

- **Hermans orientation function:** $f = \frac{3\langle\cos^{2}\theta\rangle - 1}{2}$. Boundary values: $+1$ (perfect alignment), $0$ (random), $-0.5$ (perpendicular). *Always quote this formula if asked about orientation.*

- **Three orientations, three techniques:** WAXD → $f_c$ (crystalline); Sonic modulus → $f_a$ (amorphous); Birefringence → $f_{\text{avg}}$ (total).

- **Two-phase model equation:** $\Delta n = f_c \, x_c \, \Delta n_c^{\circ} + f_a (1-x_c) \Delta n_a^{\circ}$ — examiners frequently test this.

- **Crystallinity from density:** $x_c = \frac{\rho_c(\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho(\rho_c - \rho_a)}$. *Memorize this derivation.*

- **Mechanical properties hierarchy:** Tenacity = strength, Initial modulus = stiffness, Elongation = ductility, Work of rupture = toughness (energy absorption).

- **Animal fibres:** wool (keratin + S–S crosslinks, high elongation), silk (fibroin, $\beta$-sheet crystals, high tenacity among naturals).

- **Mineral fibres:** asbestos (silicate, fireproof but carcinogenic), glass fibre (amorphous silicate, high strength and modulus, low elongation).

- **Property trade-offs:** high modulus ↔ low elongation (brittle); low modulus ↔ high elongation (tough). Applications follow this trade-off principle.

- **Effect of drawing:** Higher draw ratio → higher orientation → higher tenacity and modulus but *decreased* elongation. *This is the most common "why" question in KTU boards.*

- **Structure-property linkage:** Always link molecular features (orientation, crystallinity, crosslinks) to macroscopic mechanical behaviour in your answers — examiners reward this connecting language.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** electrical properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Properties of Polymers** refer to the response of polymeric materials when subjected to an external electric field. These properties broadly encompass **dielectric behavior** (polarization, permittivity, dielectric loss, dielectric strength), **conductive behavior** (volume/surface resistivity, conductivity), and **special electromechanical effects** (piezoelectricity, pyroelectricity, and electret formation). Polymers are primarily used as **electrical insulators** in cables, capacitors, printed circuit boards, and high-voltage equipment, but specially engineered polymers (intrinsically conducting polymers, filled composites) also serve as **semiconductors, antistatic materials, and EMI shields**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

Imagine a **garden hose carrying water** — the hose wall resists water flow (insulator), but if you sprinkle carbon black (a conducting filler) into the rubber, the hose becomes slightly leaky (semiconductive). Similarly:

- A **pristine polymer chain** (like polyethylene, PTFE) has tightly bound electrons → behaves like the **dry hose wall** → it **stores** electric energy (capacitor) and **blocks** current (insulator).
- Under an electric field, charges inside the polymer do **not flow freely**; instead, they **shift slightly** or **rotate** (dipoles orient), creating **polarization** — like a crowd of people turning to face a stage.
- The ability to "store" this energy is measured by the **dielectric constant (εr)**. The energy lost as heat during each AC cycle is the **dielectric loss (tan δ)**.
- The maximum voltage before the polymer suddenly breaks down and conducts is its **dielectric strength**.
- When conductive fillers (carbon black, metal powders, CNTs, graphene) are added above a critical concentration (**percolation threshold**), the polymer switches from insulator to conductor — like a dry sponge becoming wet and conducting water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Remember (Bold)**: **εr (relative permittivity, dimensionless)**, **ρv (volume resistivity, Ω·cm)**, **ρs (surface resistivity, Ω/sq)**, **tan δ (dielectric loss tangent, dimensionless)**, **dielectric strength (kV/mm or V/mil)**, **σ (conductivity, S/cm)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Reference Values

| Constant / Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| ε₀ (permittivity of free space) | **8.854 × 10⁻¹²** | F/m |
| 1 eV | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | J |
| 1 cm | **10⁻²** | m |
| 1 mil (thousandth of inch) | **25.4 × 10⁻⁶** | m |

> [!TIP]
> Polymer ρv values: **PE ≈ 10¹⁶ Ω·cm** (excellent insulator); **PTFE ≈ 10¹⁸ Ω·cm**; **PANI (doped) ≈ 10² Ω·cm** (conductor); **Silicone ≈ 10¹⁴ Ω·cm**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of Polymer Response to an Electric Field

When a polymer is placed between two electrodes and an electric field **E (V/m)** is applied, four distinct physical responses occur:

### Step 1 — Electronic Polarization (Pe)
- The electron cloud of each atom **displaces** slightly relative to the nucleus.
- Occurs in **all** materials; time scale ≈ **10⁻¹⁵ s**.
- Responsible for the baseline refractive index (n² ≈ εr at optical frequencies).

### Step 2 — Atomic / Ionic Polarization (Pa)
- Relative displacement of **nuclei** in a molecule (e.g., C–C bond stretching).
- Time scale ≈ **10⁻¹² to 10⁻¹³ s**.
- Significant only in ionic polymers (nylon, PVDF).

### Step 3 — Orientation (Dipolar) Polarization (Po)
- Permanent dipoles (C=O, C–Cl, –OH) **rotate** to align with the field, fighting thermal agitation.
- Time scale ≈ **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻¹⁰ s**.
- Dominant in polar polymers: PVC, PMMA, PVDF, polyimides.

### Step 4 — Space-Charge / Interfacial Polarization (Maxwell–Wagner)
- Accumulation of **mobile charge carriers** at interfaces (between amorphous/crystalline regions, or between polymer and filler).
- Time scale ≈ **10⁻¹ to 10² s** (very slow).
- Huge contributor at low frequencies in filled/composite polymers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Polarization Equation (Clausius–Mossotti foundation):**
>
> $$P_{total} = P_e + P_a + P_o + P_{sc}$$
>
> Each term peaks at a specific frequency range; this frequency dependence is precisely what gives rise to **dielectric relaxation spectra**.

## 2.2 Why Polymers are Generally Insulators

- **Band gap Eg of most polymers > 4 eV** (PTFE ≈ 10 eV, PE ≈ 8 eV). Compare to silicon (1.1 eV) and copper (≈ 0 eV).
- Electrons are localized on covalent bonds; **no free charge carriers**.
- Conductivity arises only from ionic impurities, catalyst residues, absorbed moisture.

## 2.3 Mechanisms to Make Polymers Conductive

1. **Doping** conjugated polymers (PANI, polypyrrole, PEDOT:PSS) — charge-transfer agents introduce polarons/bipolarons.
2. **Filler loading** — carbon black, graphite, CNTs, graphene, silver flakes above the **percolation threshold (φc)**.
3. **Ionic conduction** — polyelectrolytes, polymer electrolytes (PEO + Li salts) for batteries.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel-plate with polymer dielectric (F) |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{C}{C_0}$ | Relative permittivity (dielectric constant), dimensionless |
| 3 | $P = \varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_r - 1)E$ | Polarization vector (C/m²) |
| 4 | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$ | Dielectric loss tangent (dissipation factor) |
| 5 | $\varepsilon^{*} = \varepsilon' - j\varepsilon''$ | Complex permittivity |
| 6 | $\rho_v = \dfrac{R \cdot A}{L}$ | Volume resistivity (Ω·m or Ω·cm) |
| 7 | $\rho_s = \dfrac{R \cdot w}{L}$ | Surface resistivity (Ω/sq or Ω) |
| 8 | $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Conductivity (S/m or S/cm) |
| 9 | $\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{2k_BT}\right)$ | Arrhenius ionic conduction |
| 10 | $\sigma_{comp} \propto (\phi - \phi_c)^{t}$ | Percolation law (φ = filler volume fraction, t ≈ 1.6–2.0) |
| 11 | $E_{break} = \dfrac{V_{break}}{d}$ | Dielectric strength (V/m or kV/mm) |
| 12 | $\dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{\varepsilon_r + 2} = \dfrac{N\alpha}{3\varepsilon_0}$ | Clausius–Mossotti relation (N = molecules/m³, α = polarizability) |
| 13 | $\varepsilon' = n^2$ | Maxwell relation (at optical freq., for non-polar polymers) |
| 14 | $W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r E^2 \cdot V$ | Energy stored per unit volume (J/m³) |
| 15 | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_{max}}$ | Relaxation time from peak in ε'' vs f plot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Capacitors made from polypropylene (εr ≈ 2.2) store more energy per volume than air (εr = 1), while PVDF (εr ≈ 8–12) is used where higher capacitance density is needed. High-voltage cable insulation requires **low tan δ** (e.g., XLPE, EPR) to prevent thermal runaway. Antistatic PE films use carbon black at φ ≈ 0.05–0.15 to drop ρv from 10¹⁶ to ≈ 10⁹ Ω·cm.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Key Electrical Property Exploited |
|---|---|
| Power cable insulation (XLPE) | High ρv, high dielectric strength, low tan δ |
| Capacitor films (BOPP, PET) | High εr, low loss, thin-film processability |
| PCB substrate (FR-4 epoxy) | Moderate εr (~4.5), mechanical + thermal + electrical |
| EMI shielding enclosures (CNT/ABS composites) | σ > 1 S/cm gives > 40 dB shielding |
| Antistatic packaging (PE + CB) | ρs ≤ 10¹⁰ Ω/sq |
| Sensors & actuators (PVDF) | Piezoelectric coefficient d₃₃ ≈ –33 pC/N |
| Polymer electrolyte (PEO + LiTFSI) | σ ≈ 10⁻⁴ S/cm at 80 °C for Li-ion batteries |
| Photovoltaic encapsulant (EVA) | High ρv + optical transparency |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Polymer Capacitor

**Start with Gauss's Law in a dielectric:**

$$D = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E$$

**Step 1 — Relate electric field E to applied voltage V across plate separation d:**

$$E = \dfrac{V}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Electric flux density D equals the free surface charge density σ_f on the plates:**

$$D = \sigma_f = \dfrac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the constitutive equation:**

$$\dfrac{Q}{A} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot \dfrac{V}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Definition of capacitance C = Q/V:**

$$C = \dfrac{Q}{V} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$. The polymer therefore multiplies the capacitance by factor **εr** compared to an air-filled (εr = 1) capacitor of identical geometry.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Volume Resistivity from Measured Resistance

**Step 1 — Ohm's law applied to a polymer specimen of length L and cross-sectional area A:**

$$R = \dfrac{\rho_v \cdot L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for ρv:**

$$\rho_v = \dfrac{R \cdot A}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Conductivity is the reciprocal:**

$$\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho_v} = \dfrac{L}{R \cdot A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit conversion gotcha (KTU favourite trap):**
> If R is in Ω, L in cm, A in cm² → ρv has units **Ω·cm**.
> To convert to Ω·m, multiply by **0.01**.
> 1 S/m = 0.01 S/cm.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Clausius–Mossotti Relation

**Step 1 — Consider a single polarizable atom/molecule of polarizability α in a local field E_loc:**

$$p = \alpha \cdot E_{loc}$$

**Step 2 — Clausius–Mossotti evaluates the local field for a sphere of dielectric in a uniform applied field:**

$$E_{loc} = E + \dfrac{P}{3\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 3 — Polarization P = Np, where N = number of dipoles per unit volume:**

$$P = N\alpha\!\left(E + \dfrac{P}{3\varepsilon_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Using D = ε₀E + P = ε₀εrE, so P = ε₀(εr – 1)E:**

$$\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_r - 1)E = N\alpha\!\left(E + \dfrac{\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_r - 1)E}{3\varepsilon_0}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Cancel E and simplify:**

$$\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_r - 1) = N\alpha\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{3}\right)$$

$$\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_r - 1) = N\alpha\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 2}{3}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Final form:**

$$\dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{\varepsilon_r + 2} = \dfrac{N\alpha}{3\varepsilon_0}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering use:** Allows estimation of εr from chemical structure (molecular polarizability α from group contributions — e.g., C=O adds ~2.0 Å³; C–Cl adds ~6.5 Å³). Explains why PVC (εr ≈ 3.2) > PE (εr ≈ 2.3).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Dielectric Loss Tangent from Complex Permittivity

**Step 1 — AC current density lags voltage because of dipole inertia:**

$$J = j\omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon^{*} E = j\omega \varepsilon_0(\varepsilon' - j\varepsilon'')E$$

**Step 2 — Separate real (capacitive) and imaginary (resistive/lossy) parts:**

$$J = \omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon''E + j\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon'E$$

**Step 3 — Power dissipated per unit volume:**

$$P_{diss} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\omega\,\varepsilon_0\,\varepsilon''\,E_0^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Reactive power stored:**

$$P_{react} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\omega\,\varepsilon_0\,\varepsilon'\,E_0^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Ratio = loss tangent:**

$$\tan\delta = \dfrac{P_{diss}}{P_{react}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$$

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners love testing **why tan δ peaks at a specific frequency** — the answer is: at that frequency **ωτ ≈ 1**, where dipole reorientation matches the AC half-cycle, maximizing energy loss per cycle.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Percolation Conductivity in Filled Composites

**Step 1 — Percolation threshold φc** is the critical filler volume fraction where a continuous conducting network forms (analogous to gelation).

**Step 2 — Just above the threshold, conductivity scales as:**

$$\sigma_{comp} = \sigma_{filler}\!\left(\phi - \phi_c\right)^{t}, \quad \phi > \phi_c$$

**Step 3 — Just below the threshold:**

$$\sigma_{comp} = \sigma_{polymer}\!\left(\phi_c - \phi\right)^{-s}$$

**Step 4 — Typical exponents (universal, not material-specific):**

$$t \approx 1.6 \text{ to } 2.0, \qquad s \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 1.0$$

**Example calculation (worked):**
For CB/HDPE composite with σ_CB = 10² S/cm, σ_HDPE = 10⁻¹⁶ S/cm, φc = 0.04, t = 2, φ = 0.08:

$$\sigma_{comp} = 10^{2} \times (0.08 - 0.04)^{2} = 10^{2} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} = 0.16 \;\text{S/cm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 16-order-of-magnitude jump (10⁻¹⁶ → 10⁻¹ S/cm) demonstrates why **percolation is the dominant design principle** for antistatic and conductive polymer composites.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Polymer Ranking by Key Properties

| Polymer | εr (1 kHz) | tan δ (1 kHz) | ρv (Ω·cm) | Dielectric Strength (kV/mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PTFE | 2.1 | < 0.0002 | 10¹⁸ | 60 |
| LDPE | 2.3 | 0.0003 | 10¹⁶ | 40 |
| XLPE | 2.4 | 0.0004 | 10¹⁶ | 50 |
| PP | 2.2 | 0.0005 | 10¹⁶ | 25 |
| PVC (rigid) | 3.2 | 0.018 | 10¹⁴ | 15 |
| PMMA | 3.6 | 0.025 | 10¹⁵ | 18 |
| PET | 3.5 | 0.012 | 10¹⁶ | 25 |
| PVDF | 8.5 | 0.05 | 10¹⁴ | 10 |
| Nylon-6,6 | 4.0 | 0.03 | 10¹⁴ | 20 |
| Epoxy (cured) | 4.5 | 0.02 | 10¹⁵ | 22 |
| Polyimide (Kapton) | 3.4 | 0.005 | 10¹⁷ | 200 |
| PANI (doped, HCl) | — | — | ≈ 1–10⁰ | — |
| PEDOT:PSS | — | — | ≈ 10⁰–10³ | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Polymer Response to Electric Field

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Electric Field E applied across polymer between electrodes] --> B{Polymer Microstructure}
    B --> C1[Electronic Polarization Pe - 10^-15 s]
    B --> C2[Atomic Polarization Pa - 10^-13 s]
    B --> C3[Orientation Polarization Po - 10^-9 s]
    B --> C4[Space Charge Polarization Psc - 10^-1 s]
    C1 --> D[Total Polarization P_total and Complex Permittivity epsilon_star]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E1[Dielectric Storage: eps_real drives capacitance C]
    D --> E2[Dielectric Loss: eps_imag drives tan delta heat dissipation]
    D --> E3[Resistive Channel: ionic impurities plus absorbed water yield sigma]
    E1 --> F1[Application: Capacitor Film Energy Storage]
    E2 --> F2[Application: Microwave Heating of PVC Adhesive Welding]
    E3 --> F3[Application: Antistatic Packaging via Carbon Black Loading]
    D --> G[Beyond Critical Field E_break]
    G --> H[Dielectric Breakdown: avalanche plus thermal plus mechanical]
    H --> I[Conductive Short Path Permanent Failure]
```

```mermaid

    subgraph stage1[Stage 1 - phi less than phi_c - Insulating]
    A1[Polymer Matrix]
    A2[Isolated Filler Particles]
    A1 --- A2
    end

    B1 --- B2
    end

    end

    stage2 --> stage3
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Equivalent Circuit of a Real Polymer Capacitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[AC Source V omega] --> C1[Ideal Capacitance C = eps_0 eps_r A over d]
    C1 --> R1[Parallel Resistance R_loss representing tan delta]
    R1 --> Z[Total Impedance Z_star = 1 over j omega C_star]
    Z --> O[Output Current I]
    V -.->|measures| M[LCR Meter Bridge 1 kHz to 1 MHz]
    M -.->|reads| eps_r
    M -.->|reads| tan_delta
```

## 4.4 Diagram: Types of Dielectric Polarization vs Frequency

```mermaid

    F[Frequency Axis 10^-3 Hz to 10^15 Hz] --> P1[Space Charge - 10^-3 to 10^3 Hz]
    F --> P2[Orientation Dipolar - 10^2 to 10^9 Hz]
    F --> P3[Atomic Ionic - 10^9 to 10^13 Hz]
    F --> P4[Electronic - 10^13 to 10^15 Hz]
    P1 --> X1[Low Freq Dispersion Maxwell Wagner Peak in tan delta]

```

```mermaid
    Q2 -->|Low Voltage PCB| R2[Use FR4 Epoxy plus Glass Cloth]
    Q1 -->|No, Need to Conduct| Q3{Static Dissipation or Active Conduction?}
    Q3 -->|Static Dissipation| R3[Carbon Black or CNT in PE or PP rho_s approx 10^8 to 10^10 Ohm per sq]
    Q3 -->|Active Conduction| R4[Intrinsically Conducting Polymer PANI or PEDOT:PSS]
    Start --> S1{Need Energy Storage?}

    S1 -->|Yes, Battery| R6[PEO plus LiTFSI polymer electrolyte]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define dielectric constant (relative permittivity) of a polymer. Why is it higher for PVC than for polyethylene?**

**Model Answer:**
The dielectric constant (εr) of a polymer is defined as the ratio of the capacitance of a capacitor with the polymer as the dielectric to the capacitance of the same capacitor with vacuum (or air) as the dielectric:

$$\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{C_{polymer}}{C_0}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity indicating the polymer's ability to store electric energy. PVC (εr ≈ 3.2) has a higher dielectric constant than PE (εr ≈ 2.3) because PVC contains polar C–Cl bonds that contribute significant **orientation polarization** under an applied AC field. PE has only non-polar C–H and C–C bonds, so only electronic polarization is present.

**[3 marks breakdown: 1 for definition, 1 for the comparison, 1 for the polarization mechanism]**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the percolation threshold in a conducting polymer composite? State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The percolation threshold (φc) is the critical volume fraction of conductive filler (e.g., carbon black, CNT, graphene) at which a continuous, three-dimensional conductive network first forms inside the insulating polymer matrix. At φ = φc, the composite conductivity rises sharply — often by 10 to 16 orders of magnitude — following the power law:

$$\sigma \propto (\phi - \phi_c)^{t}$$

**Significance:** Below φc the composite behaves as an insulator (used in antistatic packaging and self-regulating heaters). Above φc it is a true conductor (used for EMI shielding, electrodes, sensors). Minimising φc reduces filler cost and preserves mechanical properties.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive an expression for the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor with a polymer dielectric. A polyethylene disc of εr = 2.3, area 100 cm² and thickness 0.5 mm is used. Calculate the capacitance and the energy stored at 400 V. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from Gauss's law in a dielectric:

$$D = \varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r E$$

With $E = V/d$ and $D = Q/A$:

$$C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

**Numerical substitution:**

- ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m
- εr = 2.3
- A = 100 cm² = 100 × 10⁻⁴ m² = 10⁻² m²
- d = 0.5 mm = 0.5 × 10⁻³ m = 5 × 10⁻⁴ m

$$C = \dfrac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.3 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{C = 4.07 \times 10^{-10}\;\text{F} \approx 407\;\text{pF}}$$

**Energy stored:**

$$W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 4.07 \times 10^{-10} \times (400)^{2}$$

$$\boxed{W = 3.26 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{J} = 32.6\;\mu\text{J}}$$

**[Marks: 3 derivation, 2 substitution & C, 2 energy calculation]**

---

**(b) Explain the four types of polarization in polymers with suitable examples. How do they contribute to dielectric constant and loss? [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Electronic Polarization (Pe)** — Displacement of the electron cloud relative to the nucleus. Universal, instantaneous (10⁻¹⁵ s). Examples: PE, PTFE. Contributes εr ≈ 2.0–2.5.

2. **Atomic/Ionic Polarization (Pa)** — Displacement of atomic nuclei (e.g., C–C stretch). Time scale 10⁻¹² to 10⁻¹³ s. Important in ionic polymers and ceramics.

3. **Orientation (Dipolar) Polarization (Po)** — Rotation of permanent dipoles (C=O, C–Cl, –OH). Time scale 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻¹⁰ s. Dominant in PVC, PMMA, PVDF. Strongly temperature- and frequency-dependent; produces Debye relaxation peaks in ε'' and tan δ.

4. **Space-Charge (Interfacial) Polarization (Psc)** — Accumulation of mobile ions at interfaces (amorphous–crystalline boundary, polymer–electrode interface, filler–matrix interface). Slowest, time scale 10⁻¹ to 10² s. Significant at low frequencies (< 1 kHz) and in filled/composite polymers.

**Contribution to εr and loss:**
- At **low frequency**, all four polarizations contribute → εr is maximum.
- At **high frequency**, only Pe survives → εr drops to its optical value n².
- The transition produces a **peak in ε'' and tan δ** at the characteristic relaxation frequency f = 1/(2πτ).

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) What is dielectric loss tangent? Derive the expression $\tan\delta = \varepsilon''/\varepsilon'$. A polymer capacitor has ε' = 4.5 and ε'' = 0.09 at 1 kHz. Calculate the dissipation factor and the heat dissipated per cycle at E₀ = 10⁶ V/m. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The dielectric loss tangent is the ratio of energy dissipated per cycle to energy stored per cycle in a dielectric under AC excitation.

**Derivation:**

$$J = j\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon^{*}E = j\omega\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon' - j\varepsilon'')E$$

Real (lossy) part: $J_{loss} = \omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon''E$
Imaginary (capacitive) part: $J_{cap} = j\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon'E$

$$\tan\delta = \dfrac{J_{loss}}{J_{cap}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$$

**Numerical calculation:**

$$\tan\delta = \dfrac{0.09}{4.5} = 0.02$$

Heat dissipated per unit volume per cycle:

$$W_{diss} = \pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon''E_0^{2} = \pi \times 8.854\times10^{-12}\times 0.09 \times (10^6)^2$$

$$\boxed{W_{diss} = 2.50 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{J/m}^{3}\text{ per cycle}}$$

---

**(b) Discuss the mechanism of electrical conduction in conducting polymer composites. A composite has φc = 0.05, σ_filler = 500 S/cm, σ_polymer = 10⁻¹⁶ S/cm and t = 2.0. Calculate conductivity at φ = 0.10. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A conducting polymer composite (CPC) is made by dispersing conductive fillers (carbon black, graphite, CNT, metal powder) in an insulating polymer matrix. Conduction proceeds by:

1. **Tunnelling/hopping** of electrons across small gaps (< 2 nm) between adjacent filler particles.
2. **Direct contact conduction** through continuous filler chains.
3. **Field emission** at high filler loading.

Below φc, the fillers are isolated → composite is insulating. At φc, a continuous 3D network forms → conductivity jumps 10–16 orders of magnitude. Above φc:

$$\sigma_{comp} = \sigma_{filler}(\phi - \phi_c)^{t}, \quad \phi > \phi_c$$

**Numerical:**

$$\sigma_{comp} = 500 \times (0.10 - 0.05)^{2} = 500 \times 0.0025 = 1.25\;\text{S/cm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert cm⁻¹ to m⁻¹ or vice versa** when mixing units. Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing εr (dimensionless) with ε (absolute permittivity, F/m)** — ε = ε₀εr. A common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing φ and φc**: many students write (φc – φ)^t instead of (φ – φc)^t for φ > φc.
> 4. **Wrong formula for energy stored** — it is ½CV², **not** CV².
> 5. **Missing "orientation" polarization** as a reason for higher εr in PVC vs PE.
> 6. **In Clausius–Mossotti**, dropping the factor 3 in denominator — costs 2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting to multiply by π** in dielectric dissipation per cycle (energy per cycle uses π, not ½).
> 8. **Stating percolation threshold in weight%** without specifying whether the formula uses volume or weight fraction — the percolation power law strictly requires **volume fraction φ**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Dielectric constant εr** = C/C₀, dimensionless; higher for polar polymers (PVC, PVDF, PMMA).
- [x] **Capacitance** $C = \varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r A/d$.
- [x] **Energy stored** $W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$.
- [x] **Four polarizations**: electronic (10⁻¹⁵ s), atomic (10⁻¹³ s), orientation (10⁻⁹ s), space-charge (10⁻¹ s).
- [x] **Clausius–Mossotti relation**: $\dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{\varepsilon_r + 2} = \dfrac{N\alpha}{3\varepsilon_0}$.
- [x] **Complex permittivity**: $\varepsilon^{*} = \varepsilon' - j\varepsilon''$.
- [x] **Loss tangent**: $\tan\delta = \varepsilon''/\varepsilon'$.
- [x] **Volume resistivity**: $\rho_v = RA/L$ (Ω·cm); conductivity σ = 1/ρ.
- [x] **Dielectric strength** in kV/mm — defines breakdown voltage.
- [x] **Percolation law**: $\sigma \propto (\phi - \phi_c)^{t}$ for φ > φc.
- [x] **Conducting polymers**: PANI, polypyrrole, PEDOT:PSS, polyacetylene.
- [x] **Insulating polymers**: PE, PP, PTFE, XLPE — best for cables and capacitor films.
- [x] **Piezoelectric polymers**: PVDF and P(VDF-TrFE).
- [x] **Units to remember**: ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m; 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.
- [x] **Maxwell relation**: at optical freq., ε' ≈ n² for non-polar polymers.
- [x] **Engineering mantra**: low tan δ for high-frequency insulation; high εr for capacitors; controlled σ for antistatic/EMI applications.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Moisture Absorption in Polymers — Foundational Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This experiment falls under the Polymer Testing & Characterization laboratory module. Students are expected to **conduct the test, record gravimetric data, plot absorption curves, and compute percentage moisture uptake** as per ASTM D570 / ISO 62 standards.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Moisture Absorption** is the process by which a polymer matrix takes up water molecules from its surrounding environment (typically humid air or liquid water), causing a measurable increase in mass and often a corresponding change in physical, mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties. It is a critical **mass-transfer phenomenon** governed by Fickian diffusion principles.

The standard quantitative metric used is the **percentage moisture content ($M\%$)**, calculated gravimetrically by comparing the conditioned (wet) specimen weight to the original dry weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Tests:**
> - **ASTM D570** – Standard Test Method for Water Absorption of Plastics
> - **ISO 62:2008** – Plastics — Determination of Water Absorption
> - Test conditions typically specified at **23 °C and 50 % relative humidity** (temperate climate standard).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Kitchen Sponge Model

Think of a polymer specimen as a **kitchen sponge** dropped into a bowl of water:

- A **natural cellulose sponge** (analogous to a hydrophilic polymer like **Nylon-6,6**) soaks up water rapidly and can absorb several times its own weight — its structure has plenty of "channels" and polar groups that "love" water.

- A **synthetic scouring pad made of polyethylene** (analogous to a hydrophobic polymer like **HDPE or PTFE**) barely absorbs any water — its non-polar molecular chains repel water like oil on a waxed surface.

The rate and extent of absorption depend on:
1. The **chemical affinity** of the polymer for water (polarity, hydrogen bonding sites).
2. The **microstructure** (crystallinity, crosslink density, free volume).
3. The **environmental drive** (humidity, temperature, immersion time).

This sponge analogy makes it intuitively clear *why* some plastics are used as waterproof seals, while others are intentionally hygroscopic for applications like breathable films or controlled drug release.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted in **Bold**)

- **Standard Conditioning Temperature:** **23 °C ± 1 °C** (ASTM D570)
- **Standard Immersion Time:** **24 hours** (short-term test) and until **saturation equilibrium** (long-term test)
- **Density of Water (at 23 °C):** **$\rho_w \approx 0.9975 \text{ g/cm}^3$**
- **Relative Humidity (RH) for conditioning:** **50 % ± 2 %**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why and How Polymers Absorb Moisture

### Step 1 — Molecular Mechanism of Water Uptake

Water penetrates a polymer matrix through **three sequential stages**:

1. **Surface Adsorption** – Water molecules adhere to the outer polymer surface, attracted by polar functional groups (–OH, –CONH–, –COOH, –COOR) via hydrogen bonding or dipole–dipole interactions.
2. **Diffusion into Bulk** – Water molecules migrate into the free volume (microvoids) between polymer chains, driven by a concentration gradient. The rate is governed by the **diffusion coefficient $D$**.
3. **Saturation Equilibrium** – Eventually the water uptake plateaus when the chemical potential of water in the polymer equals that of the surrounding environment.

### Step 2 — Fickian vs Non-Fickian Diffusion

- **Fickian Diffusion (Case I):** Diffusion rate << polymer relaxation rate → classic linear plot of $M_t/M_\infty$ vs $\sqrt{t}$. Common in rubbers and amorphous polymers below $T_g$.
- **Non-Fickian (Anomalous) Diffusion:** Polymer chain relaxation competes with diffusion → S-shaped curves. Common in glassy polymers and highly crosslinked networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For Fickian behavior, the **diffusion coefficient $D$** is a material constant (units: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$) and is the key design parameter for predicting long-term moisture ingress in composite laminates and electronic encapsulation.

### Step 3 — Why Polymer Structure Matters

| Polymer Structural Feature | Effect on Moisture Absorption |
|---|---|
| **Polar groups** (–OH, –CONH–, ester) | ↑↑ Strongly increases absorption |
| **High crystallinity** | ↓ Decreases absorption (crystalline regions are impermeable) |
| **High crosslink density** | ↓ Reduces free volume, lowers absorption |
| **High free volume / $T > T_g$** | ↑ Increases diffusion rate |
| **Presence of fillers (glass, talc)** | ↓ Reduces absorption proportionally |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | **Percentage Moisture Absorption** | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $M_t = \dfrac{W_t - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Moisture content at time $t$ | Dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D t}{h^2}\right]$ | Fickian diffusion (plate geometry, thickness $h$) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx \dfrac{4}{h}\sqrt{\dfrac{D t}{\pi}}$ (for $M_t/M_\infty < 0.5$) | Short-time Fickian approximation | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{\pi h^2}{16 M_\infty^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{dM_t}{d\sqrt{t}}\right)^2$ | Diffusion coefficient from slope | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 6 | $V\% = \dfrac{(W_w - W_d) \, \rho_p}{(W_d \, \rho_w)} \times 100$ | Volumetric moisture content | Dimensionless (%) |
| 7 | $\text{Soluble matter loss } \% = \dfrac{W_{di} - W_{df}}{W_{di}} \times 100$ | Mass lost to water leaching | Dimensionless (%) |

> Where: $W_d$ = dry weight, $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_t$ = weight at time $t$, $M_\infty$ = equilibrium moisture content, $h$ = specimen thickness, $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $\rho_p$ = polymer density, $\rho_w$ = water density.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Composites:** Moisture absorption in carbon-fiber/epoxy laminates causes **plasticization** and reduces the **glass transition temperature $T_g$** by 15–25 °C, risking hot-wet failure.
- **Electronic Encapsulation:** Epoxy molding compounds used for IC chips must have ultra-low moisture absorption (< 0.2 %) to prevent **"popcorn" delamination** during solder reflow.
- **Biomedical Polymers:** Hydrogels (PVA, PHEMA) are designed for **high moisture absorption** for contact lenses and wound dressings.
- **Food Packaging:** PET and HDPE bottles must resist moisture to maintain shelf life — a key quality control metric.
- **Nylon Gears & Bearings:** Nylon-6,6 absorbs 1–2.5 % water, causing **dimensional swell** (up to 0.7 % linear) which designers compensate for in tolerance stack-ups.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fickian Diffusion Equation for a Thin Plate

Starting from Fick's Second Law in one dimension (perpendicular to the plate faces):

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}
$$

**Boundary Conditions (for a thin plate of thickness $h$ immersed in infinite water bath):**

- $C(x, 0) = C_0 = 0$ (initially dry)
- $C(0, t) = C(h, t) = C_s$ (surface equilibrium concentration)
- Symmetry about mid-plane: $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}\Big|_{x=h/2} = 0$

**Solution using Separation of Variables:**

The standard eigenfunction expansion yields:

$$
\frac{C(x,t) - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2\pi^2 Dt}{h^2}\right]\cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{h}\right]
$$

**Integrating spatially over $0$ to $h$** gives the bulk-average normalized moisture uptake:

$$
\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^2}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2\pi^2 Dt}{h^2}\right]
$$

For short times ($M_t/M_\infty < 0.5$), the series collapses to:

$$
\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx \frac{4}{h}\sqrt{\frac{Dt}{\pi}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This linearization in $\sqrt{t}$ is the foundation of the **experimental method** — students plot $M_t$ versus $\sqrt{t}$ in the lab and extract $D$ from the slope.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A nylon-6,6 tensile specimen with dimensions $80 \text{ mm} \times 12 \text{ mm} \times 4 \text{ mm}$ (thickness $h = 4 \text{ mm} = 0.004 \text{ m}$) is dried in an oven at 50 °C for 24 h, yielding a dry weight $W_d = 4.8236 \text{ g}$. After immersion in distilled water at 23 °C for 720 h, the wet weight is $W_w = 4.8994 \text{ g}$. Compute (a) percentage moisture absorption, (b) the moisture uptake per unit volume, and (c) comment on the result.

### Part (a) — Percentage Moisture Absorption

$$
M\% = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100
$$

Substituting values:

$$
M\% = \frac{4.8994 \text{ g} - 4.8236 \text{ g}}{4.8236 \text{ g}} \times 100
$$

$$
M\% = \frac{0.0758}{4.8236} \times 100 = 1.5715 \text{ \%}
$$

### Part (b) — Moisture Uptake per Unit Volume

Sample volume:
$$
V = 80 \times 12 \times 4 \text{ mm}^3 = 3840 \text{ mm}^3 = 3.840 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Mass of water absorbed:
$$
\Delta m = 4.8994 - 4.8236 = 0.0758 \text{ g}
$$

Volumetric moisture content:
$$
V\% = \frac{\Delta m}{V \cdot \rho_w} \times 100 = \frac{0.0758}{3.840 \times 0.9975} \times 100
$$

$$
V\% = \frac{0.0758}{3.8304} \times 100 = 1.979 \text{ \%}
$$

### Part (c) — Analytical Interpretation

> A value of **1.57 %** is characteristic of polyamides (Nylon family) due to their abundant **amide (-CONH-) groups** that hydrogen-bond strongly with water. This places Nylon-6,6 in the **moderately hydrophilic** category — explaining its use in moist-environment gears but its dimensional instability in precision instruments.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classification of Common Polymers by Moisture Absorption

| Polymer Class | Example | Typical $M\%$ (24 h immersion) | Hydrophilicity | Typical Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Polyamide | Nylon-6,6 | 1.0 – 2.5 % | Moderate-High | Gears, textiles |
| Polyester | PET | 0.1 – 0.3 % | Low | Bottles, fibers |
| Polyolefin | HDPE | < 0.01 % | Very Low | Containers, pipes |
| Fluoropolymer | PTFE | < 0.01 % | Negligible | Seals, chemical tanks |
| Epoxy (cured) | DGEBA + TETA | 0.1 – 0.4 % | Low | Coatings, composites |
| Cellulosic | Cellulose acetate | 6 – 12 % | Very High | Films, membranes |
| Phenolic | Phenol-Formaldehyde | 0.3 – 1.0 % | Moderate | Electrical switches |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Moisture Absorption Test Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **laboratory procedure flow** as a block-level functional architecture, mapping each operational block, its inputs, and its outputs:

```mermaid

    E --> F[Data Recording at Time Intervals]

    G -- No --> E
    G -- Yes --> H[Final Wet Weight Determination Block]
    H --> I[Gravimetric Calculation Block]

    J --> K[Diffusion Coefficient Extraction Block]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00

    style I fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style L fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Moisture Diffusion Modes in Polymers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Surface Adsorption Stage] --> M2[Capillary Wicking Stage]
    M2 --> M3[Bulk Diffusion Stage]
    M3 --> M4{Saturation Plateau}
    M4 -- Yes --> M5[Equilibrium Reached]
    M4 -- No --> M2

    style M1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style M2 fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c
    style M3 fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#558b2f
```
