
## 4.3 Process Logic Matrix (Schematic Block Summary)

| Block ID | Functional Role | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specimen Preparation | Cut & polish to ASTM D570 geometry | Raw polymer sample | Machined test bars |
| Pre-Conditioning Oven | Removes residual moisture | Test bars at 50 °C / 24 h | Dried specimens |
| Dry Weight Block | Records $W_d$ to 4 decimal places | Dried specimen | $W_d$ value (g) |
| Immersion Tank | Maintains 23 °C distilled water | Distilled H₂O + specimens | Wet specimens |
| Periodic Weighing | Records $W_t$ at intervals | Wet specimen + time log | Time-series data |
| Gravimetric Calc | Applies $M\%$ formula | $W_d$, $W_t$ | $M_t$ values |
| Curve Plotting | $M_t$ vs $\sqrt{t}$ graph | Time series | Linear slope $S$ |
| Diffusion Extraction | Computes $D$ from slope | $S$, $M_\infty$, $h$ | $D$ value $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]**

### Q1. Define moisture absorption in polymers. Mention any one standard test method used to evaluate it.

**Model Answer:**
Moisture absorption is the phenomenon by which a polymer matrix takes up water molecules from its surrounding environment, resulting in a measurable increase in mass. The standard test method is **ASTM D570** (or ISO 62), in which a dried specimen is immersed in distilled water at **23 °C ± 1 °C**, and the weight gain is recorded gravimetrically at regular intervals until saturation equilibrium is achieved.

### Q2. Differentiate between hydrophilic and hydrophobic polymers with one example each.

**Model Answer:**
- **Hydrophilic polymers** possess polar functional groups (–OH, –CONH–, –COOH) that hydrogen-bond with water, leading to high moisture uptake. *Example: Nylon-6,6* (absorbs ~1.5 % in 24 h).
- **Hydrophobic polymers** have non-polar molecular backbones (–CH₂–, –CF₂–) that repel water, resulting in negligible absorption. *Example: PTFE* (absorbs < 0.01 %).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Module 2 Internal Choice]**

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the short-time Fickian approximation for moisture uptake in a thin polymer plate and explain how the diffusion coefficient $D$ can be obtained experimentally from the slope of an absorption curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A HDPE specimen of thickness **3 mm** absorbs water such that at $t = 100 \text{ h}$, $M_t = 0.005\%$ and equilibrium moisture content $M_\infty = 0.020\%$. Calculate the diffusion coefficient $D$. **(7 marks)**

#### 🔑 Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Starting from Fick's Second Law:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}
$$

For a thin plate of thickness $h$ with surface concentration $C_s$ and initial bulk concentration zero, the solution integrated over thickness yields:

$$
\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^2}\exp\!\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2\pi^2 Dt}{h^2}\right]
$$

For **short times** where $M_t/M_\infty < 0.5$, the dominant first term gives:

$$
\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx \frac{4}{h}\sqrt{\frac{Dt}{\pi}}
$$

Rearranging to isolate $M_t$:

$$
M_t \approx \frac{4 M_\infty}{h}\sqrt{\frac{D}{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{t}
$$

This is a **linear equation in $\sqrt{t}$**. Plotting $M_t$ versus $\sqrt{t}$ gives a straight line through the origin with slope:

$$
S = \frac{4 M_\infty}{h}\sqrt{\frac{D}{\pi}}
$$

Solving for $D$:

$$
D = \frac{\pi S^2 h^2}{16 M_\infty^2}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $h = 3 \text{ mm} = 0.003 \text{ m}$, $t = 100 \text{ h} = 360{,}000 \text{ s}$, $M_t = 0.005\%$, $M_\infty = 0.020\%$.

Ratio:
$$
\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{0.005}{0.020} = 0.25
$$

Slope $S$ (per $\sqrt{t}$ basis):
$$
S = \frac{M_t}{\sqrt{t}} = \frac{0.25}{\sqrt{100}} = 0.025 \text{ per }\sqrt{\text{h}}
$$

Apply the formula:
$$
D = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2 (0.003)^2}{16 (0.020)^2} \text{ m}^2/\text{h}
$$

$$
D = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}{16 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
D = \frac{1.767 \times 10^{-8}}{6.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.76 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{h}
$$

Converting to SI units ($1 \text{ h} = 3600 \text{ s}$):

$$
D = \frac{2.76 \times 10^{-6}}{3600} = 7.67 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students commonly **forget to convert $h$ from mm to m** before squaring — this introduces an error of $10^6$ orders of magnitude.
> - Always **check $M_t / M_\infty < 0.5$** to justify the short-time approximation; otherwise the full series solution must be used.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** List and explain **four factors** that influence moisture absorption in polymers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyamide (Nylon-6) specimen with dry weight **2.500 g** absorbs water over 720 h. The wet weight recorded is **2.587 g**. Calculate the percentage moisture absorption and discuss its effect on the **dimensional stability** and **tensile strength** of the part. **(7 marks)**

#### 🔑 Model Solution

**Part (a) — Four Factors:**

1. **Chemical Structure of the Polymer:** Presence of polar groups (–OH, –CONH–, –COOH, –NH₂) increases absorption due to hydrogen bonding with water. Example: Nylon-6 vs HDPE.

2. **Crystallinity:** Higher crystalline content reduces moisture absorption because crystalline regions are tightly packed and impermeable to water molecules.

3. **Crosslink Density:** Increased crosslinking reduces free volume between chains, restricting water diffusion and lowering equilibrium uptake.

4. **Environmental Conditions:** Higher temperature increases both diffusion rate and equilibrium uptake. Higher relative humidity increases the surface concentration $C_s$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

$$
M\% = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{2.587 - 2.500}{2.500} \times 100
$$

$$
M\% = \frac{0.087}{2.500} \times 100 = 3.48 \text{ \%}
$$

**Effect on Dimensional Stability:** Nylon-6 absorbs ~3.48 % water, causing a **linear dimensional swell of 0.7 – 1.0 %**. Designers must apply **negative tolerance compensation** (i.e., undersize molded parts) to ensure dimensional conformance after moisture conditioning.

**Effect on Tensile Strength:** Water acts as a **plasticizer**, increasing chain mobility and free volume. This typically **reduces tensile strength by 15 – 30 %** and **increases elongation at break**. The effect is partially reversible upon re-drying, but permanent hydrolytic chain scission may occur in prolonged hot-wet exposure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Many students report only the numerical $M\%$ and **forget the qualitative discussion** of property changes — losing up to 3 marks.
> - Always state **both** the plasticization effect AND the dimensional swell effect for full credit.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Moisture absorption is a **gravimetric test** governed by Fick's Laws of Diffusion.
- ✅ Standard tests: **ASTM D570 / ISO 62** at **23 °C in distilled water**.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $M\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$
- ✅ Hydrophilic polymers (Nylon, cellulose) absorb more than hydrophobic (PTFE, HDPE, PE).
- ✅ Short-time Fickian approximation: $M_t/M_\infty \approx \dfrac{4}{h}\sqrt{Dt/\pi}$
- ✅ Diffusion coefficient extracted from **slope of $M_t$ vs $\sqrt{t}$** plot.
- ✅ Moisture absorption causes **plasticization** (↓ $T_g$, ↓ tensile strength, ↑ elongation).
- ✅ Equilibrium moisture uptake decreases with **↑ crystallinity, ↑ crosslink density, ↑ filler loading**.
- ✅ Engineering relevance: **composite delamination, IC packaging popcorn effect, nylon gear tolerances**.
- ✅ Always convert all units to **SI (m, s, kg)** before applying the diffusion formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: moisture regain optical properties and fibre friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** moisture regain optical properties and fibre friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Moisture Regain (MR)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Moisture Regain is defined as the mass of water present in a textile material, expressed as a percentage of the **oven-dry mass** of the material, measured under standard atmospheric conditions (**65 ± 2% Relative Humidity** and **20 ± 2 °C** temperature) as per **ISO 139** and **ASTM D2654** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Atmosphere for Textile Testing:**
> - **Temperature:** $20 \pm 2^\circ \text{C}$
> - **Relative Humidity (RH):** $65 \pm 2\%$
> - **Standard Body:** ISO 139 / BIS 6359

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$MR (\%)= \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$

Where:
- $W_w$ = Mass of the conditioned (wet) fibre sample under standard atmosphere
- $W_d$ = Mass of the **oven-dry** fibre (dried at $105 \pm 3^\circ \text{C}$ until constant mass)

> [!NOTE]
> **Moisture Content (MC) vs Moisture Regain (MR)** — A common KTU trap!
> Moisture Content uses the **wet mass** as the denominator, while Moisture Regain uses the **dry mass**. They are **NOT** the same value, though they are related by:
>
> $$MR = \frac{MC}{100 - MC} \times 100 \quad \text{and} \quad MC = \frac{MR}{100 + MR} \times 100$$

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **kitchen sponge**. After you soak it and squeeze it gently, it still feels damp and holds water. The **Moisture Regain** is like asking — *"Out of the sponge's completely bone-dry weight, what percentage of water is it still clinging to?"* Different fibres behave like different sponges — *cotton is a thirsty sponge* (high MR ~8.5%), while *polyester is a hydrophobic plastic sheet* (low MR ~0.4%). This difference dictates how a fibre feels, dyes, conducts electricity, and behaves during spinning.

**Why Moisture Regain Matters (Engineering Relevance):**
- **Mass-based commercial trading** of fibres uses MR to convert between *bone-dry mass* and *commercial mass*.
- Determines **dye uptake** and **dye affinity**.
- Affects **static electricity buildup** during processing.
- Influences **dimensional stability** of yarns and fabrics.
- Governs **comfort properties** in apparel (hygroscopic fibres feel less clammy).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Anchor — Standard MR Values (must memorize):**
> | Fibre | MR (%) at 65% RH |
> |---|---|
> | Cotton | 8.5 |
> | Wool | 16.0 |
> | Silk | 11.0 |
> | Viscose Rayon | 13.0 |
> | Polyester (PET) | 0.4 |
> | Nylon 6 | 4.5 |
> | Acrylic | 2.0 |
> | Glass Fibre | 0.0 |

---

## 1.2 Optical Properties of Fibres

**Formal Definition:**
The optical properties of a fibre refer to its interactions with incident light, encompassing **luster (gloss), brightness, transparency, refractive index (RI), and birefringence (double refraction)**. These properties determine the visual appeal, dye behaviour, and aesthetic quality of textile materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Key Optical Properties Studied in KTU Module 2:**
> 1. **Luster** — Specular (mirror-like) reflection from fibre surface.
> 2. **Brightness** — Diffuse reflection in the visible spectrum (whiteness).
> 3. **Transparency** — Ability of light to pass through the fibre body.
> 4. **Refractive Index (n)** — Ratio of velocity of light in vacuum to that in the fibre.
> 5. **Birefringence (Δn)** — Difference between refractive indices along two principal axes of an anisotropic fibre.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of light hitting a **glass marble** vs. a **wax candle**:
- The **glass marble** is *transparent* — light passes through, but its surface *shines* (luster).
- The **wax candle** is *translucent* — light enters, scatters, and gives a soft, milky glow (low luster, diffused brightness).
- A fibre is like a long, thin glass cylinder — the **smoother** its surface, the more **mirror-like** the shine. If the fibre has internal crystals or voids, light scatters and you get a **matte** look.

**Refractive Index — The Core Physical Quantity:**

$$n = \frac{c}{v} = \frac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r}$$

Where $c$ is the velocity of light in vacuum and $v$ is the velocity of light in the fibre medium.

**Birefringence (Δn) — The Anisotropy Marker:**

$$\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$$

Where:
- $n_{\parallel}$ = Refractive index **parallel** to the fibre axis (along molecular chain)
- $n_{\perp}$ = Refractive index **perpendicular** to the fibre axis
- A higher $\Delta n$ indicates greater **molecular orientation** and crystallinity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Critical Insight:** *Natural fibres* (cotton, wool, silk) have low-to-moderate birefringence. *Synthetic fibres* (drawn PET, drawn Nylon) have very high birefringence because the drawing process aligns polymer chains along the fibre axis. This is why synthetic fibres shine brightly — their aligned structure acts like a *waveguide* for light.

---

## 1.3 Fibre Friction

**Formal Definition:**
Fibre friction is the **tangential resistance** developed at the contact interface between a fibre and another surface (fibre-fibre or fibre-metal) when subjected to a relative motion or a tendency to move. It is governed by the classical **Amonton's Laws of Friction** adapted for textile systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Principal Types of Fibre Friction:**
> 1. **Static Friction ($F_s$)** — Resistance to *initiate* motion. Always greater than kinetic friction.
> 2. **Kinetic / Dynamic Friction ($F_k$)** — Resistance to *sustained* motion.
>
> In textile processing, the difference $(F_s - F_k)$ is called the **"frictional differential"** and is critical for **drafting, drafting waves, and yarn formation**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine trying to push a **heavy wooden box** across a rough concrete floor.
- The moment you first push, the box *refuses to budge* — that resistance is **static friction**.
- Once it starts sliding, it becomes *slightly easier* to keep pushing — that lower resistance is **kinetic friction**.
- For fibres, this same principle governs how fibres slide past each other in a drafting zone. A high static friction means fibres *grip* each other well (good for cohesion), but excessive friction causes **drafting waves** and **yarn breaks**.

**The Empirical Equation for Fibre Friction (Capstan / Euler Equation form):**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

Where:
- $T_1$ = Incoming tension (tight side)
- $T_2$ = Outgoing tension (slack side)
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction
- $\theta$ = Wrap angle in radians

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the famous **Euler-Eytelwein Capstan Equation**, which KTU examiners frequently test under "fibre friction" or "yarn tension over guides" questions. It is foundational in **winding, warping, and weaving** machinery design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Moisture Regain — Theoretical Deep-Dive

### The Underlying Thermodynamics

The sorption of water by fibres is governed by the **D'Arcy sorption isotherm** and the **BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) multilayer adsorption theory**. Water molecules attach to:
1. **Primary sorption sites** — Hydrophilic groups (-OH, -COOH, -CONH-) via **hydrogen bonding**.
2. **Secondary sorption sites** — Capillary condensation in micro-voids (predominant in cellulosic fibres like cotton).

### The Logic Flow — Step by Step

1. **Hygroscopic fibres** (cotton, wool, silk) have polar groups that form **hydrogen bonds** with $H_2O$ molecules.
2. As **Relative Humidity (RH)** rises, water molecules progressively occupy available sorption sites.
3. The relationship is **non-linear** — a sigmoidal (S-shaped) curve is obtained when MR is plotted vs. RH.
4. At **100% RH**, the fibre reaches its **Moisture Regain at Saturation** — a value much higher than the standard 65% RH regain.

### The Conditioning Principle (Why Standard Atmosphere?)

Textile fibres are **hygroscopic** — they constantly exchange moisture with the surrounding air. To obtain **reproducible** measurements, KTU/ISO mandates conditioning at **65% RH, 20°C** because:
- This represents a typical human comfort environment.
- It corresponds to a stable mid-point of the sorption isotherm where MR changes are minimal per % RH change.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** If a question asks *"Why is oven-dry mass used as the denominator in MR?"* — the precise answer is: *Because MR is a fundamental property of the fibre's chemical structure, independent of the absolute humidity on the day of testing. Using dry mass as the reference makes MR an intrinsic, comparable property.*

### KTU Formula Sheet — Moisture Regain

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MR = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Moisture Regain (% of dry mass) | % |
| 2 | $MC = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | Moisture Content (% of wet mass) | % |
| 3 | $MR = \dfrac{100 \cdot MC}{100 - MC}$ | Conversion: MC → MR | % |
| 4 | $MC = \dfrac{100 \cdot MR}{100 + MR}$ | Conversion: MR → MC | % |
| 5 | $W_{commercial} = W_{dry} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{MR_o}{100}\right)$ | Commercial mass (with **official regain** $MR_o$) | grams |
| 6 | $\Delta W = W_d \times \dfrac{MR}{100}$ | Mass of water held in fibre | grams |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official Regain vs. Actual Regain:** Many countries assign a *legal* regain value (e.g., cotton = 8.5%, wool = 16%) for **commercial invoicing**. This is the *Official Regain* ($MR_o$). It may differ from the *actual* regain measured in the lab.

---

## 2.2 Optical Properties — Theoretical Deep-Dive

### The Physics of Fibre Optics (at a Micro-Scale)

When a light ray strikes a cylindrical fibre, four interactions occur:

1. **Specular Reflection** — From the smooth fibre surface, governed by **Fresnel's laws**.
2. **Refraction** — Light enters the fibre body, bending according to **Snell's Law**.
3. **Internal Reflection** — Light bounces off the back inner surface (if $n_{fibre} > n_{air}$).
4. **Scattering** — Caused by crystallites, voids, delustrant particles (e.g., $TiO_2$), and surface roughness.

### Luster Quantification

Luster is not a single number — it is a **ratio**:

$$L = \frac{\text{Specular Reflection Intensity}}{\text{Diffuse Reflection Intensity}}$$

- A perfectly **matte** surface has $L \to 0$.
- A perfectly **mirror-like** surface has $L \to \infty$.

### Birefringence and Molecular Orientation

$$\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp} = k \cdot f_o$$

Where:
- $k$ = Proportionality constant (fibre-specific)
- $f_o$ = **Herman's orientation function** (ranges from 0 for random to 1 for perfect alignment)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Critical Point:** *Birefringence is the SINGLE BEST indicator of molecular orientation in a synthetic fibre.* In a textile QC lab, $\Delta n$ is used to confirm whether a yarn has been adequately drawn. Undrawn yarn → $\Delta n \approx 0$. Highly drawn yarn → $\Delta n$ reaches a maximum plateau.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Optical Properties

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n = \dfrac{c}{v} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r}$ | Refractive Index (Snell's Law) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | Fresnel Reflectance (normal incidence) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$ | Birefringence | dimensionless |
| 4 | $L = \dfrac{I_{specular}}{I_{diffuse}}$ | Luster Ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta n = k \cdot f_o$ | Orientation–Birefringence relation | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T = 1 - R - A_{bs}$ | Transmittance (where $R$ = reflectance, $A_{bs}$ = absorbance) | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Birefringence measurements are used in:
> - **Process control** in melt-spinning lines (PET, Nylon, PP).
> - **Quality assurance** of carbon-fibre prepregs.
> - **Polarized-light microscopy** for fibre identification.

---

## 2.3 Fibre Friction — Theoretical Deep-Dive

### The Amonton's Laws (Applied to Fibres)

1. **Law 1:** Friction force is **proportional to the normal load** (true for macro-bodies, but fibres deviate).
2. **Law 2:** Friction force is **independent of the apparent contact area**.
3. **Law 3:** Kinetic friction is **independent of sliding velocity** (also an approximation for fibres).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** For **flexible textile fibres**, Amonton's Laws hold only **approximately**. Because fibres are viscoelastic, friction depends on:
> - Contact pressure
> - Sliding velocity
> - Time of contact (load duration)
> - Surface roughness (both at micro and nano-scale)
> - Ambient humidity (water acts as a *lubricant* for most natural fibres!)

### The Howell Equation (Friction in Textile Materials)

$$F = a \cdot N + b \cdot N^n$$

Where:
- $F$ = Frictional force
- $N$ = Normal load
- $a, b$ = Empirical constants
- $n$ = Empirical exponent (typically 0.7 to 1.0 for fibres)

This is **Amonton's Law** when $n = 1$.

### The Two Friction Coefficients in Spinning

| Coefficient | Symbol | Description | Engineering Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fibre–Fibre (static) | $\mu_{s,ff}$ | Resistance to initiate inter-fibre motion | Determines **cohesion** in sliver/roving |
| Fibre–Fibre (kinetic) | $\mu_{k,ff}$ | Resistance to sustained inter-fibre motion | Determines **drafting behaviour** |
| Fibre–Metal (static) | $\mu_{s,fm}$ | Resistance between fibre and machine parts (guides, rollers) | Determines **tension build-up** in winding |
| Fibre–Metal (kinetic) | $\mu_{k,fm}$ | Resistance during sustained contact with metal | Determines **yarn tension** at delivery |

### KTU Formula Sheet — Fibre Friction

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \mu N$ | Basic friction equation | N (Newton) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | **Capstan (Euler) equation** | dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | $F = a N + b N^n$ | **Howell equation** for fibres | N |
| 4 | $\mu = \dfrac{F}{N}$ | Coefficient of friction | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta\mu = \mu_s - \mu_k$ | Frictional differential | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\theta_{wrap} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot r}{R}$ | Wrap angle in radians (r = arc length, R = radius) | radians |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility of Capstan Equation:**
> - **Winding machines:** To design the *pig-tail guide* or *traverse* tension.
> - **False-twist texturising:** Tension build-up on heater/cooling plates.
> - **Yarn path design:** Selecting guide-eye curvatures to control $T_1/T_2$ ratio.
> - **Friction spinning (Dref, Murata):** Designing the *friction roller* surface and wrap angle.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between MR and MC

### Given:
A fibre sample has Moisture Regain $MR$ and Moisture Content $MC$.

### Definitions Recap:
$$MR = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$MC = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Goal:
Derive the relationship $MR = \dfrac{100 \cdot MC}{100 - MC}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** From equation (i), let the mass of water be denoted as $M_{water} = W_w - W_d$.

Then we can write:

$$MR = \frac{M_{water}}{W_d} \times 100 \quad \text{and} \quad MC = \frac{M_{water}}{W_w} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** From the MR expression, solve for $W_d$:

$$W_d = \frac{M_{water} \times 100}{MR}$$

**Step 3:** Note that the total (wet) mass is:

$$W_w = W_d + M_{water}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expression for $W_d$ into the MC equation:

$$MC = \frac{M_{water}}{W_d + M_{water}} \times 100 = \frac{M_{water}}{\frac{100 \cdot M_{water}}{MR} + M_{water}} \times 100$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $M_{water}$ from the denominator:

$$MC = \frac{M_{water}}{M_{water} \left(\frac{100}{MR} + 1\right)} \times 100$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $M_{water}$:

$$MC = \frac{100}{\frac{100}{MR} + 1} = \frac{100}{\frac{100 + MR}{MR}}$$

**Step 7:** Invert the denominator:

$$MC = \frac{100 \cdot MR}{100 + MR}$$

**Step 8:** Solve for MR:

$$MC \cdot (100 + MR) = 100 \cdot MR$$

$$100 \cdot MC + MC \cdot MR = 100 \cdot MR$$

$$100 \cdot MC = 100 \cdot MR - MC \cdot MR = MR \cdot (100 - MC)$$

**Step 9:** Final result:

$$MR = \frac{100 \cdot MC}{100 - MC} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Moisture Regain (KTU Pattern)

### Problem:
A 150 g sample of cotton yarn is conditioned at standard atmosphere. After oven-drying at $105^\circ C$ to constant mass, the dry mass is found to be **138.0 g**. Calculate:
- (a) The **Moisture Regain (MR)** of the yarn.
- (b) The **Moisture Content (MC)** of the yarn.
- (c) The **mass of water** in the conditioned sample.
- (d) The **commercial mass** if official regain = 8.5%.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**
- $W_w = 150$ g
- $W_d = 138.0$ g
- $MR_o = 8.5\%$

**Step 2 — Calculate mass of water:**

$$M_{water} = W_w - W_d = 150 - 138 = 12 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Moisture Regain (MR):**

$$MR = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{12}{138.0} \times 100 = 8.6957\%$$

$$\boxed{MR \approx 8.70\%}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Moisture Content (MC):**

$$MC = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 = \frac{12}{150} \times 100 = 8.00\%$$

$$\boxed{MC = 8.00\%}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using the conversion formula:**

$$MR_{check} = \frac{100 \cdot MC}{100 - MC} = \frac{100 \times 8.00}{100 - 8.00} = \frac{800}{92} = 8.6957\% \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Calculate commercial mass:**

$$W_{commercial} = W_d \times \left(1 + \frac{MR_o}{100}\right) = 138.0 \times \left(1 + \frac{8.5}{100}\right)$$

$$W_{commercial} = 138.0 \times 1.085 = 149.73 \text{ g}$$

$$\boxed{W_{commercial} \approx 149.73 \text{ g}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The actual MR (8.70%) is slightly **higher** than the official regain (8.5%), meaning the cotton holds more water than the commercial assumption. The fibre is *moist* by commercial standards.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Capstan Equation (Euler's Belt Friction)

### Setup:
A yarn passes over a cylindrical guide (capstan, roller, or eye). The contact angle (wrap angle) is $\theta$ radians. The incoming tension is $T_1$ and the outgoing tension is $T_2$, with $T_1 > T_2$.

### Free Body Diagram Description:
Consider an infinitesimal arc $d\theta$ of the yarn. The tension on the leading side is $T$, and on the trailing side is $T + dT$. A normal force $dN$ and a friction force $\mu \cdot dN$ act on the contact surface.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Force balance along the radial (normal) direction:**

$$dN = T \sin(d\theta/2) + (T + dT) \sin(d\theta/2)$$

For infinitesimal $d\theta$, $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$:

$$dN \approx 2T \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} = T \, d\theta$$

(neglecting $dT \cdot d\theta$ as a higher-order infinitesimal)

**Step 2 — Force balance along the tangential direction:**

$$dT = \mu \cdot dN$$

Substituting the result of Step 1:

$$dT = \mu \cdot T \, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate from $T_2$ to $T_1$:**

$$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Perform the integration:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate both sides:**

$$\boxed{\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}} \quad \blacksquare$$

### Physical Interpretation:
- The **exponential relationship** means even small increases in $\theta$ or $\mu$ cause large tension build-up.
- This is why **texturising, winding, and knitting machines** are highly sensitive to guide-roller surface finish.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Capstan Equation

### Problem:
A yarn passes over a cylindrical guide with a wrap angle of $120^\circ$. The coefficient of friction between yarn and guide is $\mu = 0.30$. The incoming (slack-side) tension is $T_2 = 20$ cN. Calculate the outgoing (tight-side) tension $T_1$.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert angle to radians:**

$$\theta = 120^\circ \times \frac{\pi}{180^\circ} = \frac{2\pi}{3} = 2.0944 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the capstan equation:**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.30 \times 2.0944} = e^{0.6283}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential:**

$$e^{0.6283} = 1.8745$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_1$:**

$$T_1 = T_2 \times 1.8745 = 20 \times 1.8745 = 37.49 \text{ cN}$$

$$\boxed{T_1 \approx 37.49 \text{ cN}}$$

**Step 5 — Check sensitivity:**
If $\mu$ doubles to 0.6:

$$T_1' = 20 \times e^{0.6 \times 2.0944} = 20 \times e^{1.2566} = 20 \times 3.514 = 70.28 \text{ cN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Tension nearly doubled with $\mu$ doubling** — this exponential amplification is the practical reason why KTU textile engineers obsess over **chrome-roller surface finish, ceramic guide coatings, and yarn lubrication oils**.

---

## 3.5 Birefringence Calculation — Analytical Walkthrough

### Problem:
A PET fibre shows refractive index $n_{\parallel} = 1.68$ along its axis and $n_{\perp} = 1.47$ perpendicular to it. Calculate:
- (a) The **birefringence** $\Delta n$.
- (b) The **orientation function** $f_o$ (given $k = 0.245$ for PET).

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Apply the birefringence formula:**

$$\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp} = 1.68 - 1.47 = 0.21$$

$$\boxed{\Delta n = 0.21}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the orientation function:**

$$f_o = \frac{\Delta n}{k} = \frac{0.21}{0.245} = 0.8571$$

$$\boxed{f_o \approx 0.857}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An $f_o$ of 0.857 indicates **~85.7% of maximum possible chain alignment** — a typical value for a *moderately drawn* PET filament. If $f_o = 1.0$, the chains would be perfectly aligned (theoretically impossible, but ultra-drawn UHMWPE fibres approach this).

---

## 3.6 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Property | Physical Origin | Measurement Technique | Typical Instrument | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture Regain | Hydrogen bonding + capillary condensation | Gravimetric (oven + balance) | **Moisture Analyzer / Hot-Air Oven + Analytical Balance** | Commercial invoicing, dye selection |
| Luster | Specular vs. diffuse reflection | Goniophotometry | **Goniophotometer** | Fabric aesthetics, finish selection |
| Refractive Index | Electron density of polymer | Becke line method, interferometry | **Polarized Light Microscope / Abbe Refractometer** | Fibre identification, density estimation |
| Birefringence | Molecular orientation | Polarized light microscopy with compensator | **Polarizing Microscope + Berek Compensator** | Process control in melt-spinning |
| Static Friction $\mu_s$ | Inter-fibre contact adhesion | Inclined plane, twist method | **Friction Meter (Rothschild)** | Spinning cohesion, drafting |
| Kinetic Friction $\mu_k$ | Sliding contact resistance | Capstan method, yam-on-yam | **Friction Tester (Lawson-Hemphill)** | Winding tension, knitting smoothness |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Moisture Regain Measurement Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *complete procedural flow* of the moisture regain experiment, from sample preparation to data computation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Fibre Sample] --> A1[Pre-conditioning at 10 percent RH for 24 hr]
    A1 --> A2[Conditioning at 65 percent RH and 20 C for 24 hr]
    A2 --> A3[Weigh Conditioned Mass Ww using Analytical Balance]
    A3 --> A4[Transfer to Hot Air Oven at 105 C]
    A4 --> A5[Dry to Constant Mass - 4 to 6 hr]
    A5 --> A6[Cool in Desiccator for 30 min]
    A6 --> A7[Weigh Oven Dry Mass Wd]
    A7 --> A8[Compute Moisture Regain MR equals 100 times Ww minus Wd divided by Wd]
    A8 --> A9[Compute Moisture Content MC equals 100 times Ww minus Wd divided by Ww]
    A9 --> A10[Tabulate and Report MR and MC at Standard Atmosphere]
    A10 --> A11[Compare with Standard MR Table and Identify Fibre]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Optical Property (Birefringence) Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Fibre Specimen Mounted on Glass Slide] --> B1[Place under Polarizing Microscope]
    B1 --> B2[Align Fibre at 45 degrees to Crossed Polarizers]
    B2 --> B3[Observe Interference Colours - Michel Levy Chart]
    B3 --> B4[Insert Berek Compensator]
    B4 --> B5[Measure Optical Path Difference OPD]
    B5 --> B6[Measure Fibre Diameter d]
    B6 --> B7[Compute n parallel and n perpendicular]
    B7 --> B8[Compute Birefringence delta n equals n parallel minus n perpendicular]
    B8 --> B9[Compute Orientation Function f o equals delta n divided by k]
    B9 --> B10[Correlate with Drawing Ratio and Process Parameters]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Fibre Friction Measurement (Capstan Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Fibre or Yarn Wound on Capstan of Known Radius R] --> C1[Apply Known Incoming Tension T1]
    C1 --> C2[Measure Outgoing Tension T2 using Load Cell]
    C2 --> C3[Vary Wrap Angle theta incrementally]
    C3 --> C4[Plot ln T1 over T2 versus theta]
    C4 --> C5[Slope equals Coefficient of Friction mu]
    C5 --> C6[Repeat for Different Fibre and Metal Combinations]
    C6 --> C7[Tabulate mu s and mu k values]
    C7 --> C8[Correlate with Spinning Drafting Tension and Winding Stability]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Start: Need to Test Fibre Property] --> D1{What is the Goal?}
    D1 -- Commercial Mass Verification --> D2[Moisture Regain Test]
    D1 -- Aesthetic Evaluation --> D3[Optical Luster Test]
    D1 -- Process Control --> D4[Birefringence Test]
    D1 -- Spinning Performance --> D5[Fibre Friction Test]
    D2 --> D6[Use ISO 139 Atmosphere]
    D3 --> D7[Use Goniophotometer]
    D4 --> D8[Use Polarizing Microscope]
    D5 --> D9[Use Capstan or Rothschild Method]
```
