Calculate: (i) the **volume fraction of fiber**, (ii) the **theoretical density** of the composite, and (iii) the **weight of coir** required to make a **1 kg composite batch**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**1. Definition of Alkali Treatment (2 marks):**
Alkali treatment (mercerization) is the immersion of natural fibers in a **dilute NaOH solution (typically 3–6% w/v)** for a controlled duration, followed by neutralization and drying, to modify the fiber surface chemistry and morphology.

**2. Chemistry (2 marks):**
The key reaction is the deprotonation of fiber hydroxyl groups:

$$
\text{Fiber--OH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Fiber--O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

This:
- Removes hydrogen bonds, increasing surface roughness.
- Dissolves hemicellulose and partial lignin.
- Exposes more cellulose –OH groups for bonding with the matrix.

**3. Procedure (1.5 marks):**
1. Cut fibers to 5–10 mm length.
2. Immerse in 5% NaOH for 1 hour at room temperature.
3. Wash with distilled water + dilute acetic acid (to pH 7).
4. Oven-dry at 80°C for 24 hours.
5. Store in a desiccator.

**4. Effect on Fiber Properties (1.5 marks):**
- Tensile strength: ↑ from ~110 MPa to ~175 MPa.
- Crystallinity: ↑ from 33% to 42%.
- Water absorption: ↓ from ~85% to ~62%.
- Surface roughness: ↑ (better matrix adhesion).

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:** $W_f = 0.25$, $W_m = 0.75$, $\rho_f = 1.25 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$.

**(i) Volume Fraction of Fiber (3 marks):**

$$
V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{0.25 / 1.25}{0.25 / 1.25 + 0.75 / 1.20} = \frac{0.20}{0.20 + 0.625} = \frac{0.20}{0.825} = 0.2424
$$

$$
\boxed{V_f \approx 24.24\%}
$$

**(ii) Theoretical Composite Density (2.5 marks):**

$$
V_m = 1 - 0.2424 = 0.7576
$$

$$
\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m = (0.2424 \times 1.25) + (0.7576 \times 1.20)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 0.3030 + 0.9091 = 1.2121 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_c \approx 1.21 \text{ g/cm}^3}
$$

**(iii) Weight of Coir for 1 kg Batch (1.5 marks):**

$$
W_{coir} = 0.25 \times 1000 \text{ g} = \boxed{250 \text{ g}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–4 Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**

1. **Confusing brown coir with white coir** — students often mix up extraction times and applications. Brown coir uses *mechanical decortication*; white coir uses *lengthy retting*.

2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units (e.g., writing $W_{loss} = 11$ instead of $W_{loss} = 11\%$).

3. **Writing the rule of mixture equation as** $\rho_c = W_f \rho_f + W_m \rho_m$ **(weight-fraction form)** — this is **dimensionally inconsistent**. The correct form is the **volume-fraction version** $V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$.

4. **Ignoring the "OR" instruction** — students sometimes attempt both Q.A and Q.B, wasting time. KTU's rule: **only the first-attempted choice is valued**.

5. **Not citing the 5% NaOH optimum** — if a numerical question specifies alkali treatment, the answer must reference the **5% concentration / 1 hour / room temperature** standard, not a generic concentration.

6. **Forgetting the chemical equation** in alkali treatment answers — examiners specifically look for $\text{Fiber--OH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Fiber--O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (or equivalent).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Prep):**

- ✅ **Coir** = natural fiber from coconut mesocarp; lignocellulosic.
- ✅ **Two types:** White (immature, fine, slow retting) and Brown (mature, coarse, mechanical).
- ✅ **Key composition values:** Cellulose 36–43%, **Lignin 41–45%** (highest), Hemicellulose 15–25%.
- ✅ **Retting** = microbial hydrolysis of pectin → fiber separation. **Bacillus** and *Clostridium* are key bacteria.
- ✅ **Alkali (NaOH) treatment** = 5% NaOH, 1 hour, RT → improves surface roughness, reduces hydrophilicity.
- ✅ **Reaction equation:** $\text{Fiber-OH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Fiber-O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- ✅ **Rule of mixture (density):** $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ — **always volume fraction, not weight fraction**.
- ✅ **Weight loss formula:** $W_{loss} = \frac{W_b - W_a}{W_b} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Volume fraction formula:** $V_f = \frac{W_f/\rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}$.
- ✅ **Lignin-cellulose ratio (L/C)** = 1.0–1.25 — a unique coir "fingerprint."
- ✅ **Coir applications:** ropes, geotextiles, automotive seat backs, coir-cement boards, horticultural coir-peat.
- ✅ **Kerala produces ~60% of India's coir** — cite the **Coir Board (Kochi)** in long answers for regional relevance.
- ✅ **Coir is the only natural fiber resistant to seawater** — this is a **favorite KTU viva question**.
- ✅ **Microfibrillar angle of coir = 30°–45°** — high angle = lower stiffness but higher ductility (consistent with its high elongation).
- ✅ **Standard tensile strength (treated):** ~175 MPa; **Young's Modulus:** 3–6 GPa; **Elongation:** 15–40%.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam-Day Mantra:** *"Coir is nature's lignin-rich, saltwater-resistant, biodegradable reinforcement — Kerala's green gift to the polymer composite world."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: sisal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** sisal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Sisal** is a hard, structural **natural lignocellulosic fiber** extracted from the leaves of the *Agave sisalana* plant, belonging to the family *Asparagaceae* (formerly *Agavaceae*). Chemically, it is a bio-composite of three natural polymers — **cellulose** (the reinforcement), **hemicellulose** (the matrix binder), and **lignin** (the encrusting polymer) — together with minor amounts of pectin, waxes, and ash.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Definition:** In the context of polymer technology (PCPOL607), sisal is studied as a **natural fiber reinforcement** for fabricating **Sisal Fiber Reinforced Polymer Composites (SFRP / NFPC)** — a class of sustainable, bio-based engineering materials used as a substitute for synthetic fibers like glass and aramid in polymer matrices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **reinforced concrete column**. The steel rods inside carry the load, while the cement holds them together. In sisal fiber, **cellulose microfibrils** act like the steel rods, **hemicellulose** acts like the cement binding them, and **lignin** is the protective outer coating that holds the entire structure together. When this fiber is embedded into a polymer resin (like epoxy or polyester), we get a **Sisal-Polymer Composite** — a low-cost, biodegradable, lightweight structural material.

## 1.3 Botanical & Origin Snapshot

| Property | Detail |
|---|---|
| Botanical name | *Agave sisalana* |
| Native to | Mexico (Yucatán Peninsula) |
| Major producers | Brazil, Tanzania, Kenya, Madagascar, China |
| Leaf length | 1.5 – 2.0 m |
| Fiber length (usable) | **0.6 – 1.5 m** |
| Fiber diameter | **100 – 300 μm** |
| Density | **1.26 – 1.45 g/cm³** |
| Color | Cream-white to pale yellow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students are expected to identify sisal as a **hard fiber (leaf fiber)** belonging to the **bast/leaf-fiber category** of natural fibers, distinct from cotton (seed hair) and jute/flax (bast fibers).

## 1.4 Classification of Sisal Within Natural Fibers

Natural fibers are broadly grouped as:

1. **Bast fibers** — from stem (flax, jute, hemp, kenaf).
2. **Leaf fibers** — from leaves (sisal, abaca, banana, pineapple).
3. **Seed fibers** — from seeds (cotton, coir, kapok).
4. **Fruit fibers** — from fruit shell (coir — sometimes classified here).
5. **Stalk fibers** — from agricultural stalks (rice, wheat, bamboo).

Sisal is **unambiguously a leaf fiber**.

> [!TIP]
> A common exam question: *"Classify sisal fiber based on its origin."* — Correct answer: **Leaf fiber extracted from the *Agave sisalana* plant**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microstructure of Sisal Fiber

A sisal fiber is a **multi-cellular fiber** composed of numerous **ultimate fibers (fibrils)** bundled together by the middle lamella. Each ultimate fiber is a thick-walled, hollow spindle-shaped cell with a **lumen** at the center.

The cell wall of each ultimate fiber is itself a **laminated composite**, made up of three concentric layers:

1. **Primary wall (P)** — Outer thin layer, rich in pectin, lignin, and hemicellulose.
2. **Secondary wall (S)** — Thickest layer (provides mechanical strength), made of **cellulose microfibrils** oriented at a specific angle (microfibril angle ≈ 20°–22°) embedded in a hemicellulose–lignin matrix.
3. **Tertiary wall / lumen** — Innermost hollow region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Microfibril Angle (MFA)** is the single most important parameter controlling the mechanical behavior of sisal. A low MFA = high tensile strength and modulus (fibrils aligned with fiber axis). A high MFA = more elastic but weaker.

## 2.2 Chemical Composition of Sisal Fiber

| Constituent | Content (wt %) | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Cellulose** | 60 – 78 | Primary load-bearing semi-crystalline biopolymer (reinforcement) |
| **Hemicellulose** | 10 – 22 | Amorphous, hydrophilic binder matrix |
| **Lignin** | 8 – 14 | Rigid, hydrophobic aromatic polymer (encrustant) |
| **Pectin** | 0.8 – 10 | Gums the ultimate cells together |
| **Wax** | 0.3 – 2 | Surface hydrophobicity |
| **Ash / minerals** | 0.5 – 1 | Inorganic residues |

> [!NOTE]
> The **hydroxyl groups (–OH)** of cellulose and hemicellulose are responsible for the **hydrophilic nature** of sisal, which creates **compatibility problems** with hydrophobic polymer matrices (PP, PE, polyester). This is why **chemical surface treatments (alkali, silane, acetylation)** are mandatory in SFRP fabrication.

## 2.3 Mechanical Properties of Sisal Fiber

| Property | Typical Value (Untreated Sisal) |
|---|---|
| Tensile strength | **400 – 700 MPa** |
| Young's Modulus | **9 – 22 GPa** |
| Elongation at break | **2 – 7 %** |
| Density | 1.26 – 1.45 g/cm³ |
| Specific strength (σ/ρ) | **300 – 550 MPa/(g/cm³)** |

### 2.3.1 Why Specific Strength Matters

$$SS = \frac{\sigma_t}{\rho_f}$$

where $\sigma_t$ is tensile strength and $\rho_f$ is fiber density. This metric is used to compare fibers of different densities. Glass fiber has σ ≈ 2000 MPa and ρ ≈ 2.5 g/cm³, giving SS ≈ 800. Sisal has SS ≈ 400–500. Although sisal is weaker in absolute terms, it is **competitive in weight-sensitive applications** (automotive interiors, panels).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Tensile stress | Fiber strength calculations |
| $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's modulus | Stiffness |
| $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fractions (fiber + matrix) in composite | Composite design |
| $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | **Rule of Mixtures** (longitudinal modulus) | SFRP modulus prediction |
| $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures (longitudinal strength) | SFRP strength prediction |
| $\dfrac{1}{E_t} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | **Inverse Rule of Mixtures** (transverse modulus) | Cross-direction property |
| $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Density of composite | Weight estimation |
| $SS = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\rho_f}$ | Specific strength | Fiber selection |
| $\eta_l = \dfrac{\sigma_{c,\text{exp}}}{\sigma_{c,\text{RoM}}}$ | **Reinforcement efficiency factor** | Quality of fiber–matrix bonding |
| $W_{abs} = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | Water absorption (%) | Hydrophilicity test |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students **lose marks** in numerical problems by confusing $V_f$ (volume fraction) with $W_f$ (weight fraction). Always convert: $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sisal fiber reinforced polymer composites are deployed in:

- **Automotive industry:** Door panels, dashboard inserts, parcel shelves, headrests (Mercedes-Benz, Toyota, Ford have used natural fiber composites).
- **Building & construction:** Roofing sheets, ceiling panels, partition boards, door shutters.
- **Packaging:** Cushioning, biodegradable trays.
- **Agriculture:** Plant pots, mulch mats, geo-textiles.
- **Sports & consumer goods:** Bicycle frames (experimental), luggage, musical instrument cases.

> [!TIP]
> The driving force is **sustainability**: sisal has a **negative carbon footprint** during growth (the plant absorbs ~25 tonnes of CO₂/hectare/year), making the lifecycle of SFRP composites significantly greener than glass fiber composites.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rule of Mixtures for Composite Modulus

This is the **single most important derivation** in composite mechanics and is a **guaranteed KTU Part B question**.

### Setup

Consider a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite loaded parallel to the fiber direction. Both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain condition):

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

The total load carried by the composite is the sum of the loads carried by the fiber and matrix (force balance across an iso-strain cross-section):

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

Since $F = \sigma A$ and total area $A_c = A_f + A_m$:

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

### Derivation Steps

Dividing both sides by $A_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

For a uniform cross-section, the area ratios equal volume fractions:

$$\frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

Dividing through by the common strain $\varepsilon$ (using $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$):

$$\begin{aligned}
E_c &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m
\end{aligned}$$

> This is the **longitudinal Young's Modulus** of a unidirectional composite.

### Numerical Example for KTU Exam

**Problem:** A sisal fiber / polyester composite has $V_f = 0.35$, $E_f = 15$ GPa, and $E_m = 3$ GPa. Calculate the longitudinal Young's modulus.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_c &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\
V_m &= 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.35 = 0.65 \\
E_c &= (15)(0.35) + (3)(0.65) \\
E_c &= 5.25 + 1.95 \\
E_c &= 7.20 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer:** $E_c = 7.20$ GPa.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Density of a Composite

A simple but frequently asked problem.

**Problem:** Calculate the density of a sisal–epoxy composite containing 30 wt % sisal. Given $\rho_{sisal} = 1.35$ g/cm³ and $\rho_{epoxy} = 1.10$ g/cm³.

### Step 1 — Convert weight fraction to volume fraction

$$V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \frac{0.30 / 1.35}{0.30 / 1.35 + 0.70 / 1.10} \\
V_f &= \frac{0.2222}{0.2222 + 0.6364} \\
V_f &= \frac{0.2222}{0.8586} \\
V_f &= 0.2588
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the density rule of mixtures

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m \\
\rho_c &= (1.35)(0.2588) + (1.10)(1 - 0.2588) \\
\rho_c &= 0.3494 + 0.8153 \\
\rho_c &= 1.165 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer:** $\rho_c = 1.165$ g/cm³ — substantially lighter than neat epoxy.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Critical Fiber Length (Interfacial Shear)

For sisal fibers to carry load effectively in a polymer matrix, the **critical fiber length** $L_c$ must not be exceeded:

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

where $d$ is fiber diameter, $\sigma_f$ is fiber tensile strength, and $\tau_i$ is the interfacial shear strength.

**Numerical Example:** A sisal fiber has $\sigma_f = 500$ MPa, $d = 200$ μm $= 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m, and interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 5$ MPa.

$$\begin{aligned}
L_c &= \frac{(500 \times 10^6)(2 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times (5 \times 10^6)} \\
L_c &= \frac{1.0 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^7} \\
L_c &= 0.01 \text{ m} = 10 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** Sisal fibers shorter than 10 mm will be **pulled out** before breaking, wasting reinforcement potential. Practical sisal fiber length should be **> 20 mm** to ensure effective load transfer (typically $L \geq 2 L_c$ is recommended).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effects of Surface Treatments on Sisal Fiber

This is a **frequently asked 7-mark question** under the SFRP section.

| Treatment | Reagent / Condition | Mechanism | Effect on Tensile Strength | Effect on Water Absorption |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Untreated** | None | — | Baseline | High (~60 %) |
| **Alkali (NaOH)** | 5–10 % NaOH, 1–4 h, RT | Removes pectin, wax, lignin; exposes –OH | ↑ 20–40 % | ↓ Significantly |
| **Acetylation** | Acetic anhydride + H₂SO₄ catalyst | Replaces –OH with –OCOCH₃ | ↑ 10–25 % | ↓↓ Strongly |
| **Silane (e.g., KH-550)** | 3 % silane in ethanol/water | Forms Si–O–cellulose covalent bond | ↑ 15–30 % | ↓↓ Strongly |
| **Permanganate (KMnO₄)** | 0.5 % KMnO₄ in acetone | Forms MnO₂–cellulose complex | ↑ Moderate | ↓ Moderate |
| **Maleic anhydride (MAH)** | MAH-grafted PP (MAPP) | Ester linkage with –OH | ↑↑ Strong (best for PP) | ↓↓ Strongly |
| **Benzoylation** | Benzoyl chloride + NaOH | Replaces –OH with –OCOC₆H₅ | ↑ 15–25 % | ↓ Moderate |
| **Plasma / corona** | O₂ or air plasma | Surface etching, introduces polar groups | ↑ 10–20 % | ↓ Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **most economical and widely used** treatment for sisal is **alkali (mercerization)**. The most **effective for polypropylene composites** is **MAPP coupling agent**.

## 3.5 Fiber Extraction Process Flow

The complete extraction of sisal fiber follows a sequence called **decortication**:

1. **Harvesting** → Cut mature leaves from the plant (1.5–2 m long).
2. **Decortication** → Mechanically scrape the leaf to remove the pulpy mesophyll, leaving the long fibers.
3. **Washing** → Wash extracted fibers in clean water to remove the slimy juice.
4. **Drying** → Sun-dry for 3–7 days, or hot-air oven at 60 °C.
5. **Brushing / combing** → Align fibers and remove dust.
6. **Bailing & grading** → Bundle fibers by quality (long, medium, short grades).
7. **Surface treatment (optional)** → NaOH / silane / MAPP.
8. **Cutting & drying** → Chop to required length (5–30 mm) for composite fabrication.

> [!TIP]
> The KTU lab experiment PCPOL607/Module 2 typically involves the **alkali treatment of sisal fibers** followed by **hand-layup fabrication of a sisal-epoxy composite**, with subsequent tensile and water absorption tests. Be ready to write the experimental procedure and the chemistry of NaOH treatment:
>
> $$\text{Fiber–OH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Fiber–O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O (peeling of hemicellulose)}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a Sisal Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    leafA[Agave Sisalana Leaf] --> crossA[Leaf Cross Section]
    crossA --> epidermisA[Epidermis Layer]
    crossA --> fiberBundleA[Fiber Bundle Aggregate]
    fiberBundleA --> ultimateFiberA[Single Ultimate Fiber]
    ultimateFiberA --> lumenA[Central Lumen Hollow]
    ultimateFiberA --> wallA[Cell Wall Laminate]
    wallA --> primaryWallA[Primary Wall - Pectin and Lignin]
    wallA --> secondaryWallA[Secondary Wall S - Cellulose Microfibrils]
    wallA --> tertiaryWallA[Tertiary Wall Thin Lining]
    secondaryWallA --> microFibrilA[Cellulose Microfibril MFA 20 degrees]
    microFibrilA --> cellChainA[Cellulose Chain D Glucose Units]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SFRP Composite

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sisalRawA[Raw Sisal Fiber] --> treatA[Surface Treatment NaOH 5 percent]
    treatA --> washA[Washing and Drying]
    washA --> chopA[Fiber Chopping 10 to 25 mm]
    chopA --> matA[Fiber Mat Preparation]
    matrixA[Polymer Resin Epoxy Polyester PP] --> mixA[Resin Hardener Mixing]
    matA --> layupA[Hand Layup in Mold]
    mixA --> layupA
    layupA --> cureA[Curing at RT 24 h plus Post Cure]
    cureA --> testA[Specimen Cutting and Testing]
    testA --> tensileA[Tensile Test]
    testA --> flexA[Flexural Test]
    testA --> impactA[Impact Test]
    testA --> waterA[Water Absorption Test]
    testA --> morphoA[SEM Morphology]
```

## 4.3 Chemical Treatment Reaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cellA[Cellulose OH Hydrophilic] --> naohA[NaOH Treatment]
    naohA --> removeA[Removal of Hemicellulose and Lignin]
    removeA --> exposeA[Exposure of Hydroxyl Sites]
    exposeA --> silaneA[Optional Silane Coupling KH 550]
    silaneA --> bondA[Formation of Covalent Si O C Linkage]
    exposeA --> acetyA[Optional Acetylation Acetic Anhydride]
    acetyA --> hydA[Hydrophobic Surface OCOCH3 Group]
    bondA --> finalA[Strong Fiber Matrix Interface]
    hydA --> finalA
    cellA --> mappA[Optional MAPP for Polyolefin Matrix]
    mappA --> esterA[Ester Linkage with Cellulose OH]
    esterA --> finalA
```

## 4.4 Load Transfer Mechanism (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    loadA[Applied Tensile Load] --> matrixA1[Polymer Matrix]
    matrixA1 --> shearA[Interfacial Shear Transfer]
    shearA --> fiberA1[Sisal Fiber Carries Load]
    fiberA1 --> failA1[Fiber Fracture if L greater than Lc]
    shearA --> pullA1[Pull Out if L less than Lc]
    fiberA1 --> crackA[Crack Deflection at Fiber Tip]
    matrixA1 --> bridgeA[Fiber Bridging of Matrix Cracks]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. Define sisal fiber and mention its botanical source. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer:**
Sisal is a hard, structural natural fiber extracted from the leaves of the *Agave sisalana* plant. It is a lignocellulosic fiber composed mainly of cellulose (60–78 %), hemicellulose (10–22 %), and lignin (8–14 %). It belongs to the class of **leaf fibers** of natural fibers.

### Q2. List any three chemical constituents of sisal fiber with their function. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**
1. **Cellulose** – Provides tensile strength; primary load-bearing constituent.
2. **Hemicellulose** – Binds cellulose microfibrils together; contributes to moisture absorption.
3. **Lignin** – Provides rigidity, UV resistance, and hydrophobicity to the fiber.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **Module Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Either Question A **OR** Question B must be answered in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Treatment & Composite Mechanics

#### (a) Explain the chemical composition, microstructure, and mechanical properties of sisal fiber. Discuss how alkali treatment improves the fiber–matrix adhesion in sisal–polymer composites. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Chemical Composition:**
Sisal fiber is a lignocellulosic bio-composite. Its main components are:
- **Cellulose (60–78 %):** Linear polymer of β-D-glucose linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds. Highly crystalline; provides tensile strength.
- **Hemicellulose (10–22 %):** Branched amorphous polysaccharide; binds cellulose microfibrils.
- **Lignin (8–14 %):** Three-dimensional aromatic polymer of phenylpropane units; provides rigidity and hydrophobicity.
- **Pectin, wax, ash** — minor constituents.

**2. Microstructure:**
The fiber is a bundle of **ultimate fibers** held together by the **middle lamella**. Each ultimate fiber has a hollow **lumen** and a laminated cell wall (primary, secondary S1–S3, tertiary). The **secondary wall (S2)** contains cellulose microfibrils oriented at **MFA ≈ 20°–22°** to the fiber axis — this low MFA is responsible for the relatively high stiffness of sisal.

**3. Mechanical Properties:**
- Tensile strength: 400–700 MPa
- Young's Modulus: 9–22 GPa
- Elongation: 2–7 %
- Density: 1.26–1.45 g/cm³
- Specific strength: 300–550 MPa/(g/cm³)

**4. Alkali Treatment (Mercerization):**
Treating sisal with **5–10 % NaOH for 1–4 hours at room temperature** causes:
- Dissolution and removal of **hemicellulose, pectin, wax, and surface impurities**.
- Increased surface roughness → better mechanical interlocking.
- Increased surface area → more hydroxyl groups available for bonding.
- Reduced moisture absorption → better dimensional stability.
- Increased crystallinity index of cellulose.
- Reaction: $\text{Fiber–OH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Fiber–O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

The net effect is **20–40 % improvement in tensile strength** of the composite and **significant reduction in water absorption**.

---

#### (b) A unidirectional sisal–polyester composite contains 40 vol % sisal fiber. The sisal fiber has tensile strength 500 MPa and Young's modulus 18 GPa; the polyester matrix has tensile strength 35 MPa and modulus 3.5 GPa. Calculate (i) longitudinal tensile strength, (ii) longitudinal Young's modulus, and (iii) density of the composite. Given $\rho_{sisal} = 1.35$ g/cm³ and $\rho_{polyester} = 1.20$ g/cm³. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

Given: $V_f = 0.40$, $V_m = 0.60$
$\sigma_f = 500$ MPa, $E_f = 18$ GPa
$\sigma_m = 35$ MPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
$\rho_f = 1.35$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³

**(i) Longitudinal Tensile Strength (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_c &= \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m \\
\sigma_c &= (500)(0.40) + (35)(0.60) \\
\sigma_c &= 200 + 21 \\
\sigma_c &= 221 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_c &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\
E_c &= (18)(0.40) + (3.5)(0.60) \\
E_c &= 7.2 + 2.1 \\
E_c &= 9.3 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Density of the Composite:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m \\
\rho_c &= (1.35)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60) \\
\rho_c &= 0.54 + 0.72 \\
\rho_c &= 1.26 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Summary of Answers:**
- (i) $\sigma_c = 221$ MPa
- (ii) $E_c = 9.3$ GPa
- (iii) $\rho_c = 1.26$ g/cm³

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Treatment

#### (a) Compare sisal fiber with glass fiber as a polymer reinforcement. List the advantages and limitations of sisal fiber reinforced polymer composites (SFRP). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2022]

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table: Sisal vs. E-Glass Fiber**

| Property | Sisal Fiber | E-Glass Fiber |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Natural (leaf) | Synthetic (mineral) |
| Density | 1.26–1.45 g/cm³ | 2.50–2.55 g/cm³ |
| Tensile strength | 400–700 MPa | 2000–3500 MPa |
| Young's Modulus | 9–22 GPa | 70–85 GPa |
| Cost | Very low (~0.5–1 USD/kg) | High (~3–5 USD/kg) |
| Biodegradability | Yes | No |
| CO₂ footprint | Negative | High |
| Health hazard during handling | None | Respiratory irritation (fiberglass) |
| Moisture sensitivity | High | None |
| Fiber–matrix adhesion | Poor (needs treatment) | Excellent (with coupling agents) |
| Thermal stability | < 200 °C | > 500 °C |

**Advantages of SFRP Composites:**
1. **Low density** → Lightweight structures.
2. **Low cost** → Affordable for mass-market applications.
3. **Renewable & biodegradable** → Eco-friendly.
4. **High specific strength** → Good strength-to-weight ratio.
5. **Non-abrasive** → Low wear on processing equipment.
6. **Safe handling** → No skin/Respiratory irritation.
7. **Good acoustic & thermal insulation** → Suitable for automotive interiors.
8. **Negative carbon footprint** of the plant during growth.

**Limitations of SFRP Composites:**
1. **Lower absolute strength & modulus** compared to glass/carbon fiber.
2. **High moisture absorption** due to hydroxyl groups.
3. **Poor fiber–matrix interfacial adhesion** with hydrophobic polymers (PP, PE).
4. **Low thermal stability** (degrades above 200 °C).
5. **Variability in properties** depending on plant age, soil, climate, retting.
6. **Lower impact resistance** compared to synthetic composites.
7. **Limited processing temperature** window.
8. **Hygroscopic dimensional instability** in humid environments.

---

#### (b) Describe in detail the fabrication of sisal fiber reinforced epoxy composite by the hand lay-up method. What are the testing methods used to evaluate its mechanical and physical properties? [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Hand Lay-up Method — Stepwise Procedure:**

**Materials Required:**
- Alkali-treated sisal fibers (chopped 10–25 mm or unidirectional mat).
- Epoxy resin (e.g., LY 556) + hardener (e.g., HY 951) in 10:1 ratio.
- Releasing agent (silicone spray / wax).
- Mould (steel/wooden with polished surface).
- Roller, brush, weighing balance, vacuum desiccator.

**Procedure:**
1. **Mould preparation:** Clean the mould, apply a thin layer of releasing agent.
2. **Fiber preparation:** Cut sisal fibers to required length; oven-dry at 60 °C for 2 h to remove moisture.
3. **Resin preparation:** Mix epoxy resin and hardener in 10:1 ratio (by weight); stir gently to avoid air bubbles.
4. **Layering — First layer:** Pour a thin layer of resin into the mould; place a layer of sisal fibers; roll with a hand roller to wet the fibers and remove trapped air.
5. **Layering — Second layer:** Apply another coat of resin; place the next layer of sisal; roll again.
6. Repeat until the desired thickness (typically 3–6 mm) is achieved. The top layer is a thin coat of resin.
7. **Curing:** Close the mould, apply a small dead-weight (0.5–1 kg). Cure at **room temperature for 24 hours**, followed by **post-cure at 80 °C for 2 hours**.
8. **Demoulding:** Carefully remove the cured composite panel.
9. **Specimen cutting:** Cut specimens to ASTM standard dimensions using a diamond saw:
   - Tensile: ASTM D3039 (dog-bone, 250 × 25 × 4 mm).
   - Flexural: ASTM D790 (3-point bend, 127 × 12.7 × 4 mm).
   - Impact: ASTM D256 (Izod, 63.5 × 12.7 × 4 mm, with notch).

**2. Testing Methods:**

| Test | Standard | Property Evaluated | Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile | ASTM D3039 | Tensile strength, Young's modulus, % elongation | Universal Testing Machine (UTM) |
| Flexural (3-point bend) | ASTM D790 | Flexural strength & modulus | UTM |
| Impact (Izod) | ASTM D256 | Impact energy, toughness | Izod impact tester |
| Hardness (Shore D) | ASTM D2240 | Surface hardness | Durometer |
| Water absorption | ASTM D570 | Hydrophilicity, void content | Weighing balance |
| Density | ASTM D792 | Composite density | Archimedes' principle |
| SEM | — | Fiber–matrix interface morphology | Scanning electron microscope |
| TGA | — | Thermal stability, char yield | Thermogravimetric analyzer |
| DMA | — | Storage modulus, Tg, damping | Dynamic mechanical analyzer |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose 1–2 marks by:
> 1. Forgetting the **curing schedule** (RT 24 h + post-cure 80 °C / 2 h).
> 2. Mixing up the **ASTM standard codes** (D3039 vs. D638 for tensile; D790 vs. D7264 for flexural).
> 3. Not mentioning **rolling/de-bulking** to remove voids between layers.
> 4. Confusing hand lay-up with compression molding.
> 5. Failing to mention the **weight ratio of epoxy to hardener (10:1)**.
> 6. Omitting **pre-drying of fibers** (a mandatory step).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sisal Fiber & SFRP Composites (Module 2)**

- **Botanical name:** *Agave sisalana*; **Family:** *Asparagaceae*; **Fiber type:** Leaf fiber.
- **Density:** 1.26–1.45 g/cm³; **Tensile strength:** 400–700 MPa; **Modulus:** 9–22 GPa; **Elongation:** 2–7 %.
- **Chemical composition:** Cellulose 60–78 %; Hemicellulose 10–22 %; Lignin 8–14 %; Pectin, wax, ash (minor).
- **Microfibril angle (MFA):** ~20–22° → low MFA = high stiffness.
- **Lumen:** Hollow central channel in ultimate fiber → reduces density.
- **Critical fiber length:** $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$; practical $L \geq 2 L_c$.
- **Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal):** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$; $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$.
- **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (transverse):** $\dfrac{1}{E_t} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$.
- **Composite density:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
- **Weight–Volume conversion:** $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}$.
- **Water absorption formula:** $W_{abs} = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$.
- **Most common alkali treatment:** 5–10 % NaOH, 1–4 h, RT → removes hemicellulose/wax, improves adhesion.
- **Most effective coupling for PP matrix:** Maleic anhydride grafted polypropylene (MAPP).
- **Hand lay-up:** Epoxy:Hardener = 10:1; RT cure 24 h + post-cure 80 °C/2 h.
- **Key ASTM standards:** Tensile D3039; Flexural D790; Impact D256; Water absorption D570.
- **Advantages of SFRP:** Low density, low cost, biodegradable, high specific strength, non-abrasive.
- **Limitations of SFRP:** Hydrophilic, low thermal stability, variable properties, poor adhesion to hydrophobic matrices.
- **Major applications:** Automotive interior panels, door shutters, roofing sheets, packaging, geo-textiles.
- **Specific strength comparison metric:** $SS = \sigma_t / \rho_f$ — critical for weight-critical design.
- **Carbon footprint:** Sisal absorbs ~25 tonnes CO₂/hectare/year → negative carbon footprint of raw fiber.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: pineapple
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - Polymer Composites and Processing Laboratory
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Pineapple Leaf Fiber (PALF) Reinforced Polymer Composites — Extraction, Treatment, and Mechanical Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🥥 Module Experiment 2: Pineapple Leaf Fiber (PALF) Based Polymer Composites

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — PCPOL607 Context**
> Within the scope of polymer composites and processing laboratory, the term **"pineapple"** in this experiment refers to the **Pineapple Leaf Fiber (PALF)** — a lignocellulosic natural fiber extracted from the leaves of *Ananas comosus*. PALF is increasingly studied as a sustainable reinforcement in polymer matrix composites due to its high cellulose content, low density, and excellent specific mechanical properties.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pineapple Leaf Fiber (PALF)** is a multicellular, lignocellulosic natural bast fiber obtained from the leaves of the pineapple plant (*Ananas comosus*, family *Bromeliaceae*). It is classified as a **natural leaf fiber** and is composed primarily of **cellulose (70–82%)**, **hemicellulose (15–20%)**, and **lignin (5–12%)**, along with small amounts of pectin, wax, and ash.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *"Pineapple Leaf Fiber is a cellulosic natural fiber extracted from the mature leaves of the pineapple plant via decortication or retting processes. It exhibits a tensile strength of 413–1043 MPa and Young's modulus of 6.21–34.5 GPa, making it a high-performance natural reinforcement candidate for thermoplastic and thermosetting polymer composites."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a **brick wall** 🧱:
- The **mortar** between the bricks = the **polymer matrix** (epoxy, polyester, polypropylene) — it holds everything together and transfers load.
- The **bricks themselves** = the **fibers** (PALF) — they carry the mechanical load.
- Now imagine using **lightweight, hollow bricks made from pineapple leaves** instead of heavy clay bricks. The wall becomes **lighter but still strong** — this is exactly what happens in a PALF-reinforced composite. The fiber carries the mechanical load, while the polymer protects it and distributes stress.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Think of PALF as the "**green steel**" of the 21st century 🌿 — a renewable, biodegradable fiber that can partially replace expensive, non-renewable **glass fibers** (E-glass, S-glass) in automotive dashboards, door panels, and interior parts. Companies like **Toyota, Mercedes-Benz, and BMW** already use natural fiber composites to reduce vehicle weight and improve sustainability.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Properties of PALF

| Property | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Density | **1.44 g/cm³** |
| Cellulose content | **70–82 %** |
| Hemicellulose | **15–20 %** |
| Lignin | **5–12 %** |
| Microfibrillar angle | **14°** |
| Tensile strength | **413–1043 MPa** |
| Young's modulus | **6.21–34.5 GPa** |
| Elongation at break | **1.0–3.0 %** |
| Diameter | **20–80 μm** |
| Aspect ratio (L/D) | **~450** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A common student mistake is reporting PALF density as that of pure cellulose (**1.54 g/cm³**). The correct **bulk fiber density** is lower due to the presence of **lignin, hemicellulose, and void spaces** within the cell wall.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — PALF Extraction, Treatment & Composite Mechanics

## 2.1 Why Pineapple Fiber? The Engineering Rationale

Natural fibers are categorized into **bast** (flax, jute, hemp), **leaf** (sisal, PALF, banana), **seed/fruit** (cotton, coir), and **grass/reed** (bamboo, bagasse). PALF belongs to the **leaf fiber** group and stands out because:

1. **High cellulose content** (~80%) → better tensile strength.
2. **Low microfibrillar angle (~14°)** → higher stiffness than sisal (~20°) and coir (~30–45°).
3. **High aspect ratio (~450)** → efficient stress transfer.
4. **Abundant agro-waste** → pineapple leaves are discarded after fruit harvest, making PALF a **low-cost, sustainable** resource.

## 2.2 PALF Extraction Methods (Step-by-Step)

### Method 1 — Mechanical Decortication
1. Fresh pineapple leaves are harvested and washed.
2. Leaves are fed into a **decorticator** (raspador machine).
3. The rotating blade scrapes away the epidermal layer (waxy outer skin).
4. Long, white, lustrous fibers are extracted.
5. Fibers are **sun-dried for 3–5 days** until moisture content < 10%.

### Method 2 — Water Retting (Biological)
1. Leaves are immersed in **slow-running water** for 7–14 days.
2. Microbial action (bacteria/fungi) decomposes the pectin and lignin matrix.
3. Fibers are manually separated, washed, and dried.

### Method 3 — Chemical Retting (Faster)
1. Leaves are soaked in **NaOH (5–10%)** at 60–80 °C for 1–3 hours.
2. Alkali dissolves lignin, hemicellulose, and pectin.
3. Fibers are washed with distilled water until neutral pH.
4. Dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight — Method Comparison**
> - *Mechanical decortication*: Fast, eco-friendly, but produces coarser fibers.
> - *Water retting*: Produces high-quality, fine fibers, but **time-consuming** and produces **polluted wastewater**.
> - *Chemical retting*: Fastest, gives clean fibers, but **degrades cellulose** if NaOH concentration is too high.

## 2.3 Surface Treatment — The Key to Strong Composites

Raw PALF has a **hydrophilic** nature (due to –OH groups in cellulose and hemicellulose) while most polymers are **hydrophobic**. This mismatch causes **poor interfacial adhesion** between fiber and matrix. **Chemical treatments** are used to:

- Reduce hydroxyl groups.
- Increase surface roughness for mechanical interlocking.
- Remove lignin, wax, and pectin impurities.

### Alkali Treatment (Mercerization) — Most Common for PALF
1. PALF is soaked in **NaOH (4–6%)** at room temperature for **1–4 hours**.
2. Fibers are washed thoroughly with distilled water + dilute acetic acid to neutralize.
3. Dried in an oven at 60–80 °C for 24 hours.

> [!TIP]
> **Mechanism of Alkali Treatment (KTU Favourite Question):**
> $$\text{Fiber–OH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Fiber–O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
> This reaction converts hydroxyl groups into alkoxide ions, reducing hydrogen bonding between cellulose chains. The fiber becomes **less hydrophilic, more crystalline, and rougher** — improving bonding with the matrix.

## 2.4 Composite Fabrication Techniques

| Technique | Fiber Form | Matrix | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hand Lay-up** | Short/chopped PALF | Epoxy, Polyester | Panels, dashboards |
| **Compression Molding** | Short PALF | PP, PE, PLA | Automotive parts |
| **Injection Molding** | Short PALF pellets | Thermoplastics | Mass production |
| **Resin Transfer Molding (RTM)** | PALF mat/woven | Epoxy | Structural panels |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Composite Mechanics for PALF

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are required for solving KTU numerical questions on PALF composites.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fraction balance | — |
| 2 | $W_f + W_m = 1$ | Weight fraction balance | — |
| 3 | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$ | Weight fraction from volume fraction | — |
| 4 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Density of composite | g/cm³ |
| 5 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures — Young's modulus (continuous, aligned) | GPa |
| 6 | $E_c = \eta_l \, \eta_o \, E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | **Modified ROM** (short, random fibers) | GPa |
| 7 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures — Tensile strength (continuous, aligned) | MPa |
| 8 | $\sigma_c = \eta_l \, \eta_o \, \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | **Modified ROM for strength** (short, random) | MPa |
| 9 | $\nu_c = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Poisson's ratio of composite | — |
| 10 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse modulus) | 1/GPa |

> Where: $\eta_l$ = fiber length efficiency factor (0 → 1), $\eta_o$ = fiber orientation factor (= 1 for aligned, ≈ 0.2 for random 2-D, ≈ 0.4 for random 3-D).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive:** Door panels, dashboard inserts, parcel shelves (Mercedes-Benz A-class, Toyota Lexus).
- **Construction:** Ceiling panels, partition boards, roofing sheets.
- **Packaging:** Biodegradable trays, egg cartons.
- **Sports:** Surfboards, bicycle frames, tennis rackets.
- **Agriculture:** Seedling pots, mulching mats.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Volume Fraction from Weight Fraction

Starting from the basic definitions:

Let $M_c, M_f, M_m$ be masses and $V_c, V_f, V_m$ be volumes of composite, fiber, and matrix respectively.

$$V_f = \frac{M_f}{\rho_f}, \quad V_m = \frac{M_m}{\rho_m}, \quad V_c = V_f + V_m$$

The **weight fraction of fiber** is:

$$W_f = \frac{M_f}{M_f + M_m} = \frac{M_f}{M_c}$$

The **volume fraction of fiber** is:

$$V_f = \frac{V_f}{V_f + V_m} = \frac{V_f}{V_c}$$

Now, $V_f = \dfrac{M_f}{\rho_f}$ and $V_c = \dfrac{M_f + M_m}{\rho_c}$.

Substituting:

$$V_f = \frac{M_f / \rho_f}{(M_f + M_m) / \rho_c} = \frac{M_f}{M_f + M_m} \cdot \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_f}$$

Since $\dfrac{M_f}{M_f + M_m} = W_f$:

$$\boxed{V_f = W_f \cdot \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_f}}$$

Similarly:

$$V_m = W_m \cdot \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_m}$$

Substituting these back into the expression for $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \left( W_f \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_f} \right) + \rho_m \left( W_m \frac{\rho_c}{\rho_m} \right)$$

$$\rho_c = W_f \rho_c + W_m \rho_c$$

$$\rho_c (1 - W_f - W_m) = 0$$

Since $W_f + W_m = 1$, this is identically satisfied — proving internal consistency. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation — Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal Modulus)

**Assumption 1:** Fibers are continuous, aligned parallel to the loading direction.
**Assumption 2:** Perfect bonding between fiber and matrix (no slip at interface).
**Assumption 3:** Both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain condition):

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$$

**Step 1:** The total load on the composite is the sum of loads carried by fiber and matrix:

$$P_c = P_f + P_m$$

**Step 2:** Since $P = \sigma \cdot A$, and $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $A_c$, and note that $A_f/A_c = V_f$ and $A_m/A_c = V_m$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 4:** Using $\sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon_c$, $\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon_f$, $\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon_m$ and the iso-strain condition $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$:

$$\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Modified Rule of Mixtures for Short Random PALF

For **short, randomly oriented** fibers (which is the realistic case for PALF composites made via hand layup or compression molding), two correction factors must be introduced:

**Step 1:** **Fiber length efficiency factor ($\eta_l$)** accounts for the fact that short fibers cannot reach their full load-bearing capacity. The Kelly-Tyson model gives:

$$\eta_l = 1 - \frac{\tanh(\beta L / 2)}{\beta L / 2}, \quad \text{where} \quad \beta = \sqrt{\frac{-2 \, E_m}{E_f (1 + \nu_m) \, \ln V_f} \cdot \frac{G_m}{E_f}}$$

(For simplicity in KTU exams, $\eta_l$ is usually given as a numerical value between 0 and 1.)

**Step 2:** **Fiber orientation factor ($\eta_o$)** accounts for fiber misalignment:
- $\eta_o = 1.0$ → all fibers aligned along load direction.
- $\eta_o = 0.375$ → fibers randomly oriented in a plane (2-D).
- $\eta_o = 0.2$ → fibers randomly oriented in 3-D space.

For PALF composites processed via compression molding of short fibers, $\eta_o \approx 0.2$ is typical.

**Step 3:** The **modified rule of mixtures** then becomes:

$$\boxed{E_c = \eta_l \, \eta_o \, E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A PALF-reinforced epoxy composite contains **40% volume fraction of alkali-treated PALF**. Given:
- $E_f = 62$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- $\sigma_f = 600$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 45$ MPa
- $\eta_l = 0.9$, $\eta_o = 0.2$ (short, randomly oriented PALF)
- $\rho_f = 1.44$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³

**Calculate:**
(a) Density of composite
(b) Longitudinal Young's modulus
(c) Longitudinal tensile strength
(d) Weight fraction of PALF

**Solution:**

**(a) Density:**
$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (1.44)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60)$$
$$\rho_c = 0.576 + 0.720 = \boxed{1.296 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**(b) Young's Modulus (modified ROM):**
$$E_c = \eta_l \, \eta_o \, E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$
$$E_c = (0.9)(0.2)(62)(0.40) + (3.5)(0.60)$$
$$E_c = 4.464 + 2.10 = \boxed{6.564 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(c) Tensile Strength:**
$$\sigma_c = \eta_l \, \eta_o \, \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$
$$\sigma_c = (0.9)(0.2)(600)(0.40) + (45)(0.60)$$
$$\sigma_c = 43.2 + 27.0 = \boxed{70.2 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(d) Weight Fraction:**
$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m} = \frac{(1.44)(0.40)}{(1.44)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60)}$$
$$W_f = \frac{0.576}{1.296} = \boxed{0.4444 \text{ or } 44.44\%}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** Define pineapple leaf fiber (PALF). List any four important properties that make it suitable as a reinforcement in polymer composites.

**Model Answer:**
Pineapple Leaf Fiber (PALF) is a lignocellulosic natural fiber extracted from the leaves of *Ananas comosus*. It is composed of cellulose (70–82%), hemicellulose (15–20%), and lignin (5–12%).

**Important properties:**
1. **High tensile strength (413–1043 MPa)** — comparable to E-glass fiber on a per-weight basis.
2. **Low density (1.44 g/cm³)** — provides high **specific strength** (strength-to-weight ratio).
3. **High cellulose content (~80%)** — gives excellent stiffness and crystallinity.
4. **Low microfibrillar angle (~14°)** — promotes efficient axial load transfer.
5. *Bonus:* Renewable, biodegradable, low cost, and abundantly available as agro-waste.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for properties)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** Why is chemical treatment (alkali/mercerization) essential for PALF before composite fabrication? State the chemical reaction involved.

**Model Answer:**
Raw PALF is **hydrophilic** due to free hydroxyl (–OH) groups in cellulose and hemicellulose. Most polymer matrices (epoxy, polyester, PP) are **hydrophobic**. This mismatch causes poor interfacial adhesion and weak composites.

**Alkali treatment** removes surface impurities (lignin, wax, pectin), increases surface roughness, and converts –OH groups into alkoxide ions, thereby reducing hydrophilicity and improving matrix–fiber bonding.

**Chemical reaction:**
$$\text{Cell–OH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{Cell–O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**(3 marks: 1 for problem statement + 1 for treatment explanation + 1 for reaction)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various methods of extracting pineapple leaf fiber from pineapple leaves. Compare the advantages and disadvantages of each method. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Method 1 — Mechanical Decortication**
- Fresh leaves are passed through a **decorticator** (raspador machine) that scrapes the waxy epidermal layer using rotating blades.
- Extracted fibers are washed and sun-dried.
- *Advantages:* Fast, eco-friendly, no chemical use, suitable for industrial scale-up.
- *Disadvantages:* May damage fibers, produces uneven fiber length, leaves some epidermal residue.

**Method 2 — Water Retting (Biological)**
- Leaves are immersed in **slow-running water ponds** for 7–14 days.
- Microbial action breaks down pectin and lignin that bind fibers to the leaf matrix.
- Fibers are then manually separated, washed, and dried.
- *Advantages:* Produces soft, fine, high-quality fibers; low energy input.
- *Disadvantages:* Time-consuming; produces polluted water; weather-dependent; emits foul odor.

**Method 3 — Chemical Retting**
- Leaves are treated with **NaOH (5–10%)** at elevated temperature (60–80 °C) for 1–3 hours.
- Alkali dissolves lignin, hemicellulose, and pectin, releasing the fibers.
- Fibers are neutralized, washed, and oven-dried.
- *Advantages:* Fastest method; produces clean fibers.
- *Disadvantages:* Risk of cellulose degradation if alkali concentration/temperature is excessive; chemical handling hazards.

**Comparison Table (KTU board expects this):**

| Parameter | Mechanical | Water Retting | Chemical Retting |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time | Hours | 7–14 days | 1–3 hours |
| Fiber quality | Coarse | Fine, soft | Clean, strong |
| Cost | Medium | Low | High (chemicals) |
| Environmental impact | Low | High (water pollution) | Medium (effluents) |
| Industrial viability | High | Low | Medium |

**(7 marks: 2 for each method description + 1 for comparison table)**

---

**(b)** Discuss the effect of **alkali treatment (mercerization)** on the mechanical properties of PALF and its epoxy composites. Use experimental observations to support your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Effect on PALF Fiber Alone:**
1. **Cellulose crystallinity increases** — Removal of amorphous hemicellulose and lignin exposes more crystalline cellulose regions, increasing stiffness.
2. **Fiber diameter decreases** — typically from 60 μm (raw) to 40 μm (treated), improving aspect ratio.
3. **Surface roughness increases** — Creates mechanical interlocking sites for the matrix.
4. **Hydrophilicity decreases** — Reduced –OH groups improve compatibility with hydrophobic polymers.
5. **Tensile strength** increases by ~15–30% after optimal treatment (4–6% NaOH, 1–4 hr).

**Effect on PALF/Epoxy Composites:**
1. **Tensile strength** increases by 20–40% compared to untreated PALF composites.
2. **Young's modulus** increases by 15–25%.
3. **Interfacial shear strength (IFSS)** measured by single-fiber pull-out test shows significant improvement (typically doubles).
4. **Water absorption** decreases by 30–50% — improves long-term durability.
5. **Flexural strength** and **impact strength** also improve.

**Experimental Evidence (KTU board expects numbers):**
- Raw PALF: $E_f \approx 35$ GPa; Alkali-treated PALF: $E_f \approx 45$ GPa.
- Raw PALF/epoxy (30% Vf): $E_c \approx 4.8$ GPa; Treated PALF/epoxy (30% Vf): $E_c \approx 6.2$ GPa.

**Note:** Over-treatment (>8% NaOH or >6 hr) degrades cellulose and reduces fiber strength — an important **optimum** exists.

**(7 marks: 2 for fiber effect + 3 for composite effect + 2 for experimental evidence)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** State the assumptions involved in deriving the **rule of mixtures** for a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite. Derive the expression for longitudinal Young's modulus and tensile strength. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Fibers are **continuous, uniform, and aligned** parallel to the loading axis.
2. **Perfect bonding** between fiber and matrix (no slip at interface).
3. Both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain or Voigt condition).
4. The composite is **macroscopically homogeneous** and **free of voids**.
5. Loads are applied **along the fiber direction only** (no transverse loading).

**Derivation of $E_c$:**

Under iso-strain condition:
$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

Total load carried by composite:
$$P_c = P_f + P_m$$
$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

Dividing by $A_c$ and noting $A_f/A_c = V_f$, $A_m/A_c = V_m$:
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

Using Hooke's law ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$) with iso-strain:
$$E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m$$
$$\boxed{E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

**Derivation of $\sigma_c$:** Directly from the iso-strain loading step (ultimate tensile condition):
$$\boxed{\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for assumptions + 3 for $E_c$ derivation + 2 for $\sigma_c$ derivation)**

---

**(b)** A short, randomly oriented PALF/PP composite contains **35% volume fraction of alkali-treated PALF**. Given:
- $E_f = 55$ GPa, $E_m = 1.5$ GPa
- $\sigma_f = 500$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 30$ MPa
- $\rho_f = 1.44$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 0.91$ g/cm³
- $\eta_l = 0.85$, $\eta_o = 0.2$

**Calculate:**
(i) Density of composite
(ii) Modified longitudinal Young's modulus
(iii) Modified longitudinal tensile strength
(iv) Weight fraction of PALF
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Density of Composite:**
$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (1.44)(0.35) + (0.91)(0.65)$$
$$\rho_c = 0.504 + 0.5915 = \boxed{1.0955 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**(ii) Modified Young's Modulus:**
$$E_c = \eta_l \, \eta_o \, E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$
$$E_c = (0.85)(0.2)(55)(0.35) + (1.5)(0.65)$$
$$E_c = 3.2725 + 0.975 = \boxed{4.2475 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(iii) Modified Tensile Strength:**
$$\sigma_c = \eta_l \, \eta_o \, \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$
$$\sigma_c = (0.85)(0.2)(500)(0.35) + (30)(0.65)$$
$$\sigma_c = 29.75 + 19.50 = \boxed{49.25 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(iv) Weight Fraction of PALF:**
$$W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c} = \frac{(1.44)(0.35)}{1.0955} = \frac{0.504}{1.0955} = \boxed{0.4601 \text{ or } 46.01\%}$$

**(7 marks: ~1.75 marks per sub-part)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> **Three Most Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting correction factors** $\eta_l$ and $\eta_o$ in numerical problems. For **short, random** PALF fibers, the **modified rule of mixtures MUST be used**. Writing $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ directly will give a wildly wrong answer (often 5–10× the correct value).
>
> 2. **Mixing up $\rho_c$ formula** with $\rho_c = 1/(W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m)$ (which is for filler-loaded systems where $V_f$ is unknown). For known $V_f$, **always use** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
>
> 3. **Not stating assumptions explicitly** in the derivation part. KTU board examiners allocate **at least 2 marks** for clearly listing assumptions. Write them in a bulleted list, not as a paragraph.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- ✅ PALF = **Pineapple Leaf Fiber**; extracted from leaves of *Ananas comosus*.
- ✅ Composition: **Cellulose 70–82%**, Hemicellulose 15–20%, Lignin 5–12%.
- ✅ Density: **1.44 g/cm³** | Tensile strength: **413–1043 MPa** | $E_f$: **6.21–34.5 GPa**.
- ✅ Microfibrillar angle: **14°** (lower than sisal, coir → higher stiffness).
- ✅ Three extraction methods: **Mechanical Decortication**, **Water Retting**, **Chemical (NaOH) Retting**.
- ✅ Alkali (mercerization) treatment with **4–6% NaOH for 1–4 hr** is the most common surface modification.
- ✅ Chemical reaction: Cell–OH + NaOH → Cell–O⁻Na⁺ + H₂O.
- ✅ Rule of mixtures (continuous, aligned): $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
- ✅ Modified ROM (short, random): $E_c = \eta_l \eta_o E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
- ✅ For 3-D random fibers: $\eta_o \approx 0.2$; for 2-D random: $\eta_o \approx 0.375$; aligned: $\eta_o = 1$.
- ✅ Density formula: $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
- ✅ Weight–volume relation: $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$.
- ✅ Applications: automotive (door panels, dashboards), construction (panels), packaging, sports goods.
- ✅ Advantages over glass fiber: renewable, biodegradable, low cost, low density, less abrasive to processing equipment.
- ✅ Disadvantages: hydrophilic nature → moisture absorption, poor fire resistance, variability in fiber quality.
- ✅ Common ASTM test standards: **D3039** (tensile), **D790** (flexural), **D256** (impact), **D570** (water absorption).
- ✅ Always mention **assumptions** before any derivation — examiners award 2 marks just for that.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: jute
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** jute # JUTE — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPOL607):** > *Jute* is a **lignocellulosic natural fiber** obtained from the stem/bast of plants belonging to the genus *Corchorus* (family Tiliaceae), primarily *Corchorus capsularis* (white jute) and *Corchorus olitorius* (tossa jute). Chemically, it is a **natural composite** of cellulose microfibrils embedded in a matrix of **hemicellulose and lignin**, and it is studied in polymer science as one of the most important **renewable, biodegradable reinforcing fibers** for polymer composites. ## Intuitive Analogy — "The Rope of Nature" Imagine a **steel-reinforced concrete pillar**. The **steel rods** carry the load (tension), while the **concrete** holds them together and transfers stress. Jute fiber works on the exact same principle, but nature builds it from plants: | Component in Concrete | Equivalent in Jute | Role | |---|---|---| | Steel rods (rebars) | **Cellulose microfibrils** | Provide tensile strength | | Concrete matrix | **Hemicellulose + Lignin** | Bind fibrils, transfer load | | Cement paste | **Pectins & waxes** | Lubricate and protect | > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Highlight:** Jute is called a **"golden fiber"** because of its golden-silk luster, low cost (~**₹40–60/kg**), and high specific strength (strength-to-weight ratio comparable to glass fiber). ## Botanical & Chemical Origin - **Botanical source:** Bast (stem) fibers of *Corchorus capsularis* and *Corchorus olitorius*. - **Harvesting age:** 4–6 months (one of the fastest-growing crop fibers). - **Yield:** ~**2 tonnes/hectare** of dry fiber. - **Position in the stem:** Lies between the outer epidermis (skin) and the inner woody core, held together by pectin. ## Standard Composition of Jute Fiber (KTU Board-Standard Values) | Component | Composition (% by weight) | Function | |---|---|---| | **Cellulose (α-cellulose)** | **58–63 %** | Main load-bearing biopolymer | | **Hemicellulose** | **20–24 %** | Matrix binder, hydrophilic | | **Lignin** | **12–15 %** | Rigid aromatic matrix, UV resistance | | **Pectin** | **0.2–0.5 %** | Holds fiber bundles together | | **Wax & fat** | **0.4–0.8 %** | Surface lubrication | | **Ash & minerals** | **0.5–1.2 %** | Inorganic content | | **Moisture content** | **12–14 %** | At standard atmospheric condition | > [!TIP] > **Remember for KTU exams:** Jute has the **lowest cellulose content** and **highest lignin content** among common bast fibers (flax, hemp, kenaf). High lignin makes it **stiff but less flexible** and more **UV-degradable** than flax. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 1. Hierarchical Structure of Jute Fiber Jute exhibits a **multi-level hierarchical architecture**, which is critical for understanding its mechanical behaviour in polymer composites: ``` Level 1: Stem (plant) → Several cm Level 2: Fiber bundle (tow) → 1–5 mm diameter Level 3: Technical fiber → 50–200 µm diameter Level 4: Ultimate fiber → 5–25 µm diameter Level 5: Microfibril → 0.1–0.5 µm Level 6: Cellulose chain → ~1 nm (β-1,4-linked D-glucose) ``` ## 2. Chemistry of the Three Main Constituents ### (a) Cellulose — The Load Bearer Cellulose is a **linear, syndiotactic homopolymer of β-D-glucopyranose** units linked by **β-(1→4) glycosidic bonds**. $$\text{Repeat unit: } (C_6H_{10}O_5)_n$$ Degree of polymerisation (DP) in jute cellulose ≈ **700–900** (much lower than cotton ≈ 10,000). Each glucose unit carries **three free –OH groups** (at C2, C3, and C6), enabling extensive **inter- and intra-molecular hydrogen bonding** that gives jute its high tensile strength. ### (b) Hemicellulose — The Compatibiliser Matrix - A **branched, heterogeneous polysaccharide** (xylan backbone in jute). - Contains many **–OH and –COOH groups** → highly **hydrophilic** → responsible for jute's moisture absorption. - Acts as a **coupling bridge** between cellulose and lignin. ### (c) Lignin — The Rigid Aromatic Glue - A **complex three-dimensional aromatic copolymer** of **phenylpropanoid units**: *p-coumaryl alcohol*, *coniferyl alcohol*, and *sinapyl alcohol*. - Provides **rigidity, compressive strength, and resistance to microbial attack**. - Contains both **aliphatic and aromatic –OH groups** → reactive sites for chemical modification. ## 3. KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following equations are the **most-asked formulas** in KTU ESE/IA exams on natural fibers. | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Degree of Polymerisation | $DP = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | dimensionless | $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit (162 g/mol for cellulose) | | 2 | Density of jute fiber | $\rho_f \approx 1.3\text{–}1.5$ | g/cm³ | Used for specific-strength calculations | | 3 | Specific tensile strength | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_t}{\rho_f}$ | $\dfrac{N \cdot m}{kg}$ | Compare with E-glass: ~**1400** vs jute ~**2600** | | 4 | Moisture Regain (MR) | $MR = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Jute MR ≈ **12–14 %** | | 5 | Fiber Volume Fraction (rule of mixtures) | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m}$ | dimensionless | Used in composite micromechanics | | 6 | Composite tensile strength (ROM) | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)$ | MPa | Assumes perfect fiber–matrix bonding | | 7 | Young's modulus (ROM) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ | GPa | Voigt model (isostrain) | | 8 | Inverse rule of mixtures | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{(1-V_f)}{E_m}$ | GPa | Reuss model (isostress) | | 9 | Cellulose content (Kürschner-Hoffer method) | $\%\,Cell = \dfrac{W_{residue}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | % | Alcohol–nitric acid digestion | | 10 | Hydrophilicity (water uptake) | $WU = \dfrac{W_{sat} - W_{dry}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Jute can absorb **>30 %** of its dry weight | > [!WARNING] > In KTU answer sheets, **never** confuse **Moisture Regain (MR)** with **Moisture Content (MC)**. MR is based on dry weight; MC is based on original (wet) weight: > $MC = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$ — a common board-exam trap. ## 4. Why Jute is a "Natural Composite" (KTU Conceptual Hook) A jute fiber is **already a composite before we even reinforce a polymer with it**: - **Cellulose fibrils** = high-modulus anisotropic reinforcement (E ≈ **10–30 GPa** along fibril axis). - **Hemicellulose + lignin matrix** = isotropic, low-modulus binder (E ≈ **2–4 GPa**). - The cell wall has a **multi-ply laminate structure** (primary wall → S1 → S2 → S3), with microfibrils wound at a **helix angle (microfibrillar angle, MFA)** of **~8–12°** in the S2 layer of jute. > The **lower the MFA**, the **higher the tensile strength and stiffness** — this is why jute's S2-dominated structure gives it strength comparable to flax. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## Derivation 1 — Degree of Polymerisation (DP) of Jute Cellulose The molecular weight of jute cellulose is experimentally determined via **viscometry (Mark–Houwink equation)** or **osmometry**. Given the molecular weight, DP is calculated as follows: $$\text{Step 1: Identify the repeat unit of cellulose}$$ Cellulose repeat unit = one **anhydroglucose unit (AGU)**, formula $C_6H_{10}O_5$. $$\text{Step 2: Calculate molar mass of the AGU}$$ $$\begin{aligned} M_0 &= 6 \times M_C + 10 \times M_H + 5 \times M_O \\ &= 6(12.01) + 10(1.008) + 5(16.00) \\ &= 72.06 + 10.08 + 80.00 \\ &= 162.14 \text{ g/mol} \end{aligned}$$ $$\text{Step 3: Apply the definition of DP}$$ $$\begin{aligned} DP &= \dfrac{M_n}{M_0} \\ &= \dfrac{M_n \text{ (g/mol of cellulose chain)}}{162.14 \text{ g/mol}} \end{aligned}$$ $$\text{Step 4: Numerical example (KTU-style problem)}$$ **Given:** Viscosity-average molecular weight of jute cellulose $M_v = 1.2 \times 10^5$ g/mol. $$\begin{aligned} DP &= \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^5}{162.14} \\ &= 740.0 \approx 740 \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** A DP of ~740 means each jute cellulose chain has approximately **740 glucose units** linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds. This is **10–14 times lower** than cotton, explaining jute's comparatively **lower flexibility** but still high tensile strength due to dense H-bonding. --- ## Derivation 2 — Rule of Mixtures for Jute–PP Composite **Problem:** A jute fiber-reinforced polypropylene composite contains 30 % jute fiber by weight. Calculate the longitudinal tensile strength. Given: - $\sigma_f$ (jute fiber) = **400 MPa** - $\sigma_m$ (polypropylene) = **30 MPa** - $\rho_f$ (jute) = **1.45 g/cm³** - $\rho_m$ (PP) = **0.91 g/cm³** $$\text{Step 1: Convert weight fraction to volume fraction}$$ $$\begin{aligned} V_f &= \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m} \\ &= \dfrac{0.30 / 1.45}{0.30/1.45 + 0.70/0.91} \\ &= \dfrac{0.2069}{0.2069 + 0.7692} \\ &= \dfrac{0.2069}{0.9761} \\ &= 0.2120 \approx 0.21 \end{aligned}$$ $$\text{Step 2: Apply the rule of mixtures (longitudinal tensile strength)}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_c &= \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot (1 - V_f) \\ &= (400)(0.21) + (30)(0.79) \\ &= 84.0 + 23.7 \\ &= 107.7 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$ $$\text{Step 3: Interpretation}$$ The composite strength (≈ **108 MPa**) is **3.6× higher** than neat PP and is a direct consequence of jute's high fiber strength being transferred efficiently to the matrix. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **theoretical maximum**. Actual strengths are **30–50 % lower** because of (a) weak fiber–matrix interface, (b) fiber agglomeration, and (c) moisture-induced voids. KTU examiners love asking the **"why actual strength < theoretical"** part. --- ## Derivation 3 — Specific Strength Comparison (Jute vs E-Glass) **Definition:** Specific strength = strength ÷ density. $$\begin{aligned} \text{E-Glass: } \sigma_{sp}^{glass} &= \dfrac{2400 \text{ MPa}}{2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 941 \text{ kN·m/kg} \\ \text{Jute: } \sigma_{sp}^{jute} &= \dfrac{400 \text{ MPa}}{1.45 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 2759 \text{ kN·m/kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** Jute has a **specific strength ~3× higher** than E-glass. This is the **headline reason** jute is studied as a sustainable alternative to glass fiber. --- ## Analytical Matrix — Jute vs Other Natural Fibers (KTU Comparison Table) | Property | **Jute** | Flax | Hemp | Sisal | Cotton | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Cellulose (%) | 58–63 | 70–75 | 70–78 | 60–67 | 88–96 | | Lignin (%) | 12–15 | 2–5 | 3–5 | 8–12 | <1 | | Density (g/cm³) | 1.3–1.5 | 1.4–1.5 | 1.4–1.5 | 1.3–1.5 | 1.5–1.6 | | Tensile strength (MPa) | 400–800 | 800–1500 | 600–1100 | 500–900 | 300–700 | | Young's modulus (GPa) | 10–30 | 50–70 | 30–70 | 10–25 | 5–12 | | Elongation (%) | 1.0–1.8 | 1.5–3.3 | 1.5–3.5 | 3–7 | 6–9 | | Microfibril angle (°) | 8–12 | 5–10 | 6–12 | 18–25 | 25–45 | | Moisture regain (%) | 12–14 | 8–12 | 8–10 | 10–12 | 7–8 | | Cost (USD/kg) | 0.4–0.8 | 1.5–2.5 | 1.0–2.0 | 0.6–1.0 | 1.5–2.5 | > [!TIP] > **Board Tip:** The inverse correlation between **microfibril angle (MFA)** and **modulus** is a recurring viva question. Jute's **low MFA (8–12°)** is what enables its competitive stiffness despite low cellulose content. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Structure of Jute (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["STEM OF CORCHORUS PLANT"] --> B["BUNDLE OF BAST FIBERS"] B --> C["TECHNICAL FIBER STRAND"] C --> D["ULTIMATE CELL SINGLE FIBER"] D --> E["CELL WALL LAYERS S1 S2 S3"] E --> F["CELLULOSE MICROFIBRIL"] F --> G["CELLULOSE POLYMER CHAIN"] G --> H["BETA 1 4 D GLUCOSE REPEAT UNIT"] I["LIGNIN MATRIX"] --> E J["HEMICELLULOSE BRIDGE"] --> E K["PECTIN INTERFACE"] --> D L["CHEMICAL TREATMENT NAOH SILANE"] -.-> F M["PHYSICAL TREATMENT PLASMA CORONA"] -.-> D ``` ## Diagram 2 — Cell Wall Architecture (Lignocellulosic Composite Model) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CW["CELL WALL OF JUTE FIBER"] direction TB PW["PRIMARY WALL Pectin and loose cellulose"] S1["S1 LAYER Thin, random MFA"] S2["S2 LAYER Thickest, low MFA 8 to 12 degrees, max load bearing"] S3["S3 LAYER Inner lining, smooth"] end PW --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 subgraph ML["MATRIX LIGNIN AND HEMICELLULOSE"] MA["Binds microfibrils, transfers stress"] end S2 -.is reinforced by.-> ML note["MF = Microfibrils of cellulose
MFA = Microfibrillar Angle
Lower MFA = Higher Strength"]:::note ``` ## Diagram 3 — Jute-Polymer Composite Processing Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD R["RAW JUTE BAST"] --> R1["RETING 14 to 21 days in water"] R1 --> R2["STRIPPING AND WASHING"] R2 --> R3["DRYING Sun or oven 60 to 80 C"] R3 --> S["SURFACE TREATMENT Alkali NaOH 5 percent Silane Plasma"] S --> C1["COMPRESSION MOULDING 150 to 180 C"] S --> C2["INJECTION MOULDING 180 to 200 C"] S --> C3["HAND LAYUP WITH THERMOSET"] S --> C4["EXTRUSION WITH THERMOPLASTIC"] C1 --> P1["JUTE PP COMPOSITE SHEET"] C2 --> P2["JUTE PP COMPOSITE PART"] C3 --> P3["JUTE EPOXY COMPOSITE LAMINATE"] C4 --> P4["JUTE PE COMPOSITE PROFILE"] classDef proc fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000; classDef prod fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000; classDef treat fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#000; class S,C1,C2,C3,C4 treat; class P1,P2,P3,P4 prod; class R,R1,R2,R3 proc; ``` ## Diagram 4 — Chemical Modification of Jute – OH Groups ```mermaid flowchart LR A["CELLULOSE OH"] --> B["ALKALI TREATMENT NaOH"] B --> C["CELL O NA plus Na plus OH removed"] C --> D["SURFACE ROUGHNESS INCREASED"] D --> E["BETTER MATRIX ADHESION"] A2["CELLULOSE OH"] --> F["SILANE TREATMENT R Si OR 3"] F --> G["SILOXANE BRIDGE Si O Si"] G --> H["COUPLING WITH POLYMER MATRIX"] A3["CELLULOSE OH"] --> I["ACETYLATION CH3COOH"] I --> J["CELL O CO CH3"] J --> K["HYDROPHOBIC SURFACE LOWER MOISTURE"] classDef ch fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000; class A,A2,A3,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,I,J,K ch; ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid compilation note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `S1`, etc.), no reserved keywords are used, and node labels contain only raw uppercase/lowercase alphanumeric text with hyphens to preserve diagram integrity. ## Diagram 5 — Stress Transfer Mechanism at Fiber–Matrix Interface ```mermaid flowchart TD LOAD["APPLIED TENSILE LOAD"] --> MATRIX["POLYMER MATRIX PP or Epoxy"] MATRIX --> IFACE["INTERFACIAL SHEAR STRESS tau"] IFACE --> FIBER["JUTE FIBER LOAD BEARING"] FIBER -.fiber fracture or pull out.-> FAIL["COMPOSITE FAILURE"] MOD["MODIFICATIONS Alkali Silane Maleic Anhydride"] -.enhance.-> IFACE ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List the major chemical constituents of jute fiber and state the function of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Jute is composed of three major constituents: 1. **Cellulose (58–63 %)** – a linear polymer of β-D-glucose units linked by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds; provides **tensile strength and stiffness** to the fiber (1 mark). 2. **Hemicellulose (20–24 %)** – a branched polysaccharide; acts as a **matrix binder** between cellulose and lignin and is responsible for moisture absorption (1 mark). 3. **Lignin (12–15 %)** – an aromatic copolymer of phenylpropanoid units; imparts **rigidity, UV resistance and compressive strength** (1 mark). --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is jute considered a "natural composite"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Jute is inherently a **fiber-reinforced biocomposite** at the cell-wall level: - The **cellulose microfibrils** act as the **reinforcement** phase, providing high axial strength and modulus (≈10–30 GPa) (1 mark). - The **lignin and hemicellulose** form the **matrix** phase, transferring stress and binding the fibrils together (1 mark). - The cell wall has a **multi-ply laminate structure (S1, S2, S3)** with cellulose fibrils wound at a low microfibrillar angle (~8–12°), making it structurally analogous to a synthetic fiber-reinforced composite (1 mark). --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **hierarchical structure of jute fiber** from the macroscopic stem level down to the cellulose polymer chain. Sketch the chemical structure of the cellulose repeat unit. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **merits and demerits of jute fiber** as a reinforcing material in polymer composites. Compare its specific strength with E-glass fiber. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Hierarchical structure (7 marks):** The jute bast fiber exhibits six hierarchical levels: 1. **Stem** → plant of genus *Corchorus*, harvested at 4–6 months (0.5 mark). 2. **Fiber bundle (tow)** → 1–5 mm thick, consists of many technical fibers glued by pectin (0.5 mark). 3. **Technical fiber** → 50–200 µm in diameter, the commercial unit used in composites (0.5 mark). 4. **Ultimate fiber (single cell)** → 5–25 µm diameter, polygonal cross-section, hollow lumen (0.5 mark). 5. **Cell wall layers** → Primary wall + S1 + S2 + S3; S2 is thickest with cellulose fibrils wound helically at **MFA = 8–12°** (1 mark). 6. **Cellulose polymer chain** → linear β-1,4-linked D-glucopyranose units; DP ≈ 700–900 (1 mark). **Chemical structure of cellulose repeat unit (anhydroglucose unit):** $$\text{Cellulose repeat unit: } (C_6H_{10}O_5)_n$$ The β-D-glucopyranose ring contains: - **Three –OH groups** at C2, C3 and C6 — responsible for hydrogen bonding. - **One ring oxygen** between C1 and C5. - **β-1,4 glycosidic linkage** connecting adjacent units (1 mark). **Intra-chain H-bond** between C3–OH and the ring oxygen of the adjacent unit stabilises the chain. **Inter-chain H-bonds** between C6–OH of one chain and C3–OH of the neighbouring chain form the sheets that give cellulose its high axial stiffness (1 mark). **(b) Merits & Demerits (7 marks):** **Merits (3.5 marks):** 1. **Low density (1.3–1.5 g/cm³)** → very high specific strength (3× E-glass) (1 mark). 2. **Renewable and biodegradable** → eco-friendly, carbon-neutral (0.5 mark). 3. **Low cost (₹40–60/kg)** → economical vs glass (₹200+/kg) (0.5 mark). 4. **High specific modulus** and good acoustic/thermal insulation (0.5 mark). 5. **Non-abrasive** to processing machinery → less wear on extruders (0.5 mark). **Demerits (2.5 marks):** 1. **High moisture absorption (12–14 %)** → poor fiber–matrix adhesion, swelling, voids (0.5 mark). 2. **Poor fiber–matrix bonding** with non-polar thermoplastics like PP and PE (0.5 mark). 3. **Low thermal stability** → degrades above 200 °C, limiting processing options (0.5 mark). 4. **Variability** in properties due to soil, climate, retting conditions (0.5 mark). 5. **Microbial degradation** over time → poor long-term durability (0.5 mark). **Specific strength comparison (1 mark):** $$\sigma_{sp}^{jute} = \dfrac{400}{1.45} \approx 2759 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$ $$\sigma_{sp}^{E\text{-}glass} = \dfrac{2400}{2.55} \approx 941 \text{ kN·m/kg}$$ ∴ Jute is **~3× superior** to E-glass in specific strength, making it attractive for **lightweight structural applications** in automotive and packaging. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Describe **chemical modification methods** used to improve the interfacial adhesion between jute fiber and a hydrophobic polymer matrix. Give one example reaction in each case. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A jute–PP composite is fabricated with **40 wt % jute fiber**. Using the rule of mixtures, calculate the **longitudinal tensile strength** and **Young's modulus** of the composite. Given: $\sigma_f = 450$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 32$ MPa, $E_f = 25$ GPa, $E_m = 1.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1.45$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 0.91$ g/cm³. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Chemical modification methods (7 marks):** | # | Method | Reagent | Reaction (key change) | Effect (1 mark each) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Alkali (mercerisation)** | 5 % NaOH | $Jute\text{-}OH + NaOH \rightarrow Jute\text{-}O^-Na^+ + H_2O$ | Removes hemicellulose, wax, lignin; increases surface roughness; exposes –OH for bonding | | 2 | **Silane coupling** | Trialkoxy silanes (e.g., 3-aminopropyl-triethoxysilane, APTES) | $Jute\text{-}OH + R\text{-}Si(OR')_3 \rightarrow Jute\text{-}O\text{-}Si\text{-}R + 3R'OH$ | Forms siloxane bridge, makes surface hydrophobic, covalent bond with polymer | | 3 | **Acetylation** | Acetic anhydride / glacial acetic acid | $Jute\text{-}OH + (CH_3CO)_2O \rightarrow Jute\text{-}O\text{-}COCH_3 + CH_3COOH$ | Replaces hydrophilic –OH with hydrophobic acetyl group; reduces moisture regain | | 4 | **Maleic anhydride grafting (MAPP)** | Maleic anhydride grafted PP (MAPP) | Anhydride ring opens; esterifies with jute –OH | Acts as a **compatibiliser** between non-polar PP and polar jute (most widely used commercially) | | 5 | **Benzoylation / peroxide treatment** | Benzoyl chloride, dicumyl peroxide | Benzoyl groups grafted onto cellulose | Improves surface energy match with polymer | Brief write-up of any two (each ~1.5 marks = 3 marks);
