remaining 4 marks for the table and the *MAPP* example, which is the **industry standard** for jute–PP composites. > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners particularly appreciate the **maleic anhydride grafted PP (MAPP)** example — it is the most widely used compatibiliser in jute–polyolefin composites and is a **favourite viva question**. **(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):** **Given:** $W_f = 0.40$, $W_m = 0.60$, $\sigma_f = 450$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 32$ MPa, $E_f = 25$ GPa, $E_m = 1.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1.45$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 0.91$ g/cm³. **Step 1 (1.5 marks): Volume fraction of fiber** $$\begin{aligned} V_f &= \dfrac{W_f/\rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m} \\ &= \dfrac{0.40/1.45}{0.40/1.45 + 0.60/0.91} \\ &= \dfrac{0.2759}{0.2759 + 0.6593} \\ &= \dfrac{0.2759}{0.9352} \\ &= 0.2950 \approx 0.30 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks): Volume fraction of matrix** $$V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70$$ **Step 3 (2 marks): Longitudinal tensile strength (Rule of Mixtures)** $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_c &= \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m \\ &= (450)(0.30) + (32)(0.70) \\ &= 135.0 + 22.4 \\ &= 157.4 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks): Young's modulus (Rule of Mixtures — Voigt isostrain model)** $$\begin{aligned} E_c &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\ &= (25)(0.30) + (1.5)(0.70) \\ &= 7.50 + 1.05 \\ &= 8.55 \text{ GPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** - Longitudinal tensile strength $\sigma_c \approx \mathbf{157.4 \text{ MPa}}$ - Young's modulus $E_c \approx \mathbf{8.55 \text{ GPa}}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. **Wrong conversion** of wt % to vol % → check density values. Most students forget this step. > 2. **Confusion between ROM and inverse ROM** — ROM ($\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$) is for **isostrain (longitudinal)**, inverse ROM is for **isostress (transverse)**. Wrong formula → lose 2 marks. > 3. **Unit mismatch** — strength in MPa, modulus in GPa. Mixing them → 0 marks for the final answer. > 4. **Rounding** errors beyond 3 decimals — KTU allows ±2 % tolerance. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-minute revision checklist for KTU exams:** - [x] Jute = **lignocellulosic natural fiber** from *Corchorus* sp.; "**golden fiber**" of India & Bangladesh. - [x] Composition: **Cellulose 58–63 %, Hemicellulose 20–24 %, Lignin 12–15 %**, plus minor pectin, wax, ash. - [x] Cellulose repeat unit = **β-D-glucopyranose** with **β-1,4-glycosidic bonds**; DP ≈ **700–900**. - [x] Jute is **inherently a composite**: cellulose fibrils (reinforcement) in lignin/hemicellulose matrix. - [x] **Microfibrillar angle (MFA) ≈ 8–12°** → low MFA = high stiffness. - [x] Density = **1.3–1.5 g/cm³**; specific strength ≈ **3× E-glass**. - [x] **MR ≈ 12–14 %** (high moisture absorption → poor adhesion with hydrophobic polymers). - [x] Degrades above **200 °C** → limited thermoplastic processing window. - [x] Key treatments: **Alkali (NaOH), Silane (APTES), Acetylation, MAPP** (most important commercial compatibiliser). - [x] **Rule of mixtures:** $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ — valid only for *longitudinal* loading. - [x] **MR ≠ MC** — Moisture Regain uses dry weight as base; Moisture Content uses original wet weight. - [x] Compared to flax/hemp, jute has **lower cellulose, higher lignin** → less flexible, more UV-sensitive. - [x] Applications: **automotive door panels, geotextiles, packaging, roofing, carpet backing, reinforced PP/PE composites**. > **All the best for your KTU 2024 Scheme examination! 🌾**

topics.title: silk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL607 - Polymer Chemistry & Technology > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Silk - Identification, Properties, and Chemical Analysis of a Natural Protein Fiber # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Silk** is a continuous, natural **proteinaceous filament** secreted by certain arthropods, most notably the larvae of the *Bombyx mori* (domesticated mulberry silkworm), and is classified within polymer science as a **natural polypeptide biopolymer**. Chemically, silk is composed of two principal protein components: 1. **Fibroin** (~75%) – the structural, fibrous core protein that imparts tensile strength. 2. **Sericin** (~25%) – the globular, gummy protein that binds the fibroin filaments together (the "silk gum"). The repeating monomeric units in fibroin are predominantly the amino acids **glycine (Gly)**, **alanine (Ala)**, and **serine (Ser)**, arranged in the characteristic antiparallel **β-pleated sheet** configuration, which gives silk its remarkable mechanical strength. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCPOL607 – Experiment 2):** > Students are expected to perform qualitative identification of silk using **burning test, solubility test, microscopic examination, and chemical spot tests** (e.g., Millon's reagent, Biuret test, Ninhydrin test). Silk is benchmarked against other natural and synthetic fibers as part of fiber identification protocols. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **construction crane cable** made of thousands of fine steel wires twisted together — no single wire is strong, but together they can lift enormous loads. Silk works on the exact same principle: - The **β-pleated sheets** of fibroin are like the "steel wires" (highly ordered, crystalline). - The **amorphous regions** of fibroin and the surrounding sericin act like the **twisting/braiding** that holds everything together and gives flexibility. - The **antiparallel hydrogen bonding** between adjacent protein chains is the "glue" that resists pulling forces. When a silkworm spins its cocoon, it is essentially extruding a **pre-engineered, nano-composite biopolymer cable** that is tougher, gram-for-gram, than many steels — a true masterpiece of natural materials engineering. > [!NOTE] > **Key physical constant to remember:** > - Density of silk: **ρ ≈ 1.30 – 1.38 g/cm³** > - Tenacity (dry): **0.35 – 0.55 N/tex** (≈ 3.5 – 5.5 g/denier) > - Moisture regain (standard): **11.0 %** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Silk Silk's properties arise from a **four-level hierarchical architecture**, a concept that KTU examiners frequently test in 14-mark questions. ### Level 1 — Primary Structure (Amino Acid Sequence) - Dominated by the simple, small amino acids **Gly (43.7 %)**, **Ala (28.8 %)**, and **Ser (11.9 %)**. - The repetitive motif is **(-Gly-Ala-Gly-Ala-Gly-Ser-)_n**. - Small side groups (H, CH₃, CH₂OH) → allow **close chain packing**. ### Level 2 — Secondary Structure (β-Sheets) - Chains align in an **antiparallel β-pleated sheet** stabilized by **inter-chain hydrogen bonds** (C=O···H–N). - Two crystalline polymorphs: - **Silk I** (water-soluble, helical) — the freshly spun form. - **Silk II** (insoluble, β-sheet) — the stable, spun form. ### Level 3 — Tertiary Structure (Microfibrils) - β-Sheets stack into **crystalline microfibrils** (≈ 3–5 nm diameter) embedded in a less-ordered matrix. - This is a natural **semi-crystalline polymer composite**. ### Level 4 — Quaternary Structure (Filament) - Two **brins** (fibroin filaments) are cemented by sericin to form a single **bave** (silk thread). - Typical bave diameter: **10 – 25 μm**. ## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why & How") - **Why so much glycine and alanine?** Because their tiny R-groups (–H and –CH₃) allow chains to pack tightly, maximizing hydrogen bonding and crystallinity. - **How does silk achieve both strength and elasticity?** The **crystalline β-sheet domains** bear the load (high strength, high modulus), while the **amorphous tie-chains** between them allow extension and energy dissipation (toughness). - **How is sericin removed?** By **degumming** — boiling the cocoons in an alkaline soap solution (e.g., 0.5 – 1.0 % Na₂CO₃, 95–100 °C, 30–60 min). This step is the **first stage of silk processing in the textile industry**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is a **high-yield reference** for KTU Polymer Chemistry numerical problems. Memorize the units carefully — examiners award ½ mark for correct units. | # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Standard Value (Silk) | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Degree of Polymerization (DP) | $\mathrm{DP} = \dfrac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$ | ≈ 2 000 – 4 000 (fibroin) | dimensionless | | 2 | Moisture Regain (MR) | $\mathrm{MR} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | 11.0 | % | | 3 | Moisture Content (MC) | $\mathrm{MC} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | 9.91 | % | | 4 | Tenacity (T) | $T = \dfrac{F}{L \times \rho}$ | 0.35 – 0.55 | N/tex | | 5 | Specific Stress | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0 \cdot \rho_f}{L_f \cdot m}$ | — | N/tex | | 6 | Elongation at Break | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | 18 – 25 | % | | 7 | Crystallinity Index (X_c) | $X_c = \dfrac{I_c}{I_c + I_a} \times 100$ | 40 – 50 (silk II) | % | | 8 | Glass Transition (T_g) | Empirical Fox relation | ≈ 175 – 220 | °C | | 9 | Decomposition Temperature | DSC/TGA peak | ≈ 300 – 320 | °C | | 10 | Density (ρ) | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | 1.30 – 1.38 | g/cm³ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The same β-sheet architecture seen in silk fibroin is the inspiration behind modern **regenerated silk fibroin biomaterials** used in tissue-engineering scaffolds, biodegradable sutures, and flexible bioelectronics — making this topic a high-value link between traditional textile chemistry and advanced biomaterials engineering. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Moisture Regain of a Silk Sample **Problem:** A 5.00 g sample of raw mulberry silk is conditioned at 65 % RH and weighed as $W_w = 5.55$ g. The sample is then oven-dried at 105 °C to constant weight $W_d = 5.00$ g. Calculate **(a)** Moisture Regain and **(b)** Moisture Content. ### Part (a) — Moisture Regain $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{MR} &= \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{5.55\,\text{g} - 5.00\,\text{g}}{5.00\,\text{g}} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{0.55}{5.00} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 11.0\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Logic row 1:** The numerator represents the mass of water lost on drying, expressed as a fraction of the **oven-dry mass** (the international convention for textile testing, ISO 6741). **Logic row 2:** The result matches the **standard regain of silk (11.0 %)** — confirming proper conditioning. ### Part (b) — Moisture Content $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{MC} &= \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{0.55}{5.55} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 9.91\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Logic row 3:** MC uses the **original wet mass** in the denominator, so it is always numerically smaller than MR for the same sample. MC is used in **commercial trade**, MR in **scientific specification**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Degree of Polymerization of Fibroin **Problem:** The number-average molecular weight of a silk fibroin sample is $\overline{M}_n = 392\,000$ g/mol. The fibroin repeat unit has the average amino-acid residue mass $M_0 = 98$ g/mol (average of Gly-Ala-Gly-Ala-Gly-Ser). Estimate the **degree of polymerization**. $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{DP} &= \frac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0} \\[6pt] &= \frac{392\,000\,\text{g/mol}}{98\,\text{g/mol}} \\[6pt] &= 4\,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Logic row 1:** $M_0$ is the average residue mass because the sequence is a near-regular alternation of Gly and Ala with occasional Ser. **Logic row 2:** A DP of 4 000 is consistent with **native B. mori fibroin** (literature range 2 000 – 4 000). --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Crystallinity Index from XRD **Problem:** From the X-ray diffractogram of a degummed silk sample, the integrated intensity of the crystalline peak (at $2\theta \approx 20.5°$) is $I_c = 1\,820$ counts, and the amorphous halo area is $I_a = 2\,680$ counts. Compute the crystallinity index $X_c$. $$ \begin{aligned} X_c &= \frac{I_c}{I_c + I_a} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{1\,820}{1\,820 + 2\,680} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{1\,820}{4\,500} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 40.4\,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Logic row 1:** The crystalline peak at $2\theta \approx 20.5°$ corresponds to the **(020) reflection of silk II β-sheets**. **Logic row 2:** A crystallinity of ~40 % confirms the **semi-crystalline nature** of silk — the amorphous regions are responsible for its elasticity, while the crystalline domains provide strength. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison vs Other Natural Fibers | Property | Silk | Wool (Protein) | Cotton (Cellulose) | |---|---|---|---| | Dominant Monomer | Amino acids (Gly, Ala) | Amino acids (Cys-rich) | Glucose (β-1,4) | | Secondary Structure | β-pleated sheet | α-helix + disulfide bonds | — | | Polymer Class | Polypeptide (Fibroin) | Polypeptide (Keratin) | Polysaccharide | | Moisture Regain (%) | 11.0 | 14 – 18 | 8.5 | | Density (g/cm³) | 1.34 | 1.31 | 1.52 | | Crystallinity (%) | 40 – 50 | ~30 | 60 – 70 | | Burning Test | Burns slowly, self-extinguishes, smells of burnt hair (protein) | Same as silk | Burns rapidly, paper-like smell (cellulose) | | Solubility in 70 % H₂SO₄ | Dissolves | Dissolves | Insoluble (carbonizes) | | Key Spot Test | Millon's → red; Ninhydrin → purple | Millon's → red; Ninhydrin → purple | Iodine/H₂SO₄ → blue-black | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip:** In a fiber-identification question, always justify the test outcome by linking the **chemical nature of the polymer** to the **observed response** — not just stating the result. This is how full marks are secured. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Hierarchical Structure of Silk ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Level 1 Primary Structure
Amino Acid Sequence
Gly Ala Gly Ala Gly Ser"] node2["Level 2 Secondary Structure
Antiparallel beta Pleated Sheet
Hydrogen Bonds C O dot dot dot H N"] node3["Level 3 Tertiary Structure
Crystalline Microfibril
3 to 5 nanometer diameter"] node4["Level 4 Quaternary Structure
Two Brins plus Sericin equals Bave
10 to 25 micrometer diameter"] node5["Final Product
Silk Filament or Fabric"] node6["Amorphous Tie Chains
Provide Flexibility and Toughness"] node7["Sericin Gum
Globular Binding Protein"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node2 -.intercalated with.-> node6 node4 -.bound by.-> node7 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Silk Processing & Identification Experiment ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Silk Cocoon
Bombyx mori"] nodeB["Stifling and Sorting
Hot air or steam kills pupa"] nodeC["Reeling
Multiple cocoons unwound"] nodeD["Degumming
Alkaline soap boil 95 to 100 C
Removes sericin"] nodeE["Bleaching and Dyeing
Optional chemical finishing"] nodeF["Finished Silk Yarn
Pure fibroin filament"] nodeG["Identification Tests
Burning, Solubility, Microscopy"] nodeH["Chemical Spot Tests
Millon, Biuret, Ninhydrin"] nodeI["Result Interpretation
Confirms Protein Nature"] nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeI nodeH --> nodeI ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanism of β-Sheet Stabilization ```mermaid flowchart TD pA["Polypeptide Chain 1
Direction N to C"] pB["Polypeptide Chain 2
Direction C to N Antiparallel"] pC["Hydrogen Bond 1
C equals O of Chain 1 to H N of Chain 2"] pD["Hydrogen Bond 2
C equals O of Chain 2 to H N of Chain 1"] pE["Beta Pleated Sheet
Crystalline Domain of Silk II"] pF["Result High Tensile Strength
0.35 to 0.55 N per tex"] pA --aligned against--> pB pA --donates H bond--> pC pB --donates H bond--> pD pC --> pE pD --> pE pE --> pF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this diagram matters:** The β-sheet architecture is the **single most testable concept** in the silk module. Drawing a clearly antiparallel arrangement (opposite arrow directions) and labeling the hydrogen bonds explicitly is worth **at least 3 marks** in any silk-related KTU 14-mark question. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Differentiate between fibroin and sericin in silk with respect to their chemical nature, function, and solubility.** **Model Answer (10 lines, 3 marks):** | Feature | Fibroin | Sericin | |---|---|---| | Nature | Fibrous, structural protein | Globular, amorphous protein | | Function | Provides tensile strength | Cements fibroin filaments (brins) | | Secondary structure | Antiparallel β-pleated sheet | Random coil | | Solubility in hot water | Insoluble | Soluble | | Proportion in raw silk | ~75 % | ~25 % | **Memory anchor:** *"Fibroin = the fiber; Sericin = the sticker."* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supple Paper] **Why does silk give the smell of burnt hair on burning? Name the chemical test that confirms its protein nature.** **Model Answer (4 lines, 3 marks):** - Silk, being a **polypeptide (protein)**, on thermal decomposition yields volatile amines, H₂S, and other nitrogenous fragments that produce the **characteristic odour of burnt hair/feathers** — a universal signature of protein fibers. - **Confirmatory spot test:** **Millon's reagent** gives a **red / brick-red precipitate** (with tyrosine residues), and **Ninhydrin** gives a **purple / Ruhemann's violet** colour. - These tests collectively confirm the protein backbone of silk, distinguishing it from cellulose (cotton) and synthetic fibers. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper, Module 2 Experiment]** ### Question A (14 Marks) — *Choose either A or B* **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **hierarchical structure of mulberry silk (B. mori)** from the amino acid level to the final filament. Highlight the role of **β-pleated sheets** in determining the mechanical properties. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **principle, procedure, and chemical basis** of the following identification tests for silk: (i) **Burning test** (ii) **Millon's test** (iii) **Solubility in 70 % H₂SO₄**. For each test, write the **observation and inference**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — Hierarchical Structure of Silk (7 marks)** - **Level 1 – Primary structure:** The polypeptide fibroin chain is composed predominantly of the amino acid sequence **(-Gly-Ala-Gly-Ala-Gly-Ser-)ₙ**, where Gly ≈ 43.7 %, Ala ≈ 28.8 %, Ser ≈ 11.9 %. The small R-groups permit close chain packing. **(1.5 marks)** - **Level 2 – Secondary structure (β-sheets):** Adjacent chains align in an **antiparallel β-pleated sheet** configuration. The C=O and N–H groups of neighbouring chains form **inter-chain hydrogen bonds** (C=O···H–N), which act as the "molecular crosslinks" of the fiber. This is the **silk II** polymorph, the water-insoluble, mechanically strong form. **(2 marks)** - **Level 3 – Tertiary structure:** Stacks of β-sheets assemble into **crystalline microfibrils** (≈ 3–5 nm diameter), interspersed with **amorphous tie-chains** of less-ordered fibroin segments. **(1.5 marks)** - **Level 4 – Quaternary structure:** Two **brins** (fibroin filaments) are held together by a sericin sheath to form a single **bave** (≈ 10–25 μm diameter) — the actual silk thread. **(1 mark)** - **Role in mechanical properties:** The crystalline β-sheets bear the tensile load, giving **high tenacity (0.35–0.55 N/tex)** and **high Young's modulus**; the amorphous regions allow **chain sliding and energy dissipation**, producing **toughness and ~20 % elongation**. **(1 mark)** > *[Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the schematic.]* **Part (b) — Identification Tests (7 marks)** | Test | Principle | Procedure | Observation | Inference | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Burning Test** | Proteins decompose to release volatile N-compounds (amines, H₂S) | Hold a few fibers near a flame | Burns slowly, **self-extinguishes** when flame removed, curls away from flame, **emits smell of burnt hair** | Confirms **proteinaceous / polypeptide** nature (distinguishes from cellulose & synthetics) | | **Millon's Test** | Mercuric nitrate + nitrous acid reacts with the **phenolic –OH of tyrosine** residues | Treat fiber with Millon's reagent, warm gently | **Red / brick-red precipitate or coloration** | Confirms presence of **tyrosine-bearing protein** (i.e., a protein fiber); both silk and wool give positive | | **70 % H₂SO₄ Solubility** | Concentrated H₂SO₄ hydrolyses peptide bonds, dissolving the fiber | Immerse fiber in 70 % H₂SO₄ at room temperature | **Dissolves completely** (in 2–3 min) | Confirms protein; **distinguishes from cotton** (insoluble, chars) and from synthetics (insoluble) | > *Mnemonic: "**B**urn → **M**illon → **S**ulphuric" = **BMS** — the tri-test for silk. (Examiner award 1 mark for using neat tabulation.)* **Valuation cues:** 7 marks = (a) 1.5 + 2 + 1.5 + 1 + 1 for hierarchical + role = perfect 7; (b) 2 marks for principle+procedure of each, 1 mark for observation+inference. Tabular format gets bonus 0.5 mark. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Describe the **chemical composition, degumming process, and physical properties** of mulberry silk. Why is degumming essential before dyeing? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **5.00 g sample of raw silk** is conditioned at 65 % RH, giving $W_w = 5.55$ g. After oven-drying to constant weight, $W_d = 5.00$ g. Calculate: (i) **Moisture Regain (MR)** and **Moisture Content (MC)** (ii) If the same sample shows an X-ray crystallinity index of 42.5 % and density 1.34 g/cm³, comment on the **suitability of this fiber for high-strength textile applications** based on its structure–property correlation. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — Chemical Composition, Degumming & Properties (7 marks)** - **Chemical composition of raw silk:** Two proteins — **fibroin (~75 %, structural)** and **sericin (~25 %, gummy binder)**. Trace amounts of waxes, fats, mineral salts and natural pigments (e.g., flavonoids giving yellow tint to mulberry silk). **(1.5 marks)** - **Degumming process:** Cocoons (or yarn) are treated in a hot **alkaline soap solution** (Na₂CO₃ 0.5–1.0 %, 95–100 °C, 30–60 min). This **hydrolyses sericin** into water-soluble peptides, which are washed away. **(2 marks)** - **Why degumming is essential before dyeing:** Sericin forms a **hydrophobic, impermeable layer** that blocks dye molecules from reaching the fibroin. Removal of sericin exposes the **reactive amino (–NH₂) and carboxyl (–COOH) groups** of fibroin, allowing **acid, reactive and direct dyes** to form ionic / covalent bonds with the fiber. Degumming also improves **lustre, handle, and whiteness**. **(2 marks)** - **Physical properties of degummed silk:** Tenacity 0.35–0.55 N/tex, elongation 18–25 %, moisture regain 11.0 %, density 1.34 g/cm³, thermal decomposition ≈ 300 °C, excellent drape, soft handle, natural lustre. **(1.5 marks)** **Part (b) — Numerical & Conceptual (7 marks)** **(i) MR and MC Calculation (4 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{MR} &= \frac{5.55 - 5.00}{5.00} \times 100 = 11.0\,\% \\[6pt] \mathrm{MC} &= \frac{5.55 - 5.00}{5.55} \times 100 = 9.91\,\% \end{aligned} $$ Both values are within standard specifications for mulberry silk (MR = 11.0 %). **(ii) Structure–Property Comment (3 marks):** - A crystallinity of **42.5 %** places the sample in the **upper range of native silk II** (40–50 %), implying a high proportion of load-bearing β-sheets. - Combined with the **low density of 1.34 g/cm³**, the fiber offers an **excellent strength-to-weight ratio**. - **Conclusion:** This silk is **highly suitable for high-strength textile applications** such as parachute fabric, surgical sutures, and light-weight protective garments. - A higher crystallinity (>50 %) would make the fiber stiffer and less comfortable; a lower value (<35 %) would compromise strength. The 42.5 % value is therefore **optimum**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Conflating MR and MC** — They are *not* interchangeable. MR uses $W_d$ in the denominator; MC uses $W_w$. Mixing them up costs **1 full mark** in numerical questions. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 100** when reporting percentages — leads to a wrong order of magnitude. > 3. **Writing "silk is strong because of its protein nature"** — this is too vague. Always specify the **β-pleated sheet, hydrogen bonding, and crystallinity** to earn the structural mark. > 4. **Confusing Silk I and Silk II** — Silk I is the *freshly-spun, water-soluble, helical* form; Silk II is the *stable, β-sheet, water-insoluble* form. Examiners are fond of this trap. > 5. **Omitting the labelled diagram** in 7-mark structural questions — a clean block diagram is worth **at least 1 mark** by itself. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Silk = protein fiber = polypeptide = fibroin (structural) + sericin (gum)**. - **Chemical signature:** Gly–Ala–Gly–Ala–Gly–Ser repeat; **β-pleated sheet (Silk II)**; **antiparallel** chain orientation; **inter-chain H-bonds**. - **Key constants (commit to memory):** density 1.30–1.38 g/cm³, moisture regain 11.0 %, tenacity 0.35–0.55 N/tex, elongation 18–25 %, crystallinity 40–50 %, T_decomp ≈ 300 °C. - **Degumming** with alkaline soap removes sericin → essential for dyeing, improves lustre and handle. - **Identification tri-test (BMS):** **B**urning (burnt hair smell) + **M**illon's (red) + **S**ulphuric acid 70 % (dissolves) → confirm protein, rule out cellulose and synthetics. - **Why silk is strong:** β-sheets + H-bonds + crystallinity + low density → exceptional specific strength. - **Why silk is tough:** Amorphous tie-chains between crystalline microfibrils allow energy dissipation. - **Engineering relevance:** Inspiration for **regenerated silk fibroin biomaterials** — sutures, scaffolds, flexible bioelectronics, regenerative tissue engineering. - **Trap to avoid:** Silk I (fresh, soluble, helical) vs Silk II (stable, β-sheet, insoluble). - **Formula drill:** $\mathrm{MR} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}\times 100$ ; $\mathrm{MC} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w}\times 100$ ; $\mathrm{DP} = \frac{\overline{M}_n}{M_0}$ ; $X_c = \frac{I_c}{I_c + I_a}\times 100$. ---

topics.title: banana 9 3 Manmade fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** banana 9 3 Manmade fibres # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Man-made fibres (MMF)** are fibres manufactured by humans using chemical processing of natural polymers (regenerated fibres) or synthesis from petrochemical monomers (synthetic fibres). As per the **Textile Fiber Products Identification Act** and BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) classification, MMFs are broadly classified into two categories: **Regenerated Fibres** (e.g., Viscose Rayon, Acetate, Lyocell) and **Synthetic Fibres** (e.g., Polyester, Nylon, Acrylic, Polypropylene). In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework of polymer and fibre chemistry (PCPOL607), man-made fibres are defined as polymeric filaments of **finite length or continuous filaments** whose chemical composition, morphology, and mechanical properties are engineered through controlled spinning processes such as **melt spinning, dry spinning, and wet spinning**. ### Key Terminology - **Filament**: A continuous fibre of indefinite length. - **Staple fibre**: Short, finite-length fibre (typically 1–4 inches), cut from filaments. - **Denier (D)**: Mass in grams of 9000 metres of fibre. $D = \dfrac{m}{L} \times 9000$ - **Tex**: Mass in grams of 1000 metres of fibre. $T = \dfrac{m}{L} \times 1000$ > [!NOTE] > **DTex** (Decitex) = grams per 10,000 m. $1 \, DTex = 0.9 \, Denier$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Noodle Factory" Analogy** 🍜 > > Imagine a kitchen where you make noodles. The dough is your **polymer solution/melt**, and the extrusion die is the **spinneret** (a showerhead-like plate with tiny holes). When you push the dough through the holes, long, thin noodle strands emerge — these are your **filaments**. > > - If the dough is **molten (hot plastic)** and you let it cool and solidify in air → this is **Melt Spinning** (used for Polyester, Nylon). > - If the dough is dissolved in a **volatile solvent** that evaporates in warm air → this is **Dry Spinning** (used for Acrylic). > - If the dough is dissolved in a **non-volatile solvent** and the fibre is precipitated in a chemical bath → this is **Wet Spinning** (used for Viscose Rayon). > > Just like noodles can be cut into short pieces (spaghetti vs. macaroni), continuous filaments can be cut into **staple fibres** for spinning into yarn like cotton or wool. ## 1.3 Classification Overview (Syllabus Highlight) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Classification Mandate for MMF:** > > | Category | Source | Examples | Spinning Method | > |---|---|---|---| > | **Regenerated (Cellulosic)** | Natural polymer (cellulose) chemically dissolved & re-extruded | Viscose Rayon, Modal, Lyocell, Acetate | Wet Spinning | > | **Synthetic (Non-cellulosic)** | Synthesized from petrochemical monomers | Polyester (PET), Nylon 6,6, Acrylic, Polypropylene, Spandex | Melt / Dry / Wet Spinning | **Standard Reference Temperature**: Glass transition temperature ($T_g$) and melting temperature ($T_m$) of common MMFs (in **°C**): $$T_g (PET) \approx 70-80^\circ C, \quad T_m (PET) \approx 260^\circ C$$ $$T_m (Nylon \, 6,6) \approx 255-265^\circ C, \quad T_g (Nylon) \approx 45-60^\circ C$$ # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Principal Spinning Processes ### A. Melt Spinning (Thermoplastic Polymers) - **Polymers used**: Polyester (PET), Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, Polypropylene. - **Principle**: Polymer chips are melted at a temperature **30–40 °C above $T_m$** in an extruder. The molten polymer is forced through the spinneret, and the filaments solidify by **cooling in air** (chimney quench). - **Key Engineering Parameters**: - Spinning speed: 1000–6000 m/min (POY), up to 8000+ m/min (FDY). - Draw-down ratio: $\lambda = \dfrac{V_{take-up}}{V_{extrusion}}$ ### B. Dry Spinning - **Polymers used**: Acrylic (PAN), Spandex (Polyurethane), Modacrylic. - **Principle**: Polymer is dissolved in a **volatile solvent** (DMF, acetone). Hot inert gas evaporates the solvent, leaving solid filaments. - **Advantage**: Produces fibres with smooth, round cross-section. ### C. Wet Spinning - **Polymers used**: Viscose Rayon, Lyocell, some Acrylic variants. - **Principle**: Polymer solution is extruded into a **non-solvent coagulation bath** (e.g., dilute $H_2SO_4$ + $Na_2SO_4$ for viscose). - **Disadvantage**: Slow process (50–150 m/min) due to diffusion-controlled coagulation. > [!NOTE] > **Electrospinning** (advanced) — uses electric field ($E = 1-3 \, kV/cm$) to draw nano-fibres ($d = 50-500 \, nm$). ## 2.2 Drawing & Orientation After spinning, fibres are **cold-drawn** (typically 3–6×) to align molecular chains along the fibre axis. This dramatically improves: - **Tenacity** (tensile strength) — from $\sim 1 \, g/den$ to $5-9 \, g/den$. - **Modulus** — Young's modulus increases by **5–10×**. - **Crystallinity** — X-ray diffraction shows sharp crystalline peaks. $$Tenacity \, (g/den) = \dfrac{Breaking \, Force \, (g)}{Denier}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D = \dfrac{m \, (g)}{L \, (m)} \times 9000$ | Denier (linear density) | denier | | 2 | $T = \dfrac{m}{L} \times 1000$ | Tex | tex | | 3 | $Tenacity = \dfrac{F}{D}$ | Specific tensile strength | g/den or cN/tex | | 4 | $Elongation \, \% = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | % strain at break | % | | 5 | $Moisture \, Regain \, (MR) = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | Moisture absorption capacity | % | | 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_{take-up}}{v_{extrusion}}$ | Draw-down ratio | dimensionless | | 7 | $Crystallinity \, \% = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho - \rho_a)}{\rho (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | Degree of crystallinity (density method) | % | | 8 | $T_m = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta S_f}$ | Equilibrium melting point (Gibbs) | K | | 9 | $Hooke's \, Law: \sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$ | Stress–strain in elastic region | MPa | | 10 | $\dfrac{1}{T_m} = \dfrac{1}{T_m^0} - \dfrac{R}{\Delta H_f} \cdot \ln(X_c)$ | Hoffman–Weeks (crystallite size effect) | $K^{-1}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical caution for KTU exam**: When expressing "tenacity" in cN/tex, the conversion is $1 \, g/den = 8.83 \, cN/tex$. Examiners **deduct marks** for unit inconsistency. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Man-made Fibres | Industry | Dominant MMF | Engineering Reason | |---|---|---| | **Apparel** | Polyester, Cotton-Poly blends | Wrinkle resistance, dimensional stability | | **Tyre Cord** | Nylon 6,6, Polyester, Steel | High tenacity, fatigue resistance | | **Geotextiles** | Polypropylene | Chemical inertness, low cost | | **Ropes & Nets** | Nylon, Polyethylene | High impact strength, UV resistance | | **Filtration** | Melt-blown PP, Electrospun PAN | High surface area, fine pore size | | **Composites** | Carbon fibre, Aramid (Kevlar) | Specific strength > 250 kN·m/kg | ## 2.5 Identification & Characterisation Tests (Experiment 2 Context) > [!NOTE] > In **Module Experiment 2**, the following characterisation tests are typically performed on man-made fibres: > > 1. **Burn Test** (visual flame behaviour + odour + residue ash). > 2. **Microscopic Test** (longitudinal & cross-sectional morphology under optical microscope at 100–400× magnification). > 3. **Solubility Test** (in acetone, DMF, conc. $H_2SO_4$, phenol, m-cresol). > 4. **Staining Test** (Shirlastain A / Neocarmin W — differential colour reaction). > 5. **Tensile Test** (using a single-fibre tensile tester — e.g., Instron, UTM). ### Burn Test Result Matrix (High-Yield for KTU) | Fibre | Approach to Flame | In Flame | Residue | Odour | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Polyester (PET)** | Melts & shrinks | Melts, drips, black smoke | Hard, round, black bead | Sweet, chemical | | **Nylon 6,6** | Melts & curls | Melts, drips, blue-yellow flame | Hard, grey-tan bead | Celery / burnt hair | | **Acrylic (PAN)** | Melts & shrinks | Burns with bright flame, sputtering | Brittle, irregular, black | Acrid, fishy | | **Viscose Rayon** | Burns readily | Burns rapidly, yellow flame | Soft, grey ash (no bead) | Burning paper | | **Acetate** | Melts away from flame | Melts, drips, sparking | Hard, black, irregular | Acetic acid (vinegar) | | **Polypropylene** | Melts & shrinks | Burns with blue base, yellow tip | Hard, tan/brown bead | Paraffin wax | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Tenacity and Specific Stress Conversion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this derivation matters**: KTU frequently asks numerical problems converting tenacity values between g/den and cN/tex, and relating linear density (denier) to mass and length. Board examiners award full marks only when students show the dimensional conversion chain. ### Problem Statement A polyester filament of **denier 75** is loaded with a **breaking force of 450 g**. Calculate: (a) Tenacity in **g/den** (b) Tenacity in **cN/tex** (c) Mass of **9000 m** of this filament. ### Given Data - Linear density $D = 75$ denier - Breaking force $F = 450 \, g$ ### Step 1 — Tenacity in g/den By definition: $$Tenacity = \dfrac{Breaking \, Force \, (g)}{Denier}$$ Substituting the given values: $$Tenacity = \dfrac{450 \, g}{75 \, den} = 6.0 \, g/den$$ **Logic**: The force required to break 1 denier of this yarn is 6 grams. This is a *specific* strength, independent of yarn thickness. ### Step 2 — Convert g/den to cN/tex The standard conversion factor is: $$1 \, g/den = 8.83 \, cN/tex$$ Derivation of this factor (exhaustive, as per KTU valuation expectations): $$\begin{aligned} 1 \, g/den &= \dfrac{1 \, gf}{1 \, denier} = \dfrac{1 \, gf \cdot 9000 \, m \, fibre^{-1}}{1 \, g \cdot fibre^{-1} \cdot 9000 \, m} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{1 \, gf}{1 \, g} \cdot \dfrac{9000 \, m}{9000 \, m \cdot 1} \cdot \dfrac{1 \, tex}{1 \, denier} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{1 \, gf}{0.111 \, tex} \times 0.9 \, (denier \to tex) \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{9 \, gf}{1 \, tex} = 9 \times 0.981 \, cN/tex \approx 8.83 \, cN/tex \end{aligned}$$ Using $1 \, gf = 0.981 \, cN$ (since $1 \, N = 101.97 \, gf$): $$\therefore 1 \, g/den = 9 \times 0.981 = 8.829 \, cN/tex \approx 8.83 \, cN/tex$$ Therefore: $$Tenacity = 6.0 \times 8.83 = 52.98 \, cN/tex \approx 53.0 \, cN/tex$$ ### Step 3 — Mass of 9000 m By definition of denier: $$D = \dfrac{m \, (g)}{L \, (m)} \times 9000$$ Rearranging: $$m = \dfrac{D \times L}{9000}$$ For $L = 9000 \, m$ and $D = 75$: $$m = \dfrac{75 \times 9000}{9000} = 75 \, g$$ **Logic**: This confirms the definition — 9000 m of a 75-denier yarn weighs exactly 75 g. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Draw-Down Ratio & Molecular Orientation ### Problem Statement A melt-spinning line extrudes polymer at $v_{extrusion} = 5 \, m/min$ through a spinneret hole of diameter $d_0 = 0.4 \, mm$. The take-up velocity is $v_{take-up} = 1500 \, m/min$. Calculate: (a) Draw-down ratio $\lambda$. (b) Filament diameter at take-up (assuming incompressible melt, density $\rho = 1.38 \, g/cm^3$ for PET). (c) Mass throughput per hole in g/min. ### Step 1 — Draw-Down Ratio $$\begin{aligned} \lambda &= \dfrac{v_{take-up}}{v_{extrusion}} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{1500}{5} = 300 \end{aligned}$$ This is a typical industrial value (200–600× for POY spinning). ### Step 2 — Filament Diameter After Drawing Conservation of mass (incompressible flow): $$A_0 \cdot v_{extrusion} = A_f \cdot v_{take-up}$$ Where $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4}$ and $A_f = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4}$. Therefore: $$\dfrac{d_0^2}{d_f^2} = \dfrac{v_{take-up}}{v_{extrusion}} = \lambda$$ $$d_f = \dfrac{d_0}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$$ Substituting: $$d_f = \dfrac{0.4 \, mm}{\sqrt{300}} = \dfrac{0.4}{17.32} = 0.0231 \, mm = 23.1 \, \mu m$$ This matches the typical diameter of textile PET filaments (20–25 μm). ### Step 3 — Mass Throughput Volumetric flow per hole: $$Q = A_0 \cdot v_{extrusion} = \dfrac{\pi (0.4 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} \times \dfrac{5}{60} \, m^3/s$$ $$Q = 1.256 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.0833 = 1.047 \times 10^{-8} \, m^3/s$$ Mass flow rate: $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 1.38 \times 10^6 \, g/m^3 \times 1.047 \times 10^{-8} \, m^3/s$$ $$\dot{m} = 0.01445 \, g/s = 0.867 \, g/min \, per \, hole$$ **Logic**: A typical 144-hole spinneret at this throughput produces $\sim 125 \, g/min$ of fibre, equivalent to $\sim 1666 \, denier$ per filament (i.e., 1666/144 ≈ 11.6 denier/filament — typical POY). --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Glass Transition and Tenacity Correlation For an amorphous polymer fibre (e.g., atactic PS or PMMA) the tenacity drops above $T_g$ due to chain mobility. The empirical relationship is: $$T(d) = T_0 \cdot \exp\left(-k \cdot \dfrac{T - T_g}{T_g}\right)$$ Where $T(d)$ is tenacity at temperature $T$, $T_0$ is tenacity at $T_g$, and $k \approx 0.05-0.1$ for most synthetic fibres. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight**: This is why polyester garments cannot be ironed at high temperatures — above $T_g \approx 70^\circ C$, the fabric loses dimensional stability ("glassy memory" effect). Ironing is recommended at **140–160 °C** with a pressing cloth, just below $T_m$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Properties of Major Man-Made Fibres | Property | Polyester (PET) | Nylon 6,6 | Acrylic (PAN) | Viscose Rayon | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Chemical class** | Polyester (aromatic) | Polyamide (aliphatic) | Vinyl (acrylonitrile) | Regenerated cellulose | | **Tenacity (g/den)** | 4.0–7.0 | 4.5–9.0 | 2.0–3.5 | 2.0–3.5 | | **Elongation (%)** | 15–30 | 20–40 | 25–45 | 15–30 | | **Moisture Regain (%)** | 0.4 | 4.0 | 1.5 | 11.0 | | **Specific Gravity** | 1.38 | 1.14 | 1.17 | 1.52 | | **$T_g$ (°C)** | 70–80 | 45–60 | 85–100 | None (semi-cryst) | | **$T_m$ (°C)** | 260 | 255 | 320 (decomposes) | None (chars) | | **Solvent for ID** | m-Cresol, Phenol | Formic acid 90% | DMF | Cu-ammonia complex | | **End use** | Apparel, bottles, cord | Tyres, hosiery, carpets | Knitwear, blankets | Linings, dress material | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Melt Spinning Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Polymer Chips
Dried Hopper] --> B[Extruder / Melter
Heat above Tm] B --> C[Filter Pack
Sand Metal Mesh] C --> D[Spinneret
Multi hole Die] D --> E[Quench Chimney
Cooling Air Cross Flow] E --> F[Spin Finish Applicator
Lubricant] F --> G[Godet Rolls
Take up Winder] G --> H[Drawing Zone
Cold Hot Stretching] H --> I[Stabilisation Heater
Heat Setting] I --> J[Windup
POY FDY Packages] style A fill:#f9d976 style B fill:#f39c12 style D fill:#e74c3c style E fill:#3498db style H fill:#2ecc71 style J fill:#9b59b6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: Polymer chips enter at ambient temperature, are melted in the extruder (screw + heater bands), filtered to remove gels, then pushed through a spinneret (1–5000 holes). Cross-flow cooling air in the quench chimney solidifies the filaments. Spin finish reduces static and friction. Godet rolls control take-up speed. Drawing orients the chains, and heat setting stabilises the structure. ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Wet Spinning of Viscose Rayon ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Viscose Solution
Xanthate Cellulose] --> B[Spinneret
Submerged] B --> C[Coagulation Bath
H2SO4 Na2SO4 ZnSO4] C --> D[Stretching Zone
Hot Acid Bath] D --> E[Neutralisation
Na2CO3 Wash] E --> F[Bleaching] F --> G[Drying Cans] G --> H[Winding Bobbins] style A fill:#16a085 style C fill:#c0392b style G fill:#f39c12 style H fill:#2c3e50 ``` ## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification: Man-Made Fibre Family Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Man Made Fibres] --> B[Regenerated Fibres] A --> C[Synthetic Fibres] B --> B1[Cellulosic Origin] B1 --> B11[Viscose Rayon] B1 --> B12[Modal HWM] B1 --> B13[Lyocell Tencel] B1 --> B14[Acetate] B1 --> B15[Triacetate] C --> C1[Polyamides] C1 --> C11[Nylon 6] C1 --> C12[Nylon 66] C1 --> C13[Aramid Kevlar Nomex] C --> C2[Polyesters] C2 --> C21[PET Polyester] C2 --> C22[PBT Polybutylene Terephthalate] C2 --> C23[PCDT Poly 1,4 cyclohexylene] C --> C3[Acrylics Modacrylics] C3 --> C31[Acrylic PAN] C3 --> C32[Modacrylic] C --> C4[Polyolefins] C4 --> C41[Polypropylene PP] C4 --> C42[Polyethylene PE] C --> C5[Elastomeric] C5 --> C51[Spandex Lycra] C5 --> C52[Elastane] style A fill:#34495e,color:#fff style B fill:#27ae60,color:#fff style C fill:#2980b9,color:#fff ``` ## 4.4 Cross-Section Morphology of Major MMFs ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PET X1[Round Smooth] end subgraph Nylon X2[Round Trilobal] end subgraph Viscose X3[Irregular Serrated] end subgraph Acrylic X4[Dumbbell Dog Bone] end subgraph Polypropylene X5[Round Solid] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why cross-section matters**: The serrated cross-section of viscose gives a **hairy, wool-like feel** (good for non-woven wipes). The trilobal nylon cross-section reflects light like silk (used in hosiery). Round cross-section PET has high luster and is used in apparel. ## 4.5 Burn Test Decision Flow (Diagnostic) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Sample Burn Test] --> B{Melts and Drips?} B -- Yes --> C{Sweet Chemical Smell?} B -- No --> D{Burns Like Paper?} C -- Yes --> E[Polyester PET] C -- No --> F{Celery Odour?} F -- Yes --> G[Nylon] F -- No --> H{Acrid Fishy?} H -- Yes --> I[Acrylic PAN] D -- Yes --> J[Viscose Rayon] B -- Yes --> K{Paraffin Wax Smell?} K -- Yes --> L[Polypropylene] style E fill:#e67e22 style G fill:#3498db style I fill:#9b59b6 style J fill:#1abc9c style L fill:#f1c40f ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning** ⚠️ > > - **Common error 1**: Confusing *tenacity* (g/den) with *breaking force* (g or N). Tenacity is *specific* — always **per unit linear density**. > - **Common error 2**: Writing $T_m$ for cellulose-based fibres (viscose, cotton). Cellulose **chars/decomposes** before melting. No $T_m$ exists. > - **Common error 3**: Using **denier as a unit of thickness or diameter**. Denier is a *linear density* (mass per length), not a diameter. > - **Common error 4**: Forgetting to convert $gf$ to $N$ or $cN$ when SI units are required. $1 \, gf = 0.981 \, cN = 9.81 \times 10^{-3} \, N$. > - **Common error 5**: In burn-test questions, students often write "burns with bright flame" for *all* MMFs. Examiner expects *specific* descriptors (sputtering, dripping, bead formation, odour). --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Define man-made fibres. Differentiate between regenerated and synthetic fibres with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Man-made fibres (MMF)** are textile fibres produced by chemical transformation of natural polymers or by synthesis from low-molecular-weight monomers, followed by extrusion through a spinneret. > > | Aspect | Regenerated Fibres | Synthetic Fibres | > |---|---|---| > | **Source** | Natural polymer (cellulose) | Petrochemical monomers | > | **Chemical nature** | Same as parent polymer | New polymer | > | **Examples** | Viscose Rayon, Acetate, Lyocell | Polyester (PET), Nylon 6,6, Acrylic | > | **Process** | Dissolve → re-extrude | Polymerise → melt/dissolve → extrude | > > *(Marks distribution: 1 for definition + 1 for table/differentiation + 1 for examples.)* --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` **Explain the burn test for identifying man-made fibres. Mention the observations for polyester and nylon.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **burn test** is a rapid qualitative identification method where a small fibre tuft is exposed to a flame, and its behaviour is observed in terms of approach, in-flame, residue, and odour. - **Polyester (PET)**: Melts and shrinks away from flame → in flame, melts and drips with black smoke → residue is a **hard, round, black bead** → odour is **sweet/aromatic chemical**. - **Nylon 6,6**: Melts and curls → in flame, melts and drips with a **blue-yellow flame** → residue is a **hard, grey-tan bead** → odour is **celery-like or burnt hair**. *(Marks: 1 for procedure + 1 for PET observation + 1 for nylon observation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` **A(i).** With a neat block diagram, describe the **melt spinning process** for manufacturing polyester filament yarn. List the polymers and key process parameters. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Introduction** (1 mark): Melt spinning is the most economical and widely used technique for producing thermoplastic synthetic fibres. It involves melting the polymer and extruding it through a spinneret, followed by solidification via air cooling. **Block Diagram** (1 mark): ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Polymer Chips] --> A2[Extruder] A2 --> A3[Spinneret] A3 --> A4[Quench Zone] A4 --> A5[Finish Applicator] A5 --> A6[Drawing Godets] A6 --> A7[Heat Setter] A7 --> A8[Windup] style A2 fill:#e67e22 style A3 fill:#e74c3c style A8 fill:#9b59b6 ``` **Process Description** (3 marks): 1. **Drying**: PET chips are dried to moisture < 50 ppm at 160 °C to prevent hydrolysis in the melt. 2. **Extrusion**: Polymer is melted at 280–290 °C in a screw extruder. 3. **Filtration & Spinneret**: The melt passes through a filter pack (sand + metal mesh) to remove gels, then through a spinneret (typically 24–144 holes, hole diameter 0.2–0.4 mm). 4. **Quench**: Cool air (20–30 °C) cross-flow solidifies the filaments. 5. **Spin Finish**: A lubricating oil emulsion is applied to prevent static and inter-fibre adhesion. 6. **Drawing**: Filaments are stretched 3–5× between heated godets to orient molecular chains. 7. **Heat Setting**: Stabilises the crystalline structure (180–200 °C). 8. **Winding**: Yarn is wound at 3000–6000 m/min onto bobbins. **Polymers** (1 mark): PET (Polyester), Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, Polypropylene, PBT. **Key Parameters** (1 mark): Melt temperature 280–290 °C, spin speed 3000–6000 m/min, draw ratio 3–5×, quenching air velocity 0.4–0.6 m/s, finish level 0.3–0.8% owf. --- **A(ii).** A polyester yarn has a denier of **150**. Calculate: (a) Tenacity in g/den if the breaking force is **900 g**. (b) Equivalent tenacity in cN/tex. (c) Length of yarn in metres that weighs **1 kg**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given**: $D = 150$ denier, $F = 900 \, g$. **(a) Tenacity in g/den** (2 marks): $$Tenacity = \dfrac{F}{D} = \dfrac{900}{150} = 6.0 \, g/den$$ **(b) Tenacity in cN/tex** (2 marks): Using $1 \, g/den = 8.83 \, cN/tex$: $$Tenacity = 6.0 \times 8.83 = 52.98 \approx 53.0 \, cN/tex$$ **(c) Length of 1 kg yarn** (3 marks): By definition of denier: $$m = \dfrac{D \times L}{9000}$$ For $m = 1000 \, g$, $D = 150$: $$1000 = \dfrac{150 \times L}{9000}$$ $$L = \dfrac{1000 \times 9000}{150} = 60{,}000 \, m$$ **Result**: 1 kg of 150-denier polyester yarn = 60 km. --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]` **B(i).** Classify man-made fibres with a neat hierarchical diagram. Explain the **wet spinning** process of viscose rayon. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Classification** (2 marks): > **A) Regenerated Fibres** (from natural polymers): > - Cellulosic: Viscose Rayon, Modal, Lyocell, Acetate, Triacetate. > > **B) Synthetic Fibres** (from petrochemical monomers): > - Polyamides (Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, Aramid). > - Polyesters (PET, PBT). > - Acrylics & Modacrylics (PAN, Modacrylic). > - Polyolefins (PP, PE). > - Elastomeric (Spandex/Lycra). **Viscose Wet Spinning** (5 marks): **Principle**: Cellulose xanthate (viscose dope) is extruded into an acid coagulation bath where it regenerates into cellulose filaments. **Steps**: 1. **Viscose Preparation** (1 mark): Purified cellulose sheets are steeped in **18% NaOH** to form alkali cellulose, aged, treated with **CS₂** to form cellulose xanthate, dissolved in dilute NaOH to give 8–10% viscose dope (viscosity 30–50 P). 2. **Ripening & Deaeration**: Viscose is held at 18–22 °C for 24–48 h to achieve optimum ripeness. 3. **Wet Extrusion** (1 mark): Viscose is filtered and forced through a spinneret (1000–30,000 holes) immersed in a coagulation bath. 4. **Coagulation Bath** (1 mark): Composition — $H_2SO_4$ (8–12%), $Na_2SO_4$ (15–22%), $ZnSO_4$ (1%). Acid neutralises NaOH; sulphate dehydrates the filament; zinc forms a Zn-cellulose complex that slows regeneration for better structure. 5. **Stretching & Post-treatment** (1 mark): Filaments are stretched 50–100% in a hot dilute acid bath, washed, desulphurised with NaOH, bleached, and dried. 6. **Cutting & Packaging** (1 mark): Continuous filament tow is either wound or cut into staple fibre of 1.5–6 denier. **Net Reaction**: $$(C_6H_9O_4 \cdot OCS_2Na)_n + n H_2SO_4 \rightarrow (C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + n NaHSO_4 + n CS_2$$ --- **B(ii).** Describe the **burn test, microscopic test, and solubility test** for identification of man-made fibres. Tabulate your observations for **polyester, nylon, and acrylic**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Burn Test** (1.5 marks): As described in Section 2.5 — small tuft of fibre is exposed to a flame. Observations: approach behaviour, in-flame behaviour, residue, and odour are noted. **Microscopic Test** (1.5 marks): Longitudinal and cross-sectional views of the fibre are observed under an optical microscope (100–400× magnification) using a slide-mounted preparation. - **Polyester**: Long smooth rod-like longitudinal view; **round or trilobal** cross-section. - **Nylon 6,6**: Long, smooth, lustrous; round or trilobal cross-section. - **Acrylic**: Striated, rod-like with **dog-bone / dumbbell** cross-section. **Solubility Test** (2 marks): Small fibre sample is placed in a watch glass with the test solvent. Observations are made at room temperature and on warming. | Solvent | Polyester | Nylon 6,6 | Acrylic | |---|---|---|---| | **Acetone (cold)** | Insoluble | Insoluble | Insoluble | | **Acetone (boiling)** | Swells, partly soluble | Insoluble | Swells | | **DMF (cold)** | Insoluble | Insoluble | **Soluble** | | **Formic acid 90% (cold)** | Insoluble | **Soluble** | Insoluble | | **Conc. $H_2SO_4$** | Soluble (decomposes) | Soluble (decomposes) | Soluble (decomposes) | | **m-Cresol (hot)** | **Soluble** | Soluble | Soluble | | **Phenol (hot)** | Soluble | Soluble | Insoluble | **Conclusion** (2 marks): By combining burn test, microscopy, and solubility results, even an unknown man-made fibre can be unambiguously identified. The most diagnostic tests are **DMF solubility for acrylic**, **formic acid for nylon**, and **m-cresol for polyester**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 KTU High-Yield Quick-Recall Sheet (Module Experiment 2 — Man-Made Fibres)** - **Definition**: MMF = fibres made by chemical processing (regenerated) or synthesis (synthetic) + extrusion through spinneret. - **Two Big Families**: - **Regenerated** (cellulose-based): Viscose, Modal, Lyocell, Acetate. - **Synthetic** (petrochemical-based): Polyester, Nylon, Acrylic, PP, Spandex. - **Three Spinning Methods** (must know conditions): - **Melt Spinning** → Thermoplastics (PET, Nylon, PP). No solvent. Cool with air. - **Dry Spinning** → Volatile solvent (DMF, acetone). Used for Acrylic, Spandex. - **Wet Spinning** → Non-volatile solvent + coagulation bath. Used for Viscose, Lyocell. - **Denier**: 1 denier = 1 g per 9000 m. **Tex**: 1 tex = 1 g per 1000 m. - **Tenacity Conversion**: $1 \, g/den = 8.83 \, cN/tex$ (use $9 \, cN/tex$ in quick calculations). - **Burn Test Fingerprint**: - **PET** → sweet chemical + hard black bead. - **Nylon** → celery + hard grey bead. - **Acrylic** → fishy + brittle black residue. - **Viscose** → paper-like + soft grey ash. - **PP** → paraffin wax + tan bead. - **Solubility Fingerprint**: - **PET** dissolves in m-cresol/phenol. - **Nylon** dissolves in 90% formic acid. - **Acrylic** dissolves in DMF. - **Viscose** dissolves in cuoxam (Cu-ammonia) and NaOH/CS₂ (regeneration). - **PP** is soluble only in hot xylene/decahydronaphthalene. - **Moisture Regain Trend** (memorise ranking): - Viscose (11%) > Nylon (4%) > Acrylic (1.5%) > Polyester (0.4%) > PP (~0%). - **Spinning Speed Hierarchy**: - Melt: 3000–8000 m/min (fastest). - Dry: 200–500 m/min. - Wet: 50–150 m/min (slowest). - **Engineering Use Map**: - **Tyres**: Nylon 6,6, polyester. - **Apparel**: PET/cotton blends. - **Hosiery**: Nylon (trilobal, lustrous). - **Knitwear**: Acrylic. - **Geotextiles**: Polypropylene. - **Examiner's Mantra**: - *Always show unit conversions*. - *Never use absolute values without the unit denier/tex*. - *For viscose, never quote a $T_m$ — cellulose decomposes*. - *For PP, the spinning speed is the highest, and density is the lowest (0.91 g/cm³) — this combination is often asked as a “trick” question*. - *For tensile test results, always report tenacity, not absolute force, unless the question asks for raw force*. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic** 🧠 > **"Pets Need Vivid Animals"** → > **P**olyester — **E**sther (sweet smell) / **N**ylon — **V**egetable (celery) / > **V**iscose — **P**aper / **A**crylic — **F**ish (acrid).

topics.title: physical structure of fibre forming polymers
topics.content:
# Module Experiment 2: Physical Structure of Fibre Forming Polymers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fibre forming polymers** are high molecular weight macromolecules (typically $M_n > 10,000$ g/mol) possessing a unique combination of physical and chemical attributes that enable them to be drawn into continuous filaments exhibiting high axial strength, flexibility, and an **aspect ratio (length-to-diameter) greater than 100:1**. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Course Code: PCPOL607 - Polymer Chemistry / Polymer Technology), the *physical structure* of these polymers encompasses the arrangement of macromolecular chains in space, encompassing: - **Chain geometry** (linear, branched, crosslinked, network) - **Morphology** (amorphous vs. semi-crystalline regions) - **Molecular orientation** (random coil vs. aligned chains) - **Supramolecular architecture** (lamellae, spherulites, fibrils) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Only **linear** or **slightly branched** polymers are *truly fibre-forming*. Highly crosslinked polymers (e.g., vulcanized rubber, thermosets like Bakelite) cannot be melt-spun or solution-spun because chain mobility is severely restricted. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spaghetti Analogy Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti: - **Single straight strand** = a perfectly linear polymer chain → it can be pulled, aligned, and stretched into a strong fibre (like Nylon-6,6). - **Tangled mess with no order** = amorphous polymer region → weak, rubbery, low strength. - **Parallel bundles held together** = crystalline regions → strong, stiff, and resistant to deformation. - **Strands tied together with knots at random points** = crosslinked polymer → cannot be drawn into fibres (becomes hard and brittle, like a thermoset). A good fibre must therefore be like **dry, straight spaghetti strands lined up parallel** to one another and then bundled into a thread. ### 1.3 Fundamental Requirements for a Polymer to be Fibre-Forming | S.No. | Structural Requirement | Reason | |-------|------------------------|--------| | 1 | **Linear or only slightly branched chains** | Allows chain slippage and orientation during drawing | | 2 | High molecular weight ($\bar{M}_w > 20,000$) | Ensures sufficient chain entanglements for strength | | 3 | Presence of strong **intermolecular forces** (H-bonds, dipole-dipole, van der Waals) | Provides cohesive energy density | | 4 | Regular, stereoregular chain configuration | Enables crystallization | | 5 | Sufficient chain stiffness (high $T_g$) | Prevents excessive creep and maintains fibre shape | | 6 | Ability to be melted or dissolved without decomposition | Enables melt-spinning or solution-spinning | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric**: A "useful" textile fibre must possess a **tenacity ≥ 0.2 N/tex** and an **elongation at break between 10–35%** to be commercially viable. ### 1.4 Classification of Fibre Forming Polymers ``` Fibre Forming Polymers │ ├── 1. Natural Polymers │ ├── Cellulose (Cotton, Linen, Jute) │ ├── Protein (Wool, Silk) │ └── Natural Rubber (not fibre-forming) │ ├── 2. Regenerated / Semi-Synthetic │ ├── Rayon (regenerated cellulose) │ └── Acetate (cellulose acetate) │ └── 3. Synthetic Polymers ├── Polyamides (Nylon-6, Nylon-6,6) ├── Polyesters (PET - Polyethylene terephthalate) ├── Polyacrylonitrile (PAN - Acrylic fibre) ├── Polyolefins (PP, UHMWPE) ├── Polyurethanes (Spandex/Lycra) └── Fluoropolymers (PTFE) ``` # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Physical Structure in Polymers The physical structure of a fibre-forming polymer is best understood as a **multi-scale hierarchy**, often referred to as the **"Structure-Property Hierarchy"**: ``` LEVEL 1: Monomer Unit (Å scale, ~0.1 nm) ↓ Polymerization LEVEL 2: Polymer Chain (nm scale, 10–100 nm) ↓ Chain Folding / Coiling LEVEL 3: Lamellae / Crystallites (10–50 nm) ↓ Aggregation LEVEL 4: Spherulites / Fibrils (μm scale, 0.1–10 μm) ↓ Spinning & Drawing LEVEL 5: Fibre (mm to m scale) ``` ### 2.2 Chain Geometry (Primary Structural Level) #### (a) Linear Polymers - Chains are **unbranched** and arranged in a head-to-tail sequence. - Chains can pack closely → high crystallinity → high tensile strength. - **Examples**: HDPE, Nylon-6,6, PET, PAN, isotactic PP. #### (b) Branched Polymers - Side chains of varying length protrude from the main backbone. - Branches disrupt chain packing → lower crystallinity → lower density. - **Examples**: LDPE, ethylene-propylene copolymers. #### (c) Crosslinked Polymers - Covalent bonds tie adjacent chains together at junction points (crosslinks). - These are **NOT melt-processable** and therefore **cannot be spun into fibres**. - **Examples**: Vulcanized rubber, cured phenolic resins, crosslinked polyethylene (PEX). #### (d) Network Polymers - Extreme case of crosslinking forming a single 3D giant molecule. - Completely infusible and insoluble. ### 2.3 Morphology: Amorphous vs. Crystalline Regions Real polymers are **never 100% crystalline** (except single crystals grown in lab). They are characterized by **percent crystallinity** (%C): $$ \% \, \text{Crystallinity} = \frac{\rho_c \left(\rho_s - \rho_a\right)}{\rho_s \left(\rho_c - \rho_a\right)} \times 100 $$ Where: - $\rho_c$ = density of completely crystalline polymer - $\rho_a$ = density of completely amorphous polymer - $\rho_s$ = density of the actual sample **Why this matters for fibres:** - Crystalline regions act as **"anchor points"** or **"physical crosslinks"** - Amorphous regions provide **flexibility and dyeability** - A typical PET fibre has %C ≈ **30–40%**; Nylon-6,6 has %C ≈ **40–55%** ### 2.4 Molecular Orientation in Fibres When a fibre is drawn (stretched), polymer chains align preferentially along the fibre axis. This is quantified by the **Hermans Orientation Function $f$**: $$ f = \frac{3 \langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2} $$ Where $\theta$ is the angle between the chain axis and the fibre axis. - $f = 1$ → perfect alignment (all chains parallel to fibre axis) - $f = 0$ → completely random orientation - $f = -0.5$ → perfect perpendicular alignment (theoretical) > [!NOTE] > Highly oriented fibres (e.g., UHMWPE Dyneema) achieve $f \approx 0.95$–$0.99$, yielding tenacities up to **35 g/denier** (≈ **3.5 N/tex**). ### 2.5 Spherulitic Structure On slow cooling from the melt, polymers like Nylon and PP crystallize into **spherulites** — spherical aggregates of lamellar crystallites radiating from a central nucleus. **Drawbacks for fibres:** - Spherulites are **weak points** and reduce transparency. - During fibre spinning, **rapid quenching** and **drawing** break up spherulites and align lamellae into **fibrillar structure** → high strength. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Units | Significance | |---|-------------------|--------------------------|-------|--------------| | 1 | Degree of Polymerization | $DP_n = \dfrac{\bar{M}_n}{M_0}$ | dimensionless | Number of repeat units per chain | | 2 | Percent Crystallinity | $\%C = \dfrac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | % | Determines stiffness, opacity, density | | 3 | Hermans Orientation Function | $f = \dfrac{3 \langle \cos^2\theta \rangle - 1}{2}$ | $-0.5$ to $1$ | Measures chain alignment along fibre axis | | 4 | Birefringence (Optical) | $\Delta n = n_\parallel - n_\perp$ | dimensionless | Quick lab measure of orientation | | 5 | Cohesive Energy Density (CED) | $CED = \dfrac{E_{coh}}{V_m}$ | $\text{J/cm}^3$ or MPa | Indicates strength of inter-chain forces | | 6 | Solubility Parameter (Hildebrand) | $\delta = \sqrt{CED}$ | $(\text{J/cm}^3)^{1/2}$ | Predicts polymer-solvent compatibility for solution spinning | | 7 | End-to-End Distance (Random Coil) | $\langle r^2 \rangle^{1/2} = l \sqrt{2n}$ | nm | Measures chain dimensions in amorphous state | | 8 | Characteristic Ratio | $C_n = \dfrac{\langle r^2 \rangle_0}{n l^2}$ | dimensionless | Measures chain stiffness | | 9 | Avrami Equation (Crystallization Kinetics) | $X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K t^n)$ | dimensionless | Models isothermal crystallization | | 10 | Glass Transition Temperature (Fox-Flory) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{1}{T_{g,\infty}} + \dfrac{K}{\bar{M}_n}$ | Kelvin (K) | $T_g$ increases with $\bar{M}_n$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always use **Kelvin** in the Fox-Flory equation. If given $T_g$ in °C, first convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **PET bottles** rely on biaxial orientation for transparency and impact strength. - **Bulletproof vests** (Kevlar, Dyneema) exploit near-perfect chain orientation ($f \approx 0.99$). - **Carbon fibres** start from PAN precursor — orientation of PAN chains determines final carbon fibre modulus. - **Geotextiles & tyre cords** require high-tenacity PET and Nylon-6,6 made possible by controlled crystallinity + orientation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Percent Crystallinity from Density Measurements ### Problem Statement A sample of Nylon-6,6 has the following data: - Density of fully crystalline Nylon-6,6: $\rho_c = 1.220$ g/cm³ - Density of fully amorphous Nylon-6,6: $\rho_a = 1.069$ g/cm³ - Measured density of the actual fibre sample: $\rho_s = 1.140$ g/cm³ Calculate the **percent crystallinity** of the fibre. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Recall the working equation.** $$ \%C = \frac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100 $$ **Step 2: Substitute the numerical values into the equation.** $$ \%C = \frac{1.220 \times (1.140 - 1.069)}{1.140 \times (1.220 - 1.069)} \times 100 $$ **Step 3: Compute the numerator difference first.** $$ 1.140 - 1.069 = 0.071 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$ **Step 4: Multiply by $\rho_c$.** $$ 1.220 \times 0.071 = 0.08662 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$ **Step 5: Compute the denominator difference.** $$ 1.220 - 1.069 = 0.151 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$ **Step 6: Multiply by $\rho_s$.** $$ 1.140 \times 0.151 = 0.17214 \text{ g/cm}^3 $$ **Step 7: Form the ratio.** $$ \%C = \frac{0.08662}{0.17214} \times 100 $$ **Step 8: Evaluate the final division.** $$ \%C = 0.5032 \times 100 = 50.32\% $$ ### Final Answer The Nylon-6,6 fibre is approximately **50.3% crystalline**. This is consistent with the typical commercial value of 40–55% for as-spun, undrawn Nylon-6,6 yarns. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hermans Orientation Function from Birefringence Data ### Problem Statement A drawn PET fibre shows a birefringence $\Delta n = n_\parallel - n_\perp = 0.105$. The intrinsic birefringence (for perfectly oriented chains) is $\Delta n^0 = 0.220$. Calculate the **Hermans orientation function** $f$. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Use the relationship between birefringence and orientation.** $$ f = \frac{\Delta n}{\Delta n^0} $$ **Step 2: Substitute the values.** $$ f = \frac{0.105}{0.220} $$ **Step 3: Evaluate.** $$ f = 0.4773 $$ ### Interpretation Since $f$ lies between $0$ (random) and $1$ (perfectly aligned), the PET fibre is **moderately oriented**. Higher draw ratios could push $f$ closer to $0.7$–$0.8$, increasing tenacity significantly. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fox–Flory Equation for $T_g$ vs. Molecular Weight ### Problem Statement For a PET sample: - $T_{g,\infty} = 343$ K (= 70 °C) - $K = 1.2 \times 10^5$ K·g/mol - $\bar{M}_n = 20,000$ g/mol Find the glass transition temperature $T_g$ in °C. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Write Fox–Flory equation.** $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{1}{T_{g,\infty}} + \frac{K}{\bar{M}_n} $$ **Step 2: Compute $\dfrac{1}{T_{g,\infty}}$.** $$ \frac{1}{343} = 2.915 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1} $$ **Step 3: Compute $\dfrac{K}{\bar{M}_n}$.** $$ \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{20{,}000} = 6.0 \text{ K}^{-1} $$ **Step 4: Sum.** $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = 2.915 \times 10^{-3} + 6.0 = 6.002915 \text{ K}^{-1} $$ **Step 5: Invert to get $T_g$ in Kelvin.** $$ T_g = \frac{1}{6.002915} = 0.1666 \text{ K} $$ > [!WARNING] > This result is clearly wrong physically. The student should notice that **$K$ must be entered with $\bar{M}_n$ matching units** — typical $K$ is on the order of **$10^5$ g·K/mol**, but the formula requires care with powers. Let us recheck the literature value: for many polymers, $K \approx 1.0 \times 10^5$ (units g/mol·K), but a more typical PET $K = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ K·mol/g? No — the correct form is: $$ T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\bar{M}_n} $$ Let us re-derive using the corrected form. ### Re-Derivation (Corrected Fox–Flory) **Step 1 (Corrected):** $$ T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\bar{M}_n} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute: $$ T_g = 343 - \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{20{,}000} = 343 - 6 $$ **Step 3:** Convert to °C: $$ T_g (°C) = 343 - 273.15 = 69.85 °C \approx 70 °C $$ ### Final Answer $T_g \approx 70$ °C for PET with $\bar{M}_n = 20,000$ g/mol — close to literature value (~67–81 °C). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Fibre Formers | Property | Cotton (Cellulose) | Wool (Keratin) | PET | Nylon-6,6 | PAN (Acrylic) | PP | |----------|--------------------|----------------|------|-----------|----------------|-----| | Repeat Unit $M_0$ (g/mol) | 162 | ~110 (residue) | 192 | 226 | 53 | 42 | | $T_g$ (°C) | — | 170 (dry) | 70–80 | 50–60 | 85–105 | -20 to -10 | | $T_m$ (°C) | decomposes | decomposes | 255–265 | 255–265 | 320 (softens) | 165–170 | | Tenacity (g/denier) | 2.5–3.5 | 1.0–1.7 | 4.0–6.0 | 4.5–7.5 | 2.0–3.5 | 3.5–6.0 | | Elongation (%) | 5–10 | 25–35 | 15–30 | 20–40 | 20–30 | 15–30 | | % Crystallinity (typical) | 60–70 | 0 (semi-ordered) | 30–40 | 40–55 | low | 50–65 | | Moisture Regain (%) | 7–8.5 | 14–18 | 0.4 | 4.0–4.5 | 1.0–2.0 | <0.1 | | Main Intermolecular Force | H-bonds | H-bonds, ionic | Dipole + π | H-bonds | Dipole | van der Waals | > [!NOTE] > **1 denier** = mass in grams of 9000 metres of fibre. Conversion: $1 \text{ g/den} = 0.111 \text{ N/tex} = 0.0883 \text{ N/g}$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Hierarchical Levels of Fibre Structure ```mermaid graph TD A[MONOMER
~0.1 nm scale] --> B[POLYMER CHAIN
10 to 100 nm scale] B --> C[CHAIN FOLDING
lamella formation] C --> D[SPHERULITE or FIBRIL
0.1 to 10 micrometre scale] D --> E[AS-SPUN FIBRE
after extrusion] E --> F[DRAWN FIBRE
oriented chains] F --> G[TEXTILE YARN
twisted bundle] style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFB347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#FF8C42,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FF6B35,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style F fill:#C0392B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#922B21,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Chain Geometry Classification ```mermaid graph LR A[POLYMER CHAIN GEOMETRY] --> B[LINEAR
HDPE, Nylon, PET] A --> C[BRANCHED
LDPE] A --> D[CROSSLINKED
Vulcanised rubber] A --> E[NETWORK
Bakelite, Epoxy] B --> B1[CAN BE FIBRE FORMED] C --> C1[DIFFICULTY IN SPINNING] D --> D1[NOT FIBRE FORMING] E --> E1[NOT FIBRE FORMING] style A fill:#3498DB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B fill:#2ECC71,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#F1C40F,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#8E44AD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B1 fill:#27AE60,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C1 fill:#D4AC0D,stroke:#000 style D1 fill:#922B21,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E1 fill:#5B2C6F,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fibre Spinning ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[POLYMER MELT or SOLUTION] --> S1[SPINNERET
fine holes 0.1 to 0.5 mm] S1 --> C1[COAGULATION or COOLING
solidification] C1 --> D1[DRAWING STAGE
orient chains along axis] D1 --> A1[ANNEALING
set crystallinity] A1 --> T1[TEXTURING or TWISTING] T1 --> F1[FINAL FIBRE YARN] D1 --> OR["Increases Hermans f toward 1
Increases tenacity
Decreases elongation"] A1 --> CR["Increases percent C
Improves dimensional stability"] style P1 fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#000 style S1 fill:#F8C471,stroke:#000 style C1 fill:#82E0AA,stroke:#000 style D1 fill:#F1948A,stroke:#000 style A1 fill:#BB8FCE,stroke:#000 style T1 fill:#F7DC6F,stroke:#000 style F1 fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#fff style OR fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#000 style CR fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Amorphous vs. Crystalline Domain Arrangement in a Fibre ```mermaid graph TB subgraph UNDRAWN["UNDRAWN STATE random coil"] U1[Amorphous tangle] U2[Small crystallites scattered] end subgraph DRAWN["DRAWN STATE oriented chains"] D1[Crystalline lamellae aligned along axis] D2[Amorphous tie molecules between lamellae] D3[Strong covalent backbone along fibre axis] end UNDRAWN -->|DRAWING| DRAWN style U1 fill:#F5B7B1,stroke:#000 style U2 fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#000 style D1 fill:#A9DFBF,stroke:#000 style D2 fill:#D4EFDF,stroke:#000 style D3 fill:#82E0AA,stroke:#000 style UNDRAWN fill:#FDEDEC,stroke:#333 style DRAWN fill:#E8F8F5,stroke:#333 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the term "fibre forming polymer". List the four essential structural requirements for a polymer to be suitable for fibre formation.** **Model Answer:** A fibre forming polymer is a high molecular weight macromolecule that can be processed (melt or solution) into continuous filaments with high aspect ratio (>100:1) and good tensile strength. Essential structural requirements: 1. **Linear or only slightly branched chain architecture** — to allow chain alignment during drawing. 2. **High molecular weight** ($\bar{M}_n > 20{,}000$ g/mol) — to provide chain entanglements. 3. **Strong intermolecular forces** (H-bonds, dipole-dipole) — to give cohesive strength. 4. **Regular stereoregular configuration** — to enable crystallization. **(3 Marks)** --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between amorphous and crystalline regions in a polymer fibre. How does the degree of crystallinity affect fibre properties?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Amorphous Region | Crystalline Region | |---------|-------------------|---------------------| | Chain arrangement | Random coils | Ordered, parallel chains | | Density | Lower | Higher | | Strength | Lower | Higher | | Dye uptake | Higher | Lower | | Transparency | Better | Poorer (light scattering) | | $T_g$ behaviour | Dominant | Less affected | **Effect of crystallinity on fibres:** - Higher %C → higher tenacity, higher modulus, lower elongation. - Higher %C → lower moisture absorption, lower dyeability. - Higher %C → improved thermal stability and chemical resistance. - Typical commercial fibres: PET 30–40%, Nylon-6,6 40–55%, Cotton ~65%. **(3 Marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** > 1. Always convert °C to K in any equation involving $T_g$ or $T_m$ — failure costs **1 mark**. > 2. In Hermans orientation problems, write the **formula first** before substituting values — examiners award a mark for the correct expression even if arithmetic slips. > 3. In %Crystallinity problems, **state the formula** clearly; partial marks are awarded for correct setup even if the final division is wrong. > 4. Always mention **units** in final numerical answers. --- ### **Question B — Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **physical structure of fibre forming polymers** at the molecular level. Discuss the role of chain geometry, molecular weight, intermolecular forces, and stereoregularity in determining fibre properties. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A sample of PET fibre has the following data: $\rho_c = 1.455$ g/cm³, $\rho_a = 1.335$ g/cm³, $\rho_s = 1.390$ g/cm³. (i) Calculate the **percent crystallinity**. (ii) If the same fibre has a birefringence $\Delta n = 0.165$ and intrinsic birefringence $\Delta n^0 = 0.220$, calculate the **Hermans orientation function**. (iii) Comment on the suitability of this fibre for **high-tenacity industrial applications**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to B-A (a): **1. Chain Geometry:** Fibre forming polymers must have predominantly **linear chains**. Branching disrupts close packing and prevents alignment. Crosslinking makes the polymer infusible — hence cannot be melt-spun. **2. Molecular Weight:** Number average molecular weight $\bar{M}_n$ should be ≥ 20,000 g/mol. Too low → weak fibres; too high → difficult melt processing and very high melt viscosity. **3. Intermolecular Forces:** Strong secondary forces between chains are critical. Examples: - Nylon-6,6 → strong **H-bonding** between N–H and C=O groups. - PET → strong **dipole-dipole** + aromatic π-π stacking. - PAN → strong **dipole** (C≡N group). **4. Stereoregularity:** Isotactic or syndiotactic placement of substituents allows chains to pack into **crystallites**. Atactic chains (e.g., atactic PP) are amorphous rubbers and **cannot form useful fibres**. **5. Role in Properties:** All four factors collectively determine **tenacity, elongation, $T_g$, $T_m$, dyeability**, and **chemical resistance**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to B-A (b): **(i) Percent Crystallinity** Step 1: Apply the density-based formula. $$ \%C = \frac{\rho_c (\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s (\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100 $$ Step 2: Numerator part — $\rho_s - \rho_a = 1.390 - 1.335 = 0.055$ g/cm³. Step 3: Numerator = $1.455 \times 0.055 = 0.080025$. Step 4: Denominator part — $\rho_c - \rho_a = 1.455 - 1.335 = 0.120$ g/cm³. Step 5: Denominator = $1.390 \times 0.120 = 0.1668$. Step 6: Ratio = $0.080025 / 0.1668 = 0.4798$. Step 7: $\%C = 0.4798 \times 100 \approx \mathbf{48.0\%}$. **(3 Marks)** **(ii) Hermans Orientation Function** Step 1: Apply $f = \Delta n / \Delta n^0$. Step 2: $f = 0.165 / 0.220 = \mathbf{0.75}$. **(2 Marks)** **(iii) Suitability Comment** With %C ≈ 48% and $f = 0.75$, the PET fibre is **moderately to highly crystalline** and **well-oriented**. Tenacity is expected to be in the range of **5–7 g/den** (~0.55–0.78 N/tex). This makes it **suitable for high-tenacity industrial applications** such as tyre cord, seat belts, and conveyor belts — but for bullet-proof vests (where $f \geq 0.95$ is needed), it is **not optimal**. **(2 Marks)** --- ### **Question B — Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **morphology of semi-crystalline polymers**. Explain the formation of **spherulites**, **lamellae**, and **fibrils**, and discuss how these structures influence the mechanical properties of fibres. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Using the **Fox–Flory equation**, calculate the glass transition temperature $T_g$ of Nylon-6 with $\bar{M}_n = 15,000$ g/mol, given $T_{g,\infty} = 325$ K and $K = 1.5 \times 10^5$ g·K/mol. Compare the result with another sample having $\bar{M}_n = 30,000$ g/mol. What is the practical implication for fibre spinning? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to B-B (a): **1. Semi-Crystalline Morphology:** Semi-crystalline polymers consist of an **interpenetrating two-phase structure**: ordered crystalline regions embedded in a disordered amorphous matrix. **2. Lamellae:** Polymer chains fold back and forth like a "folded accordion" to form thin plate-like crystals called **lamellae**, typically 10–50 nm thick. Chain folding is driven by kinetic factors during crystallization. **3. Spherulites:** Many lamellae grow radially from a common nucleus, forming spherical superstructures called **spherulites**, ranging from 0.1 to several hundred micrometres. Under polarized light, spherulites show a characteristic **"Maltese cross"** pattern. **4. Fibrils:** Under **stress (drawing)**, lamellae break apart and chains unfold into highly aligned **microfibrils**. These are the building blocks of high-tenacity fibres. **5. Influence on Mechanical Properties:** - Crystalline regions act as **physical crosslinks** providing strength and rigidity. - Amorphous tie-molecules between lamellae provide **toughness** and **elongation**. - Spherulites reduce transparency and create weak boundaries → minimized by rapid quenching during fibre spinning. - Fibrillar morphology (post-drawing) gives highest tenacity. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to B-B (b): **1. Fox–Flory Equation:** $$ T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\bar{M}_n} $$ **2. Calculation for Sample 1** ($\bar{M}_n = 15{,}000$ g/mol): Step 1: $K / \bar{M}_n = 1.5 \times 10^5 / 15{,}000 = 10$ K. Step 2: $T_g = 325 - 10 = 315$ K. Step 3: Convert to °C: $T_g = 315 - 273.15 = \mathbf{41.85\ °C}$. **3.
