Calculation for Sample 2** ($\bar{M}_n = 30{,}000$ g/mol): Step 1: $K / \bar{M}_n = 1.5 \times 10^5 / 30{,}000 = 5$ K. Step 2: $T_g = 325 - 5 = 320$ K. Step 3: Convert: $T_g = 320 - 273.15 = \mathbf{46.85\ °C}$. **4. Comparison:** - Sample 2 (higher $\bar{M}_n$) has **higher $T_g$** by ~5 °C. - The relationship is non-linear; $T_g$ rises sharply at low $\bar{M}_n$ and plateaus as $\bar{M}_n \to \infty$. **5. Practical Implication:** - Higher $\bar{M}_n$ improves **fibre strength, wear resistance, and dyeability uniformity**. - However, melt viscosity increases sharply with $\bar{M}_n$, making **spinning more difficult** and requiring higher temperatures and pressures. - An optimum $\bar{M}_n$ must balance **processability** and **final fibre properties**. **(7 Marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist** ### A. Must-Know Definitions - ✅ Fibre forming polymer — high MW polymer spinnable into continuous filaments. - ✅ %Crystallinity — fraction of ordered regions; calculated via density. - ✅ Hermans orientation function $f$ — measure of chain alignment. - ✅ Spherulite — spherical superstructure of lamellae. - ✅ Lamella — folded-chain crystal plate. - ✅ Tie molecule — chain segment connecting adjacent crystallites. ### B. Must-Know Formulas - ✅ $\%C = \dfrac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ - ✅ $f = \dfrac{3 \langle \cos^2 \theta \rangle - 1}{2} = \dfrac{\Delta n}{\Delta n^0}$ - ✅ $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{\bar{M}_n}$ (Fox–Flory) - ✅ $DP_n = \bar{M}_n / M_0$ - ✅ $\delta = \sqrt{CED}$ ### C. Must-Know Facts - ✅ Only **linear/slightly branched** polymers form fibres — crosslinked polymers cannot. - ✅ **Nylon-6,6** has H-bonding; **PET** has dipole + π; **PAN** has CN dipole. - ✅ Drawing increases $f$; annealing increases %C. - ✅ Higher %C → stronger, stiffer, less extensible fibre. - ✅ $f = 1$ perfect, $f = 0$ random, $f = -0.5$ perpendicular. - ✅ Commercial fibres: PET 30–40% C, Nylon-6,6 40–55% C, Cotton ~65% C. ### D. Examiner's Quick Tips - 📌 Always quote the **formula** before substitution. - 📌 Always convert **°C to K** when using $T_g$ / $T_m$ in equations. - 📌 Always include **units** in numerical answers (g/den, N/tex, %, K, etc.). - 📌 For descriptive answers, use **headings + bullets + table** for clarity. - 📌 End long answers with a **one-line conclusion** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for this.

topics.title: production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Production

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production** is the integrated engineering process of converting raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, semi-finished goods, and intellectual inputs into finished products that possess defined functional, dimensional, and quality specifications, utilizing controlled combinations of **machines**, **manpower**, **materials**, **methods**, and **measurement (5M's)**, while optimizing parameters such as **productivity**, **efficiency**, **quality**, **cost**, and **delivery time**.

In the context of **PCPOL607 – Production Laboratory / Production Engineering Practices**, *Module Experiment 2* focuses on a hands-on investigation of the production workflow, where students observe, measure, and analyse an actual manufacturing operation to evaluate the effectiveness of the conversion process.

Mathematically, the production function can be formally represented as:

$$P = f(M, L, C, E, I)$$

Where:
- $P$ = Output (finished goods)
- $M$ = Materials consumed
- $L$ = Labour hours expended
- $C$ = Capital (machinery, tooling)
- $E$ = Energy utilised
- $I$ = Information / management inputs

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bakery Analogy:** Imagine a local bakery that turns flour, sugar, eggs, and butter (raw materials) into cakes and pastries (finished products). The bakers (labour) mix the dough using ovens and mixers (machines), following a recipe (method), in a clean workspace (environment), and continuously check the taste and weight (quality control). **Production is exactly this — but applied to engineering components** such as gears, shafts, brackets, and assemblies.

For a KTU engineering student, the simplest way to internalize *production* is to think of it as:

**Inputs $\rightarrow$ Process $\rightarrow$ Outputs**, with the **process** being governed by engineering precision, repeatability, and measurable standards.

## 1.3 The 5 M's of Production — Pillars of Manufacturing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every production experiment in KTU's PCPOL607 lab will explicitly evaluate one or more of these foundational elements:

| S.No | Element | Engineering Meaning | Example in Lab |
|------|---------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Man** | Skilled/unskilled labour involved | Operator handling the lathe |
| 2 | **Machine** | Equipment used for transformation | Lathe, milling, drilling machines |
| 3 | **Material** | Raw stock being processed | Mild steel rod, aluminium billet |
| 4 | **Method** | Process route & procedure | Turning, facing, knurling sequence |
| 5 | **Measurement** | Quality control & inspection | Vernier caliper, micrometer |

## 1.4 Classification of Production Systems (Module 2 Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to identify and differentiate between the following three primary production systems during their lab experiments:

1. **Job Production** — Low volume, high customization (e.g., custom-built pressure vessel for a client order).
2. **Batch Production** — Medium volume, group of similar items (e.g., 50 identical gears produced together).
3. **Mass / Flow Production** — High volume, standardized products (e.g., 10,000 identical bolts per day).

A specific lab experiment in Module 2 may involve observing any one of these systems in action (typically a **batch production experiment** such as turning multiple identical shafts on a lathe).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of a Production Experiment

A production experiment in PCPOL607 is typically structured around these logical phases:

### Phase 1 — Pre-Production Analysis
- Reading the engineering drawing.
- Identifying raw material dimensions and required final dimensions.
- Selecting the **process** (turning, milling, drilling, etc.).
- Selecting the **machine** and **tools**.
- Estimating **cycle time**.

### Phase 2 — Production Execution
- Mounting workpiece and tool.
- Setting cutting parameters: **cutting speed ($V_c$)**, **feed ($f$)**, **depth of cut ($d$)**.
- Performing the operation.
- Recording actual time taken.

### Phase 3 — Post-Production Analysis
- Measuring final dimensions with metrology instruments.
- Calculating **productivity**, **efficiency**, **machining time**, and **metal removal rate (MRR)**.

## 2.2 Core Theoretical Equations Governing the Production Process

### 2.2.1 Metal Removal Rate (MRR)

The MRR is the volume of metal removed per unit time, a critical indicator of production efficiency.

$$\text{MRR} = V_c \times f \times d$$

Where:
- $V_c$ = Cutting speed in mm/min
- $f$ = Feed rate in mm/rev
- $d$ = Depth of cut in mm

Units: **mm³/min**.

### 2.2.2 Machining Time ($T_m$)

The actual time required to perform the cutting operation on the workpiece:

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \times N}$$

Where:
- $L$ = Total length of cut (mm) = Length of workpiece + Approach length + Over-travel
- $f$ = Feed (mm/rev)
- $N$ = Spindle speed (rev/min)

### 2.2.3 Spindle Speed (N)

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D}$$

Where $D$ = Initial diameter of workpiece (mm).

### 2.2.4 Productivity

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output (good products produced)}}{\text{Input (labour hours + machine hours)}}$$

Units: **pieces/hour** or **kg/hour** depending on context.

### 2.2.5 Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Standard Time}}{\text{Actual Time}} \times 100$$

A value greater than 100% indicates the operator is faster than standard; less than 100% indicates slower performance.

### 2.2.6 Production Cost per Piece

$$\text{Cost per piece} = \text{Material Cost} + \text{Labour Cost} + \text{Overhead Cost}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Practical Range |
|------|-----------|---------|-------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rev/min | 50 – 3000 rpm |
| 2 | Cutting Speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | 10 – 600 m/min |
| 3 | Feed Rate | $f_n = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | 0.05 – 2.0 mm/rev |
| 4 | Metal Removal Rate | $\text{MRR} = V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | – |
| 5 | Machining Time | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | min | – |
| 6 | Productivity | $\dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | pcs/hr | – |
| 7 | Efficiency | $\dfrac{\text{Standard Time}}{\text{Actual Time}} \times 100$ | % | $\leq$ 100% ideal |
| 8 | Tool Life (Taylor) | $V_c \cdot T^n = C$ | m/min, min | $n$ = 0.1 – 0.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always cross-check units. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for unit inconsistency in numerical problems (e.g., mixing mm with cm without conversion).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The production principles studied in this experiment directly apply to:

- **CNC Machining Cells** — where $V_c$, $f$, and $d$ are programmed parameters.
- **Automated Assembly Lines** — where productivity equations determine throughput.
- **Lean Manufacturing** — where efficiency calculations drive waste reduction.
- **Industry 4.0 Smart Factories** — where all production parameters are monitored in real-time via IoT sensors.

By mastering these fundamentals in Module Experiment 2, the student builds the analytical foundation needed for **Production Planning & Control (PPC)**, **Operations Research**, and **Industrial Engineering** in subsequent semesters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Total Machining Time for a Multi-Operation Turning Process

This derivation is the most commonly tested numerical problem in KTU PCPOL607 viva and written exams. We will derive the **total production time** for producing a stepped shaft on a lathe.

### Given (Typical KTU Lab Problem):

> A mild steel workpiece of initial diameter $D_0 = 60$ mm and length $L_w = 150$ mm is to be reduced to $D_f = 50$ mm by turning in a single pass. Cutting speed $V_c = 30$ m/min, feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev. Approach length = 3 mm, over-travel = 2 mm. Calculate: (a) Spindle speed, (b) Feed rate, (c) MRR, (d) Machining time.

### Step 1 — Identify the Diameters

$$D_{\text{initial}} = 60 \text{ mm}, \quad D_{\text{final}} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Depth of Cut ($d$)

Depth of cut is the radial reduction per pass:

$$d = \frac{D_0 - D_f}{2} = \frac{60 - 50}{2} = \frac{10}{2}$$

$$d = 5 \text{ mm}$$

This is the **single-pass depth of cut**.

### Step 3 — Calculate Spindle Speed ($N$)

Using the formula:

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D_0}$$

Substitute values (using the **initial diameter**, since the spindle speed is set at the start):

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 60}$$

$$N = \frac{30000}{188.496}$$

$$N \approx 159.15 \text{ rev/min}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Feed Rate ($f_n$)

$$f_n = f \times N = 0.3 \times 159.15$$

$$f_n = 47.745 \text{ mm/min}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Total Length of Cut ($L$)

$$L = L_w + L_{\text{approach}} + L_{\text{over}}$$

$$L = 150 + 3 + 2 = 155 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 6 — Calculate Metal Removal Rate (MRR)

$$\text{MRR} = V_c \times f \times d$$

Converting $V_c$ from m/min to mm/min:

$$V_c = 30 \text{ m/min} = 30000 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$\text{MRR} = 30000 \times 0.3 \times 5$$

$$\text{MRR} = 45000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

### Step 7 — Calculate Machining Time ($T_m$)

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} = \frac{155}{0.3 \times 159.15}$$

$$T_m = \frac{155}{47.745}$$

$$T_m \approx 3.247 \text{ min}$$

Converting to seconds for practical purposes:

$$T_m = 3.247 \times 60 \approx 194.8 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 8 — Calculate Productivity (assuming 1 piece per setup)

If the total cycle time including setup (3 min) and tool handling (1 min) is $T_{\text{total}} = T_m + T_{\text{setup}} + T_{\text{handling}}$:

$$T_{\text{total}} = 3.247 + 3 + 1 = 7.247 \text{ min/piece}$$

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{60}{7.247} \approx 8.28 \text{ pieces/hour}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| KTU Exam Pattern Type | Key Variables Given | Required to Find | Critical Pitfall |
|------------------------|----------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Type 1: Turning | $V_c$, $f$, $D_0$, $D_f$, $L_w$ | $N$, $T_m$, MRR | Forgetting approach + over-travel |
| Type 2: Drilling | $V_c$, $f$, $D$, $L$, $N$ | $T_m$, MRR | Using feed per rev vs per minute |
| Type 3: Milling | $V_c$, $f_z$, $z$, $D$, $L$, $a_e$ | $N$, $f_n$, $T_m$ | Number of teeth $z$ involvement |
| Type 4: Productivity | Pieces produced, time taken | Output rate | Converting min to hour |
| Type 5: Efficiency | Standard time, actual time | $\eta$ % | Sign convention (above/below 100%) |
| Type 6: Tool Life (Taylor) | $V_c$, $n$, $C$ | Tool life $T$ | Logarithmic computation |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Tool Life Using Taylor's Equation

Taylor's tool life equation is given as:

$$V_c \cdot T^n = C$$

**Given:** $V_{c1} = 30$ m/min gives $T_1 = 90$ min. If $n = 0.25$, find the tool life at $V_{c2} = 45$ m/min.

### Step 1 — Find Constant $C$

$$C = V_{c1} \cdot T_1^n = 30 \cdot (90)^{0.25}$$

$$(90)^{0.25} = (90)^{1/4} = \sqrt{\sqrt{90}} \approx \sqrt{9.487} \approx 3.080$$

$$C = 30 \times 3.080 = 92.4$$

### Step 2 — Find $T_2$ at $V_{c2} = 45$ m/min

$$T_2 = \left(\frac{C}{V_{c2}}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\frac{92.4}{45}\right)^{1/0.25}$$

$$\frac{92.4}{45} \approx 2.053$$

$$(2.053)^{4} \approx 17.78 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 17.78 \text{ min}}$$

This shows that doubling the speed roughly quarters the tool life — a critical insight for production economics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Production System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Input] --> B[Material Storage and Inspection]
    B --> C[Pre Production Setup]
    C --> D[Machine Tool Selection]
    D --> E[Process Planning and Sequencing]

    G --> H{Meets Specification}
    H -- Yes --> I[Finished Good Output]
    H -- No --> J[Re work or Reject]
    J --> F
    I --> K[Productivity and Efficiency Analysis]
    F --> K
    K --> L[Final Report and Documentation]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define production. List the 5 M's of production with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
Production is the engineering process of transforming raw materials into finished goods using controlled combinations of machines, labour, methods, materials, and measurement, while ensuring quality, productivity, and cost efficiency. The **5 M's** are:

1. **Man** — Operator handling the lathe.
2. **Machine** — Centre lathe used for turning.
3. **Material** — Mild steel rod of $\phi$60 mm.
4. **Method** — Turning operation following the machining sequence.
5. **Measurement** — Vernier caliper for diameter verification.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Job Production, Batch Production, and Mass Production.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Job Production | Batch Production | Mass Production |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Volume | One or few | Medium | Very high |
| Variety | High | Moderate | Low / standardized |
| Skill | High | Medium | Low (repetitive) |
| Example | Custom gear box | 50 identical shafts | 10000 bolts/day |
| Setup | Per job | Per batch | One-time |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a production system. List the inputs, process, and outputs of a typical turning operation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel workpiece of diameter 80 mm is to be turned to 70 mm in a single pass. Length = 200 mm. $V_c = 25$ m/min, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev. Approach = 4 mm, Over-travel = 3 mm. Calculate spindle speed, MRR, and machining time. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A production system comprises three primary stages:

1. **Input Stage** — raw material, machine, operator, process plan, energy.
2. **Processing Stage** — setup, cutting, forming, joining, assembly.
3. **Output Stage** — finished part, scrap, productivity data, cost data.

The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) illustrates the IPO flow. For a turning operation:
- **Input:** Mild steel rod, centre lathe, HSS tool, operator.
- **Process:** Facing $\rightarrow$ Turning $\rightarrow$ Chamfering.
- **Output:** Finished turned shaft of specified diameter and surface finish.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Depth of cut:**
$$d = \frac{80 - 70}{2} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Spindle speed:**
$$N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{25000}{251.33} \approx 99.47 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — MRR:**
$$V_c = 25000 \text{ mm/min}$$
$$\text{MRR} = V_c \cdot f \cdot d = 25000 \times 0.25 \times 5 = 31250 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Total cut length:**
$$L = 200 + 4 + 3 = 207 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Machining time:**
$$T_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} = \frac{207}{0.25 \times 99.47} = \frac{207}{24.87} \approx 8.32 \text{ min}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define productivity and efficiency. An operator produces 40 pieces in 8 hours against a standard of 50 pieces. Calculate the efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain Taylor's tool life equation. A HSS tool at 40 m/min has tool life of 30 minutes. If cutting speed is increased to 50 m/min, find new tool life. Take $n = 0.25$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Productivity** = Output / Input = Pieces produced / Labour hours.
**Efficiency** = (Standard output / Actual output) × 100.

$$\eta = \frac{40}{50} \times 100 = 80\%$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

Taylor's equation: $V_c \cdot T^n = C$.

**Step 1 — Find C:**
$$C = 40 \times (30)^{0.25} = 40 \times 2.340 = 93.6$$

**Step 2 — Find $T_2$ at $V_{c2} = 50$:**
$$T_2 = \left(\frac{93.6}{50}\right)^{1/0.25} = (1.872)^{4} \approx 12.29 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 12.29 \text{ min}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Leading to Mark Deductions in PCPOL607:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing $V_c$ in m/min while $f$ is in mm/rev without conversion — leads to **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Forgetting Approach + Over-travel:** Students often use only workpiece length, losing **1 mark**.
> 3. **Using Final Diameter in Spindle Speed Formula:** Always use the **initial diameter** since spindle speed is set at start.
> 4. **Not Drawing the Block Diagram:** Theory questions without diagrams lose **up to 3 marks** in Part B.
> 5. **Confusing MRR with Material Removal Rate Units:** Must be mm³/min, not mm²/min.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2: Production**

- **Production** = Input $\rightarrow$ Process $\rightarrow$ Output transformation with measurable productivity.
- The **5 M's** — Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement — form the backbone of any production analysis.
- **Three production systems:** Job, Batch, Mass — distinguished by volume and variety.
- **Spindle Speed:** $N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$ — use **initial diameter**.
- **MRR:** $V_c \cdot f \cdot d$ — fundamental productivity metric.
- **Machining Time:** $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ — always include approach + over-travel in $L$.
- **Productivity** = Output / Input; **Efficiency** = Standard Time / Actual Time × 100.
- **Taylor's Tool Life:** $V_c \cdot T^n = C$ — higher speed drastically reduces tool life.
- Always carry out **unit conversions** before plugging into formulas — KTU examiners reward numerical discipline.
- **Block diagrams** of IPO and feedback loops are high-value, high-scoring answers in viva voce.
- Real-world application: All formulas apply directly to **CNC programming, automation, and Industry 4.0 production cells**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: chemical composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** chemical composition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Composition (Polymer Analytical Context):** Chemical composition refers to the qualitative identification and quantitative determination of the constituent elements (typically Carbon, Hydrogen, Nitrogen, Sulfur, Oxygen, and halogens) and functional groups present in a polymeric or organic sample. In polymer technology, it determines the molar ratio of monomer units, the presence of additives, fillers, plasticizers, and the empirical/skeletal formula of the macromolecule.

In the context of **PCPOL607 (Polymer Chemistry Lab / Polymer Characterization Lab)**, *chemical composition* specifically addresses the experimental determination of the **percentages of elements (C, H, N, S, O)** and the **functional group analysis** that helps identify the polymer class (e.g., polyamide, polyester, polyolefin) and confirm its structural identity.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Card" of a Polymer:**
> Think of a polymer sample as a finished cake. If you want to know *what went into the cake* (flour, sugar, eggs, butter), you perform a chemical analysis — like a baker inspecting their recipe card. **Chemical composition analysis** is essentially the "recipe card reader" of a polymer chemist. It tells us:
> - **Which elements** are present (the *ingredients* — C, H, N, O, S),
> - **How much of each** is present (the *proportions* — percentages),
> - **Which functional groups** are present (the *flavor profile* — carboxyl, amine, hydroxyl, etc.).

For example, a nylon-6,6 polymer has a chemical formula of $\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{N}_2\text{O}_2$. Knowing this composition allows the polymer engineer to:
- Verify the **degree of polymerization (DP)**,
- Confirm the **monomer ratio** in copolymers,
- Detect **impurities or degradation products**,
- Cross-check the **theoretical vs. experimental** composition for quality assurance.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants Used

The following **bold** constants and metrics are universally applied in chemical composition analysis:

- **Atomic mass unit (u) = $1.66054 \times 10^{-24}$ g/mol** — the standard reference for atomic weights.
- **Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.02214076 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹** — used to convert between atomic mass and particle count.
- **Empirical formula mass ($M_{ef}$)** — the simplest whole-number ratio atomic mass.
- **Molecular formula mass ($M_{mol}$)** — based on the actual molecular chain.
- **Percentage composition formula:** $\%E = \frac{\text{Mass of element in 1 mole of compound}}{\text{Molar mass of compound}} \times 100$

## 1.4 Why Chemical Composition Matters in KTU Polymer Curriculum

| Industrial / Academic Need | Role of Chemical Composition |
|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| Polymer Identification | Distinguishes PE, PP, PS, Nylon, PET |
| Quality Control | Batch-to-batch consistency verification |
| Copolymer Analysis | Determines monomer ratio (e.g., SBR) |
| Failure Analysis | Identifies oxidation/degradation products |
| Regulatory Compliance | RoHS, REACH elemental restrictions |
| Research & Development | New polymer synthesis verification |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

The experimental determination of chemical composition is grounded in **classical quantitative organic analysis** and **modern instrumental analysis**. The logical progression is:

1. **Step 1 — Sampling and Preparation:** A known mass of the dry, pure polymer sample is weighed accurately on an analytical balance (precision $\pm 0.0001$ g).

2. **Step 2 — Elemental Decomposition:** The sample is combusted at high temperature (typically $900\text{–}1000\,^\circ\text{C}$) in a stream of pure oxygen. This converts:
   - Carbon → $\text{CO}_2$
   - Hydrogen → $\text{H}_2\text{O}$
   - Nitrogen → $\text{N}_2$ (or $\text{NO}_x$, later reduced to $\text{N}_2$)
   - Sulfur → $\text{SO}_2$ / $\text{SO}_3$

3. **Step 3 — Separation and Detection:** The combustion products are passed through selective absorbents or chromatographic columns. Mass change of absorbents → mass of element.

4. **Step 4 — Quantitative Calculation:** Using stoichiometric relations, the percentage of each element is computed.

5. **Step 5 — Empirical Formula Derivation:** Convert mass percentages to mole ratios → divide by smallest → round to nearest whole number.

6. **Step 6 — Confirmation via Functional Group Analysis:** Chemical tests (e.g., Beilstein test for halogens, Lassaigne's test for N/S/halogens) and spectroscopic methods (FTIR, NMR) corroborate the elemental data.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why combustion at high temperature?** — To ensure **complete oxidation** of all C–H, C–C, and C–N bonds; lower temperatures leave soot and carbon residues.
- **Why pure oxygen?** — To drive the reaction to completion and avoid contamination from atmospheric $\text{N}_2$ (which would falsify nitrogen analysis).
- **Why selective absorbents?** — $\text{CO}_2$ is absorbed by **soda lime / Ascarite**; $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ by **anhydrous $\text{Mg}(\text{ClO}_4)_2$ (Anhydrone)** or **$\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$**; $\text{SO}_2$ by **silver vanadate**.
- **Why functional group analysis?** — Two polymers can have identical elemental composition (e.g., polyacrylonitrile and poly(vinyl amine)) but vastly different functional groups. Functional group analysis provides **structural uniqueness**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Table for Chemical Composition Analysis**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Percentage of element $E$ | $\%E = \frac{m_E}{m_{sample}} \times 100$ | $m_E$ = mass of element; $m_{sample}$ = sample mass |
| 2 | Number of moles of element | $n_E = \frac{m_E}{A_E}$ | $A_E$ = atomic mass of element (g/mol) |
| 3 | Mole ratio (empirical) | $r_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{min}}$ | Divide all by smallest mole value |
| 4 | Empirical formula mass | $M_{ef} = \sum (n_i^{ef} \times A_i)$ | g/mol |
| 5 | Molecular formula multiplier | $k = \frac{M_{mol}}{M_{ef}}$ | Integer (rounded) |
| 6 | Degree of Polymerization (DP) | $DP = \frac{M_n}{M_0}$ | $M_n$ = number-average MW; $M_0$ = repeat unit MW |
| 7 | Combustion stoichiometry (C) | $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2$ | $n_C = n_{\text{CO}_2}$ |
| 8 | Combustion stoichiometry (H) | $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | $n_H = 2 \times n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ |
| 9 | Nitrogen by Dumas method | $\%N = \frac{V_{N_2} \times 28 \times P}{m_{sample} \times R \times T} \times 100$ | P in atm, V in L, T in K |
| 10 | Kjeldahl nitrogen | $\%N = \frac{(V_{HCl} \times N_{HCl} - V_{NaOH} \times N_{NaOH}) \times 1.4007}{m_{sample}}$ | For protein/polymer amines |
| 11 | Acetyl value (OH groups) | $AV = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 43}{m}$ | Hydroxyl group quantification |
| 12 | Saponification value | $SV = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1}{m}$ | Ester group quantification |
| 13 | Iodine value (unsaturation) | $IV = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 12.69}{m}$ | Double bond quantification |
| 14 | Acid value | $Acid\,V = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{m}$ | Free carboxylic acid groups |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, the symbols $\vert V_b - V_s \vert$ represent the absolute difference between the blank titration volume and the sample titration volume. The pipe character is intentionally replaced with the word "absolute" / written out in the formula to avoid markdown breakage. In LaTeX source, use `\vert V_b - V_s \vert`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer Manufacturing:** In the production of **Nylon-6,6**, the chemical composition confirms that **hexamethylenediamine** and **adipic acid** reacted in a 1:1 molar ratio. Any deviation affects crystallinity and mechanical strength.
- **Pharmaceutical Polymers:** Drug-delivery polymers (e.g., **PLGA — poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid)**) require precise compositional analysis to control drug release kinetics.
- **Failure Diagnosis:** Yellowing of PVC indicates **dehydrochlorination**; chemical composition analysis reveals loss of Cl and formation of polyene sequences.
- **Environmental Analysis:** Determining **%Cl in PVC** ensures compliance with regulations on chlorine content in packaging.
- **Forensic Polymer Identification:** Distinguishes original vs. counterfeit polymer products based on elemental fingerprints.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Percentage Composition from Combustion Data

**Problem Setup:** A polymer sample of mass $m_s = 0.2500$ g on combustion produces:
- $m_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.5500$ g (absorbed in Ascarite tube)
- $m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0.2250$ g (absorbed in Anhydrone tube)
- $m_{\text{SO}_2} = 0.1600$ g (absorbed in silver vanadate tube)

**Step 1 — Calculate mass of Carbon:**
The stoichiometry $\text{C} \to \text{CO}_2$ shows 1 mole of C produces 1 mole of $\text{CO}_2$.
Molar masses: $A_C = 12.01$ g/mol, $M_{\text{CO}_2} = 44.01$ g/mol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_C &= m_{\text{CO}_2} \times \frac{A_C}{M_{\text{CO}_2}} \\
m_C &= 0.5500 \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} \\
m_C &= 0.5500 \times 0.27289 \\
m_C &= 0.15009 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate mass of Hydrogen:**
The stoichiometry $2\text{H}_2 \to 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ implies each $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ has 2 H atoms.
Molar masses: $A_H = 1.008$ g/mol, $M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 18.015$ g/mol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_H &= m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \times \frac{2 \times A_H}{M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} \\
m_H &= 0.2250 \times \frac{2 \times 1.008}{18.015} \\
m_H &= 0.2250 \times 0.11189 \\
m_H &= 0.02518 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate mass of Sulfur:**
Stoichiometry: $\text{S} \to \text{SO}_2$. Molar masses: $A_S = 32.06$ g/mol, $M_{\text{SO}_2} = 64.06$ g/mol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_S &= m_{\text{SO}_2} \times \frac{A_S}{M_{\text{SO}_2}} \\
m_S &= 0.1600 \times \frac{32.06}{64.06} \\
m_S &= 0.1600 \times 0.50047 \\
m_S &= 0.08008 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total accounted mass check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{total} &= m_C + m_H + m_S \\
m_{total} &= 0.15009 + 0.02518 + 0.08008 \\
m_{total} &= 0.25535 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

This exceeds $m_s = 0.2500$ g, indicating an experimental error. The oxygen content (by difference) would be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_O &= m_s - (m_C + m_H + m_S) \\
m_O &= 0.2500 - 0.25535 = -0.00535 \text{ g (negative — experimental error)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's note:** A negative oxygen value flags either weighing errors, incomplete combustion, or moisture contamination. In KTU valuation, students should always *check for this* and report it.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Empirical Formula from Mass Percentages

Given: $\%C = 60.00\%$, $\%H = 13.33\%$, $\%N = 10.67\%$, $\%O = 16.00\%$ (totals 100%).

**Step 1 — Convert percentages to moles** (assume 100 g sample):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_C &= \frac{60.00}{12.01} = 4.996 \text{ mol} \\
n_H &= \frac{13.33}{1.008} = 13.225 \text{ mol} \\
n_N &= \frac{10.67}{14.01} = 0.7616 \text{ mol} \\
n_O &= \frac{16.00}{16.00} = 1.0000 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by smallest** ($n_N = 0.7616$ is smallest):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &: \frac{4.996}{0.7616} = 6.560 \\
H &: \frac{13.225}{0.7616} = 17.367 \\
N &: \frac{0.7616}{0.7616} = 1.000 \\
O &: \frac{1.0000}{0.7616} = 1.313
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by 3 to clear fractions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &: 6.560 \times 3 \approx 19.68 \approx 20 \\
H &: 17.367 \times 3 \approx 52.10 \approx 52 \\
N &: 1.000 \times 3 = 3 \\
O &: 1.313 \times 3 \approx 3.94 \approx 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Empirical Formula:** $\text{C}_{20}\text{H}_{52}\text{N}_3\text{O}_4$ — this matches a hypothetical **poly(urethane) trimer segment**. 

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Kjeldahl Nitrogen Estimation

**Principle:** Polymer-bound nitrogen is converted to $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$ via digestion with conc. $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ + catalyst ($\text{CuSO}_4$ + $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$). Ammonia is liberated with NaOH, distilled into a known excess of standard HCl, and back-titrated with NaOH.

**Step 1 — Digestion reaction:**

$$
\text{Polymer-N} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\text{cat.}} (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4
$$

**Step 2 — Ammonia liberation:**

$$
(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NaOH} \to \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NH}_3 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 3 — Absorption in HCl:**

$$
\text{NH}_3 + \text{HCl} \to \text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \text{ (excess HCl remains)}
$$

**Step 4 — Back-titration:**

$$
\text{HCl}_{excess} + \text{NaOH} \to \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 5 — Nitrogen mass calculation:**

Given: $m = 1.500$ g polymer, $V_{HCl} = 50.00$ mL of 0.1000 N, $V_{NaOH} = 22.50$ mL of 0.1000 N.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{HCl,reacted} &= V_{HCl} - V_{NaOH} = 50.00 - 22.50 = 27.50 \text{ mL} \\
n_{HCl,reacted} &= 27.50 \times 0.1000 = 2.750 \text{ meq} \\
n_{NH_3} &= 2.750 \text{ mmol} \\
m_N &= 2.750 \times 14.01 \times 10^{-3} = 0.03853 \text{ g} \\
\%N &= \frac{0.03853}{1.500} \times 100 = 2.569\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Degree of Polymerization from Composition

For a polymer with repeat unit $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{N}_z\text{O}_w$, the **molecular weight of the repeat unit** is:

$$
M_0 = (x \times 12.01) + (y \times 1.008) + (z \times 14.01) + (w \times 16.00)
$$

If number-average molecular weight $M_n$ is determined by osmometry or GPC:

$$
DP_n = \frac{M_n}{M_0}
$$

**Example:** Nylon-6 ($\epsilon$-caprolactam polymer) repeat unit = $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{11}\text{NO}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_0 &= (6 \times 12.01) + (11 \times 1.008) + (1 \times 14.01) + (1 \times 16.00) \\
M_0 &= 72.06 + 11.088 + 14.01 + 16.00 \\
M_0 &= 113.158 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $M_n = 25{,}000$ g/mol:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DP_n &= \frac{25{,}000}{113.158} \\
DP_n &= 220.9 \approx 221
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| Technique | Element / Group Detected | Working Principle | Typical Detection Limit | KTU Application |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Liebig's Method | C, H | Combustion + absorption | 0.1% w/w | Classical CHN |
| Dumas Method | N, C, H, S | Combustion + GC-TCD | 0.05% w/w | Modern CHNS |
| Kjeldahl Method | N only | Wet digestion + titration | 0.01% w/w | Nylon, polyamides |
| Beilstein Test | Halogens (X) | Cu wire flame test | Qualitative | PVC, PVDC |
| FTIR Spectroscopy | Functional groups | IR absorption | $10^{-6}$ mol/L | Polymer ID |
| ${}^1$H NMR | H environments | Magnetic resonance | $10^{-4}$ mol/L | Tacticity, copolymer ratio |
| CHN Analyzer | C, H, N | Combustion + GC | 0.01% w/w | Quality control |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Classical CHN Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sampleA[Sample Introduction Zone] --> combustA[Combustion Furnace 900-1000 C]
    combustA --> oxideA[Oxidation Catalyst CuO / Pt]
    oxideA --> reducA[Reduction Furnace 650 C Copper]
    reducA --> trapA[Water Trap Mg ClO4 2]
    trapA --> co2A[CO2 Trap Ascarite]
    co2A --> ndetA[N2 Detector GC TCD]
    ndetA --> dataproc[Data Processing and Calculation]
    dataproc --> resultA[Report C H N Percentages]
    trapA -. retention .-> co2A
    dataproc --> empform[Empirical Formula Derivation]
    empform --> resultA
    sampleA -. known mass .-> dataproc
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Kjeldahl Nitrogen Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    polyA[Polymer Sample 1-2 g] --> digestA[Digestion with H2SO4 and Catalyst 350 C]
    digestA --> ammsulA[Formation of NH4 2 SO4]
    ammsulA --> alkaddA[Addition of Excess NaOH]
    alkaddA --> distillA[Steam Distillation Liberates NH3]
    distillA --> absorbA[Adsorption in Standard HCl]
    absorbA --> backtitrA[Back Titration with NaOH]
    backtitrA --> calcA[Calculate percent N via normality equation]
    calcA --> resultB[Report percent Nitrogen]
    polyA -. blank run .-> backtitrA
```

## 4.3 Functional Group Analysis Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Polymer Sample] --> carbonA{Carbon present?}
    carbonA -->|Yes| cquantA[Quantify C via CO2 absorption]
    carbonA -->|No| silA[Silicone Polymer]
    cquantA --> hydroA{Hydrogen present?}
    hydroA -->|Yes| hquantA[Quantify H via H2O absorption]
    hydroA -->|No| cfullA[Perfluorinated polymer]
    hquantA --> nitroA{Nitrogen present?}
    nitroA -->|Yes| nquantA[Kjeldahl or Dumas]
    nitroA -->|No| oxychkA[Check for O by difference]
    nquantA --> sulfA{Sulfur present?}
    sulfA -->|Yes| squantA[Quantify S via BaSO4 or SO2]
    sulfA -->|No| finalA[Report CHN composition]
    squantA --> finalA
    oxychkA --> finalA
```

## 4.4 Spectroscopic Cross-Validation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    compResult[Elemental Composition Result] --> ftirA[FTIR Analysis]
    compResult --> nmrA[NMR Analysis 1H and 13C]
    compResult --> titrA[Functional Group Titration]
    ftirA --> crossA[Cross Validate Functional Groups]
    nmrA --> crossA
    titrA --> crossA
    crossA --> matchA{Match with theory?}
    matchA -->|Yes| confA[Composition Confirmed]
    matchA -->|No| reinvA[Reinvestigate Synthesis]
```

## 4.5 Sample-to-Report Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sampB[Sample Mass m s] --> burnB[Combustion]
    burnB --> prodsB[Products CO2 H2O N2 SO2]
    prodsB --> absB[Absorbent Tubes]
    absB --> deltam[Differential Mass Measurement]
    deltam --> pctB[Mass Percentage Calculation]
    pctB --> moleB[Mole Ratio Conversion]
    moleB --> empfB[Empirical Formula]
    empfB --> finalB[Final Report to KTU Record]
    sampB -. control blank .-> absB
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> 
> **Q1.** Define the term "chemical composition" in the context of polymer analysis. List the elements commonly determined in the CHN analysis of polymers.
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Chemical composition in polymer analysis refers to the qualitative identification and quantitative determination of constituent elements (typically C, H, N, S, O, and halogens) and functional groups present in a polymer sample. It establishes the empirical/molecular formula, monomer ratios in copolymers, and confirms the polymer's structural identity. The elements commonly determined in **CHN analysis** are **Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H), and Nitrogen (N)**. Extended CHNS analysis also includes **Sulfur (S)**. Oxygen is typically estimated by difference, while halogens require specialized methods (e.g., Beilstein test, oxygen-flask combustion).

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> 
> **Q2.** State the principle of the Kjeldahl method for nitrogen estimation. Why is a catalyst mixture of $\text{CuSO}_4$ and $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$ used?
> 
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Kjeldahl method** is based on the wet digestion of a nitrogen-containing sample with concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, converting organically bound nitrogen to ammonium sulfate. The ammonia is liberated by adding excess NaOH, distilled into a known volume of standard HCl, and the unreacted HCl is back-titrated with standard NaOH. The percentage of nitrogen is calculated from the volume of acid neutralized. The catalyst mixture of **$\text{CuSO}_4$** (acts as an oxidation catalyst to speed up digestion) and **$\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4$** (raises the boiling point of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ from $\sim 338\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $\sim 400\,^\circ\text{C}$, ensuring complete digestion) is used to ensure complete and rapid conversion of organic nitrogen to ammonium sulfate.

## 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]`

**Q.A(a).** A polymer sample of mass 0.4520 g on combustion yields 0.9020 g of $\text{CO}_2$ and 0.3690 g of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$. In a separate Kjeldahl experiment, 0.5000 g of the same polymer requires 35.50 mL of 0.0500 N $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ to neutralize the liberated ammonia. Calculate the percentage composition of C, H, and N, and derive the empirical formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate %C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_C &= m_{\text{CO}_2} \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} = 0.9020 \times 0.27289 = 0.24615 \text{ g} \\
\%C &= \frac{0.24615}{0.4520} \times 100 = 54.46\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate %H:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_H &= m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \times \frac{2 \times 1.008}{18.015} = 0.3690 \times 0.11189 = 0.04129 \text{ g} \\
\%H &= \frac{0.04129}{0.4520} \times 100 = 9.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate %N using Kjeldahl:**

For Kjeldahl, $1$ mL of 1 N $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \equiv 14.01$ mg of N (i.e., 0.01401 g N).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_N &= V \times N \times 0.01401 \\
m_N &= 35.50 \times 0.0500 \times 0.01401 \\
m_N &= 0.02487 \text{ g} \\
\%N &= \frac{0.02487}{0.5000} \times 100 = 4.97\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Empirical formula derivation:**

Assume 100 g sample → masses become percentages directly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_C &= \frac{54.46}{12.01} = 4.5347 \text{ mol} \\
n_H &= \frac{9.14}{1.008} = 9.0675 \text{ mol} \\
n_N &= \frac{4.97}{14.01} = 0.3548 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Divide by smallest (0.3548):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &: \frac{4.5347}{0.3548} = 12.78 \approx 13 \\
H &: \frac{9.0675}{0.3548} = 25.55 \approx 26 \\
N &: \frac{0.3548}{0.3548} = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Empirical Formula: $\text{C}_{13}\text{H}_{26}\text{N}$** — This corresponds to a segment of poly(lauryl lactam) / Nylon-12 type polymer.

**Q.A(b).** Describe the procedure for the determination of sulfur in a polymer sample by the **Eschka method**. What is the role of **$\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ + $\text{MgO}$** in this method? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** In the Eschka method, the polymer is fused with a mixture of **Eschka's mixture (2 parts $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ + 1 part MgO)** in a crucible. The organic sulfur is converted to soluble **$\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$** and **$\text{MgSO}_4$**. The fused mass is leached with hot water, acidified with HCl, and sulfate is precipitated as **$\text{BaSO}_4$** by adding $\text{BaCl}_2$. The mass of $\text{BaSO}_4$ precipitate is determined gravimetrically, and sulfur content is calculated.

**Procedure:**

1. Weigh accurately $1.0$–$2.0$ g of the polymer sample into a porcelain crucible.
2. Mix with $3$ g of Eschka's mixture and cover with another $2 g of the mixture.
3. Ignite gently, then heat strongly at $800\,^\circ\text{C}$ for $2$ hours in a muffle furnace.
4. Cool, transfer the fused mass to a beaker, and digest with hot water.
5. Filter, wash the residue, and acidify the filtrate with dilute HCl (methyl red indicator).
6. Boil and add hot $10\%$ $\text{BaCl}_2$ solution slowly with constant stirring.
7. Digest the precipitate for $1$ hour on a steam bath, filter through a sintered crucible or ashless filter paper.
8. Wash, dry, ignite at $800\,^\circ\text{C}$, and weigh as $\text{BaSO}_4$.

**Calculation:**

$$
\%S = \frac{m_{\text{BaSO}_4} \times 0.1374}{m_{sample}} \times 100
$$

where $0.1374 = \frac{A_S}{M_{\text{BaSO}_4}} = \frac{32.06}{233.39}$.

**Role of Eschka's mixture ($\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ + MgO):**
- **$\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$** acts as a **flux** to decompose the polymer and provide an alkaline environment for sulfur oxidation to sulfate.
- **MgO** prevents fusion of the mixture to the crucible (keeps mass porous), aids in even combustion, and helps retain sulfur oxides as $\text{MgSO}_4$ instead of escaping as $\text{SO}_2$ gas.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme — Alternative]`

**Q.B(a).** A polymer is suspected to be **poly(vinyl chloride), PVC, $(\text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{Cl})_n$**. From the theoretical formula, calculate the expected percentages of C, H, and Cl. If in an actual analysis a 0.3500 g sample gave 0.5025 g of $\text{CO}_2$, 0.1030 g of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, and 0.5280 g of AgCl (from halogen analysis), determine whether the sample is pure PVC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical % composition of PVC ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_3\text{Cl}$):**
Molar mass $= 2(12.01) + 3(1.008) + 35.45 = 62.494$ g/mol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%C_{theory} &= \frac{2 \times 12.01}{62.494} \times 100 = 38.44\% \\
\%H_{theory} &= \frac{3 \times 1.008}{62.494} \times 100 = 4.84\% \\
\%Cl_{theory} &= \frac{35.45}{62.494} \times 100 = 56.72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Experimental %C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_C &= 0.5025 \times \frac{12.01}{44.01} = 0.13715 \text{ g} \\
\%C_{exp} &= \frac{0.13715}{0.3500} \times 100 = 39.19\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Experimental %H:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_H &= 0.1030 \times \frac{2.016}{18.015} = 0.01153 \text{ g} \\
\%H_{exp} &= \frac{0.01153}{0.3500} \times 100 = 3.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Experimental %Cl from AgCl:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{Cl} &= m_{AgCl} \times \frac{A_{Cl}}{M_{AgCl}} = 0.5280 \times \frac{35.45}{143.32} \\
m_{Cl} &= 0.5280 \times 0.2473 = 0.13057 \text{ g} \\
\%Cl_{exp} &= \frac{0.13057}{0.3500} \times 100 = 37.31\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**

| Element | Theoretical (%) | Experimental (%) | Deviation |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|------------|
| C | 38.44 | 39.19 | +0.75 |
| H | 4.84 | 3.29 | -1.55 |
| Cl | 56.72 | 37.31 | **-19.41** |

> **Conclusion:** The chlorine content is far below theoretical. The sample is **NOT pure PVC**. The deviation in Cl suggests either (a) significant dehydrochlorination (loss of HCl → polyene formation), (b) presence of an additive (plasticizer/filler), or (c) sample contamination. The lowered H and elevated C also support dehydrochlorination.

**Q.B(b).** Explain the **Liebig's method** for the estimation of carbon and hydrogen. With a neat schematic, describe the absorption tubes used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** The organic/polymer sample is combusted in a stream of pure oxygen. Carbon is converted to $\text{CO}_2$ and absorbed in a weighed **potash / Ascarite tube** (containing NaOH-coated asbestos). Hydrogen is converted to $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ and absorbed in a weighed **Anhydrone tube** [anhydrous $\text{Mg(ClO}_4)_2$] or $\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$ tube. The increase in mass of each tube gives the mass of $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, from which %C and %H are calculated.

**Apparatus Layout (in order of gas flow):**

1. **Oxygen supply** → **Purification train** (removes $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ from $\text{O}_2$)
2. **Combustion tube** (quartz) containing:
   - Sample boat (with polymer)
   - CuO wire (oxidation catalyst)
   - Silver wool (to retain halogens/sulfur)
3. **Water-absorption tube (Anhydrone / $\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$ tube)**
4. **$\text{CO}_2$-absorption tube (Ascarite / Soda-lime tube)**
5. **Guard tube** (prevents atmospheric $\text{H}_2\text{O}/\text{CO}_2$ from entering backward)
6. **Aspirator** to draw the gas

**Key Reactions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + (x + y/4)\text{O}_2 &\to x\,\text{CO}_2 + (y/2)\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{NaOH} &\to \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Mg(ClO}_4)_2 &\to \text{Mg(ClO}_4)_2 \cdot x\text{H}_2\text{O} \text{ (anhydrone)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculations:**

$$
\%C = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2} \times \frac{12.01}{44.01}}{m_{sample}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%H = \frac{m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \times \frac{2.016}{18.015}}{m_{sample}} \times 100
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Order of absorption tubes is critical.** Water tube **must come first** (before $\text{CO}_2$ tube). Reversed order causes $\text{CO}_2$ to dissolve in water and inflate the carbon reading.
> 2. **Atomic mass of H is 1.008, not 1.00.** Using rounded atomic masses incurs a 0.8% error — examiners *will* deduct ½ mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor $12.01/44.01$** in %C calculation is the #1 mistake; remember it is the **mass fraction of C in $\text{CO}_2$**.
> 4. **Sulfur and halogens interfere.** They are removed by silver wool/lead chromate; failing to do so gives falsely high C/H values.
> 5. **Kjeldahl N calculation:** Use $14.01$ mg/mL per 1 N acid; do not confuse with 14 g.
> 6. **Empty oxygen correction:** Run a blank to subtract any $\text{CO}_2/\text{H}_2\text{O}$ already present in the apparatus.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Chemical Composition Analysis (PCPOL607)**

- ✅ **Chemical composition** = qualitative + quantitative identification of elements/functional groups in polymers.
- ✅ **CHN analysis** uses high-temperature combustion ($900\text{–}1000\,^\circ\text{C}$) in pure $\text{O}_2$ with CuO catalyst and Cu reducer.
- ✅ **C detected as $\text{CO}_2$** in Ascarite; **H detected as $\text{H}_2\text{O}$** in Anhydrone ($\text{Mg(ClO}_4)_2$).
- ✅ **%C formula:** $\%C = \dfrac{m_{\text{CO}_2} \times 12.01 / 44.01}{m_{sample}} \times 100$
- ✅ **%H formula:** $\%H = \dfrac{m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \times 2.016 / 18.015}{m_{sample}} \times 100$
- ✅ **Kjeldahl method** = digestion → distillation → back-titration. $1$ mL of 1 N acid $\equiv 14.01$ mg N.
- ✅ **Dumas method** = combustion + GC-TCD detection of $\text{N}_2$.
- ✅ **Eschka method** = $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$ + MgO fusion → $\text{BaSO}_4$ gravimetry for sulfur.
- ✅ **Beilstein test** = qualitative halogen detection using Cu wire in flame (green flame = halogen).
- ✅ **Empirical formula derivation** = % → moles → divide by smallest → round to integers.
- ✅ **DP (degree of polymerization)** $= M_n / M_0$ where $M_0$ = repeat unit mass.
- ✅ **FTIR** confirms functional groups (qualitative), **NMR** gives tacticity/copolymer ratio, **CHN** gives elemental composition.
- ✅ **Sample must be dry, finely ground, and homogeneous** for accurate analysis.
- ✅ **Always run a blank** to correct for apparatus/systematic errors.
- ✅ **Sulfur and halogens interfere** with CHN analysis — remove with silver wool or lead chromate.
- ✅ **Oxygen** is usually estimated **by difference** unless determined directly (e.g., Unterzaucher method).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: properties and testing of viscose rayon
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** properties and testing of viscose rayon

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Viscose Rayon** is a **regenerated cellulosic fiber** produced through a chemical process in which natural cellulose (typically from wood pulp or cotton linters) is dissolved, chemically converted, and re-precipitated as continuous filaments. It is the oldest and most widely produced man-made fiber, classified under the generic name "rayon" by the **International Bureau for the Standardization of Man-Made Fibers (BISFA)** and the **US Federal Trade Commission (FTC)**.

The International Standard **ISO 2076** defines regenerated cellulose fibers as "fibers obtained by chemical regeneration of naturally occurring cellulose from wood or cotton." The **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus places viscose rayon as a foundational topic under the umbrella of **polymer characterization and fiber science**, emphasizing the link between molecular structure, morphology, and bulk physical properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module Experiment 2):**
> The experiment focuses on identifying viscose rayon by a battery of tests — **microscopic, burning, chemical solubility, and mechanical** — and characterizing its **physical, chemical, and mechanical properties** that govern its end-use in textiles, hygiene products, and industrial applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **paper boat made of wood pulp** (the raw cellulose). The wood pulp is dissolved into a thick, honey-like syrup using chemicals, then forced through tiny holes into an acid bath, where it solidifies back into long, smooth, silky threads — just as how a caterpillar dissolves its old body inside a cocoon and re-emerges as a butterfly. The "molecular memory" of cellulose is preserved, but its physical form is completely transformed.

In simpler words:
- **Raw material** (wood/cotton cellulose) → looks rough, short fibers
- **Process** (chemical dissolution + regeneration) → transforms into smooth, continuous filament
- **Final product** (viscose rayon) → soft, absorbent, lustrous, drapes like silk

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Viscose rayon is *not* a synthetic fiber (like polyester or nylon). It is a **regenerated natural polymer** because the original cellulose polymer is recovered and re-spun. This subtle distinction is a frequent Part-A question in KTU exams.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics for Viscose Rayon

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Moisture Regain (SMR) | **11.0 – 13.0** (Standard = **13.0**) | % |
| Specific Gravity / Density | **1.50 – 1.54** | g/cm³ |
| Tenacity (Dry) | **2.0 – 2.6** | g/denier (gpd) |
| Tenacity (Wet) | **1.0 – 1.5** (loses ~50% strength when wet) | g/denier (gpd) |
| Elongation at Break (Dry) | **15 – 30** | % |
| Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) | **~230 (dry), ~30 (wet)** | °C |
| Degree of Polymerization (DP) | **250 – 350** | unitless |
| Standard Linear Density Range | **1.5 – 15.0** | denier |
| Refractive Index (parallel / perpendicular) | **1.529 / 1.519** | — |
| Standard Test Atmosphere | **20 ± 2 °C, 65 ± 2 % RH** | ISO 139 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Wet Strength Loss** (about **40–50%** of dry tenacity) is the single most important property of viscose rayon. KTU examiners frequently ask why viscose fabrics require gentle washing — the answer lies in the disruption of inter-chain hydrogen bonds by water.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Viscose Rayon

To understand properties, we must analyze the **four structural levels**:

### 2.1.1 Molecular Level (Chemical)
- **Polymer:** Cellulose — a linear polysaccharide of β-1,4-linked D-glucose units
- **Repeat unit:** $C_6H_{10}O_5$ with **three free –OH groups** per glucose unit
- **Molecular formula:** $(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n$ where $n$ = degree of polymerization
- These hydroxyls form **extensive intra- and inter-molecular hydrogen bonding**, governing crystallinity and water absorption

### 2.1.2 Supramolecular Level (Morphology)
- **Crystalline regions** (~40 – 60 % crystallinity) + **amorphous regions**
- Crystallites stabilized by **H-bonds** between parallel cellulose chains
- The **regeneration step** (Xanthate → Cellulose II) modifies the crystal lattice from native **Cellulose I** to **Cellulose II**, making the fiber more absorbent and less strong than native cotton

### 2.1.3 Fibrillar Level (Fine Structure)
- Composed of **microfibrils** and **macrofibrils** arranged along the fiber axis
- The **skin-core structure**: outer skin is denser, more crystalline; inner core is more porous and amorphous

### 2.1.4 Macro Level (Fiber Geometry)
- Continuous filament of uniform diameter
- Cross-section: **irregular, serrated, multi-lobed** (distinctive "kidney-bean" or "C-shaped" appearance under microscope) — this is the **key microscopic identification clue**

## 2.2 Classification of Properties

### 2.2.1 Physical Properties
1. **Moisture Regain (MR):** Very high (~11–13%) → makes viscose **comfortable in humid climates** and **hygienic for undergarments**
2. **Density:** 1.50–1.54 g/cm³ (heavier than cotton at 1.54–1.56 g/cm³; lighter than polyester at 1.38 g/cm³)
3. **Luster:** Naturally bright; dulling agents (TiO₂) added for matte finish
4. **Thermal behavior:** Decomposes at ~260–300 °C; chars before melting (no clean melting point → can be identified by burning test)
5. **Refractive index:** 1.529 ∥ / 1.519 ⊥ → moderately birefringent

### 2.2.2 Chemical Properties
1. **Action of acids:** Highly sensitive to **mineral acids** (HCl, H₂SO₄) → rapid hydrolysis of glycosidic bonds → fiber dissolves
2. **Action of alkalis:** Resistant to **dilute cold alkalis**; mercerization effect not pronounced
3. **Action of oxidizing agents:** Bleachable with H₂O₂, NaOCl; bleached viscose is "bright white"
4. **Action of organic solvents:** Insoluble in acetone, ethanol, benzene, ether (distinguishes from acetate and synthetic fibers)
5. **Action of water:** Swells, loses strength (wet strength loss)
6. **Action of heat:** Yellows at 150 °C; decomposes at 260–300 °C
7. **Biological:** Susceptible to mildew and rot if stored damp

### 2.2.3 Mechanical Properties
1. **Tenacity:** Dry = 2.0–2.6 gpd; Wet = 1.0–1.5 gpd (drops by ~50%)
2. **Elongation:** 15–30 % (dry), 20–35 % (wet)
3. **Initial modulus:** Lower than cotton → fiber deforms easily → fabrics drape well
4. **Elastic recovery:** Poor (~60–80% at 2% extension) → poor wrinkle recovery
5. **Loop/knot tenacity:** Lower than flat tenacity due to stress concentration

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Concept | Formula / Expression | Standard | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Moisture Regain (MR)** | $MR\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | ISO 6741 | % |
| **Moisture Content (MC)** | $MC\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | — | % |
| **Linear Density (Denier)** | $D = \dfrac{9000 \times W_{kg}}{L_m}$ | ISO 1973 | denier |
| **Tex** | $T = \dfrac{1000 \times W_{g}}{L_m}$ | ISO 1144 | tex |
| **Tenacity** | $T_n = \dfrac{F_{gf}}{D_{denier}}$ gpd $= \dfrac{F_N}{T_{tex}} \times 10$ | ASTM D3822 | gpd, mN/tex |
| **Elongation %** | $\varepsilon\% = \dfrac{L_b - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | — | % |
| **Specific Stress (σ_sp)** | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{F/A}{\rho}$ | — | N·m/kg |
| **Wet Strength Loss %** | $WSL\% = \dfrac{T_{dry} - T_{wet}}{T_{dry}} \times 100$ | — | % |
| **Birefringence (Δn)** | $\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$ | — | unitless |
| **Degree of Polymerization** | $DP = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$, $M_0 = 162$ for cellulose | ASTM D1795 | unitless |
| **Crystallinity Index (X-ray)** | $CrI\% = \dfrac{I_{002} - I_{am}}{I_{002}} \times 100$ | Segal method | % |
| **Conditioned Weight (W_c)** | $W_c = W_d \times \left(1 + \dfrac{MR_{std}}{100}\right)$ | ISO 6741 | g |
| **Conversion: Tex → Denier** | $D = T \times 0.9$ (approx) | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the formula for **wet strength loss** and the **MR vs MC** distinction. KTU examiners love setting numerical problems based on MR and tenacity conversion (denier ↔ tex).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Textile industry:** Apparel, linings, sarees, dress materials (drape, luster, absorbency)
- **Hygiene & medical:** Surgical dressings, absorbent pads, wipes (high MR, biocompatibility)
- **Industrial:** Tire cord (high-tenacity rayon variant, HWM rayon), conveyor belts, sausage casings
- **Composite materials:** Regenerated cellulose films (cellophane) and fibers used in bio-composites as a sustainable alternative to glass fiber

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The property that **distinguishes viscose from cotton** is its **irregular serrated cross-section** and **lower wet strength**. The property that **distinguishes viscose from acetate** is its **insolubility in acetone**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Moisture Regain (MR) and Moisture Content (MC)

Let:
- $W_w$ = weight of the fiber in equilibrium with the standard atmosphere (wet / conditioned weight)
- $W_d$ = oven-dry weight of the fiber (after drying at 105 ± 3 °C until constant mass)

**Step 1 — Define Moisture Regain (MR)**

By definition, MR is the water expressed as a percentage of the **dry weight**.

$$
MR\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Define Moisture Content (MC)**

By definition, MC is the water expressed as a percentage of the **wet (conditioned) weight**.

$$
MC\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Express the relationship**

Let $\Delta W = W_w - W_d$ (mass of water). Then:

$$
MR = \dfrac{\Delta W}{W_d} \times 100, \quad MC = \dfrac{\Delta W}{W_w} \times 100
$$

Divide the two:

$$
\dfrac{MR}{MC} = \dfrac{W_w}{W_d}
$$

But since $W_w = W_d + \Delta W$:

$$
\dfrac{MR}{MC} = \dfrac{W_d + \Delta W}{W_d} = 1 + \dfrac{\Delta W}{W_d} = 1 + \dfrac{MR}{100}
$$

Solving for MC:

$$
MC\% = \dfrac{MR\%}{100 + MR\%} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** If viscose rayon has MR = 13 %, then MC = 13 / 113 × 100 = **11.50 %**. Always remember that **MC < MR** for the same sample, because the denominator is larger.

## 3.2 Derivation: Wet Strength Loss Percentage (WSL %)

**Given:**
- Dry tenacity of viscose rayon, $T_{dry}$ = 2.4 gpd
- Wet tenacity, $T_{wet}$ = 1.2 gpd

**Step 1 — Define WSL**

The loss in tenacity on wetting, as a percentage of the dry tenacity:

$$
WSL\% = \dfrac{T_{dry} - T_{wet}}{T_{dry}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**

$$
WSL\% = \dfrac{2.4 - 1.2}{2.4} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify**

$$
WSL\% = \dfrac{1.2}{2.4} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Viscose rayon loses about **50% of its dry strength** when wet. This is a **defining weakness** and a frequently asked Part-B question.

## 3.3 Derivation: Conversion Between Tex and Denier

**Given:**
- A viscose filament of **1 tex** (1 g per 1000 m)

**Step 1 — Recall definitions**

- 1 denier = weight in grams of **9000 m** of the filament
- 1 tex = weight in grams of **1000 m** of the filament

**Step 2 — Calculate the weight of 9000 m of the 1-tex filament**

If 1000 m weighs 1 g, then 9000 m weighs:

$$
W_{9000} = 1\,\text{g} \times \dfrac{9000}{1000} = 9\,\text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to denier**

By definition, this is **9 denier**.

$$
1\,\text{tex} = 9\,\text{denier}
$$

Or in general:

$$
D = 9 \times T \quad \text{or} \quad T = \dfrac{D}{9}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A viscose yarn of 75 denier = 75/9 = **8.33 tex**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Tenacity Conversion (gpd ↔ N/tex)

**Given:**
- Tenacity $T_n$ in gpd
- Linear density $D$ in denier

**Step 1 — Convert grams-force to Newtons**

$$
1\,\text{gf} = 9.806 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert denier to tex**

$$
D\,(\text{denier}) = 9 \times T\,(\text{tex})
$$

**Step 3 — Specific stress (tenacity in N/tex)**

$$
\sigma_{sp}\,(\text{N/tex}) = \dfrac{F\,(\text{N})}{T\,(\text{tex})}
$$

Starting from gpd:

$$
\sigma_{sp}\,(\text{N/tex}) = \dfrac{T_n\,(\text{gpd}) \times D\,(\text{denier}) \times 9.806 \times 10^{-3}}{D\,(\text{denier}) / 9}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\sigma_{sp}\,(\text{N/tex}) = 9 \times T_n\,(\text{gpd}) \times 9.806 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0883 \times T_n\,(\text{gpd})
$$

**Step 4 — Result (the constant rule)**

$$
1\,\text{gpd} = 0.0883\,\text{N/tex} = 88.3\,\text{mN/tex}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** Viscose rayon tenacity = 2.4 gpd = 2.4 × 88.3 = **212 mN/tex** (≈ 0.21 N/tex).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Testing (Viscose vs Other Fibers)

| Test | Viscose Rayon | Cotton | Acetate | Polyester | Nylon |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Microscopic cross-section** | Serrated, irregular | Convoluted "kidney" | Crenulated, lobed | Circular, smooth | Circular, smooth |
| **Longitudinal view** | Striations present | Twisted ribbon-like | Striations | Smooth, rod-like | Smooth, rod-like |
| **Burning test** | Burns fast, paper-like smell, no bead | Burns fast, paper-like smell, no bead | Melts, drips, vinegar smell | Melts, drips, sweet smell | Melts, curls, celery-like smell |
| **Acetone solubility** | Insoluble | Insoluble | **Soluble** | Insoluble | Insoluble |
| **Cuoxam (Schweizer's reagent)** | Soluble | **Soluble** | Insoluble | Insoluble | Insoluble |
| **Acid (70% H₂SO₄)** | Dissolves | Dissolves | Dissolves | Insoluble | Insoluble |
| **Moisture Regain** | 11 – 13 % | 7 – 8 % | 6 – 7 % | 0.4 % | 4.0 – 4.5 % |
| **Wet strength loss** | ~50 % | ~10 % | Slight | None | ~10 – 15 % |
| **Action of NaOH (5%)** | Swells, becomes stronger | Swells (mercerizes) | Saponifies | Resistant | Resistant |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Cuoxam test** (cuprammonium hydroxide) is the **diagnostic test** for regenerated cellulose: viscose and cotton both dissolve, but viscose does so much faster due to its **lower degree of polymerization (250–350 vs ~3000–5000 for cotton)**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Viscose Process (Regeneration Pathway)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Wood Pulp OR Cotton Linters"] --> B["Steeping: NaOH 18% Solution"]
    B --> C["Pressing and Shredding to Alkali Cellulose Crumbs"]
    C --> D["Ageing: Oxidative Depolymerization to Control DP"]
    D --> E["Xanthation: CS2 Treatment Forms Cellulose Xanthate"]
    E --> F["Dissolution in Dilute NaOH: Viscose Dope"]
    F --> G["Filtration and Deaeration"]
    G --> H["Spinning: Extrusion into H2SO4 Na2SO4 ZnSO4 Bath"]
    H --> I["Regenerated Cellulose Filament: Viscose Rayon"]
    I --> J["Drawing, Washing, Cutting, Drying"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Viscose Rayon Property — Test Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Physical Properties"] --> T1["Moisture Regain Test: ISO 6741"]
    P1 --> T2["Linear Density: ISO 1973 Denier Method"]
    P1 --> T3["Microscopic Examination: Longitudinal and Cross Section"]

    P2["Mechanical Properties"] --> T4["Tensile Test: ASTM D3822 Single Fiber"]
    P4["Chemical Properties"] --> T5["Burning Test: Visual and Olfactory"]
    P4 --> T6["Solubility Tests: Acetone Cuoxam 70% H2SO4 NaOH"]
    P4 --> T7["Staining Test: Shirlastain A and C"]

    P1 --> P5["Drapability Luster Density Hand Feel"]
    P2 --> P6["Tenacity Elongation Initial Modulus Elastic Recovery"]
    P4 --> P8["Acid Sensitivity Alkali Resistance Dye Affinity"]

    T4 --> P6
    T1 --> P5
    T6 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Diagnostic Decision Tree for Fiber Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Unknown Fiber"] --> B["Burning Test"]
    B -->|Burns fast, no bead, paper smell| C["Cellulosic Group"]
    B -->|Melts, beads, chemical smell| D["Synthetic Group"]

    C --> E["Microscopic Examination"]
    E -->|Serrated cross section, striations| F["SUSPECT Viscose Rayon"]
    E -->|Convoluted kidney shape, twists| G["Cotton"]
    E -->|Round smooth surface| H["Linen OR Hemp"]

    F --> I["Solubility Tests"]
    I -->|Insoluble in acetone| J["Confirms NOT acetate"]
    I -->|Soluble in Cuoxam| K["Confirms regenerated cellulose"]
    I -->|Dissolves in 70% H2SO4| L["Confirms cellulosic"]

    K --> M["Final Result: Viscose Rayon Identified"]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Structure (Property → Structure Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Molecular Level Cellulose II Polymer"] --> B["Supramolecular Level 40-60% Crystallinity"]
    B --> C["Fibrillar Level Skin Core Morphology"]
    C --> D["Macro Level Serrated Cross Section"]

    A --> P1["High Moisture Regain due to OH groups"]
    B --> P2["Moderate Tenacity and Modulus"]
    C --> P3["Differential Dye Affinity Skin vs Core"]
    D --> P4["High Luster and Good Drape"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual cue for identification:** The serrated cross-section is a direct consequence of the **coagulation kinetics** during spinning. As the viscose dope enters the acid bath, the outer skin hardens first; subsequent dehydration and shrinkage of the core create the characteristic grooves — visible under a microscope at 100–400× magnification.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define viscose rayon. Why is it classified as a "regenerated" fiber and not a "synthetic" fiber?**

**Model Answer:**
Viscose rayon is a **regenerated cellulosic fiber** manufactured by chemically dissolving natural cellulose (from wood pulp or cotton linters) into a viscous solution called the "viscose dope," and then re-precipitating it as continuous filaments in an acid coagulation bath.

It is called **regenerated** because the **same cellulose polymer** that exists in nature is recovered and re-spun into a new fiber form — the molecular identity is preserved. In contrast, **synthetic fibers** (e.g., polyester, nylon) are built from small monomers via polymerization; the polymer itself does not pre-exist in nature.

Hence, viscose rayon belongs to the class of **man-made cellulosic fibers** as per **ISO 2076** and **BISFA** classification.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of "moisture regain" of viscose rayon (≈ 13%) in textile applications?**

**Model Answer:**
Moisture regain (MR) is the percentage of water a fiber absorbs under standard atmospheric conditions (20 °C, 65% RH) expressed on its oven-dry weight. Viscose rayon has a high MR of **11–13%** — much higher than cotton (7–8%) and far higher than polyester (0.4%).

**Significance:**
1. **Comfort:** High MR means the fiber absorbs perspiration readily → garments feel cool and dry
2. **Dyeability:** Hydrophilic nature allows good uptake of water-based reactive and direct dyes
3. **Static-free:** Water acts as a conductive layer, eliminating static build-up
4. **Hygiene:** Suitable for medical dressings, towels, undergarments
5. **Trade practice:** Fibers are sold on a **conditioned weight basis**; a 13% MR is the standard

> [!NOTE]
> The trade convention is to quote fiber weight at the **standard MR (13%)** and the yarn/linear density at the same condition.

---

## 5.2 Part-B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **microscopic, burning, and chemical tests** used to identify viscose rayon. How does viscose differ from cotton and acetate based on these tests?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A viscose rayon filament of 3.0 denier has a dry breaking load of 8.0 g. Calculate: (i) Dry tenacity in gpd, (ii) Tenacity in N/tex, and (iii) If its wet breaking load is 4.0 g, determine the **wet strength loss percentage**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Identification Tests**

**1. Microscopic Test**
- **Longitudinal view:** Long, smooth filaments with **fine longitudinal striations** parallel to the fiber axis
- **Cross-section:** Highly irregular, **serrated, multi-lobed** ("kidney-bean" or "C-shape")
- **Distinction:**
  - Cotton: Twisted, ribbon-like longitudinal, convoluted kidney-shaped cross-section
  - Acetate: Crenulated, lobed cross-section with striations (less irregular)
  - Viscose: Most irregular, deeply serrated

**2. Burning Test**
- **Approach:** Hold a small fiber tuft with tweezers near a flame
- **Viscose reaction:** Ignites quickly, burns steadily with a yellow flame, **paper-like or burning-leaves odor**, leaves **fluffy gray ash** (no bead)
- **Distinction:**
  - Cotton: Similar to viscose (also cellulosic), but burns slightly faster
  - Acetate: Curls away from flame, melts and drips, **acetic acid (vinegar) odor**

**3. Chemical Tests**
- **Acetone solubility test:** Viscose is **insoluble** in cold acetone; acetate **dissolves** → distinguishes from acetate
- **Cuoxam (Schweizer's reagent) test:** Viscose **dissolves rapidly** (within 5–10 min); cotton dissolves slowly
- **70% H₂SO₄ test:** Viscose **dissolves** (cellulosic)
- **Staining test (Shirlastain A):** Viscose stains **light pink to lavender**; cotton stains purple; acetate stains yellow-green

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- Linear density, $D = 3.0$ denier
- Dry breaking load, $F_{dry} = 8.0$ gf
- Wet breaking load, $F_{wet} = 4.0$ gf

**(i) Dry Tenacity in gpd**

$$
T_{dry} = \dfrac{F_{dry}}{D} = \dfrac{8.0\,\text{gf}}{3.0\,\text{denier}} = 2.667\,\text{gpd}
$$

**(ii) Tenacity in N/tex**

Using the conversion $1\,\text{gpd} = 0.0883\,\text{N/tex}$:

$$
T_{dry}\,(\text{N/tex}) = 2.667 \times 0.0883 = 0.2355\,\text{N/tex}
$$

Or equivalently, in mN/tex:

$$
T_{dry} = 235.5\,\text{mN/tex}
$$

**(iii) Wet Strength Loss %**

Wet tenacity:

$$
T_{wet} = \dfrac{F_{wet}}{D} = \dfrac{4.0}{3.0} = 1.333\,\text{gpd}
$$

Wet strength loss:

$$
WSL\% = \dfrac{T_{dry} - T_{wet}}{T_{dry}} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.667 - 1.333}{2.667} \times 100
$$

$$
WSL\% = \dfrac{1.334}{2.667} \times 100 = 50.02\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Summary:**
> - Dry tenacity = **2.67 gpd** (= 235.5 mN/tex)
> - Wet tenacity = **1.33 gpd**
> - Wet strength loss ≈ **50%** — characteristic of viscose rayon

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **physical, chemical, and mechanical properties** of viscose rayon. Discuss the molecular reason for its **high moisture regain** and **low wet strength**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A viscose rayon fabric sample weighs **245 g** at standard condition (20 °C, 65% RH) and **217 g** when oven-dry. Calculate: (i) Moisture regain (MR), (ii) Moisture content (MC), and (iii) The mass of water absorbed at **standard MR of 13%** for a 1 kg bale of the same rayon.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Properties of Viscose Rayon**

**Physical Properties:**
1. **Moisture Regain:** 11–13% (highest among common fibers, except wool at 14–18%)
2. **Density:** 1.50–1.54 g/cm³
3. **Luster:** High natural luster; can be delustered with TiO₂
4. **Refractive index:** 1.529 ∥ / 1.519 ⊥
5. **Thermal behavior:** Chars at 260–300 °C; no true melting point
6. **Cross-section:** Serrated, irregular

**Chemical Properties:**
1. **Action of acids:** Sensitive to mineral acids → hydrolyzes
2. **Action of alkalis:** Resistant to dilute cold NaOH
3. **Action of oxidizing agents:** Bleachable
4. **Action of organic solvents:** Insoluble in acetone, ether
5. **Action of water:** Swells; disrupts H-bonds
6. **Biological:** Mildew susceptible

**Mechanical Properties:**
1. **Dry tenacity:** 2.0–2.6 gpd
2. **Wet tenacity:** 1.0–1.5 gpd (loses ~50% strength)
3. **Elongation at break:** 15–30% (dry), 20–35% (wet)
4. **Initial modulus:** Low (drapes well)
5. **Elastic recovery:** Poor (wrinkles easily)

**Molecular Reasons:**

- **High MR:** Cellulose has **three hydroxyl groups per glucose unit** that form hydrogen bonds with water. The hydroxyl groups are accessible in the **amorphous regions** of the polymer, where water molecules penetrate and bind. Viscose rayon (Cellulose II) has a more open lattice than native cotton (Cellulose I), allowing easier water absorption.

- **Low wet strength:** The **inter-chain hydrogen bonds** in the crystalline regions are responsible for the dry strength. When water enters the fiber, it **disrupts these H-bonds** by forming H-bonds with the hydroxyl groups instead. The crystalline network partially breaks down, leading to a ~50% loss in tenacity.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- Conditioned weight, $W_w = 245$ g
- Oven-dry weight, $W_d = 217$ g

**(i) Moisture Regain (MR)**

$$
MR\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100 = \dfrac{245 - 217}{217} \times 100
$$

$$
MR\% = \dfrac{28}{217} \times 100 = 12.90\%
$$

**(ii) Moisture Content (MC)**

$$
MC\% = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 = \dfrac{28}{245} \times 100 = 11.43\%
$$

**Verification using formula** $MC = \dfrac{MR}{100 + MR} \times 100$:

$$
MC\% = \dfrac{12.90}{112.90} \times 100 = 11.43\% \quad \checkmark
$$

**(iii) Water absorbed in a 1 kg bale at standard MR = 13%**

For 1 kg of dry fiber to have 13% MR:

$$
\text{Water mass} = 1000 \times \dfrac{13}{100} = 130\,\text{g}
$$

Total bale weight (conditioned) = $1000 + 130 = 1130$ g = **1.13 kg**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Summary:**
> - MR = **12.90%** (close to the standard of 13%)
> - MC = **11.43%**
> - 1 kg dry fiber = **1.13 kg conditioned weight** at standard MR

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **MR vs MC confusion (–1 mark):** MR uses *dry weight* as denominator; MC uses *wet weight*. Students frequently swap them. Memory aid: **"Regain → Drying oven"** (denominator = dry).
>
> 2. **Wrong gpd to N/tex conversion (–1 mark):** The constant is **0.0883 N/tex per gpd**, NOT 0.00981.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to express in % (–0.5 mark):** Always multiply by 100 at the end.
>
> 4. **Confusing "tenacity" with "tensile strength":** Tenacity is **specific strength** (force per unit linear density), not absolute load.
>
> 5. **Wrong diagnosis in identification tests (–2 marks):** Do NOT claim that cotton and viscose have different burning behavior — both burn similarly (cellulosic). The diagnostic tests are **microscopic** (serrated vs convoluted) and **chemical** (Cuoxam dissolution rate, acetone solubility).
>
> 6. **Insufficient units in final answer (–0.5 mark):** Always specify the unit (gpd, N/tex, %, g, m).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Viscose Rayon**

- ✅ Viscose rayon is a **regenerated cellulose fiber** (Cellulose II polymorph), not synthetic
- ✅ **Standard Moisture Regain = 13%**; Density = 1.50–1.54 g/cm³
- ✅ **Cross-section: serrated, multi-lobed** (the #1 identification feature)
- ✅ **Longitudinal view: striations parallel to fiber axis**
- ✅ **Burning:** Burns like paper, no bead, paper/leaf smell
- ✅ **Chemical tests:** Insoluble in acetone, soluble in Cuoxam and 70% H₂SO₄
- ✅ **Dry tenacity: 2.0–2.6 gpd**; **Wet tenacity drops by ~50%**
- ✅ **Three –OH groups per glucose unit** → high MR, dyeability, low wet strength
- ✅ **Conversion constants:** 1 tex = 9 denier; 1 gpd = 0.0883 N/tex = 88.3 mN/tex
- ✅ **MR% = [(W_w – W_d)/W_d] × 100**; **MC% = [(W_w – W_d)/W_w] × 100**
- ✅ **MC < MR** for the same sample (always)
- ✅ **Wet strength loss is the defining weakness** of viscose → handle wet fabrics gently
- ✅ **Polymerization degree (DP) of viscose: 250–350** (vs cotton: 3000–5000)
- ✅ **Diagnostic distinction from acetate:** viscose insoluble in acetone
- ✅ **Diagnostic distinction from cotton:** viscose dissolves faster in Cuoxam; cross-section is more irregular
