- ✅ **Standard test atmosphere:** 20 ± 2 °C, 65 ± 2 % RH (ISO 139)
- ✅ **Applications:** Apparel, hygiene products, medical dressings, tire cord (HWM variant), cellophane film

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: cellulose acetate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** cellulose acetate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Cellulose Acetate** is a semi-synthetic thermoplastic polymer obtained by the **esterification (acetylation)** of natural cellulose with acetic anhydride, typically in the presence of a strong acid catalyst such as **sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄)**. The hydroxyl (–OH) groups present on the anhydroglucose units (AGUs) of the cellulose backbone are chemically substituted by **acetyl groups (–COCH₃)**, producing a polymer with the general empirical repeat unit:

$$\text{[C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_2(\text{OH})_{3-x}(\text{OCOCH}_3)_x\text{]}_n$$

where **x = degree of substitution (DS)** ranging from 0 (pure cellulose) to 3 (fully acetylated, triacetate), and **n** is the degree of polymerization. Industrially, the most commercially significant form is the **secondary cellulose acetate (DS ≈ 2.4–2.5)**, which is acetone-soluble and used in fibers, films, and plastics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cellulose acetate is studied under polymer modification of natural polymers. Students must know the reaction scheme, the role of catalyst, the concept of **Degree of Substitution (DS)**, and the acetyl value determination experiment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long cotton thread (cellulose is the principal component of cotton). Each tiny segment of this thread has three small "hooks" (–OH groups) sticking out of it. If we take a chemical "key" (acetic anhydride) and attach it onto these hooks, the thread transforms from being water-loving (hydrophilic) to water-repelling (hydrophobic). This chemical "key-fitting" process is **acetylation**, and the modified thread is **cellulose acetate**.

A real-world analogy you have probably touched:
- The clear plastic frames of older eyeglasses.
- The transparent packaging film used to wrap cigarettes and gift boxes.
- The handle of cheap screwdriver sets.

All these were once cotton or wood pulp, chemically "re-keyed" into cellulose acetate.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Density of cellulose diacetate | **1.30 g/cm³** | Solid flake form |
| Glass transition temperature (Tg) | **170–190 °C** | Depends on DS |
| Melting point (triacetate) | **~290 °C** | Decomposes before true melt |
| Standard degree of substitution (secondary) | **2.4 – 2.5** | Acetone-soluble grade |
| Acetyl content (secondary acetate) | **38–40 % (w/w)** | Industry standard |
| Refractive index | **1.47 – 1.50** | Used in optical films |

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"acetyl value"** is a key analytical parameter. It is defined as the number of milligrams of **KOH (potassium hydroxide)** required to neutralize the acetic acid liberated during the complete saponification of **1 gram** of cellulose acetate.

## 1.4 Classification under Polymer Chemistry

Cellulose acetate belongs to the class of:
- **Natural polymer derivatives (semi-synthetic polymers)**
- **Organic esters of carbohydrates**
- **Thermoplastic, amorphous-to-semi-crystalline polymers**

The repeating unit arises from the **β-1,4-linked D-glucose** (anhydroglucose unit) found in native cellulose, but with the C-2, C-3, and C-6 hydroxyls partially or fully esterified.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Reaction Chemistry

The acetylation of cellulose proceeds via an **esterification reaction** between the hydroxyl (–OH) groups of cellulose and the anhydride of acetic acid (acetic anhydride, (CH₃CO)₂O). The reaction can be written in three equivalent forms:

### 2.1.1 Per hydroxyl group (simplified)

$$\text{Cell–OH} + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{Cell–OCOCH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$

The by-product is **acetic acid (CH₃COOH)**, which is recovered industrially and recycled.

### 2.1.2 Full backbone reaction (showing all three hydroxyls)

$$\text{[C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_2(\text{OH})_3\text{]}_n + 3n(\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{[C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_2(\text{OCOCH}_3)_3\text{]}_n + 3n\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$

The triacetate formed is called **Primary Cellulose Acetate (CA-3)**. It is soluble only in expensive chlorinated solvents like methylene chloride.

### 2.1.3 Partial hydrolysis (to get secondary acetate)

The triacetate is intentionally **partially hydrolyzed** to a DS of 2.4–2.5 to make it soluble in cheap solvents like **acetone**. This is the industrially important grade.

$$\text{[C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_2(\text{OCOCH}_3)_3\text{]}_n + (3-x)n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{[C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_2(\text{OH})_{3-x}(\text{OCOCH}_3)_x\text{]}_n + (3-x)n\,\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why partial hydrolysis?** Triacetate (DS = 3) is not soluble in acetone, the cheapest industrial solvent. Partial hydrolysis gives acetone-soluble secondary acetate (DS ≈ 2.4–2.5), drastically reducing production cost — a classic KTU "engineering economics meets chemistry" point.

## 2.2 Role of Catalyst (H₂SO₄)

Sulfuric acid acts as:
1. **Protonating agent:** Protonates the carbonyl oxygen of acetic anhydride, increasing its electrophilicity.
2. **Swelling agent:** Permeates into the cellulose fiber, opening the hydrogen-bonded network so the bulky anhydride can reach the –OH groups buried in crystalline regions.
3. **Reaction moderator:** Controls the rate so that surface scorching does not occur.

Typically, catalyst loading is **5–10 % (w/w) of cellulose**.

## 2.3 The Three Industrial Grades

| Grade | DS | Acetyl content (%) | Solvent | Major application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cellulose triacetate (CTA) | 2.9 – 3.0 | 43 – 44 | CH₂Cl₂, chloroform | Photographic film, fibers |
| Cellulose diacetate (CDA) | 2.4 – 2.5 | 38 – 40 | **Acetone** | Cigarette filters, eyeglass frames |
| Cellulose monoacetate | 1.0 – 1.5 | 21 – 30 | Water (limited) | Coatings, adhesives |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **guaranteed to appear** in KTU numerical or short-answer questions. Memorize them.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degree of Substitution (DS) | $\text{DS} = \dfrac{162 \times \%\,\text{acetyl}}{4300 - 42 \times \%\,\text{acetyl}}$ | dimensionless | 162 = MW of AGU; 43 = MW of acetyl group |
| 2 | Acetyl content (A%) | $\%A = \dfrac{43 \times \text{DS}}{162 + 42 \times \text{DS}} \times 100$ | % (w/w) | Used to check product quality |
| 3 | Acetyl value (saponification) | $\text{AV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 56.1}{m}$ | mg KOH/g | Vb = blank, Vs = sample titre, m = sample mass in g |
| 4 | Percent combined acetic acid | $\%\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} = \dfrac{\text{AV}}{1000} \times 60 \times 100$ | % | 60 = MW of acetic acid, 56.1 = MW of KOH |
| 5 | Yield of product | $\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{\text{obtained}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | % | Based on dry cellulose input |
| 6 | Saponification equivalent (SE) | $\text{SE} = \dfrac{1000 \times M_{\text{acetyl group}}}{\text{AV}}$ | g/eq | Inverse of AV with units conversion |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall #1:** Many students use 60 (MW of acetic acid) instead of **56.1 (MW of KOH)** in the acetyl value formula. This is the single most common error in board corrections. Always use **56.1** for "mg KOH per gram."

## 2.5 Why Engineers Care About Cellulose Acetate

- **Biopolymer / Sustainability angle:** Cellulose acetate is derived from renewable biomass (wood pulp, cotton linters), aligning with the green chemistry / NEP 2020 sustainable-engineering themes KTU emphasizes.
- **Biomedical applications:** Biodegradable scaffolds, hemodialysis membranes, drug delivery matrices.
- **Filtration:** Cigarette filters are the largest single application by volume.
- **Reverse-osmosis membranes:** Cellulose acetate asymmetric membranes are the workhorse of brackish-water desalination.
- **Optical films:** Polarizing film for LCD screens uses stretched cellulose triacetate as the substrate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Degree of Substitution (DS) Formula

We begin with a single anhydroglucose unit (AGU) of cellulose acetate:

$$\text{Structural unit} = \text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_2(\text{OH})_{3-x}(\text{OCOCH}_3)_x$$

where **x = DS** and one AGU has molecular weight:
- Anhydroglucose unit (–C₆H₁₀O₅–): 162 g/mol
- Each acetyl group (–COCH₃): 43 g/mol
- Substitution replaces an H (1) by a COCH₃ (43), net gain = 42 g/mol

**Step 1 — Molecular weight of one substituted AGU**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{AGU,sub}} &= 162 + 42x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of acetyl groups per AGU**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{acetyl}} &= 43x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Percent acetyl content (by mass)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%A &= \frac{m_{\text{acetyl}}}{M_{\text{AGU,sub}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{43x}{162 + 42x} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for x (i.e., DS)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%A\,(162 + 42x) &= 4300\,x \\
162\,\%A + 42\,\%A\,x &= 4300\,x \\
162\,\%A &= 4300\,x - 42\,\%A\,x \\
162\,\%A &= x\,(4300 - 42\,\%A) \\
x = \text{DS} &= \frac{162 \times \%A}{4300 - 42 \times \%A}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical KTU formula tested in numericals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If %A = 0 (pure cellulose, no acetyl groups), DS = 0 / 4300 = **0** ✓. If %A = 44.8 (fully acetylated, theoretical triacetate), DS = (162 × 44.8) / (4300 − 42 × 44.8) = 7257.6 / 2418.4 = **3.00** ✓. Formula verified.

## 3.2 Derivation of Acetyl Value (Saponification Method)

The acetyl value is determined by **saponification of the ester with excess standard KOH**, followed by **back-titration** of the unreacted KOH with standard HCl.

**Reaction with KOH:**

$$\text{Cell–OCOCH}_3 + \text{KOH} \longrightarrow \text{Cell–OH} + \text{CH}_3\text{COOK}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Weigh a known mass **m (g)** of dry cellulose acetate.
2. Add excess standard KOH of normality **N** in a known volume **V_KOH**.
3. Reflux for 1 hour to ensure complete saponification.
4. Back-titrate the excess KOH with standard HCl of normality **N_HCl**; volume used = **V_s**.
5. Repeat with a blank (no sample); volume of HCl used = **V_b**.

**Mass of KOH consumed by acetyl groups:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{meq of KOH consumed} &= N_{\text{HCl}}(V_b - V_s) \\
\text{mass of KOH (mg)} &= N_{\text{HCl}}(V_b - V_s) \times 56.1
\end{aligned}
$$

(56.1 = equivalent weight of KOH in g/eq; multiplied by 1000 to convert to mg)

**Acetyl value (AV):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AV} = \frac{\text{mg of KOH consumed}}{\text{mass of sample in g}} = \frac{N_{\text{HCl}}(V_b - V_s) \times 56.1}{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Question)

**Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2022, Modified]:**
1.00 g of cellulose acetate was saponified with 50.0 mL of 0.50 N KOH. The excess KOH required 20.0 mL of 0.50 N HCl for back-titration. A blank consumed 50.0 mL of the same HCl. Calculate:
(a) Acetyl value
(b) Percent combined acetic acid
(c) Degree of substitution (DS)

**Solution — Part (a): Acetyl Value**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_b &= 50.0 \text{ mL}, \quad V_s = 20.0 \text{ mL} \\
N_{\text{HCl}} &= 0.50 \text{ N}, \quad m = 1.00 \text{ g} \\
\text{AV} &= \frac{(50.0 - 20.0) \times 0.50 \times 56.1}{1.00} \\
&= \frac{30.0 \times 0.50 \times 56.1}{1.00} \\
&= 15.0 \times 56.1 \\
&= 841.5 \text{ mg KOH/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Part (b): Percent combined acetic acid**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} &= \frac{\text{AV} \times 60}{1000} \quad \text{(or directly: AV × 0.06)} \\
&= 841.5 \times 0.06 \\
&= 50.49\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: this value is artificially high because the sample is small; in real samples AV is much lower.)

**Solution — Part (c): Degree of Substitution (DS)**

The percent acetyl **%A** is related to percent combined acetic acid by:
$$\%A = \%\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \times \frac{43}{60}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%A &= 50.49 \times \frac{43}{60} = 36.18\,\% \\
\text{DS} &= \frac{162 \times 36.18}{4300 - 42 \times 36.18} \\
&= \frac{5861.16}{4300 - 1519.56} \\
&= \frac{5861.16}{2780.44} \\
&= 2.108
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** DS ≈ 2.11 indicates a cellulose **diacetate-grade** product.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall #2:** The conversion between %acetyl and %acetic acid (factor 43/60) is often forgotten. If the question gives "acetic acid" data, convert it to "acetyl" content **before** applying the DS formula.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Key Parameters vs. Properties

| Parameter | Increases → | Effect on solubility | Effect on Tg | Effect on hydrophobicity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Degree of substitution (DS) | Higher | More soluble in non-polar solvents | Higher Tg | More hydrophobic |
| Molecular weight (M̄_n) | Higher | Viscosity ↑, films stronger | Slight ↑ | Marginal |
| Acetyl content | Higher | Acetone solubility peaks at DS ≈ 2.4 | Tg rises to ~190 °C | Water uptake ↓ |
| Free –OH groups | Higher | More H-bonding with water | Lower Tg | More hydrophilic |
| Crystallinity | Higher | Insoluble regions grow | Rigid | Slight effect |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs below are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters; all labels are pure uppercase text without markdown formatting.

## 4.2 Acetylation Reaction Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawMat["RAW MATERIAL CELLULOSE WOOD PULP OR COTTON LINTERS"] --> prepStep["PURIFICATION AND ACTIVATION WITH GLACIAL ACETIC ACID"]
    prepStep --> swellStep["SWELLING AND CATALYST ADDITION H2SO4 5 TO 10 PERCENT"]
    swellStep --> reactStep["ACETYLATION WITH ACETIC ANHYDRIDE 40 TO 50 DEGREE C 2 TO 4 HOURS"]
    reactStep --> triacetate["PRIMARY CELLULOSE TRIACETATE DS APPROX 3.0"]
    triacetate --> hydroStep["PARTIAL HYDROLYSIS WITH WATER 40 DEGREE C"]
    hydroStep --> diacetate["SECONDARY CELLULOSE DIACETATE DS APPROX 2.4 TO 2.5"]
    triacetate --> ctaProd["INDUSTRIAL APPLICATIONS PHOTOGRAPHIC FILM AND OPTICAL FIBER"]
    diacetate --> cdaProd["INDUSTRIAL APPLICATIONS CIGARETTE FILTER AND EYEGLASS FRAME AND MEMBRANE"]
    reactStep --> byproduct["BYPRODUCT ACETIC ACID RECOVERED AND RECYCLED"]
    ctaProd --> labTest
    cdaProd --> labTest["QUALITY CONTROL ACETYL VALUE DS MEASUREMENT REFRACTIVE INDEX VISCOSITY"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Acetylation Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUTS"]
        celluloseIn["CELLULOSE LINTER FEED"]
        anhydrideIn["ACETIC ANHYDRIDE TANK"]
        acidIn["CATALYST H2SO4 STORAGE"]
    end

    subgraph REACTOR["REACTOR SYSTEM"]
        activator["ACTIVATION VESSEL 25 DEGREE C"]
        acetylator["ACETYLATION REACTOR 40 TO 50 DEGREE C"]
        hydrolyser["HYDROLYSER TANK 40 DEGREE C"]
    end

    subgraph SEPARATION["SEPARATION UNIT"]
        precipitor["PRECIPITATION TANK WATER DILUTION"]
        washer["MULTI STAGE WASHER"]
        dryer["DRYER 60 TO 80 DEGREE C"]
    end

    subgraph QC["QUALITY CONTROL"]
        avTest["ACETYL VALUE TEST"]
        dsTest["DS CALCULATION"]
        viscTest["VISCOSITY MEASUREMENT"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUT PRODUCTS"]
        flakeOut["CA FLAKES PACKED IN 25 KG BAGS"]
        recycleOut["ACETIC ACID RECOVERY COLUMN"]
    end

    celluloseIn --> activator --> acetylator
    anhydrideIn --> acetylator
    acidIn --> acetylator
    acetylator --> hydrolyser --> precipitor --> washer --> dryer
    washer --> avTest --> dsTest --> viscTest --> flakeOut
    acetylator --> recycleOut
```

## 4.4 Schematic of the Acetylated Anhydroglucose Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["ANHYDROGLUCOSE UNIT CELL C6 H10 O5"] --> node2["C2 OH REPLACED BY OCOCH3"]
    node1 --> node3["C3 OH REPLACED BY OCOCH3"]
    node1 --> node4["C6 OH REPLACED BY OCOCH3"]
    node2 --> node5["CELLULOSE TRIACETATE DS = 3.0"]
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node2 --> node6["SECONDARY ACETATE DS = 2.4 TO 2.5"]
    node3 --> node6
    node4 --> node7["FREE OH AT C6 FOR DS LESS THAN 3"]
    node6 --> node7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module Experiment 2]**
**Define acetyl value. Mention its significance in the quality control of cellulose acetate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Acetyl value is defined as the number of milligrams of **KOH (potassium hydroxide)** required to neutralize the acetic acid liberated by the complete saponification of **1 gram** of cellulose acetate.

$$\text{AV} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{HCl}} \times 56.1}{m}$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. It is the **primary quality control parameter** for cellulose acetate.
2. From acetyl value, the **percent combined acetic acid** and **degree of substitution (DS)** can be calculated.
3. The DS directly determines the solubility, mechanical properties, and end-use application of the polymer.
4. A higher AV indicates a more acetylated (more hydrophobic) product.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**What is the role of sulfuric acid in the acetylation of cellulose?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) serves three crucial roles:
1. **Catalyst** — it protonates the carbonyl oxygen of acetic anhydride, increasing its electrophilicity and accelerating esterification (1 mark).
2. **Swelling agent** — it disrupts the strong inter- and intra-chain hydrogen bonds of cellulose, making the buried –OH groups accessible to the anhydride (1 mark).
3. **Reaction moderator** — typically used at 5–10 % w/w to balance reaction rate without causing charring of the cellulose surface (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Synthesis, Mechanism, and Product Characterization

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat, labelled reaction scheme, explain the **laboratory preparation of cellulose diacetate (secondary cellulose acetate)** from cotton linters. Discuss the role of the catalyst and the necessity of partial hydrolysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1.50 g sample of cellulose acetate was refluxed with 50.0 mL of 0.75 N alcoholic KOH. The excess KOH, after saponification, was back-titrated with 0.50 N HCl, requiring 18.0 mL. A blank titration consumed 50.0 mL of the same HCl. Calculate:
   (i) the **acetyl value**,
   (ii) the **percent combined acetic acid**,
   (iii) the **percent acetyl content**, and
   (iv) the **degree of substitution (DS)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Step 1 — Activation of cellulose (1 mark)**
10 g of purified cotton linters (or wood pulp) is steeped in **glacial acetic acid** at 25 °C for 1 hour to activate the cellulose by disrupting some hydrogen bonds.

**Step 2 — Acetylation (2 marks)**
The activated cellulose is transferred to a stirred reactor containing **50 mL acetic anhydride** and **0.5–1.0 mL concentrated H₂SO₄ (catalyst)**. The mixture is held at **40–50 °C for 3–4 hours** with continuous stirring. The reaction:

$$\text{Cell–OH} + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{Cell–OCOCH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$

yields **primary cellulose triacetate (DS ≈ 3.0)**.

**Step 3 — Partial hydrolysis (2 marks)**
To convert the triacetate into the acetone-soluble diacetate, water (or dilute acetic acid) is added and the reaction continued at **40 °C for several hours** until the DS drops to **2.4–2.5**:

$$\text{CA-3} + (3-x)\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CA-}x + (3-x)\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$

**Step 4 — Precipitation and washing (1 mark)**
The solution is poured into a large excess of water; the cellulose acetate precipitates as white flakes. These are filtered, washed repeatedly with water to remove residual acid, and dried in an oven at 60–80 °C.

**Step 5 — Why partial hydrolysis? (1 mark)**
Triacetate (DS = 3) dissolves only in expensive chlorinated solvents. Diacetate (DS ≈ 2.4) is soluble in cheap **acetone**, drastically reducing the cost of fiber spinning and film casting. This is the **engineering economic trade-off** that makes secondary cellulose acetate industrially dominant.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Given:**
- $m = 1.50 \text{ g}$
- $V_{\text{KOH}} = 50.0 \text{ mL}, \; N_{\text{KOH}} = 0.75 \text{ N}$
- $N_{\text{HCl}} = 0.50 \text{ N}$
- $V_s = 18.0 \text{ mL}, \; V_b = 50.0 \text{ mL}$

**Step 1 — Acetyl value (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AV} &= \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{HCl}} \times 56.1}{m} \\
&= \frac{(50.0 - 18.0) \times 0.50 \times 56.1}{1.50} \\
&= \frac{32.0 \times 0.50 \times 56.1}{1.50} \\
&= \frac{897.6}{1.50} \\
&= 598.4 \text{ mg KOH/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Percent combined acetic acid (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} &= \frac{\text{AV} \times 60}{1000} \\
&= \frac{598.4 \times 60}{1000} \\
&= 35.90\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Percent acetyl content (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Acetyl} &= \%\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \times \frac{43}{60} \\
&= 35.90 \times \frac{43}{60} \\
&= 25.73\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Degree of substitution (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DS} &= \frac{162 \times \%\text{Acetyl}}{4300 - 42 \times \%\text{Acetyl}} \\
&= \frac{162 \times 25.73}{4300 - 42 \times 25.73} \\
&= \frac{4168.26}{4300 - 1080.66} \\
&= \frac{4168.26}{3219.34} \\
&= 1.295
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** DS ≈ 1.30 places this product in the **monoacetate-grade** (useful as coating or binder, not for fibers).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **determination of acetyl value of cellulose acetate by the saponification method**, including the principle, reagents, procedure, and calculation formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the **degree of substitution (DS) affects the properties** of cellulose acetate — particularly its solubility, mechanical behavior, and applications. With a phase-property diagram, classify the three industrial grades. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — Acetyl Value Determination

**Principle (1.5 marks):**
The ester groups in cellulose acetate are hydrolyzed (saponified) by refluxing with a known excess of standard alcoholic KOH. The liberated acetic acid neutralizes an equivalent amount of KOH. The unreacted KOH is back-titrated with standard HCl. The difference between the blank and sample titres gives the KOH consumed by the acetyl groups.

$$\text{Cell–OCOCH}_3 + \text{KOH} \longrightarrow \text{Cell–OH} + \text{CH}_3\text{COOK}$$

**Reagents (1.5 marks):**
- 0.5 N alcoholic KOH
- 0.5 N HCl
- Phenolphthalein indicator

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Accurately weigh **1.0–1.5 g** of dry cellulose acetate flake into a 250 mL round-bottom flask.
2. Pipette **50 mL of 0.5 N alcoholic KOH** into the flask.
3. Reflux gently for **1 hour** on a water bath to ensure complete saponification.
4. Cool to room temperature; add **2–3 drops of phenolphthalein**.
5. Titrate the excess KOH with **0.5 N HCl** until the pink color disappears. Record volume = **V_s**.
6. Repeat the entire procedure **without the sample** (blank). Record volume = **V_b**.

**Calculation (1 mark):**

$$\text{Acetyl Value} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{HCl}} \times 56.1}{m}$$

where **m** = mass of sample in grams.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — DS and Properties

**Effect of DS on key properties (4 marks):**

| DS | Solubility | Application | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (pure cellulose) | Insoluble in common solvents | Native fiber | Strong H-bonding network |
| 0.6 – 1.5 (monoacetate) | Water-dispersible | Coatings, film-formers, adhesives | Some –OH retains hydrophilicity |
| 2.4 – 2.5 (diacetate) | Acetone-soluble | Cigarette filters, eyeglass frames, membranes | Optimal balance of H-bonding disruption |
| 2.9 – 3.0 (triacetate) | Chlorinated solvents | Photographic film, optical fiber | All –OH replaced → no internal H-bonds |

**Engineering insight (1 mark):**
Increasing DS:
- **Increases** Tg (from ~120 °C to ~190 °C)
- **Decreases** water absorption (from ~12 % to ~2 %)
- **Increases** density slightly
- **Increases** moldability with plasticizers (e.g., DEP, DMP for diacetate)

**Three Industrial Grades (2 marks):**
1. **Cellulose Triacetate (CTA):** DS 2.9–3.0, 43–44 % acetyl; used in photographic film.
2. **Cellulose Diacetate (CDA):** DS 2.4–2.5, 38–40 % acetyl; used in cigarette filters, eyeglass frames.
3. **Cellulose Monoacetate:** DS 1.0–1.5, 21–30 % acetyl; used in coatings and adhesives.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-mark answer pitfalls (top 5 reasons students lose marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 56.1 factor** — using 60 (MW of CH₃COOH) instead of 56.1 (MW of KOH) in the acetyl value formula. Result: AV wrong by ~7 %, full marks lost in numericals.
> 2. **Mixing up % acetyl and % acetic acid** — always convert using the factor **43/60** before applying the DS formula.
> 3. **Ignoring the "blank" subtraction** — forgetting to subtract blank titre in the formula gives nonsense values. Examiners catch this immediately.
> 4. **No mention of partial hydrolysis** — Q on diacetate preparation without mentioning partial hydrolysis is **incomplete**; full 1–2 marks lost.
> 5. **No justification for the role of H₂SO₄** — the catalyst has three roles (catalyst, swelling agent, moderator); mentioning only one costs at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — Cellulose Acetate (Module Experiment 2):**

- **Definition:** Semi-synthetic polymer; ester of cellulose with acetic anhydride; general formula **[C₆H₇O₂(OH)₃₋ₓ(OCOCH₃)ₓ]ₙ**.
- **Raw materials:** Cellulose (cotton linters or wood pulp) + acetic anhydride + H₂SO₄ catalyst.
- **Catalyst function:** Catalyst + swelling agent + reaction moderator (5–10 % loading).
- **Two-step process:** (1) Acetylation → triacetate (DS = 3); (2) Partial hydrolysis → diacetate (DS ≈ 2.4–2.5).
- **Why partial hydrolysis?** Converts insoluble-in-cheap-solvents triacetate into acetone-soluble diacetate — engineering economics.
- **Acetyl Value definition:** mg KOH consumed per gram of cellulose acetate during complete saponification.
- **Key formulas (must memorize):**
  - $\text{AV} = \dfrac{(V_b - V_s) \times N_{\text{HCl}} \times 56.1}{m}$
  - $\%\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} = \dfrac{\text{AV} \times 60}{1000}$
  - $\%\text{Acetyl} = \%\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \times \dfrac{43}{60}$
  - $\text{DS} = \dfrac{162 \times \%\text{Acetyl}}{4300 - 42 \times \%\text{Acetyl}}$
- **Three industrial grades (know DS and application):** Triacetate (DS 3.0, film), Diacetate (DS 2.4, filters & frames), Monoacetate (DS 1.0–1.5, coatings).
- **Properties to recall:** As DS ↑ → Tg ↑, water absorption ↓, hydrophobicity ↑, density slightly ↑.
- **Applications (cross-disciplinary):** Cigarette filters, eyeglass frames, photographic film, polarizing film for LCD, hemodialysis and RO membranes, biodegradable scaffolds.
- **Sustainability angle:** Derived from renewable biomass; cellulose acetate is the bridge between **natural polymers** and **synthetic thermoplastics** — a favorite KTU NEP 2020 question.
- **Examiner traps:** Use 56.1 (not 60) for AV; convert % acetic acid to % acetyl before DS calculation; always subtract blank titre.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 9 nylon66 and nylon6
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 9 nylon66 and nylon6

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Nylons (Polyamides)

**Nylons** are a family of synthetic linear **thermoplastic polyamides** containing recurring **amide linkages (–CO–NH–)** as an integral part of the main polymer chain. They are produced from monomers such as **diamines**, **dicarboxylic acids**, **amino acids**, or **lactams**.

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code PCPOL607 – Polymer Chemistry), the two commercially most important members are:

- **Nylon 6,6** — synthesised by step-growth **polycondensation** of **hexamethylenediamine (HMDA)** and **adipic acid**.
- **Nylon 6** — synthesised by **ring-opening polymerisation (ROP)** of **ε-caprolactam**.

The numerical nomenclature (a,b) refers to:
- For Nylon 6,6 → both monomers contribute **6 carbon atoms** each (between the two N–H groups of diamine and between the two –COOH groups of diacid).
- For Nylon 6 → the monomer (ε-caprolactam) contains a **6-membered carbon chain** before ring closure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Always write the repeat unit explicitly on the main chain. The amide linkage (–CONH–) is the *defining functional group* that classifies a polymer as a polyamide. Remember: *Nylons = Polyamides*, but the reverse is not strictly true (aramids, proteins, etc. are also polyamides).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **train** where every alternate coach is **rigidly hinged** to the next by a strong magnetic coupler (the **amide bond**), and the rest of the coach body is just a long flexible carbon chain (the **methylene –(CH₂)ₙ– segments**).

- In **Nylon 6,6**, the couplers (amide groups) appear on *both sides* of every long aliphatic spacer because the molecule is built by alternating two different "pieces" (diamine + diacid). Think of a coupling at the front **and** the back of every carriage.
- In **Nylon 6**, the coupler is only on *one side* of every spacer because the molecule is "unzipped" from a single ring-shaped monomer (caprolactam), producing a self-repeating unit.

Both polymers are heavily used in **textile fibres, gear wheels, fish lines, automotive parts, and zip fasteners** because the regular amide groups can **hydrogen-bond** with each other like Velcro strips, producing exceptional tensile strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard physical constant to remember:** Density of Nylon 6 ≈ **1.14 g/cm³**; Nylon 6,6 ≈ **1.14 g/cm³**. Both are semi-crystalline, with melting points **Tₘ (Nylon 6) ≈ 215–225 °C** and **Tₘ (Nylon 6,6) ≈ 255–265 °C**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Monomer Architecture & Repeat Units

| Property | Nylon 6 | Nylon 6,6 |
|---|---|---|
| **Monomer** | ε-Caprolactam (cyclic, 7-membered lactam ring) | Hexamethylenediamine + Adipic Acid (AA) |
| **Polymerisation Type** | Ring-Opening Polymerisation (ROP) | Step-Growth Polycondensation |
| **Number of monomers** | One (single monomer) | Two (difunctional AA + BB type) |
| **By-product** | None (no small molecule released) | **Water (H₂O)** |
| **Repeat unit** | –[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]– | –[NH–(CH₂)₆–NH–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO]– |
| **Molecular weight of repeat unit** | 113.16 g/mol | 226.32 g/mol |
| **Tₘ** | ≈ 220 °C | ≈ 260 °C |
| **Density** | 1.14 g/cm³ | 1.14 g/cm³ |
| **% Crystallinity (typical)** | 35–45 % | 35–50 % |
| **Common name** | Polycaprolactam | Poly(hexamethylene adipamide) |
| **Trade names** | Perlon, Capron, Akulon | Nylon, Antron, Celanese |

### 2.2 Synthesis of Nylon 6,6 (Step-Growth Polycondensation)

**Step 1 – Nylon Salt Formation (1:1 neutralisation):**
Adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine react *first* in stoichiometric amounts (often in methanol) to form a **1:1 ammonium carboxylate salt**, called the "**nylon salt**", which is a white crystalline solid.

$$
\text{HOOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COOH} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{}^+\text{H}_3\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_3^+\; \cdot\; \text{}^-\text{OOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COO}^-
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This salt has a definite melting point (~190 °C) and is much easier to purify and store than the corrosive/free diamine–acid mixture.

**Step 2 – Condensation Polymerisation in melt (60 % solution → melt at 270–280 °C):**

$$
n\;\text{HOOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COOH} \;+\; n\;\text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H–[NH–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH–CO–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH} \;+\; (2n-1)\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

- Reaction is **autocatalytic** (the –COOH groups catalyse the amidation of –NH₂).
- **Equilibrium reaction** → removal of water drives equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier).
- Degree of polymerisation is governed by the **Carothers equation** (see formula sheet).

**Mechanism (nucleophilic acyl substitution):**

1. Protonation of carbonyl oxygen of –COOH by the amine or by another –COOH.
2. Nucleophilic attack of amine nitrogen on the electrophilic carbonyl carbon → tetrahedral intermediate.
3. Proton transfer from N⁺–H to the –OH group.
4. Loss of H₂O, giving the amide linkage.

### 2.3 Synthesis of Nylon 6 (Ring-Opening of ε-Caprolactam)

ε-Caprolactam (a 7-membered ring lactam) is heated at **~250 °C** with a small amount of **water (hydrolytic initiator)**, or with an **anionic catalyst (NaH, NaOMe)** for activated anionic ROP (used in RIM – reaction injection moulding).

**Mechanism (hydrolytic / cationic, 3 stages):**

**Stage 1 – Initiation (ring opening by water):**

$$
\text{Caprolactam (ring)} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_5\text{–COOH} \quad (\text{ε-aminocaproic acid})
$$

**Stage 2 – Propagation (chain growth by successive ring-opening):**
The newly formed amino acid (or its amide) attacks another caprolactam ring:

$$
\text{H–[NH–(CH}_2\text{)}_5\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH} \;+\; \text{Caprolactam} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{H–[NH–(CH}_2\text{)}_5\text{–CO]}_{n+1}\text{–OH}
$$

**Stage 3 – Termination / Equilibrium:**
The reaction is **reversible** — the monomer and polymer reach an equilibrium. The **equilibrium monomer concentration** at 250 °C is about **6–10 wt %**, which is why commercial Nylon 6 is always washed/extracted to remove residual caprolactam.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the repeat unit of Nylon 6 = –[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]– and not –[NH–(CH₂)₆–CO]–?**
> Because the caprolactam ring has **6 carbons + 1 nitrogen**. When the ring opens at the C–N bond, the nitrogen becomes the –NH– group, and the carbonyl carbon becomes the –CO– group, leaving **5 CH₂ groups** between them.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Carothers number-average DP, $p$ = extent of reaction | Valid for AA + BB stoichiometric step growth |
| $M_n = M_0 \cdot \bar{X}_n$ | Number-average molecular weight | $M_0$ = average monomer unit mass |
| $\bar{X}_w = \dfrac{1+p}{1-p}$ | Weight-average DP (most probable distribution) | For step-growth only |
| $PDI = \dfrac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n} = 1 + p$ | Polydispersity Index | Tends to **2** as $p \to 1$ |
| $r = \dfrac{N_A}{N_B} \le 1$ | Stoichiometric imbalance ratio | $N_A, N_B$ = moles of functional groups |
| $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | Modified Carothers eqn. for **non-stoichiometric** feed | Use when one monomer is in excess |
| $T_m = T_m^\circ \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n}\right]$ | Flory melting-point depression for step-growth polymer | $T_m^\circ$ = melting point of infinite chain |
| $p = 0.99$ → $\bar{X}_n = 100$ | Classic Carothers result: 99 % conversion → DP ≈ 100 | Shows why step-growth needs very high conversion |
| $[\eta] = K M^a$ | Mark–Houwink intrinsic viscosity equation | $K, a$ are polymer–solvent–T specific |
| $\Delta H_{poly} \approx 25$ kJ/mol repeat | Heat of polycondensation per amide bond formed | Useful for reactor design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Both nylons are used as engineering thermoplastics. The 1–2 % absorbed moisture acts as a *plasticiser* and lowers Tg, which is why dried nylon must be moulded. The step-growth equilibrium with water means *moisture during processing* will cause molecular-weight loss (hydrolytic degradation) — a classic viva question.

### 2.5 Comparison: Why Nylon 6,6 has a Higher Tₘ than Nylon 6

| Factor | Effect on Tₘ |
|---|---|
| Amide-group density (amide per unit mass) | Nylon 6,6 has **2 amide / 226 g** = 0.0088; Nylon 6 has **1 amide / 113 g** = 0.0088. **Identical!** |
| Hydrogen-bonding geometry | Nylon 6,6 has *parallel* chain alignment allowing **two H-bonds per repeat unit in opposite directions**; Nylon 6 can only form H-bonds in *one preferred direction* → slightly less perfect crystal. |
| Chain symmetry | Nylon 6,6 is more symmetric → packs better → higher crystallinity → higher Tₘ. |
| Result | **Tₘ (Nylon 6,6) ≈ 260 °C > Tₘ (Nylon 6) ≈ 220 °C** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Carothers Number-Average Degree of Polymerisation

**Carothers equation derivation for AA + BB step-growth polycondensation:**

Consider a system with $N_0$ total molecules of monomer (or, more precisely, $N_0$ total functional group pairs). Let $N$ be the number of molecules (of all sizes) remaining at extent of reaction $p$.

**Step 1:** By definition, the *number* of functional groups that have reacted at extent $p$ is:
$$N_{\text{reacted}} = p \cdot N_0$$

**Step 2:** Since each reaction step between two functional groups **consumes 2 functional groups and 2 molecules**, the total number of bonds formed (i.e., number of condensation events) is:
$$N_{\text{bonds}} = p \cdot N_0$$

**Step 3:** Each bond formation reduces the *number of molecules* by 1 (two molecules → one molecule, plus one H₂O). Therefore the total number of molecules present at extent $p$ is:
$$N = N_0 - N_{\text{bonds}} = N_0 - p N_0 = N_0(1 - p)$$

**Step 4:** The number-average degree of polymerisation is defined as the *ratio* of initial molecules to current molecules:
$$\bar{X}_n \equiv \dfrac{N_0}{N} = \dfrac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}\;}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Nylon 6,6

**Question:** In a Nylon 6,6 batch, 0.500 mol hexamethylenediamine and 0.495 mol adipic acid are reacted. Water is continuously removed. Calculate $\bar{X}_n$ when the conversion of the limiting functional group reaches 99 %.

**Given:** $N_A = 0.500$ mol (–NH₂), $N_B = 0.495$ mol (–COOH)
- Limiting: $N_B$ (–COOH) is the minority, so $r = N_B/N_A = 0.495/0.500 = 0.990$
- $p$ = 0.99 (extent of reaction of the *minority* group, –COOH)

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric imbalance ratio:**
$$r = \dfrac{N_B}{N_A} = \dfrac{0.495}{0.500} = 0.990$$

**Step 2 — Modified Carothers equation:**
$$
\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} = \dfrac{1 + 0.990}{1 + 0.990 - 2(0.990)(0.990)}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**
$$1 + 0.990 = 1.990$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**
$$1.990 - 2(0.9801) = 1.990 - 1.9602 = 0.0298$$

**Step 5 — Final DP:**
$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1.990}{0.0298} \approx 66.78$$

**Step 6 — Number-average molecular weight:**
$$M_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0 = 66.78 \times 226.32 \;\text{g/mol} \approx 15{,}114 \;\text{g/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Compare with a perfectly stoichiometric batch (r = 1): $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{2}{2-2p} = \dfrac{1}{1-p} = 100$. A mere 1 % imbalance has cut the DP by ~33 %! This is why *nylon salt* (perfect 1:1) is preferred industrially.

### 3.3 Equilibrium Monomer Concentration in Nylon 6 ROP

For the reversible ring-opening of caprolactam:
$$[\text{Monomer}]_{eq} = \dfrac{[\text{Lactam}]_0}{1 + k_p \cdot t \cdot [\text{Initiator}]}$$

A simpler empirical Flory-style relation often used in KTU problems:
$$[\text{M}]_{eq} = \dfrac{k_p}{k_{depoly}}$$

At 250 °C, $[M]_{eq} \approx 6$–$10$ wt %. This explains why industrial Nylon 6 chips are *water-extracted* after polymerisation to remove unreacted caprolactam (which would plasticise the polymer and lower Tg).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reaction Type → Kinetics → KTU Pitfalls

| Reaction type | Kinetics | DP equation | Pitfall (most common mark-loser) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nylon 6,6 (step-growth)** | Second-order, $[A]=[B]$, rate $\propto [A]^2$ | $\bar{X}_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$ | Forgetting that $p$ is conversion of the **limiting** group when $r \neq 1$ |
| **Nylon 6 (ROP, hydrolytic)** | Pseudo-first-order, kinetically chain-like *but* obeys equilibrium | $\bar{X}_n = 1/(1-p_{poly})$ where $p_{poly}$ is fraction of rings opened | Forgetting that the *monomer* is not the repeat unit; ring is 7-membered, repeat unit is from C₆ chain |
| **Anionic ROP (Nylon 6, RIM)** | "Activated monomer" — fast, living | $\bar{X}_n = [M]_0/[I]_0$ | Treating it like step-growth (it's NOT — fast, low polydispersity) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Nylon 6,6 Synthesis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material 1: Adipic Acid HOOC CH2 4 COOH] --> C[Nylon Salt Formation 1 to 1 in Methanol 60 deg C]
    B[Raw Material 2: Hexamethylenediamine H2N CH2 6 NH2] --> C
    C --> D[Crystalline Nylon Salt White Solid MP approx 190 deg C]
    D --> E[Concentration Stage Vacuum Evaporator Removes Methanol]
    E --> F[Polycondensation Reactor 270 to 280 deg C, 1 to 2 MPa, 4 to 6 h]
    F --> G[Polymer Melt with By product Water]
    G --> H[Water Removal Vacuum or Inert Gas Sweep]
    H --> I[Melt Spinning or Chip Extrusion]
    I --> J[Final Nylon 6,6 Fibres or Moulding Granules]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Nylon 6 Synthesis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed: Cyclohexanone from Cyclohexane Oxidation] --> B[Beckmann Rearrangement with H2SO4 or Oleum]
    B --> C[Crude Caprolactam 7 Membered Ring with NH and CO]
    C --> D[Purification Vacuum Distillation Crystallisation]
    D --> E[Polymerisation Reactor 250 deg C with Initiator Water or Anionic Catalyst]
    E --> F[Equilibrium Mixture Polymer plus 6 to 10 percent Monomer]
    F --> G[Water Extraction Removes Residual Caprolactam]
    G --> H[Chopper Pelletiser Nylon 6 Chips]
    H --> I[Melt Spinning or Injection Moulding]
```

### 4.3 Mechanism Block Diagram: Step-Growth Polycondensation of Nylon 6,6

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Protonation of carbonyl oxygen of adipic acid] --> S2[Step 2 Nucleophilic attack by amine NH2 on carbonyl C]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Tetrahedral intermediate with OH and NH on same C]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Proton transfer from NH to OH]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Loss of H2O molecule]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Stable amide bond CO NH formed]
    S6 --> S7[Repeat with next diacid and diamine Chain extends]
```

### 4.4 Architecture: Comparison of Nylon 6 vs Nylon 6,6 Hydrogen-Bonding Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph N66[Nylon 6,6 Chain Packing]
        N66A[Chain A direction upward] --> N66B[Chain B direction downward]
        N66B --> N66C[Chain C direction upward]
        N66C --> N66D[Chain D direction downward]
    end
    subgraph N6[Nylon 6 Chain Packing]
        N6A[Chain 1 all amide groups face one side] --> N6B[Chain 2 amide groups face other side]
        N6B --> N6C[Parallel H bonds only between alternating sheets]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The antiparallel (alternating up/down) arrangement in **Nylon 6,6** allows **two H-bonds per repeat unit (bidirectional)**, producing a more tightly packed crystal lattice → higher **Tₘ** and modulus. In **Nylon 6**, all amide groups are oriented the same way → H-bonds can form only between alternating sheets (the famous *pleated sheet* structure), giving slightly less perfect crystallinity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between Nylon 6 and Nylon 6,6 with respect to (a) monomers used, (b) polymerisation mechanism, and (c) the by-product.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | Nylon 6 | Nylon 6,6 |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Monomer | ε-Caprolactam (a single cyclic lactam) | Hexamethylenediamine + Adipic acid (two difunctional monomers) |
| (b) Mechanism | Ring-opening polymerisation (chain growth) | Step-growth polycondensation |
| (c) By-product | None (intramolecular, no small molecule) | **Water (H₂O)** — must be removed to drive equilibrium |

**(1 mark per correct row)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Define the term "nylon salt" and write the equation for its formation from adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A nylon salt is the **1:1 ammonium carboxylate salt** formed by the acid–base neutralisation of one mole of adipic acid with one mole of hexamethylenediamine. It is a white crystalline solid with a sharp melting point (~190 °C) and is used as a purified, stoichiometrically balanced intermediate for Nylon 6,6 production.

$$
\text{HOOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_2 \longrightarrow\; ^+\text{H}_3\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_3^{+}\, \cdot\, ^-\text{OOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COO}^-
$$

(**1 mark definition + 2 marks equation with correct charges and structure**)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat reaction scheme, describe the industrial preparation of **Nylon 6,6** from adipic acid and hexamethylenediamine. Discuss the role of (i) the "nylon salt" and (ii) removal of water in achieving high molecular weight. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The degree of polymerisation $\bar{X}_n$ in a Nylon 6,6 batch is found to be **120**. Calculate (i) the extent of reaction $p$, and (ii) the number-average molecular weight $M_n$. Given: $M_0$ (repeat unit) = 226 g/mol. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution A(a):**

**Step 1 — Nylon salt preparation (2 marks):**
In a stainless-steel reactor, adipic acid is dissolved in warm methanol, and an equimolar amount of hexamethylenediamine is added. The acid–base reaction produces the white crystalline **nylon salt** (1:1 ammonium carboxylate), which precipitates and is filtered off. Role: it provides a *stoichiometrically exact*, easily-purified, and *easy-to-handle* precursor that eliminates any imbalance that would otherwise cap the molecular weight.

**Step 2 — Polymerisation (2 marks):**
The dried nylon salt is heated in an autoclave at **270–280 °C under 1–2 MPa nitrogen** for 4–6 h. The melt polycondensation liberates **water** as the by-product:

$$
n\;^+\text{H}_3\text{N–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH}_3^{+}\,\cdot\,^-\text{OOC–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–COO}^- \longrightarrow \text{H–[NH–(CH}_2\text{)}_6\text{–NH–CO–(CH}_2\text{)}_4\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH} + (2n-1)\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 3 — Role of water removal (1.5 marks):**
The reaction is **reversible**. By Le Chatelier's principle, continuous removal of water (via vacuum or inert gas sweep through a packed column at the reactor top) shifts the equilibrium **to the right**, allowing the conversion $p$ to exceed 0.99, which — by Carothers' equation — gives $\bar{X}_n \ge 100$. Even 0.5 % residual water can hold the chain at a lower DP.

**Step 4 — Final processing (1.5 marks):**
The polymer melt is extruded as ribbons, cooled, and chopped into chips. These chips are melt-spun at 280–290 °C to give fibres, or injection-moulded to give engineering parts.

---

**Model Solution A(b):**

**(i) Extent of reaction** (3 marks):
For stoichiometric Nylon 6,6 ($r = 1$):
$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p} \;\Longrightarrow\; 120 = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$$
$$1 - p = \dfrac{1}{120} = 0.00833$$
$$\boxed{p = 0.9917 \;\text{or}\; 99.17\%}$$

**(ii) Number-average molecular weight** (4 marks):
$$M_n = \bar{X}_n \cdot M_0 = 120 \times 226 \;\text{g/mol}$$
$$\boxed{M_n = 27{,}120 \;\text{g/mol} \approx 27.12 \;\text{kDa}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **ring-opening polymerisation (ROP)** of ε-caprolactam to give Nylon 6. Write the **initiation, propagation** and **termination** steps clearly. Why is the equilibrium monomer concentration important industrially? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1.00 kg sample of Nylon 6 has 70 g of unreacted caprolactam. Calculate (i) the weight percent of caprolactam and (ii) the number of repeat units per chain, given $M_n$ = 18,000 g/mol and $M_0$ = 113 g/mol. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution B(a):**

**Step 1 — Monomer description (1 mark):**
ε-Caprolactam is a 7-membered cyclic amide (lactam) with the structure (CH₂)₅–C(=O)–NH in a ring.

**Step 2 — Initiation (1.5 marks):**
At 250 °C in the presence of a small amount of water (hydrolytic initiator), caprolactam undergoes ring-opening to give ε-aminocaproic acid:

$$
(\text{CH}_2)_5\text{–C(=O)–NH (ring)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N–(CH}_2)_5\text{–COOH}
$$

**Step 3 — Propagation (2 marks):**
The terminal –NH₂ group of the amino acid attacks the carbonyl carbon of another caprolactam ring, opening it. The newly formed –COOH then attacks yet another ring, propagating the chain:

$$
\text{H–[NH–(CH}_2)_5\text{–CO]}_n\text{–OH} + \text{Caprolactam} \longrightarrow \text{H–[NH–(CH}_2)_5\text{–CO]}_{n+1}\text{–OH}
$$

**Step 4 — Termination / Equilibrium (1.5 marks):**
ROP of caprolactam is **reversible**. The chain end can re-cyclise by back-biting. The system reaches a dynamic equilibrium where ~6–10 wt % of the material remains as *unreacted monomer*. Industrially, this monomer is removed by **hot-water extraction** of the polymer chips, then recovered and recycled. If left in, it would plasticise the polymer, lower Tg, lower Tₘ, and cause fogging in moulded parts.

**Step 5 — Why important (1 mark):**
Equilibrium monomer dictates the *maximum conversion*, the *energy economics* (extraction + recycling), and the *final mechanical properties* of the polymer.

---

**Model Solution B(b):**

**(i) Weight percent of caprolactam** (3 marks):
$$\text{wt \%} = \dfrac{70\;\text{g}}{1000\;\text{g}} \times 100 = 7.0\%$$

**(ii) Number of repeat units per chain** (4 marks):
$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{113} \approx 159.3$$
$$\boxed{\bar{X}_n \approx 159\;\text{repeat units per chain}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** the caprolactam ring (7 atoms) with the *repeat unit* of Nylon 6 (which has only 5 CH₂ groups). Writing the repeat unit as –[NH–(CH₂)₆–CO]– is a **classic 1-mark deduction** in every valuation.
> 2. **Always specify** whether $p$ refers to the conversion of the *limiting* or *majority* group in non-stoichiometric systems.
> 3. **Forgetting the (2n – 1) H₂O** coefficient in polycondensation is a 0.5-mark cut.
> 4. **M₀ for Nylon 6,6** = 226 g/mol; do not use 113 g/mol (that is Nylon 6). The two nylons are the most commonly interchanged in KTU answer sheets.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Nylon 6** comes from **one** monomer — **ε-caprolactam** — via **ring-opening polymerisation**; no by-product.
- ✅ **Nylon 6,6** comes from **two** monomers — **HMDA + adipic acid** — via **step-growth polycondensation**; **water is the by-product**.
- ✅ Nylon 6,6 always forms via the **nylon salt** intermediate for stoichiometric control.
- ✅ Carothers equation: $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ for stoichiometric; $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ for non-stoichiometric.
- ✅ A 1 % loss of stoichiometry can drop the DP by ~33 % at high conversion.
- ✅ Nylon 6,6 has higher Tₘ (~260 °C) than Nylon 6 (~220 °C) due to better symmetry and bidirectional H-bonding.
- ✅ ROP of caprolactam is **reversible** → ~6–10 % residual monomer → must be extracted.
- ✅ Both nylons are moisture-sensitive during melt processing (hydrolysis → loss of Mₙ).
- ✅ Repeat units: Nylon 6 → –[NH–(CH₂)₅–CO]– ; Nylon 6,6 → –[NH–(CH₂)₆–NH–CO–(CH₂)₄–CO]–.
- ✅ KTU standard answer length: 1 line for definitions, 1 block for mechanisms, 1 step for every numerical substitution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: polyester
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** polyester

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Polyester

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polyester** is a category of high-molecular-weight polymers (synthetic or bio-based) whose repeating backbone units are linked together by **ester functional groups (–COO–)**. The IUPAC-recommended nomenclature describes polyesters as polymers formed by the condensation (step-growth) polymerization of **dicarboxylic acids (or their derivatives like acid chlorides/anhydrides)** with **dihydric alcohols (diols/glycols)**, or alternatively through **self-condensation of hydroxy acids** and **ring-opening polymerization (ROP) of lactones** (e.g., ε-caprolactone → polycaprolactone).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (PCPOL607 - Module Experiment 2):**
> *Polyesters are condensation polymers characterized by the presence of the ester linkage (–C(=O)–O–) in the main chain, formed by the reaction between –COOH and –OH groups with elimination of water (or HCl in Schotten–Baumann variant). The most commercially significant polyester is **Poly(Ethylene Terephthalate) [PET]** with repeat unit –[O–CH₂–CH₂–O–CO–C₆H₄–CO]–.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **train made of identical passenger cars**:
- Each **passenger car** is an ester linkage (–COO–).
- The **coupling between two cars** is the condensation bond formed when an acid (–COOH) loses –OH and an alcohol (–OH) loses –H, producing **H₂O** as the "steam" that escapes.
- When thousands of these cars link end-to-end, the train becomes a long, tough, flexible chain — the **polyester macromolecule**.

In daily life, the **PET water bottle** you hold, the **Dacron shirt** you wear, the **Mylar snack wrapper**, and the **unbreakable car tire reinforcement cord** are all polyester materials engineered to different specifications of the same chemistry.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

> [!NOTE]
> - **Bond dissociation energy of C–O (ester):** ≈ **358 kJ/mol**
> - **Bond dissociation energy of C=O (carbonyl):** ≈ **749 kJ/mol**
> - **Standard enthalpy of polyesterification:** ≈ **–20 kJ/mol** (mildly exothermic, per ester linkage)
> - **Carothers' equation constant (p = extent of reaction):** $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$
> - **PET glass transition temperature (T_g):** ≈ **75 °C (amorphous), 81 °C (crystalline)**
> - **PET melting temperature (T_m):** ≈ **260 °C**
> - **PET density (crystalline):** ≈ **1.455 g/cm³**
> - **Intrinsic viscosity marker (IV) for bottle-grade PET:** **0.70 – 0.85 dL/g**

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot (KTU Syllabus Mapping)

| Class | Example | Repeat Unit Backbone | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Saturated (Thermoplastic)** | PET, PBT, PTT | Aromatic + Aliphatic | Melt-processable, recyclable |
| **Unsaturated (Thermosetting)** | UP resin (alkyd) | Contains C=C fumarate | Crosslinkable with styrene |
| **Aliphatic (Biodegradable)** | PLA, PCL, PHB | Fully aliphatic | Hydrolyzable, biomedical |
| **Aromatic (High-Performance)** | Vectran® (LCP) | All-aromatic | Liquid-crystalline, ultra-strong |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-Growth Polymerization Logic — The Carothers Framework

Polyesterification is the **textbook example of step-growth (condensation) polymerization**. The mechanism proceeds as follows:

1. **Initiation (Monomer Collision):** A –COOH of molecule A and an –OH of molecule B approach within Van der Waals contact (≈ 3–4 Å).
2. **Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution:** The lone pair of the alcohol oxygen attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the carboxylic acid, forming a tetrahedral intermediate.
3. **Proton Transfer & Elimination:** A proton migrates from the oxonium to one of the original hydroxyl oxygens, regenerating a neutral –OH on the new oxygen.
4. **Dehydration:** This –OH leaves as **water (H₂O)**, leaving behind the **ester linkage –C(=O)–O–**.
5. **Propagation:** Every monomer, dimer, trimer, …, n-mer still carries –COOH and –OH end groups, so the reaction continues *between any two species* (not just monomer-to-monomer).
6. **Termination (Kinetic, not chemical):** Ends vanish when one functional group is consumed (stoichiometric imbalance) or when the system vitrifies / viscosity prevents further encounters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carothers' Law (1929):** In step-growth polymerization, high molecular weight is **only achievable at very high conversion** (p). To obtain $\overline{X}_n = 100$, the required conversion is **p = 0.99 (99 %)**. This is the central reason why polyester synthesis demands rigorous stoichiometry and efficient byproduct (water) removal.

## 2.2 Reaction Schemes Covered in PCPOL607 Module Experiment 2

### Scheme A — Direct Esterification (DA + DI)
$$\text{HOOC–R–COOH} \; + \; \text{HO–R'–OH} \;\xrightarrow{\;\Delta,\;\text{cat.}\;}\; \text{–[OC–R–COO–R'–O]–}_n \; + \; 2n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Catalyst: **p-toluenesulfonic acid (PTSA), H₂SO₄, or Ti(OBu)₄** (the latter for high-temperature direct esterification of PET).

### Scheme B — Schotten–Baumann (Acid Chloride Route)
$$\text{ClOC–R–COCl} \; + \; \text{HO–R'–OH} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{base (Et}_3\text{N)}\;}\; \text{polyester} \; + \; 2n\,\text{HCl}$$
Advantage: occurs at room temperature in solution; ideal for *laboratory-scale preparation* (this is the route students perform in PCPOL607 Module Experiment 2 — adipoyl chloride + ethylene glycol → poly(ethylene adipate)).

### Scheme C — Trans-esterification
$$\text{CH}_3\text{OOC–R–COOCH}_3 \; + \; \text{HO–R'–OH} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{cat.}\;}\; \text{polyester} \; + \; 2n\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$$
Industrial relevance: the **first step of PET manufacture from dimethyl terephthalate (DMT) + ethylene glycol**, using Mn, Zn, or Ti catalysts at 150–200 °C, followed by polycondensation at 270–290 °C under high vacuum.

### Scheme D — Ring-Opening Polymerization (ROP) of Lactones
$$\text{Lactone (e.g., ε-caprolactone)} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{Sn(Oct)}_2,\;\Delta\;}\; \text{polyester (e.g., PCL)} $$
This is the cleanest method for **aliphatic, biodegradable polyesters** and is featured in PCPOL607 advanced modules.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Number-average DP, conversion p | Carothers (stoichiometric balance) |
| 2 | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | Stoichiometric imbalance r = N_A/N_B | Carothers–Flory (r ≠ 1 case) |
| 3 | $\overline{X}_w = \dfrac{1+p}{1-p}$ | Weight-average DP | Polydispersity index = $\overline{X}_w/\overline{X}_n$ |
| 4 | $\overline{DP}_{\text{limit}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p_{\text{eq}})}$ | Equilibrium step-growth | Used to justify vacuum removal of H₂O |
| 5 | $K_{eq} = \dfrac{[Ester][H_2O]}{[COOH][OH]}$ | Equilibrium constant | Predicts when byproduct removal is mandatory |
| 6 | $M_n = M_0 \cdot \overline{X}_n$ | $M_0$ = repeat-unit MW | Predict MFI / IV from synthesis data |
| 7 | $\eta_{sp}/c = [\eta] + k_H[\eta]^2 c$ | Huggins equation | Determine $[\eta]$ for PET in phenol/TCE |
| 8 | Mark–Houwink: $[\eta] = K M^a$ | K, a = polymer–solvent constants | Compute M_v for PET: K = 7.44×10⁻⁴, a = 0.648 (o-chlorophenol, 25 °C) |
| 9 | Flory–Fox: $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K_{FF}}{\overline{X}_n}$ | End-group effect on T_g | Explains low-M_w PET's reduced T_g |
| 10 | Avrami: $X_c(t) = 1 - e^{-k t^n}$ | Crystallinity vs time | Predicts PET bottle preform cooling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark–Houwink constants for PET** (per ASTM D4603): $K = 7.44 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{dL/g}$, $a = 0.648$ in o-chlorophenol at **25 °C**. The intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$ is reported in dL/g and is the **standard quality-control parameter** for bottle-grade PET.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Polyester Used | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|
| Packaging | PET | Carbonated drink bottles, food trays |
| Textiles | PET (Dacron), PBT | Apparel, seat belts, fire-hose jackets |
| Automotive | PBT, UP-resin | Connectors, dashboards, body panels |
| Electronics | PEN, LCP-polyester | Flexible printed circuits, 5G substrates |
| Biomedical | PLA, PCL, PGA | Sutures, drug-eluting stents, tissue scaffolds |
| Construction | UP-resin + glass fibre | FRP doors, rebar, roofing sheets |
| Recycling | r-PET | Bottle-to-bottle, fibre-to-fibre circular economy |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Carothers' Equation for Number-Average Degree of Polymerization

### Statement
For a step-growth (condensation) polymerization between bifunctional monomers A–A and B–B (or A–B), where $N_0$ is the initial number of monomer molecules, derive the relationship $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the extent of reaction (p).**
At time *t*, the fraction of functional groups that have reacted is *p*. Therefore, the number of functional groups that have *not* yet reacted is $(1-p)$.

**Step 2 — Count the unreacted molecules.**
Each monomer initially has 2 functional groups. After a time when the extent of reaction is *p*, the *number of molecules remaining with unreacted ends* is $N = N_0(1-p)$, because for every bond formed, one molecule is "consumed" (two ends joined into one chain).

**Step 3 — Formulate the number-average degree of polymerization.**
By definition, the number-average degree of polymerization is the *ratio of initial molecules to final molecules*:
$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{N_0}{N} \\
&= \dfrac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} \\
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{1}{1-p}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at typical KTU-required conversions.**
- For *p* = 0.95 (95 %): $\overline{X}_n$ = 1/(1 − 0.95) = **20**
- For *p* = 0.99 (99 %): $\overline{X}_n$ = 1/(1 − 0.99) = **100**
- For *p* = 0.999 (99.9 %): $\overline{X}_n$ = 1/(1 − 0.999) = **1000**

**Conclusion:** Industrially, PET is spun when $\overline{X}_n \approx$ 100–150, which demands *p* ≥ 0.99 — explaining the rigorous removal of methanol/water in the polycondensation reactor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carothers–Flory Equation for Non-Stoichiometric (r ≠ 1) Case

### Statement
When the bifunctional monomers A–A and B–B are mixed in a *non-equimolar* ratio, derive $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ where $r = N_A/N_B$ (the stoichiometric ratio, with $r \leq 1$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the initial conditions.**
Let $N_A$ = initial moles of A groups, $N_B$ = initial moles of B groups. By definition, $r = N_A/N_B$ and $N_A \leq N_B$ (so $r \leq 1$).

**Step 2 — Express the number of bonds formed at extent p.**
A total of $p \cdot N_A$ A-groups have reacted (since p is the fraction of the *minority* group consumed). Each bond consumes **one A and one B**, so the number of bonds formed is $N_{\text{bonds}} = p \cdot N_A$.

**Step 3 — Count the final molecules.**
The initial total number of molecules is $(N_A + N_B)/2$. The number of newly formed bonds is $p \cdot N_A$. The number of final molecules is:
$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{N_A + N_B}{2} - p \cdot N_A \\
  &= \dfrac{N_A(1 + 1/r)}{2} - p \cdot N_A \\
  &= \dfrac{N_A}{2} \left( \dfrac{1+r}{r} - 2p \right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Formulate $\overline{X}_n$ and simplify.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{N_0}{N} = \dfrac{(N_A+N_B)/2}{N} \\
&= \dfrac{N_A(1+r)/(2r)}{N_A\bigl[(1+r)/(2r) - p\bigr]} \\
&= \dfrac{(1+r)/2r}{(1+r)/(2r) - p} \\
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Boundary check.**
For r = 1 (perfect stoichiometry): $\overline{X}_n = 2/(2 - 2p) = 1/(1-p)$ → recovers the Carothers equation. ✓

**Step 6 — Industrial implication.**
A 1 mol % excess of one monomer (r = 0.99) caps the achievable $\overline{X}_n$ at $1.99/(1.99 - 1.98p)$. At p = 0.99, $\overline{X}_n$ = 1.99 / (1.99 − 1.9602) = 1.99/0.0298 ≈ **66.8** instead of 100 — a 33 % drop. This is the practical reason KTU examiners expect you to mention **rigorous stoichiometry and side-reaction control** in any 14-mark synthesis question.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equilibrium Constant of Polyesterification

### Statement
For a reversible polyesterification with equilibrium constant $K_{eq}$, show that the **molecular weight is determined by the residual water concentration**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the elementary equilibrium.**
For the reversible step: –COOH + HO– ⇌ –COO– + H₂O
$$\begin{aligned}
K_{eq} = \dfrac{[\text{ester}][\text{H}_2\text{O}]}{[\text{COOH}][\text{OH}]}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply stoichiometric simplification.**
If equimolar and at high conversion, the residual $[\text{COOH}] = [\text{OH}] = [\text{End}]$, and $[\text{ester}]$ is essentially constant and large. Therefore:
$$K_{eq} \cdot [\text{End}]^2 = [\text{Ester}] \cdot [\text{H}_2\text{O}]$$

**Step 3 — Use the relationship $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$ and $[\text{End}] = 1/\overline{X}_n$ (in mole fraction units).**
For high conversion, $1-p \approx 1/\overline{X}_n$. Substituting:
$$K_{eq} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\overline{X}_n}\right)^2 \approx [\text{ester}] \cdot [\text{H}_2\text{O}]$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for $\overline{X}_n$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &\approx \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{eq}}{[\text{ester}] \cdot [\text{H}_2\text{O}]}} \\
\overline{X}_n &\propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{n_{H_2O}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Industrial consequence.**
To double $\overline{X}_n$, the **water concentration must be reduced by a factor of four**. This is the engineering rationale for:
- The **vacuum polycondensation reactor** (operates at ≤ 1 mbar to strip water).
- The **horizontal finishing reactor with surface renewal** (Buss-type or LIST-type).
- The use of **molecular sieves in laboratory synthesis** to sequester water.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Method Comparison

| Method | Carothers (r=1) | Carothers–Flory (r≠1) | Equilibrium (K_eq) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Independent Variable** | p | p, r | $n_{H_2O}$ |
| **Limiting Factor** | Conversion only | Conversion + stoichiometric imbalance | Water removal rate |
| **Typical Use** | Lab teaching (KTU Module Exp. 2) | Industrial scale-up | High-M_w PET spinning |
| **Final M_n Range** | Up to ~25 000 g/mol | Up to ~40 000 g/mol | > 60 000 g/mol (textile grade) |
| **Pitfall** | Ignores cyclization | Ignores side reactions (e.g., diethylene glycol formation) | Assumes ideality |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** Adipic acid (0.50 mol) and ethylene glycol (0.51 mol) are reacted in the presence of Ti(OBu)₄ catalyst at 200 °C. Calculate (a) the value of *r*, (b) the maximum $\overline{X}_n$ achievable if the reaction goes to p = 0.98, and (c) the number-average molecular weight $M_n$ (repeat unit MW of poly(ethylene adipate) = 172.18 g/mol).

**Solution:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(a)}\quad r &= \dfrac{N_A}{N_B} = \dfrac{0.50}{0.51} = 0.9804 \\[4pt]
\text{(b)}\quad \overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp} = \dfrac{1.9804}{1.9804 - 2(0.9804)(0.98)} \\
                            &= \dfrac{1.9804}{1.9804 - 1.9216} = \dfrac{1.9804}{0.0588} \\
\overline{X}_n &\approx 33.7 \\[4pt]
\text{(c)}\quad M_n &= M_0 \cdot \overline{X}_n = 172.18 \times 33.7 \approx 5802\,\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Examiner's note:** A common error is to substitute *r* = 1.01 by mistake. Always take the ratio of the **minority group to the majority group** so that $r \leq 1$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Repeat-Unit Architecture of PET (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oxygen of Glycol] --> B[Ester Carbonyl C]
    B --> C[Carbonyl Oxygen double bond]
    B --> D[Para-substituted Benzene Ring]
    D --> E[Second Carbonyl C]
    E --> F[Second Carbonyl Oxygen]
    E --> G[Second Ester Oxygen]
    G --> H[CH2 CH2 Ethylene Segment]
    H --> I[Back to A for next repeat]
    A -.-> A
    classDef aromatic fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef aliphatic fill:#FFF7E0,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef link fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    class D aromatic
    class A,B,C,E,F,G,H,I link
```

**Interpretation:** The aromatic terephthalate block provides **rigidity and high T_g**, while the aliphatic ethylene glycol block provides **flexibility and melt-processability**.

## 4.2 Polyesterification Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedA[DA Feed: Terephthalic Acid] --> mix[Slurry Mixer with EG]
    feedB[EG Feed: Ethylene Glycol] --> mix
    mix --> est[Direct Esterification Reactor 240 to 260 C]
    est --> byp1[Water + Methanol Vapor Outlet]
    est --> prep[Bis hydroxyethyl terephthalate BHET]
    prep --> pc[Polycondensation Reactor 270 to 290 C 1 mbar vacuum]
    pc --> byp2[EG Vapor to Recovery Column]
    pc --> melt[Polymer Melt IV 0.62 dL per g]
    melt --> spin[Spinneret for Filament]
    melt --> preform[Injection Molding for Preform]
    spin --> fiber[PET Fiber Dacron]
    preform --> bottle[Bottle Blowing PET]
    classDef reactor fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef product fill:#E5FFE5,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef feed fill:#E5E5FF,stroke:#000099,color:#000
    class est,pc reactor
    class fiber,bottle product
    class feedA,feedB,prep,melt feed
```

**Interpretation:** This is a two-stage industrial PET process: **low-temperature esterification** followed by **high-temperature polycondensation under vacuum** — exactly mirroring the KTU Module Experiment 2 sequence of "ester formation → chain extension."

## 4.3 Polymerization Kinetics — Conversion vs. Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 Early Reaction 0 to 60 min]
        m1[All monomers dimers trimers] --> p1[Small oligomers]
    end
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2 Mid Reaction 60 to 180 min]
        p1 --> p2[Oligomers 5 to 50 units]
    end
    subgraph Stage3[Stage 3 Late Reaction 180 to 360 min]
        p2 --> p3[High polymer DP 100 plus]
    end
    p3 --> p4[Engineered M n for spinning 20000 to 30000 g per mol]
    classDef stage fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333333,color:#000
    class m1,p1,p2,p3,p4 stage
```

**Interpretation:** This *temporal cascade* is the foundation of the **Carothers sigmoidal M_n vs. p curve** and explains why reactors are designed as long, narrow tubes (plug-flow) to maximize residence time for late-stage molecular-weight build-up.

## 4.4 Carothers Curve — Visualizing the M_n–p Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisP[Conversion p on x axis] --> curve1[Low p 0 to 0.9 M n less than 10]
    axisP --> curve2[Mid p 0.9 to 0.99 M n 10 to 100]
    axisP --> curve3[High p 0.99 to 0.999 M n 100 to 1000]
    axisP --> curve4[Asymptote p tends to 1 M n tends to infinity]
    classDef region fill:#FFFAE5,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    class curve1,curve2,curve3,curve4 region
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away:** The Carothers curve is **asymptotic**, not linear — this is why polyesters require **>99 % conversion** for engineering-grade molecular weight, and why **stoichiometric balance** and **water removal** are non-negotiable.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "polyester" with a suitable example. Write the repeat unit structure of PET.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Polyesters are condensation polymers whose backbone is composed of repeating ester linkages (–COO–), formed by the reaction between dibasic acids and diols (or their functional derivatives) with elimination of a small molecule such as water or HCl.

*Example:* **Poly(Ethylene Terephthalate) — PET**, with the repeat unit:

$$\text{–[O–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–CO–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO]–}_n$$

The reaction is typically carried out by direct esterification of **terephthalic acid (TPA)** with **ethylene glycol (EG)** at 240–260 °C, followed by polycondensation at 270–290 °C under high vacuum, yielding PET with $\overline{X}_n$ ≈ 100–150 suitable for fibres and bottles.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between saturated and unsaturated polyesters with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Saturated Polyester | Unsaturated Polyester |
|---|---|---|
| **Backbone** | Only single bonds (sp³/sp²) | Contains C=C double bonds (from maleic/fumaric acid) |
| **Crosslinking** | Thermoplastic — melt-processable | Thermosetting — crosslinks with vinyl monomer (styrene) |
| **Example** | **PET, PBT, PTT** | **Unsaturated polyester (UP) resin** (alkyd) |
| **Application** | Bottles, fibres, films | FRP composites, boat hulls, electrical insulation |
| **Recyclability** | Easily melt-recyclable | Cannot be remelted; chemical recycling only |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A:**
> Students frequently write *only one example* for each category and lose the application/distinguishing column. The KTU marking scheme requires *example + at least one distinguishing property + one application* for full 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module Experiment 2]`

**(a)** With a neat reaction scheme, explain the **step-growth polymerization of poly(ethylene terephthalate)** by the **direct esterification route**. Discuss the role of catalyst, temperature, and byproduct removal in achieving high molecular weight. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyester is synthesized from **0.30 mol adipic acid** and **0.30 mol ethylene glycol** with **Ti(OBu)₄ catalyst** at 200 °C. At a reaction time of 4 hours, the residual acid number is found to be **17.5 mg KOH/g of polymer**. Calculate: **(i)** the extent of reaction *p*, **(ii)** the number-average degree of polymerization $\overline{X}_n$, and **(iii)** the number-average molecular weight $M_n$ (repeat unit of poly(ethylene adipate) = 172.18 g/mol). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part A (a)

**Step 1 — Write the reaction scheme.**

Direct esterification of **terephthalic acid (TPA)** with **ethylene glycol (EG)**:
$$\text{HOOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} + 2\,\text{HO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–OH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–OOC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COO–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The intermediate **bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET)** then undergoes self-condensation to yield PET:
$$n\,\text{BHET} \;\xrightarrow{\;\Delta,\;\text{cat.}\;}\; \text{–[O–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–OC–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CO]–}_n + n\,\text{HO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–OH}$$

**Step 2 — Discuss the role of catalyst.**
- **Catalyst used:** antimony trioxide (Sb₂O₃) — 200–300 ppm, or titanium(IV) butoxide Ti(OBu)₄ for laboratory.
- **Role:** lowers the activation energy of the acyl substitution step, increases the rate of esterification and trans-esterification by a factor of 10³ to 10⁴, and is essential because the heterogeneous TPA/EG system has a low collision frequency without catalytic activation of the carbonyl carbon.

**Step 3 — Discuss the role of temperature.**
- **Esterification stage:** 240–260 °C, 1.0–3.5 bar (above atmospheric to keep EG liquid).
- **Polycondensation stage:** 270–290 °C, ≤ 1 mbar vacuum.
- Higher temperature increases $k$ (rate constant) and reduces melt viscosity, but excessive T (> 300 °C) causes **thermal degradation** (chain scission, acetaldehyde generation, yellowing).

**Step 4 — Discuss the role of byproduct (water/EG) removal.**
From the equilibrium analysis: $\overline{X}_n \propto 1/\sqrt{n_{H_2O}}$. To achieve $\overline{X}_n = 100$, the reactor must reduce the water partial pressure to **< 5 mbar** at 280 °C, achieved by:
- **Vacuum pump** (steam ejector or liquid-ring).
- **Surface renewal** in the horizontal finishing reactor (Luwesta, LIST, or Zimmer disc-ring design).
- **EG reflux column** to recover and recycle EG.

**Conclusion:** A combination of (i) Sb₂O₃ catalyst, (ii) staged T-profile, and (iii) deep vacuum polycondensation is the canonical recipe for bottle-grade PET with $[\eta]$ = 0.80 dL/g.

#### Model Solution to Part A (b)

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{acid, initial}}$ = 0.30 mol, $N_{\text{EG, initial}}$ = 0.30 mol.
- Acid number (AN) at 4 h = **17.5 mg KOH/g polymer**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the fraction of unreacted –COOH groups from acid number.**
The acid number measures the residual –COOH. Converting:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{AN} &= 17.5\,\text{mg KOH/g} = 17.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{g KOH/g} \\
        &= \dfrac{17.5 \times 10^{-3}}{56.11}\,\text{mol KOH/g} \\
        &= 3.12 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol –COOH per g polymer}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate the initial acid concentration per gram of polymer (assuming equimolar feed and no mass loss, approximated for a stoichiometric poly(ethylene adipate) prep with the given conditions).**
The total polymer mass is approximately $M_{\text{total}} = 0.30 \times M_{\text{adipic}} + 0.30 \times M_{\text{EG}} = 0.30 \times 146.14 + 0.30 \times 62.07 = 43.84 + 18.62 = 62.46$ g at the *start* — but for a fair comparison, we use the textbook assumption: a reference poly(ethylene adipate) prep with M₀ = 172.18 g/mol and one –COOH per repeat unit.
Initial acid capacity if *all* ends were free: $1/M_0$ mol COOH per g = $1/172.18$ = $5.808 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/g.

**Step 3 — Calculate (1 − p) and p.**
$$\begin{aligned}
1 - p &= \dfrac{\text{residual mol –COOH per g}}{\text{initial mol –COOH per g}} = \dfrac{3.12 \times 10^{-4}}{5.808 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0537 \\
p &= 1 - 0.0537 = 0.9463
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $\overline{X}_n$ (r = 1, so simple Carothers).**
$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n &= \dfrac{1}{1-p} = \dfrac{1}{0.0537} \approx 18.6
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $M_n$.**
$$\begin{aligned}
M_n &= M_0 \cdot \overline{X}_n = 172.18 \times 18.6 \approx 3202\,\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** At p = 94.6 %, the polyester has a modest DP of ~19, suitable as a *prepolymer* for chain extension in a second-stage polycondensation (industrial practice).

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module Experiment 2]` *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of Schotten–Baumann polyesterification** between adipoyl chloride and ethylene glycol. Discuss the role of base (triethylamine) and the limitations of this method for high-M_w synthesis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **physical, thermal, and mechanical properties** of PET, PBT, and PLA polyesters in a tabular format, and justify why PET dominates the packaging industry. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part B (a)

**Step 1 — The mechanism (nucleophilic acyl substitution).**

Adipoyl chloride: ClOC–(CH₂)₄–COCl
