Ethylene glycol: HO–CH₂–CH₂–OH

**Stage 1 — Nucleophilic attack:** The lone pair of the alcohol oxygen attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of adipoyl chloride, forming a tetrahedral intermediate:
$$\text{HO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–OH} + \text{ClOC–R–COCl} \longrightarrow \text{HO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–C(OH)(Cl)–R–COCl}$$

**Stage 2 — Chloride elimination:** The Cl⁻ leaves, restoring the C=O double bond and forming the first ester linkage:
$$\longrightarrow \text{HO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–CO–R–COCl} + \text{HCl}$$

**Stage 3 — Repeat on the second acid chloride group:** Another EG molecule attacks, and a second ester linkage is formed:
$$\longrightarrow \text{HO–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–O–CO–R–CO–O–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–OH} + \text{HCl}$$

**Step 2 — Role of triethylamine (Et₃N).**
Et₃N is a **non-nucleophilic, sterically hindered base** that:
- Neutralizes the liberated HCl: $\text{Et}_3\text{N} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{Et}_3\text{NH}^+\text{Cl}^-$
- Prevents the acid from protonating the alcohol (which would render it non-nucleophilic and stop the reaction).
- Drives the equilibrium to the *right*, increasing the conversion.

**Step 3 — Limitations for high-M_w synthesis.**
- Acid chlorides are **expensive** (compared to free diacids).
- HCl generated can cause **side reactions** (degradation of acid-sensitive monomers).
- The reaction must be done in **anhydrous, aprotic solvents** (CH₂Cl₂, THF, toluene) — impractical at industrial scale.
- Maximum achievable $\overline{X}_n$ is typically limited to **30–50** due to side reactions and stoichiometric control difficulties.
- Therefore, this method is reserved for **laboratory syntheses and specialty polyesters** (e.g., aromatic polyesters, liquid-crystalline polymers), not bulk PET manufacture.

#### Model Solution to Part B (b)

| Property | **PET** | **PBT** | **PLA** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Repeat unit** | –[OCH₂CH₂O–CO–C₆H₄–CO]– | –[O(CH₂)₄O–CO–C₆H₄–CO]– | –[OCH(CH₃)CO]– |
| **T_g (°C)** | 75–81 | 50–55 | 55–60 |
| **T_m (°C)** | 255–265 | 220–225 | 150–170 |
| **Density (g/cm³)** | 1.38 (amorphous) / 1.45 (crystal) | 1.31 | 1.24 |
| **Tensile strength (MPa)** | 55–75 | 50–60 | 50–70 |
| **Modulus (GPa)** | 2.8–4.1 | 2.0–2.5 | 3.0–4.0 |
| **Crystallization rate** | Moderate | **Very fast** | Slow |
| **Hydrolytic stability** | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (hydrolyzable) |
| **Transparency** | Excellent (amorphous) | Opaque | Excellent |
| **Cost (per kg, 2024)** | ~₹90–110 | ~₹180–220 | ~₹200–280 |
| **Recyclability** | Excellent (r-PET stream) | Limited | Compostable, not recyclable |
| **Primary use** | Bottles, fibres | Connectors, automotive | Biodegradable packaging, 3-D printing |

**Justification for PET dominance in packaging:**

PET dominates the packaging industry because of a unique combination of:
1. **High T_g (≈ 80 °C)** — withstands hot-fill and pasteurization.
2. **Optical clarity** — critical for beverage bottles.
3. **Excellent barrier to CO₂ and O₂** at thicknesses ≥ 250 µm.
4. **Low cost (₹90–110/kg)** — cheaper than PBT, PLA, or PTT.
5. **Mature recycling stream (r-PET)** — supports circular-economy mandates.
6. **High modulus (≈ 4 GPa)** — enables thin-wall lightweighting.
7. **Food-contact regulatory clearance (US FDA, EFSA, FSSAI)** — non-toxic monomers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (14 marks):**
> - **Mechanism arrows:** Failing to draw the *lone pair attack* and *tetrahedral intermediate* loses **2–3 marks** outright. Always show the curved arrow from the alcohol O lone pair to the carbonyl C, and the Cl⁻ leaving.
> - **Units and significant figures:** Writing $M_n$ = "3202.4" without specifying "g/mol" is penalized.
> - **Carothers equation misuse:** Forgetting the r ≠ 1 form when the monomers are non-equimolar is a **favourite trap question**.
> - **Justification of PET dominance:** Writing *only "PET is cheap"* without listing 3–4 quantified properties loses the application-marks portion (typically 2 of 7 marks).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Polyester = **–COO–** linked polymer, made by **step-growth (condensation)** polymerization.
- ✅ Two industrial routes: **Direct esterification (TPA + EG)** and **Trans-esterification (DMT + EG)**.
- ✅ Laboratory route: **Schotten–Baumann (acid chloride + diol + Et₃N)**.
- ✅ **PET** is the flagship polyester — used in bottles (35 % of global PET market), fibres (60 %), films (5 %).
- ✅ **Carothers' equation:** $\overline{X}_n = 1/(1-p)$; **Carothers–Flory:** $\overline{X}_n = (1+r)/(1+r-2rp)$.
- ✅ Achieving high $\overline{X}_n$ requires **p ≥ 0.99** and **r → 1** (strict stoichiometry).
- ✅ **Equilibrium analysis:** $\overline{X}_n \propto 1/\sqrt{n_{H_2O}}$ → mandates **vacuum polycondensation** and **EG stripping**.
- ✅ **T_g of PET ≈ 80 °C; T_m ≈ 260 °C; crystalline density = 1.455 g/cm³.**
- ✅ **Bottle-grade PET** is defined by **IV = 0.70–0.85 dL/g** in o-chlorophenol at 25 °C.
- ✅ **Catalysts:** Sb₂O₃ (industrial), Ti(OBu)₄ (lab), Zn/Mn acetates (DMT route).
- ✅ **Saturated vs Unsaturated:** PET is thermoplastic and recyclable; UP resin is thermosetting and crosslinks with styrene.
- ✅ **Biodegradable variants:** PLA, PCL, PHB, PHBV — increasingly important for **single-use plastics regulations (EU SUP Directive 2019/904)**.
- ✅ **PET is recyclable** through *mechanical* (flake → preform) and *chemical* (glycolysis, methanolysis, hydrolysis) routes — closed-loop **bottle-to-bottle** technology achieves r-PET content > 50 % in major brands.
- ✅ **Mark–Houwink for PET** (o-chlorophenol, 25 °C): $K = 7.44 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{dL/g}$, $a = 0.648$.
- ✅ **Avrami equation** predicts PET crystallinity during bottle-blow preform cooling — controls the balance of clarity vs. barrier.
- ✅ For **14-mark exam answers**, *always* include: (i) reaction scheme with **balanced stoichiometry**, (ii) catalyst + T + vacuum discussion, (iii) Carothers calculation with **explicit p and r values**, (iv) one industrial application of the resulting M_n range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Scheme Reminder:**
> Module Experiment 2 of PCPOL607 expects you to *physically perform* the synthesis of an unsaturated polyester or poly(ethylene adipate) in the lab, characterize it by **acid number, saponification number, and intrinsic viscosity**, and present results in the prescribed lab-report format. The numerical problems in this topic are typically **direct applications of the Carothers and Carothers–Flory equations** and the **equilibrium K_eq** analysis. Practice all three derivation paths before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: acrylic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - Polymer Chemistry Laboratory
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Synthesis and Characterization of Acrylic Polymer (PMMA by Free Radical Polymerization)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Acrylic polymers** are a versatile family of thermoplastic polymers derived from monomers containing the acryloyl group (CH₂=CH–COOR). The most industrially significant member of this family is **Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA)**, synthesized from the monomer **methyl methacrylate (MMA)**. PMMA is an amorphous, linear addition polymer formed via **free-radical chain-growth polymerization** of the vinyl double bond.

The general chemical structure of an acrylic polymer is:

$$[ -CH_2 - CR_1(R_2) - ]_n$$

where the side group (–COOR) is the characteristic **ester functionality** that defines the acrylic family.

### 1.2 Key Monomer: Methyl Methacrylate (MMA)

- **IUPAC Name:** Methyl 2-methylprop-2-enoate
- **Molecular Formula:** C₅H₈O₂
- **Molecular Weight:** **100.12 g/mol**
- **Density:** **0.94 g/cm³** at 25 °C
- **Boiling Point:** **101 °C**
- **Refractive Index:** **1.414**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Plastic Glass" Analogy**
>
> Think of acrylic (PMMA) as **"transparent aluminium"** — a material that looks like glass but behaves like a tough plastic. If glass is a brittle, heavy crystal (silica network), PMMA is a long chain of identical LEGO blocks (the –CH₂–C(CH₃)(COOCH₃)– unit) locked together. Each block has a bulky methyl (–CH₃) and an ester (–COOCH₃) side group, like a backpack. Because these backpacks prevent the chains from sliding past each other or crystallizing, the polymer is **optically clear, rigid, and glassy** — but unlike glass, it can be cut with a saw and bent when warmed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PCPOL607):**
> The experiment is designed to demonstrate **bulk (or solution) free-radical polymerization** of an acrylic monomer using a thermal initiator such as **benzoyl peroxide (BPO)** or **azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN)**, followed by characterization (yield, melting/softening point, molecular weight, IR spectroscopy).

### 1.4 Engineering & Industrial Significance

- **Aerospace:** Aircraft cabin windows and canopies (transparency + impact resistance).
- **Automotive:** Tail-light lenses, instrument panels.
- **Biomedical:** Bone cement (PMMA), intraocular lenses, dentures.
- **Optoelectronics:** LED light guides, optical fibres.
- **Architecture:** Skylights, sound barriers (density = **1.18 g/cm³** — half that of glass).
- **Adhesives & Coatings:** Poly(acrylic acid), poly(ethyl acrylate) for water-based emulsions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Free-Radical Polymerization (Chain-Growth)

The polymerization of MMA proceeds through the classical **three-stage radical chain mechanism**.

#### Stage 1 — Initiation
1. **Decomposition** of initiator (I–I) into two primary radicals (I•).
2. **Addition** of the primary radical to the vinyl double bond of MMA, generating the chain-initiating monomer radical (M₁•).

#### Stage 2 — Propagation
The chain radical (M•) successively adds monomer units, with the radical site migrating to the chain end after each addition. This continues hundreds to thousands of times, producing a macroradical (Mₙ•).

#### Stage 3 — Termination
Two macroradicals combine or disproportionate, ending the chain growth.

- **Combination:** Two growing chains couple head-to-head, forming a single dead polymer chain.
- **Disproportionation:** A hydrogen atom transfers from one chain to the other, producing two dead polymer chains (one saturated, one unsaturated).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why an initiator?** The C=C π-bond, although reactive, is kinetically stable at room temperature. A radical or ionic species is required to overcome the activation barrier (Eₐ ≈ **80–95 kJ/mol** for radical initiators).
- **Why free-radical, not ionic?** MMA is an electron-poor alkene (due to the ester carbonyl). Free-radical polymerization tolerates both electron-rich and electron-poor monomers — making it the universal industrial route.
- **Why bulk polymerization for the lab?** It is **solvent-free**, simple, and produces high-molecular-weight, optically pure PMMA — ideal for demonstrating polymerization kinetics.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Equation** | **Symbols & Units** | **Boundary / Typical Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rate of polymerization | $R_p = k_p \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2} [M]$ | $R_p$ = mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹; $[I], [M]$ = mol/L; $f$ = efficiency (0.5–0.8) | Steady-state radical concentration |
| Kinetic chain length | $\nu = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2(f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}$ | Dimensionless | 10² – 10⁴ |
| Number-average DP | $\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i + R_{tm}} = \dfrac{[M]_0 - [M]}{[I]\,(1 - e^{-k_d t}) + \sum \text{chain transfers}}$ | Dimensionless | 1000–10000 for PMMA |
| Carothers' conversion | $p = \dfrac{\text{weight of polymer formed}}{\text{weight of monomer charged}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Practical: 80–95 % |
| Dispersity (Đ) | $\mathrm{Ð} = \dfrac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n}$ | Dimensionless | Free-radical: 1.5–2.0 (theoretical = 1.5) |
| Viscosity-average MW (Mark-Houwink) | $[\eta] = K\,M_v^{a}$ | $[\eta]$ = dL/g; $K = 0.0043$; $a = 0.80$ for PMMA in chloroform at 25 °C | Used for intrinsic viscosity method |
| Tacticity effect on Tg | $T_g (\text{atactic PMMA}) \approx 105\,°C$ | Kelvin or °C | Syndiotactic > Atactic > Isotactic Tg |
| Heat of polymerization (MMA) | $\Delta H_p \approx -56\,\text{kJ/mol}$ | Exothermic | Removes 56 kJ per mole of C=C opened |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> The expression for $R_p$ — *first order in monomer, half order in initiator* — is the single most asked derivation in this module. The half-order originates from the steady-state assumption on radical concentration: $[M•]_{ss} = \sqrt{f k_d [I]/k_t}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The kinetics above govern **reactor design** in industry. For example, the half-order dependence on initiator concentration means that to double the molecular weight of PMMA, the initiator concentration must be *quartered*. This is exploited industrially in the casting of **PMMA sheets** for aircraft windows, where low initiator and careful cooling prevent runaway exotherms (the **Trommsdorff / gel effect**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rate Law for Free-Radical Polymerization

We use the steady-state approximation (SSA) on the radical intermediate [M•].

**Step 1 — Write the three elementary rates.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_d &= 2 f k_d [I] \quad &&\text{(initiator decomposition)} \\
R_i &= R_d \quad &&\text{(rate of initiation, assuming fast radical capture)} \\
R_p &= k_p [M][M•] \quad &&\text{(propagation)} \\
R_t &= 2 k_t [M•]^2 \quad &&\text{(termination, two radicals consumed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state approximation on [M•].**

The radical concentration is tiny and approximately constant. Its net rate of change = 0.

$$
\dfrac{d[M•]}{dt} = R_i - R_t = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for [M•].**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 f k_d [I] &= 2 k_t [M•]^2 \\
[M•]_{ss} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the rate of polymerization.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p = k_p [M] \cdot [M•]_{ss} = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite in the canonical textbook form.**

$$
\boxed{R_p = k_p \left(\dfrac{f k_d}{k_t}\right)^{1/2} [I]^{1/2} [M]}
$$

**Conversion logic of each step:**
- *Step 1 → Step 2:* We translate the chemistry into mathematical rate expressions. Each $k$ is the rate constant, and the concentration terms reflect the molecularity of each elementary step.
- *Step 2:* SSA is valid because radicals are highly reactive and short-lived. Their generation rate equals their consumption rate.
- *Step 3:* Algebraically isolate the radical concentration. The square root arises because termination is second-order in [M•].
- *Step 4–5:* Plug [M•] into the propagation rate to obtain the observable rate.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Number-Average Degree of Polymerization (X̄ₙ)

**Step 1 — Define X̄ₙ as the number of monomer units consumed per chain formed.**

$$
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{\text{rate of monomer consumption}}{\text{rate of chain termination}}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the two rates explicitly.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Monomer consumption rate} &= R_p = k_p [M][M•] \\
\text{Chain termination rate} &= R_t = 2 k_t [M•]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio and simplify using $[M•]_{ss}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_t} = \dfrac{k_p [M][M•]}{2 k_t [M•]^2} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 k_t [M•]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the SSA result for [M•].**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 k_t \sqrt{f k_d [I]/k_t}} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t)^{1/2} [I]^{1/2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final canonical form.**

$$
\boxed{\overline{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t)^{1/2} [I]^{1/2}}}
$$

**Conversion logic of each step:**
- The numerator counts *how many monomers are eaten per unit time*, while the denominator counts *how many chains are killed per unit time*. Their ratio is the *average chain length*.
- The same SSA substitution reveals an inverse square-root dependence on initiator: more initiator → more chains → shorter chains. This is the **industrial lever** for molecular-weight control.
- For chain-transfer dominated systems, an additional term is added in the denominator, but it is **not required for KTU PCPOL607 level**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — Yield and Molecular Weight Estimation

A student performs bulk polymerization of **10.0 g MMA** with **0.10 g benzoyl peroxide (BPO)** at 80 °C for 2 hours. After precipitation in methanol, **8.6 g of dry PMMA** is obtained.

**Step 1 — Moles of monomer charged.**

$$
n_{MMA} = \dfrac{10.0\,\text{g}}{100.12\,\text{g/mol}} = 0.0999\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of initiator charged.**

$$
n_{BPO} = \dfrac{0.10\,\text{g}}{242.23\,\text{g/mol}} = 4.13 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical percent conversion (yield).**

$$
\%\, \text{Conversion} = \dfrac{8.6\,\text{g}}{10.0\,\text{g}} \times 100 = 86\,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Moles of PMMA formed (each chain is one molecule).**

$$
n_{PMMA} = \dfrac{8.6\,\text{g}}{M_n}
$$

**Step 5 — If intrinsic viscosity gives $[\eta] = 0.62$ dL/g, find Mᵥ using Mark-Houwink.**

For PMMA in chloroform at 25 °C: $K = 4.3 \times 10^{-5}$ dL/g, $a = 0.80$ (note: alternate tabulated value $K = 0.0043$ with exponent $0.80$ in acetone may be used — students must cite the correct solvent).

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\eta] &= K M_v^a \\
0.62 &= 4.3 \times 10^{-5} \cdot M_v^{0.80} \\
M_v^{0.80} &= \dfrac{0.62}{4.3 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.44 \times 10^{4} \\
M_v &= (1.44 \times 10^{4})^{1/0.80} = (1.44 \times 10^{4})^{1.25}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(1.44 \times 10^4) &= 4.158 \\
1.25 \times 4.158 &= 5.198 \\
M_v &= 10^{5.198} \approx 1.58 \times 10^{5}\,\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Approximate $\overline{M}_n$ assuming $\overline{M}_w / \overline{M}_n \approx 1.8$ (typical for free-radical PMMA).**

$$
\overline{M}_n \approx \dfrac{1.58 \times 10^5}{1.8} \approx 8.8 \times 10^{4}\,\text{g/mol}
$$

**Step 8 — Number of moles of PMMA chains formed.**

$$
n_{PMMA} = \dfrac{8.6}{8.8 \times 10^{4}} = 9.77 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 9 — Kinetic chain length (Sanity Check).**

$$
\nu = \dfrac{0.0999 \times 0.86}{9.77 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 880
$$

A value of ~880 is consistent with a clean bulk polymerization of MMA at 80 °C. ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why PMMA Behaves the Way It Does

| **Property** | **Underlying Structural Reason** | **Practical Consequence** |
|---|---|---|
| Optical clarity (>92 % transmission) | Amorphous; bulky –COOCH₃ and –CH₃ prevent crystallization | Aircraft windows, lenses |
| High $T_g \approx 105\,°C$ | Restricted backbone rotation due to α-methyl + ester | Serviceable up to ~95 °C |
| Moderate strength (σ ≈ 65–80 MPa) | Long, entangled chains with dipole–dipole ester interactions | Load-bearing transparent parts |
| Soluble in acetone, chloroform, THF | Weak polar interactions; no crosslinks | Solution-cast films, adhesives |
| UV degradation | Ester side group absorbs UV; backbone scission | Yellowing outdoors — needs UV stabilizer |
| Thermal depolymerization to MMA at >300 °C | "Unzipping" chain reaction (ceiling temperature effect) | Recycling by pyrolysis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall Lab Workflow for Acrylic Polymer Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactor Setup: Three-neck RBF with stirrer, condenser, N2 inlet] --> B[Charge liquid MMA monomer]
    B --> C[Weigh benzoyl peroxide or AIBN initiator]
    C --> D[Purging with inert N2 gas for 10 min]
    D --> E[Heat to 75-85 C in oil bath]
    E --> F[Maintain temperature and stir at 200 rpm]
    F --> G[Monitor viscosity rise every 15 min]
    G --> H{Reaction time elapsed, 90 to 120 min}
    H --> I[Cool and pour into cold methanol]
    I --> J[White PMMA precipitates out]
    J --> K[Filter, wash, and dry in vacuum oven at 50 C]
    K --> L[Weigh yield and calculate percent conversion]
    L --> M[Characterize: IR, DSC, viscosity-average MW]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The vertical pipeline traces the **physical workflow** of the bench-scale experiment. Each block is a *process step* (not a chemical species), making the diagram **algorithmically safe** and **Mermaid-compatible**.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mechanistic Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INIT[Initiation Stage]
        I1[BPO or AIBN molecule] --> I2[Two primary radicals I dot]
        I2 --> I3[Add to vinyl bond of MMA]
        I3 --> I4[First monomer radical M1 dot]
    end

    subgraph PROP[Propagation Stage]
        I4 --> P1[Chain radical M dot]
        P1 --> P2[Add another MMA unit]
        P2 --> P3[Longer chain radical M dot]
        P3 --> P4[Repeat n times]
        P4 --> P5[Macroradical Pn dot]
    end

    subgraph TERM[Termination Stage]
        P5 --> T1[Combination pathway]
        P5 --> T2[Disproportionation pathway]
        T1 --> T3[Dead polymer chain single]
        T2 --> T4[Dead polymer chains two]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:** The three subgraphs correspond to the three classical kinetic stages. Subgraph names use alphanumeric labels (INIT, PROP, TERM) that satisfy Mermaid's *Node Identifier Alpha Rule*. Labels inside nodes are raw uppercase text — no markdown bold or italics.

---

### 4.3 Schematic — Monomer → Polymer Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[CH2 double bond C CH3 attached to COOCH3 group] --> P1[Long chain of repeating units]
    P1 --> P2[Atactic stereochemistry of side groups]
    P2 --> P3[Amorphous glassy solid PMMA]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **process-flow-style representation** of the chemical transformation. (A literal Lewis-structure drawing is not possible in Mermaid; instead the functional architecture is mapped textually, following the KTU diagram-fallback rule.)

---

### 4.4 Schematic — Effect of Initiator Concentration on Molecular Weight

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOW[Low initiator 0.05 mol percent] --> HIGHMW[Long chains, high Mn, high viscosity]
    MED[Medium initiator 0.5 mol percent] --> MEDMW[Moderate chains, moderate Mn]
    HIGH[High initiator 2 mol percent] --> LOWMW[Short chains, low Mn, low viscosity]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This visualizes the **inverse-square-root law** of $\overline{X}_n$ on $[I]$ derived in §3.2. As initiator increases, chain length decreases predictably — the basis of industrial MW control.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "acrylic polymer." Give two examples of monomers used to prepare acrylic polymers and mention one industrial application of poly(methyl methacrylate).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Acrylic polymers are addition polymers derived from monomers containing the acryloyl group (CH₂=CH–COOR). Examples of monomers are (i) **methyl methacrylate (MMA)** — the precursor of PMMA, and (ii) **acrylic acid (CH₂=CH–COOH)** — the precursor of poly(acrylic acid) used in superabsorbents. An industrial application of PMMA is its use in **aircraft cabin windows**, owing to its optical clarity, low density (**1.18 g/cm³**), and impact resistance superior to silicate glass. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why is benzoyl peroxide (BPO) preferred as an initiator for the bulk polymerization of methyl methacrylate in undergraduate laboratories? Mention the typical temperature range used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
BPO is preferred because (i) it is a **solid, easy to weigh accurately**, (ii) it has a **convenient half-life of ~10 hours at 70 °C**, providing a controllable rate of radical generation, and (iii) it is **safer to handle** than peroxides requiring very low decomposition temperatures. The typical temperature range for bulk polymerization of MMA using BPO is **70–90 °C** (most commonly 80 °C). At this temperature, the half-life of BPO is sufficient to maintain a steady radical flux without runaway exotherm. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Important (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Each Part B question is worth **14 marks** and typically has two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** is mandatory.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable reaction scheme, explain the **mechanism of free-radical bulk polymerization of methyl methacrylate using benzoyl peroxide as initiator**. Discuss the role of each of the three kinetic stages. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Sub-part (a):**

**Stage 1 — Initiation (2 marks)**
Benzoyl peroxide undergoes homolytic cleavage of its O–O bond on heating, generating two benzoyloxy radicals. Each radical adds to the C=C double bond of MMA, transferring the radical to the carbon that becomes the chain end. This produces the chain-initiating species.

$$
(C_6H_5COO)_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta, \, 80\,°C} 2\,C_6H_5COO• \xrightarrow{+CH_2=C(CH_3)COOCH_3} C_6H_5COO{-}CH_2{-}C•(CH_3)(COOCH_3)
$$

**Stage 2 — Propagation (3 marks)**
The chain radical successively attacks the vinyl double bonds of additional MMA molecules. The radical site migrates to the chain end after each addition. This stage is the **chain-growth** step, responsible for building up the high molecular weight of the polymer (typically $\overline{M}_n \sim 10^5$ g/mol). The rate constant $k_p$ for MMA at 80 °C ≈ **2.0 × 10³ L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹**.

$$
R{-}CH_2{-}C•(CH_3)COOCH_3 + n\,CH_2{=}C(CH_3)COOCH_3 \longrightarrow R{-(CH_2{-}C(CH_3)COOCH_3)}_n{-}C•(CH_3)COOCH_3
$$

**Stage 3 — Termination (2 marks)**
Two macroradicals meet and react by either **combination** (forming a single dead chain with a C–C bond) or **disproportionation** (hydrogen transfer, forming two dead chains, one with a saturated end and one with a vinylidene end). For PMMA at moderate temperatures, **disproportionation is dominant** (~70 % of termination events at 80 °C).

**(b)** Derive the **rate law for free-radical polymerization** and show that the rate of polymerization is first order in monomer and half order in initiator concentration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Sub-part (b):**

*Step 1:* Write the elementary rates (1 mark).

$$
R_d = 2 f k_d [I], \quad R_i = R_d, \quad R_p = k_p [M][M•], \quad R_t = 2 k_t [M•]^2
$$

*Step 2:* Apply the steady-state approximation on the radical intermediate (1 mark).

$$
\dfrac{d[M•]}{dt} = R_i - R_t = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 2 f k_d [I] = 2 k_t [M•]^2
$$

*Step 3:* Solve for $[M•]_{ss}$ (1 mark).

$$
[M•]_{ss} = \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}
$$

*Step 4:* Substitute into $R_p$ (1 mark).

$$
R_p = k_p [M] \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{1/2}
$$

*Step 5:* Rearrange and identify the orders (2 marks).

$$
R_p = k_p \left(\dfrac{f k_d}{k_t}\right)^{1/2} [I]^{1/2} [M]^1
$$

Since the exponent on $[I]$ is $\tfrac{1}{2}$ and on $[M]$ is $1$, the rate is **first order in monomer and half order in initiator**. The half-order arises because two radicals are required to terminate one chain, so the steady-state radical concentration scales with $\sqrt{[I]}$. (1 mark for interpretation)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Describe the **laboratory procedure for the bulk polymerization of MMA** using benzoyl peroxide. List the apparatus, chemicals, and the precautions to be taken. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Sub-part (a):**

**Apparatus (1.5 marks):**
Three-necked round-bottom flask (250 mL), mechanical stirrer with PTFE paddle, Leibig condenser, thermometer (0–200 °C), nitrogen inlet tube, heating oil bath, separating funnel, Buchner funnel, vacuum desiccator.

**Chemicals (1 mark):**
- Methyl methacrylate (MMA) — **20 mL** (inhibitor removed by washing with 5 % NaOH, then water, dried over anhydrous Na₂SO₄).
- Benzoyl peroxide — **0.2 g** (recrystallized from chloroform–methanol).
- Methanol — **200 mL** (for precipitation).

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Charge the cleaned MMA and weighed BPO into the three-necked flask.
2. Assemble the stirrer, condenser, and N₂ inlet. Pass a slow stream of N₂ for 10 minutes to remove dissolved O₂ (which would otherwise inhibit the chain reaction).
3. Heat the oil bath to **80 °C** and maintain for **90 minutes** with continuous stirring at 200 rpm.
4. Observe the viscosity increase as the polymer forms.
5. Cool the flask to room temperature. Add 5 mL acetone to dissolve the polymer (if it has solidified).
6. Slowly pour the viscous solution into 200 mL of cold methanol with stirring — white PMMA precipitates.
7. Filter on a Buchner funnel, wash with methanol, dry in a vacuum oven at **50 °C** to constant weight.

**Precautions (1.5 marks):**
- MMA is a volatile, flammable liquid — work in a fume hood; no open flames.
- BPO is a strong oxidizer — do not grind violently; store refrigerated.
- Ensure N₂ purging to prevent oxygen inhibition.
- Use a condenser — MMA boils at 101 °C.
- Avoid skin contact; use gloves.

**(b)** The bulk polymerization of MMA at 80 °C gives a yield of **8.6 g** from **10.0 g** of monomer charged, using 0.10 g BPO. Calculate (i) the **percent conversion**, (ii) the **moles of monomer converted**, and (iii) the **moles of initiator charged**. Comment on how doubling the initiator concentration would affect the molecular weight of the polymer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Sub-part (b):**

**(i) Percent conversion (2 marks):**

$$
\%\,\text{Conversion} = \dfrac{8.6}{10.0} \times 100 = 86\,\%
$$

**(ii) Moles of monomer converted (2 marks):**

$$
n_{MMA,\text{converted}} = \dfrac{8.6\,\text{g}}{100.12\,\text{g/mol}} = 0.0859\,\text{mol}
$$

**(iii) Moles of initiator (BPO) charged (1.5 marks):**

$$
n_{BPO} = \dfrac{0.10}{242.23} = 4.13 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol}
$$

**Effect of doubling initiator on MW (1.5 marks):**

From the derived expression $\overline{X}_n \propto [I]^{-1/2}$, doubling $[I]$ decreases $\overline{X}_n$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. Hence, the **number-average degree of polymerization decreases to 70.7 %** of its original value, and the **molecular weight is similarly reduced by ~29 %**. The polymer becomes softer, less viscous in the melt, and easier to process. In industrial practice, initiator concentration is the **primary handle** for molecular-weight control.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (PCPOL607):**
> 1. **Confusing $R_p$ (rate) with $r_p$ (rate per chain).** The half-order on $[I]$ comes from the *steady-state assumption on radicals*, not from the initiator decomposition stoichiometry. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Wrong units in $\overline{X}_n$ derivation.** Students forget that termination consumes **two** radicals, hence the factor of 2 in $R_t = 2 k_t [M•]^2$ and the resulting $\overline{X}_n$ expression.
> 3. **Mixing up the Mark-Houwink constants** $K$ and $a$ for different solvents. The values are *not universal* — examiners may quote a specific solvent and expect the corresponding constants.
> 4. **Tacticity trap:** PMMA prepared by free-radical polymerization is **predominantly syndiotactic** (~60–70 %), not "atactic" in the literal sense. Generic textbook answer of "atactic" is acceptable but loses nuance marks.
> 5. **Neglecting the Trommsdorff (gel) effect** in bulk polymerization — beyond ~20 % conversion, the rate *auto-accelerates* due to diffusion-controlled termination. This is a frequent 2-mark question.
> 6. **Inhibitor oversight:** Commercial MMA contains **hydroquinone monomethyl ether (MEHQ)** at ~10–100 ppm. Students must mention **inhibitor removal** before polymerization, or risk losing 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **PMMA** is the workhorse acrylic polymer, made by **free-radical bulk polymerization** of **methyl methacrylate** using **BPO** or **AIBN** initiator at **70–90 °C**.
- **Three kinetic stages:** Initiation, Propagation, Termination (Combination / Disproportionation).
- **Rate law:** $R_p \propto [I]^{1/2} [M]^1$ — derived from the **steady-state radical approximation**.
- **Chain length:** $\overline{X}_n \propto [I]^{-1/2}$ — more initiator → shorter chains.
- **Molecular weight of free-radical PMMA:** $\overline{M}_n \approx 10^5$ g/mol, $\mathrm{Ð} \approx 1.5$–2.0.
- **Tacticity:** Predominantly **syndiotactic** due to chain-end control; contributes to high $T_g \approx 105\,°C$.
- **Mark-Houwink parameters for PMMA** depend on solvent; students must cite the correct $K$, $a$ pair.
- **Precautions in lab:** Inert N₂ atmosphere, inhibitor removal, fume-hood use, controlled heating (Trommsdorff effect).
- **Industrial uses:** Transparent glazing, aircraft windows, biomedical bone cement, LED light guides.
- **Key numerical conversions to memorize:** $M_{MMA} = 100.12$ g/mol, $M_{BPO} = 242.23$ g/mol, $\Delta H_p = -56$ kJ/mol, $\rho_{PMMA} = 1.18$ g/cm³, $T_g = 105\,°C$.
- **Comparison hook for exams:** PMMA (amorphous, transparent, $T_g = 105\,°C$) **vs.** PS (amorphous, transparent, $T_g = 100\,°C$) — both vinyl addition polymers, but ester side group of PMMA gives better UV and chemical resistance.
- **Sustainability note (KTU 2024 NEP emphasis):** PMMA can be **thermally depolymerized back to MMA** above 300 °C, enabling chemical recycling — a key circular-economy advantage over many other plastics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** poly vinyl alcohol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Poly Vinyl Alcohol (PVA / PVOH)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Poly Vinyl Alcohol (PVA, PVOH, or PVAl)** is a **water-soluble, synthetic, atactic, linear thermoplastic polymer** whose repeat unit is the vinyl alcohol monomer unit, with the idealized chemical structure **[-CH₂-CH(OH)-]ₙ**. It is industrially classified as a **polyhydroxy polymer** because every alternate carbon atom in the backbone carries a pendant **hydroxyl (-OH)** functional group.

Unlike the vast majority of vinyl polymers, PVA **cannot be synthesized directly from its nominal monomer (vinyl alcohol, CH₂=CH-OH)** because vinyl alcohol is thermodynamically unstable and rapidly tautomerizes to acetaldehyde. Therefore, PVA is industrially produced by the **indirect route of partial or full alkaline/alcoholysis (saponification) of Poly Vinyl Acetate (PVAc)**, which itself is produced by free-radical chain polymerization of the vinyl acetate monomer (VAM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Direct Quote Style):**
> "Poly vinyl alcohol is a biodegradable, water-soluble polymer obtained by the hydrolysis of poly(vinyl acetate). It is a polyhydroxy resin with pendant –OH groups that govern its crystallinity, hydrogen-bonding capacity, adhesion, and film-forming behaviour."

> [!NOTE]
> **Stereochemistry Note:** Commercially produced PVA is typically **atactic** (random placement of –OH groups). However, even atactic PVA shows partial crystallinity (≈ 30–50%) because the small –OH group permits efficient **intermolecular hydrogen bonding** and chain packing in the solid state. The density of crystalline PVA is **≈ 1.31 g/cm³** and the glass transition temperature (Tg) of fully hydrolysed PVA is **≈ 85 °C**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **long chain of paper clips** (the carbon backbone) where **every alternate clip has a tiny sticky magnet attached to it** (the –OH group). These sticky magnets attract water molecules (because water is itself polar) and also stick strongly to other chains' magnets. The result is:

- The chain **dissolves easily in warm water** (the magnets pull water in).
- In dry form, the chain **sticks to itself**, producing a strong, transparent, glossy film — exactly like the white glue you used in school.
- If you only put magnets on **some** of the clips (partial hydrolysis), the chain is more flexible and behaves like a surfactant.
- If you put magnets on **all** the clips (full hydrolysis), the chain becomes more crystalline, more rigid, and less water-soluble at room temperature (it needs hot water to dissolve).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exam:** PVA = *"*P*aper-clip *V*inyl with *A*lternating -OH magnets*"* — the **degree of hydrolysis (%)** tells you **how many magnets are present**, which dictates every physical property.

---

### 1.3 Identity Card of PVA

| Property | Value / Range | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Repeat unit | –[CH₂–CH(OH)]ₙ– | Defines identity |
| Molecular weight (commercial) | **20,000 – 2,00,000 g/mol** | Determines viscosity grade |
| Density | **1.19 – 1.31 g/cm³** | Useful in packaging calculations |
| Tg (fully hydrolysed) | **≈ 85 °C** | Processing window |
| Tm (fully hydrolysed) | **≈ 230 °C** (with decomposition) | Cannot be melt-processed easily |
| Solubility | Hot/cold water (depends on % hydrolysis) | Unique among vinyl polymers |
| Refractive index | **1.50 – 1.53** | Optical clarity of films |
| Appearance | White to cream, granular powder | Industrial form |
| Biodegradability | Yes (under aerobic conditions) | Eco-friendly advantage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Step Synthesis Logic

PVA production is fundamentally a **two-stage process** that students must know in sequential detail for KTU ESE questions:

### Stage 1: Vinyl Acetate → Poly Vinyl Acetate (PVAc)

Vinyl acetate monomer (VAM, CH₂=CH–O–CO–CH₃) undergoes **free-radical addition polymerization**, normally initiated by **AIBN (azobisisobutyronitrile)** or **BPO (benzoyl peroxide)** at 60–80 °C.

$$\text{n } \mathrm{CH_2{=}CH{-}OCOCH_3} \xrightarrow{\text{AIBN, } 65\,^\circ\mathrm{C}} \left[{-}\mathrm{CH_2{-}CH(OCOCH_3)}{-}\right]_{\mathrm{n}}$$

### Stage 2: PVAc → PVA (Saponification / Alcoholysis)

The acetate side groups of PVAc are cleaved by **methanolic NaOH (or KOH)**. This is called **trans-esterification / alcoholysis** (more correctly than hydrolysis, because methanol is the solvent).

$$\left[{-}\mathrm{CH_2{-}CH(OCOCH_3)}{-}\right]_{\mathrm{n}} + \text{n NaOH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CH_3OH}} \left[{-}\mathrm{CH_2{-}CH(OH)}{-}\right]_{\mathrm{n}} + \text{n CH_3COONa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not polymerize vinyl alcohol directly?**
> Vinyl alcohol (CH₂=CH-OH) exists only as the enol tautomer of acetaldehyde and spontaneously rearranges (Keto–Enol tautomerism) to CH₃-CHO. The equilibrium constant strongly favours the keto (aldehyde) form, so the monomer cannot be isolated. Hence the **PVAc → PVA route is the only industrial pathway.**

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Property

| Property | Molecular Origin (the "Why") |
|---|---|
| Water solubility | –OH groups form H-bonds with H₂O; partially hydrolysed grades dissolve at room T |
| Adhesion | –OH groups form H-bonds with –OH, –COOH groups on paper, wood, cotton |
| Film clarity | Atactic but partially crystalline; amorphous regions are optically transparent |
| Crystallinity | Small –OH permits tight inter-chain H-bonding and packing (0.290 nm spacing) |
| Tg ≈ 85 °C | High due to strong inter-chain H-bonding restricting segmental motion |
| Biodegradability | Pendant –OH is recognised by microbial enzymes (e.g., oxidase / dehydrogenase) |
| Cannot be melt-processed | Decomposes near 200 °C before reaching Tm ≈ 230 °C → must be cast from solution |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is **high-yield for numerical problems**. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to keep markdown tables valid.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\%\text{Hydrolysis} = \dfrac{\text{mol } \mathrm{OH}}{\text{mol } \mathrm{OH} + \text{mol } \mathrm{OCOCH_3}} \times 100$ | Defines grade of PVA (fully vs partially hydrolysed) | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $\bar{M}_{\mathrm{n}} = M_0 \times \overline{DP}_{\mathrm{n}}$ | Number-average MW; $M_0$ of vinyl alcohol repeat unit = **44.05 g/mol** | g/mol |
| 3 | $[\eta] = K\,M^{\alpha}$ | Mark–Houwink–Sakurada equation for viscosity-average MW at 25 °C in water: $K = 4.53 \times 10^{-4}$, $\alpha = 0.64$ | dL/g |
| 4 | $\mathrm{DS} = n_{\mathrm{OH}} / n_{\mathrm{repeating\ unit}}$ | Degree of substitution = fraction of OH groups per repeat unit | 0 → 1 |
| 5 | $T_{\mathrm{g}} \approx T_{\mathrm{g,\infty}} - \dfrac{K}{\bar{M}_{\mathrm{n}}}$ | Fox–Flory relation; $K$ for PVA ≈ **$5 \times 10^{4}$ K·g/mol** | K |
| 6 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Tensile stress at break (PVA film ≈ 30–100 MPa) | MPa |
| 7 | $\%\text{Elongation} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Ductility of cast PVA film | % |
| 8 | $\rho_{\mathrm{crystal}} = 1.31\ \mathrm{g/cm^3}$, $\rho_{\mathrm{amorphous}} = 1.26\ \mathrm{g/cm^3}$ | Used in \% crystallinity by density gradient | g/cm³ |
| 9 | $\%\text{Crystallinity} = \dfrac{\rho_{\mathrm{c}}(\rho_{\mathrm{s}} - \rho_{\mathrm{a}})}{\rho_{\mathrm{s}}(\rho_{\mathrm{c}} - \rho_{\mathrm{a}})} \times 100$ | Standard density-gradient crystallinity formula | % |
| 10 | $\mathrm{Intrinsic\ viscosity}\ [\eta] = \lim_{c \to 0}\dfrac{\eta_{\mathrm{sp}}}{c}$ | Used to compute $M_{\mathrm{v}}$ | dL/g |

---

## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Utility

PVA is one of the **top-five largest-volume water-soluble polymers globally**. Its key engineering applications are:

1. **Paper & Packaging Industry** — Surface sizing and coating adhesive (replaces starch in many plants); accounts for **~30%** of global PVA consumption.
2. **Textile Industry** — Warp sizing agent for polyester/cotton blends; improves yarn strength during weaving and is later washed out.
3. **Adhesives** — White glue (Fevicol-type), book-binding, and remoistenable adhesives.
4. **Pharmaceuticals** — Tablet binder, controlled-release matrix, ocular lubricant (artificial tears), and contact-lens wetting solutions.
5. **Food Packaging** — Edible films and oxygen-barrier layers (PVA films have **O₂ permeability comparable to EVOH**).
6. **3D Printing** — Water-soluble support material for FDM (dissolves in warm water, eliminating mechanical removal).
7. **Construction** — Cement mortar modifier, re-dispersible powder in dry-mix formulations.
8. **Emulsion Polymerisation** — Protective colloid for PVAc emulsions.
9. **Biomedical** — Nerve guidance conduits, cartilage scaffolds, drug-delivery hydrogels (crosslinked with glutaraldehyde or borax).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Mechanism of PVA Formation (Board-Exam Standard)

### Stage 1 — Free-Radical Polymerisation of Vinyl Acetate

**Initiation:**

AIBN decomposes on heating to give two isobutyronitrile radicals and one N₂.

$$\mathrm{(CH_3)_2(CN)C{-}N{=}N{-}C(CN)(CH_3)_2} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ 60{-}80\,^\circ\mathrm{C}} 2\ (\mathrm{CH_3})_2\dot{\mathrm{C}}\mathrm{CN} + \mathrm{N_2}$$

The radical attacks the CH₂ end of vinyl acetate:

$$\mathrm{R^{\bullet} + CH_2{=}CH{-}OCOCH_3 \longrightarrow R{-}CH_2{-}\overset{\bullet}{C}H{-}OCOCH_3}$$

**Propagation** (repeats n times):

$$\mathrm{R{-}[CH_2{-}CH(OCOCH_3)]_{k}{-}CH_2{-}\overset{\bullet}{C}H{-}OCOCH_3} + \mathrm{CH_2{=}CHOCOCH_3}$$
$$\longrightarrow \mathrm{R{-}[CH_2{-}CH(OCOCH_3)]_{k+1}{-}CH_2{-}\overset{\bullet}{C}H{-}OCOCH_3}$$

**Termination** (combination is most common for VAM):

$$2\ \mathrm{PVAc}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \mathrm{PVAc{-}PVAc\ (dead\ polymer)}$$

---

### Stage 2 — Saponification (Alcoholysis) of PVAc

The mechanism is **base-catalysed trans-esterification**. Methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻) attacks the carbonyl carbon of the pendant acetate group, forming a tetrahedral intermediate, then expelling the alkoxide bound to the polymer backbone.

**Step 2.1 — Generation of methoxide:**

$$\mathrm{CH_3OH + NaOH \rightleftharpoons CH_3O^{-}Na^{+} + H_2O}$$

**Step 2.2 — Nucleophilic attack on carbonyl C:**

$$\mathrm{CH_3O^{-} + R{-}OCOCH_3 \longrightarrow R{-}O{-}\overset{\;\;OH}{\underset{}{\mathrm{C}}}{(}OCH_3{)}(CH_3)}$$

**Step 2.3 — Collapse of tetrahedral intermediate:**

$$\mathrm{Tetrahedral\ intermediate \longrightarrow R{-}OH + CH_3COOCH_3}$$

(The R–OH here is the newly revealed PVA hydroxyl; methyl acetate is the by-product.)

> [!NOTE]
> In methanol-rich media, the byproduct is **methyl acetate (CH₃COOCH₃)** rather than sodium acetate, hence the term **alcoholysis** rather than hydrolysis. Industrially, methanol is recovered by distillation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Degree of Hydrolysis from Conductometric / Titration Data

> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem:** 2.00 g of a PVA sample was dissolved in water and back-titrated. The volume of 0.50 M NaOH required to neutralise the residual acetic acid (liberated during hydrolysis) was **18.00 mL** for a fully-hydrolysed reference, and **4.50 mL** for the unknown grade. Calculate the **% hydrolysis** of the unknown.

### Solution (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Convert NaOH volume to moles.**

For fully hydrolysed sample:

$$n_{\mathrm{NaOH,\ full}} = 0.50\ \mathrm{mol/L} \times 18.00 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{L} = 9.00 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{mol}$$

For unknown grade:

$$n_{\mathrm{NaOH,\ unk}} = 0.50 \times 4.50 \times 10^{-3} = 2.25 \times 10^{-3}\ \mathrm{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Note the stoichiometry.**

1 mol NaOH ≡ 1 mol of unhydrolysed acetate ester (since hydrolysis releases 1 mol CH₃COOH per –OCOCH₃).

$$\text{Unhydrolysed fraction (unknown)} = \dfrac{2.25 \times 10^{-3}}{9.00 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.250$$

**Step 3 — Compute % hydrolysis.**

$$\%\text{Hydrolysis} = (1 - 0.250) \times 100 = \mathbf{75.0\%}$$

The sample is therefore **partially hydrolysed (medium-grade) PVA**, soluble in water at room temperature, suitable for adhesive and textile-sizing applications.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Molecular Weight from Intrinsic Viscosity

**Problem:** A PVA sample has $[\eta] = 0.85\ \mathrm{dL/g}$ at 25 °C in water. Using $K = 4.53 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{dL/g}$ and $\alpha = 0.64$, calculate the viscosity-average molecular weight.

### Solution

$$[\eta] = K\,M^{\alpha}$$

$$M = \left(\dfrac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/\alpha} = \left(\dfrac{0.85}{4.53 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/0.64}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the bracket.**

$$\dfrac{0.85}{4.53 \times 10^{-4}} = 1876.4$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent.**

$$\dfrac{1}{0.64} = 1.5625$$

**Step 3 — Apply the power.**

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= (1876.4)^{1.5625} \\
\log_{10} M &= 1.5625 \times \log_{10}(1876.4) \\
\log_{10}(1876.4) &= 3.273 \\
\log_{10} M &= 1.5625 \times 3.273 = 5.114 \\
M &= 10^{5.114} \approx \mathbf{1.30 \times 10^{5}\ g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore $M_{\mathrm{v}} \approx$ **1.30 × 10⁵ g/mol** (a medium-viscosity grade).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Grade Controls Property

| Property | Fully Hydrolysed (98–99 % mol) | Partially Hydrolysed (87–89 % mol) | Sub-Partially Hydrolysed (78–80 %) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water solubility (cold) | Low (needs > 80 °C) | High (room T) | Very High (room T) |
| Viscosity (4 % aq sol) | High | Medium | Low–Medium |
| Flexibility of film | Stiff | Flexible | Very flexible |
| Adhesion to hydrophilic surfaces | Medium | **Highest** | High |
| Crystallinity | High | Low | Very Low |
| Surface tension of solution | High | Medium | Low (good wetting) |
| Typical end-use | Textile sizing, films | Paper adhesive, glue | Emulsion stabiliser, surfactant |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Stage Synthesis Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vinyl Acetate Monomer CH2=CH-OCOCH3] -->|Initiator: AIBN at 65 deg C| B[Poly Vinyl Acetate PVAc]
    B -->|Methanolic NaOH alcoholysis| C[Poly Vinyl Alcohol PVA]
    C -->|Fully Hydrolysed 98 to 99 percent| D[Crystalline Film Grade]
    C -->|Partially Hydrolysed 87 to 89 percent| E[Adhesive and Sizing Grade]
    C -->|Sub Hydrolysed 78 to 80 percent| F[Emulsion Stabiliser Grade]
    D --> G[Pharma Tablet Binder, O2-barrier film]
    E --> H[White Glue, Paper Coating, Textile Warp Sizing]
    F --> I[PVAc Emulsion Protective Colloid, Detergent]
    B --> J[Wood Glue, Latex Paint, Chewing Gum Base]
```

```mermaid
        C1[CH2] --- C2[CH]
        C2 --- C3[CH2]

    end
    C2 -.pendant OH.-> O1[OH]
    C4 -.pendant OH.-> O2[OH]
    C6 -.pendant OH.-> O3[OH]

    O2 -.hydrogen bond.-> O3

    O3 -.hydrogen bond with H2O.-> W2[H2O]
```
