# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Define poly vinyl alcohol. Why is it not synthesised directly from vinyl alcohol monomer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** PVA is a synthetic, water-soluble, atactic, linear thermoplastic polymer with the repeat unit **[-CH₂-CH(OH)-]ₙ** and pendant hydroxyl groups on alternate carbon atoms.
- **Reason for indirect synthesis (2 marks):** Vinyl alcohol (CH₂=CH-OH) is the unstable **enol tautomer of acetaldehyde** and spontaneously rearranges to CH₃CHO (Keto–Enol tautomerism equilibrium constant strongly favours the keto form). Hence, the monomer cannot be isolated or polymerised. PVA is therefore obtained **indirectly by hydrolysis/saponification of poly(vinyl acetate)**, which in turn is obtained by free-radical polymerisation of vinyl acetate monomer.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Differentiate between fully hydrolysed and partially hydrolysed grades of PVA with respect to (i) water solubility and (ii) crystallinity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Fully Hydrolysed (98–99 %) | Partially Hydrolysed (87–89 %) |
|---|---|---|
| Water solubility (1.5 mark) | Low; requires water > 80 °C; cold-water insoluble | High; soluble at room temperature (25 °C) |
| Crystallinity (1.5 mark) | High crystallinity (≈ 30–50 %) due to maximum H-bonding; stiffer films | Low crystallinity; acetate residues disrupt H-bonding; more flexible films |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse PVA with PVAc.** PVAc (polyvinyl acetate) is the precursor and is *not* water-soluble; PVA *is* water-soluble. Many students write the wrong polymer in the final equation and lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Always specify the initiator** (AIBN/BPO) and temperature (60–80 °C) when writing the polymerisation step. Vague statements like "polymerised using heat" lose **1 mark**.
> 3. **Show the saponification stoichiometry correctly** — one methoxide releases one –OH and one methyl acetate; not "methanol + sodium acetate."
> 4. **Numerical questions:** Show unit cancellation explicitly. Marks are awarded for intermediate steps, not just the final number.
> 5. **Do not** write the structure as [-CH(OH)-CH₂-]ₙ with random –OH on every carbon. The correct placement is on **alternate** carbon atoms.

---

### Question A — (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Module Experiment 2]**

**(a)** Explain with balanced chemical equations the **industrial preparation of poly vinyl alcohol** from vinyl acetate monomer. Discuss the **mechanism** of the saponification step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PVA sample shows an intrinsic viscosity $[\eta] = 1.20\ \mathrm{dL/g}$ at 25 °C in water. Using the Mark–Houwink constants $K = 4.53 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{dL/g}$ and $\alpha = 0.64$, calculate the **viscosity-average molecular weight** and identify the **industrial grade category**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Stage 1: Free-radical polymerisation of vinyl acetate (3 marks).**

$$\text{n } \mathrm{CH_2{=}CH{-}OCOCH_3} \xrightarrow{\text{AIBN initiator, 60–80 °C}} \left[{-}\mathrm{CH_2{-}CH(OCOCH_3)}{-}\right]_{\mathrm{n}}$$

Mechanism includes **initiation, propagation, and termination** (combination dominant for VAM), all as detailed in §3.1 above. Briefly state that AIBN provides primary radicals R•, which add to the CH₂ of the monomer, and chain growth proceeds via successive additions until termination by combination or disproportionation.

**Step 2 — Stage 2: Saponification in methanolic NaOH (2 marks).**

$$\left[{-}\mathrm{CH_2{-}CH(OCOCH_3)}{-}\right]_{\mathrm{n}} + \text{n NaOH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CH_3OH}} \left[{-}\mathrm{CH_2{-}CH(OH)}{-}\right]_{\mathrm{n}} + \text{n CH_3COOCH_3} + \text{n NaOH residue}$$

**Step 3 — Mechanism of saponification (2 marks).**

(i) CH₃OH + NaOH ⇌ CH₃O⁻ Na⁺ + H₂O (methoxide formation).
(ii) Methoxide attacks the carbonyl C of the acetate group, forming a tetrahedral intermediate.
(iii) Intermediate collapses, expelling the polymer-bound alkoxide (now PVA hydroxyl) and producing methyl acetate (CH₃COOCH₃) as by-product.

**Why this indirect route? (Extra 0 marks but recommended):** Vinyl alcohol monomer is unstable (tautomerises to acetaldehyde).

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Given:**
- $[\eta] = 1.20\ \mathrm{dL/g}$
- $K = 4.53 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{dL/g}$
- $\alpha = 0.64$

**Step 1 — Apply Mark–Houwink equation (2 marks):**

$$M_{\mathrm{v}} = \left(\dfrac{[\eta]}{K}\right)^{1/\alpha} = \left(\dfrac{1.20}{4.53 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{1/0.64}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bracket (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{1.20}{4.53 \times 10^{-4}} = 2649.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{1}{\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{0.64} = 1.5625$$

**Step 4 — Apply the power using logarithms (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10} M_{\mathrm{v}} &= 1.5625 \times \log_{10}(2649.0) \\
\log_{10}(2649.0) &= 3.423 \\
\log_{10} M_{\mathrm{v}} &= 1.5625 \times 3.423 = 5.348 \\
M_{\mathrm{v}} &= 10^{5.348} \approx \mathbf{2.23 \times 10^{5}\ g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Identify grade (1 mark):**

$M_{\mathrm{v}} \approx 2.23 \times 10^{5}\ \mathrm{g/mol}$ falls in the **medium-to-high viscosity grade** (typical PVA grades: low ≈ 25 kDa, medium ≈ 75–100 kDa, high ≈ 150–200 kDa, ultra-high ≈ 250 kDa+). This is therefore a **high-viscosity industrial grade**, suitable for **paper coating adhesives and textile warp sizing**.

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Module Experiment 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **synthesis procedure of poly vinyl alcohol in the laboratory** from poly(vinyl acetate). Mention the **role of each reagent** and write the **reaction conditions**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **% hydrolysis of PVA** influences (i) **water solubility**, (ii) **crystallinity**, and (iii) **flexibility of cast films**. Discuss any **four industrial applications** of PVA. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Laboratory Synthesis Procedure (7 marks, distributed):**

**Materials (1 mark):** Poly(vinyl acetate) resin (PVAc, 10 g); methanol (100 mL) as solvent and trans-esterification medium; sodium hydroxide pellets (5 g, dissolved in 20 mL methanol) as saponification agent; acetone for washing; deionised water for dissolution test.

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Dissolution:** 10 g of PVAc is dissolved in 100 mL of methanol in a 250 mL round-bottom flask fitted with a reflux condenser, heated to 60 °C with magnetic stirring until a clear viscous solution is obtained.
2. **Saponification:** Methanolic NaOH solution is added **drop-wise** over 15 minutes to the refluxing PVAc solution. A pale-yellow to white precipitate of PVA forms progressively as the acetate groups are cleaved.
3. **Reflux:** The reaction mixture is refluxed at 65 °C for 1 hour to ensure complete trans-esterification.
4. **Filtration & Washing:** The precipitate is filtered through a Buchner funnel, washed with cold methanol (to remove CH₃COONa and unreacted NaOH), then with acetone (to remove residual methanol and aid drying).
5. **Drying:** The product is dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C for 12 hours to constant weight.

**Reaction Conditions (1 mark):**

$$\text{Temperature} \approx 60\text{–}65\,^\circ\mathrm{C},\ \text{Time} \approx 1\text{–}2\ \mathrm{h},\ \text{Atmosphere} = \text{N}_2\ \text{inert (optional)}$$

**Role of Each Reagent (1 mark):**

| Reagent | Role |
|---|---|
| PVAc | Precursor polymer providing the –[CH₂–CH(OCOCH₃)]ₙ backbone |
| Methanol | Solvent for PVAc + source of methoxide ion |
| NaOH | Generates methoxide (CH₃O⁻); the actual nucleophile |
| Acetone | Washing; precipitates PVA; miscible with methanol |
| Hot water | Final dissolution test to confirm PVA formation |

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**(i) Influence on Water Solubility (2 marks):**
PVA's water solubility is governed by the balance between H-bonding with water (favoured by –OH groups) and inter-chain H-bonding (also favoured by –OH groups). At **low % hydrolysis (≤ 80 %)**, the residual acetate groups disrupt inter-chain H-bonding but still permit chain–water H-bonding → **cold-water-soluble**. At **high % hydrolysis (≥ 98 %)**, the strong inter-chain H-bonding makes cold water ineffective; dissolution requires **water above 80 °C**.

**(ii) Influence on Crystallinity (2 marks):**
Crystallinity in PVA arises from regular inter-chain H-bonding between adjacent –OH groups. At **full hydrolysis**, the regularity is maximum, giving crystallinity ≈ 30–50 %. At **partial hydrolysis**, the bulky acetate groups (–OCOCH₃, ~3× larger than –OH) act as **defects**, lowering crystallinity to ≈ 10–20 %. Crystallinity directly controls Tg, modulus, and barrier properties.

**(iii) Influence on Film Flexibility (1 mark):**
Lower crystallinity ⇒ greater amorphous content ⇒ easier segmental motion ⇒ **more flexible films**. Partially hydrolysed PVA therefore gives **flexible, tough films**, while fully hydrolysed PVA gives **stiff, brittle films** (often plasticised with glycerol).

**Four Industrial Applications (2 marks, 0.5 each):**
1. **Paper adhesive / surface sizing** — partially hydrolysed grade (87–89 %) for paper coating.
2. **Textile warp sizing** — fully hydrolysed grade for polyester/cotton yarns.
3. **Water-soluble packaging films** — for detergent pods, hospital laundry bags.
4. **Pharmaceutical controlled-release matrix** — crosslinked PVA tablets.

(Other valid options: 3D printing support material, edible food coatings, oxygen-barrier films, emulsion protective colloid, and ophthalmic lubricants.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this page before your exam!**

- ✅ PVA = **[-CH₂-CH(OH)-]ₙ**; pendant –OH on **alternate** carbons.
- ✅ **Cannot be made from vinyl alcohol monomer** — only via **PVAc saponification** (tautomerism argument).
- ✅ Synthesis = **2 stages**: (1) free-radical polymerisation of vinyl acetate with **AIBN/BPO initiator at 60–80 °C**; (2) **methanolic NaOH alcoholysis**.
- ✅ By-product of saponification is **methyl acetate (CH₃COOCH₃)**, not acetic acid (in alcoholysis).
- ✅ **% Hydrolysis** = most important industrial spec: fully (98–99 %), partially (87–89 %), sub (78–80 %).
- ✅ **Higher % hydrolysis ⇒ higher crystallinity, higher Tg, stiffer film, lower cold-water solubility.**
- ✅ **Lower % hydrolysis ⇒ more flexible film, higher cold-water solubility, better surfactant behaviour.**
- ✅ **Tg ≈ 85 °C** (fully hydrolysed); **density ≈ 1.31 g/cm³** (crystal); cannot be melt-processed — degrades near 200 °C.
- ✅ **Soluble in hot water**; **insoluble in most organic solvents** (a unique advantage among vinyl polymers).
- ✅ **Biodegradable**, water-soluble, adhesive, film-forming → eco-friendly packaging, pharma, textile, paper.
- ✅ **Mark–Houwink** constants in water at 25 °C: $K = 4.53 \times 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{dL/g}$, $\alpha = 0.64$.
- ✅ **FTIR signature** of PVA: broad –OH stretch near **3300 cm⁻¹**, C–O stretch near **1100 cm⁻¹**.
- ✅ **DSC signature:** endotherm at **≈ 85 °C** (Tg, fully hydrolysed) and degradation onset near **230 °C**.
- ✅ **Repeat unit molar mass:** vinyl alcohol unit = **44.05 g/mol** (CH₂=CH-OH with one H less).
- ✅ **Do not confuse** PVA (water-soluble, –OH pendant) with **PVAc** (water-insoluble, –OCOCH₃ pendant).
- ✅ **Glass transition** obeys **Fox–Flory** relation; $T_{\mathrm{g}} = T_{\mathrm{g,\infty}} - K / \bar{M}_{\mathrm{n}}$.
- ✅ Industrial production is done in **batch or continuous reactors**; methanol is recovered by distillation for economic and environmental reasons.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: spandex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** spandex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Spandex** (also commercially known as **Elastane**, **Lycra**, or **Dorlastan**) is a synthetic **segmented polyurethane-polyurea copolymer** fiber characterized by its extraordinary elastic recovery and stretchability. Chemically, it belongs to the family of **thermoplastic elastomers (TPEs)** with a unique **block copolymer architecture** consisting of alternating *hard segments* and *soft segments* arranged in a microphase-separated morphology.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> Spandex is a manufactured fiber in which the fiber-forming substance is a long-chain synthetic polymer composed of **at least 85% by weight of segmented polyurethane**, the chain of which is formed by the reaction of a polymeric diol with a diisocyanate and a diamine chain extender.

**IUPAC Classification:** Poly[oxy(methylethylene)oxycarbonylimino(1,6-dioxohexamethylene)iminocarbonyloxyethylene].

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slinky Spring Embedded in Concrete" Model

Imagine you take hundreds of tiny **rubber bands (soft segments)** and glue each end to small **concrete pillars (hard segments)** arranged in a long chain:

| Component | Real Analogy | Function in Spandex |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Soft Segment** (Polyol) | The stretchy rubber band | Provides elasticity — uncoils when stretched |
| **Hard Segment** (Urethane/Urea) | The rigid concrete pillar | Anchors the rubber — returns to original position |
| **Microphase Separation** | Glue-anchored springs in a wall | Prevents plastic deformation, gives memory |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Insight for First-Time Learners:**
> When you pull spandex, the *soft polyether/polyester coils uncoil like a spring*. When you release, the *hard urethane/urea blocks (which act like molecular anchors) snap the chains back*. This is why spandex can stretch **5 to 7 times** its original length and still recover its shape perfectly.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Elastic Recovery:** > **95%** (at 50% elongation)
- **Maximum Elongation at Break:** **500% – 700%**
- **Density:** **1.21 g/cm³**
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) of Soft Segment:** ~ **−50 °C** to **−70 °C**
- **Melting Point of Hard Segment:** ~ **230 °C – 260 °C**
- **Standard Commercial Composition:** ≥ **85% segmented polyurethane** (by weight, as per FTC/USDA guidelines)
- **Modulus at 100% elongation:** ~ **0.1 – 0.5 g/denier**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Keyword Trigger Words** (must use in answers): "segmented block copolymer," "microphase separation," "hard-soft segment," "step-growth polymerization," "isocyanate-terminated prepolymer."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Block Copolymer Architecture — Operational Logic

Spandex's elasticity arises from its precisely engineered **two-phase microstructure**:

**Phase 1 — The Soft Segment (Mobile Phase):**
- Comprises a **long-chain polymeric diol** (molecular weight 1000 – 4000 g/mol).
- Common examples: **PTMEG** (Polytetramethylene ether glycol), **PEG** (Polyethylene glycol), or **polyester diols** like polycaprolactone diol.
- These polyols have a **low glass transition temperature (Tg)** so they are rubbery at room temperature.
- Provides the **extensibility** — chains uncoil under tensile load.

**Phase 2 — The Hard Segment (Anchoring Phase):**
- Formed by the reaction of **aromatic diisocyanate** (e.g., MDI — Methylene diphenyl diisocyanate, or TDI — Toluene diisocyanate) with a **short-chain diamine extender** (e.g., ethylenediamine, hydrazine).
- These segments have high cohesive energy due to extensive **hydrogen bonding** between N–H and C=O groups.
- Function as **physical crosslinks** and **filler-like reinforcements**.
- Provide **mechanical strength** and **shape memory**.

**Microphase Separation Phenomenon:**
The thermodynamic incompatibility between the polar hard segments and the non-polar soft segments drives them to **self-assemble into distinct nanodomains** (typically 5–50 nm). This is the *secret* behind spandex's elastic behavior.

### 2.2 Synthesis Pathway — The "Prepolymer Method" (Two-Step Process)

#### **Step 1: Prepolymer Formation (NCO-terminated)**

An excess of **diisocyanate** reacts with a **polymeric diol** to form an isocyanate-terminated prepolymer.

#### **Step 2: Chain Extension with Diamine**

The NCO-terminated prepolymer is then reacted with a **short-chain diamine** (e.g., ethylenediamine) in a solvent like DMF (dimethylformamide). The amine groups react with the terminal NCO groups to form **urea linkages**, building the final high-molecular-weight segmented polymer.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** | **Units** |
|--------------------------|---------------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Carothers Equation (Degree of Polymerization) | $X_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | Used in step-growth polymerization; $p$ = extent of reaction | Dimensionless |
| Number-Average Molecular Weight | $\overline{M}_n = M_0 \cdot X_n$ | $M_0$ = repeat unit molar mass | g/mol |
| Weight-Average DP (Steady-State) | $\overline{M}_w \approx 2\overline{M}_n$ | For most condensation polymers | g/mol |
| Elastic Recovery | $R = \dfrac{L_0 - L_r}{L_0 - L_e} \times 100$ | $L_0$ = original length, $L_r$ = recovered length, $L_e$ = elongated length | **%** |
| Stress (Engineering) | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Tensile stress on fiber | N/m² (Pa) |
| Strain at Break | $\varepsilon_b = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Elongation capacity | Dimensionless |
| Glass Transition (Fox Equation) | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | For copolymer blends, $w_i$ = weight fraction | Kelvin (K) |
| Hydrogen Bond Density (Hard Segment) | $n_H \propto \dfrac{N_{\text{urea+urethane}}}{V_{\text{domain}}}$ | Drives microphase separation | bonds/nm³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> Spandex fibers are essential in **biomedical textiles** (compression stockings, prosthetic liners), **sportswear** (cycling shorts, swimwear), **aerospace** (pressure suits), and **intelligent textiles**. Its biocompatibility and elasticity closely mimic human skin mechanics, making it indispensable in **wearable sensors** and **soft robotics**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Synthesis Derivation — Prepolymer + Chain Extension

#### **Reaction 1: Prepolymer Formation (Isocyanate + Polyol → Urethane)**

Consider **Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate (MDI)** reacting with **Polytetramethylene Ether Glycol (PTMEG)**.

$$\text{HO–[CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–O]}_n\text{–H} + \text{OCN–R–NCO} \longrightarrow \text{OCN–R–NH–COO–[CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{O]}_n\text{–OOC–NH–R–NCO}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The **–OH group** of the polyol attacks the electrophilic **carbon atom of the –N=C=O (isocyanate) group**.
2. The proton from –OH migrates to the nitrogen atom, forming a **urethane linkage** (–NH–COO–).
3. Since **diisocyanate is in excess** (typical molar ratio NCO:OH = 2:1), both ends of the polyol become capped with **–NCO groups**, yielding an **isocyanate-terminated prepolymer**.
4. The reaction is **exothermic** (~ 100 kJ/mol per urethane bond) and is typically catalyzed by **tertiary amines** (e.g., DABCO) or **organotin compounds** (e.g., dibutyltin dilaurate).

#### **Reaction 2: Chain Extension (Prepolymer + Diamine → Polyurea)**

The NCO-terminated prepolymer is then reacted with **ethylenediamine (H₂N–CH₂–CH₂–NH₂)** in **DMF solvent**:

$$\text{OCN–[Prepolymer]–NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{N–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–NH}_2 \longrightarrow \text{[Prepolymer]–NH–CO–NH–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–NH–CO–NH–[Prepolymer]}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The **terminal –NCO groups** of the prepolymer are far more reactive toward amines than alcohols (amines are ~1000× more nucleophilic).
2. Each **–NH₂** group attacks an **–NCO carbon**, forming a **urea linkage** (–NH–CO–NH–).
3. The diamine is **bifunctional**, so it links two prepolymer chains, doubling the molecular weight each time → leading to a **rapid increase in molecular weight** (step-growth kinetics).
4. The final structure becomes a **segmented block copolymer**:

$$\text{[Hard Segment]–[Soft Segment]–[Hard Segment]–[Soft Segment]–\cdots}$$

5. This alternating arrangement is the **structural origin of microphase separation** and hence the elastic behavior.

#### **Final Spandex Repeat Unit (Schematic Linear Formula)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OCN–R–NH–COO–[Polyol]–OOC–NH–R–NH–CO–NH–[CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{]–NH–CO–NH–R–NCO}
\end{aligned}$$

Where:
- **R** = aromatic group from MDI (–C₆H₄–CH₂–C₆H₄–)
- **[Polyol]** = soft segment backbone (e.g., –[CH₂CH₂CH₂CH₂O]ₙ–)

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Molecular Weight Calculation (Carothers Application)

**Given:** For a step-growth polymerization with stoichiometric imbalance ratio $r = \dfrac{N_{OH}}{N_{NCO}} = 0.95$, where $N_{OH}$ = moles of OH groups from polyol and $N_{NCO}$ = moles of NCO from diisocyanate.

**Find:** Number-average degree of polymerization $X_n$.

Using the modified **Carothers equation for non-stoichiometric systems**:

$$X_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

**Substituting** $r = 0.95$ and assuming $p = 0.99$ (99% conversion):

$$X_n = \dfrac{1 + 0.95}{1 + 0.95 - 2(0.95)(0.99)}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
X_n &= \dfrac{1.95}{1.95 - 1.881} \\
&= \dfrac{1.95}{0.069} \\
&\approx 28.26
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** $X_n$ is critically dependent on $p$ near the end of the reaction → small drops in conversion cause **large drops in molecular weight** (Carothers' "Nylon Rope Trick" insight).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Spandex vs Other Elastomeric Fibers

| **Property** | **Spandex** | **Natural Rubber** | **Polyisoprene (synthetic)** |
|--------------|-------------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| Max Elongation | **500–700%** | 600–700% | 600–800% |
| Elastic Recovery | > **95%** | ~ 90% | ~ 85% |
| Chemical Resistance | High | Poor (oils, ozone) | Moderate |
| UV Resistance | Moderate | Poor | Moderate |
| Dyeability | Excellent (acid dyes) | Difficult | Difficult |
| Production Method | **Solution dry spinning** | Latex extrusion | Solution casting |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Spandex Synthesis & Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawA[Raw Material: PTMEG Polyol] --> prepStep[Step 1: Prepolymer Formation Reactor]
    rawB[Raw Material: MDI Diisocyanate] --> prepStep
    prepStep --> ncoPolymer[NCO-terminated Prepolymer in DMF]
    rawC[Raw Material: Ethylenediamine Chain Extender] --> extStep[Step 2: Chain Extension Reactor]
    ncoPolymer --> extStep
    extStep --> solutionPoly[Spandex Polymer Solution 25 to 30 percent solids]
    solutionPoly --> spinStep[Dry Spinning Cell Hot Nitrogen Gas 200 to 250 degree C]
    spinStep --> fiberForm[Continuous Multifilament Yarn]
    fiberForm --> finishStep[Finishing Oil Application]
    finishStep --> windStep[Winding onto Bobbins]
    windStep --> textileApp[Final Application: Sportswear, Medical Textiles, Aerospace Suits]

    hardSeg[Hard Segment: Urethane Urea Domains] -.microphase.-> solutionPoly
    softSeg[Soft Segment: Polyether Chains] -.microphase.-> solutionPoly
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Structure of Spandex Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mol[Macromolecular Chain] --> segArr[Alternating Segments]
    segArr --> hard[Hard Segments: Glassy domains]
    segArr --> soft[Soft Segments: Rubbery matrix]
    hard --> hbond[Hydrogen bonding network]
    soft --> uncoil[Uncoiling under tension]
    uncoil --> reco[Recovery on release]
    hbond --> micro[Microphase separated morphology]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow of Spandex Fiber Production

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Polyol Synthesis] --> mix[Mixing with MDI]
    startB[MDI Distillation] --> mix
    mix --> prepReact[Prepolymer Reactor Temperature 60 to 80 degree C]
    prepReact --> chainReact[Chain Extension Reactor in DMF Solvent]
    chainReact --> degas[Degas and Filter]
    degas --> spinneret[Dry Spinneret 0.1 to 0.5 mm holes]
    spinneret --> tower[Evaporation Tower 200 to 250 degree C]
    tower --> falseTwist[False Twist Texturing]
    falseTwist --> package[Finished Spandex Yarn on Package]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define spandex. What is the minimum polyurethane content required for a fiber to be classified as spandex?**

**Model Answer:**
Spandex is a synthetic segmented polyurethane-polyurea copolymer fiber known for its exceptional elasticity. As per the US Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and ISO standards, a fiber must contain **at least 85% by weight of segmented polyurethane** in its polymeric backbone to be legally classified and labeled as spandex. The fiber derives its stretchability (500–700% elongation) from alternating hard (urethane/urea) and soft (polyol) segments arranged in a microphase-separated morphology. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the three main chemical components used in spandex synthesis. What is the role of the diamine chain extender?**

**Model Answer:**
The three main components are:
1. **Polymeric diol (polyol)** — e.g., PTMEG, polycaprolactone diol (forms the soft, elastic segment).
2. **Diisocyanate** — e.g., MDI or TDI (provides the –NCO groups for urethane/urea formation).
3. **Diam ine chain extender** — e.g., ethylenediamine, hydrazine (links prepolymer chains to build high molecular weight).

The **diamine chain extender** reacts with the NCO-terminated prepolymer to form **urea linkages**, dramatically increasing molecular weight and creating the **hard segment domains** responsible for mechanical strength and elastic recovery. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with chemical equations the **two-step synthesis of spandex** by the prepolymer method. Discuss why MDI is preferred over TDI in modern industrial production.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe the **microphase-separated morphology** of spandex. With a neat sketch, show how the soft and hard segments are arranged and explain how this arrangement gives rise to **elastic recovery**.

#### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Two-Step Synthesis:**

**Step 1: Prepolymer Formation**
React **PTMEG (polyol)** with excess **MDI (diisocyanate)** at 60–80 °C under nitrogen atmosphere, catalyzed by dibutyltin dilaurate:

$$\text{HO–[CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{O]}_n\text{–H} + 2\,\text{OCN–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–CH}_2\text{–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–NCO} \longrightarrow \text{NCO–R–NH–COO–[PTMEG]–OOC–NH–R–NCO}$$

**Step 2: Chain Extension**
React the NCO-terminated prepolymer with **ethylenediamine** in DMF solvent at room temperature:

$$\text{NCO–[Prepolymer]–NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{N–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–NH}_2 \longrightarrow \text{–[Prepolymer]–NH–CO–NH–CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{–NH–CO–NH–[Prepolymer]–}$$

The resulting polymer is then dry-spun through spinnerets into a hot nitrogen atmosphere to form continuous filaments.

**Why MDI > TDI (Industrial Preference):**
- MDI (Methylene diphenyl diisocyanate) is **less volatile** (b.p. ~ 200 °C) compared to TDI (~ 251 °C) → reduced inhalation hazard.
- MDI gives **more symmetrical hard segments** → better microphase separation.
- MDI yields fibers with **superior tensile strength and modulus**.
- MDI has **two NCO groups of equal reactivity** (para positions), giving more uniform polymer chains.
- MDI's **aromatic structure** provides better UV stability and dyeability.

**(b) Microphase-Separated Morphology:**

Spandex's structure is best visualized as a **molecular composite** at the nanoscale:

- **Soft segments (polyol chains):** Continuous rubbery matrix, coiled at rest. Under tension, these chains uncoil and orient along the fiber axis, allowing elongation.
- **Hard segments (urethane + urea blocks):** Dispersed glassy nanodomains (5–50 nm) held together by **3D hydrogen bonding networks** (N–H···O=C). These act as **physical crosslinks**.

**Mechanism of Elastic Recovery:**

1. **Stretching phase:** Applied force uncoils the soft polyether segments. Hard domains remain intact due to strong H-bonding.
2. **Recovery phase:** Upon release, the **entropy-driven recoil** of the soft segments pulls the chains back, while the **rigid hard domains anchor** the chain ends, preventing permanent flow or creep.
3. The fiber returns to its original dimensions with **> 95% recovery**.

**Sketch (ASCII representation):**

```
At Rest:          Stretched:           After Release:
|--[]--[]--[]--|  |-->>>>>>--|         |--[]--[]--[]--|
|--[]--[]--[]--|  |-->>>>>>--|         |--[]--[]--[]--|
|--[]--[]--[]--|  |-->>>>>>--|         |--[]--[]--[]--|
  (Hard)(Soft)     (Uncoiled Soft)      (Recovered)
```

**(14 marks total — awarded as 7 + 7)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **spandex** with **natural rubber fibers** in terms of (i) chemical structure, (ii) elastic recovery, and (iii) chemical resistance. Which is more suitable for **medical compression garments** and why?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A step-growth polymerization for spandex synthesis is carried out with a stoichiometric imbalance ratio of $r = 0.98$ and final conversion $p = 0.98$. Calculate the number-average degree of polymerization using the **Carothers equation**. Comment on the molecular weight implications.

#### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Comparison Table:**

| Feature | **Spandex** | **Natural Rubber** |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Chemical Structure** | Segmented polyurethane-polyurea (synthetic block copolymer) | *cis*-1,4-polyisoprene (natural polymer) |
| **Elastic Recovery** | > 95% | ~ 90% |
| **Chemical Resistance** | High (resistant to oils, mild acids, alkalis) | Poor (degrades with ozone, oils, sunlight) |
| **Crosslinking** | Physical (H-bonded hard domains) | Chemical (sulfur vulcanization) |
| **UV Resistance** | Moderate (better with additives) | Poor |
| **Dyeability** | Excellent | Limited |

**Most suitable for medical compression garments: SPANDEX**

Reasons:
- Superior **biocompatibility** (hypoallergenic).
- **Graduated compression** capability — sustained pressure for hours.
- Resistant to body fluids, sweat, and repeated sterilization cycles.
- Better **dimensional stability** at body temperature (~ 37 °C).
- Lightweight and breathable when blended with cotton or nylon.

**(b) Carothers Equation Calculation:**

Modified Carothers equation:
$$X_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}$$

Substituting $r = 0.98$, $p = 0.98$:

$$\begin{aligned}
X_n &= \dfrac{1 + 0.98}{1 + 0.98 - 2(0.98)(0.98)} \\
&= \dfrac{1.98}{1.98 - 1.9208} \\
&= \dfrac{1.98}{0.0592} \\
&\approx 33.45
\end{aligned}$$

**Molecular Weight Calculation:**

If the average repeat unit molar mass $M_0 = 250$ g/mol:

$$\overline{M}_n = M_0 \times X_n = 250 \times 33.45 \approx 8363 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Comment:** Achieving high molecular weight in spandex synthesis requires **strict stoichiometric control**. Even a tiny imbalance (1–2%) drastically reduces $X_n$, demonstrating the **"critical conversion threshold"** behavior of step-growth polymerization. Commercial spandex synthesis operates near $p > 0.99$ and $r \to 1$ to obtain $\overline{M}_n > 50{,}000$ g/mol necessary for fiber-grade mechanical properties.

**(14 marks total — awarded as 7 + 7)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Not writing both reactions** in the prepolymer method → loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing urethane vs urea linkages** — remember: *OH + NCO = urethane*, *NH₂ + NCO = urea*.
> 3. **Omitting the role of DMF solvent** → 1 mark deducted in process-based questions.
> 4. **Forgetting microphase separation** when explaining elasticity → marks for "theory" not awarded.
> 5. **Wrong units** in molecular weight (must be g/mol, not kg).
> 6. **Not justifying why $r < 1$** in Carothers calculation → KTU deducts for incomplete reasoning.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Spandex:**

- 🔹 Spandex = **segmented polyurethane-polyurea** fiber, ≥ **85% polyurethane** content by weight.
- 🔹 **Block copolymer architecture** → alternating **hard segments** (urethane + urea, H-bonded) and **soft segments** (polyol, coiled).
- 🔹 **Microphase separation** is the structural origin of elasticity.
- 🔹 Two-step synthesis: **(1) Prepolymer (polyol + excess diisocyanate)** → **(2) Chain extension (with diamine)**.
- 🔹 Common monomers: **MDI** + **PTMEG** + **ethylenediamine** in **DMF solvent**.
- 🔹 Commercial spinning: **Dry spinning** (solution in DMF extruded into hot N₂).
- 🔹 Key properties: **500–700% elongation**, **>95% elastic recovery**, density **1.21 g/cm³**.
- 🔹 Trade names: **Lycra® (Invista), Dorlastan®, Creora®, Roica®**.
- 🔹 Applications: Sportswear, **medical compression garments**, swimwear, aerospace pressure suits.
- 🔹 **Carothers equation** governs molecular weight → strict stoichiometric control is critical.
- 🔹 **Hard segment** = mechanical strength + shape memory; **Soft segment** = extensibility.
- 🔹 MDI preferred over TDI → lower volatility, better symmetry, improved fiber properties.
- 🔹 Spandex is **NOT a thermoplastic** in the traditional sense — it is a **thermoplastic elastomer (TPE)** with reversible physical crosslinks.
- 🔹 Hydrogen bonding density in hard segments directly correlates with **elastic recovery percentage**.
- 🔹 Tg of soft segment must be **below −40 °C** for room-temperature elasticity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: carbon fibre
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL607 - PCPOL607
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** carbon fibre

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Carbon Fibre (CF):** A high-performance, man-made fibre consisting of at least **92 wt% carbon**, produced by the controlled pyrolysis of organic precursor fibres (such as polyacrylonitrile (PAN), mesophase pitch, or rayon). The carbon atoms are arranged in a turbostratic, preferentially oriented graphitic (hexagonal) crystalline structure aligned along the fibre axis, which imparts exceptionally high specific strength and specific stiffness.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOL607 – Polymer Composites)** syllabus, carbon fibre is classified as a **continuous, high-modulus reinforcement** used predominantly in **advanced fibre-reinforced polymer composites (CFRP)** for aerospace, automotive, sporting, and civil-structural applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a bundle of **noodles made of graphite pencil lead** — each strand is thinner than a human hair, lighter than aluminum, yet stronger than steel. Now picture these strands glued together inside a plastic (epoxy) "log." The result is **Carbon Fibre Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)** — a material that is roughly **5× lighter than steel** but **5–10× stronger in tension**.

- Each carbon fibre is essentially a "rolled-up" stack of **graphene sheets** (2D layers of carbon atoms arranged in hexagons).
- The orientation of these sheets along the fibre axis is what gives carbon fibre its legendary axial strength.
- The polymer matrix (typically **epoxy**) protects the fibres, transfers load to them, and prevents crack propagation.

### 1.3 Classification of Carbon Fibres (by Modulus & Strength)

| Class | Tensile Modulus (GPa) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Typical Precursor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High Strength (HT)** | 220 – 250 | 3500 – 5000 | PAN |
| **Intermediate Modulus (IM)** | 270 – 320 | 4000 – 5500 | PAN |
| **High Modulus (HM)** | 350 – 500 | 2500 – 4000 | PAN / Pitch |
| **Ultra-High Modulus (UHM)** | > 500 | 2000 – 3500 | Mesophase Pitch |

> [!NOTE]
> The **density of carbon fibre is ~1.75–2.0 g/cm³**, which is why it is the gold standard for **specific strength** (strength-to-weight) and **specific stiffness** (modulus-to-weight) in structural composites.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Carbon Fibre's Exceptional Properties

The extraordinary performance of carbon fibre arises from three structural realities:

1. **Covalent C–C Bond Strength:** The carbon atoms within each basal plane are joined by **sp² hybridized σ-bonds**, whose bond energy is approximately **~348 kJ/mol** — one of the strongest in nature.
2. **Graphitic Crystalline Alignment:** During pyrolysis, the graphene-like layers orient themselves *parallel* to the fibre axis, maximizing load-bearing efficiency along that direction.
3. **Defect Minimization:** Modern processing (especially PAN-based) drives the carbon yield to >55% and minimizes voids, porosity, and grain-boundary defects.

### 2.2 Precursor Types — Step-by-Step Logic

- **PAN (Polyacrylonitrile) — ~90% of global production:**
  - *Why PAN?* It has a high carbon yield (~55–60%), excellent mechanical stability during oxidation, and produces fibres with controllable morphology.
  - Structure: `(–CH₂–CH(CN)–)ₙ` — nitrile groups enable ladder-polymer formation during stabilization.
- **Mesophase Pitch — for ultra-high modulus grades:**
  - Derived from petroleum or coal-tar pitch; contains pre-ordered liquid-crystal (mesophase) domains that graphitize easily.
  - Used when **E > 500 GPa** is required.
- **Rayon (Cellulosic) — historical, low yield (~20–25%):**
  - Largely obsolete; mentioned only for academic completeness.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most-asked formulas** for carbon fibre and CFRP in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fractions of fibre and matrix (rule of mixtures) | Dimensionless |
| $W_f + W_m = 1$ | Weight fractions of fibre and matrix | Dimensionless |
| $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m \, W_f}{\rho_f \, W_m + \rho_m \, W_f}$ | Volume fraction from weight fraction | $\rho$ in g/cm³ |
| $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f \, V_f}{\rho_f \, V_f + \rho_m \, (1-V_f)}$ | Weight fraction from volume fraction | — |
| $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Longitudinal Young's modulus (Voigt / ROM) | GPa |
| $E_c^{\perp} = \dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}$ | Transverse modulus (inverse rule of mixtures) | GPa |
| $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Longitudinal tensile strength (ROM) | MPa |
| $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Density of composite | g/cm³ |
| $\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Major Poisson's ratio (ROM) | Dimensionless |
| $\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c}$ | KTU favourite comparison metric | MPa·cm³/g |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for ROM validity:**
> 1. Fibres must be **continuous, parallel, and unidirectional**.
> 2. Fibre–matrix interfacial bonding must be **perfect (no debonding)**.
> 3. Both phases must experience the **same strain** ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$) — the *iso-strain* assumption.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Boeing 787 Dreamliner — **~50%** of primary structure is CFRP, reducing fuel burn by ~20%.
- **Automotive (F1 / EVs):** Carbon-fibre monocoque chassis in Formula 1; BMW i3 passenger cell.
- **Renewable Energy:** Wind turbine blade spars >80 m long.
- **Civil Engineering:** External strengthening of bridges and columns via CFRP laminates (earthquake retrofitting).
- **Sporting Goods:** Tennis rackets, racing bicycle frames, Formula 1 helmets, fishing rods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between Weight Fraction and Volume Fraction

This is the **single most-tested derivation** in PCPOL607. The complete step-by-step logic is shown below.

**Given:**
- $W_f$ = weight fraction of fibre
- $W_m$ = weight fraction of matrix = $1 - W_f$
- $\rho_f$ = density of carbon fibre
- $\rho_m$ = density of matrix (epoxy ≈ 1.2 g/cm³)

**To Prove:** The relationship between $V_f$ and $W_f$.

**Step 1 — Definition of weight fraction:**

$$
W_f = \dfrac{m_f}{m_f + m_m}
$$

where $m_f$ and $m_m$ are the masses of fibre and matrix in a representative volume.

**Step 2 — Express mass in terms of volume and density:**

Since mass = density × volume, we substitute $m = \rho \cdot V$:

$$
W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constraint $V_f + V_m = 1$, so $V_m = 1 - V_f$:**

$$
W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert the expression to solve for $V_f$ in terms of $W_f$:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{W_f} = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}{\rho_f V_f}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{W_f} = 1 + \dfrac{\rho_m (1 - V_f)}{\rho_f V_f}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange the $(1 - V_f)$ term:**

$$
\dfrac{1 - W_f}{W_f} = \dfrac{\rho_m (1 - V_f)}{\rho_f V_f}
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-multiply and isolate $V_f$:**

$$
\rho_f V_f (1 - W_f) = \rho_m W_f (1 - V_f)
$$

$$
\rho_f V_f - \rho_f V_f W_f = \rho_m W_f - \rho_m W_f V_f
$$

**Step 7 — Collect $V_f$ terms on one side:**

$$
V_f (\rho_f - \rho_f W_f + \rho_m W_f) = \rho_m W_f
$$

$$
V_f \,[\rho_f + W_f(\rho_m - \rho_f)] = \rho_m W_f
$$

**Step 8 — Final closed-form result:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m \, W_f}{\rho_f \, W_m + \rho_m \, W_f}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Because carbon fibre is **denser than polymer matrix**, a given weight fraction corresponds to a **smaller volume fraction**. This is why aerospace designers quote CFRP laminates in terms of $V_f$ (typically 0.55–0.65) rather than $W_f$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A unidirectional CFRP laminate contains **55 wt% carbon fibre** ($\rho_f = 1.8$ g/cm³) in an **epoxy matrix** ($\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³). Calculate the volume fraction of fibre and the longitudinal Young's modulus. Given: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa.

**Step 1 — Identify weight fractions:**

$$
W_f = 0.55, \quad W_m = 0.45
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the derived formula:**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.55}{1.8 \times 0.45 + 1.2 \times 0.55}
$$

$$
V_f = \dfrac{0.660}{0.810 + 0.660} = \dfrac{0.660}{1.470}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_f \approx 0.449 \approx 44.9\%\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Matrix volume fraction:**

$$
V_m = 1 - V_f = 0.551
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Rule of Mixtures for modulus:**

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
$$

$$
E_c = (230)(0.449) + (3.5)(0.551)
$$

$$
E_c = 103.27 + 1.93
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_c \approx 105.2 \text{ GPa}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice that even though the fibre has $E_f = 230$ GPa, the **composite modulus is only ~105 GPa** — a direct consequence of the iso-strain condition and the relatively low $V_f$ in this laminate. Increasing $V_f$ to 0.60 would push $E_c$ above 140 GPa.

---

### 3.3 Specific Strength Comparison (Engineering Decision Metric)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks students to **justify material selection** using specific strength and specific modulus.

$$
\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}, \quad \text{Specific Modulus} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}
$$

| Material | $\sigma$ (MPa) | $E$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (g/cm³) | $\sigma/\rho$ (MPa·cm³/g) | $E/\rho$ (GPa·cm³/g) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | 400 | 210 | 7.85 | 51 | 27 |
| Aluminium 6061-T6 | 310 | 69 | 2.70 | 115 | 26 |
| **CFRP (UD, $V_f$=0.6)** | 1800 | 140 | 1.55 | **1161** | **90** |
| E-Glass/Epoxy ($V_f$=0.6) | 1100 | 45 | 2.05 | 537 | 22 |

**Conclusion (KTU-style answer phrasing):** *"CFRP exhibits a specific strength roughly **23× that of mild steel** and a specific modulus **3.3× that of mild steel**, making it the optimum reinforcement for weight-critical structural applications."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Carbon Fibre Manufacturing (PAN Precursor Route)

The figure below summarizes the entire industrial PAN-based carbon fibre manufacturing chain as a functional block diagram (the standard KTU 2024 representation style for process flow).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acrylonitrile Monomer CH2 CHCN] --> B[Polymerization Free Radical or Anionic]
    B --> C[PAN Powder or Solution]
    C --> D[Wet or Dry Jet Wet Spinning]
    D --> E[PAN Precursor Fibre White Tow]
    E --> F[Stabilization Oxidation 200 to 300 C in Air]
    F --> G[Carbonization 1000 to 1500 C in N2 Atmosphere]
    G --> H[Optional Graphitization 2000 to 3000 C]
    H --> I[Surface Treatment Electrolytic Oxidation]
    I --> J[Sizing Epoxy or Polyurethane Size]
    J --> K[Carbon Fibre Black Tow 1k 3k 6k 12k 24k]
    K --> L[Weaving Braiding or Prepregging]
    L --> M[Composite Layup with Epoxy Resin]
    M --> N[Autoclave Curing 6 bar 180 C]
    N --> O[Final CFRP Component]
```

**Process Block Explanation:**

- **Block A→B (Polymerization):** Acrylonitrile is polymerized to PAN with number-average molecular weight $M_n \approx$ 50,000 – 100,000 g/mol.
- **Block D (Spinning):** PAN solution (in DMF or DMSO) is extruded through spinneret holes to form continuous filaments.
- **Block F (Stabilization):** Cyclization and dehydrogenation convert the linear PAN chain into a **ladder polymer** (thermo-oxidatively stable). Cyclization onset is typically **~200°C**.
- **Block G (Carbonization):** Ladder polymer loses H, N, O as gases; carbon yield rises; turbostratic graphitic domains form.
- **Block H (Graphitization):** Higher temperatures further align crystallites — only for **HM and UHM grades**.
- **Block I (Surface Treatment):** Electrolytic oxidation creates **–COOH and –OH** functional groups for chemical bonding with the matrix.
- **Block J (Sizing):** A thin polymer coating (1–2 wt%) improves handleability and fibre–matrix adhesion.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Internal Structure of a Single Carbon Filament

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIL["Single Carbon Filament 5 to 10 micrometre diameter"]
        D1[Outer Skin Dense Graphitic Sheath]
        D2[Inner Core Turbostratic Graphene Layer Stack]
        D3[Microvoids and Defects less than 1 percent]
    end
    subgraph LAYER["Basal Plane Arrangement"]
        P1[Graphene Sheet sp2 Hexagonal]
        P2[Weak van der Waals Between Sheets]
        P3[Strong Covalent Bonds Within Sheet]
    end
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **anisotropy** of carbon fibre is clearly visible: covalent in-plane bonds (extremely strong, ~7 eV) versus weak inter-sheet van der Waals (~0.05 eV). This is why carbon fibre is **strongest along the axis** and **relatively weak in transverse tension or compression**.

### 4.3 Functional Flow: Load Transfer in a CFRP Laminate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOAD[Applied Tensile Load] --> MAT[Matrix Epoxy 3 to 5 GPa]
    MAT --> ITF[Interface Sizing plus Chemical Bonding]
    ITF --> FIB[Carbon Fibre 230 to 500 GPa]
    FIB --> RESP[Load Carried 60 to 80 percent by Fibre]
    MAT --> RESP2[Matrix Carries Residual Load]
```

**Engineering Insight:** The matrix is the "load-transfer medium." Even though it is much weaker than the fibre, without it, the fibres would simply pull apart like loose threads. This is why **interfacial shear strength (IFSS)** is a critical design parameter (typically 30–80 MPa for epoxy/CF systems).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define carbon fibre. List any four of its characteristic properties that make it suitable for aerospace structural applications.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Carbon fibre is a man-made fibre containing at least 92% carbon by weight, produced by the controlled pyrolysis of organic precursor fibres, in which carbon atoms are arranged in a graphitic (hexagonal) structure preferentially oriented along the fibre axis.

> **Four Characteristic Properties (½ Mark each = 2 Marks):**
> 1. **High specific strength** ($\sigma/\rho \approx 1100$ MPa·cm³/g) — superior to steel and aluminum.
> 2. **High specific stiffness** ($E/\rho \approx 90$ GPa·cm³/g) — excellent dimensional stability.
> 3. **Low density** ($\rho \approx 1.75$–$2.0$ g/cm³) — ~5× lighter than steel.
> 4. **Excellent fatigue resistance** and **corrosion resistance** — no rust, no degradation in salt-water environments.
> 5. *(Optional 5th)* **Low coefficient of thermal expansion** (can be made *negative* or near-zero) — ideal for space structures.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between HT (High Tensile) and HM (High Modulus) grades of carbon fibre. Mention one application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Property | HT (High Tensile) | HM (High Modulus) |
|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | **3500 – 5000 MPa** (higher) | 2500 – 4000 MPa |
| Tensile Modulus | 220 – 250 GPa (lower) | **350 – 500 GPa** (higher) |
| Elongation at break | ~1.5 – 2.0% (higher) | ~0.5 – 1.0% (lower) |
| Precursor (typical) | PAN, lower final heat-treatment temp | PAN or Mesophase Pitch, graphitized |
| **Application (½ Mark each)** | Aircraft fuselage skin, **pressure vessels** (CNG tanks) | **Spacecraft structures**, satellite booms, precision optical benches |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B questions, students frequently lose 2–3 marks for **(a)** not stating the iso-strain assumption explicitly before applying the Rule of Mixtures, **(b)** confusing weight-fraction and volume-fraction formulas, and **(c)** omitting units in the final numerical answer. Always write: *State assumption → Write formula → Substitute → Compute with units → Box the answer.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the PAN-based manufacturing process of carbon fibre with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the stabilization and carbonization stages in developing the final graphitic structure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional carbon-fibre/epoxy composite contains **40% by volume of fibre**. The fibre has $E_f = 240$ GPa, $\sigma_f = 4000$ MPa, $\rho_f = 1.8$ g/cm³. The matrix has $E_m = 3.0$ GPa, $\sigma_m = 80$ MPa, $\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³. Calculate:
  (i) the longitudinal Young's modulus of the composite, **(2 Marks)**
  (ii) the longitudinal tensile strength, **(2 Marks)**
  (iii) the density of the composite, and **(1 Mark)**
  (iv) the **specific strength** of the composite. Compare it with that of mild steel ($\sigma_{steel} = 400$ MPa, $\rho_{steel} = 7.85$ g/cm³). **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $V_f = 0.40$, $V_m = 0.60$, $E_f = 240$ GPa, $E_m = 3.0$ GPa, $\sigma_f = 4000$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 80$ MPa, $\rho_f = 1.8$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³.

**(i) Longitudinal Young's Modulus** (Rule of Mixtures, iso-strain assumption):

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
$$

$$
E_c = (240)(0.40) + (3.0)(0.60) = 96.0 + 1.8 = 97.8 \text{ GPa}
$$

> **E_c = 97.8 GPa** ✅ (2 Marks)

**(ii) Longitudinal Tensile Strength** (Rule of Mixtures):

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

$$
\sigma_c = (4000)(0.40) + (80)(0.60) = 1600 + 48 = 1648 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **σ_c = 1648 MPa** ✅ (2 Marks)

**(iii) Density of Composite:**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (1.8)(0.40) + (1.2)(0.60) = 0.72 + 0.72 = 1.44 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

> **ρ_c = 1.44 g/cm³** ✅ (1 Mark)

**(iv) Specific Strength Comparison:**

For CFRP:

$$
\dfrac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{1648}{1.44} \approx 1144 \text{ MPa·cm}^3/\text{g}
$$

For Mild Steel:

$$
\dfrac{\sigma_{steel}}{\rho_{steel}} = \dfrac{400}{7.85} \approx 50.95 \text{ MPa·cm}^3/\text{g}
$$

$$
\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{1144}{50.95} \approx 22.5
$$

> **The CFRP has ~22.5× higher specific strength than mild steel.** ✅ (2 Marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Re-attempt]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the structure of a single carbon fibre filament. Explain why carbon fibre is highly anisotropic and discuss its consequences in composite design. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CFRP laminate has **60 wt% carbon fibre** ($\rho_f = 1.8$ g/cm³) in an epoxy matrix ($\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³). Calculate:
  (i) the volume fraction of fibre, **(2 Marks)**
  (ii) the weight fraction of fibre, **(1 Mark — see assumption below)**
  (iii) the longitudinal Young's modulus of the composite, given $E_f = 220$ GPa, $E_m = 3.2$ GPa, **(2 Marks)**
  (iv) the transverse Young's modulus using the inverse rule of mixtures. **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $W_f = 0.60$, $W_m = 0.40$, $\rho_f = 1.8$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.2$ g/cm³, $E_f = 220$ GPa, $E_m = 3.2$ GPa.

**(i) Volume Fraction of Fibre:**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m W_f}{\rho_f W_m + \rho_m W_f}
$$

$$
V_f = \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.60}{1.8 \times 0.40 + 1.2 \times 0.60} = \dfrac{0.720}{0.720 + 0.720} = \dfrac{0.720}{1.440}
$$

> **V_f = 0.50 = 50%** ✅ (2 Marks)

**(ii) Weight Fraction:** Stated directly in the problem as $W_f = 0.60$ = 60%. ✅ (1 Mark)

**(iii) Longitudinal Young's Modulus:**

First compute $V_m = 1 - V_f = 0.50$.

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (220)(0.50) + (3.2)(0.50)
$$

$$
E_c = 110 + 1.6 = 111.6 \text{ GPa}
$$

> **E_c = 111.6 GPa** ✅ (2 Marks)

**(iv) Transverse Young's Modulus** (Inverse Rule of Mixtures / Reuss bound):

$$
E_c^{\perp} = \dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}
$$

$$
E_c^{\perp} = \dfrac{(220)(3.2)}{(3.2)(0.50) + (220)(0.50)}
$$

$$
E_c^{\perp} = \dfrac{704}{1.6 + 110} = \dfrac{704}{111.6} \approx 6.31 \text{ GPa}
$$

> **E_c(⊥) ≈ 6.31 GPa** ✅ (2 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from Q.B(b):** The longitudinal modulus (111.6 GPa) is **~17.7× the transverse modulus** (6.31 GPa). This enormous anisotropy is the **single most important design constraint** in CFRP — structures must be designed so that the **load is applied along the fibre direction**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Carbon Fibre (PCPOL607, Module 2)**

- [x] **Definition:** ≥92 wt% carbon; graphitic turbostratic structure aligned along axis.
- [x] **Three main precursors:** PAN (~90% share), Mesophase Pitch, Rayon (obsolete).
- [x] **Manufacturing stages (PAN):** Polymerization → Spinning → Stabilization (200–300°C, air) → Carbonization (1000–1500°C, N₂) → Graphitization (optional, >2000°C) → Surface treatment → Sizing.
- [x] **Stabilization forms a ladder polymer** — essential to prevent melting during carbonization.
- [x] **Carbonization** drives off H, N, O; forms turbostratic graphene stacks.
- [x] **Graphitization** (only for HM/UHM) improves crystalline order and modulus.
- [x] **Density:** $\rho_f \approx 1.75$–$2.0$ g/cm³.
- [x] **Modulus range:** 220 GPa (HT) to >500 GPa (UHM).
- [x] **Strength range:** 2500 – 5500 MPa.
- [x] **Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal):** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — iso-strain assumption.
- [x] **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (transverse):** $E_c^{\perp} = \dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}$ — iso-stress assumption.
- [x] **Weight ↔ Volume fraction formula:** $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m W_f}{\rho_f W_m + \rho_m W_f}$.
- [x] **CFRP has ~20–25× specific strength of mild steel** — primary aerospace justification.
- [x] **Anisotropy is a design constraint, not a defect** — exploit it via ply-angle tailoring (0°, ±45°, 90°).
- [x] **Matrix role:** Load transfer, environmental protection, crack-bridging — never carries primary tensile load.
- [x] **Surface treatment + sizing** are essential for fibre–matrix adhesion (IFSS).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 METALLIC FIBRES — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):**
> **Metallic fibres** are a class of **speciality textile fibres** manufactured entirely from metals, metal alloys, or consist of a polymeric/inorganic core coated, laminated, or embedded with metals, which impart one or more characteristic metallic properties — most notably **electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, electromagnetic (EMI/RFI) shielding, antistatic behaviour, reflectivity, and antimicrobial activity** — to a textile structure.

According to the **KTU PCPOL607 (Polymer Chemistry & Technology / Specialty Fibres)** module framework, metallic fibres are classified under the broader umbrella of **"High-Performance / Functional Fibres"** and are evaluated experimentally in Module Experiment 2 for their **physical, electrical, and morphological characteristics**.

---

## 1.2 Sub-Classification of Metallic Fibres (Syllabus-Aligned)

| **Category** | **Description** | **Typical Examples** |
|:-------------|:----------------|:---------------------|
| **Truly Metallic Fibres** | Fibres spun entirely from a pure metal or alloy | Stainless steel, Titanium, Aluminium, Copper, Nickel, Gold/Silver |
| **Metallic-Coated Fibres** | Polymeric (PET, PA6, PP) fibres coated with a thin metallic layer | Ag-coated nylon, Cu-coated polyester, Al-coated PP |
| **Metallic-Laminated Fibres** | Two polymeric films bonded with an aluminium/metallic strip, slit into yarns | Lurex®-type yarns |
| **Metal-Clad / Bimetallic Fibres** | Two or more metals co-extruded or layered | Cu-Ni clad fibre, Steel-cored aluminium |
| **Carbon/Metal Hybrid Fibres** | Carbon matrix impregnated with metallic nanoparticles | Ag-CNT, Cu-loaded PAN |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wire Inside Your T-Shirt"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of metallic fibres as "tiny, flexible wires woven into your clothes."**
>
> Imagine your regular cotton T-shirt. Now imagine replacing a few of those cotton threads with extremely thin, hair-like metal wires — so thin and soft that you can still wear the shirt comfortably. These tiny metallic threads give the fabric a kind of "sixth sense":
> - They conduct electricity (so static charge can't build up).
> - They reflect radar/EMI (so the soldier becomes less visible to electronics).
> - They can heat up when current passes through them (electric blanket effect).
> - They can kill bacteria (silver fibres in medical bandages).
>
> **In short:** A metallic fibre is to a smart textile **what a nerve is to a smart body** — it transmits signals, energy, or protective functions.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (KTU Highlighted Values)

- **Density of Stainless Steel Fibre:** $\rho = 7.9 \text{ to } 8.0 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- **Electrical Resistivity of Stainless Steel:** $\rho_e \approx 7.4 \times 10^{-7} \, \Omega\cdot\text{m}$
- **Thermal Conductivity of Aluminium Fibre:** $k \approx 237 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
- **Typical Textile Diameter Range:** **8 µm to 100 µm** (for apparel), up to **500 µm** (industrial)
- **Silver Fibre Resistivity:** $\rho_e \approx 1.59 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega\cdot\text{m}$ (lowest of all metals)
- **Aspect Ratio (L/D) for staple metallic fibres:** typically **100 : 1 to 10,000 : 1**
- **Operating Temperature of Stainless Steel Fibre:** up to **600 °C** in oxidising atmospheres
- **Silver's Antimicrobial Threshold:** Effective at concentrations as low as **5–10 ppm** of Ag⁺ ions

---

## 1.5 Why Metallic Fibres Matter — KTU Engineering Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study this topic in PCPOL607?**
> 1. **Industry 4.0 Smart Textiles:** Metallic fibres are foundational to **wearable electronics, e-textiles, and IoT garments**.
> 2. **Defence Applications:** Radar-absorbing materials (RAM), electromagnetic shielding suits, and stealth fabrics.
> 3. **Medical Textiles:** Silver-copper fibre dressings accelerate wound healing via antimicrobial action.
> 4. **Energy & Filtration:** Stainless steel fibre felts for high-temperature filtration and battery current collectors.
> 5. **Polymer Composites:** Metallic short fibres act as conductive fillers in polymer matrices for EMI shielding.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The "Why & How" Behind Metallic Fibres

### A. Why Metals Are Made Into Fibres (The Scientific Motivation)

Metals in bulk form (rods, sheets) are rigid. To become **fibres**, they must satisfy three conditions:

1. **Flexibility:** The fibre diameter must be small enough ($d < 100 \,\mu\text{m}$) so that the bending stiffness drops sharply (since stiffness $\propto d^4$).
2. **Spinability:** The metal/alloy must possess sufficient ductility to undergo plastic deformation without fracture.
3. **Processability:** The metal must survive the textile processing environment (spinning, weaving, knitting, dyeing, finishing).

### B. How Metallic Properties Translate to Textile Performance

| **Metal Property** | **Textile Performance Attribute** |
|:-------------------|:-----------------------------------|
| Low electrical resistivity (Ag, Cu) | Antistatic, conductive, joule heating |
| High magnetic permeability (Fe, Ni) | EMI shielding, magnetic shielding |
| High reflectivity (Al, Ag) | IR/UV reflection, thermal regulation |
| Antimicrobial ion release (Ag, Cu) | Medical, hygiene textiles |
| High melting point (Steel, Ti) | High-temperature filtration, fire resistance |
| Catalytic activity (Pt, Pd) | Chemical protection, filtration |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Scheme exam-ready and mathematically verifiable.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Typical Units** |
|:-----:|:------------|:----------------|:------------------|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{\rho_e \cdot L}{A}$ | Electrical resistance of a fibre | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | $\rho_e = R \cdot \dfrac{A}{L}$ | Resistivity of metal | $\Omega\cdot$m |
| 3 | $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho_e}$ | Electrical conductivity | S/m |
| 4 | $I = \dfrac{V}{R}$ | Current through fibre | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $P = I^2 R$ | Joule heating power dissipated | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $SE = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_i}{E_t}\right)$ | EMI shielding effectiveness | dB |
| 7 | $SE_R = 50 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sigma \cdot f^{-1}\right)$ | Reflection loss (for good conductor) | dB |
| 8 | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{\text{metal}}}{V_{\text{total}}}$ | Volume fraction of metal in blend | dimensionless |
| 9 | $AR = \dfrac{L_f}{d_f}$ | Aspect ratio of fibre | dimensionless |
| 10 | $k_{\text{eff}} = k_m + \phi \cdot (k_f - k_m)$ | Effective thermal conductivity (rule of mixtures, parallel) | W/(m·K) |
| 11 | $k_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{k_f \cdot k_m}{k_f \cdot \phi_m + k_m \cdot \phi_f}$ | Effective thermal conductivity (series model) | W/(m·K) |
| 12 | $\sigma_{\text{comp}} = \sigma_m \cdot \phi_f^{1.5}$ | Composite conductivity (Bruggeman-type approx., $\phi_f \ll 1$) | S/m |
| 13 | $SE_{A} = 8.68 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | Absorption loss for thickness $t$ | dB |
| 14 | $\text{Linear Density (tex)} = \dfrac{m}{L} \times 1000$ | Tex of metallic fibre | tex (g/km) |
| 15 | $\dot{q} = \dfrac{V^2}{R \cdot A_{\text{fabric}}}$ | Power density per unit fabric area | W/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorise equations (1), (6), (8), (10), and (14) — these appear in over 70% of board exam numericals related to metallic fibres.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Industry Sector** | **Application** | **Metallic Fibre Used** |
|:--------------------|:----------------|:------------------------|
| **Defence & Aerospace** | Stealth uniforms, EMI shielding tents | Stainless steel, Cu-Ni |
| **Medical** | Antimicrobial wound dressings, electrodes | Ag-coated PA6, Cu-PET |
| **Automotive** | Heated seats, antistatic carpet | Stainless steel staple |
| **Electronics** | Conductive fabric circuits, RFID antennas | Ag-coated, Cu-PET |
| **Energy** | Current collectors, battery electrodes | Cu, Ni fibre mats |
| **Filtration** | Diesel particulate filters, hot-gas filtration | Fe-Cr-Al (Fecralloy) |
| **Apparel** | Antistatic workwear, fashion (Lurex) | Al-laminated PET |
| **Smart Textiles** | Stretch sensors, biometric monitoring | Ag-coated elastomeric fibres |

---

## 2.4 Manufacturing Routes — The Three Pillars

### **Route 1: Bundle Drawing (Most Common for Stainless Steel)**
1. Stainless steel wire rod → acid cleaning
2. Multi-stage cold drawing through diamond dies
3. Bundle of thousands of wires encased in a copper or iron matrix
4. Repeated drawing until final diameter (8–25 µm) achieved
5. Acid leaching of matrix → loose bundle of fine fibres

### **Route 2: Melt Spinning (Limited to Low-Melting Metals)**
- Applicable for Al (660 °C), Cu (1085 °C with care), Zn (420 °C)
- Crucible melt → spinneret → solidification → take-up
- Limited due to oxidation and brittleness issues

### **Route 3: Coating Methods (For Metallic-Coated Polymeric Fibres)**
- **Electroless plating:** Chemical reduction of metal ions (Ni, Cu, Ag) on fibre surface
- **Sputtering:** Vacuum deposition of metal atoms
- **Electroplating:** Electrochemical deposition
- **Vapour deposition (CVD/PVD):** For ultra-thin uniform coatings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module Experiment 2 typically involves characterisation of metallic fibres for **resistivity (4-probe method), diameter (SEM/optical microscopy), tensile strength (single-fibre tensile testing), and morphology (SEM imaging)**.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Electrical Conductivity of a Metallic Fibre Blend

> **Problem Setup (KTU Board Style):**
> A woven fabric contains a blend of stainless steel fibres (volume fraction $\phi_f$, conductivity $\sigma_f$) embedded in a polyester matrix (conductivity $\sigma_m$). The fibres are aligned **parallel** to the electric field direction. Derive the effective conductivity $\sigma_{\text{eff}}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the model geometry**

Consider a unit cube of side $L$. The metallic fibres are continuous rods of length $L$ aligned parallel to the applied field. The fibre volume fraction is $\phi_f$, meaning the fibre cross-section area is $A_f = \phi_f \cdot L^2$, and the matrix area is $A_m = (1 - \phi_f) \cdot L^2$.

**Step 2: Apply the parallel conduction principle**

Because fibres and matrix share the **same voltage drop** $V$ across length $L$ (parallel paths):

$$
\sigma_{\text{eff}} = \phi_f \cdot \sigma_f + (1 - \phi_f) \cdot \sigma_m
$$

This is the **Rule of Mixtures (Upper Bound — Voigt model)**.

**Step 3: Simplification for non-conducting matrix**

For textiles, the polyester matrix is essentially an insulator ($\sigma_m \approx 10^{-12}$ S/m). Hence:

$$
\sigma_{\text{eff}} \approx \phi_f \cdot \sigma_f
$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example (KTU-style)**

Suppose $\phi_f = 0.05$ (5% stainless steel) and $\sigma_f = 1.4 \times 10^6$ S/m.

$$
\sigma_{\text{eff}} = 0.05 \times (1.4 \times 10^6) = 7.0 \times 10^4 \text{ S/m}
$$

This conductivity is sufficient to dissipate static charge rapidly (antistatic threshold $\approx 10^{-6}$ S/m).

**Step 5: Percolation Threshold Insight**

In reality, when fibres are randomly oriented (2D in a fabric), conductivity follows:

$$
\sigma_{\text{comp}} \propto \sigma_f \cdot (\phi_f - \phi_c)^t
$$

where $\phi_c$ is the **percolation threshold** (typically 0.5–3 vol% for high-aspect-ratio metal fibres) and $t \approx 1.65$ is the **3D percolation exponent**. This explains why even a tiny amount of stainless steel fibre eliminates static.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Joule Heating Power in a Heated Fabric

> **Problem Setup:**
> A heated jacket contains a fabric with embedded stainless steel heating elements. Each element has resistance $R = 50\ \Omega$ and operates at $V = 24$ V DC. The jacket has $n = 12$ parallel elements. Calculate the total power output and power density.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Power dissipated per element**

$$
P_{\text{element}} = \dfrac{V^2}{R} = \dfrac{24^2}{50} = \dfrac{576}{50} = 11.52 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2: Total power for 12 parallel elements**

Since each element sees the full voltage:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = n \cdot P_{\text{element}} = 12 \times 11.52 = 138.24 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3: Power density per unit fabric area**

Let jacket heating area $= A_{\text{fabric}} = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$:

$$
\dot{q} = \dfrac{P_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{fabric}}} = \dfrac{138.24}{0.5} = 276.48 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

A power density of **276 W/m²** is more than sufficient for human thermoregulation (~100 W/m² required in cold environments).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — EMI Shielding Effectiveness (SE) of a Metallic Fibre Fabric

> **Problem Setup:**
> A stainless steel fabric (conductivity $\sigma = 1.4 \times 10^6$ S/m, thickness $t = 1.5$ mm) is used to shield a mobile phone from incoming electromagnetic radiation at $f = 2.4$ GHz (Wi-Fi band). Calculate the absorption loss.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Recall absorption loss formula**

$$
SE_A = 8.68 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}
$$

where $\mu \approx \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m (non-magnetic stainless steel, simplified).

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$
SE_A = 8.68 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times \sqrt{\pi \times (2.4 \times 10^9) \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (1.4 \times 10^6)}
$$

**Step 3: Simplify inside the square root**

$$
\pi \times (2.4 \times 10^9) \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (1.4 \times 10^6)
$$

$$
= \pi \times 4\pi \times 2.4 \times 1.4 \times 10^{9-7+6}
$$

$$
= 4\pi^2 \times 3.36 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
= 4 \times 9.8696 \times 3.36 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
\approx 132.65 \times 10^{8} = 1.3265 \times 10^{10}
$$

**Step 4: Take square root**

$$
\sqrt{1.3265 \times 10^{10}} \approx 1.152 \times 10^{5}
$$

**Step 5: Final multiplication**

$$
SE_A = 8.68 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.152 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
= 8.68 \times 172.8 \approx 1500 \text{ dB (theoretically)}
$$

**Step 6: Practical interpretation**

In practice, $SE \approx 60\text{–}90$ dB is achievable for woven stainless steel fabrics — sufficient to attenuate 99.9999% of incident EM radiation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Aspect Ratio & Tex Calculation of a Metallic Fibre

> **Problem Setup:**
> A stainless steel fibre has diameter $d = 12\ \mu\text{m}$ and length $L = 50$ mm. Calculate (i) Aspect Ratio, (ii) Linear Density in tex, (iii) Cross-sectional area, and (iv) Mass of 1000 such fibres.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Aspect Ratio**

$$
AR = \dfrac{L}{d} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}{12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-2}}{1.2 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 4167
$$

**Step 2: Cross-sectional Area**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (12 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.44 \times 10^{-10}}{4} \approx 1.131 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Linear Density (tex)** — mass in grams per 1000 m

$$
\text{mass per metre} = \rho \cdot A = 7900 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-10} \approx 8.93 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/m}
$$

Convert to tex (g per 1000 m):

$$
\text{Tex} = 8.93 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \times 1000 = 0.893 \text{ tex}
$$

**Step 4: Mass of 1000 fibres of length 50 mm**

$$
m_{1000} = 1000 \times 8.93 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.05 = 4.465 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg} = 44.65 \text{ mg}
$$

This explains why metallic fibres — though dense — can still be used in textiles without excessive weight penalties when diameters are kept in the micro-range.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Metallic Fibre Types

| **Property** | **Stainless Steel** | **Aluminium** | **Copper** | **Silver-coated PA6** | **Carbon (for reference)** |
|:-------------|:-------------------:|:-------------:|:----------:|:--------------------:|:---------------------------:|
| Density (g/cm³) | **7.9–8.0** | 2.70 | 8.96 | 1.14 + Ag layer | 1.7–2.0 |
| Resistivity ($\Omega\cdot$m) | $7.4 \times 10^{-7}$ | $2.65 \times 10^{-8}$ | $1.68 \times 10^{-8}$ | $\sim 10^{-7}$ | $1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 700–2000 | 90–200 | 200–400 | 400–700 | 3000–7000 |
| Max Service Temp (°C) | **600** | 200 | 300 | 180 | 400 (inert) |
| Corrosion Resistance | Excellent | Moderate | Poor (oxidises) | Good | Good |
| Cost Index (relative) | Medium | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Primary Use | Filtration, EMI | Decorative, heating | EMI, conductive | Antimicrobial, e-textile | Structural composites |

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Volume Fraction from Fabric Mass

> **Problem Setup:**
> A 100 g fabric sample contains stainless steel fibres. The fabric is dissolved in concentrated NaOH (which dissolves only polyester, not steel). The residue mass is 8 g. Calculate the mass fraction and approximate volume fraction (using densities 7900 kg/m³ for steel and 1380 kg/m³ for PET).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Mass fraction**

$$
w_f = \dfrac{m_f}{m_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{8}{100} = 0.08 = 8\%
$$

**Step 2: Volume fraction**

First, compute the volume of each component:

$$
V_f = \dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{8}{7900} = 1.013 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
V_m = \dfrac{m_m}{\rho_m} = \dfrac{92}{1380} = 6.667 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 3: Volume fraction**

$$
\phi_f = \dfrac{V_f}{V_f + V_m} = \dfrac{1.013 \times 10^{-3}}{1.013 \times 10^{-3} + 6.667 \times 10^{-2}} \approx \dfrac{1.013}{67.68} \approx 0.015 = 1.5\%
$$

**Step 4: Sanity Check**

A 1.5 vol% loading of high-aspect-ratio stainless steel fibre is **above the percolation threshold** — hence, the fabric exhibits good antistatic behaviour.
